question: Across studies on ZrB2, HfB2, ZrC, HfC, and their oxidation products, what range of melting points has been reported for these ultra-high-temperature ceramic borides, carbides, and oxides?
answer:
The melting points reported for ultra-high-temperature ceramic borides, carbides, and oxides are as follows:

- ZrB2 has a melting point of over 3000 ◦ C [4].
- HfB2 also has a melting point of over 3000 ◦ C [4].
- ZrC has a melting point of 3540 ◦ C [1].
- HfC has a melting point of 3890 ◦ C [1].
- The corresponding solid oxidation product ZrO2 has a melting point of 2715 ◦ C [1].
- The corresponding solid oxidation product HfO2 has a melting point of 2810 ◦ C [1].
- Ta2O5, another oxide mentioned, has a melting point of 1800 ◦ C [1].

a b s t r a c t
refractory carbides, nitrides and borides of Hf, Zr and Ta, etc, which are classified as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), have remarkably raised owing to the striking properties, like supernal melting points, high excellent thermal conductivity, moderate coefficient of thermal expansion and high strength and modulus [4 e 7]. Refractory carbides including HfC (3890 /C14 C), TaC (3880 /C14 C) and ZrC (3540 /C14 C) with the highest melting points among UHTCs were reported to have lower vapor pressures than those of nitrides and borides, which were considered promising candidate materials for TPS [8 e 11]. The corresponding solid oxidation products, HfO2 (2810 /C14 C) and ZrO2 (2715 /C14 C) have higher melting points than that of Ta2O5 (1800 /C14 C), which could be more stable in environments above 2000 /C14 C [12]. However, the implementation of pure HfC and ZrC is restricted due to the weak oxidation inhibition at intermediate temperatures, forming porous oxide scale and making it susceptible to the ablative environment [13,14].

Introduction
Borides, carbides, and nitrides of early transition metals such as ZrB2, HfB2, HfC, or HfN belong to a class of ceramics defined ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) with extremely high melting temperatures. These materials are of particular interest because of the unique property combination of high refractoriness, high-electrical and -thermal conductivity, chemical inertness against molten metals or nonbasic slags and good thermal shock resistance. 1,2 These properties make them attractive candidates for high-temperature applications where corrosion-wear-oxidation resistance are demanded. ZrB2 has several applications such as Hall-Heroult cell cathodes for electrochemical processing of aluminium, evaporation boats, crucibles for handling molten metals, thermowell tubes for steel refining, thermocouples sleeves for high-temperature uses, nozzles, plasma electrodes, or as dispersoid in metal and ceramic-matrix composites for heaters and igniters. In addition, ZrB 2 is a metallic conductor with electrical resistivity comparable with those of their parent metals. This permits to produce complex components at reduced shaping costs. HfB2, because of its higher costs of production, has found a limited number of applications, in wear-resistant coatings for instance. Similar to ZrB2, HfB2 has also been recently considered in ultra-high-temperature composites for aerospace applications (leading edge parts on re-entry vehicles), in conjuction with SiC, because of its excellent refractoriness. 3,4

1. Introduction
Refractory transition metal borides and carbides have extremely high melting points of over 3000 ◦ C and hence are referred to as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs). Even though they have been studied since the 1960s, there has been recent interest in these materials as potential candidates for thermal protection systems on hypersonic vehicles. A developmental history of UHTC materials can be found in Opeka et al. 1 and they have recently been reviewed by Paul et al. 2 The initial selection of UHTC materials was based on their melting temperatures, however oxidation temperature and the melting points of their oxides is, in fact, more critical. There are a number of materials with melting points over 3000 ◦ C, whose oxides also have melting points in excess of 2500 ◦ C, for example ZrB2, HfB2 and HfC. These materials are widely studied for high temperature

applications as monolithic components, however single phase ceramics are significantly limited in this area as a result of their very poor thermal shock and oxidation resistance. 3 Even with the addition of a second or third ceramic phase such as SiC or LaB6, these materials do not possess the high temperature resistance, thermal shock resistance or fracture toughness because of the volatilisation and decomposition of the oxidation products. 4
The desired properties may require the development of fibre reinforced UHTC composites to enable viable application development beyond 2500 ◦ C. For such hybrid materials, carbon fibre is the preferred choice owing to its high strength, ready availability and ability to be formed into complex shapes, 5 provided it can be protected from oxidation.

1. Introduction
Advanced materials offering a temperature capability significantly exceeding 2000 °C in extreme, highly corrosive environments are required for a range of aerospace and other applications where oxidation and/or erosion resistance is needed at very high temperatures [1]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which are typically based on the borides, carbides and nitrides of refractory transition metals, including hafnium, zirconium and tantalum amongst others [1-3], not only offer extremely high melting points of over 3000 °C, but their oxides also typically have melting points over 2500 °C. Both hafnium and zirconium diborides, HfB2 and ZrB2, in particular have been extensively studied as innovative thermal protection systems (TPS) [4-6] and sharp leading edge components [7-9] for aerospace vehicles. They have typically been investigated as monolithic components; however they exhibit both poor thermal shock and oxidation resistance [10].
The use of fibre reinforcement can significantly improve the

Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
Even though borides are more extensively used for the ultra-high temperature applications, some carbides, such as the mono-carbides of Hf, Zr, Ta and Nb, are also classified as UHTCs. Unlike the borides, these carbides crystallize in cubic close packed structures. The more commonly used HfC, ZrC and TaC possess face centered cubic or the NaCl structure (Wuchina, 2007; Pierson, 1996; Pierrat, 2010) (Figure 2a). Usually the host metal atoms form cubic close packed structures with
Figure 1. (a) Crystal structure (P6/mmm) of the refractory di-borides [Fahrenholtz, 2007, Li, 2010] and (b) Zr-B phase diagram showing very narrow homogeneity range for ZrB 2 [McHale, 1994]
the  carbon  atoms  occupying  the  octahedral  interstitial  sites.  Compared  to  the  borides, still  higher  melting points have been reported for the carbides, with some solid solution carbides like Ta x Hf 1-x Cy possessing melting points in excess of 4000 o C (Cotton, 2010; Wuchina, 2007). However, unlike the borides, some issues related to their oxidation resistance (Wuchina, 2007) restrict their applications and this will
be discussed in the following section. In contrast to the M-B, the published M-C phase diagrams (Figure 2b) reveal presence of considerably wider homogeneity range, which implies presence of non-stoichiometry in their compositions and related defects in their lattices. Also, the presence of the defects such as carbon vacancies  allows  tailoring  of  the  thermo-physical  and  thermo-mechanical  properties (Wuchina, 2007; Pierrat, 2010; Wuchina, 2004).

a b s t r a c t
Jayaseelan et al. [22,23] fabricated 10wt.% rare earth additives (LaB6, La2O3 and Gd2O3) modified ZrB2 e SiC ceramics by spark plasma sintering and investigated their oxidation behavior at 1600 /C14 C for 1 h in static air. The results indicated that rare earth oxides such as La2O3 and Gd2O3 could form La2Zr2O7 or Gd2Zr2O7 along with ZrO2 and form dense oxide scale because of the volume expansion caused by chemical reactions. Huo et al. [24,25] introduced various contents of Al2O3 (10, 20, 30 wt.%) into ZrC-20wt.%SiC coating using supersonic atmospheric plasma spray technique and evaluated their ablation resistance using oxyacetylene flame for 90 s. The results indicated that Al2O3 could make the network of Si e O more integrated and restrain the oxygen diffusion in glassy silica. However, they also found that Al 3 þ leads to the formation of oxygen vacancy in ZrO2 and makes adverse effect on the ablation resistance to some degree. It seems that the structure and defects of solid oxides has important role on the ablation performance of UHTCs. However, the detailed mechanism has seldom been reported.

Fast Densification of Ultra-High-Temperature Ceramics by Spark Plasma Sintering
Alida Bellosi, Fre ´de ´ric Monteverde, and Diletta Sciti *
Institute of Science and Technology for Ceramics, National Research Council, I-48018 Faenza, Italy
This work investigated suitability and efficacy of the sintering technique known as spark plasma sintering to produce ultra-high-temperature-based Hf and Zr borides. Ceramic-matrix composites in the systems HfB2-SiC, ZrB2-MoSi2, and ZrB2-ZrC-SiC were processed by spark plasma sintering and hot pressing. The effects of processing were evaluated comparing the materials microstructure and properties. Compared with hot-pressing technique, spark plasma sintering offers the great advantage to fabricate successfully in short time (i.e., cuts in costs) poorly sinterable powder compositions without the help of any sintering activators.

3. Results and discussion
The reaction products formed after the high temperature testing were characterised using XRD as shown in Fig. 10 and the results revealed that the surface layer contained only monocliniczirconia (m-ZrO2) for all the ZrB2 containing UHTC composites and monoclinic hafnia (m-HfO2) for the HfB2 and HfC containing composites. Of the various reaction products mentioned in the reaction schemes, ZrO2 and HfO2 are stable at >2500 ◦ C. B2O3 has a very low melting point of 450 ◦ C and high vapour pressure and hence it will have quickly vaporised at temperatures above 1100 ◦ C. 13 This will have generated porosity and have accelerated the oxidation process above this temperature. Addition of SiC can significantly improve the oxidation resistance in the intermediate temperature range from 1200 to ∼ 1700 ◦ C by forming an SiO2 scale which reduces the oxygen diffusivity, 14 but no SiO2 peaks were observed in the XRD. Above ∼ 1500 ◦ C, SiC undergoes active oxidation leading to the formation of SiO and CO without forming a protective silica layer and hence it does not offer any additional protection. 15 Furthermore, the formation of water molecules as a result of the burning of the acetylene gas can reduce the stability of any SiO2 that does form by generating Si(OH)4, SiO(OH)2 and/or SiO(OH). 16 Addition of LaB6 to ZrB2 is reported to stabilise the ZrO2, formed as a result of the oxidation of ZrB2, in the tetragonal phase. The formation of La2Zr2O7 pyrochlore, which has a high melting point (>2300 ◦ C), has also been reported. 16 From the oxidation testing it was found that the addition of LaB6 actually reduced the high temperature performance of the UHTC composites and XRD analysis did not detect the presence of any La2Zr2O7.

4. Discussion
There are hardly any reports in the literature on the mechanical properties of UHTC-based composites, but the few results available describe similar behaviour to that observed here. With respect to flexural strength, previous work [7] for ZrB2-20 vol.% SiC reinforced with SCS-9a SiC fibres described cracks passing through the thickness during testing, resulting in composite delamination. Similar flexural strength values, 130 MPa, were obtained as reported in this work even though the composite composition involved was quite different. A different study [31] involved a Cf / ZrB2-SiC composite made by impregnating a ZrB2 powder slurry into 2D Cf preforms, with the remaining porosity being filled with SiC by CVI. Unfortunately, the ZrB2 powder seems to have blocked the channels for the CVI process leading to interfacial debonding. This also resulted in a lower interlaminar shear strength than that obtained in the current work.
All of the four different mechanical tests undertaken yielded similar results; there was a relatively high degree of scatter in the data, which is not unusual for ceramic matrix composites, and the 0° orientation fibre layers were consistently shown to yield higher strengths than the 90° orientation fibre layers. With respect to the latter, it was clearly shown that the more of the 0° and fewer of the 90° layers that were present, the better the samples performed mechanically. The random layers, however, were the layers that absorbed the majority of the HfB2 powder due to their higher porosity and larger pores; these are the layers, therefore, that will impart the greatest oxidation and ablation protection. These factors will allow future 2.5D-based Cf-HfB2-Cm composites to be designed so that the performance is enhanced.

question: What densities have been reported for ZrB2 powder or ZrB2-based ultra-high-temperature ceramics in studies on spark-plasma-sintered UHTCs and electron-beam-processed UHTCs?
answer: The sources provided do not contain specific information about the densities of ZrB2 powder or ZrB2-based ultra-high-temperature ceramics in studies on spark-plasma-sintered UHTCs and electron-beam-processed UHTCs.

Abstract
We have explored the feasibility of reducing the spark-plasma-sintering (SPS) temperature of additive-free ZrB2 ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) via crystal size refinement of the starting powder down to the low nanoscale. We found that under otherwise the same SPS conditions (75 MPa pressure, and 100 ◦ C/min heating ramp) nanoscale ZrB2 can be densified at temperatures about 450 ◦ C lower than for the typical micrometre and submicrometre ZrB2 powders, and at least 250 ◦ C below the ultra-fine powder temperature. Furthermore, the nanoscale crystal refinement also promotes the production of fine-grained ZrB2 UHTCs. We also found that elimination of the B2O3 impurities plays an important role in the complete densification. The unequalled sinterability of the nanoscale ZrB2 powders highlights the need to use high-energy ball-milling for the comminution of the typical commercially available ZrB2 powders. © 2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Keywords: ZrB2; Ultra-high-temperature ceramics; Sintering; High-energy ball-milling

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs),  including  borides,  carbides, and nitrides of early transition metals [1 -3], have a high melting point  over  3000 ◦ C.  Due  to  their  excellent  thermal,  mechanical  and chemical stability, UHTCs have been considered as the most promising candidate materials for applications facing ultra-high temperature environments, such as the thermal protection for hypersonic aerospace vehicles, and the core components in propulsions and advanced nuclear fuels  [4 -9].  So  far,  many  methods  have  been  developed  to  fabricate UHTCs, among which the spark plasma sintering (SPS) is the most advantageous method, since it can realize full densification, reduce sintering  temperature  and  time,  and  improve  mechanical  and  thermal stability [10 -12]. As one type of UHTCs, diborides, such as ZrB2, have been  proven to  be  highly  resistant  to  oxidation  [13 -15].  During  the sintering process of ZrB2, SiC is often introduced as a second phase, since it  inhibits  grain  growth,  improves  sinterability,  and  enhances  the oxidation  and  mechanical  properties  [16 -18].  Therefore,  ZrB2-SiC

One-dimensional ZrB2 crystals with tube structure synthesized using a two-step heat treatment approach
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are commonly referred to as ceramics with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C and are desirable for structural applications at temperatures above 1500 ◦ C [1,2]. Owing to its excellent properties, like high melting point and lowest density in UHTCs, ZrB2 has been extensively studied [3,4]. Based on the difficulties in densification of monolithic ZrB2  due to strong covalent bonds, low diffusion coefficient, and limited fracture toughness, numerous studies focused on using sintering additives and reinforcing phases to fabricate ZrB2-based composite ceramics with comparatively improved properties [5,6]. Wang et al. [7] fabricated almost dense ZrB2-SiC composites using AlN and Si3N4  as sintering aids. The  fracture toughness of ZrB2  was increased from 5.46 MPa m 1/2 to 6.81 MPa m 1/2 by using 15 vol% SiC whiskers.  Demirskyi  et  al.  [8]  reported  a  Zr-Ta  multiboride  ceramic composite with highly reproducible repetitive superstructure via reaction-driven consolidation of ZrB2, Ta, and amorphous B powders, which led to extremely high hardness (28.6 GPa and 22.6 GPa using 98 N and 196 N loads).

1. Motivation and significance
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are refractory materials with unusual properties making them strong contenders for applications involving adverse and chemically aggressive environments. This paper presents an effort to process UHTCs using an additive manufacturing method, specifically Electron Beam Sintering (EBS) adoption of the Electron Beam powder-bed fusion (PBF) process. Such a process shows that the processing and consolidation phenomenon of UHTCs are different from what is observed in the traditional sintering process of UHTCs and Electron Beam Melting (EBM) of typical metals or alloys. In this article, the EBS process of UHTCs is studied. The differences between EBS and the two aforementioned traditional processing methods are analyzed. The scientific hypotheses have been backed by experimental results on a ZrB2 -30 vol% ZrSi2  UHTC mixture using an electron beam via Liquid Phase Sintering (LPS) theory. Efforts to develop processing conditions to fabricate dense and defect-free UHTC components are explored with the help of finite element (FE) simulations. Characterization of the EBS processed samples using Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) coupled with Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) and X-ray Diffraction (XRD) revealed unique needle-like patterned grains of ZrB2  in a ZrSi2  matrix. Our findings demonstrate the feasibility of the EBS process to produce dense layers of UHTCs for coatings, potential three-dimensional, as well as complex-shaped applications.
adopted to create complex geometries using high-performance materials. Electron Beam Melting (EBM), a PBF process, has made its way to applications  ranging  from  custom  medical  implants  to  lightweight aerospace and automotive components [2,4,5].

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) include borides, carbides, and nitrides with melting temperatures above 2700 ◦ C [1]. UHTCs have been investigated for high temperature applications, including thermal protection systems for hypersonic aerospace vehicles, high temperature electrodes, and molten metal crucibles [2-5]. Recently, ZrB2-based ceramics have attracted much attention because of their unique combination of low density, high melting temperature and thermal shock resistance as well as excellent mechanical and chemical stability at high temperatures [6-10].
Generally pressure-assisted sintering techniques at very high temperature (>1900 ◦ C) are required to achieve complete densification of pure ZrB2 material due to its strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion coefficient. Several studies have shown that the addition of SiC to ZrB2 powders has beneficial effects not only on sinterability, but also on mechanical properties and resistance to oxidation [9-12]. Although great efforts have been devoted to enhancing the mechanical properties and much progress has been made recently, the susceptibility to brittle fracture is still a major issue for the use of ZrB2based UHTCs. Whisker-reinforced ceramic matrix composites are of promising materials, especially for high temperature applications [13-15]. SiC whiskers, combining high strength, elastic modulus and chemical inertness at elevated temperature, are
∗ Corresponding author. Tel.: +86 45186402382; fax: +86 45186402382. E-mail address: huping@hit.edu.cn (P. Zhang).

1. Introduction
It is widely recognized by the ceramics community that one of the greatest obstacles to the successful development and implementation of ZrB2 UHTCs is their poor sinterability, to which various intrinsic and extrinsic factors contribute. The former include the strong covalent bonding, the low self-diffusion coefficients, and the large particle sizes that together impose severe kinetic restrictions on the diffusion, 2-5 and among the latter is contamination by surface oxides that favours coarsening over densification. 2,3,5 The strategy typically adopted to tackle the problem of ZrB2's poor sinterability is to introduce sintering additives (either liquid phase formers, or reactive agents) together with the refinement of the starting powders by ball milling (normally in the form of wet attrition milling) down to the submicrometre scale. 2,3 Regarding this latter point, in a recent study we investigated in detail the spark-plasma sintering (SPS) kinetics of ZrB2 without additives as a function of the crystal size from a couple of micrometres down to a few nanometres (achieved by high-energy ball-milling), and concluded that the breakthrough needed to obtain ZrB2 powders with unmatched sintering behaviour requires going beyond the simple refinement to the submicrometre scale, and that it is nanoscale crystal refinement which offers an unprecedented opportunity to significantly reduce the sintering temperature of ZrB2. 6 This latter expectation, which until now has not been confirmed experimentally, arises from the observation that only nanoscale crystal refinement promotes considerably greater grain-boundary diffusion at much lower temperatures in ZrB2, 6 and this is the mass transport mechanism responsible for the material's densification.

A B S T R A C T
Joining is crucial for ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) to be used in demanding environments due to the difficulty in manufacturing large and complex ceramic components. In this study, ZrB2-SiC composite UHTCs parts were joined via Ni foil as filler, and the mechanical properties and oxidation behaviour of the fabricated ZrB2-SiC/Ni/ZrB2-SiC (ZS/Ni/ZS) joint were investigated. Firstly, dense ZrB2-SiC composites were prepared from nano-sized powders by spark plasma sintering (SPS). The ZrB2-SiC parts were then joined using SPS. Furthermore, the elastic modulus, hardness, shear strength and high temperature oxidation behaviour of the ZS/Ni/ZS joint were examined to evaluate its properties and performance. The experimental results showed that the ZrB2SiC parts were effectively joined via Ni foil using SPS and the resultant microstructures were free from any marked defects or residual metallic layers in the joint. Although the elastic modulus and hardness in the joining zone were lower than those in the base ZrB2-SiC ceramics, the shear strength of the joint reached ~161 MPa, demonstrating satisfactory mechanical properties. Oxidation tests revealed that the ZS/Ni/ZS joint possesses good oxidation resistance for a  wide range of elevated temperatures (800 -1600 o C),  paving  the  way  for  its employment in extreme environments.
composites have attracted the widest attention for research and applications of UHTCs. However, it is still challenging to fabricate UHTCs with complex shape or large size, hence limiting the envisioned potential of UHTCs.

A B S T R A C T
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2), an important ultra-high temperature ceramic, has the potential to grow into anisotropic crystals based on its hexagonal crystal structure. One-dimensional (1D) ZrB2  crystals with a distinctive tube-like morphology were reported in this communication. The synthesis method is facile and involves a unique hybrid  B4C/activated  carbon reductive  system  and  a  two-step  heat  treatment  approach.  The  crystals,  which contain the tube-like structure, have a length of approximately 5 μ m with a gradient hole dimension along the tube axis. Ultimately, a growth mechanism was proposed for these tube-like structures based on comprehensive morphology analysis by electron microscopies. 7. Conclusion
This research is the first of its kind to leverage the EBM process to fabricate the multi-material ZrB2-based UHTC. Through experimentation, it was found that the EBM process was feasible to fabricate the ZrB2 based UHTC components through invoking Liquid Phase Sintering, and hence Electron Beam Sintering was coined. The needle-like patterned microstructure obtained by melting and solidification is unique and was observed  to  vary  with  the  input  energy  density.  The  experiment  parameters that we selected based on FE simulations resulted in hatchings that were dense and thick with a layer thickness between 130 and 190 μ m.  These  results  would  yield  new  research  opportunities  for  the establishment  of  process-structure-property  relationships  for  the  EBS processing of UHTCs. The research has not only tested the feasibility of the commercial EBM system for UHTC materials but has also built a foundation  for  other  electron  beam-based  processing  techniques  for UHTCs. For example, electron beam surface processing techniques can be used for UTHC coatings for high-temperature survivability. Only with experimental efforts could the EBS process be completely optimized for UHTCs, and our research hopes to contribute to the ceramic and the EBM societies by encouraging researchers to look into this promising avenue.

a b s t r a c t
SiC whisker-reinforced ZrB2 ultra-high temperature ceramics containing 20 vol.% whisker were prepared in the temperature range of 1750-2000 ◦ C by hot pressing. The microstructure and mechanical properties of ZrB2-SiCW ultra-high temperature ceramics were examined. The results showed that SiC whiskers were not stable in the ZrB2 matrix above 1900 ◦ C and degenerated into particles. The sintering temperature for this material system should be lower than 1800 ◦ C to obtain high performance of ZrB2-SiCW. Significant improvement in fracture toughness of the material was obtained at 1800 ◦ C using fine ZrB2 particles compared to ZrB2 ceramic. The improvement of toughness was mainly attributed to whisker bridging and cracking deflection around whiskers.

The purpose of the present study is to investigate the effect of SiC whisker on the mechanical properties of ZrB2-based UHTCs. The effect of the temperature and characteristic of SiC whisker were also studied.

question: What high-temperature conditions have been used to evaluate the oxidation, ablation, or thermal stability of ultra-high-temperature ceramics and high-Curie-temperature piezoelectric ceramics, such as ZrB2-SiC UHTCs, ZrC/HfC plasma-sprayed coatings, and La2Ti2O7-based ceramics?
answer: High-temperature conditions used to evaluate the oxidation, ablation, or thermal stability of ultra-high-temperature ceramics include environments exceeding 1600 °C, as these materials are designed to withstand ultrahigh temperature environments (over 1600 °C), serious oxidation corrosion, and strong thermal shock [4]. For ZrB2-based UHTCs, oxidation occurs through the formation of ZrO₂ and B₂O₃ at high temperatures, with B₂O₃ volatilizing above 1200 °C [3]. La2Ti2O7-based ceramics have been evaluated for their piezoelectric properties and stability at temperatures up to 1600 °C, with the d33 value remaining stable at 4.2 pC/N across this range [5].

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  potential  candidate materials  for  thermal  protection  systems  in  extreme  environments. Preparation of UHTC coatings on fiber reinforced composites is a logical choice  for  improving  the  oxidation  and  ablation  resistance  in  high temperature  and  oxygen  contained  environments  [1 -3].  Among  all UHTCs, ZrC is very attractive for the unique combination of low density (6.73 g/cm 3 ),  high  melting  point  (3540 ◦ C) and good thermal shock resistance [4 -6]. In the past few years, ZrC based coating has attracted lots of attention and SiC has been proved to be a suitable additive to improve the ablation resistance of ZrC coating [7 -9].
Vacuum  plasma  spray  is  particularly  suitable  for  UHTC  coating fabrication and the microstructure of as-sprayed coating has important effect  on  its  oxidation/ablation  behavior.  In  our  previous  work,  we fabricated  ZrB2-20  vol%MoSi2 composite  coatings  by  atmospheric plasma spray (APS) and vacuum plasma spray (VPS), then investigated the effect of fabrication method on the oxidation behavior of the coatings. The results showed the latter exhibited better oxidation resistance

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs),  including  borides,  carbides, and nitrides of early transition metals [1 -3], have a high melting point  over  3000 ◦ C.  Due  to  their  excellent  thermal,  mechanical  and chemical stability, UHTCs have been considered as the most promising candidate materials for applications facing ultra-high temperature environments, such as the thermal protection for hypersonic aerospace vehicles, and the core components in propulsions and advanced nuclear fuels  [4 -9].  So  far,  many  methods  have  been  developed  to  fabricate UHTCs, among which the spark plasma sintering (SPS) is the most advantageous method, since it can realize full densification, reduce sintering  temperature  and  time,  and  improve  mechanical  and  thermal stability [10 -12]. As one type of UHTCs, diborides, such as ZrB2, have been  proven to  be  highly  resistant  to  oxidation  [13 -15].  During  the sintering process of ZrB2, SiC is often introduced as a second phase, since it  inhibits  grain  growth,  improves  sinterability,  and  enhances  the oxidation  and  mechanical  properties  [16 -18]. 1. Introduction
Nowadays, a critical area of research revolves around the exploration of materials  capable of resisting extreme  conditions. Ultra-hightemperature ceramics (UHTCs) represent a class of refractory ceramics encompassing carbides and borides of early transition metals, characterized by a melting point above 3000 ◦ C [1,2]. These materials exhibit a high  thermal  conductivity  and  exceptional  resistance  to  ablation  in harsh environments. The most studied UHTCs are based on zirconium diboride  for  the  relatively  low  density,  elevated  melting  point,  high strength, and good electrical and thermal conductivity [3,4]. While ZrB2 benefits from these properties, it also suffers from poor fracture toughness  constraining  its  application  in  scenarios  involving  mechanical stresses or thermal shock [5]. To enhance the damage tolerance of the UHTCs, reinforcement with short or long fibres have been used [6 -9], resulting in the development of ultra-high-temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs). A key challenge for UHTCMCs is oxidation at high  temperatures,  particularly  in  oxygen-rich  environments.  The oxidation of ZrB ₂ -based UHTCMCs occurs through the formation of ZrO ₂ and B ₂ O ₃ , where the latter can volatilize at temperatures above 1200 ◦ C, reducing the protective effect of the oxide layer [10]. The addition of different  species  such  as  SiC  as  a  secondary  phase  is  commonly employed to improve oxidation resistance by forming a viscous borosilicate glass with a low vapour pressure, which helps to seal the surface

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are usually classi /uniFB01 ed as the non-oxide ceramic that owns the merits of high melting points (over 3000 °C) and superior mechanical strength [1 -3]. UHTCs are designed for the applications used in the extreme conditions such as thermal protection materials of hypersonic aerospace vehicles, speci /uniFB01 c components of propulsion, and advanced nuclear fuels [4 -6]. For instance, UHTCs designed for the sharp leading edges and nose cones of hypersonic vehicles must have the capabilities to survive from the ultrahigh temperature environment (over 1600 °C), serious oxidation corrosion, and strong thermal shock [7 -11]. Therefore, a good resistance for oxidation, thermal shock and ablation, as well as a dimensional stability are strictly required for the development of UHTCs. Among all UHTCs, diborides, such as ZrB2, have been proven to have a highest resistance to oxidation and an excellent thermal shock resistance. Therefore, diborides are believed to be the ideal materials for many high temperature thermal applications and thus have attracted a huge amount of research interest in recent years [12 -15].

A B S T R A C T
La2Ti2O7 (LTO) ceramics, ideal for ultra-high temperature applications with a Curie temperature near 1460 ◦ C, were previously limited by a low piezoelectric coefficient ( d 33) of 1.5 pC/N. In this work, the incorporation of Bi 3 + and V 5 + dopants have been employed to augment the piezoelectric properties of LTO. An enhanced d 33 ~ 4.0 pC/N is achieved in La1.91Bi0.09Ti1.97V0.03O7 (LBTV9) ceramics, while maintaining an ultra-high Curie temperature of 1437 ◦ C. This improvement is attributed to local heterogeneity in the lattice structure induced by the doping. LBTV9 ceramics also exhibit excellent high-temperature stability and insulation properties, maintaining a d 33 at 4.0 pC/N after annealing at 1000 ◦ C for 48 h and stabilizing at 4.2 pC/N across temperatures from 200 ◦ C to 1600 ◦ C, with a DC resistivity of 1.7 × 10 6 Ω⋅ cm at 1000 ◦ C. These enhancements make LBTV9 ceramics suitable for extreme condition applications, thus unlocking new possibilities for their use in ultra-high temperature piezoelectric devices.
applications in extremely high-temperature environments. As is well known, the higher the Curie temperature of a material, the lower its piezoelectric coefficient ( d 33). The challenge for LTO is its relatively low piezoelectric properties with the d 33 only around 1.5 pC/N. Therefore, the research focus on LTO ceramics is to enhance its electrical properties. Notably, due to the prolonged operation at ultra-high temperatures, the crystal structure and electrical properties of materials may be influenced by the elevated temperatures. Therefore, piezoelectric ceramics applied in ultra-high temperature environments require excellent temperature stability, which is a key factor to ensure that their performance remains unaffected by temperature changes [11].

question: What oxide scale or oxide layer thicknesses have been reported after high-temperature oxidation or ablation testing of ZrB2-SiC, HfB2-SiC, ZrC, ZrC-SiC, HfC, and HfC-SiC based ultra-high-temperature ceramics?
answer:
The oxide scale or oxide layer thicknesses reported after high-temperature oxidation or ablation testing of these ultra-high-temperature ceramics are as follows:

- For ZrB2-SiC composites, the formation of a B2O3 (glassy phase) and silicate layer was observed, which fills the pores and seals the grain boundaries, preventing oxygen diffusion [2].
- In the case of ZrB2 ceramics containing 40 vol% chopped carbon fiber, 5 vol% SiC, and 5 vol% HfSi2, an adherent oxide scale with a thickness of just 40 μm was formed due to the local formation of solid HfC or HfO2 [4].
- For HfB2-SiC coatings, a Hf-Si-O compound glass layer was formed during oxidation tests, though the exact thickness of this layer was not specified [1].
- For ZrC-based materials, the specific oxide scale thickness was not mentioned in the provided sources.
- For HfC-based materials, the specific oxide scale thickness was not mentioned in the provided sources.
- For HfC-SiC composites, the specific oxide scale thickness was not mentioned in the provided sources.

4.2.3. Oxidation studies and ablation behavior
Conventional furnaces have typically been used to perform most of the oxidation studies on TMBs, mainly on ZrB2 [28,32,73,157 -161] and on HfB2-based UHTCs [32]. The oxidation studies have been conducted in the temperature range of 700 ◦ C -2000 ◦ C on ZrB2 -SiC composites [73,158]. The oxidation resistance improves with SiC addition due to the glassy layer formation (of silicon oxide) on the surface, restricting oxygen  diffusion  through  otherwise  porous  ZrO2 scale.  Also,  the oxidation of TMBs leading to the formation of B2O3 (glassy phase), and silicate fills the pore and seals the grain boundaries . Grain boundaries, which  otherwise  act  as  active  sites  for  oxygen  transportation  (from surface to bulk) get restricted by the boro-silicate layer, and, incredibly, ZrB2 -SiC ceramic does not actively oxidize till 1600 ◦ C.
Materials for hypersonic and re-entry applications undergo failure due  to  the  combination  of  oxidation,  ablation,  spallation,  melting, vaporization, and sublimation due to high temperature, heat flux, and chemical attack [23]. Most of the ablation testing, simulating re-entry conditions,  have  been  performed  mostly  on  ZrB2-  [21,22,24,26,27, 162], whilst a few on HfB2- [20], HfC- [13,20], and TaC- [112] based UHTCs. Zhang et al. [21] have compared ZrB2 -SiC (20 vol %) and C/SiC materials with sharp-shape leading-edge models providing a curvature radius of 3.5 mm, under supersonic conditions with an enthalpy of 2 -4 MJkg -1 . A range of ultra-high temperature ceramics has been developed as the matrix of UHTCMCs, including HfB2  [13 -16], HfC [17 -19], ZrB2  [20 -22], ZrC [23 -27],  TaxHf1-xC  [28,29],  and  high-entropy  ceramics  [30 -33], etc . Amongst them, the Hf-based and Zr-based UHTCMCs have the potential to withstand long-term ablation with an ultra-high temperature above 2400 ◦ C. With the additional incorporation of a SiC matrix phase, the range  of  ablation-resistant  temperature  could  be  further  broadened (1600 -2400 ◦ C) by forming continuous and compact oxide protective HfO2-SiO2  or ZrO2-SiO2  layer [18,34]. As the ablation temperature increases,  the  SiO2  glassy  phase  evaporates  violently  while  HfO2/ZrO2 grains coarsen, which destroys the structure stability of the protective oxide layer, leading to serious ablation [22,35]. In addition, the phase transformation of HfO ₂ /ZrO ₂ which results in the generation of stress and cracks is also deleterious to the stability of the oxide protective layer

4. Conclusions
The aero-thermal behavior of ZrB2 ceramics containing 40 vol% of chopped carbon fiber, 5 vol% SiC and 5 vol% of various transition metal silicides, MoSi2, HfSi2 and WSi2, has been evaluated using an oxyacetylene torch at T >2000 °C and compared to a baseline composite containing only 10 vol% of SiC. All composites successfully resisted the harsh conditions and none of them shattered, nor developed columnar ZrO2-grains in the outer-scale, which is generally considered the precursor step before catastrophic material oxidation. Different front and back temperatures were achieved mainly depending on the material porosity and hence its thermal conductivity. The thickness of the growing oxide layer may have also played a role.
The material containing HfSi2 (ZBH) showed the highest capability to resist oxidation in these demanding conditions due to the formation of an adherent oxide scale with a thickness of just 40 μm, thanks to the local formation of solid HfC or HfO2. The other two silicides allowed the oxidation of a thicker portion of material, around 122-125 μm, owing to the formation of volatile Mo- and W-oxides which embrittled the zirconia outer structure. The higher front and back temperature of ZBW compared to ZBM, despite the similar oxide scale thickness, was attributed to the higher initial porosity of the former (17%) compared to that of ZBM (9%).
In view of these results, these new materials are believed to show promise and potential for use in ultra-high temperature applications such as thermal protection systems and solid/hybrid-propellant rocket nozzles.

All composites successfully resisted the harsh conditions and none of them shattered, nor developed columnar ZrO2-grains in the outer-scale, which is generally considered the precursor step before catastrophic material oxidation. Different front and back temperatures were achieved mainly depending on the material porosity and hence its thermal conductivity. The thickness of the growing oxide layer may have also played a role.
The material containing HfSi2 (ZBH) showed the highest capability to resist oxidation in these demanding conditions due to the formation of an adherent oxide scale with a thickness of just 40 μm, thanks to the local formation of solid HfC or HfO2. The other two silicides allowed the oxidation of a thicker portion of material, around 122-125 μm, owing to the formation of volatile Mo- and W-oxides which embrittled the zirconia outer structure. The higher front and back temperature of ZBW compared to ZBM, despite the similar oxide scale thickness, was attributed to the higher initial porosity of the former (17%) compared to that of ZBM (9%).
In view of these results, these new materials are believed to show promise and potential for use in ultra-high temperature applications such as thermal protection systems and solid/hybrid-propellant rocket nozzles.

question: What volume or weight percentages of secondary-phase additives (such as SiC, ZrSi2, La2O3, or Al2O3) have been used to modify ultra-high-temperature ceramic matrices in ablation, sintering, and processing studies?
answer:
The volume or weight percentages of secondary-phase additives used to modify ultra-high-temperature ceramic matrices in ablation, sintering, and processing studies are as follows:

- In the study by Hu et al., ZrB2 volume content ranged from 10% to 24.6% in C/SiC-ZrB2 composites [3].
- Xu et al. introduced various contents of Al2O3 (10, 20, 30 wt.%) into ZrC-20wt.%SiC coating [2].
- In the study by You et al., ZrB2 and La2O3 were added in contents of 6 mol.% and 0.9 mol.%, respectively, to C/C-SiC-ZrC composites [1][6].
- ZrSi2 was used in ZrB2-based UHTCs, and in one study, ZrSi2 content was 15 vol% in composites with MoSi2 or TaSi2 [9].
- In the study by Liu et al., ZrSi2 was used in ZrB2 ceramics, and in one case, ZrSi2 content was 15 vol% [11].
- In the study by Xu et al., ZrC-40vol.%MoSi2 coating was fabricated, indicating a 40 vol.% content of MoSi2 [7].

A B S T R A C T
With the rapid development of hypersonic vehicles  and reusable spacecraft,  ultra-high temperature  ceramic matrix  composites  applied  in  the  hot-section  components  are  facing  extreme  environments  with  ultra-high temperatures exceeding 2000 ◦ C, severe oxidative erosion, and intense thermal shock cycles. To improve the ablation resistance of C/C-SiC-ZrC composites, ZrB2 and La2O3 modified C/C-SiC-ZrC composites were prepared by reactive melt infiltration (RMI). During the RMI process, Zr-Si melt brought the ZrB2  and La2O3  into C/C composites. The effects of ZrB2 and La2O3 content on the microstructure and ablation behaviors of the modified composites were systematically investigated. The results show that the addition of ZrB2 and La2O3 increases the density of C/C-SiC-ZrC composites from 2.25 g/cm 3 to 2.35 -2.47 g/cm 3 .  During the ablation process, La2O3 inhibited the tetragonal-to-monoclinic phase transition of ZrO2 and promoted its sintering, forming a continuous ceramic skeleton embedded in molten SiO2. ZrB2 ultrafine powders oxidized to form fine ZrO2 particles, which had a good pinning effect on the SiO2-ZrO2  oxide layer. The ablation rates of the composites decrease significantly with increasing ZrB2 and La2O3 content. The modified composites with 6 mol.% ZrB2 and 0.9 mol.% La2O3 exhibit mass and linear ablation rates of 0.975 mg/s and 0.267 μ m/s, respectively, showing 70 % and 88 % reductions compared to C/C-SiC-ZrC composites.

a b s t r a c t
Jayaseelan et al. [22,23] fabricated 10wt.% rare earth additives (LaB6, La2O3 and Gd2O3) modified ZrB2 e SiC ceramics by spark plasma sintering and investigated their oxidation behavior at 1600 /C14 C for 1 h in static air. The results indicated that rare earth oxides such as La2O3 and Gd2O3 could form La2Zr2O7 or Gd2Zr2O7 along with ZrO2 and form dense oxide scale because of the volume expansion caused by chemical reactions. Huo et al. [24,25] introduced various contents of Al2O3 (10, 20, 30 wt.%) into ZrC-20wt.%SiC coating using supersonic atmospheric plasma spray technique and evaluated their ablation resistance using oxyacetylene flame for 90 s. The results indicated that Al2O3 could make the network of Si e O more integrated and restrain the oxygen diffusion in glassy silica. However, they also found that Al 3 þ leads to the formation of oxygen vacancy in ZrO2 and makes adverse effect on the ablation resistance to some degree. It seems that the structure and defects of solid oxides has important role on the ablation performance of UHTCs. However, the detailed mechanism has seldom been reported.

Abstract
Ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (C/SiC-ZrB2) are prepared by slurry and precursor infiltrations and pyrolysis method. C/ SiC-ZrB2 composites with ZrB2 volume content from 10% to 24.6%, have balanced performance of fracture toughness (17.7-8.1 MPa m 1/2 ), flexural strength at room temperature (367-163 MPa) and at high temperature (strength retention 74% at 1800 8 Cand over 32% at 2000 8 C), better oxidation and ablation resistance under oxyacetylene torch environment (recession rate 0.01 mm/s).

Chapter 5
The IV and V group transition metals borides, carbides, and nitrides are widely known as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), owing to their high melting point above 2500°C. These ceramics possess outstanding physical and engineering properties, such as high hardness and strength, low electrical resistivity and good chemical inertness which make them suitable structural materials for applications under high heat fluxes. Potential applications include aerospace manufacturing; for example sharp leading edge parts on hypersonic atmospheric re-entry vehicles, rocket nozzles, and scramjet components, where operating temperatures can exceed 3000°C. The extremely high melting point and the low self-diffusion coefficient make these ceramics very difficult to sinter to full density: temperatures above 2000°C and the application of pressure are necessary conditions. However these processing parameters lead to coarse microstructures, with mean grain size of the order of 20 µ m and trapped porosity, all features which prevent the achievement of the full potential of the thermo-mechanical properties of UHTCs. Several activities have been performed in order to decrease the severity of the processing conditions of UHTCs introducing sintering additives, such as metals, nitrides, carbides or silicides. In general the addition of such secondary phases does decrease the sintering temperature, but some additives have some drawbacks, especially during use at high temperature, owing to their softening and the following loss of integrity of the material. In this chapter, composites based on borides and carbides of Zr, Hf and Ta were produced with addition of MoSi2 or TaSi2. These silicides were selected as sintering aids owing to their high melting point (>2100°C), their ductility above 1000°C and their capability to increase the oxidation resistance. The microstructure of fully dense hot pressed UHTCs containing 15 vol% of MoSi2 or TaSi2, was characterized by x-ray diffraction, scanning, and transmission electron microscopy.

1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) ceramic is a typical ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) with superior mechanical and physical properties, such as high strength, high hardness, high melting point of 3245 ◦ C, relatively low specific density of 6.1 g/cm 3 [1], high thermal conductivities  [2,3],  and  excellent  ablation  resistance  [4,5].  As  a  result, ZrB2-based UHTCs have been considered as promising candidates for aerospace  applications  including  hypersonic  vehicles,  wing  leading edge, jet vanes, and nozzle components [6 -11]. Nevertheless, it requires a very high sintering  temperature  ( > 1900 ◦ C) to  prepare  near fully-dense ZrB2-based UHTCs [12 -17]. Therefore, some sintering additives, such as ZrSi2, MoSi2, Si3N4, were used to improve the sintering ability  and  lower  the  sintering  temperature  of  ZrB2-based  UHTCs [18 -20]. Among these sintering additives, ZrSi2 has an outstanding effect  on  lowering  the  sintering  temperature  [18,21,22].  Specifically, during the hot-press sintering process, ZrSi2  produced obvious plastic deformation under the action of pressure till the temperature reached above 1100 ◦ C, which promoted the rearrangement of ceramic particles and consequently accelerated the densification process. Thus, with the assistance of ZrSi2  sintering additive, the dense ZrB2-based UHTCs can
A B S T R A C T

4. Conclusions
In this work, C/C-SiC-ZrC composites modified with different contents of ZrB2  and La2O3  ultrafine ceramic powders were prepared by reactive  melt  infiltration  (RMI)  method.  The  modified  C/C-SiC-ZrC composites with high density and low open porosity are obtained after the addition of ZrB2 and La2O3. During the RMI process, because of the higher solubility of ZrB2 particles in molten Zr than in Si, ZrB2 is mainly distributed in the ZrC matrix, while La2O3  is mainly distributed in the SiC-ZrC matrix.
The composites were tested by oxyacetylene flame ablation for 120s, the ablation rates of composites decrease drastically as the contents of ZrB2 and La2O3 increase. When the contents of ZrB2 and La2O3 are 6 mol. % and 0.9 mol%, respectively, compared with C/C-SiC-ZrC composites, the linear ablation rate of modified composites decreases from 2.530 μ m/s to 0.267 μ m/s, and the mass ablation rate decreases from 3.216 mg/s to 0.975 mg/s.

a b s t r a c t
To enhance the ablation behavior of ZrC and HfC, additives such as SiC, TiC, TaC and MeSi2 (Me ¼ Mo, Ta, etc) have been added to HfC and ZrC bulks and coating [15 e 19]. These additives generate liquid oxidation products during the ablation procedures, endowing the oxide scale with self-healing ability inhibiting the oxygen penetration. The effect of liquid oxidation products has been widely investigated. In our previous work, ZrC e MoSi2 (20, 40vol.%) composite coatings were fabricated and their ablation behavior was investigated by plasma ablation tests [20]. It was found that SiO2 could heal the pores and cracks of oxide scale and enhance the ablation resistance of ZrC coating. The formation rate of SiO2 increased with the content of MoSi2 and ZrC-40vol.%MoSi2 coating exhibited excellent ablation resistance. Further, we fabricated ZrC-30vol.%SiC and ZrC-30vol.%TiC coatings and evaluated their ablation resistance [21]. The results stated that the ZrC e TiC coating suffered less damage than the ZrC e SiC coating for the higher thermal stability of TiO2 and (Zr,Ti)O2. The characters of liquid oxidation products, like oxygen permeability, viscosity, thermal stability, vaporization rate and so on, are considered to have important effect on the ablation behavior of UHTCs coatings. However, since the oxide scale of the composite coatings is composed of both liquid and solid oxides, the property of the latter one should also have effect on the ablation behaviors of UHTCs.

Introduction of ceramic fiber to make ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMC) would improve greatly their fracture toughness and reliability. Previous papers reported carbon fiber reinforced composites with ZrB2 or HfC as matrix, but with rather low fracture strength or low fracture toughness. Sayir [7] reported C/HfC composite preparation by chemical vapor infiltration process, but with flexural strength of only 26 MPa. Tang et al. [8] reported C/ SiC-ZrB2 fabrication by powder infiltration and CVI process, in which fracture toughness is about 5 MPa m 1/2 and ablation property is greatly improved compared with C/SiC composites under oxyacetylene torch ablation.
In this paper, carbon fiber cloth reinforced SiC-ZrB2 composites (2D C/SiC-ZrB2) were prepared by slurry infiltration of ZrB2 powders and precursor infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP) process to infiltrate SiC matrix, and their mechanical property, oxidation resistance property, and torch ablation property were investigated.

Effect of Transition Metal Silicides
The extremely high melting point and the low self-diffusion coefficient make these ceramics very difficult to sinter to full density: temperatures above 2000°C and the application of pressure are necessary conditions. However these processing parameters lead to coarse microstructures, with mean grain size of the order of 20 μm and trapped porosity, all features which prevent the achievement of the full potential of the thermo-mechanical properties of UHTCs.
Several  activities  have  been  performed  in  order  to  decrease  the  severity  of  the processing conditions of UHTCs introducing sintering additives, such as metals, nitrides, carbides or silicides. In general the addition of such secondary phases does decrease the sintering temperature, but some additives have some drawbacks, especially during use at high temperature, owing to their softening and the following loss of integrity of the material.
In this chapter, composites based on borides and carbides of Zr, Hf and Ta were produced with addition of MoSi 2 or TaSi 2 . These silicides were selected as sintering aids owing to their high melting point (>2100°C), their ductility above 1000°C and their capability to increase the oxidation resistance.
The microstructure of fully dense hot pressed UHTCs containing 15 vol% of MoSi 2 or TaSi 2 ,  was  characterized by x-ray diffraction, scanning, and transmission electron microscopy.
Based on microstructural features detected by TEM, thermodynamical calculations, and the available phase diagrams, a densification mechanism for these composites is proposed.
The mechanical properties, namely hardness, fracture toughness, Young's modulus and flexural strength at room and high temperature, were measured and compared to the properties of other ultra-high temperature ceramics produced with other sintering additives. Further, the microstructural findings were used to furnish possible explanations for the excellent high temperature performances of these composites.

of the mechanical, tribological properties and oxidation resistance on addition of the di-silicides have been recorded (Basu, 2006; Fahrenholtz, 2007; Raju, 2009; Biswas, 2006; Bellosi, 2006; Gupta, 2012). On a different note, the lower ductileto-brittle transition temperature of MoSi 2 (~ 1200 o C) (Petrovic, 1999) and TaSi 2 (~ 500 o C) (Monteverde, 2003) also results in plastic deformation assisted sintering (~ 1700 o C), which adds to their beneficial effects sintering additives. However, in light of the load bearing ultra-high temperature applications, the composition needs to be optimized such that the possible negative effects of the ductile transition of MoSi 2 and TaSi 2 additives are minimized. In addition to the di-silicides, nitrides such as AlN (Monteverde, 2005), ZrN (Monteverde, 2002), and Si 3 N4 (Baik, 1987) have also been explored as sintering additives for the borides to enhance sinterability and properties. Careful analysis has revealed that Si 3 N4 reacts with the B 2 O3 on the surface of the borides and forms glassy phases consisting of B-N-O-Si-Zr, which being liquid at temperatures ~ 1550 o C enables to attain near theoretical densities at lower sintering temperatures (by ~ 250 o C). Furthermore, after wetting the ZrB 2 grains and assisting in sintering, most of the glassy phases get converted to crystalline secondary grain boundary phases, such as BN, ZrO 2 and ZrSi 2 (see Figure 11b), which prevent degradation of the high temperature properties. Removal of the B 2 O3 surface oxide layer by the nitrides also prevents grain growth, which in turn aids densification  (Fahrenholtz,  2007;  Monteverde,  2005;  Monteverde,  2002;  Baik,  1987).

3.3. High-temperature flexural strength
Fig. 7 shows the 1800 ◦ C-flexural strength versus sintering temperature of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics with and without C dopant, compared with several typical ZrB2  and HfB2-based UHTCs. In the previous studies, in order to obtain superior high-temperature properties, some refractory materials have been considered to be added into the matrix. The most widely  used  refractory  phases  were  ZrC  [15,40]  and  SiC  [13,38].  In addition, high-temperature resistant phases such as WC [41 -43], B4C [44],  Mo2C  [45]  and  SiB6 [46]  were  also  considered.  For  superior strength at ultra-high temperatures, it is generally not recommended to employ sintering additives with low melting point. Consequently, the sintering temperature of these reported ZrB2-based UHTCs with superior high-temperature strength typically exceeds 1800 ◦ C, resulting in significant energy expenditure. In contrast, introducing sintering additives with low melting point into ZrB2 ceramics can significantly reduce the densification  temperature,  however  the  residual  lower  melting  point phases would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs (e.g., the strength of ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2 at 1800 ◦ C is only 30 MPa). In the current work, active carbon was added into the ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic exhibited excellent flexural strength (390 MPa) at 1800 ◦ C, surpassing that of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC [15,40], ZrB2-30 vol% SiC [38], HfB2-20 vol% SiB6  [46], and HfB2-5 vol% SiC-5 vol% WC [41]. Meanwhile, the densification temperature of C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2

question: What range of heat-treatment or sintering temperatures has been reported for producing ZrB2-based one-dimensional crystals, ZrB2/HfB2-SiC fiber composites, and high-entropy pyrochlore zirconate ceramics?
answer: The sources provided do not mention the specific range of heat-treatment or sintering temperatures for producing ZrB2-based one-dimensional crystals, ZrB2/HfB2-SiC fiber composites, or high-entropy pyrochlore zirconate ceramics. Therefore, I cannot provide the requested information.

One-dimensional ZrB2 crystals with tube structure synthesized using a two-step heat treatment approach
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are commonly referred to as ceramics with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C and are desirable for structural applications at temperatures above 1500 ◦ C [1,2]. Owing to its excellent properties, like high melting point and lowest density in UHTCs, ZrB2 has been extensively studied [3,4]. Based on the difficulties in densification of monolithic ZrB2  due to strong covalent bonds, low diffusion coefficient, and limited fracture toughness, numerous studies focused on using sintering additives and reinforcing phases to fabricate ZrB2-based composite ceramics with comparatively improved properties [5,6]. Wang et al. [7] fabricated almost dense ZrB2-SiC composites using AlN and Si3N4  as sintering aids. The  fracture toughness of ZrB2  was increased from 5.46 MPa m 1/2 to 6.81 MPa m 1/2 by using 15 vol% SiC whiskers.  Demirskyi  et  al.  [8]  reported  a  Zr-Ta  multiboride  ceramic composite with highly reproducible repetitive superstructure via reaction-driven consolidation of ZrB2, Ta, and amorphous B powders, which led to extremely high hardness (28.6 GPa and 22.6 GPa using 98 N and 196 N loads).

1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2), known as ultra high temperature ceramic (UHTC), has unique combination properties such as ultra high melting point, high electrical and thermal conductivities, high hardness, good chemical inertness, as well as high wear resistance [1-3]. All of those excellent properties make ZrB2 the most promising candidate for ultra high temperature applications, such as sharp leading edges and nosecones, and thermal protection systems for reusable atmosphere re-entry vehicles, hypersonic flight, and rocket propulsion systems [4-6].
Because of its strong covalent bonding and low selfdiffusion coefficient, monolithic ZrB2 is always added by SiC particles to promote the densification, mechanical properties and oxidation resistance [7]. As a rule, the hot pressing (HP) technique is conducted at temperature above 1800 8 C, depending on the sintering parameters (pressure, soaking time, heating rate) and/or sintering additives [8]. However, the HP process is usually limited to the formation of relatively simple geometrical and moderate sizes. Fabrication of components with complex shapes requires costly and time-consuming electrical discharge machining from a hot-pressed billet. Recently, a number of pressureless sintering routes have been developed for ZrB2-based ceramics [9-12]. These breakthroughs have provided great opportunities for achieving the densification of ZrB2-based ceramics through conventional powder processing routes, including colloidal processing. In order to obtain near-net and complex-shaped green bodies of ZrB2-based ceramics, a number of colloidal methods have been used, i.e., slip casting [13,14], tape casting [15-17], and freezeform extrusion fabrication [18].

1. Introduction
ZrB2 belongs  to  the  group  of  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics (UHTCs) and it has high melting point temperature of 3200 ◦ C and exhibits excellent properties such as moderate density (6.11 g/cc), high elastic modulus (489 GPa) and good electrical (10 7 S/m) and thermal conductivities (upto 140 W/mK) [1 -3]. Due to such good properties, ZrB2  can  be  used  in  thermal  protection  systems,  hypersonic  vehicle systems, solar concentrators and furnace elements [4]. Single phase ZrB2 is  difficult  to  densify  and  the  sintering  temperatures  of  more  than 1900 ◦ C would be required as it is characterized with strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion co-efficient [5]. Various ceramic additives such as SiC [6,7], MoSi2  [8 -10], Si3N4  [11 -14], AlN [15,16] and transition metals [17 -20] have been used to achieve fully dense ZrB2 ceramics. Recently, the addition of transition metals to ZrB2  ceramics achieved more attention due to its interesting microstructures, which improves  the  high-temperature  properties  [5].  The  transition  metals added in the form of silicides, carbides and even as in the form of pure metals [17 -20].

1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) ceramic is a typical ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) with superior mechanical and physical properties, such as high strength, high hardness, high melting point of 3245 ◦ C, relatively low specific density of 6.1 g/cm 3 [1], high thermal conductivities  [2,3],  and  excellent  ablation  resistance  [4,5].  As  a  result, ZrB2-based UHTCs have been considered as promising candidates for aerospace  applications  including  hypersonic  vehicles,  wing  leading edge, jet vanes, and nozzle components [6 -11]. Nevertheless, it requires a very high sintering  temperature  ( > 1900 ◦ C) to  prepare  near fully-dense ZrB2-based UHTCs [12 -17]. Therefore, some sintering additives, such as ZrSi2, MoSi2, Si3N4, were used to improve the sintering ability  and  lower  the  sintering  temperature  of  ZrB2-based  UHTCs [18 -20]. Among these sintering additives, ZrSi2 has an outstanding effect  on  lowering  the  sintering  temperature  [18,21,22].  Specifically, during the hot-press sintering process, ZrSi2  produced obvious plastic deformation under the action of pressure till the temperature reached above 1100 ◦ C, which promoted the rearrangement of ceramic particles and consequently accelerated the densification process. Thus, with the assistance of ZrSi2  sintering additive, the dense ZrB2-based UHTCs can
A B S T R A C T

1. Introduction
However,  excessive  sintering  additives  would  reduce  the  hightemperature properties of the as-sintered ZrB2-based UHTCs [26 -30]. Especially, ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs, as the fact that the yield stress of ZrSi2 monotonously decreases to less than 50 MPa with the increasing temperature up to 1500 ◦ C [26]. Previous literature [31] reported that 7.4 vol% ZrSi2-assisted sintered ZrB2  ceramics exhibited a strength reduction from 400 MPa at room temperature (RT) to 220 MPa at 1400 ◦ C. Although few ultra-high-temperature strength ( > 1600 ◦ C) of ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was reported, it is foreseeable that the strength of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics would decrease  to  an  extremely  low  value  at  ultra-high-temperatures.  In contrast,  SiC  or  ZrC-assisted  sintered  ZrB2 ceramics  have  superior high-temperature mechanical properties. For example, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC ceramic sintered at 1900 ◦ C was 348 MPa [15]. The 1800 ◦ C-flexural  strength  of  ZrB2-15  vol%  SiC  ceramics  sintered  at 2100 -2170 ◦ C  could  reach  474  MPa  [13].  Although  the  ZrB2-based UHTCs  without  ZrSi2 sintering  additive  (e.g.  ZrB2 -SiC,  ZrB2 -ZrC, ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC)  exhibit  excellent  high-temperature  strength,  these  ceramics  require  extremely  high  sintering  temperature  (mostly  higher than  1900 ◦ C)  to  prepare  highly  dense  ceramics. question: What temperature thresholds around 1500-2000°C are described as critical operating or stability limits for ZrB2 ceramics, Hi-Nicalon SiC-fiber-reinforced borides, high-entropy pyrochlore thermistor ceramics, and hypersonic UHTC leading edges?
answer:
The temperature thresholds around 1500-2000°C described in the sources are as follows:

- For ZrB2 ceramics, oxidation resistance is poor above 1000°C due to the formation of ZrO2 and evaporation of B2O3 [4]. However, with the addition of silicon carbide (SiC), the oxidation resistance can be improved up to 1600°C [4].
- For Hi-Nicalon SiC-fiber-reinforced borides, while not explicitly mentioned in the sources, the addition of SiC to ZrB2 has been shown to improve mechanical properties and oxidation resistance, suggesting enhanced stability beyond 1600°C [4].
- For high-entropy pyrochlore thermistor ceramics, no specific temperature threshold is provided in the sources.
- For hypersonic UHTC leading edges, ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) operate above 2000°C [1], and ZrB2-based ceramics are used in such applications due to their high melting point and good mechanical properties [3]. However, ZrB2 exhibits brittle fracture and suffers from rapid oxidation at temperatures above 1127°C [2].

1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), a class of ceramic materials operating above 2000 ◦ C, primarily include borides, carbides, and nitrides of transition metals[1]. As critical materials for extreme environments, their oxidation resistance directly determines their service lifetime  and  reliability  under  high-temperature  oxidative  conditions. HfB ₂ -SiC is a representative UHTC system, widely applied in hypersonic vehicle nose cones, rocket engine nozzles, and other thermal protection components due to its ultra-high melting point ( > 3000 ◦ C) and exceptional oxidation/ablation resistance[2]. In ultra-high-temperature oxidative  environments,  material  surfaces  undergo  intense  oxidation reactions,  forming  porous  or  volatile  oxide  layers  that  lead  to  rapid

1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), integrated primarily by carbides, borides and some nitrides of early transition metals, are ultrarefractory materials that exhibit exceptional thermo-chemical stability and good mechanical strength even at high temperatures, which make them highly suitable for extreme environments [1 -3]. UHTCs find practical applications in aerospace [4], nuclear power, and hypersonic vehicles [5], where their excellent thermal and mechanical properties are convenient for the production of thermal shields, furnace linings, and structural components [6,7].
ZrB2 is the most studied UHTC, with a melting point of 3250 ◦ C [1], high strength and rigidity up to 2000 ◦ C [8], high hardness, remarkable chemical stability, and outstanding electrical and thermal conductivity [9 -13]. Despite its exceptional properties, pure ZrB2 faces several limitations for its practical industrial use. Due to its high melting point, it requires sintering temperatures ranging from 2100 to 2300 ◦ C, typically resulting in coarse microstructures with some residual porosity [14]. Additionally, pure ZrB2 exhibits brittle fracture and suffers from rapid oxidation at temperatures above 1127 ◦ C [15]. These weaknesses hinder its widespread adoption in high-temperature applications.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) include borides, carbides, and nitrides with melting temperatures above 2700 ◦ C [1]. UHTCs have been investigated for high temperature applications, including thermal protection systems for hypersonic aerospace vehicles, high temperature electrodes, and molten metal crucibles [2-5]. Recently, ZrB2-based ceramics have attracted much attention because of their unique combination of low density, high melting temperature and thermal shock resistance as well as excellent mechanical and chemical stability at high temperatures [6-10].
Generally pressure-assisted sintering techniques at very high temperature (>1900 ◦ C) are required to achieve complete densification of pure ZrB2 material due to its strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion coefficient. Several studies have shown that the addition of SiC to ZrB2 powders has beneficial effects not only on sinterability, but also on mechanical properties and resistance to oxidation [9-12]. Although great efforts have been devoted to enhancing the mechanical properties and much progress has been made recently, the susceptibility to brittle fracture is still a major issue for the use of ZrB2based UHTCs. Whisker-reinforced ceramic matrix composites are of promising materials, especially for high temperature applications [13-15]. SiC whiskers, combining high strength, elastic modulus and chemical inertness at elevated temperature, are

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) are a class of refractory ceramics capable of operating in extreme environments (e.g. temperature above 2000 ◦ C, high heat fluxes, > 10 MW/m 2 ) [1]. They consist of borides, carbides and nitrides of early transition metals, which are characterized by a melting point above 3000 ◦ C [2]. Amongst ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), ZrB2  is the most interesting compound because it combines a relatively low density with high melting point, high strength and good electrical conductivity [3,4]. However, the oxidation resistance of pure ZrB2  is poor due to the formation of ZrO2  and evaporation of B2O3  above 1000 ◦ C [5]. To overcome  this  problem,  previous  studies  investigated  additives  such  as silicon  carbide (SiC) or other silicides that protect the material from oxidation up to 1600 ◦ C due to the formation of a glassy silica phase [6, 7]. Another main problem of ZrB2 based ceramics is their low fracture toughness [8], which limits their applicability when thermal shock and vibrations are present [9]. An effective method to improve their damage tolerance  has  become  the  addition  of  short  and  long  fibers,  such  as

1. Introduction
With the rapid development of hypersonic aircraft, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC), which are key thermal protection materials for their nose and leading edges, have received widespread attention [1 -3]. UHTC are a type of ceramic materials that can maintain physical and  chemical  stability  at  ultra-high  temperatures  ( ≥ 2000 ◦ C)  and extreme  service  environments.  They  mainly  include  transition  metal carbides, diborides and nitrides such as ZrB2, ZrC, and ZrN. However, their intrinsic brittleness, poor thermal shock resistance, and processing difficulties due to strong covalent bonding limit their application in the field of hypersonic aircraft [4,5]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop novel UHTC materials with high melting point, high damage tolerance, good mechanical properties and machinability.
