[15]  fabricated Carbon fibre reinforced UHTC composites having various compositions including ZrB2, HfB2 base matrix through slurry impregnation and final densification with carbon through CVI technique. The final densities of the composites were in the range of 1.86 -2.11 ± 0.001 g/cm 3 and reported  that  Halfnium  (Hf)  based  UHTC  offered  superior  oxidation resistance [15]. Reactive melt infiltration (RMI) was also used in the development of UHTCMCs, in which carbon fibre performs were infiltrated wuth Zr or Hf based alloys and facilitated in-situ formation of UHTCM phases [16 -18]. Luo et al. [17] fabricated C -SiC-HfC composite by melt infiltration of HfSi2  alloy in to the porous preform of Cf-SiC composite at 1600 -1700 ◦ C. The reported density and porosity of the composite  were  2.60 ± 0.02  g/cm 3 and  17.4 ± 0.1%,  respectively [16 -18]. 1. Introduction
Characteristics of ZrB2-SiC ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) reinforced with ZrC and carbon fiber (Cf) were investigated in this article. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) process was utilized to fabricate the samples at 1800 °C for 5 min under 30 MPa punch pressure and vacuumed atmosphere. In all samples, the volume ratio of ZrB2: SiC was equal to 4:1, and the summation of ZrC and Cf reinforcements was 7.5 vol% with different ZrC: Cf ratios. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), densitometry, flexural strength, and hardness measurements were employed for characterization of the prepared samples. Microstructural inspection revealed the formation of SiC sheath around the carbon fibers due to several reactions in the surface SiO2 layers existed on the SiC particles. Optimal flexural strength (628.4 MPa) and hardness (20.8 GPa) values were achieved for the sample co-reinforced with 6.5 vol% ZrC and 1 vol% Cf, with a relative density of 97.7%.
enhanced relative density and fracture toughness have been reported for ZrB2-SiC composites by adding 10-30 vol% carbon fiber, the hardness and flexural strength have dropped [19,20]. Adding ZrC can be a good trick for compensating such a drop in hardness and flexural strength. Adding ZrC to ZrB2-SiC composites, individually, has also been investigated by several researchers [21-25]. Table 1 summarizes the average values of relative density and mechanical properties for some of ZrB2-based ceramics containing SiC, ZrC and/or Cf additives.

Zhang et al. [215] refined the approach and achieved exceptional mechanical properties in their fabricated Cf/ZrB2 -SiC material. Starting with a PyC-coated 3D carbon architecture, a slurry impregnation process was  carried  out  with  ZrB2 particles,  utilizing  vacuum  and  vibration assistance to enhance penetration through the weave. The slurry infiltration  step  was  proceeded  by  PIP  process,  in  which  liquid  polycarbosilane  was  introduced  into  the  composite  in  a  high-pressure chamber. After five pyrolysis cycles at 1300 ◦ C, crystalline SiC from the precursor effectively filled much of the porosity between ZrB2 particles. This  procedure  is  schematically  and  experimentally  represented  in Fig.  21.  The  final  composite  achieved  92  %  density  and  showed outstanding  mechanical  properties  compared  to  other  UHTCMCs  reported in literature. A high flexural strength (603 MPa) and high fracture toughness (18.1 MPa m ½ ) were recorded from mechanical testing, and the work of fracture (11.9 kJ/m 2 ) was superior to any other values reported in literature for UHTCMCs. These results are highly encouraging for the prospects of designing dense UHTC-based composites with high mechanical toughness and tolerance.
Continuous carbon fiber composites are currently the leading area of focus for the development of UHTCMCs. Unique processing approaches have made definitive progress in overcoming the relevant manufacturing challenges, where UHTC matrices have been adequately infiltrated  and  densified  in  complex  carbon  fiber  architectures. 3.1 Density of the composites and volume percentage of constituents
The densities and volume percentages of the constituents in the composites are reported in Table 1. The density of the composites has been found to remain consistent across all the prepared samples, as confirmed by a value of 2.4 g/cm 3 obtained for the UD-0/0 ◦ composite, which is comparable with 2.5 g/cm 3 measured for both UD-0/90 ◦ and 8H-SW composites. The volume percentages of the constituent phases have been measured to be similar for both UD-0/0 ◦ and UD-0/90 ◦ composites, having C f fabric with unidirectionally oriented fibers. In contrast, the 8H-SW composite has been found to contain a higher fiber volume percentage, surpassing the amounts in each of the composites madewithUDCf fabrics by approximately 14%. The higher amount of fibers in 8H-SW composite is attributed to its woven fiber architecture, which has resulted in higher C f content in the fabric.
The porosity of the composites made with UD Cf fabric is measured to be in a range of 18%-21%. On the other hand, the amount of porosity in the 8H-SW composite is found to be less by ∼ 50% compared to both UD-0/0 ◦ and UD-0/90 ◦ composites. Such a large difference in the amounts of porosity can be attributed to the existence of lesser intra-bundle as well as inter-bundle porosity in the 8H-SW Cf fabric owing to its processing method, which involves stretching and weaving operations. Despite these differences, the matrix content of the composites with all three different fiber architectures investigated in this study has been found to be consistently similar, that is, in the range of 25%-28%, which indicates that the lower porosity content of the 8H-SW composite is compensated by its higher fiber content compared to the composites having UDfiber orientations.
TABLE 1 Density and volume percentages of fiber ( V f ), matrix ( V m), and porosity ( V p ) of composites with different fiber architectures.

2.1. Hot-pressing
Hot-pressing (HP) is a conventional method for the fabrication of structural ceramics and composites [26]. HP of monolithic UHTCs is di /uniFB03 cult because their high melting points and low self-di /uniFB00 usion constants require a high sintering temperature ( ∼ 2000 °C) [16,27,28]. Various sintering aids and sintering methods such as HP, spark plasma sintering (SPS), and pressure-less sintering (PS) have been used, and high relative densities (98 -99%) have been achieved for UHTC compacts [16,20,24,29,30]. Guo [16] has reviewed the sintering behavior and basic properties of sintered UHTCs.
For the fabrication of C/UHTC composites by HP, carbon /uniFB01 bers are generally used as an additive. Carbon /uniFB01 bers having a length of ∼ 1 mm are known as ' milled carbon /uniFB01 bers ' (Cmil), while those having a length of 1 -6 mm are known as ' short carbon /uniFB01 bers ' (Csf). Yang et al. [31 -33] prepared a 20 vol% Csf-dispersed ZrB2 -20vol%SiC composite (Csf/ZS) by HP at 2000 °C and 30 MPa for 1 h. They achieved a relative density of ∼ 99% and used the same level of relative density as that used for preparing ZrB2-based composites without /uniFB01 ber [31 -33]. Guo et al. [34] also fabricated a 0 -50-vol% Cmil-dispersed ZrB2 -20vol%SiC composite (Cmil/ZS) with a relative density of 98 -99% by HP at 2000 °C under a pressure of 20 MPa for 1 h. They also prepared a 20 -50 vol% Cmil-dispersed HfB2 -20vol%SiC composite (Cmil/HS) with a relative density of 98 -99% by HP at 2100 °C under a pressure of 20 MPa for 1 h [35].

question: What elastic (Young's) modulus values have been reported across these UHTC studies, for materials ranging from a Cf/C-UHTC composite to the ZrB2 phase and carbon-fiber reinforcement used in UHTC composites?
answer: The elastic modulus values reported in the studies range from a significant variation depending on the fiber architecture. The highest elastic modulus value reported was approximately 153.1 GPa for the UD-0/0° composite, measured along the fiber orientation [4]. In contrast, the elastic moduli of the UD-0/90° and 8H-SW composites were found to be less compared to the UD-0/0° by approximately 41.6% and 100%, respectively [4]. This indicates that the orientation of the carbon fibers significantly influences the elastic modulus of the UHTC composites.

chunks.text:
4.1. Carbon continuous fiber reinforcement
Investigations of continuous carbon reinforcements for UHTC-based materials first gained traction in the early 2000s. A study by A. Sayir [184] attempted to use chemical vapor technology to deposit HfC and TaC onto a 2D carbon fabric weave. Although the carbide was incorporated more like a coating than an infiltrated matrix, the investigation represented  an  early  success  at  demonstrating  the  compatibility  between a UHTC material and continuous carbon reinforcement. The authors  of  the  study  alluded  to  the  expected  structural  benefits  of employing a 3D architecture over the 2D fabric that was used at the time. Subsequent studies [185 -192] looked at introducing UHTC phases into composite architectures, although only in small amounts. Initially, it was more strategic to work with systems that were primarily Cf/C or M. Ammendola et al.
Fig. 18. Cross section of the Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composite. (a) The continuous and alternate distribution of long fibers, short fibers, and ZrB2 particles in a SiC matrix; (b) the distribution of carbon fibers, ZrB2  particles, and SiC matrix in the interlayer [185]. Reproduced with permission, © 2007 The American Ceramic Society.
Cf/SiC, since those materials had been developed for longer and were better understood. As such, the UHTC phase was treated as an additive of limited volume percent, with the matrix phase still primarily consisting of carbon or SiC. The microstructure of one such composite can be seen Fig. 18, which depicts ZrB2 particles in a SiC matrix with carbon fiber reinforcement. About 4 vol% of ZrB2 particles were infiltrated into a carbon fiber preform, followed by CVI processes that introduced PyC and SiC as the matrix chemistries.

4.1. Carbon continuous fiber reinforcement
Beyond the traditional UHTC -SiC systems, a number of unconventional  materials  systems  have  been  looked  at  to  enhance  the  matrix phase in continuous fiber UHTCMCs. Yaghobizadeh et al. [202] incorporated the MAX phase Ti3SiC2 into a Cf/ZrC -SiC, as MAX materials can exhibit some of the same properties as metals, including ductility and thermal shock resistance. The fracture toughness of the composite was tested to be 16.9 ± 0.6 MPa m ½ , which is among the highest metrics reported in literature for UHTCMC materials. Additionally, Ti3SiC2 also has a reported melting temperature higher than 3000 ◦ C, making it a candidate for ultra-high temperature applications with further testing and validation of good oxidation behavior. Another novel matrix phase inclusion are the rare earth oxides, which were studied in two papers [203,204]  by  Vinci  et  al.  A  series  of  Cf/ZrB2 -SiC  composites  were manufactured with 5 vol% of different rare earth additives (Y2O3, Sc2O3, La2O3,  Ce2O3).  Aside  from  the  composite  containing  Ce2O3 -which degraded  in  ambient  atmosphere -all  the  composites  showed  improvements  in  high  temperature  flexural  strength  ( > 600  MPa)  and fracture toughness ( > 10 MPa m ½ ) compared to the baseline Cf/ZrB2 -SiC without rare earth additives.  This  was attributed  to  ternary  lamellar compounds forming for La2O3 and Y2O3 composites, and solid solution M. Ammendola et al.

4.1. Carbon continuous fiber reinforcement
One study by Hu et al. [187] commented on the mechanical properties as a function of ZrB2 content in the carbon-reinforced SiC matrix, indicating that the composite ' s flexural strength and fracture toughness decreased with increasing amounts of ZrB2. According to the report, the respective values of strength and toughness dropped from 367 MPa to 23.6 MPa m ½ without any boride to 163 MPa and 8.1 MPa m ½ when the matrix contained 60 vol% ZrB2. Their reasoning was that the boride phase  tended  to  react  at  the  fiber-matrix  interface,  causing  the  mechanical properties of the composite to degrade. Similar trends were observed in later studies involving carbide UHTCs. Chen et al. [191] investigated the effects of adding TaC to a Cf/SiC composite, while Yan et  al.  did  the  same  for  ZrC  [193]  and  HfC  [194].  In  all  three  cases, flexural strength decreased with increasing UHTC volume fraction, and the fracture toughness of the Cf/SiC -UHTC composites was lower than the Cf/SiC baseline. These results indicated a need for process refinement, given the preference for higher volume fractions of the UHTC phase due to superior oxidation resistance.

3.2.1 Elastic modulus and flexural strength
The elastic modulus and flexural strength of the Cf -ZrB 2 -SiC composites prepared with different fiber architectures are reported in Table 2. Among the prepared composites, the UD-0/0 ◦ composite has exhibited the highest elastic modulus value ( ∼ 153.1 GPa), when measured along the fiber orientation. In contrast, the elastic moduli of the UD-0/90 ◦ and 8H-SW composites are found to be less compared to the UD-0/0 ◦ by ∼ 41.6% and ∼ 100%, respectively. In other words, the 8H-SW composite has exhibited the lowest elastic modulus among the fiber architectures investigated in this study. These variations in the experimentally determined values of Young's modulus may be explained by considering that the fibers oriented only along the loading direction of the test (longitudinal direction) contribute to the measured elastic constants. The anisotropy of C f is due to the elastic modulus being very high along the fiber axis, but low in the transverse direction. 1 Such anisotropy of elastic modulus is found in the C-fibers due to the strong covalent C-C bonds in the graphite structure being aligned along the fiber axis, and the presence of relatively weak interatomic (van der Waals) forces between the basal planes along the transverse direction. 55 In both UD-0/90 ◦ and 8H-SW composites, a significant fraction of the fibers have transverse orientation with respect to the test direction, and therefore has not contributed to the estimated value of Young's modulus. It should be noted that 50% of the total number of C f in the UD-0/90 ◦ fabric has a transverse orientation (i.e., five out of 10 laminae possess 90 ◦ orientation) with respect to the loading axis, while each layer of the C f fabric in the 8H-SW preform consists of both 0 ◦ and 90 ◦ -oriented fibers forming a mini-composite lamina.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) are a class of materials that are still in relatively early stages of research and development. These composites are made of an ultra-refractory ceramic matrix reinforced with fibers [1 -3]. Those based on ZrB2 and containing carbon fibers (Cf) have been rather intensively studied in the last years, with research efforts spanning basic science works up to qualification in relevant environments. These materials show indeed good promise as near-zero ablation components, demonstrating increased failure tolerance and refractory qualities [4 -7].
The  introduction  of  up  to  60  vol%  fiber  reinforcement  not  only provides increased failure tolerance and thermal shock resistance, but it also results in a notable weight reduction, and lower machining costs and time. However, the sensitivity of the fibers ' to the sintering environment, in terms of temperature and local chemistry [8,9], necessitates a maximum consolidation temperature of 1900 ◦ C under applied mechanical  pressure  in  order  to  obtain  UHTCMCs  with  the  desired
* Corresponding author. E-mail address:

KEYWORDS
Numerous studies reported during the past decade have sought to reduce the inherent risk of catastrophic failure in the UHTCs by incorporating either short or long Cf . 1,11,12 While reinforcing with short Cf does not significantly alter the failure behavior of composites, the incorporation of continuous C f as reinforcement has been reported to effectively arrest the unstable propagation of cracks during failure. 11,13,14 Therefore, the present thrust is on the development of processing methodologies for continuous fiber-reinforced UHTCMCs (Cf -UHTCMCs). The processing techniques used for the Cf -UHTCMCs have undergone significant advancements in recent years, including chemical vapor infiltration, 15 precursor infiltration and pyrolysis, 16-18 reactive melt infiltration, 19-21 and slurry infiltration. 17,22-24 While chemical vapor infiltration and polymer infiltration are the established precursor-based processes, commercial precursor for the formation of ZrB2 matrix is not available. Therefore, slurry infiltration has emerged as the preferred technique for fabricating the ZrB 2 -based UHTCMCs in recent years.
The selection of fiber architecture in this study is based on the consideration that the ability of ceramic slurry to infiltrate a given fiber preform must be strongly influenced by its overall porosity distribution. Further details have been already published elsewhere by the authors. 25 Also, the slurry infiltration is influenced by the spacing between Cf within a given Cf bundle. Although some porosity remains in the composite, yet increasing the intra-bundle fiber spacing in a controlled manner enhances the extent of homogenous infiltration. It is intuitive that greater homogeneity of slurry infiltration would lead to a more uniform and stronger interaction between the matrix and C f , resulting in superior mechanical properties of the C f -ZrB 2 -SiC composite. 26

4. Conclusions
Complex models in single composition or in functionally graded architecture  were  extruded  using  the  optimized  inks,  immersed  into  a powder  bed  and  consolidated  by  hot  pressing.  A  washer-like  and  a winglet component were safely removed from the sintered powder bed by help of a hobby drill.
This preliminary study resulted in three major findings. First, the direct ink writing of ZrB2-based pastes with varying fractions of short carbon  fibers  is  possible  through  narrow  nozzles,  down  to  250 μ m, preserving their shape and displaying good drying behaviour. Second, alignment of fibers as short as 150 -300 μ m is conceivable, opening the avenue  for  further  development  of  UHTCMC  materials  with  fracture behavior close to that of continuous fiber composites, but obtainable through relatively simpler fabrication techniques. Lastly, the feasibility of  fabricating  complex  shapes  with  a  UHTC  matrix  and  carbon fiber reinforcement was demonstrated for both single and graded compositions,  indicating  progress  in  maturing  the  manufacturability  of  this material class.
D. Gardini et al.

question: What consolidation pressures have been applied when fabricating UHTC composites such as ZrB2-SiC-TaB2 dual composites, ZrB2/TiB2-based UHTCMCs, and Cf/C-UHTC carbon-fabric composites?
answer: The sources provided do not contain information about the specific consolidation pressures applied when fabricating UHTC composites such as ZrB2-SiC-TaB2 dual composites, ZrB2/TiB2-based UHTCMCs, and Cf/C-UHTC carbon-fabric composites.

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
Authors of the present work have focused their studies on the processing of UHTCMCs, adopting the method of slurry impregnation and sintering. This method allowed to achieve high density composites, with high fractions of UHTC phase and a quite strong fibre/matrix interface [1,16,17].  The  most  investigated  option  for  the  matrix  was  ZrB2, because  it  possesses  the  best  combination  of  refractoriness  (melting point  3250 ◦ C),  oxidation  resistance  and  low  specific  weight  (6.1 g/cm 3 ). Blends of ZrB2 and SiC powders were studied to understand the effect of SiC addition on mechanical properties and oxidation resistance of  UHTCMCs  at  intermediate  temperature  (1650 ◦ C)  [18].  In  other focused  studies,  the  matrices  were  based  on  ZrC,  TaC  and  HfC.  The bending strength of these composites was measured up to 2100 ◦ C [19, 20],  showing  very  promising  results.  As  for  the  consolidation  techniques, beside hot pressing [1,16,17], it has been recently demonstrated that spark plasma sintering is an effective method to achieve densification in a few minutes for these composites, provided that the thermal cycle is finely calibrated to avoid excessive reactions at the interface with the fibre [21]. Finally, for the high temperature characterization, high enthalpy arc-jet tests demonstrated the capability of Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composites to withstand temperatures as high as 2500 ◦ C, with limited erosion,  owing  to  the  partial  healing  of  surface  flaws  let  by  fiber burn-out by readily formed silica-based glass [22 -24].

A B S T R A C T
under  oxyacetylene  torch  flame  testing.  Paul  et  al.  [15]  fabricated Carbon fibre reinforced UHTC composites having various compositions including ZrB2, HfB2 base matrix through slurry impregnation and final densification with carbon through CVI technique. The final densities of the composites were in the range of 1.86 -2.11 ± 0.001 g/cm 3 and reported  that  Halfnium  (Hf)  based  UHTC  offered  superior  oxidation resistance [15]. Reactive melt infiltration (RMI) was also used in the development of UHTCMCs, in which carbon fibre performs were infiltrated wuth Zr or Hf based alloys and facilitated in-situ formation of UHTCM phases [16 -18]. Luo et al. [17] fabricated C -SiC-HfC composite by melt infiltration of HfSi2  alloy in to the porous preform of Cf-SiC composite at 1600 -1700 ◦ C. The reported density and porosity of the composite  were  2.60 ± 0.02  g/cm 3 and  17.4 ± 0.1%,  respectively [16 -18]. Very recently, slurry infiltration followed by pressure assisted sintering (hot pressing and spark plasma sintering) was adopted for the development of UHTCMCs [19 -22]. Sciti et al. [19] fabricated carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2 base UHTCMCs by vacuum-bag infiltration and hot pressing, using 1D fabric carbon preforms, having various fiber volume fractions  from  55  to  70  %.  The  reported  bulk  density,  porosity  and flexural strength of these composites are in the range of 2.4 -3.1 g/cm 3 , 16 -28%  and  40 -95  MPa  respectively  [19].  Vinci  et  al.  studied  the oxidation behaviour of carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2 -SiC composites by thermogravimetric  analysis  up  to  1550 ◦ C  and  reported  that  of 800 -1000 ◦ C is  critical  temperature  range,  where  rapid  oxidation

Table 5
Zhang et al. [215] refined the approach and achieved exceptional mechanical properties in their fabricated Cf/ZrB2 -SiC material. Starting with a PyC-coated 3D carbon architecture, a slurry impregnation process was  carried  out  with  ZrB2 particles,  utilizing  vacuum  and  vibration assistance to enhance penetration through the weave. The slurry infiltration  step  was  proceeded  by  PIP  process,  in  which  liquid  polycarbosilane  was  introduced  into  the  composite  in  a  high-pressure chamber. After five pyrolysis cycles at 1300 ◦ C, crystalline SiC from the precursor effectively filled much of the porosity between ZrB2 particles. This  procedure  is  schematically  and  experimentally  represented  in Fig.  21.  The  final  composite  achieved  92  %  density  and  showed outstanding  mechanical  properties  compared  to  other  UHTCMCs  reported in literature. A high flexural strength (603 MPa) and high fracture toughness (18.1 MPa m ½ ) were recorded from mechanical testing, and the work of fracture (11.9 kJ/m 2 ) was superior to any other values reported in literature for UHTCMCs. These results are highly encouraging for the prospects of designing dense UHTC-based composites with high mechanical toughness and tolerance.
Continuous carbon fiber composites are currently the leading area of focus for the development of UHTCMCs. Unique processing approaches have made definitive progress in overcoming the relevant manufacturing challenges, where UHTC matrices have been adequately infiltrated  and  densified  in  complex  carbon  fiber  architectures.  The M. Ammendola et al.
Fig. 22. Photographs of C -C and Cf -UHTC powder composites after 60 s oxyacetylene torch test (T > 2300 ◦ C). The diameter of the composites was 30 mm [232]. Reproduced with permission, © 2012 Elsevier Ltd.

A B S T R A C T
Carbon fibre reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) matrix composites were fabricated coupling water-based powder slurry infiltration, Polymer Infiltration  and Pyrolysis  (PIP)  and  Hot  Pressing (HP)  techniques. This study aims to identify the best sequence of consolidation techniques to better integrate the carbon fibre cloths into an ultra-refractory sintered ceramic matrix of ZrB2-SiC. Infiltrated preforms with UHTC powder slurry were densified via: a) a pre-sintering step by HP followed by two PIP cycles with polycarbosilane, and vice versa, b) two PIP cycles followed by a cycle of HP. Flexural strengths at room temperature and at 1500 ◦ C (167 MPa and 592 MPa, respectively) were found to be significantly higher for composites obtained by the second route, suggesting that sintering of polymer-derived SiC during HP improves the structural properties of Cf/ZrB2SiC  composites. This  work  presents  an effective  method  for  UHTCMC  manufacturing in  a  shorter time  than traditional PIP process.
possessing a good strength even at 2100 ◦ C [13,14], a good fracture toughness (e.g. > 15 MPa m 1/2 ) [13,15,16], an excellent thermal shock resistance ( > 1500 ◦ C) [13], and an excellent ablation resistance above 1600 ◦ C [11,16 -21]. However, the choice of the manufacturing process remains  the  major  concern  for  these  materials.  Selection  of  the  best route is dictated by the need of fast processing/cost-effectiveness combined with quality of performance.
Several processing techniques have been used for the fabrication of UHTCMCs, re-adapted from the CMC manufacturing technologies:

KEYWORDS
hot pressing, infiltration, mechanical properties, slurry
of ceramics and makes them damage-tolerant. 5 Damagetolerant continuous fiber-reinforced ceramic matrix composites (CFCMCs), such as C/C, C/SiC, and SiC/SiC, have found applications as high-temperature structural components. 6-9 Continuous fiber-reinforced ZrB2 -based UHTC matrix composites (UHTCMCs) are being developed in the same line as CFCMCs. UHTCMCs have been also processed in the same way as conventional ceramic matrix composites (CMCs). 10,11 CMCsaremostlyproduced by using four different techniques: polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP), reactive melt infiltration (RMI), chemical vapor infiltration (CVI), and the ceramic route. 6 UHTCMC early development is mostly based on a hybrid process (a combination of two processes). For example, Tang et al. 12 and Wang et al. 13 developed Cf -ZrB 2-SiC composite using a hybrid process that included slurry infiltration followed by CVI. A Cf-ZrB2-SiC composite was also fabricated using a hybrid process that included PIP and slurry impregnation. 14-16 These are incremental advancements of a C f -SiC composite with ZrB 2 added via slurry impregnation. The RMI process is also wieldy used for the fabrication of UHTCMCs. 17-19 After adapting the ceramic route of processing of CMC, substantial progress in the processing of UHTCMCs has been made. The ceramic route consists of a slurry infiltration followed by densification through high-temperature sintering (both pressureless and pressure-assisted). The classic examples of using ceramic route processed composites are oxide-oxide CMCs 20 and nano-infiltration and transient eutectoid process for SiC-based CMCs. 21 Recently, huge developments for the processing of UHTCMCs have been carried out worldwide using a similar ceramic route. 22-27 However, in these studies, unidirectional carbon fabric were used.

question: What powder ball-milling or mixing times have been used when preparing raw powders for UHTC composites like ZrB2-SiC-TaB2 dual composites, laminated ZrB2-SiC ceramics, and Hf-Nb-B4C-TiC-TiB2 based Cf/C-UHTC composites?
answer: For the ZrB2-SiC-TaB2 dual composites, the powders were subjected to high-energy mechanical activation in a planetary ball mill for 3 min at 1820 rpm [2]. For the laminated ZrB2-SiC ceramics, the ZrB2 powder was ball milled for 36 h at 150 rpm [1], and the slurry was mixed for 12 h at 150 rpm [1]. For the Hf-Nb-B4C-TiC-TiB2 based Cf/C-UHTC composites, the information is not provided in the given sources.

chunks.text:
2.1 Raw materials and processing
The UHTC matrix phase was produced using commercially available ZrB 2 ( d 50 ∼ 1.5-3.0 µ m) and SiC powders ( d 50 ∼ 0.5 µ m) obtained from H.C. Starck. The as-received ZrB2 powder was subjected to extensive ball milling (36 h) in a planetary ball mill (Pervrerliser-5, Fritsch, GmbH) prior to its incorporation into the slurry. The ballmilled ZrB 2 powder had an average particle size ( d 50 ) of ∼ 1 µ m. Deionized (DI) water was employed as the aqueous mediumfor preparing the ceramic slurry, which contained uniformly blended 1.5 wt.% polyethyleneimine (PEI) as dispersant. The polyacrylonitrile-based C f with a diameter of ∼ 7 µ m, which were either unidirectionally oriented (T700, 6k, Toray) or in the form of an 8H-SW (T300, 3k, Toray) architecture, were used as the reinforcement.
Thepreparation of the ceramic slurry involved mixing of ZrB2 -20% SiC powder with a premixed liquid containing DI water and 1.5 wt.% PEI. To ensure thorough homogenization, the slurry was mixed in a planetary ball mill
(Pervrerliser-5, Fritsch, GmbH) at 150 rpm for 12 h. The composites, incorporating UD fiber orientations (0/0 ◦ and 0/90 ◦ ) and 8H-SW Cf fabrics, were fabricated in the form of tiles ( ∼ 120 × 120 × 3.2 mm) through ceramic slurry infiltration by a process carried out manually (by hand) to layup and stack 8-10 layers of infiltrated fabrics one above another (as shown in Figure 1), which was followed by hot pressing (Hind High Vacuum, Pvt. Ltd.). The details about the processing method can be found in our earlier publication. 26 For clarity, the sintered composites are designated as UD-0/0 ◦ , UD-0/90 ◦ , and 8H-SW based on their respective fiber architectures.

2. Materials and methods
The  investigation  was  performed  on  ceramic  composite  samples obtained from ZrB2, SiC, and TaB2 powders. The samples were prepared by high-energy mechanical activation of ZrB2, SiC, and TaB2 powders in a planetary ball mill for 3 min at 1820 rpm with Y-TZP milling balls in a protective gas environment (argon). The average particle size of ZrB2 in the mixture was 2.33 ± 1.65 μ m, TaB2  1.7 ± 0.9 μ m, and SiC 4.51 ± 3.01 μ m.
The matrix phase of the dual composites consisted of ZrB2 and SiC in the ratio of 80/20 vol %. The inclusion phase consisted of TaB2 and SiC in similar proportions (dual composite). A composite made of 72 vol % of ZrB2, 20 vol % of SiC, and 8 vol % of TaB2  with a uniform volume distribution of components was used as a control sample (homogeneous composite, HC).

2.1. Fabrication of composites
Continuous  carbon  fibers  (T700,  supplied  by  Toray  Industries, Japan)  were  used  as  the  reinforcing  materials  for  the  fabrication  of composites. Commercial ceramic powders were used for the fabrication of  Cf/ZrB2-based  composites.  ZrB2  (purity: > 99.5%,  average  particle size: 1 GLYPH<0> 3 μ m, supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd,  China)  and  ZrSi2 (purity: > 99.5%,  average  particle  size:  5 μ m, supplied  by  Shanghai  Buhan  Chemical  Technology  Co.,  Ltd.,  China) were used as the raw UHTC powders (75 vol.% ZrB2 + 25 vol.% ZrSi2).
Fig. 1. Schematic diagram of the fabrication process for the UHTC composites: (a) C fiber pre-treatment, (b) positive charge of UHTC particles, (c) electrophoretic deposition, (d) sintering and densification.
Fig. 1 illustrates the processing route for the fabrication of the Cf/ ZrB2-based composites, which includes the pre-treatment of carbon fiber with  polydopamine  (PDA)  (Fig.  1(a)),  the  positive  charge  of  UHTC particles (Fig. 1(b)), electrophoretic deposition (EPD) of UHTC particles on the carbon fibers (Fig. 1(c)), and hot pressing for densification (Fig. 1 (d)).

2.1. Processing
The UHTC matrix phase was produced by using commercially available ZrB2 (d50 ~1.5 -3.0 μ m) and SiC powders (d50 ~ 0.5 μ m) obtained from H. C. Starck, Germany. The ZrB2 powder was reduced in size to an average particle diameter of approximately 1.2 μ m using highenergy planetary ball milling (Pulverisette 5, Fritsch, GmbH, Germany). This process involved wet milling of the as-received ZrB2 powders in acetone within a tungsten carbide (WC) vial for 36 h at a rotational speed of 150 rpm, by utilizing WC balls as the milling media. The aqueous medium for preparing the ceramic slurry was de-ionized (DI) water, which was uniformly mixed with 1.5 wt% of polyethyleneimine (PEI). Unidirectional Cf-fabric, comprising PAN-based Cf filaments (TC35, 12 K, Tairyfil, Taiwan) with 7 μ m diameter, was utilized as the reinforcement. The ceramic slurry with 20 vol % solid loading was prepared by blending the ZrB2-20 % SiC composite powder (dispersed solid content) with a premix liquid consisting of DI water as the solvent and 1.5 wt% of PEI as the dispersant. To ensure complete compositional homogeneity, the slurry was mixed using a planetary ball mill (Pulverisette 5, Fritsch, GmbH, Germany) containing WC vials of 225 ml capacity. The vials were rotated at 150 RPM for 12 h with WC balls as the milling media, maintaining a ball-to-powder weight ratio of 2:1. The pre-treatment of the Cf-fabrics was performed using two methods: heat treatment and ultra-sonication. During heat treatment, the Cf-fabrics were heated to 500 ◦ C and held for 0.5 h in argon atmosphere. On the other hand, ultrasonication of the Cf-fabrics was carried out at a frequency of 40 khz for 20 min in a bath of DI water.

2.1. Raw materials
The UHTC matrix phase was fabricated using commercially available ZrB2 (d50 ~ 1.5 -3.0 μ m) and SiC powders (d50 ~ 0.5 μ m) obtained from H. C. Starck, Germany. The ZrB2 powder was ground to finer size (d50 ~ 1.2 μ m) using a high-energy planetary ball mill before use in preparing the composite slurry. The DI water served as the aqueous medium for preparing the ceramic slurry, which was uniformly mixed with 1.5 wt% of polyethyleneimine (PEI). The fabric of PAN based Cf-fibres (T700, 6k, Toray, Japan) having a diameter of 7 μ m, along with an architecture comprising either a unidirectional fibre orientation or an 8-harness satin weave was used as the reinforcement.

question: Across studies on ZrB2-SiC composites, TaC-HfC solid solutions, and HfC/TaC binary carbides, what range of melting points has been reported for these ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) systems?
answer: The melting points reported for these ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) systems range from above 3000 ℃ for UHTCs in general [1], up to approximately 4000 ℃ for Ta4HfC5 [1], and as high as 4300 K (which is approximately 4027 ℃) for Ta1-xHfxC solid solutions when x = 0.2 [5].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) typically refer to a type of ceramic material with a melting point above 3000 ℃ ,  including carbides, nitrides and borides of transition metals such as Zr, Hf, and Ta [1 -3]. Among them, TaC and HfC are two types that have received broad wide attention and research because of their advantages such as high melting  point,  high  hardness  and  high  modulus  [4 -6].  Due  to  the contraction of the lanthanide series, the atomic radii of Ta and Hf are very close, which means TaC and HfC can theoretically be infinitely soluble and can form a series of Ta-Hf-C solid solutions. According to the existing literature reports [7 -9], Ta4HfC5 synthesized with a molar ratio of 4:1 between TaC and HfC has a melting point close to 4000 ℃ , and also  possesses  excellent  thermal  and  physical  properties  as  well  as anti-oxidation and erosion resistance. Therefore, it has great potential for application in ultra-high temperature service environments [10].

1. Introduction
Generally,  Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  refer  to  ceramics with melting points above 3000 ◦ C, including carbides, nitrides and borides of transition metals such as Zr, Hf, and Ta [1 -3]. Known for their exceptionally high melting points, hardness, modulus and excellent high-temperature oxidation resistance, UHTCs have garnered significant attention in thermal protection applications [4]. Among them, HfC and TaC are extensively studied [5]. Ta and Hf, located in groups IVB and VB of  the  periodic  table,  have  similar  atomic  radii  due  to  lanthanide contraction, enabling unlimited mutual solubility to form Ta-Hf-C solid solutions [6]. Ta4HfC5  synthesized in a molar ratio of 4:1 of TaC:HfC exhibits  a  melting  point  approaching  4000 ◦ C  [7].  Due  to  the  high melting  point  and  superior  thermophysical  and  oxidation-resistant properties inherited from TaC and HfC, Ta4HfC5  holds promising applications in extreme high-temperature aerospace environments [8 -10].
To apply Ta4HfC5 in thermal protection materials, two fundamental

1. Introduction
vast majority of transition metal carbides TMC (TM = Ti, Zr, Nb, Ta and Hf)  exhibit  exceptionally  high  melting  points  and  possess  analogous NaCl-type crystalline structures, facilitating the formation of continuous solid solutions [12]. Consequently, the requirement for ultra-high temperature sintering, exceeding 2000 ◦ C, to attain complete densification of UHTCs inevitably results in the formation of a homogeneous solid solution  phase.  In  that  case,  the  achievement  of  high-density  multi-component UHTC ceramics with heterogeneous solid solution poses a significant challenge. For example, HfC and TaC have attracted significant attention as they are the only two binary UHTCs with a melting point over 4000 K, which surpasses even the flame temperature produced by rocket propellant [3]. Cedillos-Barraza et al. [13] achieved single-phase Ta0.8Hf0.2C ceramics with a relative density of 95 % by spark plasma sintering (SPS) at 2450 ◦ C for 20 min. In contrast, an uneven solid solution could be obtained at 2050 ◦ C, but the relative density was only 77 %.
In  this  study,  the  heterogeneous  solid  solution  HfC-TaC-based ceramic  was  successfully  fabricated  through  SPS,  utilizing  an  ingenious powder screening method in conjunction with the introduction of HfO2 sintering additive. 1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  have  gained  renewed attention  in  the  last  decades  due  to  their  potential  use  as  protective coatings on metal substrates in extreme environments [1 -5]. By definition, UHTCs are ceramic materials with melting points above 3000 ◦ C [6,7].  Therefore,  they  are  suitable  candidates  for  thermal  protection structures in gas-turbine engines or chemical plants, which can withstand  application  temperatures  exceeding  ~1600 ◦ C  and  aggressive environmental conditions [1,8 -11]. In addition to their high melting point,  UHTCs  combine  improved  structural  properties  such  as  high hardness,  excellent  chemical  stability,  high  Youngs  modulus,  high refractoriness, as well as high thermal and electrical conductivity [1, 8 -10,12]. UHTCs are typically binary compounds consisting of carbides, borides or nitrides of early transition metals (e.g. ZrB2, HfN, HfC or TaC) [7,8,12]. 1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  a  class  of  ceramics whose  melting  point  exceeds  3000 ◦ C  [1].  These  materials  provide various  benefits,  including  a  remarkably  high  melting  point  and exceptional  performance  at  high  temperatures.  Consequently,  these exceptional characteristics make UHTCs well-suited for diverse applications in demanding environments [2,3].
In ultra-high temperature ceramics, carbide ceramics are considered ideal materials for fabricating high-temperature structural components for hypersonic aircraft due to their high melting points and excellent thermal-physical properties [4,5]. HfC and TaC exhibit a face-centered cubic (FCC) crystal structure, with lattice constants of 0.3289 nm for HfC and 0.3169 nm for TaC, respectively. The atomic radii of Hf (0.1585 nm) and Ta (0.1457 nm) displays a difference of < 15%, allowing the formation of solid solutions known as Ta1-xHfxC. These solutions are notable  for  their  excellent  high-temperature  performance  [6,7].  According to the reports, when x = 0.2,  Ta1-xHfxC  exhibits  the  highest known melting point (4300 K) [8]. Additionally, Ta1-xHfxC solid solutions possess exceptional mechanical properties [9,10], making them widely attractive.
Currently,  three  main  methods  are  used  for  preparing  Ta1-xHfxC

5. Conclusion
In this study, Ta4HfC5  precursor was successfully prepared through the modified citric acid complexation method, which is relatively simple and  the  prepared  precursor  can  be  successfully  transformed  into Ta4HfC5  solid solution ceramics at a relatively low temperature (1600 ℃ ).  Furtherly,  C/Ta4HfC5-SiC  ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  matrix composite  material,  with  a  density  of  2.43  g/cm 3 ,  was  successfully prepared by PIP process to verify the feasibility of preparing composite materials using the self-made precursor. Notably, the composite achieved good bending strength and fracture toughness, which were 392.6 ± 12.5 MPa  and  10.4 ± 2.2  MPa ⋅ m 1/2 ,  respectively.  Based  on  the oxyacetylene ablation experimental devices, the ablation behavior of C/ Ta4HfC5-SiC  composites  were  deeply  studied,  and  the  ablation  resistance mechanisms were revealed. After ablation assessment for 60 s, the mass ablation rate and linear ablation rate were 11.6 mg/s and 9.1 µ m/s, respectively. Ta4HfC5  converted to HfO2  skeleton and molten Ta2O5 at high temperature exceeding 3000 ℃ . The material surface was covered by molten Ta2O5  which filled cracks and holes and prevented the internal matrix from oxidation atmosphere. HfO2  skeleton provided attachments  for  Ta2O5 to  reduce  airflow  erosion  consumption.  The realization in both excellent mechanical properties and erosion resistance provides a solid foundation for the application of C/Ta4HfC5-SiC composite materials in the new generation of ultra-high temperature materials. Further exploration can be conducted in reducing porosity and  optimizing phase  composition  distribution  of  C/Ta4HfC5-SiC composites.

The ablation process of the Ta4HfC5 phase is summarized in Fig. 12. Among the products, HfO2  has a high melting point of approximately 2758 ◦ C [37], making it the oxide with the highest melting point except for  radioactive  element  oxides  such  as  ThO2.  Moreover,  HfO2  has  a relatively low vapor pressure, which can be only 3 MPa at a high temperature  of  2667 ◦ C  and  is  the  lowest  among  known  oxides.  Consequently,  HfO2 exhibits  remarkable  structural  stability  at  elevated temperatures, allowing it to function effectively as a skeletal support in oxidation products. However, due to the low total content, the role of HfO2  skeleton is not significant and was not observed in the ablated images shown in Fig. 9. Ta2O5, with a lower melting point of about 1850 ◦ C [38], formed a molten state in the ablation environment where temperatures exceed its melting point in Region I, and mixed with HfO2, with slightly higher content on the surface. Generally, in the central ablation pit Region I and oxidation Region II, the content of Ta2O5 in the sheet-like oxidation products is much higher than that of HfO2. However, there may also be aggregation of HfO2  content in small dimensional regions (as indicated by point 3 in Fig. 9).

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), encompassing carbides, borides, and nitrides of transition metals from Groups IV and V, exhibit properties like high melting point, corrosion resistance, high hardness, and strength. These exceptional qualities make them ideally suited for demanding applications in extreme environments, including aerospace engines, wear-resistant components, and fuel cladding [1 -7]. However, the constant concern is that UHTCs are unable to withstand complex mechanical loads, such as impact stress and vibration, due to their low fracture  toughness  [8].  The  reinforcement  techniques  for  ceramics mainly involve the incorporation of fiber/second-phase materials and the  creation  of  solid  solutions  [9].  Classic  UHTC  composites  such  as Cf/HfC-SiC, ZrB2-SiC have been developed [10,11]. For the solid solution  strengthening  method,  the  lattice  distortion  effect  significantly improves the hardness and strength, especially in high entropy ceramics (HECs). Nevertheless, the toughness remains low (2.7 -3.4 MPa m 1/2 ) due to the lack of effective crack propagation barriers in uniform phase materials.
A potential toughening method is to control the solid solution level of ceramics to form a complex multiphase structure through sintering. The

TEM-BF, HR-TEM, SAED and EDS spectra given in Fig. 8 show a typical microstructure of a surface region. Within these surface regions, silicon carbide ( β -SiC) and coarse HfxTa1 GLYPH<0> xC grains with grain sizes up to 200 nm  are  present.  Transition  metal  carbides  are  depicted  as  dark grains  in  TEM-BF  images  (Fig.  8a,b).  The  HR-TEM  images  shown  in Fig. 8c and Fig. 8d reveal that precipitates in surface-near regions are encapsulated  by  a  thin  carbon  layer.  The  HR-TEM  micrographs  in conjunction  with  corresponding  SAED  patterns  were  employed  to identify the crystalline phases β -SiC and (Ta0.8Hf0.2)C. As reported in literature, cubic HfC and TaC with rock-salt (NaCl) structure form solid solutions (i.e., HfxTa1 GLYPH<0> xC) [9]. The relationship between the lattice parameters and the x-values obey Vergard ' s law [2,9]. The standard lattice parameter of the stoichiometric HfC and TaC are 4.641 Å and 4.456 Å, respectively  [45].  In  addition  to  detecting  TMCs,  pure  TaC  was  also confirmed, however rarely, within the phase assemblage of the Ta-rich powder sample as shown in Fig. 10d.

A B S T R A C T
According to first-principles calculations, the mechanical properties of Ta1-xHfxC (x = 0.2, 0.4, 0.5) improve as the Ta content increases due to enhanced bonding between Ta and C atoms. The negative mixing enthalpies indicate that all three solid solutions can be prepared as single-phase materials. Single-phase Ta1-xHfxC (x = 0.2,0.4,0.5) were prepared using Spark Plasma Sintering. Studies on oxidation reveal that Ta0.5Hf0.5C demonstrates the most superior oxidation resistance among the three compositions. During oxidation, forming a Ta2O5Hf6Ta2O17 layer is essential for improving oxidation resistance. The research indicates that balancing the ratio of Ta  and  Hf  is  crucial  for  achieving  optimal  mechanical  properties  and  oxidation  resistance  in  practical applications.
ceramics: carbothermal reduction [11,12], polymer precursor pyrolysis [13], and high-temperature solid solution of carbides [14,15]. The hightemperature solid solution process involves the direct solid-state solubilization reaction of TaC and HfC, which is simpler than the other two methods. Previous research indicates that temperatures above 2000 ◦ C are necessary to achieve the solid solubility of HfC and TaC due to their high melting points [10]. The process of achieving both solid solubility and dense sintering  is  quite  challenging.  The  spark  plasma  sintering (SPS)  process  has  recently  gained  widespread  attention  in  research. Using SPS to prepare Ta1-xHfxC ceramics can achieve both solid solution and  densification  simultaneously.  Therefore,  SPS  is  the  most  used method for fabricating Ta1-xHfxC solid solution ceramics. Omar CedillosBarraza [14] et al. prepared Ta1-xHfxC ceramics by SPS. They demonstrated that a homogeneous single-phase solid solution only forms when the temperature exceeds 2150 ◦ C.

question: What range of processing/sintering temperatures have been used to densify UHTC materials such as ZrB2-SiC-B4C-YAG composites, HfC-TaC carbides, and HfC-TaC-HfO2 ceramics?
answer: The specific range of processing/sintering temperatures for ZrB2-SiC-B4C-YAG composites, HfC-TaC carbides, and HfC-TaC-HfO2 ceramics is not directly provided in the sources. However, it is mentioned that the densification of Ta x Hf1 -x C ceramics typically requires high temperature and high pressure due to the strong covalent bond and low diffusion coefficient of Ta-C and Hf-C [4]. Additionally, pure ZrB2 material requires pressure-assisted sintering techniques at very high temperatures (>1900 ◦ C) to achieve complete densification [5].

1. Introduction
Ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are a class of refractory ceramics showing superior stability at temperatures higher than 2500 °C [1]. They are mainly classi /uniFB01 ed as borides, carbides and nitrides of transition metals of groups IV -VI. Most of the efforts in this /uniFB01 eld have focused on commonly used borides (HfB2, ZrB2), nitrides (ZrN, TiN), carbides (TiC, TaC) and their composites [1,2]. Tantalum carbide (TaC) and hafnium carbide (HfC) are binary compounds of metallic tantalum and hafnium with carbon which are extremely hard, brittle, refractory (melting points over 3900 °C) with metallic electrical conductivity and cubic crystal structure [2].
There are limited number of published reports on processing of HfC composites using pressureless sintering [3], hot pressing [4] and spark plasma sintering (SPS) [5]. In most of these works, 5 -15 wt.% MoSi2 or TaSi2 was used as a sintering aid. There are more works on sintering of TaC with different techniques such as pressureless sintering (PLS) with MoSi2 [6] and B4C addition [7] and without additives [8], hot pressing (HP) with MoSi2/TaSi2 [4], carbon [9], B4C [9] and TaB2 [10], high-frequency induction sintering (HFIS) [11] and hot isostatic pressing (HIP) using metallic tantalum powder [12]. 1. Introduction
With the prosperous development of the aerospace and maritime industries, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have been and will continue  to  be  used  as  multifunctional  materials  due  to  their  high melting point ( > 3300 K), high modulus, excellent electrical conductivity, and resistance to chemical attack [1 -4]. Transition-metal carbides (TMCs) are potential candidate materials for UHTCs. As typical rock salt structure TMCs, both HfC and TaC have great potential to be the next generation of UHTCs with melting temperatures around 4200 K [5,6]. Currently, the HfC and TaC ceramics can be sintered by spark plasma sintering,  hot  press  sintering, etc. [7,8].  However,  the  pure  ceramics without sintering aids are difficult to synthesize via these techniques, due  to  the  strong  Hf-C  and  Ta-C  covalent  bonding  nature  and  low self-diffusion  coefficient.  The  high  pressure  and  high  temperature (HPHT) method is a preferred method for sintering UHTCs, which can greatly reduce the sintering temperature as well as enhance the bulk density and suppress the grain growth [9,10].

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are a class of materials having melting points higher than 3000 K according to one of the definitions [1 -4]. They also exhibit other interesting thermo-mechanical properties,  including  structural  stability  at  high  temperatures  [4 -6]. UHTCs were initially developed for aerospace applications but recently, applications in the field of energy ( e.g. nuclear and concentrated solar power), materials processing and others are also envisaged [2,3,5]. The UHTCs family is mainly made up of carbides, borides and nitrides of early transition metals such as Ti, Zr, Nb, Hf, and Ta [2,3]. Among them, tantalum carbide (TaC) has the highest melting point of the stoichiometric compounds ( i.e. > 4200 K) [7,8] and the melting point of the tantalum nitride (TaN) is higher than 3000 K [9]. Recently, Hong and van  de  Walle  reported  that  the  addition  of  nitrogen  into  a  carbide structure makes it harder to melt and resulted in HfC0.56N0.38  as the compound  with  the  highest  melting  temperature  found  by ab  initio molecular dynamics [10].
The preparation and densification of TaC has been widely studied, with  or  without  additives  [11 -13].  Spark  Plasma  Sintering  (SPS)  is increasingly used to densify TaC, reduce its sintering temperature and processing time and limit grain growth [14 -16]. Despite the potentially improved  properties,  the  Ta -C -N  system  has  been  scarcely  studied

1 INTRODUCTION
Ultrahigh temperature ceramics (UHTCs) generally refer to a class of ceramic compounds with a melting point higher than 3000 ◦ C, mainly including carbides, borides, and nitrides of transition metals, 1,2 such as ZrC, HfC, TaC, HfB2, ZrB2 , HfN, and so on. These materials are endowed with high-temperature resistance, high thermal conductivity, outstanding high-temperature mechani- of ∼ 4300 K at the Hf:Ta ratio of 1:4. 6 Moreover, Ta x Hf1 -x C solid-solution ceramics also perform better mechanical and oxidation/ablation-resistant properties. 7-9 Therefore, Ta x Hf1 -x C ceramics have attracted more attention for broader application prospect.
Due to the strong covalent bond and low diffusion coefficient of Ta-C and Hf-C, the densification of Ta x Hf1 -x C ceramics usually requires high temperature and high pressure. 10 Up to now, it is still a big challenge to achieve the densification of Ta x Hf1 -x C ceramics at low temperature. In general, the densification approaches of Ta x Hf1 -x C ceramics mainly include pressureless sintering (PLS), 11,12 hot-pressing (HP), 13,14 spark plasma sintering, 15-17 and so on. With TaSi 2 , 18 MoSi2,11 ,18 VC, 19 WC, 20 and so on as sintering aids, the densification of Ta x Hf1 -x C ceramics is improved effectively. Zhang et al. 12 prepared dense Ta0.8 Hf 0.2 C-SiC ceramics by PLS at 2200 ◦ C. It is indicated that the addition of SiC could not only refine the grains of Ta 0.8 Hf0.2 C but also significantly improve the fracture toughness of the ceramics. In addition, it is reported that TaC and HfC can react with Si and form SiC in situ. 21,22

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) include borides, carbides, and nitrides with melting temperatures above 2700 ◦ C [1]. UHTCs have been investigated for high temperature applications, including thermal protection systems for hypersonic aerospace vehicles, high temperature electrodes, and molten metal crucibles [2-5]. Recently, ZrB2-based ceramics have attracted much attention because of their unique combination of low density, high melting temperature and thermal shock resistance as well as excellent mechanical and chemical stability at high temperatures [6-10].
Generally pressure-assisted sintering techniques at very high temperature (>1900 ◦ C) are required to achieve complete densification of pure ZrB2 material due to its strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion coefficient. Several studies have shown that the addition of SiC to ZrB2 powders has beneficial effects not only on sinterability, but also on mechanical properties and resistance to oxidation [9-12]. Although great efforts have been devoted to enhancing the mechanical properties and much progress has been made recently, the susceptibility to brittle fracture is still a major issue for the use of ZrB2based UHTCs. Whisker-reinforced ceramic matrix composites are of promising materials, especially for high temperature applications [13-15]. SiC whiskers, combining high strength, elastic modulus and chemical inertness at elevated temperature, are

question: What range of starting powder particle sizes have been used to fabricate ZrB2-SiC ceramics, HfC-TaC carbides, and HfC-TaC-HfO2 ceramics?
answer: The range of starting powder particle sizes used for fabricating ZrB2-SiC ceramics is ~2 mm for ZrB2 and ~1.7 mm for SiC [5]. For HfC-TaC carbides, the particle sizes range from 48 μm to 150 μm for the coarse powders and less than 6.5 μm for the fine powders [1]. For HfC-TaC-HfO2 ceramics, the HfO2 powder used has a particle size less than 14 μm [1].

2. Material and methods
Firstly, TaC and HfC powders (China Qi Jin New Materials Co. Ltd., purity ≥ 99.9 %.) were sieved to obtain coarse powders ranging from 100 to 300 mesh (Particle size: 48 μ m -150 μ m), and fine powders of 2000 mesh (Particle size ＜ 6.5 μ m) were obtained simultaneously. The two types of powders were then mixed in a 1:1 vol ratio. Secondly, 5 vol% HfO2 powder (China New Metal Materials Technology Co. Ltd., purity ≥ 99.9 %, 1200 mesh, particle size ＜ 14 μ m) was added to the HfC-TaC mixture powders. The HfC-TaC-HfO2 powders were ground in a planet ball-miller under a rotary speed of 50 rpm with ethanol and tungsten carbide (WC) ball for 20 h in a WC vacuum tank. Then the slurry was dried  at  80 ◦ C  for  24  h  in  a  vacuum  oven.  A  graphite  mold  with  a diameter of 20 mm was used, and the desired thickness of the ceramic was  set  at  5  mm.  The  sintering  process  was  performed  using  SPS equipment under 45 MPa at 1800 ◦ C for 30 min (LABOX-600 equipment, Sinter Land Inc.). A heating rate of 100 ◦ C/min was adopted. For comparison, binary TaC and HfC ceramic blocks were obtained under the same sintering conditions.

3.4.1.1  MeB 2 -SiC Ceramics. MeB 2 -SiC ceramics have an excellent combination of properties and have received the most attention among MeB 2 -based UHTCs. Zhang et al. [36] prepared ZrB 2 -SiC composites by RHP using Zr, Si, and B 4 C as starting powders under vacuum at 1900°C for 1 h, according to Reaction 3.23.

A mechanism for the RHP process was proposed as shown in Figure 3.9. During RHP, B and C atoms in B 4 C will diffuse into Zr and Si, respectively, and form ZrB 2 and SiC in situ . Because the diffusion of Zr and Si atoms is slow, the as-synthesized ZrB 2 and SiC possess features of the starting powders. The particle size of SiC was generally <3 µm, whereas that of ZrB 2 was larger, in the range of 3-10 µm, compared with starting particle sizes of <10 µm for and <43 µm for Zr. The RHP process resulted in relatively small grain sizes for both ZrB 2 and SiC, resulting in an improvement of the mechanical properties compared with ZrB 2 -SiC prepared by conventional HP. Subsequently, Zhao et al. also prepared ZrB 2 -SiC by R-SPS using the same starting materials [47, 48]. After SPS, a fine homogeneous microstructure was obtained with grain sizes of <5 µm for ZrB 2 and <1 µm for SiC. In addition, Zimmermann et al. fabricated ZrB 2 -SiC ceramics by RHP using ZrH 2 , B 4 C, and Si as given in Reaction 3.24 [49].

Commercially available starting powders were used: ZrB2 powder (average particle size ~2 m m, Grade B, H.C. Starck Corp., Newton, MA) and SiC (average particle size ~1.7 m m, Grade B-hp, b -SiC, H.C. The resulting slurries were dried, while being stirred, to obtain homogenously dispersed powder mixtures, which were then crushed in a mortar-pestle and sieved.
Small batches of the powder mixture (3 e 4 g) were place in graphite dies (20 mm diameter) lined with graphite foil, and densi /uniFB01 ed using spark plasma sintering (SPS; Thermal Technologies LLC, Santa Rosa, CA). question: What range of applied consolidation pressures have been reported for hot pressing, spark plasma sintering, and field-assisted sintering of UHTC materials like ZrB2-SiC, HfC-TaC-HfO2, and HfC-TaC?
answer: The range of applied consolidation pressures for hot pressing (HP) of UHTC materials like ZrB2-SiC, HfC-TaC-HfO2, and HfC-TaC has been reported as 20 to 60 MPa [3]. For spark plasma sintering (SPS), the applied pressure is typically around 30 MPa, as seen in the consolidation of Ta0.8Hf0.2C [4]. Field-assisted sintering techniques, such as SPS, generally apply pressures in the range of 20 to 60 MPa, similar to HP, though specific values can vary depending on the material and desired properties [3][4].

Fig. 2. (a) XRD patterns related to SPS-processed ZrB2 and ZrB2-Ti2AlN samples, peaks variation in the range of (b) 31 -34 ◦ , and (c) 40 -44 ◦ .
Different consolidation methods, including pressureless and pressure-assisted sintering techniques, have been utilized for the fabrication of UHTCs, including ZrB2-based samples, but the quality of final samples obtained from conventional sintering methods is not satisfactory.  Therefore,  to  attain  better  properties  and  densification,  more advanced  sintering  techniques  such  as  spark  plasma  sintering  (SPS) [35,36], reactive hot pressing (RHP) [1,37], and hot pressing (HP) [38] should be used. Among these methods, SPS possesses promising results in controlling grain growth by reducing sintering time down to a few minutes [39 -41].

3. Processing techniques for UHTCs
Effective sintering of UHTCs requires the initial powder to have high purity  (although  metallic  impurities  aid  in  the  densification  process, whereas oxide impurities promote grain growth and are detrimental for densification),  small  powder  particle  size,  narrow  particle-size  distribution, and low agglomeration factor [45]. The low self-diffusion coefficient and strong covalent bonds in UHTCs make sintering difficult [46]. Hot pressing (HP) has typically been employed to densify UHTCs [47 -53], while achieving 94 -97% densification in TaC-based composites sintered at 1800 -2300 ◦ C [50 -53], while 98 -100% densification is obtained in  ZrB2-  based  composites  upon  sintering  in  1600 -1870 ◦ C range [47,49]. To achieve full densification of ZrB2 -30ZrC -10SiC, the total sintering time is reduced from ~140 min in HP (with 3.7 vol % Si3N4 as a sintering aid) to ~23 min in SPS (without any sintering aid) [54].  With  HP  and  SPS,  complex  shapes  cannot  be  prepared,  which pushes utilization of pressureless sintering (with inherently poor mechanical properties attributed to poor densification). Pressureless sintering and hot pressing of ZrB2  have shown Vickers ' hardness of 14.5 GPa and 23 GPa, the elastic modulus of 454 GPa and 489 GPa, and flexural  strength  of  444  MPa  and  565  MPa, respectively  [55]. Other techniques to consolidate UHTCs are atmospheric plasma spraying [56], vacuum plasma spraying [8] and chemical vapor infiltration [57], etc. The pros and cons of these processing techniques towards enunciating microstructure  and  resulting  consolidation,  illustrated  in  Table

Production of UHTC Complex Shapes and Architectures
As a matter of fact the most straightforward method to produce high strength dense UHTCs is through the use of pressure assisted techniques ,  most often in combination with sintering additives to reduce the processing temperature as much as possible (1900°C or lower).
Hot pressing (HP) is historically the most common pressure assisted sintering technique for advanced ceramics. ZrB 2 ceramics and ZrB 2 based composites with final density in the range 70-100% have been realized by HP using different starting particle size, additives and sintering temperature in the range 1600-2000 °C, sintering times from 5 to 120 min and pressures from 20 to 60 MPa (Guo, 2009).
Recently, spark plasma sintering (SPS) has  become a  routine  technique  to  densify advanced ceramics. A pulsed electric direct current is applied contemporary to a unidirectional mechanical pressure in a graphite die. Heating up is mainly due to the Joule effect when electrically conductive materials are processed. The effectiveness of SPS in the densification of refractory borides is highlighted by the processing time, which is 10 times lower than for HP (Medri et al., 2005), and allows to obtain final relative density higher than 99% without the use of sintering aids (Bellosi et al., 2011). HfB 2 based materials processed by SPS (Bellosi et al., 2006; Sciti et al., 2008) retained their flexural strength up to 1500°C in air, with values over 800 MPa, thanks to absence of a thermally unstable grain boundary phase deriving from sintering aids. ZrB 2 and/or HfB 2 compacts with final density of 80-100% have

Abstract
Ta0.8Hf0.2C ceramic has the highest melting point among the known materials (4000 ◦ C). Spark plasma sintering is a new route for consolidation of materials, specially ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which are difficult to be sintered at temperatures lower than 2000 ◦ C.
The purpose of this study is to consolidate Ta0.8Hf0.2C by spark plasma sintering at low temperature using MoSi2 and TaSi2 as sintering aid. In this regard, effect of different amounts of sintering aids and carbides ratio on densification behavior and mechanical properties of Ta1 -x Hf x C were investigated.
Fully consolidation of Ta0.8Hf0.2C was achieved in presence of 12 vol.% sintering aid after sintering at 1650 ◦ C for 5 min under 30 MPa. The first stage of sintering was due to plastic deformation of sintering aids particles and consequent rearrangement. The second stage was occurred via Ta1 -x Hf x C solid solution and liquid phase formation.

Keywords: Spark plasma sintering; TaC; HfC; Disilicides; UHTC

A B S T R A C T
Authors of the present work have focused their studies on the processing of UHTCMCs, adopting the method of slurry impregnation and sintering. This method allowed to achieve high density composites, with high fractions of UHTC phase and a quite strong fibre/matrix interface [1,16,17].  The  most  investigated  option  for  the  matrix  was  ZrB2, because  it  possesses  the  best  combination  of  refractoriness  (melting point  3250 ◦ C),  oxidation  resistance  and  low  specific  weight  (6.1 g/cm 3 ). Blends of ZrB2 and SiC powders were studied to understand the effect of SiC addition on mechanical properties and oxidation resistance of  UHTCMCs  at  intermediate  temperature  (1650 ◦ C)  [18].  In  other focused  studies,  the  matrices  were  based  on  ZrC,  TaC  and  HfC.  The bending strength of these composites was measured up to 2100 ◦ C [19, 20],  showing  very  promising  results.  As  for  the  consolidation  techniques, beside hot pressing [1,16,17], it has been recently demonstrated that spark plasma sintering is an effective method to achieve densification in a few minutes for these composites, provided that the thermal cycle is finely calibrated to avoid excessive reactions at the interface with the fibre [21]. Finally, for the high temperature characterization, high enthalpy arc-jet tests demonstrated the capability of Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composites to withstand temperatures as high as 2500 ◦ C, with limited erosion,  owing  to  the  partial  healing  of  surface  flaws  let  by  fiber burn-out by readily formed silica-based glass [22 -24].

question: How many infiltration and pyrolysis cycles were required to consolidate the carbon-fiber-reinforced UHTC composite?
answer: The number of infiltration and pyrolysis cycles required to consolidate the carbon-fiber-reinforced UHTC composite varied depending on the specific process used. In one study, six PIP cycles were used [1]. Another study mentioned up to ten successive infiltration cycles followed by a respective pyrolysis step [4]. Additionally, a specific case used five pyrolysis cycles [5].

chunks.text:
4. Conclusions
Uncoated  PAN-based  carbon  fibre-reinforced  20 -45  vol%  ZrB2enriched composites were fabricated via slurry impregnation coupled with polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP) at 1000 ◦ C, which is a very low  temperature  to  consolidate  UHTCMCs.  Optimized  water-based slurries were found effective for a homogeneous distribution of UHTC phase around each single fibre and the mild pyrolysis conditions are the hallmark of this process and the key to improve durability of materials for extreme environments keeping affordable costs. The good homogeneity of the fibre distribution into the matrix was found even using 2.5D needle-punched preform, in which ZrB2 was penetrated in the inner part of  the  felt.  The  subsequent  six  PIP  cycles  with  allylhydrido  polycarbosilane  under  mild  conditions  allowed  to  densify  the  infiltrated preforms reaching residual matrix open porosities in the range 5 -28% as a function of fibre architecture complexity.
The mechanical properties, including flexural strength and failure strain, Young ' s and shear moduli and fracture toughness, were evaluated and compared with other UHCTCMCs including pitch-based high modulus  carbon  fibre-reinforced  UHTCMCs.  The  flexural  strength exceeded  250  MPa  for  unidirectional  sample,  while  the  modulus exceeded 250 GPa for needle-punched one. The results were found to be in the same range of other CMCs obtained by more complex or more time-consuming energy-intensive processes.

KEYWORDS
Several processes are commonly employed to incorporate UHTCs into composite materials, including chemical vapor infiltration (CVI), precursor infiltration pyrolysis (PIP), slurry infiltration (SI) and reactive melt infiltration (RMI). 6-9 Qian et al. 10 fabricated C/C-ZrB 2 -ZrC-SiC composite via 16 cycles of PIP, employing PCS and ZrB-containing organic polymers as precursors. The PIP process enables tailored precursor design but suffers from prolonged cycles, with repeated pyrolysis inducing detrimental micropores and microcracks. 11 Chen et al. 12 fabricated C/C-ZrC-ZrB 2 composite through reactive infiltration of Zr into porous C/C-B 4 C preforms. The resulting materials achieved a density of 3.07 g/cm 3 , and a flexural strength of 147.0 MPa. While reactive melt infiltration (RMI) offers high densification efficiency and rapid fabrication capability, its application with high-melting-point transition metal melts (e.g., Zr, Ti) remains challenged by the risk of fiber degradation. 13 Zhu et al. 14 achieved lowtemperature, low-pressure deposition of ZrB 2 via chemical vapor infiltration (CVI) using a Zr-BCl 3 -H 2 -Ar precursor system, effectively mitigating fiber damage. However, CVI exhibits poor penetration efficiency for Hf-, Zr-, and Tabased UHTCs. Additionally, this process is constrained by prolonged processing cycles and exorbitant energy demands, which impede its scalability. In response to the above issues, researchers often use inexpensive and simple SI process when introducing UHTCs and combine with other processes to achieve densification. Kong et al. 15 prepared Cf /SiC-ZrB 2 composite with RMI after infiltrating carbon felt in a slurry containing 30 wt.% ZrB 2 .

A B S T R A C T
Carbon fibre reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) matrix composites were fabricated coupling water-based powder slurry infiltration, Polymer Infiltration  and Pyrolysis  (PIP)  and  Hot  Pressing (HP)  techniques. This study aims to identify the best sequence of consolidation techniques to better integrate the carbon fibre cloths into an ultra-refractory sintered ceramic matrix of ZrB2-SiC. Infiltrated preforms with UHTC powder slurry were densified via: a) a pre-sintering step by HP followed by two PIP cycles with polycarbosilane, and vice versa, b) two PIP cycles followed by a cycle of HP. Flexural strengths at room temperature and at 1500 ◦ C (167 MPa and 592 MPa, respectively) were found to be significantly higher for composites obtained by the second route, suggesting that sintering of polymer-derived SiC during HP improves the structural properties of Cf/ZrB2SiC  composites. This  work  presents  an effective  method  for  UHTCMC  manufacturing in  a  shorter time  than traditional PIP process.
possessing a good strength even at 2100 ◦ C [13,14], a good fracture toughness (e.g. > 15 MPa m 1/2 ) [13,15,16], an excellent thermal shock resistance ( > 1500 ◦ C) [13], and an excellent ablation resistance above 1600 ◦ C [11,16 -21]. However, the choice of the manufacturing process remains  the  major  concern  for  these  materials.  Selection  of  the  best route is dictated by the need of fast processing/cost-effectiveness combined with quality of performance.
Several processing techniques have been used for the fabrication of UHTCMCs, re-adapted from the CMC manufacturing technologies:

Table 5
Zhang et al. [215] refined the approach and achieved exceptional mechanical properties in their fabricated Cf/ZrB2 -SiC material. Starting with a PyC-coated 3D carbon architecture, a slurry impregnation process was  carried  out  with  ZrB2 particles,  utilizing  vacuum  and  vibration assistance to enhance penetration through the weave. The slurry infiltration  step  was  proceeded  by  PIP  process,  in  which  liquid  polycarbosilane  was  introduced  into  the  composite  in  a  high-pressure chamber. After five pyrolysis cycles at 1300 ◦ C, crystalline SiC from the precursor effectively filled much of the porosity between ZrB2 particles. This  procedure  is  schematically  and  experimentally  represented  in Fig.  21.  The  final  composite  achieved  92  %  density  and  showed outstanding  mechanical  properties  compared  to  other  UHTCMCs  reported in literature. A high flexural strength (603 MPa) and high fracture toughness (18.1 MPa m ½ ) were recorded from mechanical testing, and the work of fracture (11.9 kJ/m 2 ) was superior to any other values reported in literature for UHTCMCs. These results are highly encouraging for the prospects of designing dense UHTC-based composites with high mechanical toughness and tolerance.
Continuous carbon fiber composites are currently the leading area of focus for the development of UHTCMCs. Unique processing approaches have made definitive progress in overcoming the relevant manufacturing challenges, where UHTC matrices have been adequately infiltrated  and  densified  in  complex  carbon  fiber  architectures. question: What processing temperature was employed for densifying UHTC ceramics via infiltration and sintering methods?
answer: The processing temperatures for densifying UHTC ceramics via infiltration and sintering methods vary depending on the specific technique used. For instance, pressureless sintering typically requires temperatures of at least 2000 °C [1]. Hot pressing, another method, is conducted at temperatures above 1800 °C with applied pressures ranging from 20 to 50 MPa [1]. Spark plasma sintering, which allows for high heating rates, can achieve full densification within a short period at temperatures exceeding 1800 °C [1]. Additionally, ultrafast high-temperature sintering (UHS) can achieve densification at 2300 °C for 120 seconds [5].

chunks.text:
1 INTRODUCTION
Except for the mixture design, the sintering method also substantially influences the mechanical properties of UHTCs through the phase evolution and microstructure development. 2,6 The most common unreactive sintering methods for UHTC densification are hot pressing, pressureless sintering, and spark plasma sintering. The pressureless sintering, the simplest sintering method, is developed from the conventional sintering technique, which allows producing large, complex-geometry samples. 2,4,18 To be specific, the precursor powder with additives (enhancing densification) is cold pressed in a mold with a desired shape. Then, the green body is sintered in the furnace at least 2000 ◦ Cwithout pressure. Unlike pressureless sintering, pre-pressing is not needed for the hot pressing method. The samples are sintered in molds while applying 20-50-MPa pressure at > 1800 ◦ C. 2,4,19 The hot pressing method can achieve full densification with lower temperature, but it cannot fabricate complex shapes due to the uniaxial pressure. 18,20 The spark plasma sintering is a modified hot pressing, where the pulsed electric current is directly applied to punches of graphite dies in addition to uniaxial pressure. 21,22 Due to the direct heating, the spark plasma sintering method allows a high heating rate (up to 1000 ◦ C/min), thereby densifying UHTCs within a short period (no more than 10 min). 21,22 Parameters for the sintering methods mutually affect the properties of UHTCs, which increases the difficulty of discovering the optimal processing parameters. Generally, fully densified UHTCs require high sintering temperatures. However, a high pressing pressure applied to the sample can reduce the sintering temperature, and, moreover, a high heating rate enhances the densification and grain size of UHTCs. 1,2 To achieve the optimal grain size, density, and mechanical properties, researchers require to carefully control pressure, sintering time, sintering temperature, heating rate, and cooling rate.

KEYWORDS
As outlined in the review, 19 a major disadvantage of the industry-standard carbothermal and borothermal solid-state reactive synthesis methods is the presence of residual oxygen and carbon impurities-both amorphous and crystalline-along with trace of metallic content in the final product. Additionally, most reduction reactions require high temperatures: over 1500 ◦ C for carbothermal reduction and over 1200 ◦ C for borothermal reduction, often accompanied by lengthy processing times. The selfpropagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) or combustion synthesis (CS) method offers a way to overcome these limitations. CS is based on the concept of self-sustaining chemical reactions that generate extremely high reaction temperatures (up to 4000 ◦ C) without the need for external heating sources. 30,31 The characteristic reaction time is on the order of seconds. The high temperatures involved facilitate a self-cleaning effect, resulting in highly pure powders. 32-34 In our opinion, CS, being a clean, energyefficient, and time-saving method, is particularly attractive for the fabrication of UHTC powders. Main features of CS are briefly discussed in Section 1.2.
According to the aforementioned classification for fabricating bulk ceramics through the sintering of UHTC powders, various densification techniques are employed, including hot pressing (HP), field-assisted sintering techniques (FAST), additive manufacturing (3D Printing), high-temperature vacuum sintering, plasma sintering, laser sintering, and spark plasma sintering (SPS) 35-41 To densify UHTC powders with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C, other methods are employed, specifically electric arc sintering. 42 It should be noted here, that the modified methods of plasma arc melting and floating zone melting were applied to grow centimeter-scale single crystals of TiB2 , however these methods were not developed further. 43

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC) such as TiC, ZrB2, TiB2, and HfN are a particular type of materials which are characterized by their high strength, excellent corrosion resistance, good wettability with some liquid metals, high hardness, machinability using electrical discharge machining, high melting temperature (above 3000 °C), and high electrical and thermal conductivity [1 -10]. These unique properties make these ceramics suitable candidates for high-tech and extremely hot conditions such as solar energy plants, gas turbine stator blades, heat exchangers, and cutting tools [11 -25]. Due to the high melting point, powerful covalent bonding, and low self-di /uniFB00 usion coef/uniFB01 cient of UHTCs, it is arduous to densify such materials without implementing high temperatures and pressures [26 -34]. In this regard, methods such as pressureless sintering [35,36], hot pressing (HP) [37 -40] or hot isostatic pressing [31,32,35,36,41 -46], spark plasma sintering (SPS) [47 -56] microwave sintering [57,58] and laser sintering [59,60] are used widely to manufacture the UHTCs.
