4.1.6.  Ultrafast high-temperature sintering, UHS
Ultrafast high-temperature sintering (UHS) is a cutting-edge ceramic sintering technology that achieves rapid densification by applying high temperatures in an extremely short timeframe [87-90]. Chen et al. [91] used UHS to fabricate ZrC ceramics with the addition of B4C. The ZrC ceramic with 2.5 wt% B4C achieved a density of 99.8 % after sintering at  2300 °C  for  120 s  (as  shown  in  Fig.  12).  Compared  to  traditional pressureless  sintering,  which  requires  60 min  at  2400 °C,  the  ZrC
Fig. 12. (a) Schematic of the self-built UHS system, (b) typical temperature profiles of the UHS process for ZrC-based ceramics, (c) sample before and after sintering, (d) SEM images of fracture surfaces for ZrC-B4C by UHS at 2400 °C. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [91], © Elsevier 2024.
ceramic containing 2.5 wt% B4C prepared using UHS at 2400 °C for 30 s exhibited higher relative density, smaller grain size, and higher Vickers hardness due to its faster heating rate and shorter sintering duration.
In recent years, significant progress has been made in the hot press sintering techniques used for fabricating UHTCs and their composites. From traditional hot press sintering to advanced methods such as spark plasma  sintering,  reactive  hot  pressing,  pressureless  sintering,  nanoinfiltration transient eutectic, and ultrafast high-temperature sintering, each approach offers unique advantages and specific applications. As technologies continue to advance, the combination and optimization of these sintering processes will provide more flexible and efficient solutions for the fabrication of high-performance UHTCs and composites.

question: What final density values were achieved in sintered and infiltrated UHTC materials?
answer: The final density values achieved in sintered and infiltrated UHTC materials include 92% density for the Cf/ZrB2-SiC material [1], 92-98% of theoretical density for UHTC-TPMS structures [3], and 80-100% for ZrB2 and/or HfB2 compacts [4]. Additionally, the composite fabricated by Luo et al. had a density of 2.60 ± 0.02 g/cm³ [2].

[15]  fabricated Carbon fibre reinforced UHTC composites having various compositions including ZrB2, HfB2 base matrix through slurry impregnation and final densification with carbon through CVI technique. The final densities of the composites were in the range of 1.86 -2.11 ± 0.001 g/cm 3 and reported  that  Halfnium  (Hf)  based  UHTC  offered  superior  oxidation resistance [15]. Reactive melt infiltration (RMI) was also used in the development of UHTCMCs, in which carbon fibre performs were infiltrated wuth Zr or Hf based alloys and facilitated in-situ formation of UHTCM phases [16 -18]. Luo et al. [17] fabricated C -SiC-HfC composite by melt infiltration of HfSi2  alloy in to the porous preform of Cf-SiC composite at 1600 -1700 ◦ C. The reported density and porosity of the composite  were  2.60 ± 0.02  g/cm 3 and  17.4 ± 0.1%,  respectively [16 -18]. Very recently, slurry infiltration followed by pressure assisted sintering (hot pressing and spark plasma sintering) was adopted for the development of UHTCMCs [19 -22]. Sciti et al. [19] fabricated carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2 base UHTCMCs by vacuum-bag infiltration and hot pressing, using 1D fabric carbon preforms, having various fiber volume fractions  from  55  to  70  %.  The  reported  bulk  density,  porosity  and flexural strength of these composites are in the range of 2.4 -3.1 g/cm 3 , 16 -28%  and  40 -95  MPa  respectively  [19].  Vinci  et  al.  studied  the oxidation behaviour of carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2 -SiC composites by thermogravimetric  analysis  up  to  1550 ◦ C  and  reported  that  of 800 -1000 ◦ C is  critical  temperature  range,  where  rapid  oxidation

4. Conclusions
We achieved our initial objective, proving that it is feasible to print and sinter UHTC-TPMS  structures  made  by  binder jet additive manufacturing. We found it was necessary to employ a spatial confinement method during sintering to effectively mitigate distortion. It was possible to use conventional powder feedstocks with a d50 ≅ 2 -3 µ m, sizes also used for conventional processing of UHTC materials. These materials were sintered to relative densities in the range of 92 -98 % of theoretical,  which  are  high  enough  to  prevent  heat  exchanger  fluids from permeating through the walls separating the two domains and to enable hot isostatic pressing if a higher density is needed to improve mechanical  properties.  Given  these  encouraging  results,  we  are  proposing to combine advances in design, materials, and manufacturing for CSP heat exchangers. A heat exchanger based on a TPMS offers an opportunity to increase power density when compared to designs based on tubes or plates. Our calculations using the scaffold dimensions we achieved  in  this  work  suggest  that  a  power  density  up  to  50 MW/m 3 is achievable for a UHTC heat exchanger based on the Schwarz-D TPMS with molten salt and supercritical carbon dioxide working fluids. Based on this power density, the largest binder jet printer on the market for printing ceramics has the potential to print a one-piece heat exchanger with capacity rating up to 3 MW. The potential for even higher capacities is currently limited by either the scale of an available printer or modular heat exchanger construction methods.

As a matter of fact the most straightforward method to produce high strength dense UHTCs is through the use of pressure assisted techniques ,  most often in combination with sintering additives to reduce the processing temperature as much as possible (1900°C or lower).
Hot pressing (HP) is historically the most common pressure assisted sintering technique for advanced ceramics. ZrB 2 ceramics and ZrB 2 based composites with final density in the range 70-100% have been realized by HP using different starting particle size, additives and sintering temperature in the range 1600-2000 °C, sintering times from 5 to 120 min and pressures from 20 to 60 MPa (Guo, 2009).
Recently, spark plasma sintering (SPS) has  become a  routine  technique  to  densify advanced ceramics. A pulsed electric direct current is applied contemporary to a unidirectional mechanical pressure in a graphite die. Heating up is mainly due to the Joule effect when electrically conductive materials are processed. HfB 2 based materials processed by SPS (Bellosi et al., 2006; Sciti et al., 2008) retained their flexural strength up to 1500°C in air, with values over 800 MPa, thanks to absence of a thermally unstable grain boundary phase deriving from sintering aids. ZrB 2 and/or HfB 2 compacts with final density of 80-100% have

UHTC ceramics derived from B 4 C and MAX phases by reactive sintering
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are a group of ceramic materials that is suitable for operation at very high temperatures, owing to their relatively high melting points, reaching values above 3000 °C 1 .  Most often, UHTCs are characterized mainly by their high thermal stability, high thermal conductivity, high hardness, and low density 2-4 .  Therefore, these materials have recently been considered as potential for used in extreme environments as resources for aircraft, spacecraft, nuclear reactors and others prospective applications 5 . Among these, boron-based systems have been studied because of their mechanical resistance, corrosion resistance and melting temperatures above 2500 °C. There are two types of boron-based UHTC carbides (e.g.B 4 C) and borides (for example, TiB 2 ) 6-8 .
The use of the described materials is limited by two factors: the low relative density of the final product and the high sintering temperature. Relative density is very important when any properties of covalent materials are considered. Both thermal and mechanical resistance are highly influenced by the amount of porosity introduced by the specific manufacture technology, which is an important research aspect in many reports 9-12 . High sintering temperatures are important in terms of industrial application of UHTCs materials due to possible reduction of energy consumption, which is very desired in the incoming years 13 . Several additives were considered in order to reduce the sintering temperatures and simultaneously improve density of borides and boron-based carbides. Those included: Al 2 O 3 , MoSi 2 , C, Y 2 O 3 , bringing various results and materials with mixed phases that influence their final properties 14-17 .

question: What activation energy values were determined for the densification and sintering kinetics of UHTC ceramics?
answer: The activation energy (QD) for densification during the intermediate stage of spark plasma sintering (SPS) of HfB2 was determined to be ~888 kJ/mol [2]. For the final stage of sintering (T > 1850 °C), the activation energy (Qg) related to grain growth was found to be ~475 kJ/mol [2]. Additionally, the activation energy for densification during high-pressure hot-pressing of fine HfB2 particles was reported as ~95.81 kJ/mol [5]. In another context, the oxidation activation energy of HfC-TiC sample reaches 385.39 kJ/mol [4]. Lastly, the activation energy for UHTCs in general is mentioned to be in the range of ~94 kJ/mol [21] < Q < 1050 kJ/mol [22] [2].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
This study investigates the sintering kinetics during the intermediate and final stages of spark plasma sintering (SPS) of HfB2  over a temperature range of 1750 -1950 ◦ C under uniaxial pressure of 50 MPa. During the intermediate stage (T < 1850 ◦ C), a creep deformation-based model was utilised for studying the densification kinetics.  The  evaluated stress  exponent (n) ~1  and an activation energy ( QD )  of  ~888 kJ/mol suggest lattice diffusion  to  be  the  dominant  densification  mechanism  during  this  stage.  At  1850 ◦ C,  a  transition  to  grain boundary sliding was observed, with n ~1.5, as confirmed by microstructural analysis. In the final stage of sintering (T > 1850 ◦ C), a grain growth-based model with grain growth exponent ( m ) ~ 3 and an activation energy ( Qg ) of ~475 kJ/mol supported the dominance of lattice diffusion during grain growth. At 1850 ◦ C, a relative  density  of  ~97  %  was  achieved,  marking  a  shift  from  interface-controlled  deformation  via  grain boundary sliding to lattice diffusion-driven grain growth. Thus, this study highlights the sintering mechanisms of HfB2  ceramics at various SPS temperatures, plausibly aiding in microstructure optimization for improved performance of ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) in extreme environments.

The sintering process parameters such as temperature, pressure and holding time significantly affect the densification process and grain growth.

3.2. Deposition kinetics
Fig.  5a  illustrates  the  rate  of  carbon  deposition  as  a  function  of infiltration  temperature;  it  is  effectively  the  densification  rate  of  the UHTCMCs. As can be seen, and as expected, the rate of densification increases with temperature. The CVI of PyC is a highly complex process that is driven by the rivalry between homogeneous reactions occurring in the gas phase and heterogeneous reactions occurring on the surface. Fig.  5b  shows  the  Arrhenius  plot  of  the  densification  rate  in  the processing  temperature  range  of  1173  K  to  1273  K.  The  calculated activation energy extracted from the fitting curves are 36, 25 and 14 ± 2 kJmol GLYPH<0> 1 K -1 for the deposition times of 2, 3 and 6 h respectively. A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), owing to their exceptional performance under extreme heat and harsh conditions, have become the preferred choice for critical thermal protection materials in high-speed aerospace vehicles. Here, the HfC-TiC ceramics were fabricated through pressureless sintering, and its oxidation behaivor was  investigated  using  non-isothermal  method.  The  oxidation  activation  energy  of  HfC-TiC  sample  reaches 385.39 kJ/mol. Then the ablation behavior of HfC-TiC ceramics was tested under plasma ablation conditions (3000 K, 5.0 MW/m 2 ). The mass ablation rate and linear ablation rate of HfC-TiC can reach GLYPH<0> 1.11 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3  g ⋅ s GLYPH<0> 1 and GLYPH<0> 1.40 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3  mm ⋅ s GLYPH<0> 1 after  being  ablated  for  300 seconds,  respectively.  The  oxide  layers  in  the  center consisted of a porous oxide upper layer, a dense Ti-enriched intermediate layer, and an oxide inner layer. This complex multi-layered oxidative configuration synergistically protected the underlying carbide matrix, significantly enhancing the ablation resistance of HfC-TiC ceramics.
cracks  in  the  oxide  layer  [13].Therefore,  it  is  essential  to  introduce additional components, such as SiC. The oxidation product of SiC, SiO2, forms a liquid phase at high temperatures, effectively sealing pores and cracks,  and  creating  a  dense  Hf-Si-O  oxidation  layer  [14,15].  This significantly improves the oxidation and ablation resistance of the material. However, under low oxygen partial pressure, a large amount of SiC is depleted by active oxidation, ultimately resulting in the formation of a porous layer [16,17]. Additionally, the SiO2  phase rapidly volatilizes  in  environments  exceeding  2000 ℃ ,  thereby  expediting  the decomposition of the oxide layer [18].

A B S T R A C T
Despite  advancements  in  sintering  techniques,  understanding  of sintering kinetics and grain growth mechanisms in spark plasma sintered HfB2 remains limited, particularly under varying sintering conditions. Kalish et al. [21], provided valuable insights into the final stage densification during high-pressure hot-pressing of fine HfB2 particles at 1400 -1900 ◦ C  through  analysis  based  on  the  Nabarro-Herring  creep model. The densification (~98.6 %) is driven by stress-directed vacancy diffusion, with an activation energy of ~95.81 kJ/mol. The rate-controlling process  involves either  interstitial  lattice  diffusion  of boron or grain boundary diffusion of hafnium, while initial densification occurs through particle fragmentation and rearrangement. This creep-based estimation of the densification mechanism has been successfully applied to various ceramics and intermetallics [23 -29].
Studies on related systems provide critical insights into densification mechanisms and sintering kinetics of transition metal diboride (TMB2)- S. Dubey et al.

question: What grain size range was produced in the UHTC ceramics after high-temperature spark plasma sintering?
answer: The grain size range produced in the UHTC ceramics after high-temperature spark plasma sintering was 1.48 μm for TaC ceramics [1]. For ZrB2-SiC composites, the grain size was less than 2 μm, while for ZrB2-CNT composites, some grain growth with grain size in the range 5-10 μm was observed [5].

1. Introduction
Grain coarsening normally occurs at the /uniFB01 nal stage of sintering, resulting in trapped pores within grains, which deteriorates the density and the performance of ceramics, especially for ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs). Here, we propose to sinter this class of ceramics in a speci /uniFB01 c temperature range and coupled with a relatively high pressure. The retarded grain boundary migration and pressure-enhanced di /uniFB00 usion ensure the proceeding of densi /uniFB01 cation even at /uniFB01 nal stage. A highly dense TaC ceramic (98.6 %) with the average grain size of 1.48 μ mwas prepared under 250 MPa via high pressure spark plasma sintering using a Cf/C die at 1850 °C. It was suggested that the /uniFB01 nal-stage densi /uniFB01 cation is mainly attributed to grain boundary plastic deformationinvolved mechanisms. Compared to the usual sintering route using a high temperature (> 2000 °C) and normal pressure (< 100 MPa), this work provides a useful strategy to acquire highly dense and /uniFB01 ne-grained UHTCs.

Figs. 1b-1c show the microstructure of two representative UHTCs (ZrB2 and TaC) sintered from the usual sintering route, i.e. Route I, high temperature (normally above 2000 °C [8 -15]) and normal pressure (usually less than 100 MPa [8 -15]), HT&NP. It can be observed that lots of pores are trapped within grains and they prevent the ceramics from reaching full density. As a result, the commonly adopted method to obtain highly dense UHTCs is to use ultra/uniFB01 ne starting powders, sintering additives or second phases, howbeit they sometimes have a negative e /uniFB00 ect on high temperature performance. [16 -18].

Journal of the European Ceramic Society
Tantalum carbide (TaC), one of the most refractory UHTCs, is known to be hard-to-be-densi /uniFB01 ed. [11] The usual processing method at a high temperature (Route I) favors grain growth, promoting poreboundary separation and making the ceramic more likely to a form a coarse-grained structure with large amounts of intra-granular pores [12 -15]. For instance, the relative density of TaC ceramic sintered via spark plasma sintering (SPS) at 2400 °C under 75 MPa was only 97 %. [13] The microstructure of /uniFB01 nal products showed many intra-granular pores caused by the exaggerated grain growth (from 330 nm to 9.0 μ m). Another work also indicated much residual porosity was trapped within grains of the TaC ceramics (97.5 %) pressureless sintered at 2300 °C, even if /uniFB01 ne starting powders (250 nm) were used [14].
Grain growth can be notably limited if sintered at an ultra-high pressure and particularly low temperature (Route II). [19] This approach has been applied to sinter TaC ceramics. As extreme case, TaC powder with starting grain size around 0.36 μ mand consolidated under 7.7 GPa at room temperature achieved a relative density of 89.8 % and an average grain size around 0.42 μ m [20]. At a higher temperature (1830 °C) but still far below the aforementioned sintering temperature (2300 -2400 °C), likewise, rare grain growth is found (0.50 μ m) until the relative density reaches 94.3 % under 7.7 GPa. [20] Ultra-high pressure can promote plastic deformation of powder compact, however, further densi /uniFB01 cation is hard to achieve due to the di /uniFB00 usion barrier at low temperatures.

1. Introduction
High temperature ceramics (HTC) and ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC) are ceramic materials that typically have melting points above ~2500 -3000 °C. HTC and UHTC, in the form of powders, monolithic ceramics, or composites, /uniFB01 nd many important applications from grinding and cutting materials (e.g., WC, TiC, c-BN) in machinery and mining industries to thermal insulation tiles, missile nozzles, and hypersonic vehicle leading edges (e.g., SiC, TaC, ZrB2) in the aerospace industry [1,2]. Reducing the size for HTC and UHTC powders from microns to submicron and even nanometer range (i.e., <~100 nm) o /uniFB00 ers additional bene /uniFB01 ts such as simpli /uniFB01 ed post-synthesis powder processing by reducing the need for extensive grinding, lowering the sintering temperature and associated energy consumption for materials processing and densi /uniFB01 cation, and helping produce manufactured parts with sub-micron or even nano-sized grains, which often leads to further improved mechanical properties such as strength, hardness, and toughness for the ceramic materials [3,4] . In addition, nanopowders will o /uniFB00 er /uniFB02 exibility in subsequent processing to make either bulk ceramics via powder consolidation or coatings via techniques such as plasma spray.
Although synthesis of nano-sized HTC and UHTC powders have been studied for some time, most methods reported in the literature su /uniFB00 er from some drawbacks either in terms of cost, product quality, or

4. Conclusions
A  270 μ m thick  ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  (UHTC)  coating, based on ZrB2 with additions of WC and SiC, was successfully deposited by shroud plasma spray on a ceramic matrix composite (CMC) upon proper tuning of powder feedstock compactness and granule size.
Compositional changes from the powder feedstock to the coating were  compatible  to  what  observed  on  sintered  bulks  with  the  same composition, including reaction of WC with boron oxide, yielding WB, and  negligible  ZrB2 oxidation,  which  retained  a  mean  grain  size  of around 1 -3 μ m and was surrounded by a specific (Zr,W)B2  solid solution, while SiC remained as discrete particles distributed at the triple junctions.
The  thermal  erosion  behavior  of  the  coating  was  tested  under extreme cyclic thermal-shock conditions using a combustion chamber and  different  oxygen  contents,  1  and  33 wt%  to  simulate  different phases of flights, which achieved 1500 -1600 ◦ C. Microstructural evolution upon testing highlighted minimal material recession, in the order of 100 -140 µ m and, particularly, the UHTC coating survived 10 heatingcooling cycles from 1500 ◦ C to 700 ◦ C in an air-fuel mixture with 1 wt% oxygen.  Presence  of  higher  oxygen  content  in  the  chamber  enabled longer coating duration thanks to the boosting of self-healing mechanisms. In all cases, no signs of coating delamination or crater formation were observed, indicating good adhesion strength of the coating to the Cf/C-SiC substrate. On the contrary, the uncoated same CMC reference was destroyed after 100 s.
A continuous layer of unoxidized UHTC remained well anchored to the  underlying  CMC,  thus  assessing  the  good  prospectives  of  these coatings for their implementation in next-generation thermal protection systems (TPS).

4. Conclusions
Spark plasma sintering was successfully used to sinter fine-grained ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites to near-full densification (>99% relative density). Detailed analysis of punch displacement during SPS sintering indicated that the densification is favored for the ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composite samples in early stage I (particle re-distribution stage) and final stage III (densification starting at earlier times and at lower temperatures). While the fine grains (<2 𝛍 m) were retained in ZrB2-SiC composites, some grain growth with grain size in the range 5-10 𝛍 m was observed for ZrB2-CNT composites. Raman spectroscopy analysis and high magnification SEM micrographs indicated that the multi-walled CNTs were retained in ZrB2-CNT composite samples sintered at high sintering temperature of 1900 ◦ C. The SiC and CNT reinforcement further improved the indentation fracture toughness of the composites through a range of toughening mechanisms, including particle shearing, crack deflection at the particle-matrix interface, and grain pull-outs for ZrB2-SiC composites, and CNT pull-outs and crack deflection in ZrB2-CNT composites.

question: What sintering temperature was applied to consolidate the ultra-high temperature ceramic materials?
answer: The sintering temperature applied to consolidate the ultra-high temperature ceramic materials was 1650 ◦ C [1]. Additionally, in another study, sintering temperatures of 1800, 1900, and 2000 °C were used [4].

Abstract
Ta0.8Hf0.2C ceramic has the highest melting point among the known materials (4000 ◦ C). Spark plasma sintering is a new route for consolidation of materials, specially ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which are difficult to be sintered at temperatures lower than 2000 ◦ C.
The purpose of this study is to consolidate Ta0.8Hf0.2C by spark plasma sintering at low temperature using MoSi2 and TaSi2 as sintering aid. In this regard, effect of different amounts of sintering aids and carbides ratio on densification behavior and mechanical properties of Ta1 -x Hf x C were investigated.
Fully consolidation of Ta0.8Hf0.2C was achieved in presence of 12 vol.% sintering aid after sintering at 1650 ◦ C for 5 min under 30 MPa. The first stage of sintering was due to plastic deformation of sintering aids particles and consequent rearrangement. The second stage was occurred via Ta1 -x Hf x C solid solution and liquid phase formation.

Keywords: Spark plasma sintering; TaC; HfC; Disilicides; UHTC

6.1  INTRODUCTION
Ultra-High  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  commonly  referred  to  as  a  class  of highly refractory nonoxide compounds characterized by melting points above 3000°C [1].  Among  UHTCs,  transition  metal  diborides  MB 2 (M =  Ti,  Zr,  Hf,  Ta)  occupy  a prominent position of interest and, for that reason, will be treated as a focused case in the present chapter.
Sintering of UHTCs is difficult due to their strong covalent bonds and low selfdiffusion rates [2]. For instance, the most common transport mechanisms for solid-state sintering of MB 2 are lattice and grain-boundary diffusion, which involve boron and the transition metal atoms. Several sintering studies conducted on MB 2 have concluded that control of surface chemistry during processing is required to achieve dense ceramics with desirable microstructures: oxide impurities at the particle surfaces enhance coarsening at temperatures below those necessary for densification by the formation of fast diffusion paths at the grain boundaries. The real surface chemistry is sensitive to powder processing techniques and often very different from what can be predicted by fundamental
Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics: Materials for Extreme Environment Applications , First Edition.
Edited by William G. Fahrenholtz, Eric J. Wuchina, William E. Lee, and Yanchun Zhou.
© 2014 The American Ceramic Society. Published 2014 by John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
surface studies in ideal conditions. For instance, an increase in the oxide content in ZrB 2 powders was observed after conventional powder processing [3, 4]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy studies of MB 2 [5]  suggested that a number of B x O y ,  MO y ,  and  MB x O y stoichiometries may form within MB 2 ceramics. Extending the effects of these oxygenbearing  species  to  other  Ultra-High  refractory  ceramics  belonging  to  group  IV-VI transition metal carbides (MC), like ZrC, HfC, or TaC, the presence of surface oxides is the most important factor hindering densification.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC) such as TiC, ZrB2, TiB2, and HfN are a particular type of materials which are characterized by their high strength, excellent corrosion resistance, good wettability with some liquid metals, high hardness, machinability using electrical discharge machining, high melting temperature (above 3000 °C), and high electrical and thermal conductivity [1 -10]. These unique properties make these ceramics suitable candidates for high-tech and extremely hot conditions such as solar energy plants, gas turbine stator blades, heat exchangers, and cutting tools [11 -25]. Due to the high melting point, powerful covalent bonding, and low self-di /uniFB00 usion coef/uniFB01 cient of UHTCs, it is arduous to densify such materials without implementing high temperatures and pressures [26 -34]. In this regard, methods such as pressureless sintering [35,36], hot pressing (HP) [37 -40] or hot isostatic pressing [31,32,35,36,41 -46], spark plasma sintering (SPS) [47 -56] microwave sintering [57,58] and laser sintering [59,60] are used widely to manufacture the UHTCs.

4. Conclusions
ZrB2-based ultrahigh temperature ceramics were prepared by the reactive spark plasma sintering using the vanadium powders and graphite nano/uniFB02 akes as sintering aids. The consolidation process was performed for 7 min under the pressure of 40 MPa at di /uniFB00 erent SPS temperatures of 1800, 1900 and 2000 °C. The relative density of all samples were > 99% as an evidence of approaching the theoretical density values. Compounds of VB2 and ZrC were in-situ synthesized during the reactive SPS at all temperatures. At the sintering temperature of 1800 °C, most vanadium and graphite additives were remained intact, while they completely were consumed by processing at 1900 °C. Beside the compounds of VB2 and ZrC, other complex phases of V1.54B50 and V3Zr3C were also in-situ formed at 2000 °C. Increasing the sintering temperature led to the signi /uniFB01 cant enhancement in the fracture toughness and slight drop in the hardness of ceramic matrix composites.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs),  including  borides,  carbides, and nitrides of early transition metals [1 -3], have a high melting point  over  3000 ◦ C.  Due  to  their  excellent  thermal,  mechanical  and chemical stability, UHTCs have been considered as the most promising candidate materials for applications facing ultra-high temperature environments, such as the thermal protection for hypersonic aerospace vehicles, and the core components in propulsions and advanced nuclear fuels  [4 -9].  So  far,  many  methods  have  been  developed  to  fabricate UHTCs, among which the spark plasma sintering (SPS) is the most advantageous method, since it can realize full densification, reduce sintering  temperature  and  time,  and  improve  mechanical  and  thermal stability [10 -12]. As one type of UHTCs, diborides, such as ZrB2, have been  proven to  be  highly  resistant  to  oxidation  [13 -15].  During  the sintering process of ZrB2, SiC is often introduced as a second phase, since it  inhibits  grain  growth,  improves  sinterability,  and  enhances  the oxidation  and  mechanical  properties  [16 -18].  Therefore,  ZrB2-SiC

question: What external pressure was applied during ceramic sintering or consolidation?
answer: The external pressure applied during ceramic sintering or consolidation varied depending on the specific process and material. For instance, during the sintering of B4C ceramics, an external pressure of 80 MPa was applied [1]. In the case of ZrB2-V-C composites fabricated via spark plasma sintering (SPS), an external pressure of 40 MPa was used [2]. Additionally, during the synthesis of (Ta,Hf)CN samples via SPS, an external load of 50 MPa was applied [4].

1. Introduction
Controlling grain growth during sintering is a key factor to obtain a /uniFB01 ne-structured ceramic with better performance. However, densi /uniFB01 cation and grain growth share the same driving force and they compete with each other during sintering. [1] For pressureless sintering, the feasibility of densi /uniFB01 cation without grain growth relies on the suppression of grain boundary (GB) migration while keeping GB di /uniFB00 usion active [2]. For pressure-assisted sintering, the feasibility is more likely to be achieved since external pressure enhances the densi /uniFB01 cation [3]. Previously we have demonstrated that densi /uniFB01 cation without grain growth can be realized in sintering B4C ceramics by properly choosing the sintering temperature that at, or slightly lower than, the onset point for grain growth (Tg) and applying a pressure of 80 MPa. [4,5]
However, for most structural ceramics, the competition between densi /uniFB01 cation and grain growth is hard to be completely avoided already at normal pressure level up to ∼ 100 MPa since the grain growth is more prone to dominate the kinetics at /uniFB01 nal stage (Route I in Fig. 1a). Among them, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are taken as typical example, which are characterized by ultra-high melting temperature, strong covalent bond and low self-di /uniFB00 usion coe /uniFB03 cient. [6] Sintering such kind of ceramics particularly needs to balance the tradeo /uniFB00 between densi /uniFB01 cation and grain growth since the high processing temperature and presence of oxygen impurities can accelerate the

zyfu@whut.edu.cn (Z. Fu).
A B S T R A C T

A B S T R A C T
Spark plasma sintering was employed for the fabrication of ZrB2 -V -C composites at di /uniFB00 erent consolidation temperatures (1800, 1900 and 2000 °C) using an external pressure of 40 MPa for 7 min under vacuum atmosphere. Vanadium powders and graphite nano/uniFB02 akes were applied as metallic and carbonaceous sinter additives, respectively, in the ZrB2 matrix ultrahigh temperature ceramic composites. All composites approached their theoretical densities as the relative density values were > 99%. The in-situ synthesis of VB2 and ZrC compounds, during the reactive sintering process at all temperatures, was veri /uniFB01 ed by phase and microstructural analyses. It was found that vanadium and graphite were entirely consumed via reaction with the matrix by sintering at 1900 °C, while the majority of them were left intact at the temperature of 1800 °C. However, the compounds of VB2 and ZrC were partially reacted together, at 2000 °C, which led to the synthesis of extra complex phases of V1.54B50 and V3Zr3C. The hardness of composites slightly decreased with increasing the sintering temperature, while the fracture toughness was remarkably enhanced.

4.1.4.  Pressureless sintering, PLS
Pressureless sintering (PLS) is a traditional ceramic  sintering method that densifies ceramic powders through diffusion mechanisms at high temperatures over extended periods, without applying external pressure [78-81]. Mao et al. [82] successfully fabricated PyC-Csf/ZrB2SiC-ZrC  ceramics  with  a  homogeneous  structure  using  cold  isostatic pressing  and  pressureless  sintering,  and  investigated  their  sintering mechanisms (as shown in Fig. 11). The addition of B4C improved the material's  densification  by  removing  oxide  impurities  and  inhibiting grain growth. The long-term ablation behavior and mechanisms of PyCCsf/ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramics were examined through oxyacetylene ablation tests. All samples exhibited excellent oxidation resistance and ablation performance. After 120 s of ablation at 2000 °C, the linear ablation rate and mass ablation rate of were approximately - 4.68 × 10 -3 mm/s and - 9.17 × 10 -4 g/s, respectively.
Fig. 11. Schematic diagram of B4C combustion aid mechanism (a-c), actual microscopic view of sintering (d-f). Reproduced with permission from Ref. [82], © Elsevier 2024.

2. Materials and Methods
The synthesis of carbonitrides was performed in the SHS reactor. The mechanically activated mixture was placed inside the reactor as a loose powder. Nitrogen was used as the reactive gas and pumped into the reactor at a pressure of P = 0.8 MPa. The initiation of a self-sustaining exothermic reaction was carried out using a hot tungsten coil heated by an electric current. Bulk (Ta,Hf)CN samples with a diameter of 12 mm  and  a  thickness  of  3 -5  mm  were  obtained  by  SPS  of  the  assynthesized powder on a Labox 650 device (SinterLand, Japan). Sintering was performed under vacuum conditions (20 Pa) at a heating rate of 100 ◦ C/min up to a maximum temperature of 2000 ◦ C with a 20 min dwelling time. An external load of 50 MPa was applied during the entire SPS process. Described sintering regime was chosen based on our previous  results,  dedicated  to  the  fabrication  of  ultra-high-temperature nonstoichiometric hafnium carbonitride via combustion synthesis and spark plasma sintering [13]. In this article we utilized the most effective sintering mode from the standpoint of the temperature and density of the obtained ceramic material. Sintering at a higher temperature was also carried out (2100 ◦ C), however, no significant differences in the microstructure  and  density  of  the  samples  were  found,  thus  the described mode was chosen.

4. Conclusions
ZrB2-based ultrahigh temperature ceramics were prepared by the reactive spark plasma sintering using the vanadium powders and graphite nano/uniFB02 akes as sintering aids. The consolidation process was performed for 7 min under the pressure of 40 MPa at di /uniFB00 erent SPS temperatures of 1800, 1900 and 2000 °C. The relative density of all samples were > 99% as an evidence of approaching the theoretical density values. Compounds of VB2 and ZrC were in-situ synthesized during the reactive SPS at all temperatures. At the sintering temperature of 1800 °C, most vanadium and graphite additives were remained intact, while they completely were consumed by processing at 1900 °C. Beside the compounds of VB2 and ZrC, other complex phases of V1.54B50 and V3Zr3C were also in-situ formed at 2000 °C. Increasing the sintering temperature led to the signi /uniFB01 cant enhancement in the fracture toughness and slight drop in the hardness of ceramic matrix composites.

question: What heating rate was employed during ceramic processing?
answer: The heating rates employed during ceramic processing varied depending on the study. In one study, heating rates of 1 ◦ C/min, 3 ◦ C/min, and 5 ◦ C/min were used [1]. In another study, a heating rate of 25 ◦ C/min was employed [2]. Additionally, a heating rate of approximately 100 ◦ C/min was used in another study [3]. Lastly, a heating rate of approximately 100 ◦ C/min was also used in a different study [5].

2.5 Ceramic conversion studies of ZBS
The as-synthesized ZBS for all the eight molar feed ratios were cured/dried at 175 ◦ C in an air oven for 2 h. The mass of the resin and the cured/dried product were noted. The cured samples were made into pellets in a pelletizer and subjected to heat treatment at different temperatures, that is, 900 ◦ C, 1500 ◦ C, and 1650 ◦ C, under a continuous flow of argon gas to convert to ceramics. Heat treatment of samples at 900 ◦ C was performed in an inconel-based furnace and the remaining heat treatments were carried out in a graphite furnace. Multiple heating rates, that is, 1 ◦ C/min, 3 ◦ C/min, and 5 ◦ C/min, were employed to understand the effect of heating rate on the formation of ceramics at high temperatures. A dwell duration of 3 h was given at the peak temperature for all the heat-treatment cycles. Alumina crucibles were used for all the ceramic conversion studies at different temperatures. Rietveld refinement was carried out for the XRD patterns to analyze the ceramic phase composition. Ceramic specimens were prepared by grinding the pellets manually into powders using mortar and pestle.
TABLE 1 Physical properties of zirconoborosiloxane (ZBS) formulations resulting in homogenous resinous liquid.

2.1. Materials preparation
Nano-sized ZrB2 powders (500 nm, purity > 98%, Beijing Forsman Scienti /uniFB01 c Co. Ltd., China) and SiC powders (40 nm, purity > 99%, Nanjing Emperor Nano Material Co. Ltd., China) are used as the raw materials. In the sintering process, the temperature and pressure were set as 1700 /C14 C and 40 MPa, respectively. The other parameters refer to the previous work [1,3]. The relative density of the samples was about 92.6%.
Before the joining ceramics, the surfaces of the samples were polished by diamond grinding discs up to 1200-grit. Then, the samples were ultrasonically cleaned in ethanol for 10 min. Nanosized Ni powders (500 nm, purity > 99%, Nanjing Emperor Nano Material Co. Ltd., China) were selected as brazing /uniFB01 llers (140 mg for each joint). SPS and HP methods were adopted to join the ZS/Ni/ZS specimen at 1200 /C14 C for 30 min with a pressure of 10 MPa, respectively. During the SPS joining process, a heating rate of 25 /C14 C/ minwas employed to reach 1200 /C14 C, and a cooling rate of 25 /C14 C/min employed to reach room temperature as shown in Fig. 1 (plotted by solid line). During the HP joining process, the same heating rate of 25 /C14 C/min was employed to reach 1200 /C14 C with a hot pressing apparatus (ZT-40-21Y, Shanghai Chen Hua technology Co. Ltd., China), and then cooled as furnace to room temperature, as shown in Fig. 1 (plotted by dotted line).

2. Experimental procedure
The ceramics were produced by reactive hot-pressing at 1100 -1830 °C and 30 MPa (the load applied before the powder heating) in a graphite die in argon. The die was heated with an AC (50 Hz) current in a DCS-1 hot-press produced by SRC Synthesis (Kiev, Ukraine)
and the University of Hudders /uniFB01 eld. Charge pre-heating was done in an actively pumped vacuum better than 10 -2 Pa. The heating rate (after pre-heating at 500 °C for 10 min) was approximately 100 °C/min. Argon was introduced at this stage in the sintering chamber up to atmospheric pressure. Initially, a set of ceramics was produced at di /uniFB00 erent maximum sintering temperatures to investigate the material densi /uniFB01 cation. The isothermal dwelling time is presented in Table 1.
The sintered ceramics were in the form of 10 mm discs. Fully densi /uniFB01 ed Z6 and ZN discs had a thickness around 4.2 mm.
The bulk density of the sintered ceramics was measured using the Archimedes method and the theoretical densities were estimated according to the rule of mixtures based on the right-hand side of reaction (3) (for Z5 and Z6) and taking into account the CNT content (for ZN).
The crystalline phases were identi /uniFB01 ed by X-Ray Di /uniFB00 raction (XRD) and the identi /uniFB01 cation was supported by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM). For the SEM analysis, ceramic samples were cleaved, allowing to avoid polishing artefacts in a composite with phases with a high hardness di /uniFB00 erence. Vickers hardness measurements were performed with a load of 9.8 N for 20 s on polished surfaces (20 indents per sample). The fracture toughness was estimated by measuring the crack lengths generated by the Vickers indentations with a load of 49 N (10 indents per sample). The indentation toughness was calculated according to the formula of Evans and Charles [30].

Abstract
Pre-ceramic polymers comprising of Zr, C, B, Si, and O in a single system named as family of zirconoborosiloxane (ZBS) with a high ceramic yield of 83 wt.% at 1200 ◦ C were synthesized by non-aqueous sol-gel approach. With eight different molar feed ratios, ceramic conversion studies were carried out at 1500 ◦ Cand 1650 ◦ Cin argon, and three different heating rates were employed. The polymers were analyzed for their functional groups, ceramic residue, molecular weight, viscosity, and specific gravity. The evolution of various ceramic phases by varying the heating/cooling rate, morphology, elemental composition, and mapping wasstudied and carbon content was analyzed. Zirconia in monoclinic and tetragonal crystal structures and zircon were obtained as major ceramic phases. Silica or silicon carbide was not observed with the precursor feeds, heat-treatment temperatures or heating rate combinations used in this study. The carbon content of the ceramics formed was found to be present in very low concentrations. ZBS was completely converted into oxide-free ceramics of ZrC, ZrB 2 , and SiC using polyimide and divinylbenzene as carbon source at temperatures as low as 1500 ◦ C. Thus, ZBS in combination with polyimide and divinylbenzene in liquid form is proposed as matrix resin for ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites.

2.2. Sample sintering and preparation
All ceramics were manufactured by hot pressing for 4 min under a uniaxial pressure of 30 MPa. The sintering temperature, which was 1850
◦ C for most of the samples, is presented in Table 1. The load was applied before the powder heating in a 10 mm diameter graphite die in argon at atmospheric pressure. The initial powder portion was calculated, targeting a height of 4 mm for fully dense samples. The die was heated with an AC (50 Hz) current in a DCS-1 hot-press produced by SRC Synthesis (Kiev,  Ukraine)  and  the  University  of  Huddersfield.  The  pre-heating stage  took  15 min  at  500 ◦ C,  followed  by  a  heating  rate  of  approximately 100 ◦ C/min. The cooling rate was around 200 ◦ C/min down to 500 ◦ C. The hot-pressed specimens were discs 10 mm in diameter. The sample surfaces were polished with diamond abrasive media of 25, 9, 3, 1, and 0.1 µ m.

question: What holding or dwell time was used during sintering of the ceramic materials?
answer:
The holding time during sintering of the ceramic materials varied depending on the specific study:

- For the ZrB2-SiC powder containing B4C and C as sintering additives, the sintering schedule included external isothermal holds at 600 8 C and 1600 8 C for 30 min in vacuum, and then the gelcast ceramic was pressureless sintered to 2100 8 C with a holding time of 2 h [1].
- In the production of short /uniFB01 bre reinforced ceramic composites based on ZrB2, the sintering was performed at 1800 -2300 °C with varying holding times, but specific values were not provided [2].
- For the TiB2-based materials, the dwell time was lower than that reported in literature, though specific values were not provided [3].
- In the case of TaB2-SiC granules, the sintering temperature was 1600 ◦ C without holding, and an intermediate holding for an hour at 300 ◦ C was made to remove the organic binder. The final sintering was carried out at 1800 ◦ C in a vacuum with a holding for 20 min [4].
- For the HfB2-SiC-X system UHTCs, the optimal hardness sample was achieved without employing maximum sintering temperature or holding duration, but specific holding times were not provided [5].

chunks.text:
2.1. Raw materials
The whole sintering schedule included external isothermal holds at 600 8 C and 1600 8 C for 30 min in vacuum (15 Pa), respectively, and then argon gas (1 atm) was then introduced into the furnace. The gelcast ceramic was then continually pressureless sintered to 2100 8 C with a holding time of 2 h.
Fig. 1. SEM of ZrB2-SiC powder containing B4C and C as sintering additives.
Fig. 2. Flow chart of the gelcasting process.

A B S T R A C T
Short /uniFB01 bre reinforced ceramic composites, based on ZrB2 with a carbon /uniFB01 bre content of 45 vol%, were produced by ball-milling and rapid Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) at 1800 -2300 °C by varying the holding times. Careful control of the sintering parameters, such as the heating rate, dwell temperature and holding time are required to produce a fully dense matrix and avoid /uniFB01 bre degradation. The sintered materials were characterized in terms of relative density, morphology of the /uniFB01 bres and microstructural features of the matrix. The /uniFB01 nal residual porosity ranged from 16 to 3 vol%. The carbon /uniFB01 bres maintained their original morphology and the matrix consisted of ZrB2, SiC and ZrC phases. In spite of the good adhesion between the /uniFB01 bre surface and the matrix promoted by SPS processing, extensive /uniFB01 bre pull-out was observed in the fracture surfaces of all of the composites sintered at or below 1900 °C.

3.1. Densification behaviour
High-density samples ( > 97 %, see Table 2) were obtained despite a dwell time lower than that reported in literature [48,49]. TW2 samples showed the lowest onset temperature (TON) of 1010 ◦ C. This temperature is quite low compared to other similar TiB2-based materials [50], and is in agreement with the previous work [39]. However, the sintering phenomena start to slow down before the maximum temperature is reached, as indicated by the peak of the densification rate curve occurring well before the dwell step and the plateauing of the curve at 97 % before the start of dwell time (Fig. 1a). This sintering behaviour can be attributed to the presence of Co, which promotes liquid sintering mechanisms that require lower temperatures to be activated, but were not sufficient to complete densification [51]. ZW6 samples reached full densification quickly, while HW6 samples required a longer isothermal hold due to the refractory nature of HfB2. In particular, ZrB2-based material showed the highest densification rate value of 7.3 % min GLYPH<0> 1 allowing full densification within a limited dwell time. It is worth noting that the observed TON of 1400 ◦ C is lower than those reported for ZrB2 with other ceramic sintering aids (Si3N4, SiC, Y2O3, Al2O3, ZrN) [52 -54]. A lower TON was only obtained when using Ni as the sintering aid [55]. However, Ni prevented grain growth, which is desired to increase the thermal conductivity. For this reason, although the activation of densification mechanisms at low temperature and reaching the plateau took less than 15 min, the isothermal hold was extended to 30 min to ensure a coarse and homogeneous microstructure and to raise the thermal conductivity. Noteworthy, the manufacturing process of this work allowed to reduce the starting densification temperature (TON) compared to similar ZrB2 materials [56] containing higher amount of starting WC (15 vol% instead of 4.1 vol%).

2. Materials and methods
Dispersed granular inclusions were obtained by the  method described  in  Ref.  [26].  TaB2 -SiC  powder  mixtures  were  coated  with oleic acid in a rotating ball mill for 14 h. After that, a 1% solution of polyvinyl alcohol was sprayed into an open pot rotating at an angle to the horizon at a speed of 15 rpm using an argon spray gun. The sprayed polyvinyl alcohol solution coated the particles in the mixture and stuck them together. The rotation of the pot led to an increase in the average size of the formed granules. Ceramic granules were sifted through laboratory sieves between mesh 270 and mesh 100, the size of the selected granules ranged from 50 to 150 μ m. The sintering of the granules was carried  out  to  give  them  sufficient  strength  for  further  work  on  the formation of matrix -granule powder mixtures. The sintering temperature was 1600 ◦ C without holding. During the sintering process, an intermediate  holding  for  an  hour  at  300 ◦ C  was  made  to  remove  the organic binder, achieved at a rate of 50 ◦ C h GLYPH<0> 1 . Heating from 300 to 1600 ◦ C was carried out at a rate of 200 ◦ C h GLYPH<0> 1 .
After sintering, the granules had an irregular shape with an average size of 167.3 ± 11.5 μ m, estimated according to the SEM micrograph intercept procedure described in ASTM E112, Fig. 1. A similar approach was applied to estimate the average grain size of sintered ceramics.
The matrix phase and the granules were mixed in a magnetic mixer in an ethanol medium. The final sintering of the studied materials was carried  out  under  a  pressure  of  about  150  MPa  at  a  temperature  of 1800 ◦ C in a vacuum with holding for 20 min. Fig. 2 illustrates the structure  of  the  formed  composites.  The  sample  compositions  are presented in Table 1.
Fig. 1. A general view of TaB2 -SiC granules.

3.5. Machine learning model interpretation
The peak sintering temperature also exerts a positive influence on final material hardness. Extensive studies [39] confirm that densification  behavior  and  grain  growth  in  ceramic  materials  predominantly depend on sintering temperature, holding time, and heating rate. Optimized sintering temperatures enable the fabrication of dense HfB2 materials with refined grains. While relative density increases with elevated sintering temperatures, excessive temperature gains provide diminishing returns on densification and conversely promote grain coarsening, ultimately  reducing  material  hardness  [40].  The  optimal  hardness sample in this study was achieved without employing maximum sintering temperature or holding duration, validating this conclusion and providing guidance for energy-efficient parameter optimization in highhardness UHTC fabrication.
Current  reported  hardness  values  for  HfB2-SiC-X  system  UHTCs predominantly range between 15 -30 GPa, though discrepancies exist due to variations in preparation and testing methodologies. This study successfully identified higher hardness formulations through machine learning-assisted optimization with merely two iterative cycles (8 total samples),  achieving  a  peak  hardness  of  25.13  GPa.  Compared  with conventional trial-and-error approaches, this machine learningintegrated  methodology  demonstrates  superior  efficiency  in  resource conservation while accelerating research progress, offering promising technical support for rapid performance optimization and discovery of novel UHTCs.

question: What maximum temperature stability or operating temperature was achieved or tested for the ceramic systems?
answer: The maximum operating temperature achieved for the ceramic thin-film heat flux sensor is 1250 ℃ [5]. Additionally, UHTCs are capable of withstanding temperatures exceeding 2000°C [3], and some UHTC compounds can operate in environments with projected service temperatures exceeding 2000°C [3].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
With the development of aerospace vehicles and atmospheric re -entry systems, increasing attention has been paid to ceramic matrix composites (CMCs) due to their excellent thermal, electrical, and mechanical properties such as high hardness, stability, and strength [1]. Meanwhile, the common CMCs not always meet the stringent requirements for modern industrial environments and instantaneous temperature changes. Particularly, the above goals could be mostly achieved by introducing ultra -high -temperature ceramics (UHTCs) with outstanding physicochemical properties and the ability to withstand harsh environments [2,3].
UHTCs belong to a class of refractory ceramics whose melting points can exceed 3000 /C14 C. These materials exhibit outstanding stability at temperatures above 2000 /C14 C, which can be attained within a few hours or even minutes. The impressive temperature characteristics make UHTCs attractive for the use in microelectronics, molten metal seals, high--temperature electrodes, and wear -resistant surfaces [4]. The phase composition of UHTCs includes transition metal borides, carbides, or nitrides of Zr, Hf, Ta, W, and Nb [5 -10]. However, the obstacles of using UHTCs is that they are preparation of UHTC by carbothermic reduction method is carried out using a ball -milled mixture of carbon black and oxide ceramic powder. Nevertheless, due to the incompatibility of the

A B S T R A C T
A density of less than 2 g/cm 3 , high maximum operating temperatures (2000 ◦ C) combined with high heat capacity and thermal shock resistance make light brakes capable of absorbing huge amounts of energy  in  a  short  time  [35].  However,  C/C  materials  start  to  oxidize significantly at 600 ◦ C and possess low COF in wet or cold conditions due to the inherent lubrication ability of graphite [35,36]. These disadvantages make them unsuitable for standard road vehicles. To overcome these problems, C/C-SiC composites, developed since the 1990 s, are manufactured by liquid silicon infiltration (LSI) of C/C and used in high performance cars [37]. These CMCs exhibit exceptional wear resistance, less  oxidation and a high COF in different weather conditions at the expense  of  weight  and  limited  operating  temperature  [36].  C/C-SiC disks  are  usually  paired  with  organic  or  metallic  pads  with  some research being carried out on ceramic pads for full-ceramic brake systems [38,39], but with C/C-SiC pads still having hard vibration harshness (NVH) and high thermal conductivity issues.

KEYWORDS
CMCs; UHTCs; thermal protection systems; propulsion; corrosion; ablation; self-healing
temperatures ranging from 3200 to 4000°C. For heat /uniFB02 uxes >10 MWm -2 and projected service temperature exceeding 2000°C, the thermal and chemical stability of UHTC compounds is of great importance and for this reason they are being considered as candidates for use in extreme environments, e.g. hypersonic /uniFB02 ight and propulsion. More recently, the development of suitable techniques to densify and machine UHTCs at reasonable cost triggered a considerable amount of testing. UHTC nozzle inserts were tested in small scale rocket engine motors [11] con /uniFB01 rming that they lacked the capability to withstand rapid thermal gradients without su /uniFB00 ering premature failure due to poor thermal shock resistance. However, these tests also con /uniFB01 rmed the outstanding erosion resistance displayed by this class of ceramics. TPS systems such as nose cones and leading edges entirely based on bulk, unreinforced UHTC ceramics were successfully tested in arc jet experiments [4].

1. Introduction
In recent years, with the rapid development of the aerospace field, higher requirements have been put forward for the key hot-end components of aero engines, aircraft and rocket engines, which must have the ability of long-term service in extreme environments such as high temperatures, high pressures and high-temperature water-oxygen corrosion [1 -3]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) can maintain stable service performance under high temperature environments above 3000 ◦ C, rendering them one of the most promising potential materials for high temperature thermal protection systems [4 -6]. Zr-based UHTCs have high melting point ( ≥ 3000 ◦ C), low density, excellent high temperature mechanical properties, good thermal stability, good oxidation resistance and corrosion resistance, and are therefore widely used in

A B S T R A C T
Real-time  monitoring  of  surface  heat  flux  in  high-temperature  and  temperature-changing  environments  is gaining significant attention for various promising applications, including aero engines, gas turbines, and nuclear power plants. However, the survivability of most current thin-film heat flux sensors above 1200 ◦ C still faces challenges. Here, we designed and fabricated a ceramic thin-film thermopile heat flux sensor on an alumina ceramic substrate by physical vapor deposition (PVD). The ITO-In2O3 was chosen as the sensitive layer, while a multilayered YSZ-Al2O3/Al2O3  ceramic film was used as the protective layer to enhance high-temperature stability.  Meanwhile, the microstructure and electrical properties of the sensitive films were investigated under different  annealing conditions. The results indicate that the sensitive films have a stable structure and good electrical conductivity. The heat flux sensor reaches a sensitivity of 73.4 μ V/(kW/m 2 ) and can measure heat flux up to 1.06 MW/m 2 . Interestingly, the sensor has excellent high-temperature survivability, and can withstand temperatures  up  to  1250 ℃ in  the  ambient  atmosphere.  Moreover,  the  application  tests  were  conducted  to simulate engine environments, such as flame combustion, shock resistance. These results also indicate that the fabricated sensor shows great potential for actual working applications.

In our previous work, Wang et al. [11] chose Pt/Pt-13Rh as the thermopile material, then deposited SiO2 covering the whole thermopile to avoid oxidation and rapid failure at high temperatures. The sensitivity of sensor was 6.13 μ V(kW/m 2 ) with a temperature tolerance of 1000 ℃ . Although the design of an oxidation-resistant  layer  improves  the  sensor ' s  high-temperature  stability,  metal  thermopile  thin  films  are  unreliable  because  they  are oxidized at temperatures up to 1000 ℃ in air [12].

question: What elastic modulus values were reported for the ceramic materials in these studies?
answer:
The elastic modulus values reported for the ceramic materials in these studies are as follows:

- For ZrB2 ceramics, the elastic modulus ranges from approximately 350 to approximately 530 GPa depending on porosity and additives [1].
- For fully dense ZrB2, the elastic modulus is estimated to be 511 GPa based on fitting data to models, while the measured values in previous studies range from 490 to 500 GPa [1].
- The elastic modulus of 99.8%-dense ZrB2 was measured to be 489 GPa [1].
- For ZrC ceramics, the undoped sample (W0) exhibited an elastic modulus of 387.8 GPa, while the sample with 4 wt% WC (W4) had an elastic modulus of 452.9 GPa [2].
- The elastic modulus of ZrB2-SiC ceramics decreases from 530 GPa at room temperature to approximately 100 GPa at 1600°C [5].

chunks.text:
8.2.1  ZrB 2
Elastic modulus values reported for selected ZrB 2 ceramics with and without sintering additives are summarized in Table 8.1. Elastic modulus ranges from approximately 350 to approximately 530 GPa depending on porosity and additives (Fig. 8.1). Fitting the data to the Einstein (linear) [42], Spriggs [43], and Nielson [40, 41] models (Table 8.2) gives a value for fully dense ZrB 2 of  511  GPa compared to measured values of 490500 GPa in previous studies [38, 44]. However, the materials in the historic studies were not fully dense, and fitting the historic data to similar models predicts an elastic modulus of 519 GPa for dense ZrB 2 . Zhang et al . [45] calculated the elastic modulus of ZrB 2 from first principles to be 520  GPa, in good agreement with the fitted data. Recent work by Okamoto et al .  calculated the polycrystalline elastic modulus of ZrB 2 from single crystal measurements to be 525 GPa [46]. The modulus of 99.8%-dense ZrB 2 was measured to be 489 GPa [26]. However, additions of AlN, Si 3 N 4 ,  B 4 C, and C, as well as impurities picked up during powder milling (i.e., WC, ZrO 2 ), affect the elastic modulus of ZrB 2 ceramics. Small additions of B 4 C and/or C increase the elastic modulus of ZrB 2 [20, 22, 29]. Additions of AlN and Si 3 N 4 lower the elastic modulus [11, 32]. Additions affect the elastic modulus primarily through interactions with impurities on the surface of  the  starting  powders. Additives  such  as  C  and  B 4 C remove low modulus surface ceramics

with and without sintering additives References Flexure strength Fracture toughness Hardness Elastic modulus Grain size Relative density Composition

3.2.2 Mechanical properties of the ZrC ceramics
The  effect  of  WC  doping  on  the  mechanical  properties  of  the  ZrC  ceramics  was investigated using nanoindentation tests. The measured hardness and elastic modulus values of ceramic were summarized in Table 2. The undoped sample W0 exhibited a hardness of 11.96 GPa and an elastic modulus of 387.8 GPa. The sample W4 exhibited a hardness of 17.79 ± 0.58 GPa and an elastic modulus of 452.9 ± 21.7 GPa. The measured hardness aligns with values reported in the literature, whereas the elastic modulus is notably higher than those reported for similar materials [55-57]. However, a sharp deterioration in mechanical properties was observed when the WC content was increased f to 6 wt%. The hardness and elastic  modulus decreased markedly to 12.79 GPa and 339.3 GPa, respectively, with the elastic modulus value falling below that of the undoped sample W0. Table 2 Hardness and Elastic modulus of the sintered samples. Sample Hardness (GPa) W0 11.96 ± 1.27 W2 14.93 ± 0.86 W4 17.79 ± 0.58 W6 12.79 ± 1.16 2100℃-65MPa-SPS 17.90 ± 0.60 Journal Pre-proof

8.3.1  Elastic Modulus of ZrB 2 -Based Ceramics
The elevated-temperature elastic modulus of hot-pressed ZrB 2 with and without additives is shown in Figure 8.10. Only limited data on elastic behavior of ZrB 2 have been published  for  elevated  temperatures.  Okamoto et  al .  [49]  reported  elastic  modulus  from single  crystal  values  as  being  525  GPa  at  room  temperature,  decreasing  linearly  to 490 GPa at 1100°C. Experimental results of bulk ZrB 2 have resulted in lower values of elastic modulus than those reported by Okamoto. The historical work of Rhodes et al . [25] provided the only reported values for the elastic modulus of bulk ZrB 2 tested in inert atmosphere. The modern studies by Zhu [20] and Neuman et al . [29] were conducted in air,  and  thus  are  affected  by  the  formation  of  an  oxide  scale  on  the  test  articles. Unfortunately, the effect of the oxide scale on the measured elastic modulus was not investigated. Regardless, the elastic modulus of bulk ZrB 2 decreased more rapidly than expected  based  on  single  crystal  measurements  presumably  due  to  a  grain  boundary effect. The modulus decreased linearly from room temperature to approximately 1200°C (450 GPa). Above  this  temperature,  the  elastic  modulus  decreased  more  rapidly  with temperature. The change in slope was thought to be a result of softening of grain boundary phases  combined with the  activation  of  grain  boundary  sliding  and  diffusional  creep mechanisms [20, 25, 29, 69, 90]. Reported modulus values were similar up to 1600°C

figure  8.10.  elevated-temperature  elastic  modulus  of  hot-pressed  Zrb 2 with  and  without additives [20, 25,29].
figure 8.11.  elevated-temperature elastic modulus of hot- pressed Zrb 2 -sic with and without additives [25, 69].

10.2  ELASTIC PROPERTIES
Bulk ceramics contain many grains in random orientations and can, therefore, be considered  isotropic  in  their  response  to  elastic  loading.  However,  since  this  isotropic response is a weighted average of the anisotropic response of the single-crystal grains from which the body is made, it remains instructive to consider the elastic response of a single  crystal.  The  carbides  of  the  early  transition  metals  with  melting  point  above 3000°C (ZrC, HfC, TaC, and NbC) are all cubic with the rock salt structure (space group Fm m 3 ). This means that the contracted relationship between stresses and strains
<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
which describes  the  elastic  response  of  single  crystals,  only  contains  3  independent elastic constantsC 11 (= C 22 = C 33 ), C 12 (= C 13 = C 21 = C 23 = C 31 = C 32 ),  and C 44 (= C 55 = C 66 )and all other constants are zero. Zeener defined the anisotropy number, A , of cubic crystals as [2]
<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
Calculation of the shear modulus in all possible orientations using the transformations given in Ref. [3] shows that for cubic crystals this is the ratio of the extremes of the shear moduli and therefore a good measure for the anisotropy in the elastic response.
Experimental values for the elastic constants are reported in bold in Table 10.1 alongside values reported from first-principles and other theoretical calculations. The experimental values show that A is  in  between 0.71 and 0.95. This means that shear across the {100} planes in 〈 010 〉 directions is easier than shear across the {111} planes in any direction and that pulling along <111> directions has the lowest  Young's modulus for any direction in the crystal, while pulling in <100> directions gives rise to the highest

8.3.1  Elastic Modulus of ZrB 2 -Based Ceramics
(250 MPa), despite the differences in testing methods. To date, Rhodes [25] is the only study to report the modulus above 1600°C, where it decreases to 100 GPa at 2000°C.
The  elevated-temperature  elastic  modulus  of  ZrB 2 -SiC  ceramics  is  shown  in Figure 8.11. Rhodes et al . [25] observed a steady decrease in modulus from 530 GPa at room  temperature  to  420 GPa  at  1400°C.  Above  1400°C,  the  modulus  decreased dramatically to approximately 100 GPa at 1600°C. Neuman et al . [69] observed a steady decrease from 510 GPa at room temperature to 410 GPa at 1000°C. The slope of the modulus then  changed,  decreasing  to  210 GPa  at  1500°C,  and  then  more  rapidly  to 110 GPa at 1600°C. As with ZrB 2, the changes in slope of the elastic modulus are likely the result of softening of grain boundary phases, and grain boundary sliding and diffusional creep. In ZrB 2 -SiC, the softening was expected to occur at a lower temperatures to the presence of SiO 2 (surface oxide impurity from SiC) and its interaction with the B 2 O 3 and ZrO 2 present from the ZrB 2 . Zou et al . [90] measured the internal damping in ZrB 2 -SiC prepared by milling with Si 3 N 4 balls, and found that the damping peaks just above 800°C and again around 1400°C. The reduction in modulus with temperature for ZrB 2 -SiC ceramics is enhanced by the presence of oxides at the grain boundaries and triple junctions as well as the polycrystalline nature of the material.

4 Conclusion
36 High-performance  ZrC  ceramics  with  fine-grains  (<2  μm),  high  density  and  superior mechanical  properties  were  fabricated  by  hot-pressing  with  WC  as  a  sintering  aid.  The effects of WC doping on phase evolution, micro/nanostructure and mechanical properties were systematically clarified through multiscale structural characterization combined with first-principles  calculations.  Partial  dissolution  of  WC  into  the  ZrC  lattice  led  to  the formation of (Zr x W1x )C solid solution, which reduced the diffusion barrier of carbon atoms, promoted the carbothermal reduction of ZrO2 and ZrC sintering and thereby purified grain boundaries.  Undissolved  WC  at  the  grain  boundaries  enhanced  interfacial  adhesion, generating a synergistic effect of solid-solution strengthening and grain boundary strengthening/purification. Excessive W dissolution shifted the carbon chemical potential, inducing the precipitation of supersaturated free carbon at grain boundaries and consequently degrading the mechanical performance. The elastic modulus decreased by 12.5% relative to that  of  the  undoped  ZrC.  First-principles  calculations  further  confirmed  that  ZrC-WC interfaces  which  were  characterized  by  high  adhesion  work,  low  interfacial  energy  and favorable thermodynamic stability was suitable for the improvement of interface bonding strength.  In addition, W doping was found to significantly lower the diffusion barrier by facilitating electron donation from W to the migrating C atom, thereby forming stronger CW covalent bonds that stabilized the transition state and enhanced carbon migration. This work provides fundamental insights into the role of WC in the sintering of ZrC ceramics, offering theoretical and practical guidance for tuning solid-solution and interfacial behaviors in advanced ceramics for extreme environments. question: What is the hardness of ceramic materials used in extreme thermal environments?
answer: The hardness of ceramic materials used in extreme thermal environments varies, but for example, the Vickers hardness of YB4 is measured to be approximately 28.5 GPa [2]. Additionally, UHTCs generally exhibit high hardness due to their strong covalent bonds [1].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are an emerging class of materials that have the potential for use in extreme environments [1,2]. Most often, UHTCs are de /uniFB01 ned as compounds that have melting points above 3000 °C (Fig. 1) [3]. Several alternative de /uniFB01 nitions have been proposed with the most pragmatic being that UHTCs are ceramic materials that can be used for extended times at temperatures above 1650 °C [4]. However, none of these de /uniFB01 nitions captures the wide range of extreme conditions in which UHTCs may be used. Chemically, nearly all UHTCs are binary compounds in which boron, carbon, or nitrogen combine with one of the early transition metals (TMs) such as Zr, Hf, Ti, Nb, and Ta [5]. The strong covalent bonds between the TMs and B, C, or N result in compounds with high hardness, stiffness, and melting temperature [6]. These compounds also exhibit higher electrical and thermal conductivities than oxide ceramics due to varying degrees of metallic bond character. This intriguing combination of metal-like and ceramic-like properties allow UHTCs to survive extreme temperatures, heat /uniFB02 uxes, radiation levels, mechanical loads, chemical reactivities, and other conditions that are beyond the capabilities of existing structural materials.
Reports of the synthesis of UHTCs date back to the late 1800s [7,8], but technological interest in these materials grew dramatically during the space race that started in the late 1950s and extended through the 1960s. At that time, groups in the Soviet Union and the U.S. were searching for materials to be used in rocket motors, heat shields, and structural components for the /uniFB01 rst generation of spacecraft [9,10].

1. Introduction
With  the  development  of  next  generation  hypersonic  vehicles, ensuring their stable and repeated operation under extreme environmental conditions has imposed increasingly stringent demands on the properties of ultrahigh temperature materials [1 -5]. Particularly in hot end components such as the leading edges, engine combustion chambers, and nose sections these areas are frequently subjected to extreme thermal environments reaching temperatures as high as 2500 ◦ C. As a result, the materials are required to have excellent oxidation resistance, ablation resistance, and thermal shock resistance [6 -9]. Based on the above harsh work conditions, especially the necessity for an extremely high melting point (exceeding 3000 ◦ C), ultrahigh-temperature ceramics becomes the most promising candidate materials for the hot end structures of both current and future hypersonic aerospace systems [10 -12].
Among these ultrahigh-temperature ceramic, YB4  exhibits an high melting point of 2800 ◦ C and excellent thermal stability, making it one of the most promising candidate material for extreme environment applications [13]. Early studies have demonstrated that YB4 ceramics can be successfully synthesized under vacuum conditions with a temperature range of 1500 ◦ C ~ 1900 ◦ C [14]. The compound crystallizes in the tetragonal system (space group: P4/mbm ) [15], with lattice parameters of a = 7.107  Å  and c = 4.018  Å  [16].  Experimental  measurements indicate that the Vickers hardness of YB4  is measured to be approximately 28.5 GPa [16]. Furthermore, due to the existence of B6 octahedra

1. Introduction
Ceramics, in particular the ultrahigh temperature ceramics (UHTCs), have much higher melting points (over 3000 °C) [41-49]. UHTCs are a specific type of ceramics, which can be utilized in harsh environments containing extreme temperatures and chemical reactivity. Considering the melting temperature as a criterion, a handful of carbides [50-59], borides [60-103], and nitrides [104] of transition metals can be considered as UHTCs [66,105-107]. High strength, long fatigue life, good resistance to oxidation and corrosion at high temperatures are some of the most important features of UHTCs [108-125]. Borides such as TiB2, ZrB2, and HfB2 offer high thermal and electrical conductivities, good elastic modulus and excellent hardness [99-101]. They have widely used in oxidizing environments because of their superior oxidation resistance in comparison with the other materials [126-129].

1. Introduction
Ultrahigh-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) refer to thermal structural ceramics with melting points above 3000 ◦ Cthat can stably exist in high-temperature, oxygen-rich environments exceeding 2000 ◦ C [6]. These typically include carbides, borides, oxides, and nitrides of refractory metals (Figure 2) and their multicomponent composite ceramics [7]. Table 1 lists the physical and mechanical properties of several boride, carbide, and nitride-based UHTCs [5]. It is evident that UHTCs have extremely high melting points (>3000 ◦ C), high thermal conductivity, and very high strength and hardness, making them highly promising materials for ultrahigh-temperature thermal protection [8]. Figure 1f shows the historical perspective on research related to ultrahigh-temperature ceramics and composites [5]. The earliest research on UHTCs can be traced back to the 1950s, when HfB2 and ZrB2 were used as nuclear reactor materials because of their excellent high-temperature corrosion resistance [9]. Starting from the 1960s, the UHTCs were considered as thermal protection materials by the ManLabs laboratory funded by the Air Force Materials Laboratory of the USA [5,10]. At the end of the 1990s, the demonstration tests of the thermal protection structure of UHTCs carried out by Ames and Glenn Research Center together with Sandia National Laboratory and the Air Force Space Command proved their great potential in extreme environments from the viewpoint of ablation resistance, although they exposed the intrinsic brittleness [7]. Therefore, strengthening and toughening the UHTCs have become research hotspots in recent years. The fracture toughness of bulk UHTCs can be improved to a certain extent by introducing particulates, whiskers, or platelets as reinforcements. However, the intrinsic brittleness cannot be eliminated completely. In addition, due to ultrahigh processing temperatures of bulk UHTCs, it is difficult to prepare components with complex shapes on a large scale.

1. Introduction
Among ceramics that can be used above 2000 ◦ C, ultra-high temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  (carbide,  boride  and  nitride  of  transition group IVB elements) have promising application potential in extreme environment [1 -3]. These ceramic materials are featured by strong covalent bonds, showing the high melting point ( ≥ 3000 ◦ C), high hardness, high strength and good ablation resistance, and high electrical and thermal conductivity in view of their metallic bond properties [4 -6]. Based on the above advantages, they are used in key thermal structural components  of  hypersonic  vehicles  and  thermal  protective  coatings [7 -10].
In  general,  when  UHTCs  are  used  as  high-temperature  protective coatings, they can protect the substrate materials to maintain their own structural performance advantages in ultra-high temperature environment [11,12]. C/C composites have been widely used in aerospace field due  to  their  low  density  (~2.0  g/cm 3 ),  high  strength  and  excellent high-temperature mechanical properties. Actually, C/C composites will be oxidized in oxygen-containing environments above 450 ◦ C, resulting in rapid degradation of their mechanical properties [13,14]. Therefore,

question: What maximum temperature can ultra-high-temperature ceramic materials tolerate in service?
answer: Ultra-high-temperature ceramic materials can tolerate service temperatures above 2000 ◦ C [1], and some can even maintain stable service performance under high temperature environments above 3000 ◦ C [3].

1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics are ceramic matrix composite materials that can maintain their structural stability and function normally in  working  environments  above  2000 ◦ C.  Unlike  ordinary  hightemperature ceramics, ultra-high-temperature ceramics have a higher melting  point,  lower  density,  and  better  high-temperature  chemical stability  and ablation-resistance properties. They  were  originally developed by the US Air Force. Their superior performance is mainly used in the field of national defense, such as aerospace engines, spacecraft  head  and  wing  leading  edges,  and  high-temperature  surface coating [1 -6]. Their temperature often reaches 2000 ◦ C. Above 2000 ◦ C, special materials are needed to meet heat protection and load-bearing requirements. Ultra-high-temperature ceramics were created for these purposes.
At  present,  research  on  ultra-high-temperature  alloys  is  primarily focused on binary compounds, such as borides, nitrides, and carbides. The melting point of these kinds of ceramics is generally higher than that of  other  ultra-high-temperature  ceramics,  even  reaching  3000 ◦ C. 1. Introduction
In recent years, with the rapid development of the aerospace field, higher requirements have been put forward for the key hot-end components of aero engines, aircraft and rocket engines, which must have the ability of long-term service in extreme environments such as high temperatures, high pressures and high-temperature water-oxygen corrosion [1 -3]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) can maintain stable service performance under high temperature environments above 3000 ◦ C, rendering them one of the most promising potential materials for high temperature thermal protection systems [4 -6]. Zr-based UHTCs have high melting point ( ≥ 3000 ◦ C), low density, excellent high temperature mechanical properties, good thermal stability, good oxidation resistance and corrosion resistance, and are therefore widely used in
