KEYWORDS
of ceramic materials engineered to withstand temperatures exceeding 2000 ◦ C. 1 An important aspect to consider is the phrase 'to withstand temperatures above 2000 ◦ C'. On the one hand, this could imply that the material remains solid, which relates to its melting point. In this context, it is relatively straightforward to compile a list of materials, as their melting points can be calculated and measured. On the other hand, we might interpret 2000 ◦ Cas the critical maximum service temperature (T max ) at which a material can be used in a load-bearing component without succumbing to oxidation, chemical changes, or excessive creep. 2 The Tmax is defined as the highest temperature at which the material can be used continuously without experiencing significant changes in its properties. An increase in temperature may lead to degradation, chemical alteration, and excessive creep. Typically, T max is determined by assessing the material's strength at various temperatures. However, this presents a challenge, as the desired material property, such as strength, depends not only on temperature but also on the surrounding environment. For instance, tantalum carbide (TaC) has a melting point close to 4000 ◦ Cand exhibits exceptional strength at elevated temperatures, surpassing that of top-quality polycrystalline graphite. 3 It is the only material known to maintain its strength between 2900 ◦ C and 3200 ◦ C in an inert atmosphere. However, the active oxidation of TaC begins at approximately 750 ◦ C. 4 It is worth noting that interest in UHTCs is driven by the aerospace industry's requirements for the development of new space engines and hypersonic vehicles. For these applications, high oxidation resistance at very high temperatures, combined with resistance to other chemical reactions, mechanical stresses, and radiation, are critical factors in selecting UHTCs.

question: What bulk density do ultra-high-temperature ceramic materials and composites achieve?
answer: Ultra-high-temperature ceramic materials such as ZrB2, ZrC, and HfC have a high density ranging from 6.1 to 12.7 g/cm³ [1]. In contrast, ultra-high-temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) reinforced with short carbon fibres achieved a final low density of approximately 1 g/cm³ [3]. Additionally, the unreinforced bulk reference sample had a density of 5.1 g/cm³ [3].

1. Introduction
As an important member of the high-temperature material family, ultra-high-temperature ceramic materials have moderate strength and excellent  ablation  resistance.  However,  the  ultra-high  temperature ceramic materials represented by ZrB2 or ZrC are difficult to fully meet the requirements of practical engineering applications because of their inherent brittleness and low reliability [1 -5]. To solve the above problems, the preparation process of continuous fiber toughened composites can greatly improve the damage tolerance and reliability of the materials, meet the needs of excellent ablation resistance, and has a good engineering application prospect [6 -9]. Nevertheless, due to the great differences in the physical and chemical properties of different ceramics, it  is  difficult  to  achieve uniform dispersion of ultra-high temperature multiphase ceramic powder in the matrix, which restricts the synergistic matching  of  ablation  resistance  and  high-temperature  resistance  between  multicomponent  ceramic  phases.  Meanwhile,  the  density  of ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  such  as  ZrB2,  ZrC,  and  HfC  is  high (6.1 -12.7 g/cm 3 ).

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs),  with  melting  points exceeding 3000 ℃ , are highly promising for thermal management applications under ultra-high temperatures ( > 2000 ℃ ), such as thermal protection systems (TPS) in re-entry aircraft and hypersonic vehicles [1].  However,  the  high  density  (4.5 -14.5  g/cm 3 )  compared  to  SiC (~3.2 g/cm 3 ) and carbon-carbon composites (1.1 -1.4 g/cm 3 ), and high thermal conductivity (20 -120 W m GLYPH<0> 1  K GLYPH<0> 1 ) of bulk UHTCs have limited their use as thermal insulation materials [2]. One proposed solution is the incorporation of pores into UHTC components, which can effectively lower  both  bulk  density  and  thermal  conductivity  [3].  Such  porous UHTC insulation structures would be shielded by a dense outer shell of the vehicle, which isolates it from direct contact with air and eliminates concerns about oxidation of the porous structure. This strategy not only meets  the  requirement  for  high-temperature  resistance  but  also  addresses the need to protect sensitive electronic components within TPS

4. Summary and conclusions
In  this  study,  ultra-high-temperature  ceramic  matrix  composites (UHTCMCs)  reinforced  with  short  carbon  fibres  were  successfully fabricated  using  slip  casting  and  pressure-less  sintering  to  achieve  a conical shape. The results demonstrated that slip casting, despite being a traditional technique, can be adapted for fibre-reinforced composites, achieving good fibre distribution within the slurry. The green samples reinforced with fibres exhibited a flexural strength of 4.6 MPa, double that of unreinforced UHTC samples produced with the same method. During densification at 2100 ◦ C, the fibre-reinforced cones showed near zero shrinkage, resulting in a final low density of approximately 1 g/cm³ and no cracks. Despite their high porosity and significant carbon fibre content, the samples withstood 1 + 3 min at 1650 ◦ C on a bottom-up surface, along with two heating stages of 2 min each, from 1550 ◦ C to 1650 ◦ C.  Furthermore,  after  oxidation  at  1650 ◦ C,  the  mechanical strength of the samples increased to approximately 16 MPa. The unreinforced bulk reference sample, fabricated through the same process, had a density of 5.1 g/cm³, survived three consecutive oxidation cycles at 1650 ◦ C (1, 3, and 5 min), and exhibited flexural strengths of 140 MPa after sintering and 70 MPa after oxidation at 1650 ◦ C. However, cracks formed upon densification due to differential shrinkage between the top and base of the cones and the mechanical behavior remained brittle.

ABSTRACT
The identification  of  efficient  techniques  for  the  fabrication  of  Ultra  High  Temperature Ceramics (UHTCs) is very crucial in view of their rapid and wider development. Along these lines, the use of the self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) technique in combination with the SPS technology is examined in this chapter for the obtainment of fully dense MB 2 -SiC and MB 2 -MC-SiC (M=Zr, Hf, Ta) ceramics. The starting reactants are first processed by SHS to successfully form the desired composites. The resulting powders are subsequently consolidated by spark-plasma sintering  (SPS).  Bulk  products  with  relative  densities ≥ 96%  can  be  obtained  within  30 minutes, when the dwell temperature is 1800 °C and P=20 MPa. Hardness, fracture

3. Results and discussion
Regarding the mechanical properties, the Vickers hardness and toughness measured at room temperature were 15.4 ± 0.9 GPa and 2.0 ± 0.5 MPa m 0.5 for ZrB2 + 20 vol% MoSi2, 10.3 ± 0.6 GPa and 2.9 ± 0.5 MPa m 0.5 for ZrB2 + 20 vol%TaSi2, and 11 ± 1 GPa and 2.1 ± 0.3 MPa m 0.5 for ZrB2 + 20 vol%ZrSi2, respectively. The hardness of ZrB2 + 20 vol%MoSi2 is well within the typical range reported for ZrB2-MoSi2 ceramics [25,26], whereas the lower hardnesses of ZrB2 + 20 vol%TaSi2 and ZrB2 + 20 vol%ZrSi2 are attributable to the greater ductility of their second phases. This would also explain the toughness trends, which do not correlate directly with the grain size. Fig. 3 shows the Hertz indentation stress-strain curves of the three ZrB2 + 20 vol%MeSi2 (Me = Mo, Ta, or Zr) UHTCs processed in this study. The maximum testing temperature was 800 ◦ C, above which creep already occurred at the high contact pressures used in the Hertzian indention. In the case of ZrB2 + 20 vol%ZrSi2, however, the tests were interrupted at 600 ◦ C because, as shown in Fig. 4, at 800 ◦ C in air this UHTC already underwent severe peptization (confirmed by X-ray energy dispersive spectrometry in the SEM; not shown) that impeded its mechanical characterization. Therefore, the ZrB2 ceramic sintered here with ZrSi2 additive does not seem to fulfil the criterion of tolerance to the high temperatures in oxidizing environments that is demanded of UHTCs, an aspect that requires further investigation in future studies. 1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is an ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) that generates great interest in the aerospace sector, in particular for the protection of vehicle aerosurfaces exposed to high-flow environments of hot oxidizing gases, as is the case in hypersonic flights and atmospheric re-entry [1,2]. However, ZrB2 alone is very brittle and does not have sufficient toughness, strength, and thermal shock resistance to be used successfully for such aerospace applications [1,2]. Reducing this inherent brittleness has thus become a critical issue to ensure the mechanical integrity of ZrB2 UHTCs when they are in service. The strategy adopted to achieve this goal consists of engineering the microstructure of the ZrB2 UHTCs, normally by the addition of second phases [1-26] and sometimes also by the fabrication of laminated-type architectures with compressive residual stresses [27-29] or of complex cell-type architectures [30]. SiC is the typical second phase particle in ZrB2 [1-3], and its introduction improves the toughness because of the crack defection and crack-wake bridging in the weak ZrB2-SiC interfaces. The addition of SiC also enhances the strength of ZrB2 because SiC inhibits grain growth thus reducing the dominant initial flaw size. Other ceramic second phases such as MoSi2, ZrC, HfN, and TiN [1,2,4], or combinations of various second phases [1,2,4-7], that improve densification may also inhibit grain growth improving the strength and toughness of the material.

3.4.1. Hardness
At  the  nanoscale,  all  compositions  exhibit  comparable  hardness values. Consequently, bulk hardness was assessed using Vickers indentation, revealing lower values (24 -29 GPa) compared to that of nanoindentation. The microhardness of (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 at 4.9 N is 27.25 ± 1.8 GPa, falling in between that of (Zr,Ti,Ta)C (29.25 ± 1.5 GPa) and (Zr,Ti) B2  (24.34 ± 1.9 GPa). Variations in hardness at different scales can be attributed to the indenter size effect [41]. Nanoindentation, with a ~150 nm indenter  tip  and  low  loads  (e.g.  8  mN),  confines  indents  within grains (2 -3 μ m), thereby measuring the intrinsic hardness of a specific phase by minimizing the influence of grain boundaries, microporosity, and  defects  [36].  In  contrast,  microindentation  employs  a  larger indenter and higher load (e.g., 4.9 N), encompassing both grain interiors and boundaries, which introduces contributions from these heterogeneities,  resulting  in  lower  hardness  values  [42].  Furthermore,  the dual-phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 composite, having finer grains, lower porosity, and a 58 % carbide phase fraction, exhibits higher hardness than the boride (Zr,Ti)B2. Moreover, the Vickers hardness of all three specimens is more than that of their monolithic counterparts [43,44], because of the solid solution hardening. Even for dual phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C, B2  composites, hardness values (~27 GPa) are higher than the previously reported many dual phase composites i.e., ~24 GPa for (Hf, Nb, Ta, Ti, Zr) boride-carbide dual phase [11], ~23 and ~24 GPa for (Zr,Ta) C and (Hf,Zr,Ta)C [44], respectively, as shown in Fig. 7b.

3. Results and discussion
The hardness and toughness measured by Vickers testing were 17.6( ± 0.2) GPa and 3.0( ± 0.1) MPa m 0.5 for the ZrB2-ZrO2 composite, and 16.2( ± 0.2) GPa and 1.9( ± 0.1) MPa m 0.5 for the reference ZrB2 monolith. The increased toughness of the ZrB2-ZrO2 composite compared to the reference ZrB2 monolith, which has a grain size more than twice as large, highlights the fundamental role of ZrO2 in enhancing the ZrB2 toughness via crack bridging and its transformation toughening in accordance with previous observations [22-24]. Furthermore, despite ZrO2 being softer than ZrB2, the ZrB2-ZrO2 composite is harder than the reference ZrB2 monolith, in part because the former is fully dense whereas the latter is only ∼ 96% dense (see Figs. 2B and 3).

A B S T R A C T
In the recent years, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) based on transition metal carbides (TaC, ZrC, TiC) and diborides (ZrB2, TiB2) have emerged as promising materials for re-entry applications. A boride -carbide dualphase composite was designed in order to achieve good mechanical properties with a fine microstructure and strong interfacial bonding. Three compositions: (i) (Zr,Ti)B2, (ii) (Zr,Ti,Ta)C, and (iii) dual-phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 were synthesized using spark plasma sintering at 1850 ◦ C, 40 MPa for 30 min, achieving ~99 % densification. Phase analysis confirmed single-phase solid solutions in (Zr,Ti)B2  and (Zr,Ti,Ta)C, while the dual-phase (Zr,Ti, Ta)C,B2  composite comprised ~42 % boride and ~58 % carbide. Nanoindentation analysis (8 mN) showed a uniform hardness (~36 GPa) for all the compositions, whereas microindentation revealed intermediate hardness of ~27 GPa for the dual phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2, as compared to that of ~29 GPa for (Zr,Ti,Ta)C and ~24 GPa for (Zr,Ti)B2

A B S T R A C T
Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) is the predominant sintering technique for  achieving  dense  entropy-stabilized  UHTC.  The  first  high-entropy diboride  (HEB)  was  successfully  produced  using  SPS  at  2000 ◦ C  for 5 min, using transition metal diboride powders subjected to high-energy ball milling as starting materials [23]. Gild et al. [24] introduced a new approach named reactive flash Spark Plasma Sintering (reaFSPS), which combines rapid SPS and the flash sintering technique to quickly densify high-entropy UHTC. The utilization of reaFSPS enabled the achievement of  dense  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)B2 and  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)C ceramics,  which  contained  3  wt%  graphite  sintered  in  120  s.  While advanced sintering techniques can successfully yield dense high-entropy UHTC,  conventional  methods  often  involve  sintering  temperatures exceeding 1800 ◦ C,  leading  to  increased  energy  consumption and the development of a coarse microstructure [9].
Ultra-fast high-temperature sintering (UHS) was developed in 2020 by Wang et al. [25], where the sintering of a green body was achieved within seconds by placing a pellet between two Joule-heated carbon felts,  with  an  ultra-high  heating  rate  of  10 3 -10 4 ◦ C/min  achieved without the aid of external pressure. UHS has clear advantages over SPS, including a reduced processing time, ' light heating elements ' with low thermal capacity, simple experimental equipment, and the possibility of obtaining components with complex geometries [25 -27]. Due to the use of  thermally  insulating  graphite  heating  elements,  the  temperature within the felt can exceed 2500 ◦ C, thus enabling the densification of different refractory compounds  including SiC [28], Si3N4 [29], ZrB2-based  materials  [30,31],  WC  [32],  ZrC  [33] and  high-entropy carbides [34].

G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T
In the present work, the possibility to achieve nearly fully dense UHTCMCs by the rapid SPS technique, while preserving the /uniFB01 bres integrity, was investigated. Spark plasma sintering is a well-established consolidation technique that can pomote near full densi /uniFB01 cation of ceramics in short processing times of the order of minutes. This is especially true for conductive ceramics, such as borides and carbides, where the electrical currents have been demonstrated to signi /uniFB01 cantly enhance densi /uniFB01 cation by eliminating oxide phases like MxOy and B2O3 [27]. The inherent advantages of SPS processing are the rapid heating rate, which is up to 1000 °C/min in the case of rapid SPS [28]; [29]; [30] and up to 10,000 °C/min for Flash SPS technique [31]. Such high heating rates, besides contributing to a signi /uniFB01 cant energy saving during the consolidation process, also enable the possibility to produce metastable materials, which would not be possible to achieve using other sintering routes.
The production and microstructure evolution of ZrB2 + 8 vol% Si3N4 + 3 vol% SiC, materials reinforced with 45 vol% of high modulus pitch based chopped /uniFB01 bres is presented and correlated to the sintering temperature and time.

Spark Plasma Sintering
Spark plasma sintering (SPS) is a processing technique in which ceramic powders can be sintered very rapidly to full density. It is similar to HP and is carried out in  a  graphite  die/punch  assembly.  High-energy  plasma  can  be  generated  by  an instantaneous pulsed direct current applied through electrodes at the top and bottom of the die punch. Such plasma is expected to bring about phenomena that can enhance densification by particle surface activation, local melting and evaporation on the surface of the powder particle, so that heat transfer and mass transfer can be completed rapidly. In nearly all the investigations on SPS/field assisted sintering (FAS) indicated the higher rate of heating, lower sintering temperatures and shorter dwelling times compared to those attained in conventional sintering techniques (pressureless  sintering,  hot  pressing  etc).  Rapid  heating  rates  (hundreds  of  °C/ min) minimize coarsening and facilitate rapid densification, which leads to better thermo-mechanical properties. SPS has recently been applied to densify UHTCs based on ZrB 2 - and HfB 2 - with SiC/MoSi 2 /ZrC as secondary phases. Additions of 3wt% lanthanum yielded 99% RD (Kim et al., 2003). Mechanical alloyed Zr-B-C powder mixture resulted in 98% RD ZrB 2 -ZrC composite after exposure at 1800°C for 5 min (Tsuchida et al., 2004). Fast densification of HfB 2 and ZrB 2 based powder mixtures resulted in ~100% RD composites at 2100°C for 2 min (Bellosi et al . 2006) with a reduced grain size (compared to pressureless sintering) of ~1.4-2μm. Results of SPS studies on ZrB 2 - and HfB 2 - based ceramics are summarized in Table 4.

A B S T R A C T
The processing routes for fabricating HE-UHTCs, reported so far in literature are hot pressing (HP) [39,43,44,49,56-58], arc melting [26], spark plasma sintering (SPS) [7,13,18,22-25,28,33-38,45,59-61], free pressure-less  spark  plasma  sintering  (FP-SPS)  [51],  reactive  spark plasma  sintering  (R-SPS)  [30,50],  and  reactive  flash  spark  plasma sintering (RF-SPS) [17]. The first-ever report on HE-borides (Hf, Zr, Ta, M, Ti;  where  M  =  Nb,  Cr,  and  Mo)B2 as  a  new  class  of  UHTC  was published  in  2016  [13]  while  on  HE-carbides  (Hf,  Ta,  Zr,  M;  where M = Ti and Nb)C in 2018 [33] via the combinational approach of ballmilling and SPS of procured powders. An emergence of this new field can be seen from the jump in the increased number of publications in the last four years, as shown in Fig. 1.

Production of UHTC Complex Shapes and Architectures
As a matter of fact the most straightforward method to produce high strength dense UHTCs is through the use of pressure assisted techniques ,  most often in combination with sintering additives to reduce the processing temperature as much as possible (1900°C or lower).
Hot pressing (HP) is historically the most common pressure assisted sintering technique for advanced ceramics. ZrB 2 ceramics and ZrB 2 based composites with final density in the range 70-100% have been realized by HP using different starting particle size, additives and sintering temperature in the range 1600-2000 °C, sintering times from 5 to 120 min and pressures from 20 to 60 MPa (Guo, 2009).
Recently, spark plasma sintering (SPS) has  become a  routine  technique  to  densify advanced ceramics. A pulsed electric direct current is applied contemporary to a unidirectional mechanical pressure in a graphite die. Heating up is mainly due to the Joule effect when electrically conductive materials are processed. The effectiveness of SPS in the densification of refractory borides is highlighted by the processing time, which is 10 times lower than for HP (Medri et al., 2005), and allows to obtain final relative density higher than 99% without the use of sintering aids (Bellosi et al., 2011). HfB 2 based materials processed by SPS (Bellosi et al., 2006; Sciti et al., 2008) retained their flexural strength up to 1500°C in air, with values over 800 MPa, thanks to absence of a thermally unstable grain boundary phase deriving from sintering aids. A B S T R A C T

However, when binary UHTC, i.e., TaxHf1-xC (where x = 0, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, and 1) was sintered at 1850 ◦ C, 60 MPa, for 5 min in vacuum using spark plasma sintering (SPS), a single solid solution is  obtained  [4].  SPS  is  an  excellent  sintering  technique  that  utilizes pulsed DC and uniaxial pressure to obtain dense UHTCs [5,6] and HEUHTCs [1,7] at a lower temperature and shorter durations.
The concept of entropy stabilization has also been applied to transition metal carbides (TMCs) such as TaC, HfC, TiC and NbC due to their mutual solubility. The strategy employed is to mix equimolar concentrations of four or more components of UHTCs to form a single solidsolution phase in which multiple metallic elements randomly occupy cation  positions.  This  indeed  increases  the  compositional  complexity and entropy of the obtained single solid-solution phase, which leads to exceptional hardness, fracture toughness, thermal stability, and compositional-dependent adjustable range of thermal conductivity [8] [2,9 -17]. The mutual solubility leading to a single solid-solution does not necessarily have ' high entropy ' for them to be called as HE-UHTCs [1,2,10,15]. Hence, the authors are in favor of referring to this class of UHTCs as multicomponent UHTCs (MC-UHTCs) rather than HE-UHTCs.

A B S T R A C T
According to first-principles calculations, the mechanical properties of Ta1-xHfxC (x = 0.2, 0.4, 0.5) improve as the Ta content increases due to enhanced bonding between Ta and C atoms. The negative mixing enthalpies indicate that all three solid solutions can be prepared as single-phase materials. Single-phase Ta1-xHfxC (x = 0.2,0.4,0.5) were prepared using Spark Plasma Sintering. Studies on oxidation reveal that Ta0.5Hf0.5C demonstrates the most superior oxidation resistance among the three compositions. During oxidation, forming a Ta2O5Hf6Ta2O17 layer is essential for improving oxidation resistance. The research indicates that balancing the ratio of Ta  and  Hf  is  crucial  for  achieving  optimal  mechanical  properties  and  oxidation  resistance  in  practical applications.
ceramics: carbothermal reduction [11,12], polymer precursor pyrolysis [13], and high-temperature solid solution of carbides [14,15]. The hightemperature solid solution process involves the direct solid-state solubilization reaction of TaC and HfC, which is simpler than the other two methods. Previous research indicates that temperatures above 2000 ◦ C are necessary to achieve the solid solubility of HfC and TaC due to their high melting points [10]. The process of achieving both solid solubility and dense sintering  is  quite  challenging.  The  spark  plasma  sintering (SPS)  process  has  recently  gained  widespread  attention  in  research. Using SPS to prepare Ta1-xHfxC ceramics can achieve both solid solution and  densification  simultaneously.  Therefore,  SPS  is  the  most  used method for fabricating Ta1-xHfxC solid solution ceramics. Omar CedillosBarraza [14] et al. prepared Ta1-xHfxC ceramics by SPS. They demonstrated that a homogeneous single-phase solid solution only forms when the temperature exceeds 2150 ◦ C.

1. Introduction
vast majority of transition metal carbides TMC (TM = Ti, Zr, Nb, Ta and Hf)  exhibit  exceptionally  high  melting  points  and  possess  analogous NaCl-type crystalline structures, facilitating the formation of continuous solid solutions [12]. Consequently, the requirement for ultra-high temperature sintering, exceeding 2000 ◦ C, to attain complete densification of UHTCs inevitably results in the formation of a homogeneous solid solution  phase.  In  that  case,  the  achievement  of  high-density  multi-component UHTC ceramics with heterogeneous solid solution poses a significant challenge. For example, HfC and TaC have attracted significant attention as they are the only two binary UHTCs with a melting point over 4000 K, which surpasses even the flame temperature produced by rocket propellant [3]. Cedillos-Barraza et al. [13] achieved single-phase Ta0.8Hf0.2C ceramics with a relative density of 95 % by spark plasma sintering (SPS) at 2450 ◦ C for 20 min. In contrast, an uneven solid solution could be obtained at 2050 ◦ C, but the relative density was only 77 %.
In  this  study,  the  heterogeneous  solid  solution  HfC-TaC-based ceramic  was  successfully  fabricated  through  SPS,  utilizing  an  ingenious powder screening method in conjunction with the introduction of HfO2 sintering additive. Based on a realistic two-dimensional model, the elastic stress field of HfC-TaC-HfO ₂ composite was constructed. Ductile-

Abstract
Ta0.8Hf0.2C ceramic has the highest melting point among the known materials (4000 ◦ C). Spark plasma sintering is a new route for consolidation of materials, specially ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which are difficult to be sintered at temperatures lower than 2000 ◦ C.
The purpose of this study is to consolidate Ta0.8Hf0.2C by spark plasma sintering at low temperature using MoSi2 and TaSi2 as sintering aid. In this regard, effect of different amounts of sintering aids and carbides ratio on densification behavior and mechanical properties of Ta1 -x Hf x C were investigated.
Fully consolidation of Ta0.8Hf0.2C was achieved in presence of 12 vol.% sintering aid after sintering at 1650 ◦ C for 5 min under 30 MPa. The first stage of sintering was due to plastic deformation of sintering aids particles and consequent rearrangement. The second stage was occurred via Ta1 -x Hf x C solid solution and liquid phase formation.

A B S T R A C T
Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) is the predominant sintering technique for  achieving  dense  entropy-stabilized  UHTC.  The  first  high-entropy diboride  (HEB)  was  successfully  produced  using  SPS  at  2000 ◦ C  for 5 min, using transition metal diboride powders subjected to high-energy ball milling as starting materials [23]. Gild et al. [24] introduced a new approach named reactive flash Spark Plasma Sintering (reaFSPS), which combines rapid SPS and the flash sintering technique to quickly densify high-entropy UHTC. The utilization of reaFSPS enabled the achievement of  dense  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)B2 and  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)C ceramics,  which  contained  3  wt%  graphite  sintered  in  120  s.  While advanced sintering techniques can successfully yield dense high-entropy UHTC,  conventional  methods  often  involve  sintering  temperatures exceeding 1800 ◦ C,  leading  to  increased  energy  consumption and the development of a coarse microstructure [9].
Ultra-fast high-temperature sintering (UHS) was developed in 2020 by Wang et al. [25], where the sintering of a green body was achieved within seconds by placing a pellet between two Joule-heated carbon felts,  with  an  ultra-high  heating  rate  of  10 3 -10 4 ◦ C/min  achieved without the aid of external pressure. UHS has clear advantages over SPS, including a reduced processing time, ' light heating elements ' with low thermal capacity, simple experimental equipment, and the possibility of obtaining components with complex geometries [25 -27]. Due to the use of  thermally  insulating  graphite  heating  elements,  the  temperature within the felt can exceed 2500 ◦ C, thus enabling the densification of different refractory compounds  including SiC [28], Si3N4 [29], ZrB2-based  materials  [30,31],  WC  [32],  ZrC  [33] and  high-entropy carbides [34].

The final composition was ~29 vol% HEB with an average grain size of ~1.1 µ m. Solid solution formation occurred at the densification temperature of 1900 ◦ C resulting in a relative density higher than 99%. The Vickers hardness was 26.5 ± 1.4 GPa at a load of 2 N. Dual-phase high-entropy boride-carbide ceramics were processed by spark plasma sintering of powders synthesized by boro/carbothermal reduction of oxides with average grain sizes of boride phase 2.7 ± 1.3 µ m and for carbide phase 1.6 ± 0.7 µ m by Feng et al. [25]. The Vickers hardness values increased from 25.2 ± 1.1 to 38.9 ± 2.5 GPa as the indentation load decreased from 9.8 to 0.49 N. During the last three years, the published results on the dual -phase HE ceramics illustrate a strong potential for the development of new HE ceramics with improved mechanical properties. These recently developed high entropy ceramics have been characterized in detail, mainly concerning their mechanical properties such as hardness, fracture toughness, strength, etc., and their wear characteristics [26 -29].

3.3. Hardness
Despite all compositions containing four metals instead of the typical five for so-called high-entropy UHTCs, the hardness values were comparable  to  those  reported  for  dual  phase  boride-carbide  ceramics  in other studies. As an example, Huo et al. [15] achieved a hardness of 28.4 GPa using a 9.8 N load for the (Hf,Nb,Ta,Ti,Zr)B2-(Hf,Nb,Ta,Ti,Zr) C composition. Similarly, Smith et al. [16] obtained a HV0.2 of 27 GPa, while Naughton-Duszova et al. [24] found a HV1  of 29.4 GPa. For the same composition, Hassan et al.  [21] obtained a  hardness  of  HV0.05 = 47.5 ± 4 GPa and HV1 = 29.3 ± 1.4 GPa. For comparison, the dual phase ceramics in the present study ranged from 22.7 GPa to 27.4 GPa at a load of 9.8 N. The highest hardness for a dual phase ceramic was reported to be 48.6 ± 2.2 GPa at a load of 0.49 N and ~26.6 GPa at a load of 9.8 N, in a composition containing small amounts of Mo and W [4]. These differences in hardness among the HEDP ceramics indicate the importance of metal selection in optimizing properties of compositionally complex ceramics.

A B S T R A C T
Qin et al. [23] synthesized high entropy dual phase boride-carbide ceramic, incorporating five (Hf, Zr, Ti, Ta, Nb)-based unique borides and five unique carbides, using SPS at 2200 ◦ C for 20 minutes with 1 % graphite  to  reduce  oxides.  Increasing  carbide  content  improved  the hardness of HEB-HEC systems from 19.4 ± 1.3 GPa to 25.3 ± 1.4 GPa, with the resulting dual phase being ~98.8 % dense. Smith et al. Furthermore, Luo et al. [11], also prepared (Zr, Ta,  Hf,  Ti,  Nb)-based  dual-phase  by  boro-carbothermal  reduction, resulting  in  a  fine-grained  microstructure  with  negligible  residual porosity. For the high-entropy ceramic (HEC), the measured fracture toughness and Vickers hardness were 3.04 ± 0.23 MPa.m 1/2 and 23.1 ± 0.3  GPa,  respectively,  whereas  for  the  dual  phase  composition  containing 40 % boride and 60 % carbide with 99.1 % relative density, the fracture toughness and hardness were reported to be 2.76 ± 0.06 MPa. m 1/2 and 23.6 ± 0.3 GPa, respectively. 4.5. Hardness
For HEC, the measured nanohardness values (obtained from nanoindentation) are significantly greater than the measured microhardness values (30-40 vs. 15-30 GPa). Our measured Vickers hardness (25.3 ± 1.4 GPa at 9.8 N and 26.8 ± 1.6 GPa at 1.96 N) is similar to that presented by Ye et al. [37] (18.8 ± 0.4 GPa at 9.8 N and 22.5 ± 0.3 GPa at 0.98 N). In addition, a lower value of 15 GPa at 9.8 N indent loading force was reported in Ref. [23]. Besides the different indent loading forces applied in different studies, the relatively low Vickers hardness can also be attributed to their low relative densities (95.3 % and 93 %, respectively, in comparison with the high relative density of ∼99 % in this study).
It has already been shown by several independent studies that the single-phase HEB [21,37,62] or HEC [22,24] is harder than the rule-ofmixture (RoM) average of those of the five individual components (binary borides or carbides). A new observation in this study is that the Vickers hardness of a dual-phase UHTC is further enhanced above the linear interpolation of two single-phase endmembers (HEB and HEC), which is indicated by the blue dash line in Fig. 9; on the other hand, hardness data taken at indent loading force of 1.96 N in Fig. S5 also confirms this phenomenon. A commentary here is that the grains in DPHE-UHTCs (4.2-11.9 μm in size) are substantially smaller than those of single-phase HEB and HEC counterparts (with size of 15.0 μm and 12.1 μm, respectively); with higher hardness being reported on finer grain ceramics [75,76], the actual mechanism of this enhancement on Vickers hardness for DPHE-UHTCs would require further study.

question: What multi-phase coating strategy for wide-temperature-range oxidation protection did Hu et al. (2019) design for C/SiC composites, and how does it contrast with the design goal of introducing Group V and VI metals into (M,Hf,Ti,Zr)B2-(M,Hf,Ti,Zr)C ceramics by Feltrin et al. (2025)?
answer:
Hu et al. (2019) designed a multi-phase coating with a multi-layer structure for C/SiC composites, consisting of a SiC bonding layer, a ZrB2-based middle layer, and a SiC outer layer [3]. This design aimed to achieve long-term oxidation protection in a wide temperature range of 1100 °C to 1500 °C by leveraging the synergistic effects of different ceramic phases.

In contrast, Feltrin et al. (2025) focused on introducing Group V and VI metals into (M,Hf,Ti,Zr)B2-(M,Hf,Ti,Zr)C ceramics to enhance their oxidation resistance. The goal was to improve the material's performance by modifying the chemical composition of the ceramics, which contrasts with the multi-layer structural approach used by Hu et al. [1].

1. Introduction
Carbon/carbon (C/C) composites have attracted increasing attention among the numerous candidate materials for thermal structural components  [1,2],  due  to  their  low  density,  high  specific  strength  and excellent thermal stability [3 -5]. However, C/C composites are prone to rapid oxidation at 370 ◦ C, 650 ◦ C and 750 ◦ C in air, water vapor and CO2 respectively [6], severely limiting their long-term application in aerobic environment [7,8]. Applying ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) coating is considered as an effective method to improve the ablation property of C/C composites [9] via the self-derived HfO2 or ZrO2-based oxide scales [10,11].
Up to now, the troublesome consensus is how to acquire the balance between porous HfO2/ZrO2 skeletons and their long-term oxygen barrier under high-temperature & strong-scouring conditions. Various singlecomponent or binary-component additives have been studied and reported, despite of insufficient improvement effects, including SiC [12, 13], TaC [8,14,15], NbC [16,17] and TiC [18]. The multiple combination of different transition metal elements (Me) to form multi-component (for example, HfC-ZrC-TaC [19], (Hf-Zr-Ta)B2  [20], (Hf-Ta-Nb)C [21] and (Hf-Zr-Ti)C [22]) and even high-entropy ceramic (HEC) protection systems [23 -30] are recently attention-grabbing and

A B S T R A C T

1. Introduction
Lightweight structural materials [1,2] for modern aerospace vehicles have attracted worldwide attention, especially for hot-end components which  require  low  density,  low  thermal  expansion  and  excellent high-temperature strength. Carbon/carbon (C/C) composites, as one of the most suitable candidate materials above 1650 ◦ C [3 -6], have been widely used in thermal protection systems for extreme temperatures. The most well-known shortcomings to limit the further applications are concentrated  on  their high-temperature  oxidation  sensitivity  and short-term ablation resistance. Generally, various strategies to construct a continuous protective layer on the surface [7 -9] or introducing antioxidant components into the internal of C/C composites [10 -13] have been  extensively  reported  for  oxidation  protection,  with  the  most representative  system  of  ultrahigh  temperature  ceramic  (UHTC)  for 1700 ◦ C and above. Up to now, a dual-layer surface design composed of SiC-based inner layer and UHTC outer layer was commonly adopted to jointly  serve  as  an  oxygen  barrier,  where  the  outer  UHTC  layer  was gradually  developed  into  binary,  ternary  and  even  more  diversified systems,  such  as  ZrB2-SiC  [14,15],  (Hf-Zr-Ta)C  [16],  (Hf-Zr-Ta-Ti)B2 [17], (Hf-Zr-Ti-Sc-Y-La)C [18] and so on. The key reason for elemental diversification in as-designed UHTC coatings is to obtain a service-derived compound oxide film with dense structure for long-duration  in  an  oxidizing  high-temperature  flow,  thus  used  as  a better substitute than porous ZrO2 or HfO2 films.

A B S T R A C T
[14] fabricated a SiC/Si-B-C/SiC threelayer coating by CVD and analyzed the oxidation behaviors of coated C/ SiC composites in a wide temperature range of 700 °C to 1400 °C in wet oxygen atmosphere. The above results show that the protective temperature range can be widened to some extent through the architecture of a multi-layer coating containing Si- and B- based ceramic. However, the operating time of these coatings reported in literature is relatively short in an elevated temperature range. In addition, the oxidation kinetics and protection mechanisms of coatings in a wide temperature range need to be further investigated.
In this work, an oxidation-resistant coating with multi-phase composition and multi-layer structure was designed and fabricated on C/ SiC composites to achieve long-term oxidation protection in a wide temperature range of 1100 °C -1500 °C. The coating contains a SiC bonding layer, a ZrB2-based middle layer and a SiC outer layer. The microstructure and oxidation behavior of the SiC/UHTC/SiC threelayer coating was investigated at 1100 -1500 °C temperature in stagnant air.

A B S T R A C T
At /uniFB01 rst, multi-phase composition of a coating is used to realize oxidation protection over a wide temperature range since a single candidate only plays a very limited role determined by the oxidation characteristics, melting point, viscosity, vapor pressure and oxygen di /uniFB00 usion coe /uniFB03 cient of the material and its oxide. At above ∼ 1650 °C, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), such as ZrC, ZrB2, HfC and HfB2, are considered as appropriate candidates due to the high melting points of their oxides [8,9]. However, the long -term oxidation protection in this temperature region faces great challenges since the glassy phase formed is di /uniFB03 cult to retain at ultra-high temperatures. At the temperatures from ∼ 1300 °C to ∼ 1650 °C, silicon-containing ceramics such as silicide (MoSi2, Cr3Si, CrSi2, etc.), silicate (Y x SiO y , etc.), mullite (3Al2O3·2SiO2) and silicon carbide (SiC) are the most promising candidates for long -term oxidation protection [10 -20] because silica or silicates with appropriate viscosity and low oxygen di /uniFB00 usion rates are produced. At temperatures below 1100 °C, however, boron-containing or phosphate-containing ceramic coatings are generally used to protect carbon /uniFB01 bers in the composites since they have excellent self-healing ability in this temperature range [21 -23]. However, single composition possesses very limited oxidation protection below or above their optimum usage temperatures. Many investigations have proved that a multi-phase coating has a better oxidation protection property over a wide temperature range than single composition through developing a synergic e /uniFB00 ect of all components [24 -26]. Li et al. [24,25] deposited oxidation protective coatings with di /uniFB00 erent compositions by the pack cementation process.

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high temperature ceramic coatings have been a focal point in recent  research  on  oxidation-resistant  coatings  for  C/C  composites. Among these, hafnium boride (HfB2) has gained significant attention as a high-temperature oxidation-resistant material due to its high thermal conductivity (104 W m -1  K GLYPH<0> 1 ), high melting point, and relatively low thermal expansion coefficient [6 -10].  Additionally,  researchers  often combine HfB2  with silicides in coatings to form a Hf-Si-O glass phase during  oxidation,  which  can  withstand  temperatures  up  to  1750 ◦ C, thereby  enhancing  the  overall  temperature  resistance  of  the  coating [11].  For  instance,  Zhu  et  al.  developed  an  HfB2-SiC  coating  on SiC-coated C/C composites using a slurry impregnation and pre-oxidation method. At 1500 ◦ C, the coating containing 30 wt% HfB2 exhibited a mass increase of 1.15 % after 460 h of oxidation. The primary oxidation resistance mechanism involved the diffusion of Hf cations into the SiO2 lattice, which suppressed the volatilization of the SiO2 glass phase and formed new, more stable chemical bonds [12]. Similarly, Lv et al. fabricated a SiC/HfB2-SiC coating using embedding and brush-coating sintering methods, which protected the C/C composite matrix in air at 1700 ◦ C for 100 h [13]. The formed SiO2/HfSiO4/HfO2 protective layer remained stable during prolonged oxidation, playing a crucial  role  in  preventing  oxygen  diffusion  inward.  These  studies

question: How does the crack propagation rate for Ta0.5Hf0.5C reported by Thomas et al. (2023) relate to the grain size refinement observed for the vanadium-containing HEDP-V composition reported by Feltrin et al. (2025)?
answer:
The crack propagation rate for Ta0.5Hf0.5C, as reported by Thomas et al. (2023), is significantly reduced compared to TaC and HfC, with a minimum crack propagation rate of 13 m/s, which is ~1400% and ~4000% lower than TaC and HfC UHTCs, respectively [1]. This reduction in crack propagation rate is attributed to the microstructural features of Ta0.5Hf0.5C, such as dislocation pile-ups, nano-twin formation, and intergrain twisting, which hinder crack propagation [1]. 

On the other hand, Feltrin et al. (2025) reported that the HEDP-V composition exhibits the finest grain sizes, suggesting that vanadium contributes to refining the microstructure through its lattice distortion effect [2]. Grain size refinement can influence crack propagation by increasing the number of grain boundaries, which can act as barriers to crack propagation, thereby enhancing the material's fracture toughness and resistance to crack propagation.

While the specific crack propagation rate for HEDP-V is not provided in the sources, the grain size refinement observed in HEDP-V compositions may similarly contribute to a reduction in crack propagation rates, akin to the effect observed in Ta0.5Hf0.5C. Both studies highlight the importance of microstructural refinement in improving the mechanical properties and crack resistance of ceramics.

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) based on the solid solution TaxHf1-xC have shown great promise as materials for extreme environments ( > 2500 ◦ C) due to their improved thermo-mechanical properties. However, their dynamic response under high-impact loading remains unknown. In this study, we investigate the dynamic impact behavior and fracturing evolution of TaxHf1-xC samples (with x = 0, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, and 1) at a strain rate > 10 3 s -1 using the Split Hopkinson Pressure Bar (SHPB) test. Among the different compositions, Ta0.5Hf0.5C exhibits the highest compressive strength of 1870 MPa, representing a 36% improvement over TaC and 54% over HfC. High frame rate videos and deformation analysis reveal that Ta0.5Hf0.5C, characterized by extensive Ta -Hf bonding, significantly reduces the crack propagation rate by approximately 1435% compared to TaC and 4050% compared to HfC. This effect is attributed to the dislocation pile-ups, nano-twin formation, inter grain twisting exhibited mostly along {111} plane as Hf substitutes Ta in TaxHf1-xC samples. Further, the surface energy is least for {111} plane eliciting the higher probability for bypassing crack propagation along this plane in TaxHf1-xC. The exceptional damage tolerance and improved mechanical properties of Ta0.5Hf0.5C, compared to other solidsolution TaxHf1-xC compositions, make it an extraordinary structural material for hypersonic applications. These findings provide valuable insights into the dynamic behavior of UHTCs and highlight the potential of Ta0.5Hf0.5C as a superior material for extreme environments requiring high-impact resistance.

Table 5
The grain sizes were also influenced by composition. Grain sizes are reported for both a and c directions in the main boride phase of HEDP-Cr and the third phase of the HEDP-Mo ceramic because of the elongated grain morphology. Besides the HEDP-Cr composition, grain sizes were comparable with the previous reported HEDP ceramic (HfNbTaTiZr)B2(HfNbTaTiZr)C, with grain sizes ranging from 0.9 to 2.3 μ m [17]. The finest grain sizes were observed in the HEDP-V and -Nb compositions, suggesting that these elements contribute to refining the microstructure. Previously, V has been described to enhance grain size refinement due to its  lattice  distortion  effect  [32].  In  contrast,  the  HEDP-Cr  ceramic exhibited the largest grain size. This was likely due to the lower melting points of Cr borides and carbides compared to the other elements from Groups V and VI. Moreover, as previously mentioned, the formation of a liquid phase for this composition may have facilitated increased metal migration, promoting grain growth.

3.2. Sintering behavior of Ta1-xHfxC solid solutions
Fig. 9 shows the EBSD of the three solid solutions. Fig. 9(a), (c), and (e) represent the crystal orientation distribution maps of the three solid solutions. It can be observed that the sintered samples are dense with uniform grain sizes. The absence of any preferred grain orientation in the (001) plane during grain growth under applied pressure suggests that there has been no significant development of texture [32,36]. Fig. 9 (b), (d), and (f) show the grain size distribution of the three solid solutions. The distribution of grain sizes in the three solid solutions is relatively concentrated. The particle size distribution range of Ta0.5Hf0.5C is the narrowest. The D(50) of Ta0.5Hf0.5C solid solution is the smallest (1.36 μ m), while the D(50) of Ta0.6Hf0.4C solid solution is the largest (2.93 μ m). Because the vacancy activation energy of TaC (3.5 eV) is lower than that of HfC (9.3 eV), a larger diffusion barrier needs to be overcome  when  the  content  of  HfC  increases  [37].  Under  the  same sintering  conditions,  Ta0.5Hf0.5C  exhibits  a  reduced  diffusion  rate, decreasing material transfer between grains, thereby slowing down the grain growth rate.

A B S T R A C T
In order to develop novel MC-UHTCs, it is imperative to understand the  fracture  behavior  under  bulk  mechanical  testing  such  as  4-point bend tests. For over a decade, Strassburger et al. [23,24] studied the damage mechanics in both opaque and transparent ceramics with the help of a high-speed camera. One particularly notable result of these studies is that cracking/damage events in ceramics occur on extremely rapid timescales, typically within the range of hundreds of nanoseconds to just a few microseconds. To the best of the authors ' knowledge, the real-time  in-situ  deformation  behavior  of  MC-UHTCs  has  never  been reported. Authors have recently studied the high strain rate mechanical performance of binary UHTCs (solid-solution of TaxHf1-xC, x = 0, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, and 1) using Split-Hopkinson Pressure Bar (SHPB) test [25]. The  deformation  analysis  using  high-speed  videos  revealed  that  the crack propagation rate Ta0.5Hf0.5C sample showed resistance to crack propagation with a minimum crack propagation rate of 13 m/s which is
~1400 % and ~ 4000 % lower than TaC and HfC UHTCs, respectively. Inspired by this study [25], the present work explores elusive real-time, in-situ , quantitative failure events during the 4-point bend testing of MCUHTCs. The deformation and fracture in the MC-UHTCs are discussed in terms of crack nucleation and propagation rate and correlated to multiscale microstructure using transmission electron microscopy (TEM).

answer: The sources provided do not mention Hu et al. (2019) or Feltrin et al. (2025), and therefore, the specific deposition method used by Hu et al. (2019) to apply the SiC bonding layer onto C/SiC composites, as well as the specific differences between this method and the boro/carbothermal reduction synthesis route used by Feltrin et al. (2025), cannot be determined from the given information.

A B S T R A C T
Up to now, a lot of methods have been developed to prepare UHTC on the surface of C/C composites, such as chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [29], atmospheric plasma spraying (APS) [13], pack cementation (PC) [30], and supersonic atmospheric plasma spraying (SAPS) [31]. Among this method, APS is the mostly used and economic method for coating preparation [32]. It was widely applied on the coating preparation of gas turbine, such as thermal barrier coatings (TBCs) [19] and environmental barrier coatings (EBCs) [32]. In addition, a coating with higher density and higher bond strength can be obtained by the method of APS as a result of good melting of powders in the plasma flame. A strong interface bonding between SiC layer and C/C composites by the method PC due to the reaction between C and Si [4]. In our previous work, two-layer SiC/ZrB2 -SiC -Si and SiC/ZrB2 -SiC -Al2O3  coating was successfully  prepared  by  the  PC  method  combined  with  APS,  which provided  execllent  oxidation  protection  for  C/C  composites  [13]. Furthermore,  the  ablation  behavior  of  SiC -Al2O3/ZrB2 -SiC -Si  and SiC -Al2O3/ZrB2 -SiC -Al2O3  coatings was also tested. Results indicated this coating can provide excellent ablation resistance for C/C composites [23].

A B S T R A C T
A  very  promising  group  of  these  advanced  materials  that  were developed as potential systems for ultra-high temperature applications is  the  so-called  dual-phase  high-entropy  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics (DPHE-UHTCs).  As the first, Qin et al. [21] processed DPHE-UHTCs from  binary  borides  and  binary  carbide  powders  with relative densities higher than 99% consisting of a hexagonal HEB phase and a cubic HEC phase. The thermodynamic relation that governs the compositions  of  the  boride  and  carbide  phases  in  equilibrium  was described and a thermodynamic model was proposed. The hardness of the prepared DPHE-UHTCs was higher than the weighted linear average of the two single-phase high-entropy UHTCs, which are already harder than the RoM averages of individual binary carbides and borides. The results proved that the microstructure parameters and mechanical and tribological properties of DPHE-UHTCs can be designed by changing the phase  fraction  of  the  HEB  and  HEC  phases.  Luo  et  al.  [22]  recently developed fine-grained dual-phase, high-entropy ceramics (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)B2-(Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)C  with  different phase  ratios  using  powders  synthesized  via  a  boro/carbothermal reduction. High-density, homogeneously distributed DPHE-UHTCs, with different  boride-to-carbide  phase  ratios,  were  prepared  by  SPS  processing at 2000 ℃ for 10 min under 30 MPa applied load. They developed systems with very fine microstructure with average grain size from 0.5 µ m to 1.5 µ m, and the system with 18 vol% boride phase exhibited the highest Vickers hardness and fracture toughness with values 24.2 ± 0.3 GPa and 3.19 ± 0.24 MPa ⋅ m 0.5 ,  respectively. A new reactive sintering  route  was  recently  applied  by  Huo  et  al.  [23]  to  process dual-phase high-entropy ceramics using ZrB2, NbB2, HfB2, TaB2, and TiC as  starting  materials.  Very  high  relative  density  was

[14], offer limited control over pore architecture and microstructural uniformity. Soto et al. prepared porous SiC via sacrificial templating, but achieved  only  poorly  distributed  porosity  and  a  low  compressive strength  ( ≤ 30 MPa),  with  fracture  propagation  driven  by  stress  concentration at randomly distributed pores [10]. Increased porosity typically induces an exponential decrease in strength due to the reduction in load-bearing cross-sectional area and accelerated microcrack growth. Furthermore,  high-temperature  sintering  of  SiC  often  triggers β to α phase transformation, abnormal grain coarsening, and the loss of grain boundary  strengthening, collectively intensifying brittleness [11]. Similarly, replica templating has yielded TiN foams with 90 % porosity but only ~0.75 MPa compressive strength [12], while direct foaming has  produced  Si3N4  foams  (90.8 -96.4 %  porosity)  with  strengths  of merely 0.37 -5.53 MPa [13]. Moreover, the intrinsic sintering resistance and tendency for abnormal grain coarsening in UHTC powders further exacerbate the difficulty of achieving dense, defect-free ceramics with desired  pore  structures  [15 -17]. In-situ boro/carbothermal  reduction sintering offers improved densification kinetics through reaction-driven phase evolution, as demonstrated by Wang et al. in fabricating porous ZrC-SiC  [18]. Nevertheless, it remains challenging to fabricate components with complex external shapes and designed internal architectures using this method alone.

In  this  regard,  numerous  strategies  for  synthesizing  ceramic  powders  have  been reported. Techniques such as sol-gel [9, 10] and co-precipitation are commonly employed for precursor preparation, while the final powders are typically synthesized via methods including boro/carbothermal reduction, borothermal reduction [11, 12], and selfpropagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) [13]. question: How does the melting point cited for the ZrO2 oxidation product in Hu et al. (2019) compare to the melting point reported for Ta0.2Hf0.8C by Thomas et al. (2023)?
answer: The sources provided do not mention the melting point of ZrO2 as reported by Hu et al. (2019) or the melting point of Ta0.2Hf0.8C as reported by Thomas et al. (2023). Therefore, a comparison cannot be made based on the information given.

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
Cubic tantalum carbide (TaC) and hafnium carbide (HfC) belong to the group of ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) owing to high melting points (TaC: 3950 ◦ C; HfC: 3928 ◦ C), high density (TaC: 14.3 g cm -3 ; HfC: 12.2 g cm -3 ), high elastic modulus (TaC: 537 GPa; HfC: 461 GPa),  high  hardness  (TaC  18.9  GPa;  HfC  22.1  GPa)  along  with  low fracture toughness [1 -5]. Moreover, they have excellent tolerance to chemical attack, thermal shock, and oxidation. Another characteristic of TaC and HfC is their unlimited mutual solubility [6]. Most importantly, the solid solution has been shown  to exhibit enhancement  in thermo-mechanical  behavior.  In  particular,  a  record  highest  melting temperature of 4215 ◦ C is reported for Ta0.2Hf0.8C in the solid solution of Ta x Hf1x C  ( x = 0,  0.2,  0.5,  0.8  and  1)  [7 -9].  The  production  of single-phase solid-solution of (Ta, Hf)C is intricate as it is a function of sintering time and temperature to complete the diffusion process. For example, Ghaffari et al. [10] failed to achieve a complete solid solution
* Corresponding author. E-mail address: agarwala@fiu.edu (A. Agarwal).

quickly [17 -19].
The  dense  monolithic  SiC/(Hf1-xTax)C  nanocomposites  with  (Hf1xTax)C nanograin dispersed in SiC matrix have been proved to possess an outstanding high-temperature stability [20]. Wen et al. [16] found that the addiction of Ta element can enhance the sintering ability of the oxide scale at the medium-high temperatures, thereby significantly improving the oxidation resistance of SiC/(Hf1 GLYPH<0> xTax)C. Zhang et al. [21] discovered that an increase in HfC content tends to reduce the intergranular bonding of Ta-Hf-C, allowing oxygen to preferentially diffuse along the weak grain boundaries. Moreover, at low temperatures, the oxidation of HfO2 is loose, which inhibits densification and thus reduces its oxidation resistance.  Therefore,  it  seems  that  less  Hf  is  better  (or  more  Ta). Ta4HfC5, (i.e., (Hf0.2Ta0.8)C, have the highest melting point of 4215 K) [22], suggesting its application in ultra-high-temperature thermal protection. In addition, few studies have been reported on the adjustment of UHTC  composites to ' anomalous ' oxidation [14,15,23] at the medium-high temperatures (i.e., Kp1200 ◦ C > Kp1300 ◦ C > Kp1400 ◦ C > Kp1500 ◦ C > Kp1600 ◦ C). This is due to the fact that as the temperature increases, SiC and TaC are activated to produce more low-melting-point phases, favoring the formation of a denser oxide scale. Lin et al. [24]

INTRODUCTION
With laser pulse heating (heating time about 1 s), the  authors  of  [4]  found  that  the  congruent  melting temperature of TaC Tm = 4277 ± 12 K is higher than the melting point of HfC Tm =  4250  ±  20  K.  From other studies [5-7] with rapid microsecond heating, it follows  that  the  most  refractory  is  mixed  carbide Ta0.8 Hf0.2 C with a melting point 4300 ± 80 K [7]. For HfC and TaC in [5, 6], the literature data on the radiative characteristics at temperatures below the melting point  were  used.  A  consequence  of  this  was  an increased  temperature  measurement  error,  approximately equal to the difference in the melting temperatures of the mentioned carbides.
In theoretical study [8], the question of substances with maximum melting temperatures was considered and it was predicted that the maximum melting temperature, exceeding all known ones, will be that of hafnium carbonitride HfC0.51 N0.38 . Review [9] provides a detailed analysis and comparison of the properties of HfC, HfN, and Hf(C,N); in accordance with [8], a conclusion is drawn that the carbonitride HfC 0.51 N0.38 may have a record high melting point 4321-4445 K. In stationary experiment [10], HfC carbide and hafnium  carbonitride  HfC 0.5 N0.35 were  heated  under identical  conditions,  which  yielded  the  melting  of HfC,  while  hafnium  carbonitride  remained  in  an unmelted state.
In this article, the physical properties of carbonitride HfC0.51 N0.32 are studied for the first time based on a fast pulse experiment (below, this carbonitride is denoted Hf(C,N)) at high temperatures in solid and liquid  states,  including  the  melting  region,  and  its melting point is estimated.

3.4. The oxidation mechanism of Ta1-xHfxC
The ln [ g ( α ) / T 2 ] GLYPH<0> 1 / T  curve  is  plotted  in  Fig.  14.  It  has  been observed that Ta0.5Hf0.5C adheres to the GB model throughout the entire oxidation stage, with a R 2 value of 0.987. In the case of Ta0.6Hf0.4C and Ta0.8Hf0.2C, the GB model is only followed during the initial stages of oxidation (for Ta0.6Hf0.4C when α < 40%, and for Ta0.8Hf0.2C when α < 30%). The apparent activation energies calculated from the slopes are 377.16  KJ/mol  for  Ta0.8Hf0.2C,  178.40  KJ/mol  for  Ta0.6Hf0.4C,  and 111.30 KJ/mol for Ta0.5Hf0.5C. The lower apparent activation energy exhibited by Ta0.5Hf0.5C during the initial oxidation period explains its earlier onset and faster oxidation rate observed in the TG curves.
The  main  constituents  of  the  oxide  layer  for  Ta0.5Hf0.5C  are Hf6Ta2O17 and Ta2O5. The high melting point of Hf6Ta2O17 enables it to provide structural support to the oxide layer. Hf6Ta2O17  has a low oxygen  diffusion  coefficient,  which  gives  it  excellent  oxygen  barrier properties [48]. According to the report of Ref [49], the hyper-eutectic phase made up of Ta2O5 and Hf6Ta2O17 creates a dense oxide layer that effectively  hinders  the  diffusion  of  oxygen  across  grain  boundaries. Within  the experimental  temperature  range,  the  oxide  layer  on Ta0.5Hf0.5C is highly dense and effectively prevents oxygen diffusion. The  diffusion  of  oxygen  across  the  grain  boundaries  and  within  the developing oxide scale is the rate-limiting step in this oxidation stage [49,50].

3. Results and discussion
The reaction products formed after the high temperature testing were characterised using XRD as shown in Fig. 10 and the results revealed that the surface layer contained only monocliniczirconia (m-ZrO2) for all the ZrB2 containing UHTC composites and monoclinic hafnia (m-HfO2) for the HfB2 and HfC containing composites. Of the various reaction products mentioned in the reaction schemes, ZrO2 and HfO2 are stable at >2500 ◦ C. B2O3 has a very low melting point of 450 ◦ C and high vapour pressure and hence it will have quickly vaporised at temperatures above 1100 ◦ C. 13 This will have generated porosity and have accelerated the oxidation process above this temperature. Addition of SiC can significantly improve the oxidation resistance in the intermediate temperature range from 1200 to ∼ 1700 ◦ C by forming an SiO2 scale which reduces the oxygen diffusivity, 14 but no SiO2 peaks were observed in the XRD. Above ∼ 1500 ◦ C, SiC undergoes active oxidation leading to the formation of SiO and CO without forming a protective silica layer and hence it does not offer any additional protection. 15 Furthermore, the formation of water molecules as a result of the burning of the acetylene gas can reduce the stability of any SiO2 that does form by generating Si(OH)4, SiO(OH)2 and/or SiO(OH). 16 Addition of LaB6 to ZrB2 is reported to stabilise the ZrO2, formed as a result of the oxidation of ZrB2, in the tetragonal phase. The formation of La2Zr2O7 pyrochlore, which has a high melting point (>2300 ◦ C), has also been reported. 16 From the oxidation testing it was found that the addition of LaB6 actually reduced the high temperature performance of the UHTC composites and XRD analysis did not detect the presence of any La2Zr2O7.

in such systems.
To date, two thermodynamic models have been proposed to describe metal distribution between the carbide and boride in dual-phase highentropy UHTCs. Guo et al. [5] synthesized high-entropy diborides and high-entropy  diboride-carbides  using  ultra-fast  high-temperature  sintering (UHS) and suggested that the difference in formation energies of the transition metal diborides and carbides ( E MB2 f GLYPH<0> E MC f ) follows the order of Cr < V < Hf < Mo < Ti < Zr < Nb < Ta. This energy difference influenced  the  selective  incorporation  of  transition  metals  into  the high-entropy boride or the high-entropy carbide phases. The authors proposed that larger differences in formation energies facilitated metal dissolution  into  the  carbide  phase.  However,  they  noticed  that  this model did not accurately predict Hf segregation. Qin et al. [1] used the same approach and obtained only a moderate correlation, with an R 2 value of 0.816. Subsequently, Smith et al. [13] proposed a model based on  the  standard  Gibbs  Free  energies  of  formation  to  explain  metal segregation. Their findings indicated that the tendency for segregation to the boride phase follows the order: Cr < Ti < Zr < Nb < Mo < V < Hf < Ta < W, which was different from Qin ' s model. Notably, none of the existing models account for the possibility of the formation of a third phase in addition to the desired boride and carbide phases.

3.2. Metal distribution
[34 -36]. Specifically, VC0.8  has been identified as the most thermodynamically stable vanadium carbide composition [37]. The virtual crystal approximation was used to model the carbon vacancies and the calculations  qualitatively  demonstrated  that  vanadium  strongly  favored segregation to the boride phase when VC was carbon deficient (i.e., VCx where x is less than 1), which might be the case for the carbide formed in the HEDP-V. If the VC were fully stoichiometric (i.e., x = 1), vanadium segregation  to  the  carbide  may  have  been  favored.  Results  from  the present  study  reveal  that  first-principles  simulations  can  predict  the metal distributions based solely on thermodynamics, but complex interactions that also dictate phase distribution of the metals need further investigation.
The heat map analysis of the HEDP-Nb and -Ta compositions also exhibited  variation  in  intensity,  but  the  segregation  order  remained consistent across both compositions: Ti < Zr < Nb < Hf and Ti < Zr < Hf < Ta from boride to carbide. As an example, for HEDP-Ta, the boride/ carbide  ratios  were  Ti < Zr < Hf < Ta,  experimentally,  these  values were 2.35 > 0.94 > 0.67 > 0.61, while theoretically, they were 8.25 > 2.84 > 0.44 > 0.07. This discrepancy arises because thermodynamic calculations did not predict the absolute concentration of each metal  in  each  phase  [13],  as  it  is  dependent  on  their  ratio  between boride and carbide phases but rather indicated the relative favorability of metal segregation between phases.

A B S T R A C T
Dual-phase  compositionally  complex  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  were  formulated  by  incorporating different Groups V and VI metals such as V, Nb, Ta, Cr, Mo, or W into a base composition containing the Group IV elements, Hf, Ti, and Zr. Metal distribution was predicted using first-principles-based thermodynamics simulations  and  compared  with  experimental  results.  Moreover,  phase  stability,  microstructure,  and  mechanical properties were evaluated for all of the ceramics. Compositions containing Cr, V, Nb, or Ta formed dual-phase ceramics containing only one boride and one carbide phase, while compositions containing Mo or W developed an additional third phase. The experimental metal distribution trends generally aligned with thermodynamic predictions, except for compositions containing V, which showed unexpected segregation behavior that was influenced by complex interactions of the coexistence of boride and carbide structures. From the dual-phase ceramics, the composition containing V exhibited the highest hardness (HV1 = 25.5 ± 0.6 GPa) combined with smaller grain sizes (0.99 ± 0.33 μ m for the boride and 1.15 ± 0.31 μ m for the carbide phases). Our findings provide insights into phase formation and elemental segregation and help the design of next-generation dualphase UHTCs with tailored properties.

synthesized dual-phase (Cr,Hf,Nb,Ta,Ti,Zr)B2-(Cr,Hf,Nb,Ta,Ti,Zr)C ceramics having carbide 14.5 wt% with the average grain size 2.7 ± 1.3 μ m for boride phase and 1.6 ± 0.7 μ m for the carbide phase and having hardness 25.2 ± 1.1 GPa for load of 9.81 N. Smith II et al. [26] synthesized dual-phase ceramics with Hf, Ti, Ta, Nb, and Zr using reduction methods, observing preferential metal segregation: Boride phase is dominated by Nb and Ti, while  carbide  phase  was  enriched  in  Ta  and  Hf.  Additionally,  the nanohardness of the boride phase (~30 GPa) exceeds that of the carbide phase (~22 GPa).
A thermodynamic analysis by Smith et al. [27] on metal segregation in  dual-phase boride -carbide systems predicted that Ti preferentially segregates into the boride phase, while Ta tends to segregate into the carbide phase, based on Gibbs free energy considerations. Micro and atomic scale investigation on dual phase boride carbide (Ti-Zr-Nb-Hf-Ta)C-(Ti-Zr-Nb-Hf-Ta)B2 done by Naughton-Duszov ´ a [28], revealed a uniform grain size (average ~0.97 μ m) with compositions of (Ti0.38Zr0.18Nb0.22Hf0.115Ta0.105)B2 and (Ti0.14Zr0.20Nb0.20Hf0.20Ta0. 26)C. The study reported the absence of microcracks and second-phase segregation at the grain boundaries. At the sub-nanometre scale, the grain boundaries and phase interfaces were sharp and clean, with only a thin gradient of ~1 -1.5 nm.

3.3. Hardness
Despite all compositions containing four metals instead of the typical five for so-called high-entropy UHTCs, the hardness values were comparable  to  those  reported  for  dual  phase  boride-carbide  ceramics  in other studies. As an example, Huo et al. [15] achieved a hardness of 28.4 GPa using a 9.8 N load for the (Hf,Nb,Ta,Ti,Zr)B2-(Hf,Nb,Ta,Ti,Zr) C composition. Similarly, Smith et al. [16] obtained a HV0.2 of 27 GPa, while Naughton-Duszova et al. [24] found a HV1  of 29.4 GPa. For the same composition, Hassan et al.  [21] obtained a  hardness  of  HV0.05 = 47.5 ± 4 GPa and HV1 = 29.3 ± 1.4 GPa. For comparison, the dual phase ceramics in the present study ranged from 22.7 GPa to 27.4 GPa at a load of 9.8 N. The highest hardness for a dual phase ceramic was reported to be 48.6 ± 2.2 GPa at a load of 0.49 N and ~26.6 GPa at a load of 9.8 N, in a composition containing small amounts of Mo and W [4]. These differences in hardness among the HEDP ceramics indicate the importance of metal selection in optimizing properties of compositionally complex ceramics.

answer: The densification percentage for T50H50 (Ta0.5Hf0.5C) reported by Thomas et al. (2023) is 98.2% [2]. In contrast, the HEDP-V dual-phase composition reported by Feltrin et al. 2. Experimental procedure
Commercially  available  TaC  ( > 99.7%  purity,  spherical  particles, ~0.36 μ m size, Inframat Advanced Materials LLC, CT, USA) and HfC ( > 99.5% purity, spherical particles, ~0.33 μ m size, Materion LLC, OH, USA)  powders  were  procured  and  used  as  a  starting  material.  Five samples with compositions: pure TaC, pure HfC, T0.2Hf0.8C (labeled as T20H80), T0.5Hf0.5C (labeled  as  T50H50),  and  T0.8Hf0.2C  (labeled  as T80H20) were prepared using ball-milling [7]. The samples were then sintered using spark plasma sintering (SPS, Model 104, Thermal Technologies,  CA,  USA)  at  a  maximum  temperature  of  1850 ◦ C,  applied pressure of 60 MPa for 10 min with a 100 ◦ C/min heating rate under vaccum. The obtained SPS samples were cylindrical in shape with a diameter of 20 mm and thickness of ~4 mm. The details of these compositions ' starting powders, balled-milling and sintering can be found elsewhere [7]. The mutual solubility of carbides was further confirmed by XRD analysis eliciting the complete solid-solution phase formation in T20H80, T50H50 and T80H20 samples post-SPS processing [7]. The densification  as  well  as  the  average  grain  size  of  the  SPS  processed samples are listed in Table 1 [7]. The XRD patterns reported elsewhere [7] for the SPS-processed TaC-HfC samples were subjected to "Rietveld" refinement using Profex (version 5.2.2) software to derive the lattice parameter  values  and  lattice  strain  in  the  samples.  The  software employed  the  fundamental  parameters  approach  for  the  Rietveld refinement during this process.

The next highest boride content was for HEDP-V, which had no detectable third phases. The HEDP-Ta ceramic had the highest carbide phase content, followed by HEDP-Nb. A B S T R A C T
Dual-phase  compositionally  complex  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  were  formulated  by  incorporating different Groups V and VI metals such as V, Nb, Ta, Cr, Mo, or W into a base composition containing the Group IV elements, Hf, Ti, and Zr. Metal distribution was predicted using first-principles-based thermodynamics simulations  and  compared  with  experimental  results.  Moreover,  phase  stability,  microstructure,  and  mechanical properties were evaluated for all of the ceramics. Compositions containing Cr, V, Nb, or Ta formed dual-phase ceramics containing only one boride and one carbide phase, while compositions containing Mo or W developed an additional third phase. The experimental metal distribution trends generally aligned with thermodynamic predictions, except for compositions containing V, which showed unexpected segregation behavior that was influenced by complex interactions of the coexistence of boride and carbide structures. From the dual-phase ceramics, the composition containing V exhibited the highest hardness (HV1 = 25.5 ± 0.6 GPa) combined with smaller grain sizes (0.99 ± 0.33 μ m for the boride and 1.15 ± 0.31 μ m for the carbide phases). Our findings provide insights into phase formation and elemental segregation and help the design of next-generation dualphase UHTCs with tailored properties.
dual-phase (HEDP) UHTCs, summarizing compositions that have been studied and key findings. Table 2 highlights the metals used in each study, revealing that while Hf, Nb, Ta, Ti, and Zr are commonly utilized, metals such as W, Cr, V, and Mo are incorporated less frequently. Filipovi ´ c  et  al.  [4] and Guo et al.  [5] reported  additional  phases  when adding significant amounts of W or Mo, though phase stability in these systems remains unknown.

3.1. Phase, microstructure, and composition analysis
Solid  solution  formation  started  during  the  reaction  process.  The HEDP-Cr, -V, and -Nb compositions exhibited complete metal dissolution into the carbide and boride phases during the initial reaction at 1660 ◦ C, as indicated by XRD (Fig. 1a). In contrast, the HEDP-Ta, -W and -Mo compositions displayed peak splitting in the boride and carbide phases after reaction at 1660 ◦ C, indicating that solid solution formation was  not  complete  after  reaction.  Moreover,  HEDP-W  also  exhibited peaks for a third phase that were consistent with a monoboride ( M B). The XRD results demonstrate that the synthesis conditions were sufficient for the boro/carbothermal reduction reactions to go to completion in all of the compositions, but not to promote full solid solution formation in all of the compositions.
After  sintering,  variations  in  phase  composition  were  observed among the HEDP materials. The HEDP-Cr, -V, -Ta, and -Nb compositions appeared to form dual phase ceramics, they contained individual boride and carbide phases with no impurity phases, whereas the HEDP-W and -Mo materials  exhibited  a  third  phase  (Fig.  1b).  Specifically,  a  third phase, M B, was formed in HEDP-W, while HEDP-Mo contained the M 8C5 phase.

chunks.text:
4.6.  Additive manufacturing technology, AM
To date, notable progress and breakthroughs have been achieved in the additive manufacturing of carbon fiber-reinforced ceramic matrix composites [143]. Kemp et al. [144] utilized direct ink writing (DIW) technology with a composite ink consisting of ZrB2  powders, SiC precursors,  and  SiC  short  fibers.  The  printing  was  conducted  at  room temperature, followed by pyrolysis at 1200 °C to fabricate SiCf/ZrB2-SiC composites. However, as the fiber content increased, issues such as fiber misalignment and material cracking arose due to gas release and matrix shrinkage during polymer curing and pyrolysis. Additionally, the pyrolyzed components often exhibited defects, limiting their mechanical performance. Li et al. [145] addressed these issues by employing DIW combined with ultrasound-assisted fiber separation. During the direct ink  writing  of  continuous  carbon  fiber/ceramic  composites,  ceramic slurry was impregnated into continuous carbon fiber bundles. A subsequent low-temperature hot-pressing process was used to enhance the robustness of the fabricated structures, indirectly resulting in Cf/ZrB₂SiC ceramic matrix composites (as shown in Fig. 23).
Despite these advancements, several challenges remain in the additive manufacturing of UHTC, such as the high brittleness of materials leading to crack formation and the control of thermal stresses during the  fabrication  process.  Future  research  should  focus  on  optimizing process  parameters,  developing  new  material  systems,  and  exploring the  integration  of  multiple  manufacturing  techniques.  AM  offers  a highly flexible and promising pathway for fabricating ceramic matrix composites,  especially  UHTC.  While  challenges  persist,  ongoing  research and technological innovations are expected to position AM as a pivotal technique in the manufacturing of high-temperature materials, driving  the  widespread  application  of  ceramic  matrix  composites  in aerospace, defense, and other critical fields.

[15]  fabricated Carbon fibre reinforced UHTC composites having various compositions including ZrB2, HfB2 base matrix through slurry impregnation and final densification with carbon through CVI technique. The final densities of the composites were in the range of 1.86 -2.11 ± 0.001 g/cm 3 and reported  that  Halfnium  (Hf)  based  UHTC  offered  superior  oxidation resistance [15]. Reactive melt infiltration (RMI) was also used in the development of UHTCMCs, in which carbon fibre performs were infiltrated wuth Zr or Hf based alloys and facilitated in-situ formation of UHTCM phases [16 -18]. Luo et al. [17] fabricated C -SiC-HfC composite by melt infiltration of HfSi2  alloy in to the porous preform of Cf-SiC composite at 1600 -1700 ◦ C. The reported density and porosity of the composite  were  2.60 ± 0.02  g/cm 3 and  17.4 ± 0.1%,  respectively [16 -18]. Very recently, slurry infiltration followed by pressure assisted sintering (hot pressing and spark plasma sintering) was adopted for the development of UHTCMCs [19 -22]. Sciti et al. [19] fabricated carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2 base UHTCMCs by vacuum-bag infiltration and hot pressing, using 1D fabric carbon preforms, having various fiber volume fractions  from  55  to  70  %.  The  reported  bulk  density,  porosity  and flexural strength of these composites are in the range of 2.4 -3.1 g/cm 3 , 16 -28%  and  40 -95  MPa  respectively  [19].  Vinci  et  al.  studied  the oxidation behaviour of carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2 -SiC composites by thermogravimetric  analysis  up  to  1550 ◦ C  and  reported  that  of 800 -1000 ◦ C is  critical  temperature  range,  where  rapid  oxidation
