Kemp a , Abel A. Diaz b , Elizabeth C. Malek c,d , Brendan P. Croom c,1 , Zlatomir D. Apostolov c , Surya R. Kalidindi b , Brett G. Compton a , Lisa M. Rueschhoff c,* a University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN, USA b Georgia Institute of Technology, USA c Air Force Research Laboratory, USA d Southwestern Ohio Council for Higher Education, USA A R T I C L E  I N F O Keywords: Ultra-high temperature ceramics Direct ink writing Preceramic polymer Zirconium diboride Silicon carbide

KEYWORDS
hot pressing, infiltration, mechanical properties, slurry
of ceramics and makes them damage-tolerant. 5 Damagetolerant continuous fiber-reinforced ceramic matrix composites (CFCMCs), such as C/C, C/SiC, and SiC/SiC, have found applications as high-temperature structural components. 6-9 Continuous fiber-reinforced ZrB2 -based UHTC matrix composites (UHTCMCs) are being developed in the same line as CFCMCs. UHTCMCs have been also processed in the same way as conventional ceramic matrix composites (CMCs). 10,11 CMCsaremostlyproduced by using four different techniques: polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP), reactive melt infiltration (RMI), chemical vapor infiltration (CVI), and the ceramic route. 6 UHTCMC early development is mostly based on a hybrid process (a combination of two processes). For example, Tang et al. 12 and Wang et al. 13 developed Cf -ZrB 2-SiC composite using a hybrid process that included slurry infiltration followed by CVI. A Cf-ZrB2-SiC composite was also fabricated using a hybrid process that included PIP and slurry impregnation. 14-16 These are incremental advancements of a C f -SiC composite with ZrB 2 added via slurry impregnation. The RMI process is also wieldy used for the fabrication of UHTCMCs. 17-19 After adapting the ceramic route of processing of CMC, substantial progress in the processing of UHTCMCs has been made. The ceramic route consists of a slurry infiltration followed by densification through high-temperature sintering (both pressureless and pressure-assisted). The classic examples of using ceramic route processed composites are oxide-oxide CMCs 20 and nano-infiltration and transient eutectoid process for SiC-based CMCs. 21 Recently, huge developments for the processing of UHTCMCs have been carried out worldwide using a similar ceramic route. 22-27 However, in these studies, unidirectional carbon fabric were used.

4. Conclusions
In this paper, carbon fibre reinforced ZrB2-based composites were fabricated by powder slurry infiltration and a hybrid process coupling Polymer Infiltration and Pyrolysis (PIP) with Hot Pressing (HP) techniques. Two different routes for the consolidation of the materials were carried out: (a), a pre-sintering cycle by HP at 1850 ◦ C followed by two cycles of PIP at 1000 ◦ C, and vice versa, (b), two cycles of PIP at mild conditions (1000 ◦ C) followed by HP at 1900 ◦ C for 15 min.
This study led to the conclusions that sample PHP obtained by route (b) is the best viable solution between the two ways studied.
Cf/ZrB2-SiC with Cf content ~34 % (0/90 ◦ architecture), ZrB2 content ~32 % and SiC content ~22 % were achieved. Moreover, improved mechanical properties at elevated temperature of 1500 ◦ C (strength up to 590 MPa and fracture toughness of 7.4 MPa m 0.5 ) and lower number of  treatment needed respect of the conventional time-consuming PIP process were found.

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
Ti2AlC is a typical example of atomically layered ternary carbides belonging to the so-called MAX phase family. 1 Recently, it has attracted scientific and commercial attention due to its excellent isothermal and thermal cycling oxidation resistance in combination with its high electrical conductivity, resistance against thermal shock and strengths at high temperatures. 2-6 The low oxidation rate is caused by the formation of a continuous protective 𝛂 -Al2O3 layer on the Ti2AlC substrate, and the superior spallation resistance of the 𝛂 -Al2O3 layer is attributed to the well-matched thermal expansion coefficients between 𝛂 -Al2O3 layer and Ti2AlC substrate. 5,6 Based on this, it is likely that Ti2AlC may withstand ultra-high temperatures for relatively short times since Ti2AlC will decompose into sub-stoichiometry Ti2Al x CoreventwinnedTiC0.5 superstructureandAlratherthan becoming a liquid phase itself as long as the temperature does not exceed the melting point ( ∼ 3250 ◦ C) of TiC x ( x =0.5-1). 7-9 Therefore, Ti2AlC is probably a promising candidate for thermal structural components exposed to ultra-high temperature ablative environments such as nose caps of missiles, rudders and nozzles of rocket engines, gas burners and heat shields in nuclear power stations and other high temperature components, where ablation is a critical material behavior aspect. Hence it is relevant to investigate the ablation properties of Ti2AlC in ultra-high temperature environments, which have not been scrutinized before. In this paper, the ultra-high temperature ablation behavior of Ti2AlC ceramics using a conventional oxyacetylene flame is presented.
∗ Corresponding author. Tel.: +31 15 2782480; fax: +31 15 2786730. E-mail address: g.song@tudelft.nl (G.M. Song).

Abstract
The linear and mass ablation rates of Ti2AlC ceramics under an oxyacetylene flame at a temperature up to 3000 ◦ Cwere examined by measuring the dimensions and weight change of the ablated samples. The linear ablation rate was decreased from 0.14 𝛍 ms -1 for the first 30 s of the ablation to 0.08 𝛍 ms -1 after 180 s. Ti2AlC ceramics gained small amounts of weight upon ablation, which is attributed to the formation of oxidation products on the ablated surface. The ablation surface exhibits a two-layer structure: an oxide outer layer, consisting mainly of 𝛂 -Al2O3 and TiO2 and some Al2TiO5, and a porous sub-surface layer containing Ti2Al1 -x C and TiC x O y . With increasing ablation time, the content of TiO2 and Al2TiO5 in the outer layer increased, and more pores developed in the sub-surface layer. The thermal oxidation of Ti2AlC under the flame and scouring of the viscous oxidation products by high-speed flow of gas torch are the main ablation mechanisms.

Keywords: Ti2AlC; Ablation; Oxyacetylene flame; Oxidation

Table 3
Actually, the oxidising conditions generated in the bottom-up air furnace, the target temperature apart, differ a lot from those experienced by the discs exposed to the hot flame: in the former case equilibria very likely prevailed, while in the second case very fast exposure to gases imposed conditions far from thermodynamic equilibrium. In the presence of oxygen, the prevailing reactions thermodynamically favour the transformation of the (Ta,Ti)C phase to rutile-type Ti-rich dioxide and orthorhombic type Ta-rich pentoxide. Based on the calculated thermo-chemical equilibria involving only stoichiometric and pure TiC and TaC with oxygen, the release of gaseous CO2 seems to be favoured when compared to that of CO. On one side, protection against oxidation/ablation is afforded as long as solid and strongly refractory oxide products are formed. On the other hand, the in-situ formed silica glass at 1873 K, wherever is present, is stable and provides protection to the adjacent areas. An additional protective contribution might derive from specific eutectics between SiO2-Ta2O5 [10] and TiO2-Ta2O5 [17], mostly in those areas of the Cf /C-STT composite not directly facing the harsh external environment. When the testing environment becomes harsher in term of temperature, pressure and gas reactivity (this is the case of the oxyacetylene flame test), the aforementioned in-situ generated glasses have fewer (or none at all) changes to survive due to rapid volatilization, and the only solid oxidation products of (Ta,Ti)C remain as the ultimate wall capable of delaying the ongoing oxidation/ablation of the bulk composite. Traces of the Ta2O5-SiO2 eutectic, which has the appearance of a molten phase, were found in the ablated Cf /C-STT composite (see Fig. 16). The EDX spectrum of such an molten phase is compared to that of an oxide sub-layer which formed during oxyacetylene flame shot.

A B S T R A C T
Ablation-resistant performance and damage mechanism of carbon fiber reinforced ZrC based ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composite prepared via alloyed reactive melt infiltration were investigated using a laser ablation testing method under different laser power densities (LPD) and time periods. Ablation damage of the composite was obviously enhanced tested with increasing LPD and reduced with prolonging tested time. A fused ZrO2 protecting layer was in situ formed during ablation testing, significantly improving the composite's ablation-resistant performance. Ablated microstructures of the composites tested with different LPD were characterized and an ablation mechanism going through three distinct periods was proposed. In the first period, the testing temperature is low and ablation damage was thought to be mainly controlled by the oxidation of carbon, ZrC and Zr2Si phases. With the increase of testing temperatures, ablation damage became controlled by evaporating of ZrO2 and SiO2 formed in oxidizing stage. In the final period with the highest testing temperatures, ablation damage became synergistically dominated by repeatedly scouring away and new formation of the fused ZrO2 protecting layer.
and oxyacetylene flame testing were employed to test and study the ablation resistant properties of materials. For decades, a significant amount of work is done to test material's ablation resistance and behavior using these above methods. Wind-tunnel and plasma ablation testing are frequently used and can simulate the environment with strong gas flowing and high enthalpy. Nevertheless, both of them have extremely high cost [6-8]. By contrast, oxyacetylene flame ablation testing is demonstrated to be a simple and economical method to evaluate material's ablation resistance [9,10]. However, the testing temperature is restricted by oxyacetylene flame temperature (about 3000 °C). Additionally, complex combustion products (O2, CO2, etc. [11]) are included in testing environment, which may sometimes influence the reasonability of the testing results.

2. Experimental
Bulk Ti2AlC ceramic samples were prepared via an in situ solid-liquid reaction between Ti, Al and graphite powders, heated to a high temperature. Ti, Al and graphite powders with the desired atomic ratio of 2:1.1:1 were mixed by ball-milling for 20 h in an ethanol solution. The slurry was dried at 60 ◦ C and cold-pressed into blocks with 8 MPa in a graphite die, and then hot-pressed with 30 MPa at 1450 ◦ Cfor 5 h in flowing argon gas. Disk shaped samples with a size of 30 mm in diameter and 10 mm in thickness were cut from the sintered bulk Ti2AlC by using electrical discharge wire machining for ablation tests. The planar surfaces of the disks were grinded using SiC emery papers with 400 down to 1200 grit, followed by polishing with 1 𝛍 m diamond paste. The ablation tests were implemented according to the standard GJB 323A-96 with an oxyacetylene flame. 10,11 The pressure and the flow rate of acetylene were respectively, 0.095 MPa and 1.116 m 3 h -1 , and the pressure and the flow rate of oxygenwererespectively, 0.4 MPa and 1.512 m 3 h -1 . The distance between the flame gun mouth and ablation surface of the disk was fixed at 10 mm. The centres of the flame and the torch were aligned horizontally. Under such conditions the material to be tested would be locally exposed to a flame temperature up to 3000 ◦ C 10-12 leading to hot gas flow induced material removal.
The resulting linear ablation rate, Rd , and the mass ablation rate, Rm , are defined as:

question: How do the oxide scale species that form on ZrB2-HfB2-SiC material B in Alfano et al. 2010 compare to those forming on Ti2AlC ceramics in Song et al. 2011 during high-temperature oxidative exposure?
answer: The provided sources do not mention Alfano et al. 2010 or Song et al. 2011, nor do they specifically discuss the oxide scale species that form on ZrB2-HfB2-SiC material or Ti2AlC ceramics during high-temperature oxidative exposure. Therefore, a comparison cannot be made based on the given sources.

chunks.text:
Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
It can be seen that no glassy product (such as B 2 O3 for the borides) form during oxidation of the carbides. Also, further oxidation of the elemental carbon formed (according to Equation 3) leads to evolution of gaseous oxidation products like CO 2 and CO which result in formation of porous oxide scales. Hence, the porous oxide scales do not lead to passive oxidation or protection via parabolic kinetics. Furthermore, these scales do not adhere well at the interface and fall off, a phenomenon known as 'pesting' (Wuchina, 2007). Due to such problems related to oxidation behavior of the carbides, most of them can be used for only those applications which involve very rapid heating to temperatures beyond ~ 1800 o C. Among the carbides, HfC has been shown to possess much better oxidation resistance due to the formation of three distinct surface layers on high temperature exposure to oxidative environment, with the intermediate layer composed of a dense oxide (HfC x Oy ) acting as oxygen diffusion barrier (Sayir, 2004). The oxidation behavior of TaC is a bit different from the rest and is not suitable for very high temperature application at high P O2 . This
is because, unlike the oxide scales of the other carbides (like HfC or ZrC), Ta 2 O5 formed on the surface of TaC has a relatively lower melting point of ~ 1870 o C (Wuchina, 2007). It has also been found that at low P O2 , the oxide scales might not actually form on TaC (Wuchina, 2007; Agte, 1930).

4. Oxidation studies
On the top side the zirconia layer did not offer effective protection against oxidation and the scale thickness was about 205 m m, which is comparable to that reported for pure ZrB2 [29]. A sample prepared by crushing DS samples prepared as detailed in Ref. [30] had a random orientation with respect to the inward oxygen /uniFB02 ux. The scale formed on this sample was 160 m mthick, as expected this thickness is intermediate between that for the DS top and its side. Comparing all of the DS samples seen in Fig. 8 reveals that the SiC front facing the inward oxygen /uniFB02 ux has a dominant role in controlling the thickness of the oxide layer. According to Zhu et al. [31] the oxidation resistance in ZrB2-SiC is improved by having /uniFB01 nely dispersed (distance between SiC particles of about 10 m m) SiC particles in the matrix because of the improved tendency of /uniFB01 ne particles ( < 3 m m) to form a continuous silica protective layer. As a result the SPS samples prepared at 2373 K results had a scale thickness of 80 m m. Cross sections of solid state sintered samples had a morphology of the scale similar to that described in a recent review [32]. The sample side had quite a distinctive oxidation behaviour which was not previously reported as the oxygen inward /uniFB02 ux was retarded by the SiC/ZrB2 lamellar structure. The composition of borosilicate glass formed during the oxidation product is dif /uniFB01 cult to determine because of the dif /uniFB01 culties in measuring B content. On the side surface the silica layer formed from SiC continuous lamella is likely to contain a reduced boria content.

3.5. ZrB2  and HfB2; W and Nb inclusions
Dehdashti et al. formed a (Zr,Nb)B2 solid solution by incorporating 6 mol% Nb in the form of powder during hot pressing [97]. After 3 h in air at 1500 ◦ C, the scale on the unmodified ZrB2  ceramic was comprised entirely of porous ZrO2 while the modified ceramic maintained an outer glassy layer above a porous oxide scale, but no sintering/densification was observed in the porous region. During further study, Dehdashti et al. found that W additions increased the stability of the liquid boria phase [98]; prepared B2O3-WO3 glasses were found to have significantly lower volatility rates then B2O3. The diborides modified with W demonstrated a strong propensity to maintain an outer glass scale, although at 1600 ◦ C no glass layer was found on any of the ceramics. The effect was also found to be present for Mo and Nb 4 mol% modifications [99], which were  more  effective  than  W  in  decreasing  scale  thickness.  During oxidation of ZrB2-WSi2, Silvestroni et al. reported that borates were still present in the outer silica-rich glass scale at 1800 ◦ C and attributed it to W stabilization of the glass. [32] Similar to MoB formation effectively increasing melt viscosity during oxidation of ZrB2-MoSi2  [32], W increases melt viscosity by consuming boria during oxidation [47].
Fig. 11. Comparison of scale morphology of (a) ZrB2-20 vol%SiC and (b) ZrB2-20 vol%-20 vol%TaSi2 after ten 10 minute cycles at 1627 ◦ C in air [25,96].

3.2. HfB2-SiC and ZrB2-SiC Composites
Continuing  with  the  innovation  of  their  earlier  ZrB2  TGA  study, Tripp, David, and Graham performed oxidation experiments on ZrB2-20 vol%SiC [68] over 800 ◦ -1500 ◦ C. Below 1200 ◦ C, composite reaction rates were found to be comparable to monolithic ZrB2. An abrupt change in reaction rate was observed at 1300 ◦ C and surprisingly, the oxidation rate at 1400 ◦ C was lower than at 1300 ◦ C. At temperatures ≥ 1300 ◦ C, vaporization measurements were linear early in exposure but decreased with time. Microprobe scans of the scales formed at 1300 ◦ and 1400 ◦ C revealed the presence of B and Si, with less B being present at 1400 ◦ C, and no Zr detected. X-ray diffraction determined the scale to be amorphous  at  both  temperatures,  indicating  glassy  borosilicate  formation began to occur at ~1300 ◦ C. They postulated that once silica formation becomes  appreciable,  borosilicate  formation  begins,  and  being  more viscous  and  less  susceptible  to  volatilization  than  boria,  extends  the protective oxidation regime to higher temperatures. It was concluded that the inclusion of SiC imparted significant improvements to oxidation resistance up to at least 1400 ◦ C by enabling borosilicate formation.

3.2. Quantitative XRD analysis
 (wt%), AZrMS = 3.36. HfB 2 (wt%), CZrSS = 1.81. HfB 2 (wt%), Change(%) = GLYPH<0> 46.1. B 2 O 3 (wt%), ZrB 2 powder = 4.38. B 2 O 3 (wt%), AZrMS = -. B 2 O 3 (wt%), CZrSS = -. B 2 O 3 (wt%), Change(%) = -
Fig. 4. Comparison of XRD patterns for the ZrB2  powder feedstock and deposited ZrB2  coatings, note the final Rwp  determined for each sample during Rietveld refinement has been indicated, due to contamination the CZrCC did not undergo Rietveld analysis.
B.A. Kahl et al.
Boron oxide has not been detected in any coatings; whereas it was present in the feedstock, therefore, it has been vaporized on exposure to the plasma plume. Finally, the existence of ZrC was confirmed in all ZrB2 coatings and provides an additional pathway for forming oxides.
The HfB2 powder feedstock and CHfSS coating also underwent XRD analysis with the Rietveld approach, Fig. 5. The results from Rietveld analysis reveal that the HfB2  powder feedstock contains trace monoclinic  oxide  (1.63  wt%)  that  probably  formed  on  exposure  to  atmospheric water during storage [12]. The CHfSS sample exhibited a high amount of monoclinic oxide (47.60 wt%) with some cubic oxide (7.89 wt%) and trace quantities of tetragonal oxide (0.41 wt%), as shown in Table 3. This suggests that the majority of the oxidised powder particles experienced  conditions  associated  with  the  formation  of  monoclinic oxide, unlike the deposited ZrB2 coatings. The lack of additional oxides may be significant since it is possible that some powder particles are not reaching high enough temperatures to become completely molten or form the allomorphs that occur at higher temperatures.

question: What sintering aid or processing agent enabled densification in Alfano et al. 2010's ZrB2-SiC UHTC composites, versus the binder-free approach used to consolidate ZrB2 in Kemp et al. 2021's direct ink writing inks?
answer: In Alfano et al. 2010's ZrB2-SiC UHTC composites, the sintering aid used was 2 vol.% MoSi2 [3]. In contrast, Kemp et al. 2021's direct ink writing inks used a binder-free approach, which involved a composite ink consisting of ZrB2 powders, SiC precursors, and SiC short fibers, without the addition of a specific sintering aid [4].

chunks.text:
3. Processing techniques for UHTCs
Effective sintering of UHTCs requires the initial powder to have high purity  (although  metallic  impurities  aid  in  the  densification  process, whereas oxide impurities promote grain growth and are detrimental for densification),  small  powder  particle  size,  narrow  particle-size  distribution, and low agglomeration factor [45]. The low self-diffusion coefficient and strong covalent bonds in UHTCs make sintering difficult [46]. Hot pressing (HP) has typically been employed to densify UHTCs [47 -53], while achieving 94 -97% densification in TaC-based composites sintered at 1800 -2300 ◦ C [50 -53], while 98 -100% densification is obtained in  ZrB2-  based  composites  upon  sintering  in  1600 -1870 ◦ C range [47,49]. To achieve full densification of ZrB2 -30ZrC -10SiC, the total sintering time is reduced from ~140 min in HP (with 3.7 vol % Si3N4 as a sintering aid) to ~23 min in SPS (without any sintering aid) [54].  With  HP  and  SPS,  complex  shapes  cannot  be  prepared,  which pushes utilization of pressureless sintering (with inherently poor mechanical properties attributed to poor densification). Pressureless sintering and hot pressing of ZrB2  have shown Vickers ' hardness of 14.5 GPa and 23 GPa, the elastic modulus of 454 GPa and 489 GPa, and flexural  strength  of  444  MPa  and  565  MPa, respectively  [55]. Other techniques to consolidate UHTCs are atmospheric plasma spraying [56], vacuum plasma spraying [8] and chemical vapor infiltration [57], etc. The pros and cons of these processing techniques towards enunciating microstructure  and  resulting  consolidation,  illustrated  in  Table

A B S T R A C T
Authors of the present work have focused their studies on the processing of UHTCMCs, adopting the method of slurry impregnation and sintering. This method allowed to achieve high density composites, with high fractions of UHTC phase and a quite strong fibre/matrix interface [1,16,17].  The  most  investigated  option  for  the  matrix  was  ZrB2, because  it  possesses  the  best  combination  of  refractoriness  (melting point  3250 ◦ C),  oxidation  resistance  and  low  specific  weight  (6.1 g/cm 3 ). Blends of ZrB2 and SiC powders were studied to understand the effect of SiC addition on mechanical properties and oxidation resistance of  UHTCMCs  at  intermediate  temperature  (1650 ◦ C)  [18].  In  other focused  studies,  the  matrices  were  based  on  ZrC,  TaC  and  HfC.  The bending strength of these composites was measured up to 2100 ◦ C [19, 20],  showing  very  promising  results.  As  for  the  consolidation  techniques, beside hot pressing [1,16,17], it has been recently demonstrated that spark plasma sintering is an effective method to achieve densification in a few minutes for these composites, provided that the thermal cycle is finely calibrated to avoid excessive reactions at the interface with the fibre [21]. Finally, for the high temperature characterization, high enthalpy arc-jet tests demonstrated the capability of Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composites to withstand temperatures as high as 2500 ◦ C, with limited erosion,  owing  to  the  partial  healing  of  surface  flaws  let  by  fiber burn-out by readily formed silica-based glass [22 -24].

2.1. Materials
Nearly full dense UHTC composites were obtained by hotpressing of the following powder compositions (vol.%):
- Material A: ZrB2 + 15 SiC
- Material B: ZrB2 + 15 SiC + 10 HfB2
with 2 (vol.%) MoSi2 as a sintering aid.
ZrB2 and 𝛃 -SiC raw powders were from H.C. Starck, HfB2 from Cerac Inc., and MoSi2 from Sigma-Aldrich. Table 1 summarizes some characteristics of the raw powders used. The as-sintered materials A and B had bulk densities, evaluated using the Archimedes method (water as immersing medium), of 5.61 and 6.06 g/cm 3 , respectively, which corresponds to relative densities of 99 and 98%. The powder mixtures were uniaxially hot-pressed in vacuum using an inductively heated graphite die, lined with a BN-sprayed graphitized sheet. Peak temperatures/dwell times/applied pressures were 1820 ◦ C/15 min/30 MPa for material A, and 1900-1940 ◦ C/45 min/40 MPa for material B, with an average heating rate of 20 ◦ C/min. The temperature was measured by means of an optical pyrometer focused on the graphite die.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) investigations were performed by a Ni-filtered CuK 𝛂 radiation X-ray diffractometer (model D500, Siemens, Karlsruhe, Germany). In the case of material A, the main identified phases were ZrB2 and SiC. Instead, in material B, obvious changes in the starting composition took place during sintering. XRD analyses showed the formation of ZrB2/HfB2 solid solutions. Such solid solutions uphold the hexagonal structure of the main ZrB2 phase, however the lat- tice readily accommodates substitution of Hf atoms for Zr. The thermo-physical and mechanical properties of the two UHTC compositions have been extensively characterized in previous studies. 12,14

4.6.  Additive manufacturing technology, AM
To date, notable progress and breakthroughs have been achieved in the additive manufacturing of carbon fiber-reinforced ceramic matrix composites [143]. Kemp et al. [144] utilized direct ink writing (DIW) technology with a composite ink consisting of ZrB2  powders, SiC precursors,  and  SiC  short  fibers.  The  printing  was  conducted  at  room temperature, followed by pyrolysis at 1200 °C to fabricate SiCf/ZrB2-SiC composites. However, as the fiber content increased, issues such as fiber misalignment and material cracking arose due to gas release and matrix shrinkage during polymer curing and pyrolysis. Additionally, the pyrolyzed components often exhibited defects, limiting their mechanical performance. Li et al. [145] addressed these issues by employing DIW combined with ultrasound-assisted fiber separation. During the direct ink  writing  of  continuous  carbon  fiber/ceramic  composites,  ceramic slurry was impregnated into continuous carbon fiber bundles. A subsequent low-temperature hot-pressing process was used to enhance the robustness of the fabricated structures, indirectly resulting in Cf/ZrB₂SiC ceramic matrix composites (as shown in Fig. 23).
Despite these advancements, several challenges remain in the additive manufacturing of UHTC, such as the high brittleness of materials leading to crack formation and the control of thermal stresses during the  fabrication  process.  Future  research  should  focus  on  optimizing process  parameters,  developing  new  material  systems,  and  exploring the  integration  of  multiple  manufacturing  techniques.  AM  offers  a highly flexible and promising pathway for fabricating ceramic matrix composites,  especially  UHTC.  While  challenges  persist,  ongoing  research and technological innovations are expected to position AM as a pivotal technique in the manufacturing of high-temperature materials, driving  the  widespread  application  of  ceramic  matrix  composites  in aerospace, defense, and other critical fields.
Fig. 23. Schematic illustration of Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites fabricated by direct ink writing and hot-press sintering. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [145], © Elsevier 2022.

4.2.1. Processing, microstructure, and the role of impurities
Carbon- and carbon-based additives have essentially been found to assist sintering of ZrB2 by removing surface oxides, which are prevalent on ZrB2  particles ' surfaces  [122].  Apart  from  densification,  sintering additives, such as SiC, TaB2, B4C, and MoSi2, have improved the mechanical, thermal, and oxidative properties of UHTCs. SiC among these is  considered one of the best additives [7,86,123 -131]. SiC has been used in different contents (~up to 40 vol %), in different powder particle sizes  (micron, sub-micron or nano-sized) and in different forms (like whiskers, fibres and particulates) [122]. The particulate SiC reinforcement  is  discussed  in  this  section,  whereas  aspects  of  SiC  fiber  reinforcement  are  discussed  in  section  5  and  7.  Different  content  of  SiC (5 -40 vol %) has been reinforced in ZrB2  [132] sintered using SPS at 1900 ◦ C. Complete densification ( > 99%) occurred with 20 vol % SiC reinforcement. Though hardness increased with increasing SiC content (22.7 GPa with 40 vol % SiC), highest flexural strength (553 MPa) and highest fracture toughness (2.66 MPam 1/2 ) was obtained with 10 vol % SiC and 20 vol % SiC addition, respectively. On the other hand, 10 vol % SiC  reinforced  ZrB2 exhibited  the  lowest  ablation  and  oxidation resistance as compared to that of 15 vol % and 30 vol % reinforcement when tested under different heat fluxes in plasma wind tunnel [133]. The presence of SiC can be observed at the triple point junction of ZrB2 grains (Fig. 13a), however, HRTEM image from one of such interfaces shows a thin (3 -6 nm) amorphous oxide layer, Fig. 13b [130]. Further, carbon-based  additives  such  as  CNT  and  GNP  have  also  shown

question: How does the average particle size of the ZrB2 raw powder used by Kemp et al. 2021 for direct ink writing inks compare to the ZrB2 powder characteristics reported by Alfano et al. 2010 for hot-pressed UHTCs?
answer: The sources provided do not mention Kemp et al. 2021 or Alfano et al. 2010, and therefore, the average particle size of the ZrB2 raw powder used by Kemp et al. 2021 for direct ink writing inks cannot be compared to the ZrB2 powder characteristics reported by Alfano et al. 2010 for hot-pressed UHTCs.

chunks.text:
4.3.2. Starting powder size distribution
Sintering is more efficient when the starting powder characteristics enable quicker densification without being prone to grain growth. As such, smaller particle size generally exhibits better sintering characteristics. Specifically, for an LPS process, the satisfaction of the following criteria  would  enable  the  ideal  conditions  for  densification:  good wettability of the liquid phase, solubility of the solid grains in the liquid phase (the opposite is undesirable), and smaller grain sizes that result in shorter traveling distances for the liquid [16]. This has been demonstrated by Silva et al. in their experiments when sintering WC-Co [40]. For ZrB2-30 vol% ZrSi2, S. Guo et al. [10] have achieved, through LPS, a 99.7% densification with powders with an average size of 2.1 μ m and 2.5 μ m for  ZrB2  and  ZrSi2, respectively.  In  this  study,  the  suggested particle size is 45 -105 μ m, as recommended by Arcam. The particle size distribution of our powder is shown in Fig. 8.

2.1. Materials and process
In this research, commercially available ZrB2 powder with an average particle size of 2 m m (Leung Hi-tech Co., China) and B4C powder with an average particle size of 20 m m (Mudanjiang Jingangzuan Boron Carbide Co., China) were purchased and used as raw materials. Such relatively large B4C particles were applied to promote the formation of open porosities in as-sintered sample. ZrB2 powder was dry-mixed with 40 vol% B4C powder by ball mixing at 120 rpm for 60 min in zirconia media. Then the sample with a diameter of 25 mm and a thickness of 5 mm was loaded into a graphite die, which was sprayed with boron nitride (BN) and lined with /uniFB02 exible graphite foils. Sintering process was performed in a vacuum hot pressing furnace (Shenyang Weitai Science & Technology Development Co. Ltd., China). The powder mixture was initially heated at a rate of 15 ° C/min up to 1000 ° C and then the temperature was kept at 1000 ° C for 30 min. Finally the sample was heated at a rate of 12 ° C/min up to 1800 ° C. An external pressure of 12 MPa was applied to the sample, for 30 min, after the temperature reached 1800 ° C. The sintering furnace was cooled down to the room temperature in a normal path gradually.

2.1. Materials
Porous C/C tubes (Fig. 1) of 17.75 mm OD × 3.90 mm ID × 45 mm long were used in the present study for ZrB2 infiltration. The porous C/C composite tubes had a total porosity of ∼ 60% (measured using Archimedes method) and bimodal pore size distribution (determined using laser diffraction particle size analyser) with average pore sizes of 150 𝛍 mand10 𝛍 m, respectively. ZrB2 powder ( d 50 ∼ 45 𝛍 m, 99% purity, Good Fellow, Huntingdon, UK) was used as the UHTC material. The as-received ZrB2 powder was further dry-milled for 30 min in a 'swing-mill' shatter box using a steel container coated with Teflon to reduce the average particle size to 5.11 ± 0.5 𝛍 m. Longer milling times (>30 min) did not result in significant reduction in the particle size. Particle size distribution analysis for milling times of 45 min and 60 min resulted in 50 wt.% of the materials being below 5.04 𝛍 mand 4.89 𝛍 m, respectively.
Fig. 1. Optical photograph of a porous C/C tube.

2.1. Sample preparation
Commercial ZrB2 and SiC powders were used as the raw materials. The ZrB2 powder (Beijing Zhongjinyan Micropowder Co. Ltd., Beijing, China) had a reported purity above 99% and a particle size of 2 μ m. The SiC powder (Weifang Kaihua Micro-powder Co. Ltd., Weifang, China) was predominantly α -SiC, having a reported purity of 99% and a particle size of 1 μ m. The powders were batched in the ratio of 70 vol% ZrB2 to 30 vol% SiC. The powder mixture was ballmilled in ethanol for 8 h with zirconia ball media in a planetary mill (MCA-10B, Nanjing Nanda Instrument Plant Co. Ltd., China) at 240 rpm (revolutions per minute). After ball-milling, ethanol was removed by a rotating evaporator (R-202, Shanghai Shensheng Biotech Co. Ltd., China) at 80 1 C to minimize segregation. The as-received ZrB2 -SiC powder mixtures were sieved through a 200 mesh to abtain homogeneous powder mixtures and then uniaxially hot-pressed in a BN coated graphite die at 1950 1 C for 60 min under vacuum and 30 MPa applied pressure.
36 mm /C2 4 mm /C2 3 mm (Length /C2 width /C2 thickness) testing bars were cut from the hot-pressedZrB2 -30 vol% SiC billet using electro spark wire-electrode cutting technique. All testing bars were fabricated with the tensile surface perpendicular to the hot-pressing direction. Prior to pre-oxidation treatment, each specimen was ground and polished with diamond slurries down to a 1 μ m /uniFB01 nish, and the edges of all the specimens were chamfered to minimize the effect of stress concentration.

3. Results and discussion
Fig. 2A shows a representative SEM micrograph of the UHTC fabricated from the 180-min ball-milled ZrB2 powder. The average ZrB2 grain size is ∼ 5 𝛍 m, which is relatively small compared to the ∼ 15 𝛍 m grain size of the reference ZrB2 monolith prepared from the as-received ZrB2 powder (Fig. 2B). The reference monolith contains pores located at triple joints, grain edges, and especially within the grains, as seen more clearly in the optical micrograph of Fig. 3. The SEM micrograph of Fig. 2A also displays the presence of smaller particles (i.e., ∼ 1 𝛍 m) that exhibit a slight compositional contrast. The EDXS analysis of these small particles shown in Fig. 4A indicates that they are ZrO2, and the XRD analysis of the compact shown in Fig. 4B that this ZrO2 crystallized in its monoclinic and tetragonal variants with the total amount of ZrO2 representing ∼ 8 wt%. Thus, the combination of the SEM, EDXS, and XRD analyses reveals that the UHTC fabricated from the ball-milled ZrB2 powder is indeed a ZrB2-ZrO2 composite.
1 To minimize the powder contamination during high-energy ball-milling, a first milling cycle was carried out with the objective of depositing a thin film of ZrB2 on the surface of the container and of the balls, and the resulting powder was thrown away. Then, the milling cycle was repeated utilizing the same container and balls, using the resulting powder for this study.
Fig. 1. (A) TEM bright-field image and (B) TEM dark-field image of the ZrB2 powder with 180 min of high-energy ball-milling in air, showing both primary nanoparticles with ∼ 10 nm size.

question: What toughening or reinforcement strategy does Kemp et al. 2021 use to address brittle failure in ZrB2-based UHTCs, and how does this contrast with the microstructural failure modes discussed for Ti2AlC ablation in Song et al. 2011?
answer:
Kemp et al. 2021 used short SiC fibers (SiCf) reinforced in a ZrB2 matrix through additive manufacturing, specifically via direct ink writing (DIW) of a SiC polymer precursor blended with ZrB2 particles [1]. This approach aimed to improve the mechanical properties of ZrB2-based ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) by incorporating short fibers, which can promote crack deflection and improve fracture toughness. However, the resulting composites exhibited considerable porosity and cracking after pyrolysis, leading to lower fracture strengths (38 - 52 MPa) compared to those produced by hot pressing (HP) or spark plasma sintering (SPS).

In contrast, the microstructural failure modes discussed for Ti2AlC ablation in Song et al. 2011 are not directly addressed in the provided sources. The sources do not mention Song et al. 2011 or any specific details about Ti2AlC ablation failure modes. Therefore, a direct comparison between Kemp et al.'s strategy and the microstructural failure modes of Ti2AlC ablation cannot be made based on the given information.

chunks.text:
3.2. SiC short fiber reinforcement
Some studies have explored more inventive approaches to incorporating SiCf  into UHTC matrices. Sciti et al. [145] predicted synergistic toughening  effects  from  incorporating  both  SiC  fibers  and  whiskers within the same ZrB2 matrix, where fibers would promote crack bowing and  whiskers  would  promote  crack  deflection.  The  best-performing composites exhibited fracture toughness value of 6.1 MPa m ½ ,  which is comparable to values reported for composites only using SiCf  as reinforcements. Another novel approach was taken by Kemp et al. [146] via additive manufacturing, where ZrB2  particles and short SiCf  were blended into a SiC polymer precursor for processing by direct ink writing (DIW). The DIW synthesis did enable the manufacturing of UHTCMCs with complex shapes, although the printed body exhibited considerable porosity and cracking after pyrolysis. These microstructural defects were reflected in the fracture strengths of the composites (38 -52 MPa), which are  an  order  of  magnitude  lower  than  SiCf-reinforced  materials  produced by HP or SPS. However, the authors did propose potential solutions (deposition refinement, chemical initiators, thermal pre-treatments,  etc.)  to  optimize  the  composite  microstructure  and address the observed shortcomings.

1. Introduction
The main weaknesses of UHTCs are their brittle fracture behavior and low damage tolerance, which have limited their applications. Cook and Gordon [5] were the /uniFB01 rst to introduce the idea that it is possible to control crack propagation in a brittle material by considering particular microstructural features that can change the crack path. Later, Clegg et al. [6] showed that by separating strong phase layers with weak interphases, a brittle composite ceramic can fail in a non-brittle manner by de /uniFB02 ecting the crack through different phases. Several attempts have been made to process and characterize mechanical and thermal properties of different engineered architectures for ZrB2based ceramics [2,7 -14]. For example, Fig. 1 shows load-displacement curves for a single phase ZrB2 ceramic and a laminated ZrB2-C composite; ZrB2 by itself is brittle, and exhibits no visible inelastic work of fracture after reaching the breaking load. On the other hand, the laminate composite showed non-brittle fracture behavior through crack de /uniFB02 ection in a non-elastic form. Similar studies have been done on bioinspired microstructure design of laminates to increase their toughness by providing preferential paths for propagating cracks [15 -19]. Chen et al. [20] fabricated Al2O3/reduced graphene oxide (rGO) /uniFB01 brous monolithic ceramics with bamboo-like structures that had 475% and 1075% higher fracture toughness and work of fracture than those of the mono-lithic Al2O3 ceramics. In a different study, TaC-based/graphite /uniFB01 brous monolithic ceramics were investigated by Shahedifar et al. [21] to study the /uniFB01 ber core/shell volume ratio and /uniFB01 ber orientation on the fracture toughness and the work of fracture. Experimental efforts for developing ceramics with engineered microarchitectures have laid out the initial path for improving the fracture toughness of UHTCs; however, these were mostly based on trial and error experiments.

3.2. SiC short fiber reinforcement
Other recent investigations have proposed functionally graded materials (FGM) for embedding SiCf  in UHTC matrices. Silvestroni et al. [147] synthesized ZrB2-based composites where the fiber volume fraction  increases  as  a  function  of  the  material  depth,  where  the  thin, outermost layer of the material contains the lowest fiber fraction. In principle, this outermost skin should possess a high resistance against oxidation and ablation, and it serves to protect the inner layers of the FGM  that  are  embedded  with  higher  volume  fractions  of  SiCf,  The described  architecture  merited  a  composite  with  reasonable  fracture toughness values (~6 MPa m ½ ) while maintaining low overall densities; this  suggested  a  potential  for  lightweight  component  design  using fiber-reinforced FGMs. Furthermore, load-displacements curves (Fig.  9b)  indicated  failure  behavior  that  was  not  completely  brittle, based  on  the  tailed  shape  at  higher  displacements.  The  subsequent investigation by Stricker et al. [148] further developed the ZrB2  FGM approach by refining the buffer layer architecture. In this design, the outermost  layer  of  the  composite  contained  a  MoSi2 additive  for enhanced oxidation protection, followed by a ' buffer layer ' that mixed ZrB2 with Si3N4, SiCN, or SiHfBCN. The buffer layer served to obstruct diffusion of Mo species from the outer skin to the inner composite, where it could detrimentally react with SiCf during sintering.
In terms of mechanical properties, SiCf-reinforced composites have reported  advantages  over  monolithic  ceramics,  specifically  in  higher fracture toughness values and retained flexural strength up to 1200 ◦ C. Nevertheless, reaction between the fibers and the matrix phase during sintering  remains  an  ongoing  issue,  which  severely  limits  effective toughening  by  fiber  pullout.  Next  generation  SiC  fibers  with  higher purities  have  the  potential  to  resist  matrix  interaction  during  high temperature pressing, but these materials have not yet been explored as reinforcements for UHTCMCs [149].

1. Introduction
ZrB2 -based UHTCs present a lots of excellent properties, such as high hardness and extremely high melting point, high electrical and thermal conductivity, and good high temperature stability [1 -4], which make them useful for many high temperature structural applications, including atmospheric re-entry [2,5], electrodes [3,6], hypersonic vehicles [2,4,7] and high-temperature shielding [6,8]. However, the intrinsic characteristics for ZrB2-based UHTCs such as low fracture toughness must be settled before they can be used widely, especially for high temperature conditions. Some new methods for enhancing the low fracture toughness of ceramic materials have emerged in recent years. The layered structure with the introduction of surface compressive stresses is an e /uniFB00 ective strategy to improve the fracture toughness of brittle ceramics [9,10], which is obviously di /uniFB00 erent from the conventional methods to achieve ' /uniFB02 aw tolerance ' in opposition to the ' /uniFB02 aw elimination ' of the traditional ceramics [11,12]. Due to the excellent advantages of layered structures, much e /uniFB00 ort is being done to the development of laminates to improve the mechanical properties of ceramic materials. It is expected that the layered

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.exm.2025.01.001
In  summary,  this  paper  systematically  reviews  recent  progress  in UHTCs,  focusing  on  their  fabrication  methods,  strengthening  and toughening strategies, oxidation resistance and ablation mechanisms, and lightweight design. It discusses various fabrication techniques, including pressure sintering, slurry impregnation, precursor infiltration and pyrolysis,  reactive  melt  infiltration,  solid-liquid  hybrid  methods, and additive manufacturing. Additionally, it elaborates on toughening approaches involving particles, soft phases, short fibers, and continuous fibers, along with their underlying mechanisms. Furthermore, the paper examines  the  oxidation  resistance  and  ablation  behavior  of  UHTCs, highlighting  their  application  potential.  Finally,  it  summarizes  the challenges in current research and outlines future development trends. Moving forward, the research on UHTCs should continue to focus on enhancing their comprehensive performance and application reliability, exploring more advanced fabrication techniques and material systems to meet the stringent demands of TPS under extreme conditions in highspeed aerospace applications.

question: How does the machining method used to shape ZrB2-SiC-based billets in Alfano et al. 2010 differ from the near-net-shape forming method for ZrB2-SiC composites demonstrated by Kemp et al. 2021?
answer: The provided sources do not mention Alfano et al. 2010 or Kemp et al. 2021, and therefore, it is not possible to compare the machining methods used to shape ZrB2-SiC-based billets in these studies with the near-net-shape forming method for ZrB2-SiC composites.

Tallon et al. [17] showed that solvent-based colloidal processing  routes  could  be  used  to  produce  ZrB2  compacts  with  93% theoretical density as well as near-net complex shapes [18]. Stable ZrB2 dispersions with high solids loading in aqueous systems have also been reported  [19,20]  and  used  to  create  complex  shapes  via  freeze-form extrusion and pressureless sintering [19]. Medri et al. [21] studied the slip casting and pressureless sintering of aqueous-based ZrB2-SiC composites.  The  authors  showed  that  the  colloidal  processing  technique could  produce  near-net  shapes  with  higher  densities  than  from  cold isostatic pressing, albeit the slip cast samples exhibited some segregation of larger particles.

R.V. KRISHNARAO * , G. MADHUSUDHAN REDDY
Defence Metallurgical Research Laboratory, Kanchanbagh, Hyderabad 500058, India
Received 11 March 2015; revised 16 March 2015; accepted 17 March 2015 Available online 18 April 2015
The components of ZrB2 e SiC composite are generally made by pressureless sintering (PS) or hot pressing (HP). The difficulty in fabricating the large size or complex shapes limits the application of ZrB2 e SiC composites. Joining is an alternative and cost effective method for fabricating the large size or complicated shape components of ceramics. Joining of engineering ceramics such as Al2O3, SiC, C/C, C/SiC has been widely studied [6 e 11]. Recently, several investigations on joining of ZrB2 e SiC composites have been reported in the literature. Bellosi et al. [12] joined ZrB2 e SiC composites using Ca e Al e Si e O, and Y e Al e Si e O glass powders as bonding inter layers at 1440 /C14 C and reported a bending strength of 277 MPa at room temperature. Among other reports, brazing is the most common method. Muolo et al. [13] studied the wetting behavior of ZrB2 with different metals (Cu, Ag, Au) and their alloys and optimal results were obtained with silver-based alloys. Due to low melting point of these solders, the joints cannot be useful at temperature above 1000 /C14 C.
Singh et al. [14] studied the joining of ZrB2 e SiC9 e SiC, ZrB2 e SiC e C and ZrB2 e SiC to themselves and to commercially pure Ti using boron containing Ni base braze alloys (MBF-20 and MBF-30). Asthana et al. [15] studied the joining of similar composites to themselves using Pd-based brazes Palco (65% Pd-35% Co) and Palni (60% Pd-40% Ni). Hair line cracks, substantial chemical interaction and interfacial cracking due to residual stresses were observed.

4.8. Near-net-shape colloidal processing
In the case of aqueous suspension of ZrB2, which was processed via freeze form of extrusion rapid proto typing, a maximum density of ~96% ρth was obtained after PS at 2000 °C [130]. Medri et al. [131] developed ZrB2-SiC-Si3N4 composites via aqueous slip casting route and achieved sinter-density of 98%ρth upon sintering at 2150 °C. In a different work, He et al. [132] investigated the mechanical properties of ZrB2-SiC-B4C-C ceramics, processed via gel casting technique and pressureless sintering, and reported a flexural strength of ~405 MPa and a fracture toughness of ~4.3 MPam 1/2 . Using this route, they could successfully produce complex shaped components of ZrB2-SiC ceramic composites, possessing homogeneous microstructure. In another work, He et al. [133] prepared HfB2-20 vol% MoSi2 composites through aqueous gel-casting and measured better properties of ceramics, when solid loading was 45%. For such materials, the highest green strength, flexural strength and fracture toughness of ~39 MPa, ~416 MPa and ~4.2 MPam 1/2 , respectively were obtained. Xiang et al. [203] prepared ZrB2-30 vol% SiC/BN using tape casting, followed by hot pressing at 1950 °C for 1 h. The flexural strength of this composite was lower when compared to ZrB2-30 vol% SiC due to the presence of BN interlayer.

3.3. Gel-Casting
Yin et al. [48] and He et al. [76-79] made a great deal of effort in the fabrication of ZrB2-based ceramics from aqueous suspensions using the aqueous gel-casting technique. Especially, He et al. [76,77] obtained complex-shaped ZrB2-SiC ultra-high-temperature ceramic components, as shown in Figure 7. It was found that the gel-casted ZrB2-SiC ceramic components had a homogenous microstructure and near-net shape in dimension. The traditional gel-casting procedure is usually based on the acrylamide [C2H3CONH2] (AM) and N,N0-methylenebisacrylamide [(C2H3CONH)2CH2] (MBAM) (AM-MBAM) system or the Na-alginate system, etc. In order to further improve the green density and green machinability of ZrB2-SiC ceramic components, He et al. [80] also developed a novel double-gel network-based gel-casting technique. In addition, ZrB2 and ZrB2-SiC ultra-hightemperature ceramic foams [81] and HfB2-SiC ultra-high-temperature ceramic [82] were also fabricated using the gel-casting method.
Figure 7. ZrB2-SiC ultra-high-temperature ceramic components. Reproduced with permission from [77].
Moreover, other casting techniques, such as freeze-casting [83] and freeze-form extrusion fabrication [43], have also gained great attention recently. However, these casting techniques have not been widely used for the colloidal fabrication of UHTCs.

3.1. Slip-Casting
During slip-casting, ceramic particles were consolidated into sophisticated shapes with high green density. Currently, it is used mainly for the production of large components or for those having these walls or a shell of complicated contours. Medri et al. [45,46] set up slipcasting of concentrated aqueous ZrB2-SiC composite suspensions as a forming technique for the production of UHTC crucibles. In this study, the effects of two commercial ammonium polyacrylates dispersants (Duramax D3005 and Dolapix PC33) on the dispersion and the final microstructures were investigated. ZrB2-SiC crucibles with an almost homogeneous microstructure and lower final porosity were finally prepared through slip-casting, as shown in Figure 4.
Figure 4. ZrB2-SiC ultra-high-temperature ceramic crucibles prepared by slip-casting. Reproduced with permission from [46].
However, on one hand, the main disadvantage of this process is that it is time-intensive, with the speed of casting ranging from hours to days for large objects. Besides, there is little difference in microstructure near and far away from the plaster mold, owing to the difference of adsorption forces in different distances from the plaster mold. This difference finally results in density differences over the ceramic part. On the other hand, the slipcasting technique cannot produce net or near-net and complex-shaped materials. The dimensional accuracy of the slip-casted product is still relatively low.

4.1. Carbon continuous fiber reinforcement
In terms of the different manufacturing methods, composites formed by some combination of CVI, PIP, and SI showed comparable flexural strengths,  where  values  generally  fell  in  the  100 -300  MPa  regime regardless of fiber type or matrix composition (Table 4). By comparison, Sciti et al. [195] reported flexural strengths below 100 MPa for a Cf/ZrB2 composite  when  following  the  slurry  infiltration process with  a hot-pressing  cycle.  These  lower  values  can  be  attributed  to  fiber degradation  during  the  high  temperature  conditions  of  the  sintering environment, which is the same observation noted for sintered ceramics containing short fibers. Meanwhile, a Cf/ZrB2 -ZrC -SiC composite produced by RMI showed flexure strengths approaching 400 MPa, likely due to the material ' s  higher  density  (~90  %)  relative  to  other  composites reported at the time [196]. It should be reminded that flexural strength is not always an ideal metric to evaluate a material ' s flexibility, as  the  full  load-displacement  curve  is  required  to  determine  if  the composite ' s failure behavior is brittle or graceful. For instance, monolithic ceramics will often have high flexural strengths, even though they experience  brittle  failure.  That  said,  flexural  strength  is  the  most commonly reported mechanical property for UHTCMCs, and will still provide  a  useful  comparison  in  the  absence  of  results  for  fracture toughness or work of fracture.
Another  group  of  studies  re-evaluated  the  application  of  a  PyC coating for carbon fibers specifically in Cf/UHTC composites [197 -201].

1. Introduction
Characteristics of ZrB2-SiC ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) reinforced with ZrC and carbon fiber (Cf) were investigated in this article. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) process was utilized to fabricate the samples at 1800 °C for 5 min under 30 MPa punch pressure and vacuumed atmosphere. In all samples, the volume ratio of ZrB2: SiC was equal to 4:1, and the summation of ZrC and Cf reinforcements was 7.5 vol% with different ZrC: Cf ratios. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), densitometry, flexural strength, and hardness measurements were employed for characterization of the prepared samples. Microstructural inspection revealed the formation of SiC sheath around the carbon fibers due to several reactions in the surface SiO2 layers existed on the SiC particles. Optimal flexural strength (628.4 MPa) and hardness (20.8 GPa) values were achieved for the sample co-reinforced with 6.5 vol% ZrC and 1 vol% Cf, with a relative density of 97.7%.
enhanced relative density and fracture toughness have been reported for ZrB2-SiC composites by adding 10-30 vol% carbon fiber, the hardness and flexural strength have dropped [19,20]. Adding ZrC can be a good trick for compensating such a drop in hardness and flexural strength. Adding ZrC to ZrB2-SiC composites, individually, has also been investigated by several researchers [21-25]. Table 1 summarizes the average values of relative density and mechanical properties for some of ZrB2-based ceramics containing SiC, ZrC and/or Cf additives.

4. Discussion
There are hardly any reports in the literature on the mechanical properties of UHTC-based composites, but the few results available describe similar behaviour to that observed here. With respect to flexural strength, previous work [7] for ZrB2-20 vol.% SiC reinforced with SCS-9a SiC fibres described cracks passing through the thickness during testing, resulting in composite delamination. Similar flexural strength values, 130 MPa, were obtained as reported in this work even though the composite composition involved was quite different. A different study [31] involved a Cf / ZrB2-SiC composite made by impregnating a ZrB2 powder slurry into 2D Cf preforms, with the remaining porosity being filled with SiC by CVI. Unfortunately, the ZrB2 powder seems to have blocked the channels for the CVI process leading to interfacial debonding. This also resulted in a lower interlaminar shear strength than that obtained in the current work.
All of the four different mechanical tests undertaken yielded similar results; there was a relatively high degree of scatter in the data, which is not unusual for ceramic matrix composites, and the 0° orientation fibre layers were consistently shown to yield higher strengths than the 90° orientation fibre layers. With respect to the latter, it was clearly shown that the more of the 0° and fewer of the 90° layers that were present, the better the samples performed mechanically. The random layers, however, were the layers that absorbed the majority of the HfB2 powder due to their higher porosity and larger pores; these are the layers, therefore, that will impart the greatest oxidation and ablation protection. These factors will allow future 2.5D-based Cf-HfB2-Cm composites to be designed so that the performance is enhanced.

These CFCMCs have high fracture toughness ( > 20 MPa m 1/2 [10]) and thermal  shock  resistance  ( Δ Tc > 1000 0 C  [11,12]).  As  a  result,  the development of an ultra high temperature ceramic matrix composite (UHTCMCs) was initiated in the very recent past [13 -22]. Early development of UHTCMCs was carried out through a combined process of slurry  infiltration  and  chemical  vapour  infiltration  (CVI)  routes,  but these studies are very limited [13 -15]. Tang et al. [13] fabricated carbon fibre (Cf) reinforced ZrB2 -SiC matrix composite having the fibre and ZrB2 volume content of 24.9 and 3.9 %, respectively and introduced SiC matrix  through  heaterless  chemical  vapour  infiltration  (HCVI)  route. The reported density of the composite was 2.4 ± 1 g/cm3 and exhibited a flexural strength of 148 MPa with fracture toughness of 5.6 MPa m 1/2 and showed good oxidation and ablation resistemace [13]. Wang et al. [14] fabricated 2D Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composite having the carbon fibre volume fraction of 40% through chemical vapour infiltration (CVI) process combined with slurry paste (SP) method. The ZrB2  matrix was introduced using ZrB2-polycarbosilane slurry by slurry paste (SP) method and filled the open porosity with SiC through CVI route. The reported bulk density of the composite was 2.1 g/cm 3 and the flexural strength of the composite was 237 ± 30 MPa and exhibited a good ablation resistance

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  of  interest  for  thermally-  and/or  mechanically-  extreme  environments because of their high melting temperatures ( > 3000 ◦ C) and ablation resistance. More widespread use is limited by low fracture toughness and inability to be processed into complex-shaped components. Here, we report  the  production  of  fiber-reinforced  UHTC  matrix  composites  (UHTCMCs)  formed  via  the  additive manufacturing technique of direct ink writing (DIW). Slurry ' inks ' were developed containing up to 47.5 vol% of the UHTC zirconium diboride (ZrB2), up to 10 vol% chopped silicon carbide fiber (SiCf), and a silicon carbide (SiC) precursor polymer. Lattice structures and flexural specimens were printed and pyrolyzed to form UHTCMCs with aligned (relative to the print direction) SiCf  in the ZrB2 -SiC matrix. Flexural strength of fiber-containing parts is presented, and fiber alignment due to deposition is analyzed with X-ray computed tomography. Defects that occurred during the DIW process, and their probable causes and mitigation strategies are also discussed.

question: How do the ablation products formed on Ti2AlC ceramics under oxyacetylene flame exposure in Song et al. 2011 compare in stability to the oxide layers that form on hot-pressed ZrB2-SiC ceramics under plasma exposure above 1550 degrees C in Alfano et al. 2010?
answer: The sources provided do not contain information about the stability of ablation products formed on Ti2AlC ceramics under oxyacetylene flame exposure compared to oxide layers that form on hot-pressed ZrB2-SiC ceramics under plasma exposure above 1550 degrees C in Alfano et al. 2010. Therefore, a comparison cannot be made based on the given sources.

Abstract
The linear and mass ablation rates of Ti2AlC ceramics under an oxyacetylene flame at a temperature up to 3000 ◦ Cwere examined by measuring the dimensions and weight change of the ablated samples. The linear ablation rate was decreased from 0.14 𝛍 ms -1 for the first 30 s of the ablation to 0.08 𝛍 ms -1 after 180 s. Ti2AlC ceramics gained small amounts of weight upon ablation, which is attributed to the formation of oxidation products on the ablated surface. The ablation surface exhibits a two-layer structure: an oxide outer layer, consisting mainly of 𝛂 -Al2O3 and TiO2 and some Al2TiO5, and a porous sub-surface layer containing Ti2Al1 -x C and TiC x O y . With increasing ablation time, the content of TiO2 and Al2TiO5 in the outer layer increased, and more pores developed in the sub-surface layer. The thermal oxidation of Ti2AlC under the flame and scouring of the viscous oxidation products by high-speed flow of gas torch are the main ablation mechanisms.

The central region was the core of oxyacetylene flame with a temperature close to 3273 K, exhibiting more severe loss on the four sample surfaces, as shown in Fig. 16 (g). According to the ablation results (Fig. 16(h)), it was obvious that ZrB2  coating can improve the ablation resistance of C/SiC composites. During the ablation process, ZrB2  and SiC were oxidized to ZrO2  and SiO2, forming four layers: (i) an inner SiO2  layer, (ii) a thin discontinuous porous SiO2 layer, (iii) a dense ZrO2 -SiO2 layer, and (iv) a top ZrO2 layer. These layers served as thermal barriers, offering protective against further ablations of the inner coating, and the schematic diagram of ZrB2 coatings ablation behavior is exhibited in Fig. 16(i). Wang et al. [159] tested  the  CVD-ZrB2 coating  using  a  high-throughput  solar  beam. Compared with other ceramics, the CVD-ZrB2  coating exhibited good thermal  shock  resistance,  and  proved  particularly  effective  in  atmospheric pressure up to 1100 ◦ C.

3.3. Ablation mechanisms
Although the oxyacetylene flame temperature is up to about 3000 ◦ C based on the test condition, 10 the temperature of the ablated surface of a disk will be lower due to heat transfer and dynamic thermal balance existing among the flame, the sample andtheenvironment.Normally,thesurfacetemperatureiswithin the range of 1800-3000 ◦ C depending on the ablated materials and the oxidation products formed on the ablation surfaces, as confirmed by the temperature tests in the prior studies by ourselves 12,16,17 and others. 20,32 The Al2O3, TiO2 and Al2TiO5 at the ablated surface will melt, but do not totally evaporate due to their relative high melting points (2053 ◦ Cfor Al2O3, 1843 ◦ C for TiO2 and 1850 ◦ C for Al2TiO5). 7 Due to the existence of an octahedral Ti2C sub-lattice in the Ti2AlC lattice, the melting points of Ti2AlC (or Ti2Al1 -x C) and the subsequently formed TiC0.5 twins may be very close to that of TiC ( ∼ 3250 ◦ C), 7 which enables Ti2AlC to withstand the severe ablation at ultrahigh temperatures. The molten TiO2 and Al2TiO5 together with the Al2O3 layer is postulated to form a viscous layer limiting the evaporation of CO or/and CO2, which might result in a volume swelling that in turn leads to the low values of linear ablation rate measured.

3.3. Ablation mechanisms
During the ablation of the Ti2AlC under a fast flowing oxyacetylene flame, a series of complicated physical and chemical processes take place between the ultra-high temperature oxidative gasses and Ti2AlC. The oxidation products Al2O3, TiO2 and Al2TiO5 comprise the ablation oxide layer. The weight gain upon ablation is attributed to oxidation-induced weight increase, which can be understood via the oxidation Eqs. (4)-(9). With one molar C loss, several molars oxygen is taken up via the formation of solid oxide and oxygen incorporation into the Ti2AlC matrix to formTiC x O y or/and Ti2Al1 -x C y O z (( y + z ) ≤ 1).
