question: How does the room-temperature-to-1800°C flexural strength trend of Demirskyi et al. (2017) TiB2–10 wt.% boron ceramic composites compare with the compressive-to-flexural strength degradation trend of Yan et al. (2025) C/C–ZrC–SiC (CZS) composites processed at 1800–2100 °C?
answer: The sources do not contain information about the compressive-to-flexural strength degradation trend of Yan et al. (2025) C/C–ZrC–SiC (CZS) composites processed at 1800–2100 °C. Therefore, a comparison cannot be made.

chunks.text:
8.3. High temperature strength
The effects of temperature on the flexural strength of various di-borides with or without sinter-additives addition are shown in Fig. 18. A general observation is that ZrB2 and HfB2 ceramics exhibit better strength properties. More importantly, it is to be noted here that monolithic TiB2 and also TiB2 with small amount of sinter-additive show increasing strength with temperature (up to 1000 °C) (See Fig. 18a) [45,100,328,329]. Though the exact reasoning is still under debate, it is possible that the residual stress gets relieved as a result of temperature increase during the elevated temperature tests. This, in turn increased the grain boundary strength and hence the flexural strength of the di-boride-based ceramic/composites. However, upon incorporation of larger amount of sinteradditive (i.e. 10 wt% MoSi2) to TiB2, the strength decreased considerably at 1000 °C. As shown in Fig. 19, it is evident that with the increase in sinter-additive content and test temperature, the mode of fracture for TiB2 changed from predominantly transgranular to intergranular. Additionally, an increased fraction of grain pull-outs is noticeable for TiB2-10 wt% MoSi2 when compared to the other TiB2 ceramics. Demirskyi et al. [171] reported high flexural strength of 910 MPa at room temperature and, interestingly, further increase to 1105 MPa with temperature up to 1600 °C. Nevertheless, the strength decreased rapidly to 620 MPa with further increase in temperature to 1800 °C for TiB2-10 wt% B (developed via reactive SPS). It was proposed that the presence of amorphous boron improved the strength to fracture, in addition to plastic deformation and stress relief of material at high temperature. In a recent work, Demirskyi et al. [211] revealed that the strength of SPS processed TiB2 could be improved with the addition of NbB2 (up to 50 wt%) even up to 1800 °C in Ar environment.

8.3. High temperature strength
These studies demonstrates the potential of TiB2 composites at elevated temperatures under protective environments.
Although reports on the strength retention at the real ultra-high temperatures (i.e. > 2000 °C) are sparse, a careful comparison of the reported data reveals that ZrB2 tends to exhibit fairly good strength properties even at such ultra-high temperatures (~700 MPa at RT and ~260 MPa at 2200 °C) (see Fig. 18b) [138,333-335]. Typically, the ZrB2 ceramics are tested in argon atmosphere, while measuring the strengths at elevated temperatures (Fig. 18b) [330]. Recently, Silvestroni et al. [333] were successful towards developing ZrB2 ceramics that could retain flexural strength of > 800 MPa at high temperatures (1500-2100 °C). The achievement of such considerably higher strength at even such elevated temperatures was attributed to core-shell microstructure and in-situ toughening.
The effects of elevated temperatures on the flexural strength properties of ZrB2 ceramics processed via different routes are presented in Table 10. It is interesting to note that hot pressed ZrB2 ceramics show better room temperature strength in comparison to other processes. However, the pressureless sintered samples show better high temperature mechanical properties and could almost retain room temperature strength. The decrease in strength of ZrB2 samples (both HP and SPS) at elevated temperatures was attributed to the presence of micro-cracking and soft secondary phases. Neuman et al. [330] successfully measured the flexural strength of ZrB2 up to very high temperatures of ~2300 °C under atmosphere control. The flexural strength of ZrB2 reportedly varied between ~400-176 MPa, when tested in air between room temperature and 1600 °C. However, the strength shows relatively marginal decrease (i.e. varying between ~300 and 173 MPa) when the flexural tests were carried out at higher temperatures of up to 2300 °C in argon atmosphere.

4.2.2. Mechanical behavior
The flexural strength of hot pressed ZrB2 tested between room temperature and 1600 ◦ C showed a value of 360 MPa at room temperature decreasing to a minimum of 170 MPa. The same ceramic when tested in Ar  between  1500 ◦ C  and  2300 ◦ C  raised  its  strength  to  300  MPa  at 2200 ◦ C, which then lowered down to 220 MPa at 2300 ◦ C [151]. A historic study by Rhodes [143] demonstrated the increase in bending strength  of  ZrB2  from  325  MPa  at  room  temperature  to  420  MPa  at 800 ◦ C. Further, increase in temperature reduced the strength to 145 MPa at 1400 ◦ C.  Bend strength again raised to 200 MPa at 1900 ◦ C followed by a drop at 2200 ◦ C to 50 MPa. The initial rise in bend strength was ascribed to the thermal residual stresses relieving at higher temperatures. The second rise in bend strength was attributed to the plastic flow from creep, again relieving the stresses [143]. ZrB2  ceramic composite (with 5 vol % WC + 3 vol % SiC) developed by Silvestroni et al. [152] retained a flexural strength of 836 MPa at 1800 ◦ C. The elevated temperature bend strength of some ZrB2  and HfB2  based ceramics is illustrated  in  Fig.  18.  Particulate  reinforced  di-borides  especially  SiC
reinforced composites display improved fracture toughness up to ~6 MPam 1/2 , hardness ~26 GPa and flexural strength ~1000 MPa [136] (while most others have exhibited flexural strength of ~650 MPa), while retaining flexural strength at high temperatures (~850 MPa at 1500 ◦ C).

a b s t r a c t
For the sake of reliability and safety of high temperature structural components of future hypersonic aircraft or re-entry vehicles, the strength of ZrB2-based composites at elevated temperatures is an important factor for applications in extreme environments. However, the study of high temperature properties has been mostly limited to oxidation behavior, an area which is also well understood [7,15-17]. Very few studies focus on high temperature mechanical properties [18-21]. Neuman et al. reported the flexure strength of ZrB2 with 0.5 wt% carbon to be 216 MPa at 2300 /C176 C in argon [18]. Bellosi et al. [19] fabricated ZrB2-10 vol% SiC ceramics with or without ZrC additions by using spark plasma sintering (SPS) and studied the strengths at 1500 /C176 C in argon or air atmosphere. The flexural strength of the ZrB2-10 vol% SiC sample tested at 1500 /C176 C did not decrease appreciably compared with the values at room temperature. The favorable effects were mainly attributed to the clean grain boundaries in the as-SPSed samples. Zou et al. [20] reported the high-temperature bending strength, internal friction, and stiffness of ZrB2-20 vol% SiC ceramics up to 1600 /C176 C in high purity argon. The high temperature bend strength of ZS ceramics started to decrease above 1000 /C176 C, whereas E -modulus decreased with the increase of the temperature. The bend strength at 1600 /C176 C was 460 ± 31 MPa, and E -modulus at 1300 /C176 C was 443.5 GPa. The corresponding strength and E modulus retention was 84% and 88%, respectively. Hu and Wang [21] reported that the flexural strength and fracture behavior of the ZrB2-SiC composites at ultra-high temperature significantly depends on the grain size and SiC content. The flexural strength of the ZrB2-15 vol%

4. Conclusion
Thermal shock resistance of the Zr-Si alloyed melt in /uniFB01 ltrated C/CZrC composite was tested using an induction heating equipment from ultra high temperatures (particularly > 1500 °C) to the room temperature. Both strength degradation and microstructure evolution were characterized in order to reveal the thermal shock induced damage to the composite. The following conclusions are obtained:
- (1) Flexural strength of the post thermal shocked C/C-ZrC composite slightly decreases initially without weight loss below 1300 °C, while it decreases with relatively high values and then sharply decreases over 1900 °C with obvious increase of weight loss.
- (2) Flexural strength of the post thermal shocked composites is dependence of thermal cycles. Flexural strength decreases gradually with the increasing number of thermal shocked cycles without obvious weight loss from 1700 °C to the room temperature.
- (3) Matrix cracking, interfacial debonding and matrix pores are the main reasons for the strength degradation of the composite thermally shocked for di /uniFB00 erent cycles and temperatures.

question: How does the simple manufacturing route for TiB2–B4C ceramic composites proposed by Demirskyi et al. (2017) contrast with the reactive melt infiltration (RMI) route used by Yan et al. (2025) to fabricate C/C–ZrC–SiC (CZS) composites?
answer: The simple manufacturing route for TiB2–B4C ceramic composites proposed by Demirskyi et al. (2017) involves the use of spark plasma sintering (SPS) with commercially available raw powders of TiB2 and amorphous boron, allowing for the in-situ formation of the B4C phase during reaction-driven consolidation at 1900 ◦ C [4]. In contrast, the reactive melt infiltration (RMI) route used by Yan et al. (2025) to fabricate C/C–ZrC–SiC (CZS) composites involves the infiltration of a Zr-Si alloy melt into a porous carbon preform under controlled vacuum conditions, followed by in-situ carbothermal reactions between the metallic melt and the carbon substrate to form ZrC and SiC phases [2]. The RMI process is noted for its rapid processing time and low cost, and it can achieve near-net shape manufacturing [1]. However, the RMI process requires high reaction temperatures, generally above 1800 ◦ C, which can lead to coupled damage mechanisms such as thermal damage to the fibers and damage induced by melt penetration [2]. The SPS method used by Demirskyi et al. does not involve such high-temperature infiltration processes and instead relies on the reaction during sintering to form the composite material.

chunks.text:
Reactive Melt Infiltration of Carbon Fiber Reinforced Ceramic Composites
CVI, PIP and RMI with different advantages are the main fabrication route for ceramic matrix composite. The combination of different fabrication route is beneficial to improve the composite performances, shorten the fabrication period and reduce the cost.
RMI can achieve near-net shape manufacturing with quick processing time and low cost. The present investigations mainly focus on the fabrication process of simple shaped materials and property evaluation of the as-produced materials. Complex shaped components with excellent ablation resistance and high strength are needed in the future applications. Efforts should be made to fabricate these complex shaped components with excellent properties by RMI.
In summary, RMI is a promising fabrication route for ultra high temperature ceramic matrix composite. Further efforts should be made to perfect the RMI process. It is believed that RMI can emerge as a low cost process to provide high performance C/UHTC components for ultra high temperature applications in the future.

A B S T R A C T
The fabrication of ZrC-based ceramic modified C/C composites is predominantly achieved through the following methods: Reactive Melt Infiltration  (RMI),  Precursor  Infiltration  and  Pyrolysis  (PIP),  and Chemical Vapor Infiltration (CVI). In industrial practice, RMI is widely used  in  manufacturing  UHTCs-C/C  composites,  driven  by  its  dual capability to maintain dimensional precision in net-shape forming and production  cost  minimization.  A  Zr -Si  alloy  melt  is  utilized  as  the infiltration  medium, whereby the molten precursor is infiltrated into porous  carbon  preforms  under  controlled  vacuum  conditions.  Subsequent  in-situ  carbothermal  reactions  between  the  metallic  melt  and carbon substrate are initiated, resulting in the simultaneous formation of ZrC  and  SiC  phases,  ultimately  yielding  C/C -ZrC -SiC  composites [16 -21].  However,  in  silicon-containing  systems,  the  decomposition temperature  of  the  melt  infiltrant  during  RMI  is  typically  high, exceeding 1600 ◦ C. Concurrently, to ensure sufficient content of UHTCs, which necessitates the complete reaction between the melt infiltrant and carbon, the reaction temperature  is  generally  maintained  above 1800 ◦ C. This process inevitably leads to coupled damage mechanisms: thermal damage to the fibers and damage induced by melt penetration [22,23].  Furthermore,  the  mechanical  failure  modes  of  UHTCs-C/C composites  are  significantly  influenced  by  fiber  damage  and  anisotropy in ceramic phase distribution. The failure mechanism is typically characterized by multiphase damage involving interfaces, fibers, and ceramic phases, ultimately leading to structural failure [24 -26]. However, over an extended period of research, the investigation into mechanical failure mechanisms of UHTCs-C/C composites has predominantly relied on optical analysis techniques such as scanning electron  microscopy  (SEM),  while  the  incorporation  of  digital  image correlation (DIC) analysis methods has not yet been implemented. As early  as  2013,  Yang  et  al.  [27]  fabricated

7.2.3  Reactive Melt Infiltration
Reactive melt infiltration (RMI) is a rapid and low-cost manufacturing process widely used for the manufacture of Cf/SiC structural components. The driving force behind infiltration is capillary force and the whole process is finished within minutes to a few hours. RMI can be used effectively when one of the ceramic matrix elements possesses a relatively low melting temperature and readily wets the fibers [91]. As mentioned already,  UHTC materials are characterized by very high melting temperatures and, hence, it is extremely difficult to use them for liquid or melt infiltration because of the high temperature requirements and the reactions of the melt with the fibers at the infiltration temperatures. The process, in general, also leaves residual metal that could melt at  higher  temperatures and enhance creep or attack constituent phases. In a typical RMI process for the preparation of Cf-ZrC UHTC composite, the Cf is coated with a suitable interfacial material. The coated fibers are then woven into a 2D fabric preform, which is subsequently deposited with C to form a C/C skeleton. Molten Zr is then  infiltrated  into  the  porous  skeleton  using  a  wicking  action.  The  molten  metal reacts with the previously deposited C to form a ZrC matrix. Detailed microstructural analysis  of  the  matrix  phases  indicated  the  presence  of  ZrC  along  with α -Zr+ZrC eutectic. Some of the ZrC grains contained nanosized α -Zr and/or α -Zr-ZrC eutectic inclusions (Fig. 7.1) [91].

a b s t r a c t
Spark plasma sintering of TiB2-boron ceramics using commercially available raw powders is reported. The B4C phase developed during reaction-driven consolidation at 1900 ◦ C. The newly formed grains were located at the grain junctions and the triple point of TiB2 grains, forming a covalent and stiff skeleton of B4C. The flexural strength of the TiB2-10 wt.% boron ceramic composites reached 910 MPa at room temperature and 1105 MPa at 1600 ◦ C. Which is the highest strength reported for non-oxide ceramics at 1600 ◦ C. This was followed by a rapid decrease at 1800 ◦ C to 480-620 MPa, which was confirmed by increased number of cavitated titanium diboride grains observed after flexural strength tests.
© 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
This class of TiB2-based composites is naturally restricted to TiB2-AlN [8,9], TiB2-SiC [10,11] and B4C-TiB2 [12-17] ceramic composites, which show high room-temperature strength and toughness. High-temperature performance of these TiB2-based composite is scarcely reported and is limited to the B4C-TiB2 eutectic composites [14-17].
The B4C-TiB2 eutectic composites are known to have a complex preparation route, and have a certain size limitation. Therefore, it was an objective of this study to propose a simple route for manufacturing TiB2-B4C composites, that is by the reaction spark plasma sintering [18-20] using commercially available powders of TiB2 and amorphous boron. A reactive consolidation approach was used in order to ensure homogeneous distribution of secondary phase, which is formed in situ during spark plasma sintering. In the framework of this study, characterization of high-temperature mechanical properties was performed using the three-point flexural strength tests at temperatures between 25 ◦ C and 1800 ◦ C.

KEYWORDS
hot pressing, infiltration, mechanical properties, slurry
of ceramics and makes them damage-tolerant. 5 Damagetolerant continuous fiber-reinforced ceramic matrix composites (CFCMCs), such as C/C, C/SiC, and SiC/SiC, have found applications as high-temperature structural components. 6-9 Continuous fiber-reinforced ZrB2 -based UHTC matrix composites (UHTCMCs) are being developed in the same line as CFCMCs. UHTCMCs have been also processed in the same way as conventional ceramic matrix composites (CMCs). 10,11 CMCsaremostlyproduced by using four different techniques: polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP), reactive melt infiltration (RMI), chemical vapor infiltration (CVI), and the ceramic route. 6 UHTCMC early development is mostly based on a hybrid process (a combination of two processes). For example, Tang et al. 12 and Wang et al. 13 developed Cf -ZrB 2-SiC composite using a hybrid process that included slurry infiltration followed by CVI. A Cf-ZrB2-SiC composite was also fabricated using a hybrid process that included PIP and slurry impregnation. 14-16 These are incremental advancements of a C f -SiC composite with ZrB 2 added via slurry impregnation. The RMI process is also wieldy used for the fabrication of UHTCMCs. 17-19 After adapting the ceramic route of processing of CMC, substantial progress in the processing of UHTCMCs has been made. The ceramic route consists of a slurry infiltration followed by densification through high-temperature sintering (both pressureless and pressure-assisted). The classic examples of using ceramic route processed composites are oxide-oxide CMCs 20 and nano-infiltration and transient eutectoid process for SiC-based CMCs. 21 Recently, huge developments for the processing of UHTCMCs have been carried out worldwide using a similar ceramic route. 22-27 However, in these studies, unidirectional carbon fabric were used.

question: What high-temperature application challenge motivates the material design in both Demirskyi et al. (2017) TiB2-based ceramics and Yan et al. (2025) UHTCs-C/C composites?
answer: The high-temperature application challenge that motivates the material design in both Demirskyi et al. (2017) TiB2-based ceramics and Yan et al. (2025) UHTCs-C/C composites is the need to maintain mechanical integrity and oxidation resistance in oxygen-rich environments at elevated temperatures. Specifically, the challenge involves preserving the structural integrity of materials under high-temperature conditions, where oxidation and thermal degradation can significantly reduce the performance of the materials [2][3].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
To  overcome  this  challenge,  the  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics (UHTCs)  have  been  strategically  integrated  into  the  C/C  composites through prior research initiatives. UHTCs are defined as a class of refractory compounds comprising carbides and borides such as HfC [7], ZrC [8], TaC [9], HfB2 [10,11], and ZrB2 [12], which are recognized for their synergistic combination of ultra-high melting points, exceptional mechanical  strength,  and  enhanced  oxidation  resistance  at  elevated temperatures. It has been demonstrated that the incorporation of UHTCs into  the  matrix  architecture  can  effectively  preserve  the  intrinsic high-temperature mechanical integrity of C/C composites when exposed to  oxygen-rich  atmospheres.  Particular  attention  has  been  directed Z. Yan et al.
toward ZrC-based ceramics, which have been extensively investigated owing to their balanced performance in ablative protective capability and cost-effectiveness [13 -15].

1. Introduction
Titanium diboride (TiB2) is an important ceramic material which is extensively used as a cutting tool material, wear components and lightweight armour for ballistic protection. High elastic modulus and good strength at room temperature is often reported for monolithic TiB2 and TiB2-based ceramic composites [1-6]. In view of its refractory nature, high temperature applications are also within reach. In the latter case the significant decrease of strength after 1400 ◦ C is expected due to activation of the high-temperature plastic deformation mechanisms such as grain-boundary sliding and creep [6].
An obvious solution to this problem is the consolidation of composites [6-14]. TiB2-TaC composites have demonstrated a flexural strength of 480 MPa at 1600 ◦ C, which is twice of that for monolithic TiB2 ceramics [6]. It was summarized that even in TiB2-TaC case a decrease in strength is expected at higher temperatures owing to electro-covalent nature of bonding in titanium diboride. Therefore in order to reach higher strength values TiB2-based ceramics armored with covalent compounds is proposed.

1. Introduction
Nowadays, a critical area of research revolves around the exploration of materials  capable of resisting extreme  conditions. Ultra-hightemperature ceramics (UHTCs) represent a class of refractory ceramics encompassing carbides and borides of early transition metals, characterized by a melting point above 3000 ◦ C [1,2]. These materials exhibit a high  thermal  conductivity  and  exceptional  resistance  to  ablation  in harsh environments. The most studied UHTCs are based on zirconium diboride  for  the  relatively  low  density,  elevated  melting  point,  high strength, and good electrical and thermal conductivity [3,4]. While ZrB2 benefits from these properties, it also suffers from poor fracture toughness  constraining  its  application  in  scenarios  involving  mechanical stresses or thermal shock [5]. To enhance the damage tolerance of the UHTCs, reinforcement with short or long fibres have been used [6 -9], resulting in the development of ultra-high-temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs). A key challenge for UHTCMCs is oxidation at high  temperatures,  particularly  in  oxygen-rich  environments.  The oxidation of ZrB ₂ -based UHTCMCs occurs through the formation of ZrO ₂ and B ₂ O ₃ , where the latter can volatilize at temperatures above 1200 ◦ C, reducing the protective effect of the oxide layer [10]. The addition of different  species  such  as  SiC  as  a  secondary  phase  is  commonly employed to improve oxidation resistance by forming a viscous borosilicate glass with a low vapour pressure, which helps to seal the surface

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are a family of transition-metal composites that have melting points higher than 3000 /C176 C, such as ZrB2, ZrC, HfB2, HfC, HfN and TaC. The UHTCs have an excellent combination of mechanical, physical, chemical stability, high electrical and thermal conductivities and oxidation resistance properties, which can be used in the high temperature and oxidizing environments [1-4]. These unique properties make them attractive candidates for use in a variety of high-temperature structural applications, such as rocket propulsion, furnace elements, hypersonic aircraft, reusable launch vehicles, or rocket engines and thermal protection structures for leading edge parts on hypersonic re-entry space vehicles [5-8].
Considering that the UHTCs suffer from high temperature and rapid complex temperature change in high-temperature applications, the thermal shock resistance and strength of materials at high temperatures are the critical factors. The thermal shock resistance and the fracture strength of the UHTCs under severe environments are considered as two of the primary issues in the design process of thermal protection systems. Owing to the inherent brittleness of ceramic, low toughness induced thermal shock resis-

1.1. General
Ultra-High Temperature Ceramic Matrix Composites (UHTCMCs) are a relatively new class of materials designed to withstand extreme thermal  environments,  particularly  in  aerospace,  nuclear,  and  miningrelated applications. They have been developed to overcome the limitations of traditional ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which, while resistant to high temperatures, are brittle and have low thermal shock resistance [1 -5].
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) generally consist of transition metal carbides, nitrides, and borides, with Group IV compounds (Ti, Zr, Hf) and TaC being the primary focus due to their exceptional melting points above 2000 ◦ C and stable oxides formed in situ at high temperatures  [1 -3].  These  materials  are  particularly  valued  in  environments where temperatures surpass 2000 ◦ C, intense heat fluxes, and highly  reactive  dissociated  gases  [6]. 8.3. High temperature strength
The effects of temperature on the flexural strength of various di-borides with or without sinter-additives addition are shown in Fig. 18. A general observation is that ZrB2 and HfB2 ceramics exhibit better strength properties. More importantly, it is to be noted here that monolithic TiB2 and also TiB2 with small amount of sinter-additive show increasing strength with temperature (up to 1000 °C) (See Fig. 18a) [45,100,328,329]. [171] reported high flexural strength of 910 MPa at room temperature and, interestingly, further increase to 1105 MPa with temperature up to 1600 °C. 1. Introduction
Titanium diboride (TiB2) is an important ceramic material which is extensively used as a cutting tool material, wear components and lightweight armour for ballistic protection. High elastic modulus and good strength at room temperature is often reported for monolithic TiB2 and TiB2-based ceramic composites [1-6]. In view of its refractory nature, high temperature applications are also within reach. In the latter case the significant decrease of strength after 1400 ◦ C is expected due to activation of the high-temperature plastic deformation mechanisms such as grain-boundary sliding and creep [6].
An obvious solution to this problem is the consolidation of composites [6-14]. TiB2-TaC composites have demonstrated a flexural strength of 480 MPa at 1600 ◦ C, which is twice of that for monolithic TiB2 ceramics [6]. It was summarized that even in TiB2-TaC case a decrease in strength is expected at higher temperatures owing to electro-covalent nature of bonding in titanium diboride. Therefore in order to reach higher strength values TiB2-based ceramics armored with covalent compounds is proposed.

3. Results and discussion
TiB2-boron ceramics show good flexural strength at room temperature ( 𝛔 25 ◦ C) ranging from 740 to 910 MPa, with a mean value among six tested specimens of 820 MPa, (Fig. 1) [3-6,12-14,21,22]. Fig. 1 shows data for three-point (open figures) and four-point (closed figures) configurations obtained for TiB2, B4C and TiB2based composites at room and elevated temperatures. It is known that the difference in configuration for threeand four-point methods does not allow comparing them directly. In this respect data for four-point method presented in Fig. 1 show general trends observed in original studies. Furthermore, in order to verify high values obtained by the three-point method, we also conducted some reference tests using the four-point method (using a 20/10 mm span of the supports and specimens with size 2 × 2.5 × 26 mm) using four specimens. The mean value obtained was 829 ± 17 MPa. These data points are not presented in Fig. 1. When comparing two testing methods, we should note that, since only six samples were tested using the three-point set-up, it is clear that the difference in strength can be treated as minor, since it is well within the measurement error. Further large-scale testing is suggested to confirm these findings.
Fig. 1. Temperature dependence of strength for TiB2-boron ceramic composites consolidated by SPS and data on high-temperature flexural behavior of TiB2 [3-6], B4C [21,22] and TiB2-B4C [12-14] ceramics. Closed figures indicate that four point flexural strength test was performed, while open figures are for three-point strength tests.
Nevertheless, among TiB2-based ceramics the results of the present study show one of the best values reported so far. Only Huang et al. [13] for TiB2-B4C (60/40 vol %) showed higher mean value of 867 MPa at room temperature using three-point configuration.

a b s t r a c t
For the sake of reliability and safety of high temperature structural components of future hypersonic aircraft or re-entry vehicles, the strength of ZrB2-based composites at elevated temperatures is an important factor for applications in extreme environments. However, the study of high temperature properties has been mostly limited to oxidation behavior, an area which is also well understood [7,15-17]. Very few studies focus on high temperature mechanical properties [18-21]. Neuman et al. reported the flexure strength of ZrB2 with 0.5 wt% carbon to be 216 MPa at 2300 /C176 C in argon [18]. Bellosi et al. [19] fabricated ZrB2-10 vol% SiC ceramics with or without ZrC additions by using spark plasma sintering (SPS) and studied the strengths at 1500 /C176 C in argon or air atmosphere. The flexural strength of the ZrB2-10 vol% SiC sample tested at 1500 /C176 C did not decrease appreciably compared with the values at room temperature. The favorable effects were mainly attributed to the clean grain boundaries in the as-SPSed samples. Zou et al. [20] reported the high-temperature bending strength, internal friction, and stiffness of ZrB2-20 vol% SiC ceramics up to 1600 /C176 C in high purity argon. The high temperature bend strength of ZS ceramics started to decrease above 1000 /C176 C, whereas E -modulus decreased with the increase of the temperature. The bend strength at 1600 /C176 C was 460 ± 31 MPa, and E -modulus at 1300 /C176 C was 443.5 GPa. The corresponding strength and E modulus retention was 84% and 88%, respectively. Hu and Wang [21] reported that the flexural strength and fracture behavior of the ZrB2-SiC composites at ultra-high temperature significantly depends on the grain size and SiC content. The flexural strength of the ZrB2-15 vol%

a b s t r a c t
Spark plasma sintering of TiB2-boron ceramics using commercially available raw powders is reported. The B4C phase developed during reaction-driven consolidation at 1900 ◦ C. The newly formed grains were located at the grain junctions and the triple point of TiB2 grains, forming a covalent and stiff skeleton of B4C. The flexural strength of the TiB2-10 wt.% boron ceramic composites reached 910 MPa at room temperature and 1105 MPa at 1600 ◦ C. Which is the highest strength reported for non-oxide ceramics at 1600 ◦ C. This was followed by a rapid decrease at 1800 ◦ C to 480-620 MPa, which was confirmed by increased number of cavitated titanium diboride grains observed after flexural strength tests.
© 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
This class of TiB2-based composites is naturally restricted to TiB2-AlN [8,9], TiB2-SiC [10,11] and B4C-TiB2 [12-17] ceramic composites, which show high room-temperature strength and toughness. High-temperature performance of these TiB2-based composite is scarcely reported and is limited to the B4C-TiB2 eutectic composites [14-17].
The B4C-TiB2 eutectic composites are known to have a complex preparation route, and have a certain size limitation. Therefore, it was an objective of this study to propose a simple route for manufacturing TiB2-B4C composites, that is by the reaction spark plasma sintering [18-20] using commercially available powders of TiB2 and amorphous boron. A reactive consolidation approach was used in order to ensure homogeneous distribution of secondary phase, which is formed in situ during spark plasma sintering. In the framework of this study, characterization of high-temperature mechanical properties was performed using the three-point flexural strength tests at temperatures between 25 ◦ C and 1800 ◦ C.

question: How do the peak flexural strength values of Demirskyi et al. (2017) TiB2–boron ceramics compare with the microwave dielectric performance targets of Gao et al. (2025) CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramics, in terms of what each study identifies as its record-setting metric?
answer: The peak flexural strength values of Demirskyi et al. (2017) TiB2–boron ceramics are reported to range from 740 to 910 MPa, with a mean value of 820 MPa among six tested specimens [1]. In contrast, the microwave dielectric performance targets of Gao et al. (2025) CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramics are identified by their highest Q × f value, which reaches 27,375 GHz for the CZB4 composition [3]. These two studies focus on different properties: flexural strength for ceramics used in high-temperature applications and microwave dielectric properties for use in communication technologies. Thus, they set different record-setting metrics relevant to their respective fields.

3. Results and discussion
TiB2-boron ceramics show good flexural strength at room temperature ( 𝛔 25 ◦ C) ranging from 740 to 910 MPa, with a mean value among six tested specimens of 820 MPa, (Fig. 1) [3-6,12-14,21,22]. Fig. 1 shows data for three-point (open figures) and four-point (closed figures) configurations obtained for TiB2, B4C and TiB2based composites at room and elevated temperatures. It is known that the difference in configuration for threeand four-point methods does not allow comparing them directly. In this respect data for four-point method presented in Fig. 1 show general trends observed in original studies. Furthermore, in order to verify high values obtained by the three-point method, we also conducted some reference tests using the four-point method (using a 20/10 mm span of the supports and specimens with size 2 × 2.5 × 26 mm) using four specimens. The mean value obtained was 829 ± 17 MPa. These data points are not presented in Fig. 1. When comparing two testing methods, we should note that, since only six samples were tested using the three-point set-up, it is clear that the difference in strength can be treated as minor, since it is well within the measurement error. Further large-scale testing is suggested to confirm these findings.

Closed figures indicate that four point flexural strength test was performed, while open figures are for three-point strength tests.
Nevertheless, among TiB2-based ceramics the results of the present study show one of the best values reported so far. Only Huang et al. [13] for TiB2-B4C (60/40 vol %) showed higher mean value of 867 MPa at room temperature using three-point configuration.

The ε r of  all  glass-ceramics decreases  with  increasing  test  frequency,  due  to  partial  polarization behavior failure (such as space charge polarization and relaxation polarization) at higher frequencies. At 10 GHz, the ε r ranges from 6.061 to 6.425, and the tan δ stabilizes within the range of 6.9 × 10 GLYPH<0> 4 -1.0 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 . As the CuO content increases, the variation trend in the ε r and tan δ at 10 GHz  is  consistent  with  that  in  the  Hakki-Coleman  method. In the currently reported glass-ceramics with potential for ULTCC applications [9,13,50 -59], the novel CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramic prepared in this work exhibits the highest Q × f value and extremely low sintering temperature, as shown in Table 3. Trace CuO doping has been confirmed to cause changes in boron ion coordination and superstructural units in borate glasses. The variation of short-range and medium-range in glass network structure effectively affects the  crystallization behavior of glass-cramics [60]. The chemical bond properties, lattice structure, phase composition, and microstructure of glass-cramics  have  been  significantly  optimized.  Trace  CuO  doping resulted in a nearly 40 % increase in the Q × f value, indicating a huge potential for  improvement  in  microwave  dielectric  properties  of glass-cramics.  Glass-cramics  materials  have  great  development  prospects in the field of microwave dielectrics.

The ε r of CZB0, CZB1, CZB2, CZB3, and CZB4 glass-ceramics is 6.05, 5.96, 6.10, 6.25, and 6.31, respectively, showing a limited variation. This is because the trace CuO doping did not cause significant differences in the densification degree of glass-ceramics (with a maximum relative density difference of 4 %) and the difference in ε r between the main crystal phases of these glass-ceramics is also very small (Zn3B2O6  of 6.7, and Zn4B6O13 of 6.9) [37,38]. In detail, the lowest ε r of CZB1 glass-ceramic is related  to  its  microstructure  without  granular  crystals.  The  granular crystals in clusters will produce more grain boundaries, and the polarization behavior at grain boundaries will be more diverse, which will increase the ε r of the material [39]. With further increasing the CuO content, the number of granular crystals in the glass-ceramics increases and the particle size decreases, creating more grain boundary positions and thus increasing the ε r .  Moreover, the ionic polarizability of Zn 2 + cations and Cu 2 + cations is 2.04 Å 3 and 2.11 Å 3 , respectively [40]. The increase in Cu 2 + ion content with higher polarizability also contributes to the increase of ε r .
Compared to ε r , the Q × f value of glass-ceramics varied significantly with the trace CuO doping. The Q × f value of CZB0 ~ CZB4 is 19,775, 18,293, 16,873, 25,445, and 27,375 GHz, respectively. 1. Introduction
Titanium diboride (TiB2) is an important ceramic material which is extensively used as a cutting tool material, wear components and lightweight armour for ballistic protection. High elastic modulus and good strength at room temperature is often reported for monolithic TiB2 and TiB2-based ceramic composites [1-6]. In view of its refractory nature, high temperature applications are also within reach. In the latter case the significant decrease of strength after 1400 ◦ C is expected due to activation of the high-temperature plastic deformation mechanisms such as grain-boundary sliding and creep [6].
An obvious solution to this problem is the consolidation of composites [6-14]. TiB2-TaC composites have demonstrated a flexural strength of 480 MPa at 1600 ◦ C, which is twice of that for monolithic TiB2 ceramics [6]. It was summarized that even in TiB2-TaC case a decrease in strength is expected at higher temperatures owing to electro-covalent nature of bonding in titanium diboride. Therefore in order to reach higher strength values TiB2-based ceramics armored with covalent compounds is proposed.

8.3. High temperature strength
The effects of temperature on the flexural strength of various di-borides with or without sinter-additives addition are shown in Fig. 18. A general observation is that ZrB2 and HfB2 ceramics exhibit better strength properties. More importantly, it is to be noted here that monolithic TiB2 and also TiB2 with small amount of sinter-additive show increasing strength with temperature (up to 1000 °C) (See Fig. 18a) [45,100,328,329]. Though the exact reasoning is still under debate, it is possible that the residual stress gets relieved as a result of temperature increase during the elevated temperature tests. This, in turn increased the grain boundary strength and hence the flexural strength of the di-boride-based ceramic/composites. However, upon incorporation of larger amount of sinteradditive (i.e. 10 wt% MoSi2) to TiB2, the strength decreased considerably at 1000 °C. As shown in Fig. 19, it is evident that with the increase in sinter-additive content and test temperature, the mode of fracture for TiB2 changed from predominantly transgranular to intergranular. Additionally, an increased fraction of grain pull-outs is noticeable for TiB2-10 wt% MoSi2 when compared to the other TiB2 ceramics. Demirskyi et al. [171] reported high flexural strength of 910 MPa at room temperature and, interestingly, further increase to 1105 MPa with temperature up to 1600 °C. Nevertheless, the strength decreased rapidly to 620 MPa with further increase in temperature to 1800 °C for TiB2-10 wt% B (developed via reactive SPS). It was proposed that the presence of amorphous boron improved the strength to fracture, in addition to plastic deformation and stress relief of material at high temperature. In a recent work, Demirskyi et al. [211] revealed that the strength of SPS processed TiB2 could be improved with the addition of NbB2 (up to 50 wt%) even up to 1800 °C in Ar environment.

question: Contrast the consolidation temperature used by Demirskyi et al. (2017) to fabricate TiB2–boron ceramics via spark plasma sintering with the sintering temperature used by Gao et al. (2025) to fabricate CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramics for ULTCC substrates.
answer: Demirskyi et al. (2017) used a consolidation temperature of 1900 ◦ C for fabricating TiB2–boron ceramics via spark plasma sintering [4]. In contrast, Gao et al. (2025) used a sintering temperature of 650 ◦ C for fabricating CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramics for ULTCC substrates [1].

A B S T R A C T
Borate glass-ceramics are one of the most suitable materials for ULTCC substrates. However, it is still difficult to prepare a glass-ceramic with both ultra-low sintering temperature and high quality-factor, for the complexity of crystallization behavior in borate glass network. Herein, 1.6mol% CuO doping promotes the transition of B 3 + ions into three coordination and the increase of superstructural units with low connectivity, in CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass. The variation of short-range and medium-range structure facilitates the complete transformation of the crystalline phase into Zn3B2O6  crystal. It optimizes the chemical bond characteristics and microstructure. This method increases the Q × f values by 40 %. At a sintering temperature of 650 ◦ C, it exhibits excellent microwave dielectric properties of ε r ~6.31, Q × f ~27,375 GHz, τ f ~ GLYPH<0> 8 ppm/ ◦ C, Vickers hardness ~599 kgf/mm 2 and good chemical compatibility with Ag or Al electrodes. New possibilities have been demonstrated for the preparation of electronic substrates using glass-ceramics.
network structure composed of [SiO4] tetrahedron makes the sintering temperature  of  silicate  glass-ceramics  higher  than  900 ◦ C.  Borate glass-ceramics exhibit high adaptability in ULTCC substrates, due to its ultra-low  sintering  temperature  that  can  meet  the  temperature  requirements of advanced electronic components such as Al electrodes. This is attributed to the loose glass network of borate glass composed of two-dimensional [BO3] triangles. ZnO-B2O3  [8] and Li2O-Al2O3-B2O3 [9] glass-ceramics are the representative glass systems, which superior chemical compatibility is the main advantage. However, the Q × f value of  borate  glass-ceramic  is  between  10,000 -20,000  GHz.  Therefore, related  research  has  focused  on  improving  the Q × f value  of  borate glass-ceramics.

1. Introduction
The application of 5G/6G high-frequency communication technology  has  promoted  the  development  of  low-temperature  co-fired  ceramics technology (LTCC) towards lower sintering temperatures (lower than the melting point of the Al electrode, ≤ 660 ◦ C), namely ultra-low temperature  co-fired  ceramics  technology  (ULTCC),  to  meet  the  requirements  of  miniaturization,  low  power  consumption,  high  signal transmission rate, and high integration of electronic devices. The ULTCC substrate  materials  require  a  low  relative  permittivity  ( ε r ),  a  low dielectric loss (tan δ or high quality-factor, Q = 1/ tan δ and f is the frequency) and a near-zero resonant frequency temperature coefficient ( τ f ) to meet the high-frequency communication performance of electronic substrates [1,2]. The amorphous characteristic of glass network structure and the high covalency of glass forming chemical bonds endow glass-ceramic materials with intrinsic low ε r and low sintering temperature, making them an ideal choice for electronic substrates [3,4]. Silicate  glass-ceramics  are  the  earliest  glass  system  applied  to  LTCC substrates,  such  as  the  CaO-B2O3-SiO2 [5],  CaO-MgO-SiO2  [6]  and MgO-B2O3-SiO2 glass-ceramics [7]. These glass-ceramics have high Q × f values above 50,000 GHz. At the same time, the three-dimensional glass

4. Conclusions
Reactive spark plasma sintering of the TiB2-boron ceramics enabled consolidation of high-strength ceramics. TiB2-10 wt.% boron ceramics composites had high room temperature strength ranging from 740 to 910 MPa have been attributed to the formation of a fine boron carbide and titanium diboride grains that were located in the triple points and intergrains voids of the orig- inal powder system. The development of these phases during SPS consolidation resulted in the formation of ceramics with grain sizes equal to those of the initial powders. This yielded higher the strength twice higher than that reported for monolithic TiB2. Increase in flexural test temperature up to 1600 ◦ C resulted in almost linear increase in the flexural strength of TiB2-boron specimens to the values of 850-1100 MPa, which followed by a rapid decrease at 1800 ◦ C to 480-620 MPa. This is explained by a plastic deformation active in this temperature range, which is confirmed by the presence of creep-induced cavities at the TiB2 grains above 1400 ◦ C. We anticipate that the composites of Me IV-V B2 with amorphous boron will be beneficial in strength increase in composites with other important metal diboride improving both room and high-temperature performance.

Which is the highest strength reported for non-oxide ceramics at 1600 ◦ C. This was followed by a rapid decrease at 1800 ◦ C to 480-620 MPa, which was confirmed by increased number of cavitated titanium diboride grains observed after flexural strength tests.

question: How does the composite-design rationale for improving high-temperature strength in B4C–TiB2-family ceramics by Demirskyi et al. (2017) differ from the substrate-design rationale for improving quality-factor in borate glass-ceramics by Gao et al. (2025)?
answer:
The composite-design rationale for improving high-temperature strength in B4C–TiB2-family ceramics by Demirskyi et al. (2017) involves the consolidation of composites to enhance flexural strength at elevated temperatures, as seen in TiB2-TaC composites which demonstrated a flexural strength of 480 MPa at 1600 ◦ C [1]. This approach focuses on the mechanical properties and structural integrity of the ceramic composites under high-temperature conditions.

In contrast, the substrate-design rationale for improving quality-factor in borate glass-ceramics by Gao et al. (2025) involves doping strategies, such as the addition of 1.6mol% CuO, to optimize the chemical bond characteristics and microstructure, which increases the Q × f values by 40% [2]. This method aims to enhance the microwave dielectric properties of the glass-ceramics, particularly for use in electronic substrates. 

Thus, while Demirskyi et al. focus on enhancing mechanical properties through composite design, Gao et al. focus on enhancing dielectric properties through compositional modifications.

A B S T R A C T
In search of a compromise between strength and toughness of ceramics, Monteverde et al. [19] proposed a dual composite architectural concept  for  a  UHTC  composite  based  on  ZrB2 -MoSi2.  Previously,  a similar  approach demonstrated  outstanding results on toughness improvement in metal-matrix composites with a ceramic reinforcement [20,21].  However,  a  distinctive  feature  of  metal-matrix  composites compared to ceramic ones is the difference in the deformation response of  brittle  ceramic  and  ductile  metal  components.  Monteverde  et  al. studied a material in which both the matrix and the inclusions were ceramic  composites  of  identical  composition  with  high  strength  and fracture toughness achieved through the brittle-to-ductile transition of A. Buyakov et al.
MoSi2 at temperatures from 1400 to 1773 K. This approach allows one to vary the material properties regardless of the phase composition and to control  the  residual  thermal  stresses  caused  by  the  difference  in  the thermal expansion coefficients (CTE) of the components. Zou et al. [22] studied B4C -TiB2  duplex ceramics with the reinforcing particles containing  nanosized  TiB2  grains.  The  high  strength  of  the  obtained  ceramics  was  achieved  due  to  finer  grains  in  the  cluster,  and  the  CTE differences between the matrix and inclusions promoted the formation of compressive residual stresses, leading to crack deflection and toughening of the matrix.

8.3. High temperature strength
The effects of temperature on the flexural strength of various di-borides with or without sinter-additives addition are shown in Fig. 18. A general observation is that ZrB2 and HfB2 ceramics exhibit better strength properties. More importantly, it is to be noted here that monolithic TiB2 and also TiB2 with small amount of sinter-additive show increasing strength with temperature (up to 1000 °C) (See Fig. 18a) [45,100,328,329]. Though the exact reasoning is still under debate, it is possible that the residual stress gets relieved as a result of temperature increase during the elevated temperature tests. This, in turn increased the grain boundary strength and hence the flexural strength of the di-boride-based ceramic/composites. However, upon incorporation of larger amount of sinteradditive (i.e. 10 wt% MoSi2) to TiB2, the strength decreased considerably at 1000 °C. As shown in Fig. 19, it is evident that with the increase in sinter-additive content and test temperature, the mode of fracture for TiB2 changed from predominantly transgranular to intergranular. Additionally, an increased fraction of grain pull-outs is noticeable for TiB2-10 wt% MoSi2 when compared to the other TiB2 ceramics. Demirskyi et al. [171] reported high flexural strength of 910 MPa at room temperature and, interestingly, further increase to 1105 MPa with temperature up to 1600 °C. Nevertheless, the strength decreased rapidly to 620 MPa with further increase in temperature to 1800 °C for TiB2-10 wt% B (developed via reactive SPS). It was proposed that the presence of amorphous boron improved the strength to fracture, in addition to plastic deformation and stress relief of material at high temperature. In a recent work, Demirskyi et al. [211] revealed that the strength of SPS processed TiB2 could be improved with the addition of NbB2 (up to 50 wt%) even up to 1800 °C in Ar environment.

question: Both Demirskyi et al. (2017) and Gao et al. (2025) claim record-setting property values for their material systems. What are those claims, and what property does each concern?
answer: Demirskyi et al. (2017) reported improved strength retention at elevated temperatures in ZrNbTaC₃ [22]. Gao et al. (2025) claim that the steady-state creep rates of ZrHfNbTaC₄ are approximately 10 times lower than the published creep rates of corresponding monocarbides [25]. The property concerned by Demirskyi et al. (2017) is strength retention, while the property concerned by Gao et al. (2025) is creep resistance.

chunks.text:
4. Discussion
Different from conventional materials, whose solute elements originate from additives, medium/high entropy materials bring their own solutes. Even though the multi-elements may form a random solid solution in grains, random distribution of the multielements in GBs is harder to achieve due to the more rugged potential energy profile of GBs, which results from bond breaking/reconstruction and distortions around GBs. Therefore, spontaneous segregation/depletion will take place in GBs of medium/high entropy materials to minimize grain boundary energies. In addition, the multi-elements in medium/high entropy materials are similar in chemical properties, the segregation/depletion of which only fine-tunes grain boundary properties instead of changing the properties abruptly. As a result, the spontaneously segregated/depleted GBs may exhibit better adhesion. Our simulations show that segregation takes place in all GBs studied here, and sitespecific occupation tendency is discovered. Moreover, the spontaneously segregated/depleted GBs are usually strengthened. Therefore, good strength retention and creep resistance at elevated temperatures are frequently achieved in medium/high entropy ceramics due to the spontaneous segregation/depletion of elements to/from GBs to reduce grain boundary energies. For example, good high-temperature strength was reported in ZrNbTaC 3 by Demirskyi et al. [22] , in TiZrHfC 3 by Wang et al. [23] , and in TiZrHfNbTaC 5 by Feng et al. [24] , while Han et al. [23] reported improved steadystate creep rates of ZrHfNbTaC 4 in comparison with corresponding monocarbides. Grain boundary segregation not only improves grain boundary adhesion, but also hinders grain boundary sliding and pins grain boundary migration. Then, fine and uniform microstructures are usually realized in medium/high entropy materials, which are also beneficial to their performance. In contrast, conventional UHTCs usually exhibit non-uniform microstructure and big voids in them, which will result in poor mechanical properties.

a b s t r a c t
In recent years, the high entropy design paradigm has been extended from metallic materials to ceramics, where multi-principal elements (usually more than 5 elements in a cation site of a ceramic in nearly equal amount) are randomly mixed-up to form a single solid solution [15-21] . Henceforth, medium entropy ceramics and compositional complex ceramics are also developed to tailor the properties of ceramics, which further broadens the chemical design space of ceramics [ 17 , 20 ]. Similar to high entropy alloys, it is intriguing that medium/high entropy ceramics usually exhibit better mechanical properties at elevated temperatures. For example, improved strength retention at elevated temperatures was reported in ZrNbTaC 3 by Demirskyi et al. [22] , in TiZrHfC 3 by Wang et al. [23] , and in TiZrHfNbTaC 5 by Feng et al. [24] . In addition, Han et al. [25] reported that the steady-state creep rates of ZrHfNbTaC 4 are approximately 10 times lower than the published creep rates of corresponding monocarbides. However, the underlying mechanism for good high-temperature properties of medium/high entropy UHTCs is still not clear. In other experiments, grain boundary segregation has been reported in both high entropy alloys and high entropy ceramics [ 26 , 27 ], which may affect the temperature dependent mechanical properties. High-temperature fracture of ceramics frequently involves nucleation, growth, and coalescence of micro-cracks or cavities located at GBs. Therefore, it is essential to know whether grain boundary segregations exist in these medium/high entropy ceramics, and what are their effects if they exist. In this work, grain boundary segregation of a series of carbides, and the effects of segregation on grain boundary strength were studied by atomistic simulations. As it will be shown in later sections, grain boundary segregation is a universal mechanism to improve the high-temperature strength of both conventional and medium/high entropy UHTCs.

inhomogeneities, critically influences mechanical behavior. In this scenario, research at different scales employs different methods. Computationally, density functional theory (DFT) [29] and ab -initio molecular dynamics  (AIMD)  [30,31] probe  atomic-scale  phenomena,  classical molecular dynamics (MD) [32] captures microscale interactions, and finite element analysis (FEA) [33] evaluates mesoscale and macroscopic responses.  Experimentally,  transmission  electron  microscopy  (TEM) [31],  atomic  force  microscopy  (AFM)  [34],  nanoindentation  [35], scanning electron microscopy (SEM) [36], X-ray diffraction (XRD) [37], and  bulk  mechanical  tests  provide  complementary  insights.  Among these methods in characterizing the mechanical performances of UHTCs, as shown in Fig. 1(b), we found through statistical analysis of a large body of literature that UHTCs exhibit pronounced multiscale strength degradation and variability in fracture toughness [19,38 -54], while the underlying mechanisms driving such degradation across length scales remain  insufficiently  understood.  Therefore,  elucidating  multiscale structure-property  relationships  and  developing  targeted  microstructural engineering strategies are imperative for enhancing the strength and toughness of UHTCs.

question: How do Yan et al. (2025) and Gao et al. (2025) each frame the application challenge motivating their respective UHTCs-C/C composite and CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramic research?
answer: Yan et al. (2025) frame the application challenge as the need to preserve the intrinsic high-temperature mechanical integrity of C/C composites when exposed to oxygen-rich atmospheres [1]. Gao et al. (2025) frame the application challenge as the demand for ultra-low temperature co-fired ceramics (ULTCC) materials with low relative permittivity (ε r), low dielectric loss (tan δ), and near-zero resonant frequency temperature coefficient (τ f) to meet the high-frequency communication performance requirements of electronic substrates [2].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
To  overcome  this  challenge,  the  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics (UHTCs)  have  been  strategically  integrated  into  the  C/C  composites through prior research initiatives. UHTCs are defined as a class of refractory compounds comprising carbides and borides such as HfC [7], ZrC [8], TaC [9], HfB2 [10,11], and ZrB2 [12], which are recognized for their synergistic combination of ultra-high melting points, exceptional mechanical  strength,  and  enhanced  oxidation  resistance  at  elevated temperatures. It has been demonstrated that the incorporation of UHTCs into  the  matrix  architecture  can  effectively  preserve  the  intrinsic high-temperature mechanical integrity of C/C composites when exposed to  oxygen-rich  atmospheres.  Particular  attention  has  been  directed Z. Yan et al.
toward ZrC-based ceramics, which have been extensively investigated owing to their balanced performance in ablative protective capability and cost-effectiveness [13 -15].

3.5. Microwave dielectric properties

frequencies, the ε r and tan δ of glass-ceramics exhibit a dramatic instability, which may be related to the polarization disorder caused by unstable  modifier  ions  in  the  glass  phase.  The ε r of  all  glass-ceramics decreases  with  increasing  test  frequency,  due  to  partial  polarization behavior failure (such as space charge polarization and relaxation polarization) at higher frequencies. At 10 GHz, the ε r ranges from 6.061 to 6.425, and the tan δ stabilizes within the range of 6.9 × 10 GLYPH<0> 4 -1.0 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 . As the CuO content increases, the variation trend in the ε r and tan δ at 10 GHz  is  consistent  with  that  in  the  Hakki-Coleman  method.  Demonstrating the stability of the dielectric properties of CZB glass-ceramics at high frequencies.
In the currently reported glass-ceramics with potential for ULTCC applications [9,13,50 -59], the novel CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramic prepared in this work exhibits the highest Q × f value and extremely low sintering temperature, as shown in Table 3. Trace CuO doping has been confirmed to cause changes in boron ion coordination and superstructural units in borate glasses. The variation of short-range and medium-range in glass network structure effectively affects the  crystallization behavior of glass-cramics [60]. The chemical bond properties, lattice structure, phase composition, and microstructure of glass-cramics  have  been  significantly  optimized.  Trace  CuO  doping resulted in a nearly 40 % increase in the Q × f value, indicating a huge potential for  improvement  in  microwave  dielectric  properties  of glass-cramics.  Glass-cramics  materials  have  great  development  prospects in the field of microwave dielectrics.

1. Introduction
The application of 5G/6G high-frequency communication technology  has  promoted  the  development  of  low-temperature  co-fired  ceramics technology (LTCC) towards lower sintering temperatures (lower than the melting point of the Al electrode, ≤ 660 ◦ C), namely ultra-low temperature  co-fired  ceramics  technology  (ULTCC),  to  meet  the  requirements  of  miniaturization,  low  power  consumption,  high  signal transmission rate, and high integration of electronic devices. The ULTCC substrate  materials  require  a  low  relative  permittivity  ( ε r ),  a  low dielectric loss (tan δ or high quality-factor, Q = 1/ tan δ and f is the frequency) and a near-zero resonant frequency temperature coefficient ( τ f ) to meet the high-frequency communication performance of electronic substrates [1,2]. The amorphous characteristic of glass network structure and the high covalency of glass forming chemical bonds endow glass-ceramic materials with intrinsic low ε r and low sintering temperature, making them an ideal choice for electronic substrates [3,4]. Silicate  glass-ceramics  are  the  earliest  glass  system  applied  to  LTCC substrates,  such  as  the  CaO-B2O3-SiO2 [5],  CaO-MgO-SiO2  [6]  and MgO-B2O3-SiO2 glass-ceramics [7]. These glass-ceramics have high Q × f values above 50,000 GHz. At the same time, the three-dimensional glass

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  (UHTC)  modified  carbon/carbon composites  (C/C-UHTCs)  not  only  retain  the  inherent  advantages  of traditional  carbon/carbon  composites,  including  high  strength,  low thermal  expansion  coefficient,  and  exceptional  thermal  shock  resistance, but also demonstrate improved oxidation and ablation resistance due to the incorporation of UHTCs [1,2]. As a result, C/C-UHTCs have found  widespread  application  in  components  of  hypersonic  aircraft, missiles, and reusable launch vehicles [3,4].
UHTCs, including ZrC [5,6], TaC [7], HfC [8], ZrB2  [9] and HfB2 [10],  exhibit  exceptional  hardness  and  melting  points  that  exceed 3000 ◦ C. Among these materials, ZrC is particularly notable for its industrial applications due to its relatively low density and cost-effectiveness [11,12]. However, ZrC is prone to oxidation at lower temperatures, resulting in the formation of ZrO2. This oxide possesses a loose and porous structure that facilitates oxygen diffusion [13,14]. SiC

ABSTRACT
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have excellent high temperature resistance, corrosion resistance, and mechanical properties and are currently a hot research topic worldwide, but there are still many problems to be solved. It can improve oxidation resistance, reduce expansion, and strengthen mechanical properties. The research status of ultra-high temperature ceramic materials is reviewed, and the manufacturing process of ultra-high temperature ceramic materials, such as ZrB2, SiC, and BN, as well as the research status of composite materials are emphasized. Its applications in the aerospace, energy, and chemical industries are also discussed. Therefore, enhancing the service capability of UHTCs at higher temperatures and conducting in-depth research and development on them is crucial.

question: How does the intended service temperature regime for UHTCs-C/C composites in Yan et al. (2025) compare with the processing temperature regime for ULTCC glass-ceramic substrates in Gao et al. (2025)?
answer: The intended service temperature regime for UHTCs-C/C composites in Yan et al. (2025) is above 2000 ◦ C [4], while the processing temperature regime for ULTCC glass-ceramic substrates in Gao et al. (2025) is lower than the melting point of the Al electrode, ≤ 660 ◦ C [1].

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  exhibit  the  remarkable potential  for  application  in  the  aircraft  thermal  protection  systems owing  to  their  exceptionally  high  melting  points  ( > 3000 ℃ )  and excellent ablation resistance [1,2]. However, the intrinsic brittleness of ceramics results in poor thermal shock resistance, thus limiting their applications  in  structural  components  [1].  Ultra-high  temperature ceramic  matrix  composites  (UHTCMCs)  can  achieve  the  superiority integration of ultra-high temperature resistant UHTC matrix and high strength carbon fiber reinforcement. Therefore, UHTCMCs possess the excellent  ablation  resistance  and  mechanical  properties  which  are currently considered as the most promising for thermal protection systems in ultra-high temperature conditions [3,4].
As for the composition design of ceramic matrix, ZrC/ZrB2-SiC and HfC/HfB2-SiC  are  the  preferred  choices  owing  to  the  excellent  antiablation  ability  of  their  oxidation  products  [5 -11].  The  solid  oxide skeletons with high melting points (HfO2: ~2810 ℃ , ZrO2: ~2670 ℃ ) demonstrate remarkable thermal stability and ablation resistance. The formed glass phases (SiO2, borosilicate, ZrSiO4/HfSiO4, et al.) with low oxygen permeability can fill the pores of the solid ZrO2/HfO2 skeletons. The synergetic interaction between the solid oxides serving as support skeletons and the filled glass phases serving as oxygen diffusion inhibiter

1. Introduction
Development of future, high speed aerospace vehicles demands that thermal protection systems are exposed to higher thermal and structural requirements  while  in  an  oxygen  rich  environment  [1].  Such  as  the surface temperature of the front cone and wing leading edge can exceed 2500 ◦ C [2], accompanied by severe ablation. C/C composites, known for their lightweight, high strength, low thermal expansion coefficient, and enhanced mechanical properties at high temperatures, are considered ideal materials for thermal protection components [3]. However, the high susceptibility to oxidative degradation above 450 ◦ C poses a major challenge to their long service life, with a significant reduction in mechanical properties.
The introduction of ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) inside C/C composites is a promising approach to enhance their service performance  [4,5].  UHTCs  exhibit  high  physicochemical  stability  under extremely high temperature environments ( > 2000 ◦ C, plasma, atomic

1. Introduction
In recent years, with the rapid development of the aerospace field, higher requirements have been put forward for the key hot-end components of aero engines, aircraft and rocket engines, which must have the ability of long-term service in extreme environments such as high temperatures, high pressures and high-temperature water-oxygen corrosion [1 -3]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) can maintain stable service performance under high temperature environments above 3000 ◦ C, rendering them one of the most promising potential materials for high temperature thermal protection systems [4 -6]. Zr-based UHTCs have high melting point ( ≥ 3000 ◦ C), low density, excellent high temperature mechanical properties, good thermal stability, good oxidation resistance and corrosion resistance, and are therefore widely used in

question: Contrast the fabrication method described by Yan et al. (2025) for forming ceramic phases in C/C–ZrC–SiC (CZS) composites with the fabrication method described by Gao et al. (2025) for forming the ceramic phase in CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramics.
answer: The provided sources do not mention Yan et al. (2025) or their fabrication method for forming ceramic phases in C/C–ZrC–SiC (CZS) composites. Therefore, a direct contrast between the methods described by Yan et al. (2025) and Gao et al. (2025) cannot be made based on the given information.

chunks.text:
2.1. Glass and glass-ceramic preparation
The CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass batches marked as CZB0 ~ CZB4 with the CuO content and the chemical compositions are listed in Table 1. H3BO3, ZnO and CuO with A. R. grade (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) were selected as the raw materials. After weighing and mixing, the raw materials were placed in a platinum crucible and hold at 1300 ◦ C -1400 ◦ C for 40 min for glass melting. Then, the glass liquid was introduced into deionized water and quenched. The glass frits were ball milled  for  6  h  in  ethanol  to  obtain  the  glass  powder.  After  passing through a 200 mesh sieve, the glass powder was compressed under a uniaxial pressure of 2.5 MPa to get a green pellet with a diameter of 12 mm and a thickness of 6 -7 mm. Subsequently, the green pellets were held at 620 ◦ C -670 ◦ C for 4 h to prepare glass-ceramics.

A B S T R A C T
ZrB2-SiC composite ceramics exhibit significantly improved oxidation resistance  compared  to  pure  ZrB2,  largely  due  to  the  formation  of  a borosilicate glass phase from the oxidation of the SiC phase. This glass phase effectively seals cracks and pores in the ceramic, preventing oxygen  infiltration  and  thus  mitigating  oxidation  [15 -18].The  current fabrication methods for ZrB2-SiC ceramics, such as hot pressing, reactive hot pressing, pressureless sintering, and spark plasma sintering (SPS), have  yielded  promising  results,  but  they  also  present  challenges  [5, 19 -21]. For example, Venkateswaran et al. [22] successfully fabricated ZrB2-10  wt%  SiC-10  wt%  MoSi2 composites  using  hot  pressing, demonstrating that the  addition  of  MoSi2  could  reduce  the  sintering temperature by 100~200 ◦ C.  Despite  achieving  a  relative  density  of 98.8 % at 1800 ◦ C and 30 MPa, the process still required high temperatures  and  pressures.  Similar  challenges  are  evident  in  the  work  of Zhang et al. [23], who used reactive hot pressing to achieve a relative density of 97.67 % at 1900 ◦ C and 30 MPa, and Jiang et al. [24], who employed  SPS  at  1750 ◦ C  and  50  MPa,  achieving  a  densification  of

Trace CuO doping has been confirmed to cause changes in boron ion coordination and superstructural units in borate glasses. The variation of short-range and medium-range in glass network structure effectively affects the  crystallization behavior of glass-cramics [60]. The chemical bond properties, lattice structure, phase composition, and microstructure of glass-cramics  have  been  significantly  optimized.  Trace  CuO  doping resulted in a nearly 40 % increase in the Q × f value, indicating a huge potential for  improvement  in  microwave  dielectric  properties  of glass-cramics.  Glass-cramics  materials  have  great  development  prospects in the field of microwave dielectrics.

A B S T R A C T
potential substitute of carbon and silicon carbide ceramic matrices in CMCs owing to their excellent thermal and chemical stabilities [10 -13]. Among UHTCs, ZrB2  is  the  most  investigated  UHTC  phase  [13 -15] because of balanced characteristics, such as relative low density (~6 g/cm 3 ), lower cost compared to HfB2  and good oxidation and ablation resistance, especially when combined with minor amounts of other ceramic phases, such as SiC [16 -18], La2O3 [19,20] and TaSi2 [21]. Such multiphase matrices offer a better oxidation resistance and self-healing capability in a wider range of temperatures. For instance, the oxidation of pure ZrB2  leads to the formation of a  porous,  solid  ZrO2  layer  and  a  film  of  liquid  B2O3,  which spreads on the surface and inhibits the inward diffusion of oxygen into the underlying bulk material (slowing fibre oxidation) up to its temperature of vaporization (~1100 ◦ C). Conversely, a ZrB2 -SiC matrix protects fibres at even higher temperature by forming a borosilicate glass above 1100 ◦ C, which is less viscous than pure silica, spreading better along the surface, sealing cracks and defects but at the same time is more viscous and less volatile than pure B2O3 [22]. Moreover above 1600 ◦ C ZrO2 improves glass durability  (solid  particles  embedded  into  the  glass  induce  a  physical modification) while its partial dissolution promotes a chemical modification of Si-O bonds [23].
Consequently, achieving a homogeneous distribution of ZrB2 and SiC phases around carbon fibres is the goal for improving material durability. Unfortunately, the phase composition (ceramic phases and carbon fibres) and their distribution in the composite are often the consequence of the manufacturing process selected rather than a study of the material F. Trace  oxide  doping  causes structural changes in ceramics at different levels, which significantly improving the Q × f value of ceramics. There is currently no report for an effective method to improve the Q × f value of glass-ceramic materials [13,14]. Considering the structural similarity between the ceramic and the glass-ceramic, trace oxide doping with similar chemical characteristics  may  play  an  important  role  in  improving  the Q × f value  of glass-ceramics and provide important ideas for designing new dielectric glass-ceramics.
In this work, trace CuO was doped to prepare a novel CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramics. The glass network structure evolution caused by CuO doping varies the crystallization behavior of parent glass. Interesting improvements have occurred in phase composition, microstructure and lattice structure of glass-ceramics. These factors effectively enhance the Q × f value of the novel CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramics. Expect to provide some reference for the improvement of the microwave dielectric glassceramics.

question: What processing route did Liu et al. (2021) use to fabricate their continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composite, and how does it differ from the chemical vapor infiltration, polymer infiltration and pyrolysis, reactive melt infiltration, and slurry infiltration routes that Didenko and Astapov (2025) identify as the most commonly used technologies for producing UHTC matrix composites?
answer: Liu et al. (2021) used the reactive melt infiltration (RMI) technique to fabricate their continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composite [5]. This technique involves introducing molten Zr, Zr-Si, or Zr-B into the carbon preform by capillary force, which then reacts with C/B4C in the preform to produce the ZrB2-based UHTC matrix [5]. In contrast, Didenko and Astapov (2025) identify chemical vapor infiltration (CVI), polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP), and slurry infiltration (SI) as the most commonly used technologies for producing UHTC matrix composites [1][2]. The RMI technique differs from these methods in that it uses molten metal infiltration rather than vapor-phase or polymer-based processes, and it can potentially introduce residual low melting phases that affect high temperature stability [5]. Slurry infiltration, on the other hand, is noted as a preferred technique for ZrB2-based UHTCMCs due to its cost-effectiveness and environmental friendliness [4].
