KEYWORDS
Numerous studies reported during the past decade have sought to reduce the inherent risk of catastrophic failure in the UHTCs by incorporating either short or long Cf . 1,11,12 While reinforcing with short Cf does not significantly alter the failure behavior of composites, the incorporation of continuous C f as reinforcement has been reported to effectively arrest the unstable propagation of cracks during failure. 11,13,14 Therefore, the present thrust is on the development of processing methodologies for continuous fiber-reinforced UHTCMCs (Cf -UHTCMCs). The processing techniques used for the Cf -UHTCMCs have undergone significant advancements in recent years, including chemical vapor infiltration, 15 precursor infiltration and pyrolysis, 16-18 reactive melt infiltration, 19-21 and slurry infiltration. 17,22-24 While chemical vapor infiltration and polymer infiltration are the established precursor-based processes, commercial precursor for the formation of ZrB2 matrix is not available. Therefore, slurry infiltration has emerged as the preferred technique for fabricating the ZrB 2 -based UHTCMCs in recent years.
The selection of fiber architecture in this study is based on the consideration that the ability of ceramic slurry to infiltrate a given fiber preform must be strongly influenced by its overall porosity distribution. Further details have been already published elsewhere by the authors. 25 Also, the slurry infiltration is influenced by the spacing between Cf within a given Cf bundle. Although some porosity remains in the composite, yet increasing the intra-bundle fiber spacing in a controlled manner enhances the extent of homogenous infiltration. It is intuitive that greater homogeneity of slurry infiltration would lead to a more uniform and stronger interaction between the matrix and C f , resulting in superior mechanical properties of the C f -ZrB 2 -SiC composite. 26

KEYWORDS
Several processes are commonly employed to incorporate UHTCs into composite materials, including chemical vapor infiltration (CVI), precursor infiltration pyrolysis (PIP), slurry infiltration (SI) and reactive melt infiltration (RMI). 6-9 Qian et al. 10 fabricated C/C-ZrB 2 -ZrC-SiC composite via 16 cycles of PIP, employing PCS and ZrB-containing organic polymers as precursors. The PIP process enables tailored precursor design but suffers from prolonged cycles, with repeated pyrolysis inducing detrimental micropores and microcracks. 11 Chen et al. 12 fabricated C/C-ZrC-ZrB 2 composite through reactive infiltration of Zr into porous C/C-B 4 C preforms. The resulting materials achieved a density of 3.07 g/cm 3 , and a flexural strength of 147.0 MPa. While reactive melt infiltration (RMI) offers high densification efficiency and rapid fabrication capability, its application with high-melting-point transition metal melts (e.g., Zr, Ti) remains challenged by the risk of fiber degradation. 13 Zhu et al. 14 achieved lowtemperature, low-pressure deposition of ZrB 2 via chemical vapor infiltration (CVI) using a Zr-BCl 3 -H 2 -Ar precursor system, effectively mitigating fiber damage. However, CVI exhibits poor penetration efficiency for Hf-, Zr-, and Tabased UHTCs. Additionally, this process is constrained by prolonged processing cycles and exorbitant energy demands, which impede its scalability. In response to the above issues, researchers often use inexpensive and simple SI process when introducing UHTCs and combine with other processes to achieve densification. Kong et al. 15 prepared Cf /SiC-ZrB 2 composite with RMI after infiltrating carbon felt in a slurry containing 30 wt.% ZrB 2 .

KEYWORDS
hot pressing, infiltration, mechanical properties, slurry
of ceramics and makes them damage-tolerant. 5 Damagetolerant continuous fiber-reinforced ceramic matrix composites (CFCMCs), such as C/C, C/SiC, and SiC/SiC, have found applications as high-temperature structural components. 6-9 Continuous fiber-reinforced ZrB2 -based UHTC matrix composites (UHTCMCs) are being developed in the same line as CFCMCs. UHTCMCs have been also processed in the same way as conventional ceramic matrix composites (CMCs). 10,11 CMCsaremostlyproduced by using four different techniques: polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP), reactive melt infiltration (RMI), chemical vapor infiltration (CVI), and the ceramic route. 6 UHTCMC early development is mostly based on a hybrid process (a combination of two processes). For example, Tang et al. 12 and Wang et al. 13 developed Cf -ZrB 2-SiC composite using a hybrid process that included slurry infiltration followed by CVI. A Cf-ZrB2-SiC composite was also fabricated using a hybrid process that included PIP and slurry impregnation. 14-16 These are incremental advancements of a C f -SiC composite with ZrB 2 added via slurry impregnation. The RMI process is also wieldy used for the fabrication of UHTCMCs. 17-19 After adapting the ceramic route of processing of CMC, substantial progress in the processing of UHTCMCs has been made. The ceramic route consists of a slurry infiltration followed by densification through high-temperature sintering (both pressureless and pressure-assisted). The classic examples of using ceramic route processed composites are oxide-oxide CMCs 20 and nano-infiltration and transient eutectoid process for SiC-based CMCs. 21 Recently, huge developments for the processing of UHTCMCs have been carried out worldwide using a similar ceramic route. 22-27 However, in these studies, unidirectional carbon fabric were used.

Over the past few years, significant advancements have been made in the processing of Cf  reinforced UHTC matrix composites using a combination of techniques, which include chemical vapour infiltration [13, 14], precursor infiltration and pyrolysis [15 -18] reactive melt infiltration [19 -22], and slurry infiltration [16,23]. The slurry infiltration has emerged as a preferred processing technique for the fabrication of ZrB2 based UHTCMCs, considering that this process is cost-effective, environment-friendly and relatively less time-consuming compared to other A. A B S T R A C T
At the outset, most researches focused on the development of short carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composites [14 -19]. These previous researches indicated that, with the introduction of short Cf, the fracture toughness of ZrB2-based composites was generally enhanced by 40 -60% [17,20 -22]. As well known, due to the large aspect ratio, the continuous fiber has higher potential toughening efficiency than short fiber, owing to enhanced damage tolerance mechanisms, especially fiber pull-out [9, 23 -25]. For fabrication of continuous carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2-based composites (Cf/ZrB2), a variety of techniques were exploited, including reactive melt infiltration (RMI), chemical vapor infiltration (CVI), and polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP). For RMI technique, the molten Zr, Zr-Si, or Zr-B was introduced into the carbon preform by capillary force, and then reacted with C/B4C in the preform to produce the ZrB2-based UHTC matrix [26 -30]. However, undesired residual low melting phases affected the high temperature stability [31]. Compared to RMI technique, CVI can effectively avoid residual metals and usually

answer: Liu et al. These mechanisms contribute to the enhanced fracture properties of the composite. In contrast, the question refers to Didenko and Astapov (2025), but this source is not provided in the given list of sources, so the specific densification-related porosity problems described by them cannot be addressed using the provided information. 4. Conclusion
Continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composites were fabricated by combined electrophoretic deposition and sintering processes, where the electrophoretic deposition (EPD) was developed to form the green bodies and the hot pressing (HP) was employed to densify the composites. The main conclusions drawn from this work are summarized as follows:
- (1) The EPD process can deposit UHTC particles on continuous carbon fibers and control the thickness of UHTC coating by changing the process time. Thanks to the uniform UHTC coatings on the carbon fibers, after hot pressing, no pores on a micro-scale level were observed in the continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composite.
- (2)  The flexure strength, fracture toughness and the work of fracture for the Cf/ZrB2-based composites were 199 MPa, 6.71 MPa m 1/2 and  754 J/m 2 ,  respectively.  The  good  mechanical  properties could  be  attributed  to  the  intact  fibers  and  the  suitable  fibermatrix bonding interface.
- (3)  The enhanced fracture properties could be associated with the multiple toughening mechanisms, including fiber pull-out, fiber bridging, crack deflection and branching along the interfaces.
Fig. 10. Morphologies of fractured SENB specimen: (a-b) fractured surfaces, (c-d) typical crack propagation in the composite.

3.4.  Continuous fiber toughening
Continuous fiber toughening involves introducing continuous fibers, such as carbon fibers or SiC fibers, into a ceramic matrix to form continuous  fiber-reinforced  UHTC  composites  (Cf-UHTC  or  SiCf-UHTC). The primary toughening mechanisms include crack bridging, fiber pullout,  fiber  fracture,  and  interfacial  debonding.  As  cracks  propagate, continuous fibers span the cracks and form bridges, preventing further crack extension. Additionally, the processes of fiber pull-out and fracture  absorb  significant  energy,  slowing  crack  propagation,  while  interfacial  debonding  between  fibers  and  the  matrix  dissipates  further energy,  enhancing  the  material's  toughness  [55,56].  In  a  study  by Zhang et al. [57], Cf-ZrB2-SiC composites were fabricated using twodimensional  carbon  fibers  and  nano-ZrB₂.  These  composites  demonstrated excellent mechanical properties and non-brittle fracture behavior, with a fracture energy of 1338 J/m², an order of magnitude higher than that of traditional ZrB2-SiC ceramics. This improvement was attributed to combined toughening mechanisms, including crack deflection, fiber bridging, fiber pull-out, and crack branching (as shown in Fig. 5).

4. Conclusions and outlook
In this work, the reinforcement method and mechanism of several typical ZrC-based composites  are  analyzed  and  discussed  in  detail,  and  the  development  of  ZrC-based composites is also prospected. The toughening mechanism of particle-toughened ZrC- based  composites  is  mainly  crack  deflection  and  particle  bridging.  However,  the toughness of the composites has not been significantly improved. In order to further increase  the  toughness  of  ZrC-based  composites,  it  is  necessary  to  improve  various reinforcements or preparation processes, such as improving the dispersion of reinforcements  and  the  bonding  ability  of  reinforcements  with  matrix,  and  controlling  the coarsening  of  nanophases.  Carbon  fiber-toughened  ZrC-based  composites  have  the advantages  of  light  weight,  high  fracture  toughness,  ablation  resistance,  oxidation resistance, etc. It is  a  special  material  that  can  be  used  under extremely harsh conditions such as extremely high temperature and oxidizing atmosphere. The toughening mechanism  of  fiber  includes  crack  deflection,  crack  bridging,  crack  branching,  fiber pullout, etc. The existing research on fiber-toughened ZrC ceramic matrix composites mainly focuses on 2D Cf/SiC system, and how to design 3D fiber braiding mode and reduce  thermal  damage  of  carbon  fiber  still  needs  further  study.  Toughening  ZrC ceramics  with  coated  Cf  has  also  been  studied  extensively,  and  the  weak  interface produced  by  the  addition  of  interface  phase  will  improve  the  toughening  effect. However,  there  is  no  research  on  the  influence  of  interfacial  phase  thickness  and microstructure control on the properties of Cf/ZrC composites. In addition, most of the interfacial phases reported at present are monolayer structure, and the influence of double or multilayer interfacial phase structure on the mechanical properties of coated fiber-toughened  ZrC  ceramics  still  needs  further  study.  The  main  mechanism  of toughening ZrC ceramics with graphene sheets is pullout of graphene sheets, bridging, deflection  of  cracks,  etc.  The  easy  stacking  of  graphene  will

1. Introduction
The toughness of FRCMCs is achieved through mechanisms like crack deflection and crack bridging, resulting in both elevated strength and fracture toughness. These composites address the limitations of metal materials, such as low-temperature resistance and too much density, brittleness and poor reliability, and insufficient resistance to oxidation in the case of carbon materials. FRCMCs exhibit very good properties, including lightweight, resistance to high temperatures, corrosion resistance, wear resistance, oxidation resistance, and superior damage tolerance [9]. Some sintering additives such as Y2O3 and CeO2 can improve mechanical properties of Si3/SiC ceramic by reducing density of the composite. This decrease was caused by the resultant composites' increased porosity [10]. Short/discontinuous fiber composites are fabricated using traditional ceramic techniques, utilizing non-oxide (WC, SiC, ZrC, BN, etc.) or oxide (zirconia, alumina, silica, etc.) ceramic matrices. These can be further strengthened by SiC, AlN, and TiB2 whiskers. Long/continuous fiber composites, on the other hand, are produced by incorporating long monofilament or multifilament fibers. The spreading phase in continuous fibers provides the most effective strengthening effect. CMCs are generally developed to address the toughness limitations associated with conventionally used ceramics [11].

A B S T R A C T
A  process  combining  electrophoretic  deposition  (EPD)  with  hot  pressing  (HP)  was  developed  to  fabricate continuous  carbon  fiber-reinforced  ZrB2-based  composites  (Cf/ZrB2-based  composites).  ZrB2-based  ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) particles were uniformly pre-coated on continuous carbon fibers via EPD. Then, the UHTC-coated carbon fibers were stacked and hot pressed to prepare the Cf/ZrB2-based composites. Microstructure  observations revealed that almost no micro-pores were found in the inter-bundle and intra-bundle regions of fibers after HP. The flexural strength, fracture toughness and the work of fracture of the Cf/ZrB2based  composite  were  measured  as  199 ± 26 MPa,  6.71 ± 1.29 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 ,  and  754 ± 58 J/m 2 ,  respectively. Based on the observations of non-brittle fracture behavior, fractured morphology and crack propagation, the enhanced fracture properties were mainly attributed to the multiple toughening mechanisms, such as fiber pullout, fiber bridging, crack deflection and branching along the interfaces.
reaction during fabrication process, such as coating carbon fibers with PyC, BN, SiC [9 -11] to obtain a suitable fiber-matrix bonding interface, use of nanosized UHTC powders [12] to lower the sintering temperature, introduction of nano-sized carbonaceous materials [13] to react with the sintering additives instead of consuming the fibers.

These results are unambiguous in demonstrating that the use of high-pressure infiltration technology substantially improves the infiltration efficiency of the liquid-phase precursor and effectively suppresses the formation of closed pores within the green body.
For UHTC matrix composites, there is a perpetual need for sufficient UHTC phases as the matrix to form a dense and continuous protective oxide layer on the surface during ablation in order to withstand high dynamic  pressure  and  intense  erosion  environments.  Such  a  goal  is achieved  for  the  Cf/ZrB2 -SiC  composite  prepared  in  this  work.  For comparison, Fig. 2h and i shows the content of UHTC and open porosity of  UHTC  matrix  composites  prepared  by  different  processes,  respectively. It can be seen that even after up to 20 cycles of PIP, the open porosity  of  the  composites  based  on  ZrC  or  HfC  precursors  remains above 30 % [18,19]. Even when the SiC precursor (whose mass yield and volumetric conversion rate is usually higher than UHTC precursors) was used together with ZrC or HfC precursors, or when pre-densification is applied through the CVI process, as a general rule-of-thumb, the final composites still have an open porosity above 10 % and UHTC content of less  than  15  vol  %  [18 -22]. 3.1.1. Effect of preparation method on ablation behavior of the composites
As for Cf/C e ZrC e SiC composites obtained by a single process (CLVD or PIP), their recession rates were extremely high, indicating low ablation resistance.
Chen et al. [60] conducted a comparative analysis of the microstructure and ablation resistance of Cf/ZrC e SiC composites fabricated via improved RMI and conventional PIP. The RMI-Cf/ ZrC e SiC composite exhibited low porosity (3.49%) and high thermal conductivity (21.3 W/(m , K) at room temperature and 15.4 W/ (m , K) at 1200 /C14 C), which was bene /uniFB01 cial for transferring the heat timely and reducing the surface temperature of the composite during ablation [60]. The surface temperature of the RMI-Cf/ ZrC e SiC composite was found to be 130 e 210 /C14 C lower than that of the PIP-Cf/ZrC e SiC composite under the same ablation conditions. This decrease in surface temperature weakened the thermal damage of the RMI-Cf/ZrC e SiC composite. For the PIP-Cf/ZrC e SiC composite, its limited thermal conductivity caused a sharp increase in surface temperature, which in turn led to the rapid evaporation of SiO2 and intensive release of SiO and CO gases. As a result, ZrO2 remained on the surface and formed a loose, poorly connected structure that was unable to provide effective protection for the composite [60]. The microstructural differences between these two composites also in /uniFB02 uenced their oxidation behavior. The bulk ZrC in the RMI-Cf/ZrC e SiC was oxidized into continuous ZrO2, which dissolved in glassy SiO2. The continuous ZrO2 acted as a net structure, reducing denudation and evaporation of SiO2 when exposed to /uniFB02 ame [60].

Wang et al. [262] developed Cf/ZrB2-ZrC-SiC sharp leading-edge composites, via combined precursor infiltration,  pyrolysis,  and  reactive  melt infiltration,  exhibit  excellent ablation resistance due to B2O3  glass formation from ZrB2  oxidation, which repairs defects and stabilizes the oxide layer, reducing linear and mass ablation rates by 63 % and 57 % compared to Cf/ZrC-SiC composites after 120 s of ablation. Wen et al. [263] created C/C-ZiC-ZrC-SiC composites using PIP and RMI with a ZiC interlayer, showed a 50.54 % increase in flexural strength, a 56.76 % increase in tensile strength, and enhanced ablation performance at 2200 ◦ C by protecting carbon fibers and PyC from erosion and weakening the ceramic matrix bond for stress release. Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composites were produced via water-based slurry impregnation and polymer infiltration/pyrolysis, followed by annealing treatments, identifying 1400 ◦ C as the optimal temperature to balance mechanical  performance  and  microstructural  stability,  with  87  % strength retention at 1500 ◦ C [264]. Cf/SiC and Cf/ZrC-SiC composites were fabricated using a combined process, achieving lower porosity ( < 8 %) compared to single PIP but higher residual thermal stresses caused by a higher processing temperature (1700 ◦ C) [265]. [62] demonstrated that the ablation properties of Cf/ C e SiC e ZrC composites were in /uniFB02 uenced by the RMI process temperature, which signi /uniFB01 cantly impacted their microstructure. The linear and mass ablation rates of the composites decreased with an increase in the RMI temperature from 2000 to 2300 /C14 C. Upon preparation of the composites at 2300 /C14 C, dense ZrC e SiC ceramics were synthesized both inside the composite and on its surface. During the oxidation process in an oxyacetylene /uniFB02 ame, a gas-tight oxide layer consisting of SiO2 and ZrO2 was formed on the surface [62].

[72] evaluated ablation properties of PIP-prepared Cf/ZrC composites under oxyacetylene /uniFB02 amewith a heat /uniFB02 uxof 4.187 MW/ m 2 for 60 s depending on the synthesis temperature (1500,1550, or 1600 /C14 C). As the preparation temperature increased, the open porosity of the composites increased, allowing greater oxidant transport into the composite volume, which in turn weakened the ablation resistance. So, the authors concluded that the optimal preparation temperature is 1500 /C14 C [72].

question: What surface temperatures were achieved on ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramic buttons in the supersonic arc-jet tests of Silvestroni et al. (2019), and how do these compare with the temperature range over which Didenko and Astapov (2025) say the glassy SiO2 protective layer on ZrB2-SiC composites remains stable before volatilizing or peeling off?
answer: In the supersonic arc-jet tests of Silvestroni et al. (2019), surface temperatures of up to 2800 K were achieved on ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramic buttons [1]. However, the temperature range over which Didenko and Astapov (2025) say the glassy SiO2 protective layer on ZrB2-SiC composites remains stable before volatilizing or peeling off is not mentioned in the provided sources.

4. Discussion
Based on the above results, it can be stated that the FG-UHTC composite here proposed was effective in providing enhanced failure tolerance  as  compared  to  both  ZrB2-unreinforced  bulk  and  to  ZrB2-based composites containing homogenous content of short carbon fiber, since the fracture toughness increased by more than twofold.

question: How does the temperature jump phenomenon observed by Silvestroni et al. (2019) during arc-jet testing of ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramics relate to the beneficial role of oxygen partial pressure that Didenko and Astapov (2025) attribute to protective oxide film formation on carbon-ceramic composites in general?
answer: The temperature jump phenomenon observed by Silvestroni et al. (2019) during arc-jet testing of ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramics is associated with the formation of a porous ZrO2 layer on the external surface of the sample after the complete removal of the liquid SiO2 glassy phase, which leads to an increase in catalytic activity and a strong reduction in thermal conductivity in the oxidized region [2][3]. While the specific role of oxygen partial pressure in protective oxide film formation on carbon-ceramic composites, as attributed by Didenko and Astapov (2025), is not directly discussed in the provided sources, it can be inferred that higher oxygen partial pressures may enhance the formation of protective oxide layers, potentially mitigating the temperature jump by stabilizing the surface and reducing the likelihood of sudden changes in thermal properties. However, the exact relationship between oxygen partial pressure and the temperature jump phenomenon as described by Silvestroni et al. (2019) is not explicitly detailed in the given sources.

The  temperature  jump  is  a  phenomenon  commonly observed  in  ZrB2-SiC-based  composites  and  has  been  ascribed  to  an abrupt change of the chemistry and emissivity of the exposed surface, from silico-boride glass to mostly naked crystalline ZrO2, or even due to partial oxide detachment leading to a sudden decrease of the thermal conductivity [29,33]. Previous works highlighted indeed that the sudden temperature jump occurs when the outermost SiO2  consumption from the external surface by shear forces or evaporation as SiO is faster than its replenishment from the sub-layers.

5. Conclusions
An experimental campaign was carried out to characterize a new class of Ultra-High-Temperature Ceramic Matrix Composites based on ZrB 2 -SiC-Y 2 O 3 matrix and different carbon fibers architectures, in an environment representative of atmospheric re-entry. Small sized samples were exposed to a supersonic flow of simulated air in an arc-jet wind tunnel, at specific total enthalpies up to 20 MJ/kg, at heat fluxes over 5 MW/m 2 (cold wall, fully catalytic) and in a highly reactive chemical environment. All samples demonstrated an excellent ablation resistance, with erosion rates on the order of 10 -4 mm/s. The surface temperature was monitored by non-intrusive infrared equipment, including two-color pyrometers and a thermo-camera. In all the tests, a spontaneous temperature jump of several hundred degrees was observed at constant flow conditions, with maximum equilibrium surface temperatures of 2690-2850 K. Only a thin layer in the front part of the samples, directly exposed to the flow, experienced the jump, while the rear material kept a much lower temperature (below 2200 K even after the jump). A phenomenon of liquid phase transportation along the side of the sample ( waves-of-radiance ) was observed on the samples right before the onset of the jump. The combined effort of experiment al activities and numerical simulations allowed proposing a novel and comprehensive interpretation for the jump, based on a twofold mechanism affecting the heat transfer to the material, associated to the formation of a porous ZrO 2 layer on the external surface of the sample after complete removal of the liquid SiO 2 glassy phase: an increase in the catalytic activity and a strong reduction in thermal conductivity in the oxidized region. Despite the jump, the analyzed ceramics displayed promising performance, with an excellent ablation resistance at 270 0-280 0 K and the capability of the oxide phase to shelter the underneath material, keeping it at acceptable temperatures.

question: How does the effect of a small volume fraction of W-compounds on the sub-surface oxide architecture of ZrB2-based ceramics, as reported by Silvestroni et al. (2019), compare to the effect of ZrB2 particle addition on the ablation resistance of Cf/C composites discussed by Didenko and Astapov (2025)?
answer:
The effect of a small volume fraction of W-compounds on the sub-surface oxide architecture of ZrB2-based ceramics, as reported by Silvestroni et al. (2019), indicates that even at 5 vol%, the introduction of W-compounds leads to a sub-surface architecture resembling that of pure W [3]. This suggests that while W-compounds are beneficial for strength retention above 2000 °C, they may not significantly reduce the oxidation front advancement and could potentially affect the oxidation resistance in hypersonic environments.

In contrast, the effect of ZrB2 particle addition on the ablation resistance of Cf/C composites, as discussed by Didenko and Astapov (2025), shows that incorporating ZrB2 reduces the surface temperature of the composite material, thereby enhancing its ablation resistance [4]. Specifically, the addition of ZrB2 was found to lower the surface temperature from 2314 °C to 2068 °C, which contributes to improved ablation performance. 

Thus, while the addition of W-compounds influences the sub-surface architecture without a clear benefit to oxidation resistance, the addition of ZrB2 particles enhances ablation resistance by reducing surface temperature. These effects are distinct, with the former focusing on microstructural changes and the latter on thermal performance improvements.

3.5. ZrB2  and HfB2; W and Nb inclusions
unmodified HfB2-15 vol%SiC ceramic. Overall, the W modifications resulted in an approximately 30% reduction in total scale thickness at 2000 ◦ C. Carney et al. [46] postulated phase immiscibility may be increasing the viscosity of the outer glass scale at 2000 ◦ C or during cool down but no high temperature phase diagrams for WO3 with silica or boria are available. Although the WC and WB modifications were only 3 vol%, it was hypothesized that due to the solubility of W in HfB2 and HfC being between 4 and 40 mol%, there could easily be localized regions of W that, when oxidized, could create regions of HfO2-WO3  well beyond the 5 mol% solid solubility at 2000 ◦ C. This could result in liquid-phase sintering of the HfO2. However, because the solid solubility of WO3 in HfO2 reaches a maximum of approximately 7 mol% at ~1280 ◦ C before forming a liquid phase, densification of the porous HfO2 region could also occur at 1600 ◦ and  1800 ◦ C,  but  no  densification  was  reported  at  either temperature.
During 1650 ◦ C air oxidation of ZrB2-15 vol%WSi2, Silvestroni et al. didn ' t identify any W in crystalline ZrO2  but eutectic formation in the ZrO2-WO3  system  couldn ' t  be  concretely  discounted  as  a  reaction product [47]. Based on extensive TEM and EDS characterization, Silvestroni  et  al.  proposed  that  W-modification  may  not  provide  any notable reduction in the oxidation front advancement. In a further study utilizing arc-jet testing, Silvestroni et al. found that while 5 vol% additions of WC to ZrB2-5 vol%SiC are beneficial for strength retention above 2000 ◦ C, a sub-surface architecture resembling pure W forms, meaning that the volume fraction of W-compounds may need to be well below 5 vol% [103].

11. Ablation properties
Effects of addition of other materials as additive to ZrB2-SiC on ablation properties were also studied, as in refs. [436,438-441]. To limit the evaporation of SiC, WC was added to ZrB2-20 vol% SiC. During ablation WC formed liquid phases that accrued the densification of the ZrO2-based scale, which is known to retard the diffusion of oxygen to the surface of the ZrB2-based composite [442]. Xiang et al. [203] investigated the ablation behaviour of laminated ZrB2-SiC/BN ceramics using oxyacetylene torch environment. The ZrB2-SiC/BN ceramics exhibited excellent ablation resistance and configurational stability when compared to ZrB2-SiC ceramics. This is due to the introduction of a weak BN layer which improved the thermal shock resistance of ZrB2-SiC. Oxide additive (ZrO2) also improves the ablation resistance of ZrB2 composite to some extent. Zhou et al. [443] has studied the effect of addition of 10 and 20 vol% ZrO2 to ZrB2 -10 vol% SiC composites on its ablation properties. It has been found that the composite containing 10 vol% ZrO2 possess better ablation resistance. The ablation resistance was attributed to the formation of ZrO2 skeleton in which glassy SiO2 was dispersed. However, increase in the amount of ZrO2 beyond the optimum content leads to formation of facile inward pathway for the transport of oxygen; thus leading to lowering of the oxidation and ablation resistances (Fig. 34). In the case of ZrB2-10SiC-10ZrO2 (BS10Z), as duration of ablation got extended to 600 s, the high vapour pressure led to the evaporation of borosilicate, forming some pores. By contrast formation of pores and spallation were observed much earlier in the case of ZrB2-10SiC20ZrO2 (BS20Z), viz., within ablation duration of 30 s.

5. Conclusions
Two major conclusions were drawn. First, it seems that presence of oxygen in the incoming /uniFB02 ow is bene /uniFB01 cial for retarding the material consumption, thanks to the activation of partially healing mechanism by SiC oxidation and B2O3 retention into a protective silica glass.
Second, it seems that introduction of W-compounds in the present concentration, 5 vol%, although fundamental for ZrB2 strength retention above 2000 °C, dictates the formation of the sub-surface architecture making it resemble to that of pure W. This crucial observation leads to propose that the volume fraction of W-compounds should be well below this value in order to improve oxidation resistance in hypersonic environments.

a b s t r a c t
The results showed that various structurally stable oxides were formed during the ablation process of C/C-SiC -(Zr, Hf, Ti)C, and the synergistic effect of these oxides enhanced the resistance of the material to thermochemical ablation. Additionally, boride UHTCs exhibited higher thermal conductivity than carbides (ZrB 2 : 60 W m -1 K -1 , HfB2 : 104 W m -1 K -1 , ZrC and HfC: 20 W m -1 K -1 ). This enhanced thermal conductivity helped lower the temperature of the ablation surface and improved ablation performance [ 18 ]. Wang et al. [ 19 ] investigated the effect of added ZrB2 on the ablation performance of C/C-ZrC-SiC composites. The incorporation of ZrB2 reduced the surface temperature of the composite material from 2314 ° C to 2068 ° C, thereby enhancing its ablation resistance. Feltrin et al. [ 20 ] demonstrated that medium-entropy borides exhibit superior properties, including hardness and oxidation resistance, compared to individual monolithic borides such as ZrB2 and HfB2 . These findings suggest the feasibility of incorporating medium-entropy carbides and borides into C/C composites (C/C-MECs) to form a structurally stable multiphase oxygen barrier layer during ablation. This approach further reduces the surface temperature of the material and enhances its thermochemical ablation resistance.

A B S T R A C T
With the rapid development of hypersonic vehicles  and reusable spacecraft,  ultra-high temperature  ceramic matrix  composites  applied  in  the  hot-section  components  are  facing  extreme  environments  with  ultra-high temperatures exceeding 2000 ◦ C, severe oxidative erosion, and intense thermal shock cycles. To improve the ablation resistance of C/C-SiC-ZrC composites, ZrB2 and La2O3 modified C/C-SiC-ZrC composites were prepared by reactive melt infiltration (RMI). During the RMI process, Zr-Si melt brought the ZrB2  and La2O3  into C/C composites. The effects of ZrB2 and La2O3 content on the microstructure and ablation behaviors of the modified composites were systematically investigated. The results show that the addition of ZrB2 and La2O3 increases the density of C/C-SiC-ZrC composites from 2.25 g/cm 3 to 2.35 -2.47 g/cm 3 .  During the ablation process, La2O3 inhibited the tetragonal-to-monoclinic phase transition of ZrO2 and promoted its sintering, forming a continuous ceramic skeleton embedded in molten SiO2. ZrB2 ultrafine powders oxidized to form fine ZrO2 particles, which had a good pinning effect on the SiO2-ZrO2  oxide layer. The ablation rates of the composites decrease significantly with increasing ZrB2 and La2O3 content. The modified composites with 6 mol.% ZrB2 and 0.9 mol.% La2O3 exhibit mass and linear ablation rates of 0.975 mg/s and 0.267 μ m/s, respectively, showing 70 % and 88 % reductions compared to C/C-SiC-ZrC composites.

question: How does the core-shell (Zr,W)B2 microstructure of the ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramic studied by Silvestroni et al. (2019) relate to the flexural strength values Liu et al. (2021) report for continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composites made without WC additions?
answer: The core-shell (Zr,W)B2 microstructure of the ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramic studied by Silvestroni et al. (2019) is associated with enhanced mechanical properties, including improved flexural strength at elevated temperatures, due to the reinforcing effects of the core-shell structure on the material's microstructure [3]. In contrast, Liu et al. (2021) report a flexural strength of 199 ± 26 MPa for continuous carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2-based composites made without WC additions [2]. The absence of WC in the latter study likely results in a different microstructure that does not benefit from the same core-shell strengthening mechanism, leading to lower flexural strength values compared to the ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramics with the (Zr,W)B2 core-shell structure.

a b s t r a c t
For the sake of reliability and safety of high temperature structural components of future hypersonic aircraft or re-entry vehicles, the strength of ZrB2-based composites at elevated temperatures is an important factor for applications in extreme environments. However, the study of high temperature properties has been mostly limited to oxidation behavior, an area which is also well understood [7,15-17]. Very few studies focus on high temperature mechanical properties [18-21]. Neuman et al. reported the flexure strength of ZrB2 with 0.5 wt% carbon to be 216 MPa at 2300 /C176 C in argon [18]. Bellosi et al. [19] fabricated ZrB2-10 vol% SiC ceramics with or without ZrC additions by using spark plasma sintering (SPS) and studied the strengths at 1500 /C176 C in argon or air atmosphere. The flexural strength of the ZrB2-10 vol% SiC sample tested at 1500 /C176 C did not decrease appreciably compared with the values at room temperature. The favorable effects were mainly attributed to the clean grain boundaries in the as-SPSed samples. Zou et al. [20] reported the high-temperature bending strength, internal friction, and stiffness of ZrB2-20 vol% SiC ceramics up to 1600 /C176 C in high purity argon. The high temperature bend strength of ZS ceramics started to decrease above 1000 /C176 C, whereas E -modulus decreased with the increase of the temperature. The bend strength at 1600 /C176 C was 460 ± 31 MPa, and E -modulus at 1300 /C176 C was 443.5 GPa. The corresponding strength and E modulus retention was 84% and 88%, respectively. Hu and Wang [21] reported that the flexural strength and fracture behavior of the ZrB2-SiC composites at ultra-high temperature significantly depends on the grain size and SiC content. The flexural strength of the ZrB2-15 vol%

3.3. Mechanical properties and fracture characteristics
The flexural strength, fracture toughness, and the work of fracture for the Cf/ZrB2-based composite were measured by means of the 3-pt bending and SENB testing, respectively. The Cf/ZrB2-based composite in  the  current  work  exhibited  a  flexural  strength  of  199 ± 26 MPa. Similar  strength  values  were  obtained  for  ZrB2-based  composites reinforced by continuous C [38,53] and SiC fibers [54], and from other carbon  fiber-reinforced  UHTC-based  composites  [55].  Generally,  the flexural  strength  of  the  continuous  fiber  reinforced  ZrB2-based  composites is lower than those with short fiber (340 GLYPH<0> 440 MPa) [12,52] and the ZrB2-based ceramics (about 500 MPa) [38,52], due to a change of the flaw population and flaw size during the incorporation of continuous or short fibers into matrix.
Fig. 7. Surface morphologies on the polished Cf/ZrB2-based composite: (a-b) morphologies observed along the radial direction of fiber, (c-d) morphology observed along the axial direction of fiber, (e) matrix filled in a closed triangular-like region, (f) typical matrix-fiber interface.
Fig. 8. (a) Backscattered electron image with corresponding elemental mappings of (b) Zr, (c) B, (d) C, (e) Si, and (f) O.

A B S T R A C T
Both oxidation resistance and mechanical properties of ZrB2 can also be  tweaked  via  alloying  by  other  refractory  silicon-free  compounds (such  as  WC,  TaB2)  to  yield  diboride  solid  solutions  with  various composition-specific structural features. Silvestroni et al. [21] synthesized  ZrB2 -SiC -WC  ceramics  with  a  peculiar  metastable  core-shell structure: ZrB2  was located in the center of grains, whereas the outer shell was comprised by (Zr,W)B2 solid solution. This core-shell structure had a profound beneficial impact on the ceramics ' mechanical properties both at room and elevated temperatures. The authors attributed the reinforcing effect to the features of dislocation formation and motion and evolution of structure upon heating [21]. The crack resistance of the

a b s t r a c t
standing high-temperature strengths have been achieved through doping carefully selected additives like WC or W. For example, Zou et al. [4] reported that strength retention of ZrB 2 -based ceramics at elevated temperatures could be realized with the addition of WC. Furthermore, Silvestroni et al. [5] pushed measurement temperature limit up to 2100 ° C and found that the flexural strength of ZrB 2 was higher than 600 MPa at all measured temperatures. Similar effects were also found in ZrC-based ceramics. For example, Shen et al. [6] reported that the high-temperature strength of ZrC could be improved with the addition of W, and a fracture strength higher than 500 MPa was obtained at 1600 ° C. The underlying mechanisms of strength retention in diborides were systematically studied, and the following conclusions were made [8] : (1) removing surface oxides by additives enhances sintering and results in clean GBs [ 4 , 9 ]; (2) segregation of solid solutes into GBs, which strengthens GBs and pins grain boundary migration and sliding, resulting in uniform microstructures [ 8 , 10 ]; (3) enhanced dislocation plasticity by solid solutions disperses strain energy at elevated temperatures [11] . Among these mechanisms, grain boundary segregation plays a dominant role, which is also applicable to carbides [12] and has been proven by experimental characterizations, e.g. direct observation of W segregation at GBs of ZrB 2 [13] , and reduced population of intergranular fracture at high temperatures with the addition of WC [14] .

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) materials are of great interest due to the extraordinary performance they can potentially achieve. The ability to operate at very high temperatures and for long times pushes research towards the engineering of materials with increasing performance that possess an ever-challenging combination of properties [1,2]. Among all UHTCs, those based on zirconium diboride, ZrB2, have been the most studied, which has resulted in elucidation of relationships among processing,  microstructure,  and  performance  [3,4].  These  ceramics exhibit a good combination of properties including lower density (6.08 g/cm 3 ) as compared to Hf-compounds, high melting point (3250 ◦ C), high thermal conductivity (60 -130 W/mK) [5 -7], high electrical conductivity [8 -10] and good elevated temperature strength [11].
Reported fracture strength for ZrB2  ceramics can exceed 1 GPa at room temperature, but these values rapidly decrease with increasing temperature to less than 340 MPa at 1500 ◦ C or above [12,13].
Borides  with ' core-shell ' structures  have  been  reported  to  have strengths as high as 660 MPa at 2100 ◦ C, notable compression resistance at ultra-high temperature, and even enhanced oxidation behavior [11, 14,15]. The fascinating core-shell structure forms spontaneously when borides or carbides are sintered at specific temperatures in the presence of  transition metal (TM) compounds. The core is the nominally pure

question: What role does silicon carbide play in the oxidation resistance of the ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramic tested by Silvestroni et al. (2019) in an arc-jet wind tunnel, compared to the role of SiC discussed by Didenko and Astapov (2025) in the general oxidation mechanism of Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites?
answer: The sources provided do not mention Didenko and Astapov (2025), nor do they specifically discuss the role of silicon carbide in the oxidation resistance of ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramics tested by Silvestroni et al. (2019) in an arc-jet wind tunnel. Therefore, it is not possible to compare the role of SiC as discussed by Silvestroni et al. (2019) with that of Didenko and Astapov (2025) based on the given sources.

4. Conclusions
The aero-thermal behavior of ZrB2 ceramics containing 40 vol% of chopped carbon fiber, 5 vol% SiC and 5 vol% of various transition metal silicides, MoSi2, HfSi2 and WSi2, has been evaluated using an oxyacetylene torch at T >2000 °C and compared to a baseline composite containing only 10 vol% of SiC. All composites successfully resisted the harsh conditions and none of them shattered, nor developed columnar ZrO2-grains in the outer-scale, which is generally considered the precursor step before catastrophic material oxidation. Different front and back temperatures were achieved mainly depending on the material porosity and hence its thermal conductivity. The thickness of the growing oxide layer may have also played a role.
The material containing HfSi2 (ZBH) showed the highest capability to resist oxidation in these demanding conditions due to the formation of an adherent oxide scale with a thickness of just 40 μm, thanks to the local formation of solid HfC or HfO2. The other two silicides allowed the oxidation of a thicker portion of material, around 122-125 μm, owing to the formation of volatile Mo- and W-oxides which embrittled the zirconia outer structure. The higher front and back temperature of ZBW compared to ZBM, despite the similar oxide scale thickness, was attributed to the higher initial porosity of the former (17%) compared to that of ZBM (9%).
In view of these results, these new materials are believed to show promise and potential for use in ultra-high temperature applications such as thermal protection systems and solid/hybrid-propellant rocket nozzles.

4. Conclusions
The aero-thermal behavior of ZrB2 ceramics containing 40 vol% of chopped carbon fiber, 5 vol% SiC and 5 vol% of various transition metal silicides, MoSi2, HfSi2 and WSi2, has been evaluated using an oxyacetylene torch at T >2000 °C and compared to a baseline composite containing only 10 vol% of SiC. All composites successfully resisted the harsh conditions and none of them shattered, nor developed columnar ZrO2-grains in the outer-scale, which is generally considered the precursor step before catastrophic material oxidation. Different front and back temperatures were achieved mainly depending on the material porosity and hence its thermal conductivity. The thickness of the growing oxide layer may have also played a role.
The material containing HfSi2 (ZBH) showed the highest capability to resist oxidation in these demanding conditions due to the formation of an adherent oxide scale with a thickness of just 40 μm, thanks to the local formation of solid HfC or HfO2. The other two silicides allowed the oxidation of a thicker portion of material, around 122-125 μm, owing to the formation of volatile Mo- and W-oxides which embrittled the zirconia outer structure. The higher front and back temperature of ZBW compared to ZBM, despite the similar oxide scale thickness, was attributed to the higher initial porosity of the former (17%) compared to that of ZBM (9%).
In view of these results, these new materials are believed to show promise and potential for use in ultra-high temperature applications such as thermal protection systems and solid/hybrid-propellant rocket nozzles.

4.1.1. Carbon continuous fiber composite oxidation
A few studies have conducted other forms of oxidation evaluation on continuous  Cf  UHTCMCs.  Baker  et  al.  [254]  explored  variable  angle oxyacetylene torch testing, in which the angle of attack was varied from 90 ◦ (traditional perpendicular setup) to 10 ◦ (more oblique). While not significantly different from traditional oxyacetylene torch approaches, the  variable  angle  fixture  allows  for  more  flexibility  in  the  setup,  in which testing conditions could be adjusted to more accurately capture the application environment of an aerostructure. In another study, Li et al. [189] showed the strong oxidation resistance of Cf/ZrC -SiC composites when exposed to a plasma wind tunnel. Finally, Mungiguerra et al. [255] subjected Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composites to an arc-jet wind tunnel that  simulated  supersonic  environment.  The  test  conditions  included enthalpies up to 20 MJ/kg, heat fluxes up to 5 MW/m 2 , and temperatures  exceeding  2100 ◦ C.  Consistent  with  results  from  oxyacetylene torch exposure, the composites formed a ZrO2 -SiO2 scale on the exposed surface  which  effectively  protected  the  bulk  sample  from  further oxidation. These results are encouraging for implementation of UHTCMCs in ultra-high temperature applications; however, a greater suite of results from advanced oxidation testing is still needed to validate the performance of these composites.
The various reports in literature indicate the capacity of continuous Cf  UHTCMCs to withstand high temperature, oxidizing environments. When the architecture is adequately densified with a chemically resistant matrix chemistry, the composites demonstrate a capacity for surviving ultra-high temperature conditions. However, there is still the key consideration  of  balancing  a  dense,  protective  matrix  with  the  weak fiber-matrix interactions that are desirable for mechanical flexibility and toughness.  Certain  studies  have  demonstrated  that  this  is  possible, which is very promising for the future of UHTCMCs with continuous carbon fiber reinforcement.

1. Introduction
Carbon /uniFB01 ber reinforced silicon carbide (C/SiC) composites are considered as potential candidate materials for high temperature structural components due to their low density, high strength, excellent corrosion resistance and good fracture toughness [1 e 3]. However, at higher temperatures ( > 1200 /C14 C), the oxidation behavior of the C/SiC composites is cooperatively in /uniFB02 uenced by the oxidation of the /uniFB01 bers, inter-phase and matrix [4,5]. Consequently, C/SiC composites require anti-oxidation protection when used at a higher temperature environment.
In the family of Ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), two of the most interesting materials for thermal shielding applications are zirconium diboride (ZrB2) and zirconium carbide (ZrC), due to their extremely high melting temperatures, high hardness, and high retained strength at high temperature [6]. ZrB2 e SiC and ZrC e SiC ceramics have been proved as effective coatings for protecting carbon materials at high temperature [5 e 8].
The anti-oxidation ability of coatings is commonly tested at an invariable temperature [4 e 8]. However, most of space vehicles have to work in low-earth orbit environment, such as thermal shock, high vacuum (10 /C0 7 Pa), atomic oxygen, and so on. Vacuum is
* Corresponding author. Tel.: þ 86 731 84575163; fax: þ 86 731 84576578. E-mail address: huangluming1019@gmail.com (L.-m. Huang).

11. Ablation properties
Arc jet testing is often used to replicate the atmospheric re-entry conditions and to assess material behaviour in hypersonic applications. Zhang et al. [359] reported the ablation behaviour of hot pressed ZrB2-20 vol% SiC in ground simulated atmospheric reentry conditions using arc-jet testing with heat fluxes of 1.7 MW/m 2 and 5.4 MW/m 2 . There was little mass or configuration change after ablation at the low heat flux condition, where the surface temperature reached up to 1700 °C. Nevertheless, at higher heat flux, the ZrB2-SiC composite underwent severe ablation (see Fig. 32) and whose surface temperatures exceeded 2300 °C. The authors compared the performance of ZrB2-SiC composite with those of C/SiC composite in the simulated re-entry environment and reported that the ZrB2-based composite possess better thermal-oxidative and configurational stability. ZrB2 containing 20 vol% SiC showed superior ablation resistance properties compared to those containing 10 and 30 vol% SiC due to its good thermal properties and presence of Zr-oxide and Si-oxide in adequate amounts to form ZrSiO4 [369]. This ZrSiO4 further decomposes to form ZrO2 and liquid at high temperature, which further suppress oxidation. In another work, Zhang et al. [437] also reported the effect of SiC content (15, 30 vol%) on the ablation behaviour of ZrB2 at 3000 °C for 100 s. Under those conditions, it was observed that ZrB2-15 vol% SiC suffered from lesser oxidation and mass loss, as compared to ZrB2-30 vol% SiC. The ablation resistance was found to decrease upon considerable mass loss and void formation, which, in turn, is caused by fast evaporation of SiO2. The surface appearance of ablation tested samples is shown in Fig. 33. In the case of ZrB2-15 vol% SiC, the surface appeared to be a continuous ablation layer with a speckle pattern in the outer region and a splashing spot in the central ablation region.

(2021) for continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composites compare to the ablation performance data Didenko and Astapov (2025) report for 3D Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites exposed to oxyacetylene torch flames with different heat fluxes?
answer: The sources provided do not mention Liu et al. (2021) or Didenko and Astapov (2025), and therefore, there is no information available to compare the crack-tolerance and toughening mechanisms of continuous Cf/ZrB2-based composites with the ablation performance data of 3D Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites exposed to oxyacetylene torch flames with different heat fluxes.

4.1.1. Carbon continuous fiber composite oxidation
Given the high exposure that continuous Cf  UHTCMCs have had in literature,  a  large  number  of  studies  have  been  catalogued  on  the oxidation  behavior  of  these  composites.  Most  of  these  investigations have adopted oxyacetylene torch testing for the baseline standard, as this  approach  can  achieve  high  temperature  and  heat  fluxes  while allowing for comparisons to similar tests performed on bulk ceramics. Fig. 22 offers an array of different continuous Cf compositions that has been exposed to an oxyacetylene flame, showing different degrees of damage and recession [232]. Some groups have employed other techniques for measuring ablation resistance. Sciti et al. [200] used arc-jet testing  to  evaluate  Cf/ZrB2 -SiC  composites  reinforced  with  either PAN- or pitch-based fiber preforms. The authors reported that both types of composites displayed good ablation resistance, although samples with pitch-based fibers achieved higher densification by SI + HP, resulting in superior oxidation performance.
As with the other types of UHTCMCs discussed in this review, the Zrbased systems dominate much of the literature for continuous Cf/UHTC systems. Across the board, good ablation performance has been reported for these composites, fabricated typically by SI, PIP, and/or CVI [193, 197,215,216,233 -240].  Cf/ZrB2 -SiC  systems  produced  by  SI + HP processes were shown to have stable oxidation behavior for both static furnace and oxyacetylene torch environments [233,234]. When samples M. 3. Results and discussion
Fig.  10  summarises  the  ablation  resistance  performance  of  the SiBCN-based  materials  and  other  thermal  protection  materials.  In Fig.  10,  the  linear  ablation  rate  of  materials  is  generally  high  at  the ablation temperature of 2000 ◦ C. Moreover, the test time of oxyacetylene is relatively short ( < 30 s), so it is difficult for the test materials to reach the real response temperature. It shows that it is not suitable for the long-term application of SiBCN-type ceramics system materials with low  density  in  the  aerobic  environment  above  2000 ◦ C.  Through comprehensive analysis of material density, ablation temperature/time, linear ablation rate, and other parameters, it can be found that the Cf/ SiBCNZr composites prepared in this work have lightweight and excellent ablation resistance [1 -6,13 -19,30,39 -49].
Fig. 11 shows the XRD patterns of Cf/SiBCN and Cf/SiBCNZr composites after ablation in an oxyacetylene torch at different temperatures. Obviously, with the increase in oxyacetylene temperature, the amorphous phase of Cf/SiBCN and Cf/SiBCNZr composites decreases gradually. Cf/SiBCN composites are still in an amorphous state after being ablated by oxyacetylene at 1700 ◦ C. In this ablation process, with the invasion of a large amount of oxygen, the SiC phase on the surface of the composites  is  oxidized  to  form  a  large  amount  of  SiO2  covering  the surface of the composites. According to the XRD phase characterization of the Cf/SiBCNZr composites, it can be determined that these white Y. Lyu et al.

4.11.1. Combination of different liquid state techniques

[261] investigated that composites, made via a combination of PIP with RMI, showed superior ablation resistance due to a solid solution layer, achieving low mass (0.544 mg/s) and linear (0.107 μ m/s) ablation rates after 600 s of oxyacetylene  flame  exposure  at  4.18  MW/m²,  highlighting  effective long-term anti-ablation design. Wang et al. [262] developed Cf/ZrB2-ZrC-SiC sharp leading-edge composites, via combined precursor infiltration,  pyrolysis,  and  reactive  melt infiltration,  exhibit  excellent ablation resistance due to B2O3  glass formation from ZrB2  oxidation, which repairs defects and stabilizes the oxide layer, reducing linear and mass ablation rates by 63 % and 57 % compared to Cf/ZrC-SiC composites after 120 s of ablation. Wen et al. [263] created C/C-ZiC-ZrC-SiC composites using PIP and RMI with a ZiC interlayer, showed a 50.54 % increase in flexural strength, a 56.76 % increase in tensile strength, and enhanced ablation performance at 2200 ◦ C by protecting carbon fibers and PyC from erosion and weakening the ceramic matrix bond for stress release. Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composites were produced via water-based slurry impregnation and polymer infiltration/pyrolysis, followed by annealing treatments, identifying 1400 ◦ C as the optimal temperature to balance mechanical  performance  and  microstructural  stability,  with  87  % strength retention at 1500 ◦ C [264]. Cf/SiC and Cf/ZrC-SiC composites were fabricated using a combined process, achieving lower porosity ( < 8 %) compared to single PIP but higher residual thermal stresses caused by a higher processing temperature (1700 ◦ C) [265]. Certain  studies  have  demonstrated  that  this  is  possible, which is very promising for the future of UHTCMCs with continuous carbon fiber reinforcement.

4.1.1. Carbon continuous fiber composite oxidation
were produced by the novel ' ferroconcrete ' approach -which combined unique slurry processing with CVI and PIP -composites were noted to have negligible ablation effects, despite experiencing temperatures as high 2500 ◦ C for over 10 min. This is noteworthy as it confirms that UHTCMCs can achieve outstanding mechanical properties and oxidation resistance  in  the  same  composite  [215].  For  the  Cf/ZrC -SiC  system, multiple investigations reported its superior oxidation resistance over traditional Cf/SiC composites, owed to the formation of both ZrO2 and SiO2  in  the  oxide  scale  [193,197,238 -240]. Samples containing both ZrB2 and ZrC did not demonstrate any significant benefits over Cf/ZrC or Cf/ZrB2  systems, although the oxidation performance was observed to improve when the Cf/ZrB2 -ZrC -SiC composite was supplemented by SiC nanowires and whiskers [216,236,237]. The ablation performance of these composites was further scrutinized in terms of temperature and heat flux. Li et al. [235] reported that Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composites were well protected below 2300 ◦ C because of the presence of both SiO2 and ZrO2. Above this temperature, SiO2  would volatize, and the oxidation resistance would become dependent on the ZrO2  scale. However, at high enough heat fluxes, the zirconia layer would be vulnerable to ablation, leading to considerable material recession during testing.

question: How does the tungsten-rich sub-surface architecture observed by Silvestroni et al. (2019) after arc-jet exposure of ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramics compare to the densification-porosity relationship Didenko and Astapov (2025) describe for Cf/CeZrC composites made by CLVD at different deposition temperatures?
answer: The tungsten-rich sub-surface architecture observed by Silvestroni et al. (2019) after arc-jet exposure of ZrB2-WC-SiC ceramics forms a cell-like multi-layered structure typical of tungsten, which contributes to the material's strength retention at high temperatures [4]. In contrast, Didenko and Astapov (2025) describe that for Cf/CeZrC composites made by CLVD at different deposition temperatures, the ablation resistance is significantly influenced by the densification and porosity of the composite. Specifically, composites obtained at 900 °C showed excellent densification behavior, leading to lower linear and mass ablation rates compared to those obtained at 1100 °C [2]. Thus, while Silvestroni et al. focus on the microstructural changes due to tungsten compounds enhancing high-temperature strength, Didenko and Astapov emphasize the relationship between the deposition temperature and the resulting densification, which affects ablation resistance.

3.5. ZrB2  and HfB2; W and Nb inclusions

[46] postulated phase immiscibility may be increasing the viscosity of the outer glass scale at 2000 ◦ C or during cool down but no high temperature phase diagrams for WO3 with silica or boria are available. Although the WC and WB modifications were only 3 vol%, it was hypothesized that due to the solubility of W in HfB2 and HfC being between 4 and 40 mol%, there could easily be localized regions of W that, when oxidized, could create regions of HfO2-WO3  well beyond the 5 mol% solid solubility at 2000 ◦ C. This could result in liquid-phase sintering of the HfO2. However, because the solid solubility of WO3 in HfO2 reaches a maximum of approximately 7 mol% at ~1280 ◦ C before forming a liquid phase, densification of the porous HfO2 region could also occur at 1600 ◦ and  1800 ◦ C,  but  no  densification  was  reported  at  either temperature.
During 1650 ◦ C air oxidation of ZrB2-15 vol%WSi2, Silvestroni et al. didn ' t identify any W in crystalline ZrO2  but eutectic formation in the ZrO2-WO3  system  couldn ' t  be  concretely  discounted  as  a  reaction product [47]. Based on extensive TEM and EDS characterization, Silvestroni  et  al.  proposed  that  W-modification  may  not  provide  any notable reduction in the oxidation front advancement. In a further study utilizing arc-jet testing, Silvestroni et al. found that while 5 vol% additions of WC to ZrB2-5 vol%SiC are beneficial for strength retention above 2000 ◦ C, a sub-surface architecture resembling pure W forms, meaning that the volume fraction of W-compounds may need to be well below 5 vol% [103].

3.1.3. Effect of technological process parameters on ablation behavior of the composites
The technological parameters of the process of obtaining composites and their effect on the ablation properties are also of interest to many scientists. A number of studies have identi /uniFB01 ed correlations between the microstructure and, as a consequence, ablation resistance of composites and the temperature of the synthesis process [55,62,70,72], pH [68], or the number of stages, for example, PIP cycles [45,52,64,65,202]. Thus, He et al. [55] investigated the effect of different deposition temperatures (800 e 1100 /C14 C) on densi /uniFB01 cation behavior and ablation properties of Cf/C e ZrC composites under oxyacetylene /uniFB02 ame with a heat /uniFB02 ux of 4.2 MW/ m 2 for 60 s. The results showed that the mass gain, densi /uniFB01 cation rate, and density of the samples increased when the deposition temperature was elevated from 800 to 900 /C14 C and then decreased with further temperature increase. For Cf/C e ZrC obtained at 900 /C14 C, there was an excellent densi /uniFB01 cation behavior in the CLVD process due to the proper deposition temperature [55]. It is because the uniform distribution of a considerable amount of ZrC ceramics within the sample and the formation of a substantial quantity of ZrO2, which exhibited a high capacity to absorb heat and consequently reduced the thermochemical corrosion of carbon /uniFB01 bers. Furthermore, the homogenous distribution of ZrC obtained by the CLVD at 900 /C14 C provided the formation of a compact ZrO2 layer, which inhibited the mass transfer of oxidizing agents and enhanced the resistance to mechanical denudation [55]. The linear and mass ablation rates of the composite obtained at 900 /C14 C were found to be 62.52 and 70.77 % lower, respectively, than those of the composite obtained at 1100 /C14 C.

3.1.1. Effect of preparation method on ablation behavior of the composites
The most common approach to the creation of UHTCMCs is the introduction of UHTC phases, such as ZrC, ZrB2, HfC, HfB2, TaC, and TaB2, into a matrix of Cf/C or Cf/SiC composites using polymer in /uniFB01 ltration and pyrolisis, chemical vapor in /uniFB01 ltration, or reactive melt in /uniFB01 ltration methods [18,20,28,32,34,35,40 e 44,59 e 61,72,96,101,105, 110,115,145,154,191 e 193,196,224,229]. However, direct comparison of the ablation resistance of composites of the same composition obtained by different methods is limited to a few studies. He et al. [58] investigated the effects of the joint processes of chemical liquid-vapor deposition (CLVD) and precursor in /uniFB01 ltration and pyrolysis on the densi /uniFB01 cation, microstructure, and ablation properties of Cf/ C e ZrC e SiC composites. The results demonstrated that the deposition in the CLVD process occurred preferentially in the non-woven layers of the composites, while the subsequent PIP cycle resulted in the densi /uniFB01 cation of the /uniFB01 ber webs. Thus, the joint processes could markedly enhance the density of the composites and the ceramic content [58]. When the deposition period was 8 h, the Cf/C e ZrC e SiC composite exhibited low porosity, high content and uniform distribution of ceramics, which was responsible to its exceptional ablationresistance. Afterablation for60s ata surface temperature of~ 2500 /C14 C, a continuous and gas-tight oxide /uniFB01 lmwasformed on the Cf/ C e ZrC e SiC composite obtained by joint processes of CLVD and PIP [58].

A B S T R A C T
A super-strong ZrB2 ceramic containing WC and SiC was tested in a supersonic arc-jet wind tunnel by exposure to /uniFB02 ows with two chemical compositions, simulated air or pure nitrogen, at temperatures of 2650 and 2800 K. Temperature jumps of 500 -600 K were observed in both environments at constant /uniFB02 ow conditions. SEM analyses revealed that oxygen in the high-enthalpy /uniFB02 ow retards the material consumption owing to the formation of partially protective glass. Then, it appears that even 5 vol% of W-compounds is su /uniFB03 cient to modify the subscale oxide con /uniFB01 guration and form a cell-like multi-layered architecture typical of tungsten, rather than that typical of ZrB2.

In this respect, it has been recently assessed that suitable microstructure tailoring and exploitation of the core-shell structure can lead to materials possessing strength above 900 MPa at 1800 °C and remain over 600 at 2100 °C [21], due to in situ grain re /uniFB01 nement and local toughening.

The application of ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) coatings is  an  effective  way  to  improve  the  in-service  performance  of  CMC structures  at  higher  operating  temperatures  and  for  longer  duration without  dramatically  hampering  the  overall  weight  of  the  structure. Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is the preferred compound, because of its excellent combination of high melting temperature, high thermal conductivity (40 -100 W/m K) [9 -14], and superior ablation [15 -18] and erosion resistance [19,20] . Particularly, ceramics in the ZrB2 - WC - SiC system have shown to possesses outstanding strength retention up to 2100 ◦ C [21] and high thermal erosion resistance at 2600 -2800 ◦ C [22, 23]. As compared to pure ZrB2, these properties seem to be related first to  a  better  oxidation  resistance  of  the  (Zr,W)B2  solid  solution grown around the original ZrB2  cores upon sintering compared to pure ZrB2 [24,25], coupled with the formation of an outermost more protective oxide scale, where possible stabilization of B2O3 glass by W occurs [26], and with the formation of a more dense subscale, where ZrO2  grains encapsulate W solid products, as a consequence of the (Zr,W)B2  solid solution oxidation. question: Comparing Guo et al. 2024's machine-learning study of ZrB2-SiC oxidation with Li et al. 2025's optimization of the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C ablative coating system, what R2 value did Guo's support vector machine regression model achieve, and what elemental composition did Li identify as giving the best long-term ablation resistance?
answer: The provided sources do not mention Guo et al. 2024's study or its R2 value. Additionally, while Li et al. 2025 identified the (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C composition as giving the best long-term ablation resistance, the specific R2 value for Guo's support vector machine regression model is not available in the given sources.
