4. Conclusions
Relationships  between  element  types,  composition  and  ablation property in the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating systems have been discussed step by  step  via  SAPS  coating  preparation  and  performance  tests  under oxyacetylene ablation (2.4 MW/m 2 ). The synergistic effects of Hf, Zr, Ta and  Nb  elements  were  studied  through  different  procedures:  data collection, mechanism analysis, composition optimization and experiment  verification,  where  an  optimized  coating  system  for  long-term ablation protection above 2000 ◦ C was obtained. Main conclusions are as follows:
- (1)  Non-equimolar design based on (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating systems was found to be related to intrinsic porosity and self-derived low-
Fig. 12. Surface BSE images (a) and cross-sectional BSE images (b-c) of Zr70Nb10, Nb10 and Zr20Nb10 coatings after 300 s ablation.
melting-point phases. When the content ratio of high-meltingpoint  (Hf/Zr-based  oxides)  and  low-melting-point  phases  (Ta/ Nb-based oxides) is close to 1:1 in the as-prepared coatings systems (~15 % porosity), they are difficult to provide long-term effective protection ( < 120 s) above 2000 ◦ C.
- (2)  The decline of Ta or Nb relative content in the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating  systems  was  found  effective  in  improving  ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases on the coating structure.
- (3)  The optimized (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C coating system exhibited the best  long-term  ablation  property  (-0.01 μ m/s,  300 s),  corresponding to the Zr/Hf molar ratio around 1:1 in the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system via first-principles calculation screening and experimental verification.

A B S T R A C T
Recent advancements in machine learning (ML) offer new opportunities for accelerating the design of oxidation-resistant UHTCs. Bianco et al. [15] developed a random forest regression model to predict oxide layer thickness after oxidation tests based on composition, processing parameters, and microstructural features. Trained on 107 experimental data points extracted from literature, the model achieved a mean absolute error (MAE) of 37.45 μ m and an R² of 0.83. However, data heterogeneity  from  diverse  sources  introduced  uncontrollable  biases, limiting its predictive accuracy and capacity to identify superior compositions. Hao et al. [16] explored multiple ML algorithms to predict oxidation/ablation  resistance  of  UHTC  coatings,  demonstrating  that random forest regression achieved the best performance (R² = 0.87) using a high-fidelity experimental dataset. Nevertheless, conventional ML models demand large datasets and exhibit constrained predictive accuracy under small-sample conditions. To address these challenges, strategies such as data augmentation (e.g., generative adversarial networks  (GANs)[17,18],  variational  autoencoders  (VAEs)[19]),  active learning[20 -22], Bayesian optimization[23], and transfer learning[24] have been proposed to alleviate the problem of underfitting caused by sparse  samples  and  enhance  model  robustness.  Among  them,  active learning has great potential in effectively identifying potential high-performance  materials  and  in  the  exploration  of  formulae.  Kim et al. [25] used active learning combined with experiments to search for the optimal multi-metal alloy catalyst. Feng et al. [26] utilized Bayesian optimization to achieve the best molecular formula, thereby accelerating the discovery of a high-performance methane combustion catalyst composed of multi-component PtPd@CeZrOx core-shell nanospheres.
Kim et al. [27] accelerated the discovery of the solvation structure of stable  aqueous  rechargeable  zinc  batteries  through  physically-guided Bayesian active learning.

1. Introduction
Efficient and appropriate composition design of multi-elemental UHTCs for extremely thermal protection is in great demand and promising for carbon-based materials. Based on (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C protective coating system via plasma spraying, the synergistic effects were clarified through several steps: data collection, mechanism analysis, composition optimization and experiment verification. Results showed that, (Hf0.25Zr0.25Ta0.25Nb0.25)C coating exhibited insufficient protective ability (2000 ◦ C, < 120 s), due to the similar content ratio of high-melting-point and low-melting-point phases. The decline of Ta or Nb relative content effectively improved ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2  skeleton phases. After further screening for the proper composition of Hf, Zr, Ta and Nb elements, the (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C protective system was optimized and verified step by step, which delightfully showed long-term ablation property (2000 ◦ C, > 300 s) with nearly zero linear ablation rate.

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high-temperature  ceramic  coatings  are  critical  for  thermal  protection  systems  in  hypersonic  vehicles. However,  their  compositional  optimization  is  constrained  by  the  inefficiency  of  traditional  trial-and-error method. In this study, a machine learning-driven design strategy is proposed for the ZrC-TaC-SiC ternary system (denoted Z n T m S t , n , m , t = 0 -100) to enhance ablation resistance. A random forest regression model was constructed using 170 literature data to predict surface ablation temperature, mass ablation rate, and linear ablation rate across the full compositional space of the ZrC-TaC-SiC ternary system. Four optimized formulations (Z60T30S10, Z60T10S30, Z80T15S5, Z80T5S15), each exhibiting stable mass and linear ablation rates in the order of 10 GLYPH<0> 4 g/s and 10 GLYPH<0> 4 mm/s, respectively, were selected through the model and fabricated via supersonic atmospheric plasma spraying. Oxyacetylene ablation test results confirmed the reliability of the model, with prediction  errors  for  linear  ablation  rate  within  the  order  of  10 GLYPH<0> 5 mm/s.  Microstructural  characterization revealed that high-TaC content (15 -30 wt%) led to excessive formation of Zr6Ta2O17 during ablation, resulting in reduced thermal stability of the oxide scale and discontinuities in structure. Moreover, non-uniform sintering caused  an  increase  in  porosity.  Interestingly,  the  low-TaC  (5  wt%)  Z80T5S15  sample  achieved  a  dense  and continuous oxide layer by a proper Zr/Ta content ratio in oxide phase, exhibiting excellent ablation resistance. This study establishes a machine-learning -assisted strategy combined with experimental validation, which can provide a new paradigm for intelligent design and performance prediction of ultra-high-temperature ceramic coatings.

1. Introduction
considered  promising.  Wang  et  al.  [25] reported  that  the  oxidation resistance of (Hf-Ta-Zr-Nb)C ceramic benefited from synergistic effect of multiple components, resulting in greater structural stability and higher onset temperature than monocarbides. Ye et al. [31,32] reported the high-temperature oxidation behavior of (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)C at 800 -1500 ◦ C in air, exhibiting a laminated structure in the oxide layers due to the residual thermal stress and differential creep temperatures. However,  unstable  precipitation  of  low-melting-point  phases  and increased porosity in the oxide scales are also common due to inappropriate elemental molar ratios [22,26,33]. Lv et al. [33] found the synergistic effects of Ti-dominated liquid phase and Ta-induced lamellar architecture  but  only  reported  the  short-term  ablation  property  of (Hf-Zr-Ta-Ti)B2 coating, suggesting further adjustment of Me content for more extreme conditions. Hence, non-equimolar design for utilizing the synergistic effects of multi-elements and clarifying their mechanisms to empower improved high-temperature oxidation resistance are worthy of more in-depth discussion and analysis.
In  this  work,  a  combination  of  first-principles  calculations  and experimental  analyses  were  proposed  to  conduct  the  optimization design. The detailed procedures are shown in Fig. 1. The (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C system was primarily selected and optimized on the basis of A-B-O binary systems calculation [34], accompanied by experimental verification. Subsequently, the mechanism of various element types and complex morphology were investigated. Then guided by the optimization  idea,  a  non-equimolar  coating  system  was  proposed  and  its long-duration oxyacetylene ablation property was further verified. This work is expected to provide a more insightful composition design route for thermal protection systems of carbon-based materials.

question: How does the role of SiC as a second-phase additive in ZrB2-SiC ceramics, as studied by Guo et al. 2024, compare to the general oxidation-resistance limitations of high entropy ceramics (HEBs, HENs, HECbs) described in Ujah and Olubambi 2025?
answer: The role of SiC as a second-phase additive in ZrB2-SiC ceramics, as studied by Guo et al. 2024, is not directly mentioned in the provided sources. However, the general oxidation-resistance limitations of high entropy ceramics (HEBs, HENs, HECbs) described in Ujah and Olubambi 2025 include incomplete oxidation resistance above 1500 ◦ C [2]. In contrast, studies such as those by Hassan et al. 2024 indicate that the addition of SiC can enhance the properties of high entropy ceramics, such as densification and mechanical properties, although specific oxidation resistance improvements in this context are not detailed in the provided sources.

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
The advent of high entropy alloys (HEAs) has considerably improved the advancement of materials science and engineering, especially in the enhancement of sustainable structural and functional materials. HEAs are class of alloys discovered in the 2000s by Cantor and Yeh which consist of five (5) or more principal elements combined in equi-atomic or near equi-atomic proportions [1,2]. High Entropy Ceramics (HECs), on the other hand, are a class of composite material consisting of HEA matrix and ceramic reinforcement dispersed as the secondary phase on the  matrix  [3].  Contrary  to  conventional  alloys  and  ceramics  that contain a single principal element, HEAs and HECs must contain at least five principal elements. The multiple principal elements stimulate high entropy  effect  in  the  composite  system,  promoting  the  evolution  of stable single phase solid solution microstructure, and creating unique chemical, mechanical, thermal and tribological properties [4 -7].
HECs, especially high entropy borides (HEBs), high entropy nitrides
* Corresponding author.
E-mail addresses: chikau@uj.ac.za (C.O. Ujah), polubambi@uj.ac.za (P.A. Olubambi).
2949-8228/© 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY license (  http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/  ).

6. Conclusion and recommendations
This research has critically evaluated the structural, thermal, mechanical, electrical, and functional characteristics of high entropy borides (HEBs), carbides (HECbs), and nitrides (HENs) and their future applications. The results prove that the synergistic role of configurational entropy, slow diffusion, distortion of the lattice, and cocktail effects  are  the  basis  of  the  excellent  hardness,  thermal  stability,  and oxidation resistance of these ceramics. HECbs have the best hardness and  better  tunability  via  compositional  and  stoichiometric  control, making them useful in cutting and machining tools; HENs have the best fracture toughness and stiffness enabling applications in cyclic loading and strain-resistant applications, like turbine blades, spacecraft actuators and nuclear reactor casing. The HEBs, in their turn, are characterized by their high resistance to oxidation, thermal conductivity, and the possibility to operate in an environment with ultra-high temperatures, making  it  useful  in  aerospace  and  harsh  electronics/photovoltaic applications.
Notwithstanding the prospective advantages of the studied HECs, we identified some issues militating against their optimal performance and their universal adoption as the next generation advanced materials. The limitations include: (i) the low fracture toughness of most compositions, (ii) incomplete oxidation resistance above 1500 ◦ C, (iii) difficulties in scaling up the synthesis process and (iv) a lack of data on long-term service performance with cyclic thermal, oxidative and irradiation environments. However, their new use in aerospace, hypersonic planes, nuclear technology, cutting tools, and electronic equipment highlight their  transformational  capacity  in  very  harsh  conditions.  When  optimized, high entropy ceramics have the potential to traverse the chasm between discovery-based material science research and high-scale industrial application.
The research, therefore, recommends the following:

A B S T R A C T
in average values of 43.0 ± 2.9 GPa for the HEB and 37.4 ± 2.3 GPa for the HEC. The microhardness value reported by the authors was 29.4 GPa at 9.81 N, which is the highest value reported so far in any dual phase high entropy UHTC.
A few studies have introduced second phase particles of SiC into high entropy ceramics [16 -19], but the emphasis mainly has been on the synthesis of single-phase ceramics or, recently, dual phase, ceramics. The  addition  of  a  reinforcing  second  phase  in  the  form  of  particles, whiskers or fibres is known for strengthening and toughening the ceramics. For example, the addition of 10 -30 vol% SiC promotes densification in UHTCs [20 -22]. A study on 20 vol% SiC addition in an HEC carbide  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)C  showed  higher  relative  density  in the SiC reinforced (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)C ceramic as compared to (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)C, throughout the sintering temperature range [19].  Zhang  et  al.  observed  a  higher  relative  density  ( > 99  %)  in (Hf0.2Zr0.2Mo0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)B2 and (Hf0.2Mo0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)B2 ceramics with 20 vol% SiC [18] when compared to the same ceramics without SiC (95% -98.5 %) additions [23,24]. Thus, the current study involves preparing (Hf,Ta,Ti,W,Zr)B2-(Hf,Ta,Ti,W,Zr)C dual phase ceramics with and without SiC incorporation, to investigate the SiC effect on densification, mechanical properties, and low temperature thermal characteristics of the given dual phase high entropy ceramic.

1. Introduction
In  recent  years,  there  has  been  a  growing  demand  for  materials capable to withstand extreme conditions encountered in aerospace applications. This has led to research efforts focusing on a novel category of refractory ceramics known as ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) [1 -4]. These ceramics consist of carbides, borides and nitrides of early transition metals, characterized by a melting point exceeding 3000 ◦ C [5]. Among the extensively studied UHTCs, ZrB2  stands out due to its high  mechanical  properties  (strength  and  toughness)  and  moderate density, 6.1 g/cm 3 [6 -10]. Its unique characteristics make it suitable for various  applications  susceptible  to  wear,  such  as  engine  valves  and machine tools [11 -13]. ZrB2 can also be combined with a wide range of secondary phases to improve densification and oxidation resistance. In particular, ZrB2-SiC composites were extensively studied for aerospace applications. The addiction of SiC particles in the range of 10 -30 vol% enhances  the  mechanical  properties  such  as  strength  and  oxidation resistance [14]. However, the primary limitation of UHTC materials is their low fracture toughness, which restricts their application in environments  with  thermal  shocks  and  vibrations  [15],  particularly  in tribological fields. One possible approach to improve damage tolerance
* Corresponding authors.
- E-mail addresses: matteo.mor@issmc.cnr.it (M. Mor), matthias.meiser@gmx.de (M. Meiser).
1 These authors contributed equally to this work.

0955-2219/© 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
Available online 4 June 2024

A B S T R A C T
Second phases, such as MoB [13], Si3N4 [14] and SiC [15], are widely reported to be added to improve the mechanical properties and/ or oxidation resistance of MoSi2 ceramic at elevated temperatures and admirable results have been achieved [16,17]. Among those potential additives, SiC shows a great advantage in strengthening the mechanical properties and oxidation resistance of MoSi2 ceramic. Unfortunately, because the electrical conductivity of SiC is much lower than MoSi2, SiC additives always leads to a huge decrease in electrical conductivity of MoSi2 ceramic, limiting the application for heating element. Thus, it is necessary that a third phase should be added into MoSi2 ceramic in order to obtain the ceramic that has good mechanical properties, good electrical properties, and excellent oxidation resistance behavior simultaneously. Recently, ZrB2 ceramic with 20 vol% SiC additive, known as ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC), have been widely reported to exhibit excellent mechanical properties, oxidation resistance and thermal shock resistance behaviors [18 -20]. Besides, in our previous work, ZrB2-20 vol% SiC ceramic was also found to have excellent electrical properties [21]. Therefore, it is believed that the ultra-high temperature heating elements, which has not only high mechanical strength, but also excellent electrical conductivity, can be obtained by adding ZrB2-20 vol% SiC ceramic into MoSi2 ceramic. However, to the best knowledge of the authors, there is no reports about the MoSi2 ceramic containing ZrB2-20 vol% SiC additive for ultra-high temperature heating element applications.

question: In terms of high-temperature protective performance, how does the reduction of Ta or Nb content in Li et al. 2025's (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C ablative coatings compare with the general thermal conductivity behavior of high entropy carbides (HECbs) described by Ujah and Olubambi 2025?
answer:
In terms of high-temperature protective performance, the reduction of Ta or Nb content in Li et al. 2025's (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C ablative coatings was found to be effective in improving ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases on the coating structure [5]. This suggests that lower Ta or Nb content enhances the material's ability to withstand ablation at high temperatures.

In contrast, the general thermal conductivity behavior of high entropy carbides (HECbs), as described by Ujah and Olubambi 2025, indicates that thermal conductivity tends to rise with temperature. For instance, a five-cation HECb showed increased thermal conductivity up to 1200 ◦ C, and the thermal conductivity of (Hf, Ta, Zr, Nb)C HECb rose from 10.7 W/mK at ambient to 39.9 W/mK at 2000 ◦ C [1]. However, altering carbon stoichiometry in some HECbs can lead to a decline in thermal conductivity due to increased scattering of electron and phonon by carbon vacancies [1].

Thus, while the reduction of Ta or Nb content in (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coatings improves ablation resistance, the thermal conductivity behavior of HECbs is influenced by factors such as temperature and carbon stoichiometry, which may have different implications for high-temperature performance.

chunks.text:
A) Thermal properties of high entropy carbides
High entropy carbides (HECbs) form a class of ultra-high temperature  ceramics  with  distinct  thermal  behavior,  making  them  more appropriate than traditional ceramic materials in high temperatures and extreme environment applications like those of hypersonic cars and high temperature structures. It was reported that the thermal conductivity of HECbs  tends  to  rise  with  temperature.  Just  as  a  five-cation  HECb exhibited increased thermal conductivity up to 1200 ◦ C, which is in line with single-metal carbides [43]. More so, there was a report that the thermal conductivity of (Hf, Ta, Zr, Nb)C HECb rose from 10.7 W/mK at ambient to 39.9 W/mK at 2000 ◦ C as a result of electron influences [81]. Further research shows that thermal conductivity of HECbs is affected by the amount of carbon in the composite. As such, carbon deposition exceeding the stoichiometric quantity showed decline in thermal conductivity  with  retention  of  some  temperature-dependent  curve  [43]. Meanwhile, altering carbon stoichiometry in (TiZrHfVNbTa)Cx (x = 0.6 -1) showed a decline in the thermal conductivity from 10.6 W/mK at ambient to 6.4 W/mK because of increased scattering of electron and phonon by carbon vacancies [82]. It was revealed also that HECbs like (HfTaZrTi)C and (HfTaZrNb)C have high thermodynamic stability with negative enthalpy of formation, which shows that they have good phase integrity  at  elevated  temperatures  [83].  Table  4 shows  the  thermal properties of some high entropy carbides.

3 High-entropy UHTC for performance optimization
Apart from metal diborides, the performance of high-entropy metal carbides has also been investigated.[93, 94] Wang et al . [88] prepared a high-entropy (TiZrNbTaMo)C ceramic by using a two-step hot pressing method with a hardness value of 25.3 GPa at 9.8 N, which was higher than that of ordinary ultra-high temperature ceramics. The lattice  distortion  caused  by  the  difference  of  atomic  size  leads  to  the  increase  of hardness, which is consistent with the principle of high entropy borides. Apart from the mechanical properties, the oxidation resistance behavior of high-entropy metal carbides is an important aspect as thermal protective coatings for space applications, especially considering the relatively poor oxidation resistance behaviors of metal carbides UHTCs comparing to metal diborides. Recently, Li et al . [95] systematically investigated the ablation  resistance  of  high-entropy  carbides,  including  (Hf-Ta)C,  (Hf-Ta-Nb)C,  and (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C, prepared via pressurelessly sintering at 2100 ℃ for 2h. After 30 s of ablation at 2.4 MW/m² oxyacetylene flame, the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C exhibited the thinnest oxide layer, only 51.5% of (Hf-Ta)C. This performance enhancement is attributed to the enrichment of Ta in HfO2/ZrO2 lattices, which forms low-melting-point phases that compromise the stability of the oxide layer, while the mutual dissolution of Zr and HfO2 forms a (Hf, Zr)O2 solid solution, effectively delaying the structural destabilization of the oxide layer. Journal Pre-proof

3.1. Ablation behavior of protective (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C system at high temperatures
As  characterized  in  Fig.  2b,  the  sprayed  Ta25Nb25  coating  is composed of carbides, (Hf, Zr)O2 and (Ta, Nb)Ox. The detailed PDF cards for  as-sprayed  coatings  with  different  composition  are  presented  in Fig. S2c. Within the 2 θ range of 30 -40 ◦ , two typical diffraction peaks of carbides were detected, corresponding to the (111) and (200) planes of their  FCC  structures,  while  some  (Ta,  Nb)Ox  oxides  (around  37.5 ◦ ) related  with  Ta  or  Nb  elements  were  also  observed.  Limited  by  the spraying process carried out in an atmospheric environment, the melted and semi-melted carbide powders were inevitably oxidized. In our previous  work  [35],  the  ball  milling  and  agglomeration  (spray  drying technique) process were found to have little effect on the oxidation of original powders. Generally, the maximum temperature during agglomeration process is about 300 ◦ C, lower than the initial oxidation temperatures of carbides. The XRD pattern of the Ta25Nb25 agglomerated powders was supplemented in Fig. S2b. It can be detected that there is no oxide observed in the agglomerated powders, suggesting that the  remaining oxides  in  the  sprayed  coatings  were  generated  by  the thermal spray process.
Fig. 2. Analytical results of Ta25Nb25 agglomerated powder and sprayed coating:(a) surface BSE images of agglomerated powders and sprayed coating; (b) XRD patterns of sprayed coating; (c1-c2) cross-sectional BSE images and corresponding TEM image.

3.1. Ablation behavior of protective (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C system at high temperatures
Fig. 4e compares the roughness and cross-sectional BSE images of different (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C ablated samples with changing Ta/Nb molar ratios. It can be seen that with the addition of Nb element, the coatings ' roughness gradually decreased and typical pore defects developed towards the interior of the coatings. Numerous pores and defects appeared in  the  oxide  layer  of  all  the  coatings  (Ta50,  Ta25Nb25  and  Nb50), indicating that none of these coatings could provide effective long-term protection ( < 120 s) in an oxyacetylene ablation environment under the heat flux of 2.4 MW/m 2 . The reason is, when the content ratio of high- melting-point (Hf/Zr-based oxides) and low-melting-point phases (Ta/ Nb-based oxides) is close to 1:1, the excessive Ta or Nb element will result  in  the  massive  loss  of  (Ta,  Nb)2O5 and  residual  (Hf,  Zr)6(Ta, Nb)2O17 oxides [48].

4. Conclusions
Relationships  between  element  types,  composition  and  ablation property in the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating systems have been discussed step by  step  via  SAPS  coating  preparation  and  performance  tests  under oxyacetylene ablation (2.4 MW/m 2 ). The synergistic effects of Hf, Zr, Ta and  Nb  elements  were  studied  through  different  procedures:  data collection, mechanism analysis, composition optimization and experiment  verification,  where  an  optimized  coating  system  for  long-term ablation protection above 2000 ◦ C was obtained. Main conclusions are as follows:
- (1)  Non-equimolar design based on (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating systems was found to be related to intrinsic porosity and self-derived low-
Fig. 12. Surface BSE images (a) and cross-sectional BSE images (b-c) of Zr70Nb10, Nb10 and Zr20Nb10 coatings after 300 s ablation.
melting-point phases. When the content ratio of high-meltingpoint  (Hf/Zr-based  oxides)  and  low-melting-point  phases  (Ta/ Nb-based oxides) is close to 1:1 in the as-prepared coatings systems (~15 % porosity), they are difficult to provide long-term effective protection ( < 120 s) above 2000 ◦ C.
- (2)  The decline of Ta or Nb relative content in the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating  systems  was  found  effective  in  improving  ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases on the coating structure.
- (3)  The optimized (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C coating system exhibited the best  long-term  ablation  property  (-0.01 μ m/s,  300 s),  corresponding to the Zr/Hf molar ratio around 1:1 in the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system via first-principles calculation screening and experimental verification.

question: How does the temperature-and-time-dependent trend of oxide layer growth in ZrB2-SiC ceramics reported by Guo et al. 2024 relate to the ablation morphology results for the Nb10-doped (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating reported by Li et al. 2025?
answer: The provided sources do not mention any study by Guo et al. 2024 on ZrB2-SiC ceramics or their temperature-and-time-dependent oxide layer growth trends. Therefore, it is not possible to relate this to the ablation morphology results for the Nb10-doped (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating reported by Li et al. 2025.

chunks.text:
3.5. Verification on ablation properties of (Hf-Zr-Nb)C systems
As shown in Table 3, as the Zr content decreases, the Nb content in the outermost layer of the oxide layer gradually increased (Spot 15, Nb/ (Hf + Zr) = 0.16;  Spot  16,  Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.29;  Spot  17,  Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.35),  which  is  consistent  with  the  EDS  analysis  results  in  surface oxide layer. Additionally, as the distance from the surface increased, the Nb content in the oxide layer initially increased  and then decreased (Spot 16, Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.29; Spot 18, Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.44; Spot 19, Nb/ (Hf + Zr) = 0.32). This trend aligns with the distribution characteristics of Ta in the Zr-Ta-O oxide layer under oxyacetylene ablation conditions as reported by Hu et al. [59], which is primarily due to the outward diffusion and surface consumption of Nb element.
Overall, the Nb10 coating in the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system exhibited the most outstanding ablation property ( > 300 s).  When  the  Nb  content remained constant (10 mol%), the ablation property of the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coatings  initially  increased  and  then  decreased  as  the  Zr  content decreased. This trend is primarily due to the optimal amount of Hf, Zr and Nb elements which enhances the densification and integrity of the oxide layer, thereby improving its ablation property.

3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
To visually compare the effect of varying Nb element content on the surface temperature distribution of coatings, Fig. 7a presents the surface temperature curve and surface temperature field distribution of Nb50, Nb20  and  Nb10  coatings  during  120 s  ablation,  where  the  surface temperatures increased with more Nb content. Fig. 7b shows the average linear ablation rate and average mass ablation rate of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10  coatings  after  120 s  ablation.  The  Nb20  and  Nb10  coatings exhibited thickness increase and weight gain and Nb10 showed smaller change in mass and thickness ( GLYPH<0> 0.65 ± 0.04 μ m/s, GLYPH<0> 0.47 mg/s) change, while Nb50 coating showed significant thickness decline. As reported by Li et al. [19], the service-derived (Hf, Zr)O2 layer of (Hf0.5Zr0.5)C coating under 2.4 MW/m 2 oxyacetylene flame was porous and loose after 120 s ablation, with an average linear ablation rate of GLYPH<0> 0.88 μ m/s, which is also higher than that of Nb10 coating in this work.
Figs. 7c1-c3 present the surface BSE images of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10
Fig. 6. The optical photos (a1-a3) and surface morphology (b1-b3) of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10 coatings after ablation.

3.5. Verification on ablation properties of (Hf-Zr-Nb)C systems
Fig. 9. The optical photo (a), surface morphology and ablation depth variation (b) of Zr70Nb10, Nb10 and Zr20Nb10 coatings after 300 s ablation.
Fig.  10. (a)  The  maximum surface temperature curves during the 300 s ablation cycles of Zr70Nb10,  Nb10 and Zr20Nb10 coatings; (b) XRD patterns of the Zr70Nb10, Nb10 and Zr20Nb10 coatings after 300 s ablation.
Fig.  11. The average linear ablation  rate  (a1)  and  mass  ablation  rate  (a2)  of  the  Zr70Nb10,  Nb10  and  Zr20Nb10  coatings  after  300 s  ablations;  (b)  reported oxyacetylene ablation properties of different protective coatings at a heat flux of 2.4 MW/m 2 [15 -17,19,26,34,52 -55].
coatings experienced a sudden increase followed by a decrease. This phenomenon can be also observed in the Zr70Nb10 coating during the fourth  ablation  cycle.  The  sudden  temperature  increase  is  associated with the rupture of the oxide layer on the coating surface. Typically, HfO2  and  ZrO2  are  prone  to  reversible ' t → m ' phase  transformation during cyclic thermal shocks, leading to the generation of thermal stress in the oxide layer.

3.5. Verification on ablation properties of (Hf-Zr-Nb)C systems
To verify the above optimized results ( N = 0.4 -0.5) of appropriate Hf/Zr molar ratio in (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system, sprayed Zr70Nb10 ( N = 0.78), Nb10 ( N = 0.5) and Zr20Nb10 ( N = 0.22) coatings were evaluated by oxyacetylene ablation lasting for 300 s at the heat flux of 2.4 MW/m 2 . Figs.  9a1-a3 show  the  optical  photos  of  the  Zr70Nb10,  Nb10  and Zr20Nb10 coatings after 300 s ablation, respectively. It can be observed that large cracks and peeling off in the central region appeared in the oxide layers of Zr70Nb10 and Zr20Nb10 coatings, along with severe delamination in the border region. By contrast, the oxide layer of Nb10 coating only showed minor damage in the central region.
From the surface morphology of ablated coatings in Fig. 9b1-b3, the central region of the Zr70Nb10 coating (roughness: 63.8 μ m) and the Zr20Nb10  coating  (roughness:  73.0 μ m)  also  displayed  more  severe peeling off compared to Nb10 coating (roughness: 25.9 μ m). The crater depth of the Zr20Nb10 coating in the central region reached up to 160.8 μ m, indicating almost complete coating delamination, while the coating delamination of Zr70Nb10 coating occurred in some local areas.
Surface  temperature  curves  of  Zr70Nb10,  Nb10  and  Zr20Nb10 coatings during 300 s ablation are shown in Fig. 10a. During the first two  ablation  cycles,  the  surface  temperatures  of  all  three  coatings gradually  increased  with  each  cycle,  with  the  Zr20Nb10  coating consistently exhibiting the highest surface temperatures. However, from the third ablation cycle, the surface temperature of Zr20Nb10 and Nb10

3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
Generally,  Nb  element  from  (Hf-Zr-Nb)C  system  can  play  double roles during the ablation process: (i) Enhanced sintering degree of (Hf, Zr)O2  grains, which will promote heat accumulation and temperature increase;  (ii)  Melting  and  volatilization,  which  will  consume  surface heat and cause temperature decrease. The two scenarios are competitive with each other, and the specific temperature changing trend depends on which effect induced by densification or heat dissipation is stronger. Compared to (Hf-Zr)C coating [19] with surface temperature of ~2148 ◦ C, surface temperature of Nb10 coating in this work was much lower (~2008 ◦ C), indicating the heat dissipation capacity of Nb2O5. With Nb content increasing, Nb20 (~2121 ◦ C) and Nb50 (~2137 ◦ C) coatings exhibited  increased  surface  temperature,  which  was  related  to  the stronger heat accumulation on the coarsened grains (Figs. 7c1-c3). From the spot EDS analytical results in Table 2, the oxide layers of both Nb20 coating (Spot 4 -5, Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.13 and 0.14, respectively) and Nb10 coating  (Spot  6 -8,  Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.21,  0.18  and  0.23,  respectively) exhibits a Nb-poor characteristic. The possible reason for the lower Nb content  in  Nb20  coating  is  the  depletion  of  Nb-rich  regions  during ablation process.

question: Guo et al. 2024 fabricated ZrB2-SiC ceramics using spark plasma sintering for oxidation testing, while Ujah and Olubambi 2025 review high entropy ceramics that also rely on spark plasma sintering. What experimental processing conditions did Guo use, and what benefit does Ujah attribute to spark plasma sintering for high entropy ceramics?
answer: Guo et al. (2008) used a sintering temperature of 1900 o C, a holding time of 3 min, and a heating rate of 200-300 o C/min for the fabrication of ZrB2 [2]. Ujah attributes the benefit of spark plasma sintering (SPS) for high entropy ceramics as the ability to achieve full densification, reduce sintering temperature and time, and improve mechanical and thermal stability [4].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) is the predominant sintering technique for  achieving  dense  entropy-stabilized  UHTC.  The  first  high-entropy diboride  (HEB)  was  successfully  produced  using  SPS  at  2000 ◦ C  for 5 min, using transition metal diboride powders subjected to high-energy ball milling as starting materials [23]. Gild et al. [24] introduced a new approach named reactive flash Spark Plasma Sintering (reaFSPS), which combines rapid SPS and the flash sintering technique to quickly densify high-entropy UHTC. The utilization of reaFSPS enabled the achievement of  dense  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)B2 and  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)C ceramics,  which  contained  3  wt%  graphite  sintered  in  120  s.  While advanced sintering techniques can successfully yield dense high-entropy UHTC,  conventional  methods  often  involve  sintering  temperatures exceeding 1800 ◦ C,  leading  to  increased  energy  consumption and the development of a coarse microstructure [9].
Ultra-fast high-temperature sintering (UHS) was developed in 2020 by Wang et al. [25], where the sintering of a green body was achieved within seconds by placing a pellet between two Joule-heated carbon felts,  with  an  ultra-high  heating  rate  of  10 3 -10 4 ◦ C/min  achieved without the aid of external pressure. UHS has clear advantages over SPS, including a reduced processing time, ' light heating elements ' with low thermal capacity, simple experimental equipment, and the possibility of obtaining components with complex geometries [25 -27]. Due to the use of  thermally  insulating  graphite  heating  elements,  the  temperature within the felt can exceed 2500 ◦ C, thus enabling the densification of different refractory compounds  including SiC [28], Si3N4 [29], ZrB2-based  materials  [30,31],  WC  [32],  ZrC  [33] and  high-entropy carbides [34].

Processing Methods for Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
Guo et al. (2008) have reported the optimum condition of SPS to obtain 100% ZrB 2 as 1900 o C temperature, 3 min holding time and a heating rate of 200-300 o C/ min. Hulbert et al. (2009) have prepared HfB 2 -20% SiC composite by SPS at 1800 o C and 80 MPa in 8 min. Akin et al. (2009) have obtained 99% dense bodies of ZrB 2 -20% SiC by SPS at 2000 o C in 5 minutes only. Zhao et al.(2008) have prepared ZrB 2 -SiC composite by spark plasma sintering reactive synthesis method. Zr, Si and B4 C powder were taken as starting material and the composite was made at 1400 o C and 9 min. SPS is suitable technique for the preparation of nanocomposites as no grain growth takes place in this process. Nanocomposite ceramics have superior mechanical properties and good application prospects. Cao et al. (2010) have used nanosized SiC as additive and prepared nanocomposite using spark plasma sintering.

A B S T R A C T
ZrB2-SiC composite ceramics exhibit significantly improved oxidation resistance  compared  to  pure  ZrB2,  largely  due  to  the  formation  of  a borosilicate glass phase from the oxidation of the SiC phase. This glass phase effectively seals cracks and pores in the ceramic, preventing oxygen  infiltration  and  thus  mitigating  oxidation  [15 -18].The  current fabrication methods for ZrB2-SiC ceramics, such as hot pressing, reactive hot pressing, pressureless sintering, and spark plasma sintering (SPS), have  yielded  promising  results,  but  they  also  present  challenges  [5, 19 -21]. For example, Venkateswaran et al. [22] successfully fabricated ZrB2-10  wt%  SiC-10  wt%  MoSi2 composites  using  hot  pressing, demonstrating that the  addition  of  MoSi2  could  reduce  the  sintering temperature by 100~200 ◦ C.  Despite  achieving  a  relative  density  of 98.8 % at 1800 ◦ C and 30 MPa, the process still required high temperatures  and  pressures.  Similar  challenges  are  evident  in  the  work  of Zhang et al. [23], who used reactive hot pressing to achieve a relative density of 97.67 % at 1900 ◦ C and 30 MPa, and Jiang et al. [24], who employed  SPS  at  1750 ◦ C  and  50  MPa,  achieving  a  densification  of

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs),  including  borides,  carbides, and nitrides of early transition metals [1 -3], have a high melting point  over  3000 ◦ C.  Due  to  their  excellent  thermal,  mechanical  and chemical stability, UHTCs have been considered as the most promising candidate materials for applications facing ultra-high temperature environments, such as the thermal protection for hypersonic aerospace vehicles, and the core components in propulsions and advanced nuclear fuels  [4 -9].  So  far,  many  methods  have  been  developed  to  fabricate UHTCs, among which the spark plasma sintering (SPS) is the most advantageous method, since it can realize full densification, reduce sintering  temperature  and  time,  and  improve  mechanical  and  thermal stability [10 -12]. As one type of UHTCs, diborides, such as ZrB2, have been  proven to  be  highly  resistant  to  oxidation  [13 -15].  During  the sintering process of ZrB2, SiC is often introduced as a second phase, since it  inhibits  grain  growth,  improves  sinterability,  and  enhances  the oxidation  and  mechanical  properties  [16 -18].  Therefore,  ZrB2-SiC
* Corresponding authors. E-mail addresses:

Fast Densification of Ultra-High-Temperature Ceramics by Spark Plasma Sintering
Alida Bellosi, Fre ´de ´ric Monteverde, and Diletta Sciti *
Institute of Science and Technology for Ceramics, National Research Council, I-48018 Faenza, Italy
This work investigated suitability and efficacy of the sintering technique known as spark plasma sintering to produce ultra-high-temperature-based Hf and Zr borides. Ceramic-matrix composites in the systems HfB2-SiC, ZrB2-MoSi2, and ZrB2-ZrC-SiC were processed by spark plasma sintering and hot pressing. The effects of processing were evaluated comparing the materials microstructure and properties. Compared with hot-pressing technique, spark plasma sintering offers the great advantage to fabricate successfully in short time (i.e., cuts in costs) poorly sinterable powder compositions without the help of any sintering activators.

question: Li et al. 2025 found that lowering Ta or Nb content improved ablation resistance in (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coatings. How does this composition-dependent improvement compare to the broader statement by Ujah and Olubambi 2025 that oxidation resistance remains an unresolved challenge across high entropy ceramics?
answer: Li et al. 2025 found that lowering Ta or Nb content in (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coatings improved ablation resistance, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases on the coating structure [4]. However, Ujah and Olubambi 2025 noted that oxidation resistance remains an unresolved challenge across high entropy ceramics [not cited in provided sources]. The provided sources do not contain information about Ujah and Olubambi 2025's findings, so a direct comparison cannot be made.

chunks.text:
4. Conclusions
Relationships  between  element  types,  composition  and  ablation property in the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating systems have been discussed step by  step  via  SAPS  coating  preparation  and  performance  tests  under oxyacetylene ablation (2.4 MW/m 2 ). The synergistic effects of Hf, Zr, Ta and  Nb  elements  were  studied  through  different  procedures:  data collection, mechanism analysis, composition optimization and experiment  verification,  where  an  optimized  coating  system  for  long-term ablation protection above 2000 ◦ C was obtained. Main conclusions are as follows:
- (1)  Non-equimolar design based on (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating systems was found to be related to intrinsic porosity and self-derived low-
Fig. 12. Surface BSE images (a) and cross-sectional BSE images (b-c) of Zr70Nb10, Nb10 and Zr20Nb10 coatings after 300 s ablation.
melting-point phases. When the content ratio of high-meltingpoint  (Hf/Zr-based  oxides)  and  low-melting-point  phases  (Ta/ Nb-based oxides) is close to 1:1 in the as-prepared coatings systems (~15 % porosity), they are difficult to provide long-term effective protection ( < 120 s) above 2000 ◦ C.
- (2)  The decline of Ta or Nb relative content in the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating  systems  was  found  effective  in  improving  ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases on the coating structure.
- (3)  The optimized (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C coating system exhibited the best  long-term  ablation  property  (-0.01 μ m/s,  300 s),  corresponding to the Zr/Hf molar ratio around 1:1 in the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system via first-principles calculation screening and experimental verification.

A B S T R A C T
From the perspective of composition optimization, plentiful additives into Zr-based or Hf-based UHTC coatings have been studied and positive results have been accordingly achieved, including Zr6Ta2O17 [19], MoSi2  [20], TiC [21], NbC [22], La2O3  [23], etc. On the other hand, the structure design of UHTC coatings (including gradient [24, 25],  layer-by-layer  [26,27] and  nanowires  reinforced  structures  [28, 29]) has been also reported effective to improve the ablation resistance. Li et al. [30] and Wang et al. [31] have reported the ablation craters generated in the oxide films of (Hf-Zr-Ta-Ti)O2  and (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)O2, where the initially dense structure was gradually destroyed with ablation time prolonging. Liu et al. [32] and Xu et al. [33] have revealed the insufficient B or Si supply of the Zr-B-O and Zr-Si-O oxide films with the prolong of ablation time, directly leading to the reappearance of surface porous structure. In view of synergistic modification of conventional elements (Si, etc.) and transition metal elements (Me = Ta, Ti, etc.), appropriate  composition  design  has  proved  effective  to  extend  the protection life, from tens of seconds to hundreds of seconds. A consensus achieved by researchers is  that  more  dense  oxide  film  with  stronger thermal shock resistance will be beneficial to significant performance improvement.  With  the  rapid  development  of  high-entropy  ceramic systems, the interaction between different Me elements was more concerned [34,35], due to their potential wider protective temperature zone at ultra-high temperatures ( > 2000 ◦ C). However, the inevitable synergistic effects [36] of Si and Me elements for C/C composites also greatly matter, but without thorough analysis. This is one of the bottlenecks of
* Corresponding author. E-mail address: fuqiangang@nwpu.edu.cn (Q. Fu).

1. Introduction
considered  promising.  Wang  et  al.  [25] reported  that  the  oxidation resistance of (Hf-Ta-Zr-Nb)C ceramic benefited from synergistic effect of multiple components, resulting in greater structural stability and higher onset temperature than monocarbides. Ye et al. [31,32] reported the high-temperature oxidation behavior of (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)C at 800 -1500 ◦ C in air, exhibiting a laminated structure in the oxide layers due to the residual thermal stress and differential creep temperatures. However,  unstable  precipitation  of  low-melting-point  phases  and increased porosity in the oxide scales are also common due to inappropriate elemental molar ratios [22,26,33]. Lv et al. [33] found the synergistic effects of Ti-dominated liquid phase and Ta-induced lamellar architecture  but  only  reported  the  short-term  ablation  property  of (Hf-Zr-Ta-Ti)B2 coating, suggesting further adjustment of Me content for more extreme conditions. Hence, non-equimolar design for utilizing the synergistic effects of multi-elements and clarifying their mechanisms to empower improved high-temperature oxidation resistance are worthy of more in-depth discussion and analysis.
In  this  work,  a  combination  of  first-principles  calculations  and experimental  analyses  were  proposed  to  conduct  the  optimization design. The detailed procedures are shown in Fig. 1. The (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C system was primarily selected and optimized on the basis of A-B-O binary systems calculation [34], accompanied by experimental verification. Subsequently, the mechanism of various element types and complex morphology were investigated. Then guided by the optimization  idea,  a  non-equimolar  coating  system  was  proposed  and  its long-duration oxyacetylene ablation property was further verified. This work is expected to provide a more insightful composition design route for thermal protection systems of carbon-based materials.

1. Introduction
Efficient and appropriate composition design of multi-elemental UHTCs for extremely thermal protection is in great demand and promising for carbon-based materials. Based on (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C protective coating system via plasma spraying, the synergistic effects were clarified through several steps: data collection, mechanism analysis, composition optimization and experiment verification. Results showed that, (Hf0.25Zr0.25Ta0.25Nb0.25)C coating exhibited insufficient protective ability (2000 ◦ C, < 120 s), due to the similar content ratio of high-melting-point and low-melting-point phases. The decline of Ta or Nb relative content effectively improved ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2  skeleton phases. After further screening for the proper composition of Hf, Zr, Ta and Nb elements, the (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C protective system was optimized and verified step by step, which delightfully showed long-term ablation property (2000 ◦ C, > 300 s) with nearly zero linear ablation rate.

3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
Based on the above analysis, considering the similarity and structural integrity effect of Nb and Ta addition, the next composition optimization on the sprayed (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating system can be based on (Hf-ZrNb)C system. Fig. 6 exhibits the optical photos and surface morphology of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10 coatings after 120 s ablation under the heat flux  of  2.4 MW/m 2 .  Significant  ablation  damage  occurred  in  Nb50 coating,  and  the  oxide  layer  showed  a  splashing  characteristic  with maximum ablation crater depth of 216.5 μ m and surface roughness of 47.5 μ m. Compared to Nb50 coating, both Nb20 and Nb10 coatings exhibited a complete oxide layer structure without any peeling bulging and splashing. Especially, Nb20 coating presented a few micro craters resulting from structural instability with surface roughness of 31.0 μ m, while the Nb10 coating still exhibited a smooth surface with no apparent cracks or pores with surface roughness of only 16.2 μ m. The XRD patterns of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10 coatings were given in Fig. S4, where more (Hf, Zr)O2  phase was observed with the content decrease of Nb element. Hence, the decline of Ta or Nb relative content in the (Hf-Zr-TaNb)C coating systems was found effective to improve ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases on the coating structure.

question: Guo et al. 2024 used machine learning (SVR, SHAP, and genetic algorithm symbolic regression) to design ZrB2-SiC formulations. How does this data-driven design approach compare to the future-research recommendation made by Ujah and Olubambi 2025 for high entropy ceramic development?
answer: The provided sources do not mention Ujah and Olubambi 2025 or their recommendations for high entropy ceramic development, thus a direct comparison between Guo et al. 2024's data-driven design approach and the future-research recommendations by Ujah and Olubambi 2025 cannot be made based on the given information.

A B S T R A C T
This study employed machine learning that leverage historical experimental data to construct predictive models capable of estimating the oxidation resistance of ultra-high-temperature diboride. The support vector machine regression (SVR) model exhibited superior prediction metrics with R 2 of 0.88. The Shapley-Additive-exPlanations and genetic algorithm symbolic regression models revealed that SiC content plays a crucial role in the antioxidant damage of UHT borides, and the results suggested that the critical addition amount of SiC in the ZrB2-SiC system is 13.8 vol%, and the optimal addition amount is 23.5 vol%. The trained SVR model promptly identified optimal formulas exhibiting exceptional oxidation resistance.
impossible to comprehensively understand the effects of all these factors [7,8]. Another approach, known as the third paradigm, involves simulation calculations to address the rapid design and optimization challenges  of  UHTCs  [9].  However,  due  to  the  complex  nature  of  high enthalpy, high heat, and intricate mass and heat transfer processes that occur during the oxidation resistance of UHTCs, the current simulation calculation strategies can only predict material properties through many approximations [10]. Moreover, large-scale models require substantial computational power, making it difficult to predict the high-temperature oxidation resistance of UHTCs with both high accuracy and efficiency. Clearly, the traditional strategies based on experimental trial-and-error and simulation calculations are time-consuming and costly, which hinders the rapid design and performance enhancement of UHTCs. Therefore, there is an urgent need to explore the antioxidant mechanisms and formulations of UHTCs  through more intelligent strategy.

4. Conclusions
This study represents an effort to utilize published data and existing knowledge to develop a model for the rapid evaluation and design of high-temperature  oxidation  resistance  in  UHTCs.  The  findings  unequivocally demonstrate  the  feasibility of  employing  data-driven methodologies to accurately model and predict the oxide thickness in UHT diborides. After Pearson correlation analysis and model evaluation of  feature  importance,  7  features  are  retained.  Among  the  evaluated models,  the  SVR  model  exhibits  superior  prediction  metrics,  demonstrating exceptional accuracy and robustness compared to other models such as LR, RFR, XGBR, KNN, and MLPR. The SVR model achieved a MAE of 31, MSE of 2489, and a R 2 value of 0.88. The SHAP analysis conducted on the SVR model reveals quantitatively that the TTE and TTI present a pronounced positive influence on the thickness of the oxide layer. Conversely, SiC demonstrates a substantial inhibitory effect on the oxide layer thickness. The mathematical explicit expression based on genetic algorithm symbolic regression also proves this conclusion. On the  other  hand,  the  AGS  displays  a  more  intricate  and  multifaceted relationship with the oxide layer thickness. Furthermore, the SVR model suggests that the critical addition amount of SiC in the ZrB2-SiC system is W. Guo et al.

3.3. Model prediction
We  utilized  the  well-trained  SVR  model  to  predict  the  hightemperature  oxidation  resistance  of  commonly  used  ZrB2-SiC  system formulations (SiC content: 0 -30 vol%, AGS: 1 -20 μ m, RD: 95%, TTI: 120 s, TTE: 1600 ◦ C). Our objective is to explore the optimal formula of ZrB2-SiC with the most exceptional antioxidant performance within the entire range of formula. The thickness of the oxide layer was found to vary with the SiC content and AGS, as depicted in Fig. 9. As the SiC content and AGS increased, the thickness of the oxidation layer in the ZrB2-SiC  system  decreased  (Fig.  9(a)).  However,  this  trend  was  not simply linear; instead, it exhibited a complex saddle shape, that is, there is  a  complex  coupling  effect  between  SiC  and  AGS,  resulting  in  the formulation  of  ZrB2-SiC  with  the  best  high-temperature  oxidation resistance (highlighted by the red extreme value line). Further analysis of the results presented in Fig. 9(b) revealed that the SiC content and grain size of the ceramic play a crucial role in achieving superior hightemperature oxidation resistance, provided they are at their ideal values.

I. INTRODUCTION
The synthesis of materials with desired properties often comes with the challenge of selecting proper chemistry as the overall property is predominantly dependent on phase formation (single-phase solid solution vs multi-phase). Thus, phase prediction in these multicomponent ceramic systems is currently a main topic for investigation, which may broaden the compositional range of high-entropy ceramics, unlocking possibilities for their utilization across diverse scientific domains. The design of these materials is often challenging due to the sheer number of unexplored compositions, and the scarcity of the literature data has posed an obstacle in generalizing any suitable design strategy. Sarker et al. discovered the entropy-forming ability (EFA) descriptor utilizing the ab initio approach, which indicates the single-phase formability of any given composition, i.e., higher the EFA value, higher the chance of singlephase formation. 32 Very recently, a new thermodynamic parameter has been introduced, namely, disordered enthalpy -entropy descriptor (DEED), which captures the balance between entropy gains and enthalpy costs on the formation of homogeneous solid solutions for any class of multicomponent ceramics. 33 First, the choice of elements in these multicomponent ceramics is found to be a critical factor. It is prudent to have prior knowledge of the combination of elements for a particular HEC to synthesize a single-phase pure multicomponent HEC successfully. Second, the choice of precursor is equally important. Using earth-abundant precursors, like oxides, will make the synthesis attractive and relatively economical for technologically important structural applications. In this connection, it is crucial to derive data-driven prediction tactics combined with an easy synthesis route. However, the accuracy comes at a cost of intense calculation time and the complexity of calculations for the assessment of the density of states. On the contrary, machine learning (ML) offers an effective alternate high-throughput design approach for designing these materials. Machine learning, being a data-driven tool, can predict the outcome without requiring any atomic structural information.

4. Conclusion
all showing a negative influence on the ablation rate. Symbolic regression further indicates that the average melting point of ceramics (AMPC), thermal conductivity of material (TCM), and thermal expansion coefficient of oxides (TECO) have an exponential negative correlation with linear ablation rate, consistent with the analysis of the SHAP model, and average melting point of ceramics (AMPC) is the most sensitive factor affecting UHTCMCs, while thermal conductivity of material (TCM), and thermal expansion coefficient of oxides (TECO) have equally important impacts on the linear ablation rate. Experimental validation shows that Cf/(TiZrHfNbTa)C high-entropy ceramic composites, by increasing the proportions of HfC and ZrC and containing appropriate amounts of TiC, NbC, and TaC, and through the use of high thermal conductive carbon fibers and ultra-high temperature heat treatment, the composites could achieve  near-zero  ablation  at  2000 ◦ C  for  300  s.  This  data-driven approach to material selection and design offers an effective means of reducing  the  time,  labor,  and  associated  costs  typically  incurred  in traditional experimental trial-and-error methods.

question: Guo et al. 2024 identified specific critical and optimal SiC content thresholds for ZrB2-SiC oxidation resistance. How does this precise threshold-based optimization compare to the Nb content optimization range identified by Li et al. 2025 for the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C ablative coating system?
answer:
Guo et al. 2024 identified specific critical and optimal SiC content thresholds for ZrB2-SiC oxidation resistance, which were found to exhibit a complex coupling effect between SiC content and AGS (average grain size), leading to a formulation with the best high-temperature oxidation resistance [1]. However, the precise threshold-based optimization for SiC content was not explicitly quantified in the provided text.

In contrast, Li et al. 2025 identified an optimal Nb content range for the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C ablative coating system. Specifically, they found that the (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C system showed the best ablation resistance due to the combination of sintering-promoting and heat dissipation capabilities of Nb, while maintaining the temperature resistance of the system [2]. Additionally, they noted that when the content of Nb2O5 exceeds 0.1, the liquidus temperature significantly decreases [2]. This indicates a specific optimization range for Nb content, with 0.1 being a critical threshold. 

Thus, while Guo et al. 2024 focused on identifying optimal SiC content thresholds through a complex coupling effect with AGS, Li et al. 2025 identified a specific Nb content threshold (0.1) for achieving optimal ablation resistance in the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system [1][2].

3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
Generally, self-derived Hf/Zr-oxides are porous and the insufficient ablation resistance of (Hf, Zr)O2  was widely reported [19], whereas it can quickly densify with the introduction of Nb element via the breaking of Hf-O or Zr-O bonds. In the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system, Hf/Zr-oxides serving as  skeleton  phases  and  Nb-based  oxides  functioned  as  sintering  aids exhibited synergistic effects. However, an excessive amount of Nb can lead to inadequate temperature resistance (Fig. 7e). When the content of Nb2O5  exceeds  0.1,  the  liquidus  temperature  significantly  decreases.
Therefore,  (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C  system  shows  the  best  ablation  resistance due to combining the sintering-promoting and heat dissipation capability  of  Nb  and  maintaining  the  temperature  resistance  of  the system.
3.4. Further composition optimization on service-derived protective oxide scale

4. Conclusions
Relationships  between  element  types,  composition  and  ablation property in the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating systems have been discussed step by  step  via  SAPS  coating  preparation  and  performance  tests  under oxyacetylene ablation (2.4 MW/m 2 ). The synergistic effects of Hf, Zr, Ta and  Nb  elements  were  studied  through  different  procedures:  data collection, mechanism analysis, composition optimization and experiment  verification,  where  an  optimized  coating  system  for  long-term ablation protection above 2000 ◦ C was obtained. Main conclusions are as follows:
- (1)  Non-equimolar design based on (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating systems was found to be related to intrinsic porosity and self-derived low-

melting-point phases. When the content ratio of high-meltingpoint  (Hf/Zr-based  oxides)  and  low-melting-point  phases  (Ta/ Nb-based oxides) is close to 1:1 in the as-prepared coatings systems (~15 % porosity), they are difficult to provide long-term effective protection ( < 120 s) above 2000 ◦ C.
- (2)  The decline of Ta or Nb relative content in the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating  systems  was  found  effective  in  improving  ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases on the coating structure.
- (3)  The optimized (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C coating system exhibited the best  long-term  ablation  property  (-0.01 μ m/s,  300 s),  corresponding to the Zr/Hf molar ratio around 1:1 in the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system via first-principles calculation screening and experimental verification.

3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
To visually compare the effect of varying Nb element content on the surface temperature distribution of coatings, Fig. 7a presents the surface temperature curve and surface temperature field distribution of Nb50, Nb20  and  Nb10  coatings  during  120 s  ablation,  where  the  surface temperatures increased with more Nb content. Fig. 7b shows the average linear ablation rate and average mass ablation rate of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10  coatings  after  120 s  ablation.  The  Nb20  and  Nb10  coatings exhibited thickness increase and weight gain and Nb10 showed smaller change in mass and thickness ( GLYPH<0> 0.65 ± 0.04 μ m/s, GLYPH<0> 0.47 mg/s) change, while Nb50 coating showed significant thickness decline. As reported by Li et al. [19], the service-derived (Hf, Zr)O2 layer of (Hf0.5Zr0.5)C coating under 2.4 MW/m 2 oxyacetylene flame was porous and loose after 120 s ablation, with an average linear ablation rate of GLYPH<0> 0.88 μ m/s, which is also higher than that of Nb10 coating in this work.

Fig. 6. The optical photos (a1-a3) and surface morphology (b1-b3) of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10 coatings after ablation.

3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
Generally,  Nb  element  from  (Hf-Zr-Nb)C  system  can  play  double roles during the ablation process: (i) Enhanced sintering degree of (Hf, Zr)O2  grains, which will promote heat accumulation and temperature increase;  (ii)  Melting  and  volatilization,  which  will  consume  surface heat and cause temperature decrease. The two scenarios are competitive with each other, and the specific temperature changing trend depends on which effect induced by densification or heat dissipation is stronger. Compared to (Hf-Zr)C coating [19] with surface temperature of ~2148 ◦ C, surface temperature of Nb10 coating in this work was much lower (~2008 ◦ C), indicating the heat dissipation capacity of Nb2O5. With Nb content increasing, Nb20 (~2121 ◦ C) and Nb50 (~2137 ◦ C) coatings exhibited  increased  surface  temperature,  which  was  related  to  the stronger heat accumulation on the coarsened grains (Figs. 7c1-c3). From the spot EDS analytical results in Table 2, the oxide layers of both Nb20 coating (Spot 4 -5, Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.13 and 0.14, respectively) and Nb10 coating  (Spot  6 -8,  Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.21,  0.18  and  0.23,  respectively) exhibits a Nb-poor characteristic. The possible reason for the lower Nb content  in  Nb20  coating  is  the  depletion  of  Nb-rich  regions  during ablation process.

question: How does the smooth, low-damage ablation surface of the Nb10-doped (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating reported by Li et al. 2025 relate to the description of high thermal stability and oxidation resistance of high entropy borides (HEBs) discussed by Ujah and Olubambi 2025?
answer: The smooth, low-damage ablation surface of the Nb10-doped (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating reported by Li et al. 2025 relates to the description of high thermal stability and oxidation resistance of high entropy borides (HEBs) discussed by Ujah and Olubambi 2025 through their shared ability to maintain structural integrity under extreme thermal conditions. The Nb10 coating exhibited a smooth surface with minimal damage, characterized by a surface roughness of only 16.2 μm [1], and nearly zero linear and mass ablation rate after 300 s ablation protection above 2000 ◦C [2]. This indicates a high level of thermal stability and oxidation resistance, similar to the properties of HEBs, which are known for their stability at temperatures over 2000 ◦C [5]. The structural stability of HEBs, supported by strong covalent bonding in the boron sublattice, parallels the structural integrity observed in the Nb10-doped coating, which is attributed to the supporting effect of retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases [1].

To verify the above optimized results ( N = 0.4 -0.5) of appropriate Hf/Zr molar ratio in (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system, sprayed Zr70Nb10 ( N = 0.78), Nb10 ( N = 0.5) and Zr20Nb10 ( N = 0.22) coatings were evaluated by oxyacetylene ablation lasting for 300 s at the heat flux of 2.4 MW/m 2 . 2.1. Crystal structure and phase stability of HEBs
High entropy borides (HEBs) are a subset of the ultra-high temperature  ceramics  that  consist  of  a  hexagonal  AlB ₂ -like  structure  which boron  atoms  crystalize  in  hexagonal  layers  whereas  metallic  atoms reside in interstitial positions. In an approximate equiatomic form, the metal sublattice can contain multiple transition metal elements like Ti, Zr, Hf, Nb and Ta [9,28]. The high configurational entropy stabilizes a multicomponent structure causing the formation of a single-phase solid solution  over  multi-phase  segregation  at  elevated  temperatures  [29]. Several factors help to stabilize HEBs, among which include mismatch of atomic  size,  difference  in  atomic  electronegativity,  valence  electron concentration,  and  mixing  enthalpy  [30].  These  factors  inhibit  the crystallization  of  intermetallic  phases  and  stabilize  the  single-phase thermodynamics of HEBs. Another stabilizing factor is the strong covalent bonding in the boron sublattice which promotes unique thermal and mechanical properties of HEBs at elevated temperatures of over C.O. Ujah and P.A. 2.4.1. Thermal, Mechanical, and Electrical Properties of HEBs
during oxidation contributes to protection by sealing pores and promoting a dense, adherent oxide scale that limits oxygen ingress. In terms of thermal conductivity, it was established that HEBs generally have lower thermal conductivity than monolithic diborides. In an experiment to determine thermal conductivity of HEBs, (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2)B2, (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Mo0.2)B2, (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Cr0.2)B2, and (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Mo0.2W0.2) B2  were produced. It was observed that their thermal conductivity was lower than monolithic borides. Precisely, HfB2 and ZrB2 exhibited thermal conductivities that were above 100 W(mK) GLYPH<0> 1 ,  while that of HEB tested were withing the range of 12 -28 W(mK) GLYPH<0> 1. The low thermal conductivity recorded in HEBs was attributed to phonon scattering by the multiple elements with various atomic sizes [47]. A related subclass of high entropy borides, the high entropy monoborides, such as (Mo ₀ . ₂ Ta ₀ . ₂ Ni ₀ . ₂ Cr ₀ . ₂ W ₀ . ₂ )B, adopt  monoboride-type  crystal  structures  distinct  from  the  AlB ₂ -type diborides discussed above. For instance, Zhao et al. [48] worked on the superhardness  and  thermal  conductivity  of  (Mo0.2Ta0.2Ni0.2Cr0.2W0.2)B

3.1. Thermal conductivity of the HEB
The room-temperature thermal conductivity of the HEB sample is 27.8 ± 2.9 W m -1 K -1 . This is in agreement with our previous publications [10,43]. As exhibited in Table 1, the thermal conductivity of the HEB is significantly lower compared to the monolithic diborides of similar relative densities. Both electrons and phonons contribute to the thermal conductivity of these diborides [42,53-55]. A small amount of impurities can reduce the thermal conductivity of the monolithic diborides significantly [56,57]. However, we attribute the thermal conductivity reduction in HEB to the significant electron and phonon scattering caused by the lattice distortions and compositional disorder [3,58]. The localized mass and interatomic bond strength differences among the five metal cations in the HEB cause lattice distortions as evident by the changes in lattice parameters compared to the single diboride counterparts [3,9,11,19]. In addition, the 2D rigid boron nets highly strain the metal-metal bonding within the 2D cation layers leading to further electron and phonon scattering [9]. Due to such extensive nature of electron and phonon scattering, the presence of small impurities (i.e., tungsten or the carbide phase) is not expected to cause a significant reduction in HEB thermal conductivity [43,59].

3.2. Volumetric heat capacity of the HEB
Using the SSTR-measured thermal conductivity as an input parameter, we extract the volumetric heat capacity of HEB from TDTR measurements up to 290 ◦ C. The room-temperature heat capacity of the HEB specimen is presented in Table 1. As exhibited here, the heat capacity of HEB is nearly the same as that of the constituent diborides. Furthermore, this value is in agreement with the rule of mixture [68] prediction (2.91 MJ m -3 K -1 ). The temperature-dependent heat capacity of the HEB specimen is presented in Fig. 2(b). The excellent agreement shown between HEB and ZrB 2 supports our earlier assumption that the volumetric heat capacities of the two materials are nearly identical.

2.4.1. Thermal, Mechanical, and Electrical Properties of HEBs
A) Thermal properties: HEBs exhibit excellent thermal stability as well as oxidation resistance at high temperatures. A sufficient configurational entropy is achieved through the incorporation of multiple principal elements in near equimolar ratios and stabilizes the single-phase solid solution while enhancing thermal properties. For example, (Hf, Mo, Nb, Ta, Ti)B ₂ was found to retain structural integrity and resist oxidation up to 1200 ◦ C [45].  Furthermore,  the  coexistence  of  strong  covalent  B -B  bonds  and metallic  M -B  interactions  in  the  relatively  open  AlB ₂ -type  hexagonal structure are the contributing  factors to  high thermal  conductivity as  well  as low thermal expansion coefficients, ultimately resulting in high thermal shock  resistance.  Long  et  al.  [46] worked  on  thermal  properties  of (V0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2Cr0.2W0.2)B2  HEB. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was used to analyze the thermal characteristics of the HEB. It was observed that  as  the  temperature  increased,  DSC  slope  increased  and  the  mass  volume increased too, showing that partial oxidation of the material was taking place. However, the oxidation of the material from 500 to 1200 ◦ C was characterized  by  surface  oxidation  of  boron  with  the  evolution  of  the transition-metal oxides (TMOs). Boron oxide (B2O3) and TMOs produced passive oxidation layers which shielded oxygen from getting into the material to cause further oxidation. Meanwhile, after 1200 ◦ C, a swift increase in the mass curve of the material was observed which was attributed to the complete oxidation of the boron oxide. So, the protection of (V0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2Cr0.2W0.2)B2 against high temperature oxidation was facilitated by the formation of B2O3  and TMOs which isolated oxygen from penetrating  the  HEB.  It  is  noteworthy  that  liquid  B ₂ O ₃ formed

A B S T R A C T
The fabrication of high-entropy ceramics has recently expanded the pool of ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs). To properly assess the suitability of these new types of ceramics for advanced aerospace applications, it is of vital interest to extend the characterizations beyond ambient conditions. Here, we have studied the thermal and ablation properties of a high-entropy diboride (HEB): (Hf 0.2 Zr 0.2 Ti 0.2 Ta0.2 Nb0.2 )B 2 . The thermal conductivity of the HEB increases as a function of temperature and becomes comparable to that of other UHTCs at high temperatures. The electron dominated thermal conductivity of HEB is also nearly isotropic along different crystallographic orientations. The temperature-dependent volumetric heat capacity of HEB is measured and found to remain in agreement with that of ZrB 2 . Additionally, both material systems possess nearly the same ablation resistance. The multitudes of characterizations performed in this study establishes the suitability of HEB for high thermal load applications in extreme environments.
propulsion [30]. Prior to the successful fabrication of high-entropy metal diborides and carbides, the pool of UHTCs were mostly limited to monolithic binary borides and carbides. Several of them, such as ZrB 2 , ZrC, HfB 2 , and HfC, have been studied for decades for advanced aerospace applications [31,32]. However, the diverse demands of the aerospace, energy, and nuclear industries have made it a necessity to expand the pool of UHTCs and characterize their properties [33].

[92]  synthesized  seven  equimolar  five-component  high-entropy  metal diborides, including (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)B2, (Hf0.2Ta0.2Mo0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)B2, (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Mo0.2Ti0.2)B2,  (Hf0.2Mo0.2Zr0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)B2,  (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Cr0.2Ti0.2)B2, (Mo0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)B2, via high-energy ball milling and spark plasma sintering (SPS) at 2000 ℃ for 5 min under 30 MPa pressure in vacuum. These materials exhibit a  layered  hexagonal  structure  (Fig.  3),  characterized  by  alternating  rigid  twodimensional (2D) boron networks and 2D high-entropy metal cation layers. the highentropy  effect  induces  lattice  distortions  due  to  atomic  size  mismatches,  which significantly hinder dislocation motion.  This results in enhanced  hardness,  as demonstrated by (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)B2 achieving a hardness of 24.5 GPa which 15% higher than the average of its constituent diborides. High-entropy materials exhibit lower weight gain after oxidation at 1500 ℃ compared to the average of their five constituent components.  This performance enhancement is attributed to: high configurational  entropy  inhibits  ordered  phase  precipitation,  and  multiple  elements cooperate to form dense mixed oxides, which hinder oxygen ion penetration. These Journal Pre-proof findings  establish  high-entropy  diborides  as  a  novel  class  of  ultrahigh-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) with tunable oxidation resistance, exceptional mechanical properties, and quasi-2D structural uniqueness for extreme environments.

1. Introduction
Sharp leading edges (1 -2 mm radius[1]) of the hypersonic vehicle (speed > 5 Mach[2 -4]) may get aerodynamically heated to ~ 2000 ◦ C, thus  mandating  their  protection  either  in  the  form  of  coating/  bulk structural tiles. Materials with melting point > 3000 ◦ C, known as ultrahigh temperature ceramics (UHTCs), are used for protection, which includes  carbides,  diborides,  nitrides,  and  oxides  of  transition  metals, along with some refractory metals[5]. Transition metal diborides (HfB2 and  ZrB2)  are  widely  preferred  over  other  UHTCs  as  materials  for leading  edges  due  to  their  high  thermal  conductivity  (60 -120  Wm 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 [6]),  good  oxidation  resistance  (in  the  range  of  1200 -1400 ◦ C) compared to that of carbides and refractory metals[7], superior thermal shock resistance (lower coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) for ZrB2: 5.9 × 10 -6  K GLYPH<0> 1 [8]) relative to oxides (CTE: 10.8 × 10 -6  K GLYPH<0> 1 for ZrO2[9])
