A B S T R A C T
A highly dense and single phase (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Mo0.2W0.2)B2 ceramic product is obtained in this work at 1950 ◦ C (20 min, 20 MPa) by Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) from powders prepared by Self-propagating High-temperature Synthesis (SHS). The formation of the (W,Mo)B2 secondary phase is avoided using fine W precursors and adding 1 wt% graphite to the SHS powders before SPS. Kinetic limitations responsible for hindering the synthesis of the high entropy boride are correspondingly eliminated. The resulting 98.5 % dense sample exhibits a homogeneous microstructure, with Vickers hardness of 26.8 GPa. The introduction of 20 vol% SiC produces an increase of the KIC  values from 2.32 to 5.11 MPa m 1/2 . Very relevant is that the volatilization of Mo- and W-oxides occurring during sample oxidation at high temperature, which leads to its rapid degradation with the formation of a very porous oxide scale, can be strongly inhibited by the silicate phases generated in the composite ceramic.
purity  W,  Re,  Ru  and  B  powders  [9].  The  (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2) B2-SiC ceramic composite was also prepared by reactive Spark Plasma Sintering using the corresponding high-entropy carbide, B4C and Si as starting materials [10].

4.4.1. Sintering techniques modification and advancements
Recently,  alongside  property  enhancements,  SPS  was  used  to enhance  the  sintering  process  of  double-phase  TiB2-SiC  UHTCMCs, reducing sintering temperatures to 1200 -1400 ◦ C and achieving optimal properties  at  1400 ◦ C,  including  high  hardness  (24.85  GPa),  tensile strength (309 GPa), fracture toughness (4.80 MPa · m 1/2 ), and low density [138]. However, UHTCMCs exhibit enhanced oxidation resistance due to the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure formed during plasma exposure; Nisar et al. synthesized Hf6Ta2O17 via solid-state reaction between HfO2 and Ta2O5 during SPS, with compositions of  50HO-50TO  and 70HO-30TO.  Rietveld  analysis  confirmed  Hf6Ta2O17  as  the  principal phase,  and  high-velocity  plasma  jet  tests  showed  50HO-50TO  withstanding intense plasma due to higher Hf6Ta2O17 content (~84 %), with its phase increasing to 94.7 % post-exposure, maintaining mechanical integrity with minimal hardness change (~13.3 GPa to 11.2 GPa) [139]. Qin et al. examined W-containing HE-carbide and Si reactions at high temperatures, preparing (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Ta0.2W0.2)CxSi ( x = 2.5, 5, 10 wt %) samples via spark plasma sintering at 1500 ◦ C and 1700 ◦ C. Adding 2.5 or 5 wt % Si forms SiC and stable (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Ta0.2W0.2)C1-x, while wt  %  Si  forms  transient  phases  decomposing  into  non-equimolar HE-carbide  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Ta0.2W0.2-y)C1-z and  WSi2.  Thus,  SPS

3.1. Sintering process
Successful spark plasma sintering processes were conducted in the temperature range of 1200 -1400 º C. Fig. 2 shows the exemplary sintering curves for reactant powders of B4C and TiSi2  (refer to the supplementary section for all curves). In the case of this sample group, the highest  material  shrinkage  was  recorded  for  samples  sintered  at 1250 ◦ C, while the shrinkage of other samples was comparable. Samples sintered at 1300 ◦ C, reached their maximum shrinkage at a lower temperature than the set temperature. Samples sintered at 1400 ◦ C reached their  maximum shrinkage  before  heating  the  system  to  1400 ◦ C  and during  the  following  holding  time  at  this  temperature  decreased  the shrinkage over time.
The  materials  obtained  from  reactant  powders  of  B4C  and  Ti5Si3 showed a similar tendency above 1300 ◦ C (Fig. 3). Samples sintered at 1300 ◦ C and 1400 ◦ C, achieved the maximum shrinkage of the material before  the  maximum  temperatures  of  the  process  were  reached  and subsequently expanded their volume a bit. Samples sintered at 1250 ◦ C

Fig. 2. The sintering curves of the samples obtained from B4C -TiSi2  reactant powders at a) 1250 º C, b) 1300 º C.
Fig. 3. The sintering curves of the samples obtained from B4C -Ti5Si3  reactant powders at a) 1250 º C, b) 1300 º C.

A B S T R A C T
Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) is the predominant sintering technique for  achieving  dense  entropy-stabilized  UHTC.  The  first  high-entropy diboride  (HEB)  was  successfully  produced  using  SPS  at  2000 ◦ C  for 5 min, using transition metal diboride powders subjected to high-energy ball milling as starting materials [23]. Gild et al. [24] introduced a new approach named reactive flash Spark Plasma Sintering (reaFSPS), which combines rapid SPS and the flash sintering technique to quickly densify high-entropy UHTC. The utilization of reaFSPS enabled the achievement of  dense  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)B2 and  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)C ceramics,  which  contained  3  wt%  graphite  sintered  in  120  s.  While advanced sintering techniques can successfully yield dense high-entropy UHTC,  conventional  methods  often  involve  sintering  temperatures exceeding 1800 ◦ C,  leading  to  increased  energy  consumption and the development of a coarse microstructure [9].
Ultra-fast high-temperature sintering (UHS) was developed in 2020 by Wang et al. [25], where the sintering of a green body was achieved within seconds by placing a pellet between two Joule-heated carbon felts,  with  an  ultra-high  heating  rate  of  10 3 -10 4 ◦ C/min  achieved without the aid of external pressure. UHS has clear advantages over SPS, including a reduced processing time, ' light heating elements ' with low thermal capacity, simple experimental equipment, and the possibility of obtaining components with complex geometries [25 -27]. Due to the use of  thermally  insulating  graphite  heating  elements,  the  temperature within the felt can exceed 2500 ◦ C, thus enabling the densification of different refractory compounds  including SiC [28], Si3N4 [29], ZrB2-based  materials  [30,31],  WC  [32],  ZrC  [33] and  high-entropy carbides [34].

4. Conclusions
ZrB2-based ultrahigh temperature ceramics were prepared by the reactive spark plasma sintering using the vanadium powders and graphite nano/uniFB02 akes as sintering aids. The consolidation process was performed for 7 min under the pressure of 40 MPa at di /uniFB00 erent SPS temperatures of 1800, 1900 and 2000 °C. The relative density of all samples were > 99% as an evidence of approaching the theoretical density values. Compounds of VB2 and ZrC were in-situ synthesized during the reactive SPS at all temperatures. At the sintering temperature of 1800 °C, most vanadium and graphite additives were remained intact, while they completely were consumed by processing at 1900 °C. Beside the compounds of VB2 and ZrC, other complex phases of V1.54B50 and V3Zr3C were also in-situ formed at 2000 °C. Increasing the sintering temperature led to the signi /uniFB01 cant enhancement in the fracture toughness and slight drop in the hardness of ceramic matrix composites.

question: What was the measured electrical conductivity of the high-entropy diboride (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2)B2 specimen reported by Hoque et al. (2023), and what does this value correspond to for electron contribution to thermal conductivity?
answer: The measured electrical conductivity of the high-entropy diboride (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2)B2 specimen reported by Hoque et al. (2023) is (2.42 ± 0.12) × 10⁶ S/m. This value corresponds to an electron contribution of 18.1 ± 0.9 W m⁻¹ K⁻¹, which is nearly 65% of the total thermal conductivity of HEB [2].

In an experiment to determine thermal conductivity of HEBs, (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2)B2, (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Mo0.2)B2, (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Cr0.2)B2, and (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Mo0.2W0.2) B2  were produced. It was observed that their thermal conductivity was lower than monolithic borides. Precisely, HfB2 and ZrB2 exhibited thermal conductivities that were above 100 W(mK) GLYPH<0> 1 ,  while that of HEB tested were withing the range of 12 -28 W(mK) GLYPH<0> 1. The low thermal conductivity recorded in HEBs was attributed to phonon scattering by the multiple elements with various atomic sizes [47]. A related subclass of high entropy borides, the high entropy monoborides, such as (Mo ₀ . ₂ Ta ₀ . ₂ Ni ₀ . ₂ Cr ₀ . ₂ W ₀ . ₂ )B, adopt  monoboride-type  crystal  structures  distinct  from  the  AlB ₂ -type diborides discussed above. For instance, Zhao et al. [48] worked on the superhardness  and  thermal  conductivity  of  (Mo0.2Ta0.2Ni0.2Cr0.2W0.2)B

3.1. Thermal conductivity of the HEB
To approximate the electron contribution ( 𝜅 𝑒 ) to the thermal conductivity of the HEB, we use the Wiedemann-Franz law: 𝜅 𝑒 = 𝜎 𝑒 𝐿𝑇 , where 𝜎 𝑒 , L , and T represent electrical conductivity, Lorentz number, and temperature, respectively [55]. For this purpose, we measure the electrical conductivity via the four-point probe method and use a Lorentz number of 2.45 × 10 -8 W Ω K -2 . This ideal Lorentz number has been previously used to estimate the 𝜅 𝑒 values of ZrB 2 [66], HfB 2 [67], ZrB 2 -TiB 2 [66], and ZrB 2 -SiC [55] ceramics. The measured electrical conductivity of the HEB specimen is (2.42 ± 0.12) × 10 6 S/m. This value corresponds to an electron contribution of 18.1 ± 0.9 W m -1 K -1 , nearly 65% of the total thermal conductivity of HEB. Thus, our measurement provides the first empirical evidence that the thermal conductivity of HEB is electron dominated.

3.4. Thermal conductivity
0.082 at 200 ◦ C. The decrease could be due to increased phonon scattering with increasing temperature, which is consistent with the diffusivity behavior reported for SiC [30,31]. The room temperature thermal conductivity of HE was 15.1 W/(m.K) while HES had a higher room temperature thermal conductivity of 22.9 W/(m.K). Thermal conductivity values for UHTC borides have been reported to be as high as 140 W/(m.K) for single crystal ZrB2  [32] and for polycrystalline ZrB2  with low dissolved impurity contents [33], though typical values lie around 90  W/(m.K) [34]. In  comparison to typical  values  for  ZrB2,  thermal conductivities of the HE and HES ceramics produced in this study were lower. The reduction in thermal conductivity compared to single metal borides and carbides is due to phonon scattering caused by multiple metals in the lattice. The electron contribution to thermal conductivity in  HE  at  room  temperature  was  estimated  to  be  12.2  W/(m.K), contributing ~81 % to the total thermal conductivity, which makes it the dominant contribution to thermal conductivity. Earlier studies have also  shown  electrons  as  the  dominant  thermal  carriers  for  diborides [27], and the current study shows that the same behavior occurs in the high entropy dual phase UHTC. With SiC incorporation, the total thermal conductivity increased from 15.1 W/(m.K) in HE to 22.9 W/(m.K) in HES. However, the contribution of k e  decreased from ~81 % in HE to ~34 % in HES, which is attributed to higher electrical resistivity of SiC, which increases the overall electrical resistivity of HES (94 μΩ cm) as compared to that of HE (60 μΩ cm). Although the electron contribution is lower for HES,  total room  temperature  thermal

4. Conclusions
The thermal and ablation properties of a HEB are presented in this work. Our study reveals that the electrons contribute to nearly 65% of the total thermal conductivity of HEB. We used multiple thermal characterization techniques to measure the HEB thermal conductivity up to 1267 ◦ C. The comparable thermal conductivity of HEB with that of other UHTCs at such temperatures provides evidence that the HEB can be a promising material for use in hypersonic aerospace vehicles and advanced rocket nozzles. The HEB also possesses a nearly isotropic thermal conductivity along all crystallographic directions, contrary to that of other binary diborides, such as ZrB 2 . The volumetric heat capacities of HEB and ZrB 2 remain nearly the same as a function of temperature. We further found that the HEB and ZrB 2 possess nearly identical ablation resistance. The characterizations performed in this study mark a significant advancement towards employing of HEB for ultra-high temperature applications.

i) Metal -like conductivity with low resistivity: High entropy borides and dodecaborides (HEB12) demonstrate unusually high electrical conductivities far more than individual binary borides. As an example, a dodecaboride composite consisting of atomic-scale interlocks has both superhardness and metal-like conductivity, with coherent boundaries, which hardly scatter electrons, so the structure is highly conductive in spite of its complexity [58]. Also, a nonary diboride ceramics has been found to possess very low coefficient of resistivity at low temperatures, which is strategically lower than that of ZrB ₂ [59].
Fig.  7. Plot  of  Hardness  Values  of  (Hf0.2,  Zr0.2,  Ti0.2,  Ta0.2,  Mo0.2)B2  HEBs Prepared Via Borocarbon Thermal Reduction (BCTR) and Borothermal Reduction (BTR) (Tp = Top, Bt = Bottom) [55].
ii) Structural mechanisms behind conductivity: The noble electrical conductivity of HEBs is as a result of their intricate disorganized crystal structures with complicated design, giving the pathways of the electrons to remain mostly free [60]. In the referred dodecaboride, the interfaces where  atoms  are  situated  are  coherent  and  this  enables  the  flow  of electrons  with  strengthened  hardness  [58].  Again,  the  unique  lattice distortion  that  is  prevalent  in  multiple  element  systems  expands  the electronic  bands  and  enhances  electron  migration,  promoting  the metal-like behavior of HEBs despite the configurational entropy [9]. An outstanding quality in new high-entropy borides is that they are highly insensitive to temperature. The borides beyond those of nine elements exhibit a temperature coefficient of resistivity that is essentially zero, among the lowest known, and so are promising candidates in situations requiring thermal-stable conductors [7].

question: In the Hoque et al. (2023) ablation study, what were the measured laser ablation thresholds reported for the high-entropy diboride (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2)B2 and ZrB2, and what does this indicate about their bond strengths?
answer: In the Hoque et al. (2023) ablation study, the measured laser ablation thresholds for the high-entropy diboride (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2)B2 and ZrB2 were 0.018 ± 0.005 J cm$^{-2}$ and 0.023 ± 0.009 J cm$^{-2}$, respectively [1]. This indicates that the HEB and ZrB2 possess very similar bond strengths and hence, nearly-identical thermal ablation resistances.

chunks.text:
3.4. Ablation resistance of HEB and ZrB 2
where D is the diameter of the ablation region, 𝜔𝑜 is the beam waist of the laser, and F is the incident fluence. Due to the short pulse duration used here ( ∼ 200 fs), the ablation threshold is dictated by the atomic bonding energy of the material with minimal contributions from thermal diffusion into the surrounding region [76,77].
As exhibited in Fig. 4(b), the ablation thresholds of HEB and ZrB 2 are measured to be 0.018 ± 0.005 and 0.023 ± 0.009 J cm -2 , respectively; nearly the same within uncertainty. This indicates that the HEB and ZrB 2 possess very similar bond strengths and hence, nearly-identical thermal ablation resistances. To further verify this, we compare the elastic modulus of the two materials as it is also an indicator of bond strength [3,78]. Qin et al. [43] recently reported the elastic modulus of the HEB to be ∼ 525 GPa. Using nanoindentation [79,80], we measure the elastic modulus of the ZrB 2 specimen to be 502 ± 30 GPa. In short, through a combination of laser-induced ablation measurements and nanoindentation, our results strongly suggest that the HEB studied in this work has very similar mechanical properties to those of ZrB 2 . We note that this conclusion may change based on the relative density and purity level of the HEB or ZrB 2 specimen [81]. As ZrB 2 is widely regarded as a promising material for high temperature structural applications [42,66,82], our measurements potentially suggest that highly dense HEB may be a strong competing material candidate for similar applications.

3.4. Ablation resistance of HEB and ZrB 2
The thermal characterizations of the HEB suggests that it could be used as a suitable material in extreme environments in lieu of metal diborides. Therefore, to assess the performance of the HEB under extreme thermal loads, we studied the ablation resistance of the HEB and ZrB 2 specimens. For this purpose, we performed laser ablation threshold measurements on both materials. In our laser ablation setup, a sub-picosecond, 520 nm laser is focused onto the sample surface with varying pulsed fluences. We report the threshold for 1200 pulses incident upon the sample to eliminate the role of surface inhomogeneities that may alter the optical absorption of single pulse measurements [73, 74]. Additional details regarding the ablation setup are provided in the Supporting Information.

[92]  synthesized  seven  equimolar  five-component  high-entropy  metal diborides, including (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)B2, (Hf0.2Ta0.2Mo0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)B2, (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Mo0.2Ti0.2)B2,  (Hf0.2Mo0.2Zr0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)B2,  (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Cr0.2Ti0.2)B2, (Mo0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)B2, via high-energy ball milling and spark plasma sintering (SPS) at 2000 ℃ for 5 min under 30 MPa pressure in vacuum. These materials exhibit a  layered  hexagonal  structure  (Fig.  3),  characterized  by  alternating  rigid  twodimensional (2D) boron networks and 2D high-entropy metal cation layers. the highentropy  effect  induces  lattice  distortions  due  to  atomic  size  mismatches,  which significantly hinder dislocation motion.  This results in enhanced  hardness,  as demonstrated by (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)B2 achieving a hardness of 24.5 GPa which 15% higher than the average of its constituent diborides. High-entropy materials exhibit lower weight gain after oxidation at 1500 ℃ compared to the average of their five constituent components.  This performance enhancement is attributed to: high configurational  entropy  inhibits  ordered  phase  precipitation,  and  multiple  elements cooperate to form dense mixed oxides, which hinder oxygen ion penetration. These Journal Pre-proof findings  establish  high-entropy  diborides  as  a  novel  class  of  ultrahigh-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) with tunable oxidation resistance, exceptional mechanical properties, and quasi-2D structural uniqueness for extreme environments.

question: According to the Hoque et al. (2023) paper, what was the average grain size of the high-entropy diboride specimen, and what crystal structure does this material possess?
answer: The average grain size of the high-entropy diboride (HEB) specimen was 15±9.5 μm, and this material possesses a single-phase, hexagonal crystal structure (AlB 2 ) [1].

chunks.text:
2. Microstructural details of the HEB specimen
The HEB studied in this work possesses a single-phase, hexagonal crystal structure (AlB 2 ) [42] with high symmetry [9,19]. Fig. 1 shows the highly anisotropic layered crystal structure of the HEB consisting of alternating rigid two-dimensional (2D) boron nets and 2D layers of five metal cations (Hf, Zr, Ti, Ta, and Nb) [9]. The polycrystalline HEB sample for this study was fabricated by reactive spark plasma sintering of ball milled elemental powders [43]. During spark plasma sintering, the powders were first held at temperatures of 1400 and 1600 ◦ C for 80 min each to allow for sufficient outgassing and native oxide reduction. The temperature was then raised at 30 ◦ C/min to the final sintering temperature of 2200 ◦ C and held for 30 min, prior to cooling to room temperature in 15-20 min. A pressure of 80 MPa was applied at the maximum temperature to enable full densification. The prepared specimen was highly dense ( > 98% of theoretical density) and contained negligible amount of native oxide contaminants. The composition of the sample was measured by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) to be 98% (Hf 0.19 Zr 0.19 Ti 0.22 Ta 0.19 Nb0.18 W 0.03 )B 2 with residual amount of carbide phase [43]. The average grain size of the HEB specimen was 15±9 . 5 μm , as measured by electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD). A detailed description of the HEB sample fabrication and characterization can be found in Qin et al. [43] The HEB specimen of this study and that of Ref. [43] were prepared following the same methodology.

3.2. Universal strategy for diboride-based compounds synthesis
Fig. 6a) shows the Rietveld refinement for HfB2 synthesized by FJHBCTR under current controlled ramp at an applied electric field of 15 V cm GLYPH<0> 1 and a maximum current density of 4 A mm GLYPH<0> 2 . The structural parameters and confidence factors are included in Table S1, proving that the sample corresponds to a pure specimen adopting the typical hexagonal  AlB2-type  structure  in  which  TMB2 crystallize  [1].  The  SEM micrographs reveal quasicolumn particles, attributed to the anisotropic nature of the crystal structure, with an average particle size of 645 nm, and some level of agglomeration (Fig. 6b)). For further information, see Fig. S5 where a particle size distribution histogram of primary particles can be found. The EDX maps show a homogeneous distribution of each constituent element (Fig. 6c)), where the presence of oxygen and carbon (not shown) is just residual with values below 3 % and 4 %, respectively, according to the semiquantitative analysis of the EDX spectrum. These results are similar to those previously reported by other HfB2 synthetic methodologies [66 -70]. Note that some of them have been even dedicated to the reduction of the particle size and improvement of the dispersibility of the as-synthesized  powders, such as the modified carbothermal/borothermal reduction methodology proposed by Ni et al. [66] or the dispersant assisted co-precipitation method combined with carbothermal reduction recently proposed by Wang et al. [67]. Thus, the potential of the FJH-BCTR methodology to achieve sub-micron powders of TMB2  is evidenced, which production is essential to smooth the afterwards  sintering  process,  preventing  excessive  grain  growth  and facilitating the production of high performance materials with uniform properties [1,68].

CRYSTAL STRUCTURE AND BASIC PROPERTIES OF UHTCS
Borides, or more precisely the di-borides such as ZrB 2 ,  TiB 2 ,  HfB 2 ,  TaB 2 (in general terms; MB 2 , where M = Zr, Ti, Hf or Ta), usually possess primitive hexagonal crystal structure of the AlB 2 prototype having space group P6/mmm (Fahrenholtz,  2007;  Li,  2010).  The  layers  of  B  atoms  usually  form  two  dimensional graphite-like rings that alternate with the metal layers in a hexagonally close-packed array. In analogy with the usual notation of ABABAB for HCP structure, the stacking sequence of M atoms are AAA, with the boron (B) atoms located interstitially between the A-layers, forming a strong covalently bonded hexagonal network structure (Figure 1a). In other words, the M atoms are located at (0,0,0) and B atoms at (1/3, 2/3, 1/2) and (2/3, 1/3, 1/2) lattice sites. Since the bond strengths usually determine the basic properties of materials, the high strengths of the B-B rings and M-B bonds bestow these materials with the refractoriness. In addition to the high melting point (> 3000 o C for the di-borides under consideration), their room temperature hardness can be up to 30 GPa and elastic modulus ~ 500 GPa, with relatively suppressed deterioration with rise in temperature (Cutler, 1991; Telle, 1994; Cotton, 2010; Basu, 2006; Wuchina, 2007; Fahrenholtz, 2007; Pierson, 1996). Also, their thermal conductivities can be of the same order of magnitude as most metallic materials such as Cu or Al. It must be noted that even though the properties within individual crystals of the di-borides will be anisotropic due to the hexagonal crystal structure, the data usually reported in literature (and summarized in Table 1) are

The relative densities of the prepared ceramics with different added graphite contents are also shown in Fig. 3(b). It is seen that the relative density continuously increased with the addition of graphite, indicating that both the oxygen-removing process and the lubricating effect of the remaining graphite particles enhanced the densification process.
Fig. 5. Schematic diagram of the microstructure evolution of the high-entropy diboride ceramics with the addition of graphite.
Fig. 4 shows the BSE images of the polished surfaces and fracture surfaces of the HEBx C specimens. Three distinct phases are observed in the HEB-10 C sample, including the HEB phase (grey), M C phase (light), and  graphite  phase  (dark).  For  the  HEB-20 C  specimen,  most  of  the added graphite remained in the HEB-20 C specimen and was oriented perpendicular to the pressure direction of SPS. In addition, it is difficult to distinguish the grain boundaries of the HEB phase owing to the rough polished surface resulting from the pull out of a large amount of residual graphite phase in HEB-20 C ceramic. The average grain size of the HEB phase and the content of the M C phase are listed in Table 1. The grain size of the HEB phase ranges from 1.05 µ m in HEB-0 C to 2.55 µ m in HEB-10 C and the amount of M C phase increase from 0.2 vol% in HEB1 C to 4.8 vol% in HEB-10 C. In non-oxide ceramics, the existence of the oxide impurity phase usually impedes grain growth [24]. Therefore, it can be assumed that the t -(Zr, Hf)O2 phases inhibit the grain growth of the HEB phase in this study.

3.1. Microstructural analysis
Fig. 2 shows the recorded XRD pattern of the (Ta-Hf-Zr-Nb-Ti)C-(TaHf-Zr-Nb-Ti)B2 composite  ceramics  with  indexed  peaks.  The  XRD pattern confirms that a dual-phase high-entropy system was synthesized with a negligible amount of oxides (HfO2/ZrO2 < 1 wt%) where the carbide and boride phases are denoted by HEC and HEB in the present work. The lattice constants are a = 4.5246(7) Å for the cubic carbide (Fm-3 m) and a = 3.0974(6) Å, c = 3.3643(8) Å for the hexagonal boride (P6/mmm) phases. Microstructure characteristics, grain size distribution and chemical composition of the investigated dual-phase ceramic sample are illustrated in Figs. 3 and 4. According to the results, both the carbide (light grey in SEM) and boride grains (dark grey in SEM) are relatively homogenously distributed with a small amount of porosity, resulting in a relative density of 99%. The grain size varies between 1 µ m to 8 µ m with a slightly lower average size for carbides (~2 µ m) than for borides  (~3 µ m),  forming  larger  clusters  of  grains.  Very  small  pores were  detected  at  the  grain  boundaries  with  a  size  of  approximately 100 nm. The grain sizes in the developed system are similar to the grain sizes for the system published by Qin et al. [21] who reported a definite correlation between the grain sizes of the carbide and boride phases with the phase fraction and the lowest grain sizes approximately 7 µ m with similar mol% of carbide/boride in the systems. Luo et al. [22] reported significantly lower average grain sizes of 0.64 ± 0.2 µ m for the boride and 1.5 ± 0.4 µ m for the carbide in their dual-phase ceramic, processed using different processing routes.

question: In the Kelly et al. (2022) binder jet additive manufacturing study on ZrB2-MoSi2 ceramic heat exchangers, what target relative density was specified as the goal for the fabricated parts, and what is the significance of this density level?
answer: The target relative density specified as the goal for the fabricated parts in the Kelly et al. (2022) study was ≥ 92 % of theoretical [1]. This density level is significant because it indicates the transition from intermediate to final stage sintering, which is a requirement to inhibit gas permeability and to sinter complex near net shapes to full density with sinter-HIP technology [1].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
Triply periodic minimal surface (TPMS) geometries can only be fabricated by additive manufacturing methods and are of interest for heat exchangers. Ceramic TPMS heat exchangers can operate at higher temperatures and pressures with superior performance and increased operating efficiencies compared to metal heat exchangers. The properties of ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) materials are also favorable for heat exchangers and potentially suited to concentrated solar power (CSP) systems, such as those based on a molten chloride salt thermal energy storage (TES) medium used to heat CO2  in a closed-loop Brayton power cycle. We intended to demonstrate binder jet additive manufacturing feasibility of a UHTC-TPMS structure by printing and sintering a null candidate. We aimed to achieve parts with a relative density ≥ 92 % of theoretical and to provide a TPMS part  demonstration.  The  target  density  indicates  the  transition  from  intermediate  to  final  stage  sintering,  a requirement to inhibit gas permeability and for sintering complex near net shapes to full density with sinter-HIP technology. The goal of the TPMS part demonstration was to determine if printing and sintering parameters developed from test coupons apply to the complex geometries that will eventually be used in a heat exchanger design. Our objective was to print cubic TPMS parts with a 9 cm 3 volume and sinter it without distorting and cracking. We were able to sinter ZrB2-MoSi2 composite parts based on the Schwarz-D TPMS and achieve isotropic shrinkage up to 60 % by volume, resulting in densities ranging from 92 % to 96 % of theoretical.
and manufacturing to achieve a CSP heat exchanger with best-in-class performance in molten chloride salts and aim to demonstrate feasibility.

3.2. Printing and sintering development
Initial printing was done either with The ExOne Company ' s Innovent or Innovent + printers and with either PhenolFuse or AquaFuse binders. The  Innovent + printer  with  Triple  ACT  technology  combined  with AquaFuse binder provided the best printing results. Powder spreading was a challenge but not unexpected for finer, cohesive powders. Higher roller speeds and addition of a binder set time tended to yield better quality coupons. Green densities of printed parts ranged from 31 % to 39 % of theoretical, generally lower than those obtained by conventional ceramic processing. Optimization of powder rheology and printing parameters are expected to improve powder spreading behavior and yield higher green densities. Fig. 3 shows images of printed coupons from five different print trials.
Initial development to determine suitable sintering parameters utilized cylindrical coupons from the printing trials, such as those shown in Fig. 3. Our goal was to obtain relative densities ≥ 92 % of theoretical, roughly corresponding to the transition from open porosity to closed porosity in the microstructure. 3.2. Printing and sintering development

Fig. 3. Representative images of sample coupons from initial print trials with nominal diameters of 1.25 in. Coupons from Run 5 were the most robust and had a smoother surface. The parameters used for these print trials are summarized in the Supplementary Information (see Table S1).

Silvestroni and Sciti  [28]  were  able  to  obtain  ZrB2-MoSi2 composites  with  relative densities  ranging  from  86  %  to  99  %  of  theoretical  by  sintering  for 60 min at 1800 -1950 º C in flowing argon. Our attempts to replicate these results  with  parts  made  by  binder  jet  additive  manufacturing  using similar parameters only resulted in densities up to 69 % of theoretical and  is  believed  to  be  due  to  the  lower  green  density  of  the  parts. Increasing the time at 1900 º C to 18 hrs resulted in one sample with a density of 5.66 g/cm 3 (93 % of theoretical) while sintering three samples  at  2000 º C  for  18  hrs  resulted  in  densities  ranging  from  5.65  to 5.94 g/cm 3 or 93 -98 % of theoretical.

A B S T R A C T
Binder jet printing has been combined with reactive infiltration to fabricate  UHTC  heat  exchanger  plates  [13],  but  has  not  yet  been employed  to  fabricate  UHTC  TPMS  structures  that  are  subsequently sintered to high relative densities. Specific challenges to enable additive manufacturing of  sintered  UHTC  TPMS  ceramics  comparable  to conventionally processed materials include printing with small particle sizes typical of advanced ceramics with high strengths and developing post-processing  protocols  that  account  for  the  lower  green  densities achieved by the forming process. Lessons learned from the sintering of nanomaterials suggests that low green densities from forming processes are not necessarily problematic and that it is more important to obtain good uniformity [25]. Thus, we decided to focus on printing with fine particle sizes to address this challenge. Herein, we describe binder jet additive manufacturing combined with sintering to fabricate UHTC-TPMS cells based on ZrB2-MoSi2 and using particle sizes that are typically used in conventional processes. The ZrB2-MoSi2  system was strategically selected as a null candidate to demonstrate UHTC-TPMS heat exchanger feasibility until the best UHTC material for the application can be identified because ZrB2-MoSi2  has favorable processing characteristics and properties [26 -29].

4. Conclusions
We achieved our initial objective, proving that it is feasible to print and sinter UHTC-TPMS  structures  made  by  binder jet additive manufacturing. We found it was necessary to employ a spatial confinement method during sintering to effectively mitigate distortion. It was possible to use conventional powder feedstocks with a d50 ≅ 2 -3 µ m, sizes also used for conventional processing of UHTC materials. These materials were sintered to relative densities in the range of 92 -98 % of theoretical,  which  are  high  enough  to  prevent  heat  exchanger  fluids from permeating through the walls separating the two domains and to enable hot isostatic pressing if a higher density is needed to improve mechanical  properties.  Given  these  encouraging  results,  we  are  proposing to combine advances in design, materials, and manufacturing for CSP heat exchangers. A heat exchanger based on a TPMS offers an opportunity to increase power density when compared to designs based on tubes or plates. Our calculations using the scaffold dimensions we achieved  in  this  work  suggest  that  a  power  density  up  to  50 MW/m 3 is achievable for a UHTC heat exchanger based on the Schwarz-D TPMS with molten salt and supercritical carbon dioxide working fluids. Based on this power density, the largest binder jet printer on the market for printing ceramics has the potential to print a one-piece heat exchanger with capacity rating up to 3 MW. The potential for even higher capacities is currently limited by either the scale of an available printer or modular heat exchanger construction methods.

question: What particle size range was observed for the ZrB2 and MoSi2 powders selected for binder jet printing in the Kelly et al. (2022) study, and what manufacturing objectives did the choice of these fine particles support?
answer: The particle size range observed for the ZrB2 and MoSi2 powders selected for binder jet printing in the Kelly et al. (2022) study was 1 - 5 µm [1]. The choice of these fine particles supported the manufacturing objectives of retaining good sintering characteristics and better properties, as well as addressing the challenge of achieving high relative densities through sintering with low green densities [2].

chunks.text:
3.1. Materials selection and powder characterization
Powder  characteristics  can  impact  sintering  behavior  [25].  For example, sintering rates can vary by several orders of magnitude due to differences in particle sizes, resulting in different sintering temperatures, and preference for smaller particle sizes [39]. Other factors that depend on particle size such as bulk density, tap density, and powder flowability are also important for the binder jet additive manufacturing process and creates a preference for larger sizes [40,41]. We wanted to retain the good sintering characteristics and better properties afforded by using smaller particles and push the limits of what is typical for binder jet additive  manufacturing.  We  evaluated  the  flow  behavior  of  multiple ZrB2  and MoSi2  powders and the results are shown in Table 2. ZrB2 Powder B and MoSi2  Powder D were selected for further testing and binder jet additive manufacturing trials. Images demonstrating particle morphology and particle size distributions for these powders are shown in  Fig.  2.  The  powders  both  have  an  irregular,  fragmented  particle morphology  that  suggest  the  powders  were  made  by  comminution. There isn ' t any substantial phase contrast in the images, suggesting good phase purity. Most of the particles were in the 1 -5 µ m range and no particles greater than 7 µ m were observed.

A B S T R A C T
Binder jet printing has been combined with reactive infiltration to fabricate  UHTC  heat  exchanger  plates  [13],  but  has  not  yet  been employed  to  fabricate  UHTC  TPMS  structures  that  are  subsequently sintered to high relative densities. Specific challenges to enable additive manufacturing of  sintered  UHTC  TPMS  ceramics  comparable  to conventionally processed materials include printing with small particle sizes typical of advanced ceramics with high strengths and developing post-processing  protocols  that  account  for  the  lower  green  densities achieved by the forming process. Lessons learned from the sintering of nanomaterials suggests that low green densities from forming processes are not necessarily problematic and that it is more important to obtain good uniformity [25]. Thus, we decided to focus on printing with fine particle sizes to address this challenge. Herein, we describe binder jet additive manufacturing combined with sintering to fabricate UHTC-TPMS cells based on ZrB2-MoSi2 and using particle sizes that are typically used in conventional processes. The ZrB2-MoSi2  system was strategically selected as a null candidate to demonstrate UHTC-TPMS heat exchanger feasibility until the best UHTC material for the application can be identified because ZrB2-MoSi2  has favorable processing characteristics and properties [26 -29].

3.2. Printing and sintering development

Silvestroni and Sciti  [28]  were  able  to  obtain  ZrB2-MoSi2 composites  with  relative densities  ranging  from  86  %  to  99  %  of  theoretical  by  sintering  for 60 min at 1800 -1950 º C in flowing argon. Our attempts to replicate these results  with  parts  made  by  binder  jet  additive  manufacturing  using similar parameters only resulted in densities up to 69 % of theoretical and  is  believed  to  be  due  to  the  lower  green  density  of  the  parts. Increasing the time at 1900 º C to 18 hrs resulted in one sample with a density of 5.66 g/cm 3 (93 % of theoretical) while sintering three samples  at  2000 º C  for  18  hrs  resulted  in  densities  ranging  from  5.65  to 5.94 g/cm 3 or 93 -98 % of theoretical.

3.1. Materials selection and powder characterization
UHTC materials  are  mostly  sintered  using  pressure-assisted  technology -either  hot  pressing  or  spark  plasma  sintering -due  to  the general challenges when trying to sinter these materials to high density. However, pressureless sintering is required to sinter  UHTC materials with complex shapes enabled by additive manufacturing. We chose to evaluate ZrB2 based UHTC materials to demonstrate binder jet additive manufacturing  feasibility  because  ZrB2  is  arguably  the  most  studied UHTC material. We screened literature to identify pressureless sintered ZrB2  compositions that resulted in high relative densities and list examples in Table 1. The particles sizes from these studies are typical for conventional  UHTC  manufacturing  and  preferred  for  sintering,  but smaller than  what  is  typically  preferred for binder  jet additive manufacturing to ensure good flowability of the powder for spreading [24]. Of the materials evaluated, we selected the ZrB2-MoSi2  material system  to  evaluate  manufacturability  because  these  compositions improve the oxidation resistance of ZrB2, have a relatively low sintering temperature, and have other beneficial properties and characteristics.

2. Materials and methods
A TPMS geometry divides the domain into two spatially distinct and intertwined flow domains. Due to the convoluted nature of the flow path through  these  channels,  the  convective  heat  transfer  can  be  greatly enhanced with a moderate pressure drop, resulting in more efficient heat exchanger design. Although there are a range of TPMS geometries that can be fabricated, our previous studies have shown that the Schwarz-D geometry exhibits the best heat transfer performance among the many candidates  we  evaluated  [4].  For  this  reason,  we  have  selected Schwarz-D surface for our heat exchanger designs and representative structures  that  we  selected  to  evaluate  printing  and  post-processing feasibility are provided in Fig. 1.
Two different ZrB2  powders were evaluated: ZrB2  Powder A (3 µ m APS, H ¨ ogan ¨ as AB, H ¨ ogan ¨ as, Sweden) and ZrB2 Powder B (ZR-201, GLYPH<0> 325 mesh, Micron Metals, Saddle River, NJ, USA). Three different MoSi2 powders  were  evaluated:  MoSi2  Powder  C  (3 µ m  APS,  H ¨ ogan ¨ as  AB, H ¨ ogan ¨ as,  Sweden),  MoSi2  Powder  D  (MO-701,  5 -7 µ m  APS,  Micron Metals,  Saddle  River,  NJ,  USA),  and  MoSi2  Powder  D  ( GLYPH<0> 325  Mesh, Stanford Advanced Materials, Lake Forest, CA, USA). Flow characteristics of these powders were measured with a powder rheometer (FT4, Freeman Technology, Tewkesbury, UK) and electron images were taken with a desktop SEM (Phenom Pro, Micromeritics Instrument Corporation,  Norcross,  GA,  USA).  Particle  size  distributions  of  the  ZrB2  and MoSi2 powders were obtained by image analysis of electron micrographs.

question: According to Kelly et al. (2022), what sintering temperature and duration were ultimately used to achieve the target density for ZrB2-MoSi2 TPMS structures, and what volumetric shrinkage resulted from this process?
answer: According to Kelly et al. (2022), the sintering temperature and duration ultimately used to achieve the target density for ZrB2-MoSi2 TPMS structures were 2000 º C for 18 hrs, which resulted in densities ranging from 5.65 to 5.94 g/cm 3 or 93 -98 % of theoretical [1]. The volumetric shrinkage resulting from this process was up to 60 % by volume [1].

Silvestroni and Sciti  [28]  were  able  to  obtain  ZrB2-MoSi2 composites  with  relative densities  ranging  from  86  %  to  99  %  of  theoretical  by  sintering  for 60 min at 1800 -1950 º C in flowing argon. Increasing the time at 1900 º C to 18 hrs resulted in one sample with a density of 5.66 g/cm 3 (93 % of theoretical) while sintering three samples  at  2000 º C  for  18  hrs  resulted  in  densities  ranging  from  5.65  to 5.94 g/cm 3 or 93 -98 % of theoretical.

3.1. Structure
Shrinkage. As shown in our previous work [46], densification by pressureless sintering of ceramics based on ZrB2  and ZrB2 -10 wt% SiC required sintering temperatures ~ 2200 ◦ C. However, this high temperature did not lead to fully dense ceramics and resulted in a significant increase of the grain size from 2 µ m (after milling) to 20 µ m. A significant increase in grain size of the ceramic sharply reduces the mechanical properties, and, as such the flexural strength at room temperature was 150 MPa or below.
The  addition  of  activators  such  as  Cr3C2,  Mo2C,  and  WC  led  to densification of ceramics at much lower temperatures. Dependences of the shrinkage vs sintering temperature for binary and triple systems are presented in Fig. 2. According to the obtained results, the highest activation ability was measured for Cr3C2, and then lowest for WC. This is quite consistent with the melting temperatures of the additives: Cr3C2 Tm = 1895  o C, Mo2C Tm = 2520 o C, and WC Tm = 2870 o C, and, as a consequence, with the rate of a diffusion process. Here, despite the fact that the sintering temperature was higher than the melting point of the Cr3C2,  all  the  added  Cr3C2 reacted  with  the  matrix  and  was  fully consumed by chemical interaction. Also, this fact explains the high value of the final shrinkage.

5.2. ZrB2 ceramics
The sintering temperature range for densification of ZrB2 is influenced by melting point of the silicides used as the sinter aids [34,134,136,137]. The densification temperature (1600 °C) is lower for ZrSi2-reinforced ZrB2 when compared to other silicides since the melting point of ZrSi2 is relatively low. Sciti et al. [112,134,149] extensively studied the use of MoSi2 sinter-additive for ZrB2/ HfB2. Their work revealed that achievement of near-theoretical sinter-density was possible for ZrB2 with the use of MoSi2 (3-20 vol %) via PS at 1900 °C, HP at 1800 °C and SPS at 1750 °C. The densification was assisted by transient liquid phase that formed due to the reaction between MoSi2 and surface oxide impurities on ZrB2, as was shown by Eq. (30). During hot pressing, the plastic deformation of silicides at high temperatures (> 800 °C) would also contributes to the densification of ZrB2 as the deformation forces soft silicide particles to fill in the voids left by ZrB2 particles [112,134].

A B S T R A C T
Triply periodic minimal surface (TPMS) geometries can only be fabricated by additive manufacturing methods and are of interest for heat exchangers. Ceramic TPMS heat exchangers can operate at higher temperatures and pressures with superior performance and increased operating efficiencies compared to metal heat exchangers. The properties of ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) materials are also favorable for heat exchangers and potentially suited to concentrated solar power (CSP) systems, such as those based on a molten chloride salt thermal energy storage (TES) medium used to heat CO2  in a closed-loop Brayton power cycle. We intended to demonstrate binder jet additive manufacturing feasibility of a UHTC-TPMS structure by printing and sintering a null candidate. We aimed to achieve parts with a relative density ≥ 92 % of theoretical and to provide a TPMS part  demonstration.  The  target  density  indicates  the  transition  from  intermediate  to  final  stage  sintering,  a requirement to inhibit gas permeability and for sintering complex near net shapes to full density with sinter-HIP technology. The goal of the TPMS part demonstration was to determine if printing and sintering parameters developed from test coupons apply to the complex geometries that will eventually be used in a heat exchanger design. Our objective was to print cubic TPMS parts with a 9 cm 3 volume and sinter it without distorting and cracking. We were able to sinter ZrB2-MoSi2 composite parts based on the Schwarz-D TPMS and achieve isotropic shrinkage up to 60 % by volume, resulting in densities ranging from 92 % to 96 % of theoretical.
and manufacturing to achieve a CSP heat exchanger with best-in-class performance in molten chloride salts and aim to demonstrate feasibility.

question: In the Kelly et al. (2022) study of binder jet-printed ZrB2-MoSi2 TPMS heat exchangers, what estimated power density was calculated for a heat exchanger based on Schwarz-D TPMS dimensions, and what maximum heat exchanger capacity could theoretically be produced with current commercial printers?
answer: In the Kelly et al. (2022) study of binder jet-printed ZrB2-MoSi2 TPMS heat exchangers, an estimated power density of up to 50 MW/m³ was calculated for a heat exchanger based on Schwarz-D TPMS dimensions [1][3]. The maximum heat exchanger capacity that could theoretically be produced with current commercial printers is up to 3 MW [1][3].

chunks.text:
4. Conclusions
We achieved our initial objective, proving that it is feasible to print and sinter UHTC-TPMS  structures  made  by  binder jet additive manufacturing. We found it was necessary to employ a spatial confinement method during sintering to effectively mitigate distortion. It was possible to use conventional powder feedstocks with a d50 ≅ 2 -3 µ m, sizes also used for conventional processing of UHTC materials. These materials were sintered to relative densities in the range of 92 -98 % of theoretical,  which  are  high  enough  to  prevent  heat  exchanger  fluids from permeating through the walls separating the two domains and to enable hot isostatic pressing if a higher density is needed to improve mechanical  properties.  Given  these  encouraging  results,  we  are  proposing to combine advances in design, materials, and manufacturing for CSP heat exchangers. A heat exchanger based on a TPMS offers an opportunity to increase power density when compared to designs based on tubes or plates. Our calculations using the scaffold dimensions we achieved  in  this  work  suggest  that  a  power  density  up  to  50 MW/m 3 is achievable for a UHTC heat exchanger based on the Schwarz-D TPMS with molten salt and supercritical carbon dioxide working fluids. Based on this power density, the largest binder jet printer on the market for printing ceramics has the potential to print a one-piece heat exchanger with capacity rating up to 3 MW. The potential for even higher capacities is currently limited by either the scale of an available printer or modular heat exchanger construction methods.

A B S T R A C T
Singh et. al. analyzed a CSP heat exchanger based on plates and found the potential to achieve up to 3.5 MW/m 3 power density [2]. Femmer and  coworkers  studied  heat  transfer  across  triply  periodic  minimal surface (TPMS) boundaries and showed 1 -2 orders of magnitude higher heat transfer when compared to conventional designs based on plates or tubes [3]. A more recent analysis of several TPMS and periodic nodal surfaces as heat exchangers showed that the Schwarz-D TPMS surface in particular  has  exceptional  heat  transfer  performance  and  at  a  fixed pressure drop, a Schwarz-D TPMS heat exchanger can achieve the same performance in an order of magnitude smaller volume than a tubular heat exchanger [4]. A recent study by Li et al. [5] sheds light on the role of turbulent heat transfer inside TPMS geometries. They showed that small scale separations occur near the wall that disrupt the boundary layer  and  enhance  heat  transfer.  When  they  compared  results  to  a J.P. Kelly et al.
conventional printed circuit heat exchanger (PCHE), the overall thermal performance  was  enhanced  by  15 -100  %  for  gyroid  and  Schwarz-D TPMS structures when also accounting for pressure drop and these enhancements were attributed to improved turbulent kinetic energy production. Our own analysis using the methods described elsewhere [3,5, 6]  and  with  CSP  inputs  based  on  a  molten  chloride  salt  TES  [1,7,8] confirm the potential to achieve a power density of up to 50 MW/m 3 for a  UHTC-TPMS  heat  exchanger  based  on  the  Schwarz-D  TPMS  dimensions we achieved and describe within.

3.5. Implications for Heat Exchangers
The feasibility to fabricate UHTC-TPMS heat exchangers by binder jet additive manufacturing has been demonstrated with a research grade printer  by  successfully  printing  and  sintering  the  basic  structural building block of the heat exchanger. The largest commercial binder jet printer currently on the market for powder processing has a build volume of 0.8 × 0.5 × 0.4 m or 0.16 m 3 . Applying the sintering shrinkage that  we  observed  to  those  dimensions  suggests  that  heat  exchanger builds with the following dimensions are possible: 0.59 × 0.37 m crosssection  with  length  up  to  0.30 m,  0.59 × 0.30 m  cross  section  with length up to 0.37 m, or 0.37 × 0.30 m cross-section with length up to J.P. Kelly et al.
Fig. 6. Printed UHTC-TPMS cells are shown on the left with 90 º rotations to show different orientations and the sintered parts are shown on the right. The relative sizes between printed and sintered parts depicted in the images are approximately representative of the shrinkage that occurs during sintering of the printed parts. The printed parts were designed to be cubes with side lengths of 3 cm, unit cell size with side lengths of 2 cm, and wall thicknesses of either 0.2, 0.3, or 0.4 cm. All dimensions shrunk about 26 % during sintering.

A heat exchanger block of the TPMS internals at this scale and a representative sub-scale component  showing  flow  domain  surfaces  are  demonstrated  in  Fig.  7.  The power density for this heat exchanger block calculated using methods described  elsewhere  [3,5,6]  and  with  CSP  inputs  based  on  molten chloride salt TES [1,7,8] can be as high as 50 MW/m 3 . Combining the size of this heat exchanger block with this power density estimate results in a heat exchanger capacity rating of up to 3 MW. Calculations and assumptions are provided in the Supplemental Information.

A B S T R A C T
Triply periodic minimal surface (TPMS) geometries can only be fabricated by additive manufacturing methods and are of interest for heat exchangers. Ceramic TPMS heat exchangers can operate at higher temperatures and pressures with superior performance and increased operating efficiencies compared to metal heat exchangers. The properties of ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) materials are also favorable for heat exchangers and potentially suited to concentrated solar power (CSP) systems, such as those based on a molten chloride salt thermal energy storage (TES) medium used to heat CO2  in a closed-loop Brayton power cycle. We intended to demonstrate binder jet additive manufacturing feasibility of a UHTC-TPMS structure by printing and sintering a null candidate. We aimed to achieve parts with a relative density ≥ 92 % of theoretical and to provide a TPMS part  demonstration.  The  target  density  indicates  the  transition  from  intermediate  to  final  stage  sintering,  a requirement to inhibit gas permeability and for sintering complex near net shapes to full density with sinter-HIP technology. The goal of the TPMS part demonstration was to determine if printing and sintering parameters developed from test coupons apply to the complex geometries that will eventually be used in a heat exchanger design. Our objective was to print cubic TPMS parts with a 9 cm 3 volume and sinter it without distorting and cracking. We were able to sinter ZrB2-MoSi2 composite parts based on the Schwarz-D TPMS and achieve isotropic shrinkage up to 60 % by volume, resulting in densities ranging from 92 % to 96 % of theoretical.
and manufacturing to achieve a CSP heat exchanger with best-in-class performance in molten chloride salts and aim to demonstrate feasibility.

3.5. Implications for Heat Exchangers
The primary heat exchanger capacity rating of up to 3 MW does not include any design optimization and it may be possible to achieve higher capacity ratings. Furthermore, a modular approach to constructing the heat exchanger, such as is utilized in conventional heat exchanger designs, could be used to produce much larger heat exchangers where the rating would be controlled by the number of modules incorporated into the design. For example, Fig. 8 shows a tubular heat exchanger element based on the Schwarz-D surface. In mass and heat transfer systems, a common challenge  is  how  to  increase  the  available  surface  area  for contact with the fluids without creating diffusion limitations. Reducing the channel size uniformly yields high surface area but leads to a narrow diffusion pathway and high pressure drop. Increasing the channel size eases flow but results in less surface area for heat or mass transfer. Hierarchical systems are common in nature to overcome this dilemma, such as in plant vasculature and lung alveoli [48]. They overcome this limitation through optimized patterning between low and high specific surface area structures to obtain high diffusion and interaction between fluids, while having larger channels for bulk flow and efficient distribution. The design concept in Fig. 8 has large channels with thicker walls next to the inlets and outlets to facilitate flow distribution into the J.P. Kelly et al.
Fig. 7. Example of a TPMS heat exchanger block of dimensions enabled by commercial binder jet systems (left) and a more limited cell to the right showing the two flow domain surfaces for molten salt and supercritical CO2  (sCO2) working fluids of a hypothetical CSP system.
Fig. 8. The image to the left shows a design concept for a tubular heat exchanger element with internal features based on a Schwarz-D TPMS. The cutaway to the right shows the two independent fluid flow domains. Also incorporated in the design is innovative combinations of size domains with local wall thickness variations to control performance outcomes.

question: What challenge related to warping was identified during the sintering process in Kelly et al. (2022), and what mitigation strategy proved effective for preventing distortion of the ZrB2-MoSi2 parts?
answer: The challenge related to warping during the sintering process in Kelly et al. (2022) was significant distortion of the parts, as evidenced by a 6 % mass loss and a high h/t ratio of 2.36 ± 0.15 mm for the part on the left in Fig. 4 [1]. The effective mitigation strategy for preventing distortion of the ZrB2-MoSi2 parts was the use of spatial confinement in an inverted crucible packed with a carbon powder bed, which reduced the h/t ratio to 1.07 ± 0.06 mm, demonstrating negligible distortion [1].

chunks.text:
3.3. Sintered microstructures
Fractures  surfaces  from  three  replicates  were  imaged  with  backscattered electrons and Fig. 5 demonstrates the results. Two of three samples had higher density, 5.90 -5.94 g/cm 3 or 97 -98 % of theoretical, whereas one sample had a lower density (5.65 g/cm 3 or 93 % of theoretical). The low-density sample had a residual phase with dark phase contrast in the microstructure. Residual SiO2  in the microstructure of ZrB2-MoSi2  composites has been shown to have similar phase contrast [27,47]. Indeed, we have observed silicon, oxygen, and carbon in this phase with electron probe microanalysis using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. It is unclear exactly why this sample had a greater amount of this impurity phase since all three parts were from the same print cycle and were subjected to the same thermal processing. It is possible that the purity of the furnace gas atmosphere for the binder burnout or sintering  of  this  sample  could  have  contributed  to  these  differences, from a gas leak for example.
J.P. Kelly et al.
Fig. 4. Cylindrical parts were warping significantly  during  sintering  (e.g.,  left  image)  and was accompanied by a 6 % mass loss. Spatial confinement  in  an  inverted  crucible  packed with a carbon powder bed placed inside a secondary  crucible  also  packed  with  a  carbon powder bed and sintered otherwise with identical conditions as a distortion control method mitigated warping (e.g., right image). We used measurements of the overall sample height (h) relative to the thickness (t) at the edge of the
sample as a measure of distortion. The part on the left has h/t = 2.36 ± 0.15 mm and the part on the right has h/t = 1.07 ± 0.06 mm based on a series of 6 measurements and a 95 % confidence interval, thus demonstrating negligible distortion after applying the distortion control method.

3.2. Printing and sintering development
It  became  evident  from  the  initial  sintering  development  that warping is an issue if left unmitigated. Distortion can be problematic for manufacturing  larger  parts  or  parts  with  complex  geometry.  Non- uniform  structure  or  a  non-uniform  vaporization  process  are  both known to cause non-uniform shrinkage that can result in warping [43, 44].  The  fundamental  nature  of  additive  manufacturing,  building layer-by-layer, creates the potential to generate a non-uniform structure. However,  one  of  the  primary  advantages  of  binder  jet  additive manufacturing is the ability to achieve isotropic structure and properties [24]. We were also able to rule out non-uniform structure by inverting the part orientation in the furnace, but still observing warping in the same direction. We noted about 6 % mass loss occurring during the sintering  process,  which  supports  the  notion  that  warping  occurs because of a vaporization process. Spatial confinement has been used to inhibit vaporization when sintering non-oxide ceramics [45] or volatile oxide ceramics [46]. Indeed, we found that spatial confinement in a carbon packing was able to effectively mitigate warping (see Fig. 4).

A B S T R A C T
A novel approach for manufacturing complex parts of ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) is proposed, combining freeze casting with pressureless spark plasma sintering (SPS). With the aid of MoSi2 sintering additive (15 vol% or more), this combination enables the production of fully dense ZrB2-based UHTC parts at 1900 ◦ C. Producing complex-shaped samples including an intricate network of internal microchannels seems feasible, by using additive manufacturing techniques in the production of templates for the external silicone mould and the internal microchannel network to be used during the freeze casting process. Although defects are prone to occur either during freeze drying, due to thermal stresses arising from the expansion mismatch between the internal resin template and the ceramic preform, or during the resin burn-out or SPS cycles, the resulting samples exhibited a fully dense microstructure and similar hardness (17 ± 1 GPa) as the bulk material. Thus, the proposed approach shows promise in the production of arbitrarily complex-shaped UHTC parts that may find application in numerous industrial sectors including aerospace, aviation, and energy generation and storage.
Additives like Si3N4 [16], AlN [17] or SiC [18 -20] have been used to enhance ZrB2 sintering behaviour, fracture toughness, and oxidation resistance, with the latter showing the most promising results. However, conventional sintering of ZrB2-SiC composites can only achieve densities of up to 75-80 %, and pressure-assisted sintering is required to produce materials with lower residual porosity. Fortunately, MoSi2 has proved to be a successful sintering additive for enhancing sinterability of ZrB2, enabling the pressureless sintering of fully dense ZrB2-based UHTCs [21 -24]. Like SiC, MoSi2 also improves ZrB2 mechanical properties and, especially, its high-temperature oxidation resistance by forming a protective glassy borosilicate layer.

A B S T R A C T
ZrB2-SiC composite ceramics exhibit significantly improved oxidation resistance  compared  to  pure  ZrB2,  largely  due  to  the  formation  of  a borosilicate glass phase from the oxidation of the SiC phase. This glass phase effectively seals cracks and pores in the ceramic, preventing oxygen  infiltration  and  thus  mitigating  oxidation  [15 -18].The  current fabrication methods for ZrB2-SiC ceramics, such as hot pressing, reactive hot pressing, pressureless sintering, and spark plasma sintering (SPS), have  yielded  promising  results,  but  they  also  present  challenges  [5, 19 -21]. For example, Venkateswaran et al. [22] successfully fabricated ZrB2-10  wt%  SiC-10  wt%  MoSi2 composites  using  hot  pressing, demonstrating that the  addition  of  MoSi2  could  reduce  the  sintering temperature by 100~200 ◦ C.  Despite  achieving  a  relative  density  of 98.8 % at 1800 ◦ C and 30 MPa, the process still required high temperatures  and  pressures.  Similar  challenges  are  evident  in  the  work  of Zhang et al. [23], who used reactive hot pressing to achieve a relative density of 97.67 % at 1900 ◦ C and 30 MPa, and Jiang et al. [24], who employed  SPS  at  1750 ◦ C  and  50  MPa,  achieving  a  densification  of

1. Introduction
To thrive under extreme high-temperature conditions, the design of UHTC coatings prioritizes exceptional ablation resistance, high mechanical performances and interfacial adhesion, homogeneous material distribution, and a matched coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) with the substrate. For a coating to be deemed qualified, it must endeavor to fulfill these stringent design criteria to the highest possible extent. In order to enhance the performance and service life of UHTC coatings in long-term high temperature and high velocity airflow, researchers began to study UHTC coatings in terms of both structural design and material optimization. Hu et  al .  [13]  designed a SiC/UHTC/SiC  three-layer  coating  on  C/SiC  composites via CVD  and  brushingsintering. The inner SiC layer (CVD at 1050-1150 ℃) reduced thermal mismatch. The ZrB2-based UHTC middle layer (ZrB2/SiC/SiO2/B2O3/Al2O3) was sintered at 900 ℃ in Ar/air, forming borosilicate glass to seal cracks. The outer SiC layer (CVD at 12001250  ℃)  inhibited  B2O3  evaporation  and  oxidized  into  SiO2  at  high  temperatures. During oxidation at 1100-1500 ℃, ZrB2 reacted with oxygen to form ZrO2 and B2O3, while the B2O3-SiO2 glass phase exhibited fluidity to seal cracks. This synergy achieved low  mass  losses  (0.36-3.45  mg/cm²  after  68-114  h),  enabling  long-term  protection through oxygen barrier formation, self-healing, and thermal stress mitigation. Feng et al .  [14]  developed  a  spent  MoSi2-modified  ZrB2-SiC-MoSi2  composite  coating  on carbon substrates via spark plasma sintering (SPS). The coating was sintered at 1500 ℃ under 30 MPa pressure, forming a pore-free structure (~800 μm thick) with continuous and tight metallurgical bonding at  the  interface. question: Which element segregates to grain boundaries in the medium entropy carbide TiZrHfC3 according to Dai et al. 2022?
answer: According to Dai et al. 2022, the element that segregates to grain boundaries in the medium entropy carbide TiZrHfC3 is Ti [1][2].

5. Summary
In the present work, we fit a DP model for transition metal carbides, and adopted it to simulate grain boundary segregation and the effect of segregation on grain boundary strength in a series of transition metal carbides. Four transition metal carbides were selected, including conventional Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C, medium entropy TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , and high entropy TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , and five GBs, including /Sigma1 3(112), /Sigma1 5(210), /Sigma1 5(310), /Sigma1 3(221), /Sigma1 11(113), were studied for each carbide. The results are summarized as follows:
- (1) Segregation with the site-specific occupation is realized for every grain boundary, which is dominated by size effect, which means that small atoms tend to occupy sites with compression strains, while big atoms tend to occupy sites with expansion strains. On average, GBs of TiZrHfC 3 are segregated with Ti and depleted with Hf. GBs of ZrHfNbTaC 4 are segregated with Nb and depleted with Hf. GBs of TiZrHfNbTaC 5 are segregated with either Ti or Zr, which are compensated with depletion of Hf and/or Ta.
- (2) It is suggested that spontaneous grain boundary segregation is an intrinsic phenomenon in medium/high entropy materials due to the rugged potential energy profile of GBs. Therefore, indepth understanding of the composition-structure-property relations of GBs is essential for medium/high entropy materials.
- (3) Tensile simulations reveal that grain boundary strengths are usually increased or just slightly decreased due to segregation, which means that grain boundary segregation is a universal mechanism to strengthen GBs of both conventional UHTCs and medium/high entropy UHTCs. Therefore, a medium/high entropy UHTC frequently exhibits better high-temperature strength in comparison with its counterparts.

3.1. Grain boundary segregation
Table 1 lists the exceeding normalized concentration δ i ( i = Ti, Zr, Hf, Nb, Ta) in different GBs of medium entropy TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 and high entropy TiZrHfNbTaC 5 . The exceeding normalized concentration δ i is the summation of c n of sites within 5 ˚ A from the mathematical grain boundary plane. δ i = 0 means that there is no segregation of element i in the grain boundary, while δ i > 0 ( δ i < 0) means that there is segregation (depletion) of element i in the grain boundary. Therefore, evident grain boundary segregations for the medium/high entropy transition metal carbides can be found. For the medium entropy TiZrHfC 3 , all the investigated GBs are enriched with Ti, which is mainly complemented by depletion of Hf. For the medium entropy ZrHfNbTaC 4 , all the investigated GBs are enriched with Nb, which is mainly complemented by depletion of Hf. For the high entropy TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , the results are more complicated, where the /Sigma1 3(112), /Sigma1 5(210) and /Sigma1 5(310) are mainly segregated with Ti, while /Sigma1 9(221) and /Sigma1 11(113) are mainly segregated with Zr. The enrichment of Ti or Zr is mainly complemented by depletion of Hf and/or Ta. Even though Zr and Hf exhibit similar atomic size and preferred site-specific occupation, their overall segregations/depletions are still different, which means that the segregation does not solely depend on size effect. Table 1 reveals that Hf is the element that most prefers to deplete from GBs, which may be followed by Ta. It is perhaps that HfC and TaC are the most refractory carbides.

4. Discussion
Different from conventional materials, whose solute elements originate from additives, medium/high entropy materials bring their own solutes. Even though the multi-elements may form a random solid solution in grains, random distribution of the multielements in GBs is harder to achieve due to the more rugged potential energy profile of GBs, which results from bond breaking/reconstruction and distortions around GBs. Therefore, spontaneous segregation/depletion will take place in GBs of medium/high entropy materials to minimize grain boundary energies. In addition, the multi-elements in medium/high entropy materials are similar in chemical properties, the segregation/depletion of which only fine-tunes grain boundary properties instead of changing the properties abruptly. As a result, the spontaneously segregated/depleted GBs may exhibit better adhesion. Our simulations show that segregation takes place in all GBs studied here, and sitespecific occupation tendency is discovered. Moreover, the spontaneously segregated/depleted GBs are usually strengthened. Therefore, good strength retention and creep resistance at elevated temperatures are frequently achieved in medium/high entropy ceramics due to the spontaneous segregation/depletion of elements to/from GBs to reduce grain boundary energies. For example, good high-temperature strength was reported in ZrNbTaC 3 by Demirskyi et al. [22] , in TiZrHfC 3 by Wang et al. [23] , and in TiZrHfNbTaC 5 by Feng et al. [24] , while Han et al. [23] reported improved steadystate creep rates of ZrHfNbTaC 4 in comparison with corresponding monocarbides. Grain boundary segregation not only improves grain boundary adhesion, but also hinders grain boundary sliding and pins grain boundary migration. Then, fine and uniform microstructures are usually realized in medium/high entropy materials, which are also beneficial to their performance. In contrast, conventional UHTCs usually exhibit non-uniform microstructure and big voids in them, which will result in poor mechanical properties.

3.1. Microstructural analysis
EDS analyses  of  the  polished  surface  revealed  that  the  elemental distribution of the component metallic elements is not perfectly homogeneous. The carbide phase abounds in Hf and Ta while Nb, Zr and Ti segregate predominantly in the boride phase. In addition to the principal elements, W and O impurity elements are also detected, which is in agreement with the presence of a negligible amount of oxides confirmed by XRD. Tungsten was found mainly in the carbide phase rather than in boride grains which was introduced most probably during ball milling using WC balls. Oxygen was found to form an oxide phase at the grain boundaries mainly at triple junctions.
Fig. 2. : XRD pattern of the sintered dual-phase (Ta-Hf-Zr-Nb-Ti)C-(Ta-Hf-ZrNb-Ti)B2  ceramics.
Fig. 3. : Characteristic microstructure of the investigated dual-phase high-entropy carbide-boride system: a, b) SEM images, c) grain size distribution of the boride and carbide grains.
Fig. 4. : SEM image of high-entropy carbide and boride grains with corresponding element distribution maps measured by EDS, showing the Ta, Hf, Zr, Nb, Ti, C, B principal elements and W and O impurities.

a b s t r a c t
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) exhibit a unique combination of excellent properties, including ultra-high melting point, excellent chemical stability, and good oxidation resistance, which make them promising candidates for aerospace and nuclear applications. However, the degradation of hightemperature strength is one of the main limitations for their ultra-high temperature applications. Thus, searching for mechanisms that can help to develop high-performance UHTCs with good high-temperature mechanical properties is urgently needed. To achieve this goal, grain boundary segregation of a series of carbides, including conventional, medium entropy, and high entropy transition metal carbides, i.e., Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C, TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , were studied by atomistic simulations with a fitted Deep Potential (DP), and the effects of segregation on grain boundary strength were emphasized. For all the studied carbides, grain boundary segregations are realized, which are dominated by the atomic size effect. In addition, tensile simulations indicate that grain boundaries (GBs) will usually be strengthened due to segregation. Our simulation results reveal that grain boundary segregation may be a universal mechanism in enhancing the high-temperature strength of both conventional UHTCs and medium/high entropy UHTCs, since GBs play a key role in controlling the fracture of UHTCs at elevated temperatures.

question: What cutoff distance was used in the deep potential model for multi-element transition metal carbides in Dai et al. 2022?
answer: The cutoff distance used in the deep potential model for multi-element transition metal carbides in Dai et al. 2022 was 6.5 ˚A [1].

chunks.text:
2.1. Construction of GBs
The machine learning potential proposed by Zhang et al. [ 32 , 33 ] is adopted to train an interatomic potential for multi-element transition metal carbides from a dataset generated by DFT calculations. The model is called DP, which maps the local atomic configurations to atomic energies by a deep neural network [ 32 , 33 ], and has been proven to be applicable to high entropy materials [ 34 , 35 ]. The DP model is comprised of two sets of neural networks. The first one is a descriptor net, which automatically encodes local atomic configurations to symmetry-preserving descriptors. The second one is a fitting net, which maps the symmetry-preserving descriptors to atomic energies. In this work, the architecture of DP was set as follows: (1) the descriptor was a smooth edition descriptor from reference [32] , which consisted of 3 layers of residual net with each layer having 40, 80, and 160 nodes; (2) the cutoff was set to be 6.5 ˚ A with a smooth function imposed from 6.0 ˚ A; (3) the projection dimension of the descriptor was set to be 16; (4) the 'type-one' technology was adopted to reduce the scale of the descriptor net; (5) the fitting net included 4 layers with each layer having 160, 160, 160, and 160 nodes. For details of the DP parameters, please refer to the original papers and the open-source software [ 32 , 33 ].

2.1. Construction of GBs
Transition metal carbides with four different com positions, including Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C, TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , were selected to represent a change from conventional transition metal carbide to medium entropy transition metal carbide, and finally to high entropy transition metal carbide. For each carbide, we constructed five GBs under the guidance of coincident site lattice (CSL) theory [28] . Fig. 1 shows an illustrative CSL in two-dimension. The two square crystals are rotated with respect to each other, and a portion of their lattice points are coincident with each other, which are called coincident sites. The lattice formed by these coincident sites is a CSL. A CSL is usually characterized by the /Sigma1 value, which is the volume ratio between its primitive cell and that of the original crystal. For example, in Fig. 1 the /Sigma1 value is 5. Since transition metal carbides are in rock-salt structure with face-centered cubic lattice, the typical CSLs of a cubic lattice can be found in a textbook [28] . The constructed GBs are /Sigma1 3(112), /Sigma1 5(210), /Sigma1 5(310), /Sigma1 3(221), /Sigma1 11(113), where the plane index of a grain boundary follows the /Sigma1 value, e.g. (210) plane in /Sigma1 5(210). Fig. 1 can also be viewed as a planar section of the three-dimension /Sigma1 5 CSL, and the middle line illustrates the trace of the /Sigma1 5(210) grain boundary.
2.2. Training a deep potential (DP) for multi-element transition metal carbides
