in such systems.
To date, two thermodynamic models have been proposed to describe metal distribution between the carbide and boride in dual-phase highentropy UHTCs. Guo et al. [5] synthesized high-entropy diborides and high-entropy  diboride-carbides  using  ultra-fast  high-temperature  sintering (UHS) and suggested that the difference in formation energies of the transition metal diborides and carbides ( E MB2 f GLYPH<0> E MC f ) follows the order of Cr < V < Hf < Mo < Ti < Zr < Nb < Ta. This energy difference influenced  the  selective  incorporation  of  transition  metals  into  the high-entropy boride or the high-entropy carbide phases. The authors proposed that larger differences in formation energies facilitated metal dissolution  into  the  carbide  phase.  However,  they  noticed  that  this model did not accurately predict Hf segregation. Qin et al. [1] used the same approach and obtained only a moderate correlation, with an R 2 value of 0.816. Subsequently, Smith et al. [13] proposed a model based on  the  standard  Gibbs  Free  energies  of  formation  to  explain  metal segregation. Their findings indicated that the tendency for segregation to the boride phase follows the order: Cr < Ti < Zr < Nb < Mo < V < Hf < Ta < W, which was different from Qin ' s model. Notably, none of the existing models account for the possibility of the formation of a third phase in addition to the desired boride and carbide phases.

2. Theoretical methods
For  Ta-C  binary  carbides,  the  total  energy,  elastic  properties  and electronic structure were calculated by using the first-principles calculations, as implemented in CASTEP code [29]. The electronic exchange function of these Ta-C carbides was treated by the generalized-gradient-approximation  (GGA)  with  PBE  approach  [30]. The interactions between ions and elections were treated by the ultrasoft pseudopotentials [31]. The cutoff energy of these Ta-C carbides was 400 eV. The k -points were set as the 5 × 11 × 16 for the orthorhombic ( Pnma ) Ta2C, 11 × 11 × 11 for the cubic ( Pm-3m ) Ta4C3, 11 × 11 × 4 for the rhombohedral ( P3112 ) Ta6C5, 8 × 8 × 8 for the cubic ( P4332 ) Ta8C7 and 11 × 11 × 11 for the cubic ( Fm-3m )  TaC, respectively. The SCF convergence of these Ta-C carbides was less than 1.0 × 10 GLYPH<0> 6 eV/mol. During the structural optimization, the lattice parameters and atomic position of all Ta-C carbides were fully relaxed [32 -35]. In addition, to check  the  dynamical  stability,  the  phonon  dispersion  of  these  Ta-C carbides was calculated by the phonon model [36 -38].

a b s t r a c t
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) exhibit a unique combination of excellent properties, including ultra-high melting point, excellent chemical stability, and good oxidation resistance, which make them promising candidates for aerospace and nuclear applications. However, the degradation of hightemperature strength is one of the main limitations for their ultra-high temperature applications. Thus, searching for mechanisms that can help to develop high-performance UHTCs with good high-temperature mechanical properties is urgently needed. To achieve this goal, grain boundary segregation of a series of carbides, including conventional, medium entropy, and high entropy transition metal carbides, i.e., Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C, TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , were studied by atomistic simulations with a fitted Deep Potential (DP), and the effects of segregation on grain boundary strength were emphasized. For all the studied carbides, grain boundary segregations are realized, which are dominated by the atomic size effect. In addition, tensile simulations indicate that grain boundaries (GBs) will usually be strengthened due to segregation. Our simulation results reveal that grain boundary segregation may be a universal mechanism in enhancing the high-temperature strength of both conventional UHTCs and medium/high entropy UHTCs, since GBs play a key role in controlling the fracture of UHTCs at elevated temperatures.

question: How frequently were metal atom pairs swapped during Monte Carlo simulations in Dai et al. 2022 segregation study?
answer: Metal atom pairs were swapped once per 2 steps during Monte Carlo simulations in Dai et al. 2022 segregation study [1].

chunks.text:
2.3. Simulating segregation and strength of GBs
Grain boundary segregation was simulated by a hybrid method combining Monte Carlo (MC) and MD (MC/MD). The simulations were performed by using the LAMMPS package [40] under NPT ensemble with T = 2,0 0 0 K and P = 0 Pa. The time step was set to be 1 fs, and the damping parameters for temperature and pressure were 200 fs and 500 fs, respectively. Each system was first equilibrated for 100 ps before swapping atoms by MC. During MC, a pair of metal atoms were swapped once per 2 steps, and the acceptance probability of the new configuration is min(1, exp(/Delta1 E / k B T )). /Delta1 E is the potential energy change due to swapping. k B is the Boltzmann constant. The total steps of MC simulation were 50 0,0 0 0.
Grain boundary strength was simulated by virtual tensile experiments by MD. The tensile deformation normal to the grain bound- ary plane was applied at a strain rate of 2 × 10 9 s -1 . The simulations were carried out under NPT ensemble with T = 2,0 0 0 K and other stress components being 0 Pa except for the one normal to the grain boundary plane. The time step and damping parameters were the same as those in segregation simulations.

3.1. Grain boundary segregation
The segregation tendency can be well illustrated by element distribution profiles, which were generated by averaging the MC/MD simulated configurations from step 250,0 0 0 to 50 0,0 0 0 with sampling frequency being every 100 steps. Take the /Sigma1 3(112) grain boundary of TiZrHfNbTaC 5 as an example, Fig. 3 shows the element distribution profile of Ti, Zr, Hf, Nb, Ta, where the profile is illustrated by a normalized concentration c n = ( c r -c i )/ c i . c r is the real concentration of a given element at a given site, while c i is the ideal concentration of the element at the site. c i is 0.2 for the randomly occupied state of TiZrHfNbTaC 5 . It can be seen that the concentrations in grains are evenly distributed with small fluctuations, where the c n is approximately a Gaussian distribution (see Ta in Fig. 3 for example). In contrast, the concentrations in GBs deviate evidently from the ideal concentrations, especially for Ti, Zr, and Hf, which present site-specific characteristics. For example, Ti tends to occupy site 2 and hates to occupy site 3. In contrast, Zr and Hf tend to occupy site 3 and hate to occupy site 2. The deviations from the ideal concentration of Nb and Ta are not so significant as those for Ti, Zr, and Hf. Similar to conventional UHTCs [ 8 , 10 , 12 ], the selective occupation tendencies mainly result from size effect. It means that a grain boundary site with com- pressed volume strain, e.g. site 2, tends to be occupied by small atoms, while a grain boundary site with expansion volume strain, e.g. site 3, tends to be occupied by large atoms. Atom size in transition metal carbides can be assumed to follow the relation: Ti < Nb < Ta < Zr ≈ Hf, according to the lattice parameter of each carbide predicted by the DP model (see SI).

3.2. Metal distribution
[34 -36]. Specifically, VC0.8  has been identified as the most thermodynamically stable vanadium carbide composition [37]. The virtual crystal approximation was used to model the carbon vacancies and the calculations  qualitatively  demonstrated  that  vanadium  strongly  favored segregation to the boride phase when VC was carbon deficient (i.e., VCx where x is less than 1), which might be the case for the carbide formed in the HEDP-V. If the VC were fully stoichiometric (i.e., x = 1), vanadium segregation  to  the  carbide  may  have  been  favored.  Results  from  the present  study  reveal  that  first-principles  simulations  can  predict  the metal distributions based solely on thermodynamics, but complex interactions that also dictate phase distribution of the metals need further investigation.
The heat map analysis of the HEDP-Nb and -Ta compositions also exhibited  variation  in  intensity,  but  the  segregation  order  remained consistent across both compositions: Ti < Zr < Nb < Hf and Ti < Zr < Hf < Ta from boride to carbide. As an example, for HEDP-Ta, the boride/ carbide  ratios  were  Ti < Zr < Hf < Ta,  experimentally,  these  values were 2.35 > 0.94 > 0.67 > 0.61, while theoretically, they were 8.25 > 2.84 > 0.44 > 0.07. This discrepancy arises because thermodynamic calculations did not predict the absolute concentration of each metal  in  each  phase  [13],  as  it  is  dependent  on  their  ratio  between boride and carbide phases but rather indicated the relative favorability of metal segregation between phases.

5. Summary
In the present work, we fit a DP model for transition metal carbides, and adopted it to simulate grain boundary segregation and the effect of segregation on grain boundary strength in a series of transition metal carbides. Four transition metal carbides were selected, including conventional Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C, medium entropy TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , and high entropy TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , and five GBs, including /Sigma1 3(112), /Sigma1 5(210), /Sigma1 5(310), /Sigma1 3(221), /Sigma1 11(113), were studied for each carbide. The results are summarized as follows:
- (1) Segregation with the site-specific occupation is realized for every grain boundary, which is dominated by size effect, which means that small atoms tend to occupy sites with compression strains, while big atoms tend to occupy sites with expansion strains. On average, GBs of TiZrHfC 3 are segregated with Ti and depleted with Hf. GBs of ZrHfNbTaC 4 are segregated with Nb and depleted with Hf. GBs of TiZrHfNbTaC 5 are segregated with either Ti or Zr, which are compensated with depletion of Hf and/or Ta.
- (2) It is suggested that spontaneous grain boundary segregation is an intrinsic phenomenon in medium/high entropy materials due to the rugged potential energy profile of GBs. Therefore, indepth understanding of the composition-structure-property relations of GBs is essential for medium/high entropy materials.
- (3) Tensile simulations reveal that grain boundary strengths are usually increased or just slightly decreased due to segregation, which means that grain boundary segregation is a universal mechanism to strengthen GBs of both conventional UHTCs and medium/high entropy UHTCs. Therefore, a medium/high entropy UHTC frequently exhibits better high-temperature strength in comparison with its counterparts.

2. Computational details
First-principles simulations were performed by using CASTEP [18] with exchange-correlation described by generalized gradient approximation [19]. In the simulations, Vanderbilt-type ultrasoft pseudopotential [20] was employed with cutoff energy of the plane wave being 400eV. k -points mesh with separation of 0.04 Å /C0 1 was sampled according to Monkhorst-Pack method [21] in the Brillouin zone. All of the supercells containing grain boundaries were fully optimized with respect to both lattice parameters and atom positions by using Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) minimization scheme [22]. The tolerances for geometry optimization were set as follows: difference in total energy within 5 /C2 10 /C0 6 eV/atom, maximum ionic Hellmann-Feynman force within 0.01 eV/Å, maximum ionic displacement within 5 /C2 10 /C0 4 Å and maximum stress within 0.02 GPa.
To assess the effects of solute atom segregation on grain boundary strengths, tensile stress-strain relations of grain boundaries were simulated. During the simulation, the supercell was incrementally deformed in the imposed strain direction. At each step, the supercell was fully relaxed by employing BFGS minimization scheme until all other stress components vanished. Then, the relaxed supercell was taken as the starting structure for the next step to ensure the stress-strain curves being continuous.

question: How many grain boundaries did Dai et al. 2022 construct for each carbide composition in their study?
answer: Dai et al. 2022 constructed five grain boundaries for each carbide composition in their study [1].

5. Summary
In the present work, we fit a DP model for transition metal carbides, and adopted it to simulate grain boundary segregation and the effect of segregation on grain boundary strength in a series of transition metal carbides. Four transition metal carbides were selected, including conventional Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C, medium entropy TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , and high entropy TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , and five GBs, including /Sigma1 3(112), /Sigma1 5(210), /Sigma1 5(310), /Sigma1 3(221), /Sigma1 11(113), were studied for each carbide. The results are summarized as follows:
- (1) Segregation with the site-specific occupation is realized for every grain boundary, which is dominated by size effect, which means that small atoms tend to occupy sites with compression strains, while big atoms tend to occupy sites with expansion strains. On average, GBs of TiZrHfC 3 are segregated with Ti and depleted with Hf. GBs of ZrHfNbTaC 4 are segregated with Nb and depleted with Hf. GBs of TiZrHfNbTaC 5 are segregated with either Ti or Zr, which are compensated with depletion of Hf and/or Ta.
- (2) It is suggested that spontaneous grain boundary segregation is an intrinsic phenomenon in medium/high entropy materials due to the rugged potential energy profile of GBs. Therefore, indepth understanding of the composition-structure-property relations of GBs is essential for medium/high entropy materials.
- (3) Tensile simulations reveal that grain boundary strengths are usually increased or just slightly decreased due to segregation, which means that grain boundary segregation is a universal mechanism to strengthen GBs of both conventional UHTCs and medium/high entropy UHTCs. Therefore, a medium/high entropy UHTC frequently exhibits better high-temperature strength in comparison with its counterparts.

3.1. Microstructural analysis
Fig. 2 shows the recorded XRD pattern of the (Ta-Hf-Zr-Nb-Ti)C-(TaHf-Zr-Nb-Ti)B2 composite  ceramics  with  indexed  peaks.  The  XRD pattern confirms that a dual-phase high-entropy system was synthesized with a negligible amount of oxides (HfO2/ZrO2 < 1 wt%) where the carbide and boride phases are denoted by HEC and HEB in the present work. The lattice constants are a = 4.5246(7) Å for the cubic carbide (Fm-3 m) and a = 3.0974(6) Å, c = 3.3643(8) Å for the hexagonal boride (P6/mmm) phases. Microstructure characteristics, grain size distribution and chemical composition of the investigated dual-phase ceramic sample are illustrated in Figs. 3 and 4. According to the results, both the carbide (light grey in SEM) and boride grains (dark grey in SEM) are relatively homogenously distributed with a small amount of porosity, resulting in a relative density of 99%. The grain size varies between 1 µ m to 8 µ m with a slightly lower average size for carbides (~2 µ m) than for borides  (~3 µ m),  forming  larger  clusters  of  grains.  Very  small  pores were  detected  at  the  grain  boundaries  with  a  size  of  approximately 100 nm. The grain sizes in the developed system are similar to the grain sizes for the system published by Qin et al. [21] who reported a definite correlation between the grain sizes of the carbide and boride phases with the phase fraction and the lowest grain sizes approximately 7 µ m with similar mol% of carbide/boride in the systems. Luo et al. [22] reported significantly lower average grain sizes of 0.64 ± 0.2 µ m for the boride and 1.5 ± 0.4 µ m for the carbide in their dual-phase ceramic, processed using different processing routes.

3.2. Nanoindentation and microcantilever bending
modulus of the carbide phase is also consistent with results reported for (Hf-Ta-Zr-Nb-Ti)C [14] while the Young ' s modulus of the boundaries is roughly the average of the values measured in the carbide and boride phases.

Characteristic geometries and compositions of the FIB-milled microcantilevers before testing are illustrated in Fig. 5. The randomly selected locations of the cantilevers assume different characteristics of the  fracture  behaviour  during  bending  tests  that  make  it  possible  to study the strength of the grains and grain boundaries in the present dualphase high-entropy ceramic system.

3.2. Nanoindentation and microcantilever bending
Nanoindentation  was  carried  out  to  assess  the  basic  mechanical behaviour of the constituents of the present dual-phase high-entropy ceramics. Indents that were located either entirely in grains or located roughly  at  the  boundaries  (i.e.  crossing  the  projected  surface  of  the imprints) but far from any defect were sorted into three main groups, such as carbide grains, boride grains and grain boundaries. The rest of the indents were excluded from the analysis. In this work, the hardness and Young ' s  modulus values associated with grain/phase boundaries mean the local elastic and plastic response of the sample at the boundaries (i.e. within a distance of a half imprint diameter) during indentation, respectively. Naturally, this response derives from the combined effect of the boundaries and adjacent grains/phases since the diameter of  imprints  is  ~1600 nm  while  the  boundaries  are  only  a  couple  of nanometres thin [37]. Therefore, the boundaries cannot be measured directly by nanoindentation only their influence, which is denoted here as 'grain boundary hardness and modulus ' . The measured hardness-displacement  and  modulus-displacement  curves  were  averaged in the depth range of 100 -300 nm to be consistent with our earlier reports on high-entropy ceramics [7,14,36] and the calculated values were averaged for each group. The average nanohardness and Young ' s modulus of the constituents are summarized in Table 1. The table shows that high-entropy boride grains are harder than carbide ones which is in agreement with literature of the constituent carbides and borides. Taking into consideration the random crystallographic orientation of the grains (about 10 per phase), hardness measurement suggests that bonds in the boride phase are stronger than those in the carbide phase, inferring different strength values. The grain boundaries were found to be the weakest with similar values that were reported for the boundaries in a monolithic high-entropy (Hf-Ta-Zr-Nb-Ti)C in our recent work [14]. The Young ' s

Fig. 10).
Phase and grain boundaries together with average grain size play a very important role in plastic deformation and failure of metals and alloys [38], but also in advanced engineering ceramics. In some cases, the grain-boundary geometry, structure, and chemical composition is even more important than the apparent grain size. Advances in the capabilities  of  analytical  microscopy  techniques  are  able  to  provide essential  atomic-scale  structural  and  chemical  information  for  the necessary understanding needed to effectively design highly progressive advanced  ceramic  materials  with  the  required  mechanical  and  functional properties. Fracture processes leading to failures are also often connected to interfaces, their atomistic structure and chemistry, therefore a detailed understanding of the structural and chemical ordering at the grain interface is essential. The dual-phase high-entropy ceramics, where the grain boundary characteristics are very important are processed in three main routes in the literature [21 -25], using spark plasma sintering  as  in  the  present  study,  boro-carbo  thermal  reduction  and spark plasma sintering and reaction sintering.
The phase and grain boundary characteristics of newly developed fine-grained dual-phase high-entropy (Ti0.14Zr0.2Nb0.2Hf0.2Ta0.26)C/ (Ti0.38Zr0.18Nb0.22Hf0.115Ta0.105)B2 ceramic was investigated from micron to sub-nanometer level [37]. According to the results, the phase and grain boundaries are typically clean and sharp with indistinct 1 -1.5 nm  thin  gradient  of  HE  metallic  elements  at  boride/boride  and carbide/carbide interfaces. At the boride/boride interface, the gradient means lower Ti and slightly lower Hf with higher Ta and slightly higher Nb, and at the carbide/carbide interface on the other hand, lower Hf with slightly higher Ti. The sharp phase/grain boundaries do exhibit elemental enrichment from a trace amount of iron being incorporated in interstitial  positions  of  carbide  and  boride  grains  locally  at  boride/carbide boundaries or are present in boride and carbide grains [37].

question: What were the energy and force prediction errors of the deep potential model trained by Dai et al. 2022 compared to DFT calculations?
answer: The energy and force prediction errors of the deep potential model trained by Dai et al. 2022 compared to DFT calculations were 15 meV/atom for energy and 400 meV/Å for force [1].

2.1. Construction of GBs
A dataset generated by DFT-based calculations was used to train and test the DP model. Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package (CASTEP) [36] was adopted to perform DFT-based calculations. Ultrasoft pseudopotential [37] and Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof generalized gradient approximation (PBE-GGA) [38] were applied in the calculations. The cutoff energy of the plane-wave basis set was set to be 500 eV. The k -points mesh according to the Monkhorst-Pack method [39] with a separation of 0.03 ˚ A -1 was adopted in the Brillouin zone. Supercells for non-defective crystals, crystals with vacancies, melts, surfaces, and GBs were constructed. For each supercell, 10 steps of MD simulation under NVT ensemble with time step being 5 fs and temperature randomly chosen from 0 K to 5,0 0 0 K were implemented to generate the DFT dataset. One-tenth of the data were left as a test set, while the others were used to train the DP model. Fig. 2 compares the energies and forces between the DP predictions and the DFT calculations for the test set. Statistics on the prediction errors reveal that the prediction errors in energy and force are 15 meV/atom and 400 meV/ ˚ A, respectively, which guarantees reliable simulations in this work. In addition, the supplemental information (SI) provides more comparisons between the DP model and the DFT-based calculations, which reveal good reliability of the DP model.

4.3. Equilibrium compositions of the HEB and HEC phases

II. Calculation Methods
First-principles calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) were performed using the Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package (CASTEP) code 19 in order to elucidate the electronic structure, chemical bonding and mechanical properties of NiB. The Vanderbilt-type ultrasoft pseudopotential 20 and generalized gradient approximation based on the Wu-Cohen (WC) scheme 21 for the exchange-correlation functional were used for DFT calculations. Upon calculation, 'spin-polarized' function was checked so that the calculation was performed using different wave functions for different spins. The plane-wave basis set cutoff was 500 eV. The special points sampling integration over the Brillourin zone was employed by using the Monkhorst Pack method with 9 9 3 9 8 special k-points mesh. 22 To minimize the enthalpy and interatomic forces, lattice parameters were modified. In geometry optimization, the Broyden -Fletcher -Goldfarb - Shanno minimization scheme 23 was used. The tolerances for geometry optimization are difference on total energy within 5 9 10 /C0 6 eV/atom, maximum ionic Hellmann -Feynman force within 0.01 eV/ /C23 A, and maximum ionic displacement within 5 9 10 /C0 4 /C23 A and maximum stress within 0.02 GPa. The calculations of projected density of states (PDOS) were performed using a projection of the plane-wave electronic states onto a localized linear combination of atomic orbitals basis set.

2.2. Computational methods
The first-principles calculations were performed within the framework of density functional theory (DFT) using the Cambridge Sequential Total  Energy  Package  (CASTEP)  code  [23].  The  interaction  between valence electrons and ion nuclei was denoted by ultrasoft pseudo-potential [24]. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof represented exchange-correlation energy (PBE) scheme within generalized gradient approximation (GGA) [25]. To ensure convergence of the total energy in the ground state, the plane-wave cutoff energy was set to 440 eV. The integration  in  the  Brillouin  zone  (BZ)  was  done  on  a  set  of  special k-points  determined  according  to  the  Monkhorst-Pack  scheme,  the k-points grid of NiB, Ni2B, Ni3B and Ni4B3  are 5 × 2 × 5, 3 × 3 × 3, 3 × 2 × 3 and 1 × 5 × 2 respectively. Geometry optimization was carried out using the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) method, which minimizes the energy and enhances stability by optimizing the system parameters  [26].  During  the  optimization  process,  the  total  energy convergence threshold was set to 5.0 × 10 ⁻⁶ eV/atom, the force on each atom was kept below 0.01 eV/Å, the maximum atomic displacement was within 5.0 × 10 ⁻⁴ Å, and the maximum stress was below 0.02 GPa. After geometry optimization, the elastic constants (Cij) of the Ni-B material were determined based on the stress-strain relationship and fitted linearly using Hooke ' s law [27]. The results are shown in Table S1.

2.3. DFT calculations
First-principles  calculations  were  performed  with  the  Vienna  Abinitio Simulation Package ( VASP ) code based on the density functional theory (DFT). The exchange-correlation effects were processed by the generalized  gradient  approximation  (GGA)  with  the  Perdew-BurkeErnzerhof (PBE) function [22 -24]. Interactions between ionic real and valence electrons were determined by applying the Projector-Augmented Wave (PAW) pseudopotential. The valence configuration are 5 d 2 6 s 2 ,  5 d 3 6 s 2 and 2 s 2 2 p 2 for Hf, Ta and C, respectively. After the convergence test, the plane wave cut-off energy was set to 600 eV, and the matching Monkhorst GLYPH<0> Pack k-points grid sampling was set to be 15 × 15 × 15 for the Brillouin zone [25]. After the full optimization of the cell, the total energies were converged well to 5 . 0 × 10 GLYPH<0> 6  eV/atom, the maximum ionic Hellmann-Feynman forces convergence value was no more than 0.01 eV/Å, and the maximum of the stress tensor was less than 0.02 GPa.

KEYWORDS
By using first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations we have evaluated the cleavage energies and the surface formation energies of M B2 compounds along the { 0001 } , { 10 /C22 10 } , { 10 /C22 11 } , { 11 /C22 20 } , { 11 /C22 22 } , and { 11 /C22 23 } planes. Here we rank the relative stability of (0001) M , (10 /C22 10) M , (11 /C22 23) M , (10 /C22 11)B( M ) , and (0001)B surfaces, providing a basic model for the construction of the interface structures of UHTCMCs.

4.  Potential pathways to address challenges involved with HEUHTCs
A variety of HE-UHTCs has been synthesized and explored in experiments without the guidance of ab-initio computing to be ensured whether the constituents would result in a single-phase or not. In this regard,  theoretical  simulations  such  as  thermodynamic  predictions utilizing ab-initio, CALPHAD, and density functional theory (DFT), can be proved to be a valuable toolset to this conundrum of HE-UHTCs. High throughput first-principles calculations combined with machine learning (ML) approach may be utilized to provide a quantitative database  for  the  formation  ability  of  thermodynamically  stable  HEUHTCs. It will enable us to narrow down the compositional spectrum with  a  focus  on  fabricating  them,  followed  by  the  properties  assessment.  Ab-initio  techniques  [35,42,53,85]  have  been  utilized  for  determining the effective role of entropy to identify composition with a high or low propensity to form a single-phase. Sarker et al. [35] proposed  an  entropy  forming  ability  descriptor  to  predict  the  entropy forming ability (EFA) of five component metal carbide solid-solution based on the high throughput first principle calculations. The energy distributions of each set of compositions are calculated using DFT. EFA is  inversely  proportional  to  the  width  of  energy  distribution,  so  its higher value corresponds to the composition that easily forms a singlephase.  In  another  study  [42],  a  descriptor  predicted  the  formation possibility of 56 types of five component UHTC carbides, but only 9 such types were successfully synthesized and explored. Hence, such an approach  would  narrow  down  compositional  space  pinpointing  the right  combination  of  compositions,  fast-tracking  the  research  rather than relatively expensive trial and error method. Establishing accurate formation criterion is essential for accelerating the discovery and design of HE-UHTCs with desirable properties.

II. Calculation Methods
First-principles calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) were performed using the Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package (CASTEP) code 19 in order to elucidate the electronic structure, chemical bonding and mechanical properties of NiB. The Vanderbilt-type ultrasoft pseudopotential 20 and generalized gradient approximation based on the Wu-Cohen (WC) scheme 21 for the exchange-correlation functional were used for DFT calculations. Upon calculation, 'spin-polarized' function was checked so that the calculation was performed using different wave functions for different spins. The plane-wave basis set cutoff was 500 eV. The special points sampling integration over the Brillourin zone was employed by using the Monkhorst Pack method with 9 9 3 9 8 special k-points mesh. 22 To minimize the enthalpy and interatomic forces, lattice parameters were modified. In geometry optimization, the Broyden -Fletcher -Goldfarb - Shanno minimization scheme 23 was used. The tolerances for geometry optimization are difference on total energy within 5 9 10 /C0 6 eV/atom, maximum ionic Hellmann -Feynman force within 0.01 eV/ /C23 A, and maximum ionic displacement within 5 9 10 /C0 4 /C23 A and maximum stress within 0.02 GPa. The calculations of projected density of states (PDOS) were performed using a projection of the plane-wave electronic states onto a localized linear combination of atomic orbitals basis set.

A B S T R A C T
Trial  and  error  experimental  method,  based  on  a  strong  dose  of chemical  intuition,  is  the  most  common  way  to  study  the  oxidation behavior of UHTCs in the past decades. However, it shows congenital inefficiency  to  discover  new  materials,  especially  applied  for  those multicomponent materials due to the prohibitively large search space.
Y. Yan et al.
Current computational methods, such as density functional theory (DFT) calculation, CALculation of PHAse Diagrams (CALPHAD) approach, and parametric criteria, have been applied for the research and development of materials aiming  for targeted microstructures and  properties. Regarding the application of DFT calculation in the oxidation of UHTCs, it is only effective for the simple materials system due to the expensive computation cost and high uncertainties [39-41]. In addition, CALPHAD is not able to simulate the oxidation process for such complex materials systems thus far [42,43]. These conventional computational methods are accordingly hard to be applied in modeling the oxidation behavior of multicomponent ceramic composites due to the very complex oxidation process.
As  a  powerful  data-driven  technique,  machine  learning  (ML)  becomes  one  of  the  most  exciting  modeling  tools  in  materials  science community  very  recently  [44-59].  ML  methods  have  already  been proved  to  be  capable  of  improving  material  development  efficiency. Although ML methods have successfully been applied in multicomponent UHTCs, like phase prediction [60 -62] mechanical properties [63, 64], etc, only few studies attempted to apply ML in predicting oxidation recession of simple ceramic systems [65,66]. While the application of ML method in oxidation behavior of multicomponent TMB2-SiC ceramic composites remains un-researched.

brittle transition behavior of Hf-Ta-C solid solution was explained by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The synergistic effect between the second-phase HfO2  particles and the heterogeneous matrix significantly enriches the crack deflection and bridging sites, resulting in a highly efficient enhancement of toughness. The results provide a new perspective for the toughening design of multicomponent UHTCs.

question: What plane-wave basis set cutoff energy was used in the DFT calculations for Dai et al. 2022?
answer: The plane-wave basis set cutoff energy used in the DFT calculations for Dai et al. 2022 was 500 eV [1].

2.1. Construction of GBs
A dataset generated by DFT-based calculations was used to train and test the DP model. Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package (CASTEP) [36] was adopted to perform DFT-based calculations. Ultrasoft pseudopotential [37] and Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof generalized gradient approximation (PBE-GGA) [38] were applied in the calculations. The cutoff energy of the plane-wave basis set was set to be 500 eV. The k -points mesh according to the Monkhorst-Pack method [39] with a separation of 0.03 ˚ A -1 was adopted in the Brillouin zone. Supercells for non-defective crystals, crystals with vacancies, melts, surfaces, and GBs were constructed. For each supercell, 10 steps of MD simulation under NVT ensemble with time step being 5 fs and temperature randomly chosen from 0 K to 5,0 0 0 K were implemented to generate the DFT dataset. One-tenth of the data were left as a test set, while the others were used to train the DP model. Fig. 2 compares the energies and forces between the DP predictions and the DFT calculations for the test set. Statistics on the prediction errors reveal that the prediction errors in energy and force are 15 meV/atom and 400 meV/ ˚ A, respectively, which guarantees reliable simulations in this work. In addition, the supplemental information (SI) provides more comparisons between the DP model and the DFT-based calculations, which reveal good reliability of the DP model.
Fig. 2. Comparison of energies and forces predicted by the DP model with the DFT-based calculations, and probability density distributions of prediction errors on energy and force.

II. Calculation Methods
First-principles calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) were performed using the Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package (CASTEP) code 19 in order to elucidate the electronic structure, chemical bonding and mechanical properties of NiB. The Vanderbilt-type ultrasoft pseudopotential 20 and generalized gradient approximation based on the Wu-Cohen (WC) scheme 21 for the exchange-correlation functional were used for DFT calculations. Upon calculation, 'spin-polarized' function was checked so that the calculation was performed using different wave functions for different spins. The plane-wave basis set cutoff was 500 eV. The special points sampling integration over the Brillourin zone was employed by using the Monkhorst Pack method with 9 9 3 9 8 special k-points mesh. 22 To minimize the enthalpy and interatomic forces, lattice parameters were modified. In geometry optimization, the Broyden -Fletcher -Goldfarb - Shanno minimization scheme 23 was used. The tolerances for geometry optimization are difference on total energy within 5 9 10 /C0 6 eV/atom, maximum ionic Hellmann -Feynman force within 0.01 eV/ /C23 A, and maximum ionic displacement within 5 9 10 /C0 4 /C23 A and maximum stress within 0.02 GPa. The calculations of projected density of states (PDOS) were performed using a projection of the plane-wave electronic states onto a localized linear combination of atomic orbitals basis set.

2. Calculation methods
First-principles calculations were performed based on DFT using the Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package (CASTEP) code [16]. The Vanderbilt-type ultrasoft pseudopotentials [17] were employed to represent the interactions between the ionic cores and the valence electrons. The exchange-correlation energy was treated under generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) scheme [18]. The cut-o /uniFB00 energy of plane-wave basis set was /uniFB01 xed at 450 eV for all calculations. The special point sampling integration over the Brillouin zone was employed by using the Monkhorst-Pack method [19], and the k -points mesh were set as 7 × 2 × 7. Lattice parameters of Y2B3C2 were optimized by using the Broyden-Fletcher-GoldfarbShanno (BFGS) minimization scheme [20]. The convergence criteria for geometry optimization are di /uniFB00 erence on total energy within 5 × 10 -6 eV/atom, maximum ionic Hellmann-Feynman forces within 0.01 eV/Å, maximum stress within 0.02 GPa and maximum ionic displacements within 5 × 10 -4 Å.

2 | CALCULATION METHODS
First-principles calculations are performed with the VASP code, 9 which implements DFT within the framework of the plane-wave basis projector augmented wave (PAW) method. 10 The semicore p states of Ti, Zr, and Hf are treated as valence electrons in the pseudopotentials. The plane-wave cutoff is set to 500 eV. The exchange-correlation energy is described by the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the parametrization of Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE). 11 Furthermore, the contribution to the bonding due to the van der Waals (vdW) interactions is accounted for by using the semiempirical Grimme corrections PBE + D2 12 and PBE + D3, 13 and the Tkatchenko-Scheffler method with iterative Hirshfeld partitioning (PBE + TSH). 14
In addition, the all-electron full-potential electronic structure code FHI-aims 15 is employed to verify our numerical accuracy. All FHI-aims calculations are carried out by using the tier-2 tight basis set and the GGA-PBE functional. For both VASP and FHI-aims calculations, a k -point mesh of 18 9 18 9 16 is used for the unit cell of bulk M B2. Those settings converge the total energy within 1 meV/atom. The Brillouin zone of the (0001), (10 /C22 10), (11 /C22 20), (11 /C22 23), (11 /C22 22), and (10 /C22 11) surface supercells (see the structural details in the following section) are sampled with a k -point grids of 16 9 16 9 1, 12 9 7 9 1, 11 9 14 9 1, 18 9 22 9 1, 14 9 24 9 1, and 14 9 22 9 1, respectively.

2.3 Calculation models
All calculations were performed using DFT-based molecular dynamics (MD) simulations via the Quickstep module of the CP2K package (version 2025) and the Multiwfn program [46-48].  The  hybrid  Gaussian  and  Plane  Wave  (GPW)  scheme  was  employed  for  these simulations. All  structural  models  shown  in  Fig.  2  were  constructed  using  the  VESTA program [49]. The bulk structures of ZrC (Fm-3m space group: a=b=c= 4.71 Å) and WC (P6m2 space group: a=b=2.92 Å, c=2.84 Å) were used as the initial models (Fig. 2a). The lattice constants of ZrC (111) (1×1) surface slab were a=b=3.33 Å. For (1×1) WC (0001) surface slab, the lattice constants were a=b= 2.92 Å. Therefore, the (5×5) ZrC (111) surface and  (6×6) WC  (0001)  surface  were  selected  to  match  the  interface  with  minimal  lattice mismatch.  As  shown  in  Fig.  2b  and  2c,  surface  energy  calculations  and  structural optimizations were performed on slab models consisting of six atomic layers. A vacuum region  of  20  Å  was  introduced  to  prevent  interactions  between  periodic  images.  The supercell dimensions were 17.5 Å × 17.5 Å × 30 Å. The (5 × 5) ZrC (111) surface slab contained 75 Zr atoms and 75 C atoms, while the (6 × 6) WC (0001) surface slab contained 108 W atoms and 108 C atoms. During geometric relaxations,  two  atomic  layers  at  the bottom were fixed in their bulk positions, while all other atoms were allowed to fully relax. A plane-wave cutoff energy of 450 eV was consistently applied for all calculations. As shown in Fig. 2c, five interfacial models were constructed, namely ZrC-C-Zr-ZrC, ZrC-C-W-WC, ZrC-Zr-W-WC,  ZrC-Zr-C-WC,  and  ZrC-C-C-WC.  The  Brillouin  zone  of  the

question: What was the sampling frequency used for analyzing grain boundary segregation configurations in Dai et al. 2022?
answer: The sampling frequency used for analyzing grain boundary segregation configurations in Dai et al. 2022 was every 100 steps [1].

chunks.text:
3.1. Grain boundary segregation
The segregation tendency can be well illustrated by element distribution profiles, which were generated by averaging the MC/MD simulated configurations from step 250,0 0 0 to 50 0,0 0 0 with sampling frequency being every 100 steps. Take the /Sigma1 3(112) grain boundary of TiZrHfNbTaC 5 as an example, Fig. 3 shows the element distribution profile of Ti, Zr, Hf, Nb, Ta, where the profile is illustrated by a normalized concentration c n = ( c r -c i )/ c i . c r is the real concentration of a given element at a given site, while c i is the ideal concentration of the element at the site. c i is 0.2 for the randomly occupied state of TiZrHfNbTaC 5 . It can be seen that the concentrations in grains are evenly distributed with small fluctuations, where the c n is approximately a Gaussian distribution (see Ta in Fig. 3 for example). In contrast, the concentrations in GBs deviate evidently from the ideal concentrations, especially for Ti, Zr, and Hf, which present site-specific characteristics. For example, Ti tends to occupy site 2 and hates to occupy site 3. In contrast, Zr and Hf tend to occupy site 3 and hate to occupy site 2. The deviations from the ideal concentration of Nb and Ta are not so significant as those for Ti, Zr, and Hf. Similar to conventional UHTCs [ 8 , 10 , 12 ], the selective occupation tendencies mainly result from size effect. It means that a grain boundary site with com- pressed volume strain, e.g. site 2, tends to be occupied by small atoms, while a grain boundary site with expansion volume strain, e.g. site 3, tends to be occupied by large atoms. Atom size in transition metal carbides can be assumed to follow the relation: Ti < Nb < Ta < Zr ≈ Hf, according to the lattice parameter of each carbide predicted by the DP model (see SI).

5. Summary
In the present work, we fit a DP model for transition metal carbides, and adopted it to simulate grain boundary segregation and the effect of segregation on grain boundary strength in a series of transition metal carbides. Four transition metal carbides were selected, including conventional Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C, medium entropy TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , and high entropy TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , and five GBs, including /Sigma1 3(112), /Sigma1 5(210), /Sigma1 5(310), /Sigma1 3(221), /Sigma1 11(113), were studied for each carbide. The results are summarized as follows:
- (1) Segregation with the site-specific occupation is realized for every grain boundary, which is dominated by size effect, which means that small atoms tend to occupy sites with compression strains, while big atoms tend to occupy sites with expansion strains. On average, GBs of TiZrHfC 3 are segregated with Ti and depleted with Hf. GBs of ZrHfNbTaC 4 are segregated with Nb and depleted with Hf. GBs of TiZrHfNbTaC 5 are segregated with either Ti or Zr, which are compensated with depletion of Hf and/or Ta.
- (2) It is suggested that spontaneous grain boundary segregation is an intrinsic phenomenon in medium/high entropy materials due to the rugged potential energy profile of GBs. Therefore, indepth understanding of the composition-structure-property relations of GBs is essential for medium/high entropy materials.
- (3) Tensile simulations reveal that grain boundary strengths are usually increased or just slightly decreased due to segregation, which means that grain boundary segregation is a universal mechanism to strengthen GBs of both conventional UHTCs and medium/high entropy UHTCs. Therefore, a medium/high entropy UHTC frequently exhibits better high-temperature strength in comparison with its counterparts.

a b s t r a c t
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) exhibit a unique combination of excellent properties, including ultra-high melting point, excellent chemical stability, and good oxidation resistance, which make them promising candidates for aerospace and nuclear applications. However, the degradation of hightemperature strength is one of the main limitations for their ultra-high temperature applications. Thus, searching for mechanisms that can help to develop high-performance UHTCs with good high-temperature mechanical properties is urgently needed. To achieve this goal, grain boundary segregation of a series of carbides, including conventional, medium entropy, and high entropy transition metal carbides, i.e., Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C, TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , were studied by atomistic simulations with a fitted Deep Potential (DP), and the effects of segregation on grain boundary strength were emphasized. For all the studied carbides, grain boundary segregations are realized, which are dominated by the atomic size effect. In addition, tensile simulations indicate that grain boundaries (GBs) will usually be strengthened due to segregation. Our simulation results reveal that grain boundary segregation may be a universal mechanism in enhancing the high-temperature strength of both conventional UHTCs and medium/high entropy UHTCs, since GBs play a key role in controlling the fracture of UHTCs at elevated temperatures.
© 2022 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.

a b s t r a c t
In recent years, the high entropy design paradigm has been extended from metallic materials to ceramics, where multi-principal elements (usually more than 5 elements in a cation site of a ceramic in nearly equal amount) are randomly mixed-up to form a single solid solution [15-21] . Henceforth, medium entropy ceramics and compositional complex ceramics are also developed to tailor the properties of ceramics, which further broadens the chemical design space of ceramics [ 17 , 20 ]. Similar to high entropy alloys, it is intriguing that medium/high entropy ceramics usually exhibit better mechanical properties at elevated temperatures. For example, improved strength retention at elevated temperatures was reported in ZrNbTaC 3 by Demirskyi et al. [22] , in TiZrHfC 3 by Wang et al. [23] , and in TiZrHfNbTaC 5 by Feng et al. [24] . In addition, Han et al. [25] reported that the steady-state creep rates of ZrHfNbTaC 4 are approximately 10 times lower than the published creep rates of corresponding monocarbides. However, the underlying mechanism for good high-temperature properties of medium/high entropy UHTCs is still not clear. In other experiments, grain boundary segregation has been reported in both high entropy alloys and high entropy ceramics [ 26 , 27 ], which may affect the temperature dependent mechanical properties. High-temperature fracture of ceramics frequently involves nucleation, growth, and coalescence of micro-cracks or cavities located at GBs. Therefore, it is essential to know whether grain boundary segregations exist in these medium/high entropy ceramics, and what are their effects if they exist. In this work, grain boundary segregation of a series of carbides, and the effects of segregation on grain boundary strength were studied by atomistic simulations. As it will be shown in later sections, grain boundary segregation is a universal mechanism to improve the high-temperature strength of both conventional and medium/high entropy UHTCs.

3.1. Grain boundary segregation
Fig. 4 (a) shows the Pearson correlation coefficients of c n for Ti, Zr, Hf, Nb, Ta in the /Sigma1 3(112) grain boundary of TiZrHfNbTaC 5 . The sites within 5 ˚ A from the mathematical grain boundary plane are taken into statistics, which means that the grain boundary thickness is assumed to be 1 nm. The correlations reveal that Ti and Nb exhibit similar occupation tendencies, which are almost opposite to the tendencies of Zr and Hf. Ta exhibits low correlations to other elements, since it does not exhibit a strong site-specific occupation tendency. Nevertheless, Ta still exhibits slight positive correlations with Ti and Nb, and slight negative correlations with Zr and Hf. The correlations agree well with the atomic radius of Ti, Zr, Hf, Nb, and Ta in transition metal carbides, which confirms that the segregation is dominated by size effect. Fig. 4 (b) illustrates why site-specific selective occupation is preferred in GBs. In principle, any site in grains is equivalent to each other only with small fluctuations caused by differences in local chemical configuration, wherein the fluctuations can be overcome by thermal excitation. Then, an even distribution of the elements is achieved to form medium/high entropy ceramics. However, except for chemical configurational fluctuations, there are extra fluctuations for sites in GBs, i.e. bond breaking and local distortions. The extra fluctuations enhance local potential energy fluctuations perceived by different elements, which cannot be fully overcome by thermal excitation. Then, element occupations are dominated by local enthalpy instead of entropy, which results in site-specific selective occupation. Therefore, grain boundary segregations may be common in medium/high entropy materials due to the more rugged potential energies of GBs.

question: How many total Monte Carlo simulation steps were performed in Dai et al. 2022 segregation simulations?
answer: The total steps of MC simulation were 50 0,0 0 0 [1].

chunks.text:
2.3. Simulating segregation and strength of GBs
Grain boundary segregation was simulated by a hybrid method combining Monte Carlo (MC) and MD (MC/MD). The simulations were performed by using the LAMMPS package [40] under NPT ensemble with T = 2,0 0 0 K and P = 0 Pa. The time step was set to be 1 fs, and the damping parameters for temperature and pressure were 200 fs and 500 fs, respectively. Each system was first equilibrated for 100 ps before swapping atoms by MC. During MC, a pair of metal atoms were swapped once per 2 steps, and the acceptance probability of the new configuration is min(1, exp(/Delta1 E / k B T )). /Delta1 E is the potential energy change due to swapping. k B is the Boltzmann constant. The total steps of MC simulation were 50 0,0 0 0.
Grain boundary strength was simulated by virtual tensile experiments by MD. The tensile deformation normal to the grain bound- ary plane was applied at a strain rate of 2 × 10 9 s -1 . The simulations were carried out under NPT ensemble with T = 2,0 0 0 K and other stress components being 0 Pa except for the one normal to the grain boundary plane. The time step and damping parameters were the same as those in segregation simulations.

2. Computational details
First-principles simulations were performed by using CASTEP [18] with exchange-correlation described by generalized gradient approximation [19]. In the simulations, Vanderbilt-type ultrasoft pseudopotential [20] was employed with cutoff energy of the plane wave being 400eV. k -points mesh with separation of 0.04 Å /C0 1 was sampled according to Monkhorst-Pack method [21] in the Brillouin zone. All of the supercells containing grain boundaries were fully optimized with respect to both lattice parameters and atom positions by using Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) minimization scheme [22]. The tolerances for geometry optimization were set as follows: difference in total energy within 5 /C2 10 /C0 6 eV/atom, maximum ionic Hellmann-Feynman force within 0.01 eV/Å, maximum ionic displacement within 5 /C2 10 /C0 4 Å and maximum stress within 0.02 GPa.
To assess the effects of solute atom segregation on grain boundary strengths, tensile stress-strain relations of grain boundaries were simulated. During the simulation, the supercell was incrementally deformed in the imposed strain direction. At each step, the supercell was fully relaxed by employing BFGS minimization scheme until all other stress components vanished. Then, the relaxed supercell was taken as the starting structure for the next step to ensure the stress-strain curves being continuous.

2. Experimental section
To gain deeper insights into the polymer-derived silicon oxycarbide (SiOC) material and the interactions among Si, O, and C constituents, a ReaxFF  energy  potential  in  combination  with  LAMMPS  (Large-scale Atomic/Molecular  Massively  Parallel  Simulator)  was  employed  to conduct molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. ReaxFF is a robust MD simulation method extensively utilized in this study. In the simulations, the polymer chains were subjected to the NPT ensemble, allowing for relaxation throughout 80,000 timesteps at a timestep of 0.2 fs (femtoseconds) per timestep. The deletion interval between consecutive steps was set at 5 ps (picoseconds). Throughout the simulation, the types of molecules that were deleted remained consistent, ensuring consistency and accuracy in the analysis of the simulation results.

3.2. Grain boundary strength
To evaluate the effects of grain boundary segregation on grain boundary strength, tensile stress-strain relations normal to grain boundary plane were simulated. Fig. 5 compares the grain boundary strengths of Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C, TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , and TiZrHfNbTaC 5 before and after MC/MD simulations. Before MC/MD simulations, there is no segregation, and elements are randomly distributed. After MC/MD simulations, equilibrium concentrations are realized, where there are evident grain boundary segregations. It can be seen from Fig. 5 (a)-(c) that grain boundary strengths in conventional Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C and medium entropy TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , are mostly improved due to segregation. The results are consistent with our previous simulations in ZrB 2 and ZrC [ 8 , 10 , 12 ]. In these systems, GBs are enriched with small atoms, which imposes compressive strains to GBs, and increases grain boundary strengths. Segregation of W in ZrC GBs, Ti in TiZrHfC 3 GBs, and Nb in ZrHfNbTaC 4 GBs (see Table 1 ) all belong to this category, where significantly increased grain boundary strengths can usually be realized due to segregation. For TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , the results are more complicated, where segregation exhibits negligible effect on grain boundary strengths, or usually slightly decreases the strengths. For example, /Sigma1 9(221) and /Sigma1 11(113) GBs are segregated with big atoms, which may induce extra expansion strains to GBs and reduces grain boundary strengths. Nevertheless, the base strengths of GBs without segregation are high enough for TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , which can be seen by comparing Fig. 5 (d) with Fig. 5 (b, c).

2.1. Simulation method
TaC and HfC both possess the Fm3m space group, with each unit cell containing four Ta/Hf atoms and four C atoms. Three compositions were chosen with x values being 0.2, 0.4, and 0.5. We set up Ta1-xHfxC solid solution models with special quasi-random structures (SQS) approach [19] using mcsqs code of the Alloy Theoretic Automated Toolkit (ATAT) software  [20,21].  The  SQS  method  uses  a  Monte  Carlo-simulated annealing loop with an objective function to find the perfect match of correlation functions of a disordered state [22]. The model we get is shown in Fig. 1, which is a supercell containing 80 atoms.
The electronic structure, mechanical properties, and phase stability of three solid solutions were investigated using the density functional theory (DFT). All the calculations were carried out by the plane-wavebased Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) code [23] on Beijing  Super  Cloud  Computing  Center  (Beijing,  China).  Generalized gradient  approximation  (GGA)  base  on  the  Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE)  scheme  [24]  was  used  to  describe  the  exchange-correlation function.  The K-point was set to 1 × 2.5 × 5.  The  plane-wave cutoff and  the  ionic  relaxation  loop  was  set  to  400  eV  and  10 GLYPH<0> 8 eV, respectively.

question: What was the strain rate applied to grain boundaries in the virtual tensile experiments performed by Dai et al. 2022?
answer: The strain rate applied to grain boundaries in the virtual tensile experiments performed by Dai et al. 2022 was 2 × 10⁹ s⁻¹ [1].

2.3. Simulating segregation and strength of GBs
Grain boundary segregation was simulated by a hybrid method combining Monte Carlo (MC) and MD (MC/MD). The simulations were performed by using the LAMMPS package [40] under NPT ensemble with T = 2,0 0 0 K and P = 0 Pa. Then, the relaxed supercell was taken as the starting structure for the next step to ensure the stress-strain curves being continuous.

4.3. Grain boundary strength
To assess the in /uniFB02 uences of solute atoms on grain boundary strengths, tensile stress-strain curves of grain boundaries with the grain boundary sites occupied by different solute atoms were simulated, as shown in Fig. 3. In principle, the solute atoms were introduced as columns due to the periodic repetition of supercell. Such a 2D periodic occupation of solute atoms at grain boundary were frequently observed in experiments [13 e 15,17], which are called ' grain boundary complexions ' by some investigators [17]. It means that our simulations are close representations of real grain boundaries when solute atoms tend to segregate into grain boundaries. For each grain boundary, tensile stress-strain curve of the boundary without any solute atom and tensile stress-strain curve of bulk ZrB2 normal to the boundary plane were simulated for comparison. As can be seen from the /uniFB01 gures, solute atoms change the stress-strain responses of grain boundaries remarkably, indicating the strong in /uniFB02 uences of solute atoms on grain boundary properties.

3.2. Grain boundary strength
An interesting phenomenon related to higher grain boundary strength without segregation is grain boundary structure reconstruction during tensile. Fig. 6 (a) shows the tensile stress-strain curve of the /Sigma1 3(112) grain boundary with and without segregation of TiZrHfNbTaC 5 . It can be seen that there is a discontinuity in the stress-strain curve without segregation at a stress of ∼ 9 GPa. The curve first decreases and then increases again at the discontinuity, which finally reaches a value of ∼ 15 GPa. The discontinuity in the curve is associated with a shear along the grain boundary plane, which changes the grain boundary structure. Fig. 6 (b, c) shows the grain boundary structure before and after the shear deformation, respectively. Atoms on either side of the grain boundary are aligned as metal-metal and carbon-carbon before shear, which changes to metal-carbon and metal-carbon after shear. If the sheared structure is quenched, and the tensile simulation is redone, a continuous stress-strain curve is obtained with its final strength (15.1 GPa) similar to the original one (14.9 GPa), as shown in Fig. 6 (d). In addition, if the quenched structure is first simulated by MC/MD to obtain its equilibrium element distribution profile (segregated grain boundary), then redo the tensile simulation, a higher strength (17.0 GPa) can be achieved, which is much higher than those of the unsheared and segregated structure (14.8 GPa). A similar phenomenon is also observed in the /Sigma1 9(221) grain boundary without segregation, where a discontinuity appears at a stress of ∼ 9 GPa. If we quench the sheared structure, and redo the tensile simulation, a continuous curve is obtained with a final stress of 16.2 GPa, which is comparable to the original one of 15.8 GPa.

3.2. Grain boundary strength
To evaluate the effects of grain boundary segregation on grain boundary strength, tensile stress-strain relations normal to grain boundary plane were simulated. Fig. 5 compares the grain boundary strengths of Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C, TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , and TiZrHfNbTaC 5 before and after MC/MD simulations. Before MC/MD simulations, there is no segregation, and elements are randomly distributed. After MC/MD simulations, equilibrium concentrations are realized, where there are evident grain boundary segregations. It can be seen from Fig. 5 (a)-(c) that grain boundary strengths in conventional Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C and medium entropy TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , are mostly improved due to segregation. The results are consistent with our previous simulations in ZrB 2 and ZrC [ 8 , 10 , 12 ]. In these systems, GBs are enriched with small atoms, which imposes compressive strains to GBs, and increases grain boundary strengths. Segregation of W in ZrC GBs, Ti in TiZrHfC 3 GBs, and Nb in ZrHfNbTaC 4 GBs (see Table 1 ) all belong to this category, where significantly increased grain boundary strengths can usually be realized due to segregation. For TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , the results are more complicated, where segregation exhibits negligible effect on grain boundary strengths, or usually slightly decreases the strengths. For example, /Sigma1 9(221) and /Sigma1 11(113) GBs are segregated with big atoms, which may induce extra expansion strains to GBs and reduces grain boundary strengths. Nevertheless, the base strengths of GBs without segregation are high enough for TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , which can be seen by comparing Fig. 5 (d) with Fig. 5 (b, c).

question: Which elements show enrichment and depletion at grain boundaries in the high entropy carbide TiZrHfNbTaC5 according to Dai et al. 2022?
answer: In the high entropy carbide TiZrHfNbTaC5, the elements that show enrichment at grain boundaries are Ti and Zr, while the elements that show depletion are Hf and/or Ta, according to Dai et al. 2022 [1].

chunks.text:
3.1. Grain boundary segregation
Table 1 lists the exceeding normalized concentration δ i ( i = Ti, Zr, Hf, Nb, Ta) in different GBs of medium entropy TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 and high entropy TiZrHfNbTaC 5 . The exceeding normalized concentration δ i is the summation of c n of sites within 5 ˚ A from the mathematical grain boundary plane. δ i = 0 means that there is no segregation of element i in the grain boundary, while δ i > 0 ( δ i < 0) means that there is segregation (depletion) of element i in the grain boundary. Therefore, evident grain boundary segregations for the medium/high entropy transition metal carbides can be found. For the medium entropy TiZrHfC 3 , all the investigated GBs are enriched with Ti, which is mainly complemented by depletion of Hf. For the medium entropy ZrHfNbTaC 4 , all the investigated GBs are enriched with Nb, which is mainly complemented by depletion of Hf. For the high entropy TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , the results are more complicated, where the /Sigma1 3(112), /Sigma1 5(210) and /Sigma1 5(310) are mainly segregated with Ti, while /Sigma1 9(221) and /Sigma1 11(113) are mainly segregated with Zr. The enrichment of Ti or Zr is mainly complemented by depletion of Hf and/or Ta. Even though Zr and Hf exhibit similar atomic size and preferred site-specific occupation, their overall segregations/depletions are still different, which means that the segregation does not solely depend on size effect. Table 1 reveals that Hf is the element that most prefers to deplete from GBs, which may be followed by Ta. It is perhaps that HfC and TaC are the most refractory carbides.

5. Summary
In the present work, we fit a DP model for transition metal carbides, and adopted it to simulate grain boundary segregation and the effect of segregation on grain boundary strength in a series of transition metal carbides. Four transition metal carbides were selected, including conventional Zr 0.95 W 0.05 C, medium entropy TiZrHfC 3 , ZrHfNbTaC 4 , and high entropy TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , and five GBs, including /Sigma1 3(112), /Sigma1 5(210), /Sigma1 5(310), /Sigma1 3(221), /Sigma1 11(113), were studied for each carbide. The results are summarized as follows:
- (1) Segregation with the site-specific occupation is realized for every grain boundary, which is dominated by size effect, which means that small atoms tend to occupy sites with compression strains, while big atoms tend to occupy sites with expansion strains. On average, GBs of TiZrHfC 3 are segregated with Ti and depleted with Hf. GBs of ZrHfNbTaC 4 are segregated with Nb and depleted with Hf. GBs of TiZrHfNbTaC 5 are segregated with either Ti or Zr, which are compensated with depletion of Hf and/or Ta.
- (2) It is suggested that spontaneous grain boundary segregation is an intrinsic phenomenon in medium/high entropy materials due to the rugged potential energy profile of GBs. Therefore, indepth understanding of the composition-structure-property relations of GBs is essential for medium/high entropy materials.
- (3) Tensile simulations reveal that grain boundary strengths are usually increased or just slightly decreased due to segregation, which means that grain boundary segregation is a universal mechanism to strengthen GBs of both conventional UHTCs and medium/high entropy UHTCs. Therefore, a medium/high entropy UHTC frequently exhibits better high-temperature strength in comparison with its counterparts.

4. Discussion
Different from conventional materials, whose solute elements originate from additives, medium/high entropy materials bring their own solutes. Even though the multi-elements may form a random solid solution in grains, random distribution of the multielements in GBs is harder to achieve due to the more rugged potential energy profile of GBs, which results from bond breaking/reconstruction and distortions around GBs. Therefore, spontaneous segregation/depletion will take place in GBs of medium/high entropy materials to minimize grain boundary energies. In addition, the multi-elements in medium/high entropy materials are similar in chemical properties, the segregation/depletion of which only fine-tunes grain boundary properties instead of changing the properties abruptly. As a result, the spontaneously segregated/depleted GBs may exhibit better adhesion. Our simulations show that segregation takes place in all GBs studied here, and sitespecific occupation tendency is discovered. Moreover, the spontaneously segregated/depleted GBs are usually strengthened. Therefore, good strength retention and creep resistance at elevated temperatures are frequently achieved in medium/high entropy ceramics due to the spontaneous segregation/depletion of elements to/from GBs to reduce grain boundary energies. For example, good high-temperature strength was reported in ZrNbTaC 3 by Demirskyi et al. [22] , in TiZrHfC 3 by Wang et al. [23] , and in TiZrHfNbTaC 5 by Feng et al. [24] , while Han et al. [23] reported improved steadystate creep rates of ZrHfNbTaC 4 in comparison with corresponding monocarbides. Grain boundary segregation not only improves grain boundary adhesion, but also hinders grain boundary sliding and pins grain boundary migration. Then, fine and uniform microstructures are usually realized in medium/high entropy materials, which are also beneficial to their performance. In contrast, conventional UHTCs usually exhibit non-uniform microstructure and big voids in them, which will result in poor mechanical properties.

Fig. 10).
Phase and grain boundaries together with average grain size play a very important role in plastic deformation and failure of metals and alloys [38], but also in advanced engineering ceramics. In some cases, the grain-boundary geometry, structure, and chemical composition is even more important than the apparent grain size. Advances in the capabilities  of  analytical  microscopy  techniques  are  able  to  provide essential  atomic-scale  structural  and  chemical  information  for  the necessary understanding needed to effectively design highly progressive advanced  ceramic  materials  with  the  required  mechanical  and  functional properties. Fracture processes leading to failures are also often connected to interfaces, their atomistic structure and chemistry, therefore a detailed understanding of the structural and chemical ordering at the grain interface is essential. The dual-phase high-entropy ceramics, where the grain boundary characteristics are very important are processed in three main routes in the literature [21 -25], using spark plasma sintering  as  in  the  present  study,  boro-carbo  thermal  reduction  and spark plasma sintering and reaction sintering.
The phase and grain boundary characteristics of newly developed fine-grained dual-phase high-entropy (Ti0.14Zr0.2Nb0.2Hf0.2Ta0.26)C/ (Ti0.38Zr0.18Nb0.22Hf0.115Ta0.105)B2 ceramic was investigated from micron to sub-nanometer level [37]. According to the results, the phase and grain boundaries are typically clean and sharp with indistinct 1 -1.5 nm  thin  gradient  of  HE  metallic  elements  at  boride/boride  and carbide/carbide interfaces. At the boride/boride interface, the gradient means lower Ti and slightly lower Hf with higher Ta and slightly higher Nb, and at the carbide/carbide interface on the other hand, lower Hf with slightly higher Ti. The sharp phase/grain boundaries do exhibit elemental enrichment from a trace amount of iron being incorporated in interstitial  positions  of  carbide  and  boride  grains  locally  at  boride/carbide boundaries or are present in boride and carbide grains [37].

3.1. Grain boundary segregation
Therefore, the smallest one (Ti) and the biggest ones (Zr and Hf) reveal strong grain boundary segregation/depletion tendency and site-specific selectivity in high entropy TiZrHfNbTaC 5 , while Nb and Ta only reveal weak selective occupation tendency.

question: In Khoeini et al. 2015 study on ZrB2-SiC composites with pressureless sintering, what maximum relative density was achieved in the ZS10C2 composite sample?
answer: In Khoeini et al. 2015 study on ZrB2-SiC composites with pressureless sintering, the maximum relative density achieved in the ZS10C2 composite sample was 99.65% [1].

chunks.text:
3.1. Densi /uniFB01 cation and microstructure evolutions
The variations of relative density of the sintered samples (as function of the sintering temperature and SiC volume fraction) are shown in Table 1 and Fig. 2. Fig. 3 indicates that the optimum temperature to achieve maximum relative density by pressureless sintering is 2000 1 C, while the values of relative densities of the composites sintered at 1800 -2100 1 C have been found to be less. However, the results of an earlier study on ZrB2 (1 -10 wt%) SiC composites with 1 -2 wt% C have not shown signi /uniFB01 cant changes [24]. From the results shown in Fig. 5(b), the relative densities of the ZrB 2 -SiC composites are found to be about 99.52% of the corresponding theoretical calculated densities, while that of the ZBC is only /C24 88%. Moreover, the values of relative densities are found to increase in a steady state manner with increase in volume fraction of SiC. These observations suggest that processing of completely dense pure ZrB2 is very dif /uniFB01 cult, while the presence of SiC as reinforcement is signi /uniFB01 cantly bene /uniFB01 cial for promoting the densi /uniFB01 cation. In this study, maximum relative density of 99.65% has been achieved in ZS10C2 composite, which was sintered at 2000 1 C for 2 h after holding at 1650 1 C for 2 h. The values of relative densities obtained in this study are comparable with those of the pressure-less sintered ZrB2 -SiC composites, reported in previous studies [22,24].

Signi /uniFB01 cance of hot pressing parameters and reinforcement size on densi /uniFB01 cation behavior of ZrB2 -25 vol% SiC UHTCs

Table 1 presents some of the studies that have been accomplished on ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composites manufactured by hot pressing at different conditions. All of the composites were prepared by high external pressures; hence, the aim of this study was to use relatively low pressures. Accordingly, in the present study, the in /uniFB02 uences of four factors consisting of the sintering temperature; the applied pressure; the holding time and the SiC particle size on the density of hot pressed ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composites were studied using the Taguchi method.

3.1. Relative density and microstructure
The variation of relative density of ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites with reinforcement content (SiC and CNT) is presented in Fig. 1. The relative density of monolithic ZrB2 is also indicated. For the given SPS processing parameters (sintering temperature of 1900 ◦ C, soaking time of 15 min, and pressure of 70 MPa), the relative density of monolithic ZrB2 was about 85%. For the similar processing parameters, the ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites exhibited much higher relative densities (>95%). In general, the relative density of the composites increased with the reinforcement content. The data on relative density showed relatively larger scatter at lower reinforcement levels (10% SiC and 2% CNT). For ZrB2-SiC composites, the samples reached near-full densification (relative density >99%) at all reinforcement levels (10-40% SiC). While similar trend of densification was observed for ZrB2-CNT composites, the near-full densification was achieved at reinforcement levels of 4-6% CNTs. The relative density of ZrB2-CNT composite reinforced with 2% CNTs was found to be about 95%. Clearly, very small amount of CNT reinforcement is sufficient to achieve full densification in these composites.

4.8. Near-net-shape colloidal processing
In the case of aqueous suspension of ZrB2, which was processed via freeze form of extrusion rapid proto typing, a maximum density of ~96% ρth was obtained after PS at 2000 °C [130]. Medri et al. [131] developed ZrB2-SiC-Si3N4 composites via aqueous slip casting route and achieved sinter-density of 98%ρth upon sintering at 2150 °C. In a different work, He et al. [132] investigated the mechanical properties of ZrB2-SiC-B4C-C ceramics, processed via gel casting technique and pressureless sintering, and reported a flexural strength of ~405 MPa and a fracture toughness of ~4.3 MPam 1/2 . Using this route, they could successfully produce complex shaped components of ZrB2-SiC ceramic composites, possessing homogeneous microstructure. In another work, He et al. [133] prepared HfB2-20 vol% MoSi2 composites through aqueous gel-casting and measured better properties of ceramics, when solid loading was 45%. For such materials, the highest green strength, flexural strength and fracture toughness of ~39 MPa, ~416 MPa and ~4.2 MPam 1/2 , respectively were obtained. Xiang et al. [203] prepared ZrB2-30 vol% SiC/BN using tape casting, followed by hot pressing at 1950 °C for 1 h. The flexural strength of this composite was lower when compared to ZrB2-30 vol% SiC due to the presence of BN interlayer.

A B S T R A C T
interparticle forces and produce a stable and homogenous colloid. This is key to yield high particle packing in a powder compact or green body during the shaping stage. During sintering, the high particle packing facilitates densification even at relatively low temperatures, and without applied pressure. In the work of Tallon et al. [10], slip cast green bodies were produced with a relative density of 63% as compared to 48% for dry pressed green bodies. After pressureless sintering at 2100 ◦ C, the resulting samples had densities of 93% and 70%, respectively, even with no sintering aids. It was later reported by Leo et al. [11] that the addition of sintering aids such as carbon black produced 98% dense components with slip casting followed by pressureless sintering, even at the reduced temperature of 1900 ◦ C. He et al. [12] also produced 98% dense sintered ZrB2 -SiC composites fabricated by gelcasting after pressureless sintering at 2100 ◦ C with the addition of C and B4C as sintering aids, and Huang et  al.  [13]  reported  a  density  of  96%  in  ZrB2  prepared  by  aqueous Freeform Extrusion Fabrication (FEF) followed by pressureless sintering at 2000 ◦ C, also with the addition of C and B4C as sintering aids. Even with routes that are less traditionally used to fabricate dense components, such as freeze casting, densities of 91.5% were achieved using ZrB2  in CYC along with pressureless sintering at 2100 ◦ C by Leo et al. [14].  This  result  demonstrates  that  colloidal  processing  can  produce sufficiently dense ZrB2 components without the application of pressure, leading the way to more complex shaping capabilities.

question: According to Khoeini et al. 2015, what is the melting temperature of zirconium diboride (ZrB2)?
answer: According to Khoeini et al. 2015, the melting temperature of zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is 3250 °C [2].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is gaining substantial interest due to its high melting point (3245 °C), good oxidation resistance from room temperature to 2500 °C, low theoretical density, high thermal conductivity and ability to retain strength at elevated temperature. Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) exhibits the intriguing combination of both metal-like and ceramic-like properties such as high melting point (3245 °C), low coe /uniFB03 cient of thermal expansion, high thermal conductivity, low theoretical density and ability to retain strength at elevated temperature [1]. These properties enable ZrB2 to survive extreme temperatures, chemical attack, erosive environments and heat /uniFB02 uxes, which makes it a potential candidate for hypersonic vehicles and Reusable Launch Vehicles (RLVs). ZrB2 exhibit hexagonal close pack arrangement with a strong covalent bond between B -B atoms and Zr -B atoms and the close-packed metallic layer (Zr -Zr) exhibit metallic bond characteristics like high thermal conductivity, high electrical conductivity [2]. The properties of the ZrB2 ceramic generally depend on the pyrolysis temperature and the method employed for its synthesis. Most common methods employed for the synthesis of ZrB2 is by using zirconium oxide (ZrO2), diboron trioxide (B2O3) and carbon (carbothermal reduction), ZrO2 and boron carbide (B4C) (boron carbide reduction) and ZrO2, B4C and carbon (combined reduction) [3,4] and the reactions involved are given in equations (1) -(3). 1 INTRODUCTION
Much research on zirconium diboride (ZrB2 )-based ultrahigh-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) has been carried out in the recent past. Its notable features include a high melting point ( ∼ 3246 ◦ C), high thermal conductivity ( ∼ 85-90 W/m/K 1 ), and strength retention at very high temperature ( ∼ 600 MPa at 1600 ◦ C 2 and 200 MPa at 2300 ◦ C 3 ). Over other engineering ceramics such as SiC and Si 3 N 4 , zirconium diboride has the additional advantage of high melting point of its oxide product ( ∼ 2500 ◦ C). However, the main limitation of ZrB2 -based UHTCs is related to its low fracture toughness and poor thermal shock resistance. 4 Therefore, toughening of ceramics is the prime goal for the use of these ceramics. The reinforcing allows for ceramic toughening. Particulate, whiskers, short fibers, and continuous fibers can all be used as reinforcements. When compared to monolithic ceramics, incorporating particulate, whiskers, and short fibers improves toughening. 5 Continuous fiber, on the other hand, considerably improves the fracture toughness

1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is an ultra high temperature ceramic (UHTC) possessing high thermomechanical strength and chemical resistance. With a melting point of ∼ 3200 ◦ C it is one of the leading candidates for thermal protection systems in hypersonic flight and refractory materials such as crucibles for high temperature metals processing [1,2]. However, its densification is challenging even by the use of pressure and pulsed electric current/spark plasma sintering (SPS) methods.
