1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is a strong covalent compound with a hexagonal AlB2 structure. Among its properties, ZrB2 has an ultrahigh melting point (>3000 ◦ C), high Young's modulus, high hardness, low electrical resistivity ( ∼ 10 -5 /DEL cm), high thermal conductivity(>60W/mK),andgoodresistancetochemicalattack[1-7]. The combination of these characteristics makes ZrB2 ceramics useful for high temperature structural applications, such as cutting tools, thermal protection for hypersonic vehicles, molten metal crucibles, high temperature electrodes, and thermowell tubes for steel refining [1-8]. Historic studies implied that phase pure diborides could not be pressurelessly sintered to full density due to strong covalent bond and low self-diffusion as well as the hexagonal crystal structure that allowed for anisotropic grain growth and entrapped porosity (i.e., coarsening is more favorable than densification) [4,7]. The need for dense ZrB2 ceramics was initially driven bynuclear applications that required high neutron absorption cross sections. Later, research about densification is driven by aerospace applications because the low densification causes the reduction in combination properties of ZrB2 ceramics [9]. Without sintering additives, in historic studies, ZrB2 ceramics have only been densified by HP at 2000 ◦ C or higher with pressures of 20-30MPa, or at reduced temperatures (1790-1840 ◦ C) with much higher pressures (800-1500MPa) [10]. Although these dense ZrB2 ceramics have many advantages, their intrinsic characteristics, such as low fracture toughness (premature failure due to brittle fracture) and

question: In the Khoeini et al. 2015 pressureless sintering study, at what temperature was the maximum relative density achieved for ZrB2-SiC composites?
answer: In the Khoeini et al. 2015 pressureless sintering study, the maximum relative density for ZrB2-SiC composites was achieved at 2000 1 C [1].

chunks.text:
3.1. Densi /uniFB01 cation and microstructure evolutions
The variations of relative density of the sintered samples (as function of the sintering temperature and SiC volume fraction) are shown in Table 1 and Fig. 2. Fig. 3 indicates that the optimum temperature to achieve maximum relative density by pressureless sintering is 2000 1 C, while the values of relative densities of the composites sintered at 1800 -2100 1 C have been found to be less. However, the results of an earlier study on ZrB2 (1 -10 wt%) SiC composites with 1 -2 wt% C have not shown signi /uniFB01 cant changes [24]. From the results shown in Fig. 5(b), the relative densities of the ZrB 2 -SiC composites are found to be about 99.52% of the corresponding theoretical calculated densities, while that of the ZBC is only /C24 88%. Moreover, the values of relative densities are found to increase in a steady state manner with increase in volume fraction of SiC. These observations suggest that processing of completely dense pure ZrB2 is very dif /uniFB01 cult, while the presence of SiC as reinforcement is signi /uniFB01 cantly bene /uniFB01 cial for promoting the densi /uniFB01 cation. In this study, maximum relative density of 99.65% has been achieved in ZS10C2 composite, which was sintered at 2000 1 C for 2 h after holding at 1650 1 C for 2 h. The values of relative densities obtained in this study are comparable with those of the pressure-less sintered ZrB2 -SiC composites, reported in previous studies [22,24].

Table 1 presents some of the studies that have been accomplished on ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composites manufactured by hot pressing at different conditions. All of the composites were prepared by high external pressures; hence, the aim of this study was to use relatively low pressures. Accordingly, in the present study, the in /uniFB02 uences of four factors consisting of the sintering temperature; the applied pressure; the holding time and the SiC particle size on the density of hot pressed ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composites were studied using the Taguchi method.

4.8. Near-net-shape colloidal processing
In the case of aqueous suspension of ZrB2, which was processed via freeze form of extrusion rapid proto typing, a maximum density of ~96% ρth was obtained after PS at 2000 °C [130]. Medri et al. [131] developed ZrB2-SiC-Si3N4 composites via aqueous slip casting route and achieved sinter-density of 98%ρth upon sintering at 2150 °C. In a different work, He et al. [132] investigated the mechanical properties of ZrB2-SiC-B4C-C ceramics, processed via gel casting technique and pressureless sintering, and reported a flexural strength of ~405 MPa and a fracture toughness of ~4.3 MPam 1/2 . Using this route, they could successfully produce complex shaped components of ZrB2-SiC ceramic composites, possessing homogeneous microstructure. In another work, He et al. [133] prepared HfB2-20 vol% MoSi2 composites through aqueous gel-casting and measured better properties of ceramics, when solid loading was 45%. For such materials, the highest green strength, flexural strength and fracture toughness of ~39 MPa, ~416 MPa and ~4.2 MPam 1/2 , respectively were obtained. Xiang et al. [203] prepared ZrB2-30 vol% SiC/BN using tape casting, followed by hot pressing at 1950 °C for 1 h. The flexural strength of this composite was lower when compared to ZrB2-30 vol% SiC due to the presence of BN interlayer.

3.1. Relative density and microstructure
The variation of relative density of ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites with reinforcement content (SiC and CNT) is presented in Fig. 1. The relative density of monolithic ZrB2 is also indicated. For the given SPS processing parameters (sintering temperature of 1900 ◦ C, soaking time of 15 min, and pressure of 70 MPa), the relative density of monolithic ZrB2 was about 85%. For the similar processing parameters, the ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites exhibited much higher relative densities (>95%). In general, the relative density of the composites increased with the reinforcement content. The data on relative density showed relatively larger scatter at lower reinforcement levels (10% SiC and 2% CNT). For ZrB2-SiC composites, the samples reached near-full densification (relative density >99%) at all reinforcement levels (10-40% SiC). While similar trend of densification was observed for ZrB2-CNT composites, the near-full densification was achieved at reinforcement levels of 4-6% CNTs. The relative density of ZrB2-CNT composite reinforced with 2% CNTs was found to be about 95%. Clearly, very small amount of CNT reinforcement is sufficient to achieve full densification in these composites.

3. Results and discussions
The relative densities of ZrB2-30 vol% ZrO2f ceramics sintered by SPS, compared with literature reports of densi /uniFB01 cation achieved via conventional processing techniques [14], are shown in Table 1. The results revealed that the sintering temperature had remarkable in /uniFB02 uence on the relative density. The activation of the powder particle surface due to spark and possible plasma generation, along with superior Joules heating (for conducting materials) would result in enhanced mass transport and hence faster densi /uniFB01 cation kinetics during SPS [17,18]. With sintering temperature rising from 1600 1 C to 1750 1 C, the relative density of the bulk compact was increased. The maximum densi /uniFB01 cation of /C24 99.2% ρ th was obtained at 1750 1 C by SPS. However, the sintered density of ZrB2 -ZrO2f was lowered from /C24 99.2% ρ th to /C24 96.6% ρ th, as the sintering temperature increasing from 1750 1 C to 1800 1 C during SPS process. In our earlier research [15], /C24 98.1% ρ th can be obtained by hot pressing at 1950 1 C for 2 h for the same starting powder. However, much less sintered density of 78.7% ρ th was obtained by pressureless sintering at 1950 1 C for 10 h. Such observations clearly indicated the advantage offered by SPS, which was a comparatively novel sintering process that allowed the compaction of ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics to be sintered at lower temperatures, with shorter processing time.

question: What were the amounts of open-type and closed-type porosities measured in the Nayebi et al. 2016 ZrB2-40 vol% B4C composite?
answer: The amounts of open-type and closed-type porosities measured in the Nayebi et al. 2016 ZrB2-40 vol% B4C composite were 24% and 7%, respectively [1].

chunks.text:
3. Results and discussion
Fig. 1 shows the SEM micrographs of fracture surface of the sample. The amounts of open-type and closed-type porosities in the microstructure of ZrB2 -40 vol% B4C ceramic composite were calculated to be /C24 24% and 7%, respectively. Although two types of porosities are presented in the structure of the discussed sample, it appears that they have been initiated from different motivators. The formation of closed pores can be related to the production of gaseous phases which release during the sintering process due to the reduction of surface oxide impurities of ZrB2 (zirconia and boria) by B4C (or free carbon existence). The presence of open channels is perhaps due to the intensive dissimilarity in the particle size of the starting raw materials or gas-caused gaps between adjacent particles which disturb the sinterability.
The relatively low sintering temperature of 1800 ° C, the mainly covalent nature of ZrB2 and B4C bonds and also the large size of the B4C particles caused low sinterability in this research. As it can be clearly seen in Fig. 1a, mostly the particles with small dimensions ( e. g . ZrB2) are either joined together or with other large particles. However, there is no considerable connections between the large particles of B4C.

a b s t r a c t
The micro/nanostructural development, sintering process and its controlling mechanisms as well as the interfaces between ZrB2 and B4C particles were investigated using high resolution SEM, XRD and TEM analyses in a hot pressed ZrB2 -40 vol% B4C ceramic composite. Two types of porosities (open and closed) were observed in the microstructure of fabricated porous composite, which were caused by different factors. The results showed that the sintering process was encouraged by the formation of liquid phases at the interfaces, elimination of oxide impurities from the surfaces of ZrB2 particles (due to the chemical reactions with B4C particles and their free carbon), plastic deformation of ZrB2 grains and the diffusion of carbon and/or boron atoms from B4C toward ZrB2 particles. A mixture of nano-particles and nano-porosities at the submicron thickness amorphous ZrB2/B4C interfaces is detected and discussed in detail.

3. Results and discussion

Fig. 2 presents the XRD pattern of as-sintered ZrB2 -40 vol% B4C composite. Apparently, the main detected crystalline phases are ZrB2 and B4C; however, the traces of ZrO2 and graphite are also perceivable. Moreover, no detectable sign of amorphous phase is observed in XRD pattern, which is probably due to the very small amounts of such phases in the /uniFB01 nal microstructure of the composite. Subsequently, it can be concluded that the above-mentioned interfacial phases are consisted of crystalline ZrO2 and graphite nano-particles lied in an amorphous B2O3 bed.
Although around 30% of porosities have been /uniFB01 nally remained in the structure of hot pressed sample, it seems that the sintering process has remarkably progressed due to the above-mentioned phenomena at the ZrB2/B4C interfaces.

Fig. 5. TEM nanograph of ZrB2 particles in ZrB2 -B4C system.

a b s t r a c t
He et al. reported the effect of ZrB2 contents on B4C -ZrB2 ceramics fabricated by hot pressing at 1950 ° C for 60 min under an applied pressure of 30 MPa. They found that the addition of ZrB2 not only considerably assists the sintering process and produces the fully dense samples, but also improves the mechanical properties of the hot pressed composites [10].
Although the presence of the porosity may play a negative role in case of mechanical properties for ZrB2-based composites as well as their oxidation resistance, it has been concluded that the thermal shock resistance of the composite can be signi /uniFB01 cantly enhanced due to the presence of deliberately formed porosities [32 -34]. From another point of view, porosities can lead to a lower density composite formation, which has probably a broader structural applications and in particular for ZrB2-based composites usages e.g . aerospace components. In the latter case, recent developments in surface treatments have grown opportunities to counteract the porosity-derived drawbacks in ZrB2-based materials. For example, researches have proved that laser treatments can form a glassy borosilicate phase [35] and a dense composite layer [36] on the surfaces of such porous composites. The formed dense layers can enhance the surface oxidation resistance of the designed synthesized material and also protect the inner regions of the composite from diffusing oxygen [35]. In addition, very important subsequent properties such as low thermal conductivity, controlled permeability, high surface area, high speci /uniFB01 c strength and low dielectric constant have been introduced as complementary advantages to the tailored porosities in the engineered ceramic materials [33].
We have recently reported the sintering process of porous ZrB2 -B4C binary ceramics which contained 0 -100 vol% B4C and hot pressed at 1800 ° C for 30 min under 12 MPa. SEM analysis of fracture surfaces showed that the sintering process controls by the formation of ZrO2 phases [37].

question: According to Nayebi et al. 2016, what were the average particle sizes of the ZrB2 and B4C powders used in the hot pressing process?
answer: The average particle size of the ZrB2 powder was 2 μm, and the average particle size of the B4C powder was 20 μm [1].

chunks.text:
2.1. Materials and process
In this research, commercially available ZrB2 powder with an average particle size of 2 m m (Leung Hi-tech Co., China) and B4C powder with an average particle size of 20 m m (Mudanjiang Jingangzuan Boron Carbide Co., China) were purchased and used as raw materials. Such relatively large B4C particles were applied to promote the formation of open porosities in as-sintered sample. ZrB2 powder was dry-mixed with 40 vol% B4C powder by ball mixing at 120 rpm for 60 min in zirconia media. Then the sample with a diameter of 25 mm and a thickness of 5 mm was loaded into a graphite die, which was sprayed with boron nitride (BN) and lined with /uniFB02 exible graphite foils. Sintering process was performed in a vacuum hot pressing furnace (Shenyang Weitai Science & Technology Development Co. Ltd., China). The powder mixture was initially heated at a rate of 15 ° C/min up to 1000 ° C and then the temperature was kept at 1000 ° C for 30 min. Finally the sample was heated at a rate of 12 ° C/min up to 1800 ° C. An external pressure of 12 MPa was applied to the sample, for 30 min, after the temperature reached 1800 ° C. The sintering furnace was cooled down to the room temperature in a normal path gradually.

4. Conclusions
The reactive hot pressing of powder mixtures of ZrC-B4C-Si, ZrC-B4CZrSi2,  ZrC-B-ZrSi2,  and  ZrB2-SiC-C  at  1850 ◦ C and 30 MPa for 4 min resulted  in  consolidated  ZrB2-SiC-C  ceramics  with  different  compositions.  The  samples  pressed  using  in  situ  reactions  exhibited  a  fine microstructure with an average grain size ranging from 1.2 to 1.7 µ m. The average grain size of the non-reactively pressed series decreases from 3.2 µ m to 1.6 µ m with increased graphite content.
When elemental silicon was used in the green bodies (ZrC-B4C-Si powder composition), the sintered composite displayed clear stratification. The internal region of the sample demonstrates (i) an elevated concentration of oxygen and carbon, (ii) a finer microstructure with an average grain size of a quarter of that of the outer region, (iii) a distinct interface bordering it from the outer region. The stratification interface was  (i)  porous,  (ii)  enriched  with  silicon  carbide  from  the  internal sample region, and (iii) enriched with zirconium diboride from the outer side. The stratification was not observed after the reactive hot-pressing of  ZrC-B4C-ZrSi2 and  ZrC-B-ZrSi2 precursor  mixtures  or  the  nonreactively sintered ZrB2-SiC-C powders.
The hardness of all the manufactured composites primarily depended on the graphite inclusion content, ranging from 20 GPa for the ZrB2-SiC composition, with about 0.8 vol% graphite, to 9 GPa for the material with 27.7 vol% C reactively sintered sample. The indentation toughness increased with the graphite content up to 5.4 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 .

Moreover, the addition of micronsized SiC particles to ZrB2 matrix, which dispread intergranularly along ZrB2/ZrB2 interfaces inhibited the grain growth of the ZrB2 grains [21]. A ZrB2 ( /C24 3 μ m) ceramic composite containing 10 vol% /uniFB01 ne α -SiC (0.8 m m), was fully densi /uniFB01 ed by hot pressing at 1900 1 C for 20 min under 40 -50 MPa. It was indicated that, introducing ultra /uniFB01 ne α -SiC particles was a key factor in enabling both the control of the diboride grain growth and the attainment of full density [17]. In another study, the in /uniFB02 uence of SiC particle size on the density of ZrB2 -SiC composites was investigated. ZrB2 -30 vol% SiC ceramic composites, using four different α -SiC powders with mean particle sizes ranging from 0.45 to 10 m m, were prepared by hot pressing at 1900 1 C for 45 min under 32 MPa. It was indicated that decreasing the average size of the starting SiC particles, from 10 to 0.45 m m, had improved the relative density of ZrB2 -SiC composites from 97.4 to 99.8% [22].

2.3. Pressureless sintering
(ZrB2 e SiC e B4C) composite was pressurelessly sintered with addition of YAG (Y2O3 þ Al2O3). The ZrB2 powder used in this work was synthesized by reacting ZrO2 with B4C according to reaction 7ZrO2 þ 5B4C / 7ZrB2 þ 5CO þ 3B2O3. To obtain a single phase ZrB2 without impurities like unreacted ZrO2, B4C, and free C, the excess of B4C was taken in wt. ratio of ZrO2/B4C ¼ 2.5 where the stoichiometric wt. ratio was ~3.0. The details of synthesis were reported else in Ref. [23]. The average particle size of the as-synthesized ZrB2 was < 1 m m ZrB2, SiC, Y2O3 and Al2O3 were taken in volume ratio of (ZrB 2 e 20 vol.% SiC e 8 vol.% B4C e 7 vol.% YAG).

2.2.1. Material preparation
Bulk YbB6 was prepared by hot pressing of borothermal reaction synthesized YbB6 powders. The starting materials for the synthesis of YbB6 powders are commercially available Yb2O3 powders (99% purity, -200 mesh, Huizhou Reagent Co., Ltd., Guangdong, China) and B4C powders (98% purity, -200 mesh, Mudanjiang Qianjin Reagent Co. Ltd., Heilongjiang, China). The as-received Yb2O3 and B4C powders were weighed according to the stoichiometric composition of YbB6 and ball-milled with ethyl alcohol in a polyurethane jar for 6 h. After drying in an oven and being sieved through a 120mesh screen, the Yb2O3/B4C powder mixture was heated to 1750 ◦ C and held for 2 h in a flowing argon atmosphere to synthesize phasepure YbB6 powders. More details on the synthesis temperature and reaction path were described elsewhere. question: In the Nayebi et al. 2016 hot pressing study, what temperature and pressure conditions were used to sinter the ZrB2-B4C composite?
answer: The Nayebi et al. 2016 hot pressing study used a temperature of 1800 °C and a pressure of 12 MPa to sinter the ZrB2-B4C composite [1].

chunks.text:
4. Conclusions
Porous ZrB2 -40 vol% B4C composite were fabricated via hot pressing at 1800 ° C for 30 min under a pressure of 12 MPa. The interfacial and microstructural characteristics of the composite were investigated by SEM, TEM and XRD analyses in order to understand the role of interfacial phenomena in progression of sintering process. The results revealed that the sintering process is controlled by a combination of interfacial phenomena including the formation of liquid phase at the interfaces, plastic deformation of the particles and the diffusion of carbon and/or boron atoms from B4C toward ZrB2 particles. Two types of porosity in the aforementioned composite and their roles in sintering process were also discussed. In addition, a summary of interfacial phenomena were depicted by a schematic model to simply present the investigation discussion.

1900 -2200 °C held for 1 -3 hours [7,6,16,17]. Conventional hot pressing of ZrB2-SiC powder mixtures at 30 MPa resulted in almost nonporous material only after 60 min at 1900 °C [5]. Spark plasma sintering of ZrB2-SiC mixtures allows slightly less demanding conditions with bulk composites obtained after only 5 min at 1900 °C and 20 MPa [4]. The latter sintering parameters are however still too high to avoid CNT degradation.
It is common practice to use sintering aids to decrease the processing temperature. To achieve full density while preserving SiC nanowhiskers, Wang and Wang [9] added Si3N4 and AlN to a ZrB2-SiCw matrix. A non-porous material was produced by SPS at 1550 °C and 40 MPa for 5 min. Aluminum nitride addition allowed Liang et al. [18] to densify the UHTC with conventional hot pressing at 1850 °C and 30 MPa for 30 min. Alumina and yttria addition allowed pressureless densi /uniFB01 cation of ZrB2-SiC at 1680 °C for 60 min [19]. Nickel and aluminum were successfully used for the same purpose by MelendezMartinez [20] and Mohammadpour [21]. However, while improving the manufacturing process parameters, the low-melting point sintering aids lead to a dramatic high-temperature strength degradation [20,1] and should if possible be avoided in UHTCs.
Reactive hot pressing (RHP) with the /uniFB01 nal composite phases forming via the chemical reactions between the initial low-melt components during the material densi /uniFB01 cation presents a possibility of essential process temperature and time reduction. The RHP also allows avoiding sintering aids.

4.2. Hot pressing
Density of 98% ρth was reported after hot pressing of HfB2 at 2100 °C for 45 min. Upon incorporation of MoSi2 as sinter-additive, the hot pressing temperature was reportedly reduced, with the highest sinter-density of 95% ρth was achieved for hot pressed TaB2-MoSi2 at 1680 °C for just 8 min under 30 MPa pressure [99,152]. Recently, Neuman et al. [168] reported achievement of almost near-theoretical sinter density for ZrB2-(5-40 vol%) B4C composites after hot pressing at 1900 °C for 30 min under pressure of 32 MPa. It was proposed that B4C enhanced densification by removing surface oxides from the starting powders and restricted the grain growth of ZrB2.
A careful look at Tables 4-7 indicates that the mechanical properties of di-boride ceramics processed via pressure assisted sintering techniques are relatively better when compared to those developed via pressureless sintering. Such differences are partly due to the finer microstructures, along with the slightly superior sinter-densities for the di-borides developed via pressure assisted sintering techniques. Chamberlin et al. [104] prepared high strength ZrB2-SiC ceramic composite via hot pressing at 1900 °C for 45 min and under uniaxial pressure of 32 MPa. They reported good combination of mechanical properties, such as flexure strength of Table 10

4.3. Reaction Processing
As an example, for developing TiB2-based ceramics via RHP, the use of 3Ti:B4C and 4.8Ti:B4C compositions, in combination with heating in one single step at a temperature of ~1700-1800 °C, resulted in the formation of only TiB2 and TiC0.5 phases, in addition to allowing achievement of near-theoretical sinter-density. The enhanced densification was attributed to possible plastic flow of TiCx grains under the processing conditions [159]. Platelet shaped TiB2 grains were observed upon sintering using the 4.8:1 composition, which was also associated with the improved mechanical properties (strength and fracture toughness). In another work, TiH2, BN and B4C mixtures with Ni led to the formation of highly dense TiB2 ceramics at 1850 °C [160]. The reactive hot pressing of attrition milled Zr-B and SiC powder mixtures with B4C addition resulted in ZrB2-SiC composites at 1650 °C [121]. Zhang et al. [108] developed ZrB2SiC ceramic composites from attrition milled Zr, Si and B4C starting powders via RHP under vacuum at 1900 °C for 1 h. The formation of nano-sized ZrB2 phase during attrition milling possibly also assisted in the densification. The ZrB2-based composites were reported to have fine grain structure and, accordingly, improved mechanical properties, as compared to similar composites developed via conventional HP. In another work, Wu et al. [109] reported achievement of sinter-density of ~97% for ZrB2-SiC-ZrC composite at 1800 °C with the use of Zr, Si and B4C as starting powders. It was hypothesized that the formation of ZrC helped to improve the sinterdensity of the composite at low temperature.

Abstract
ZrB2 -25 vol% SiC ceramic composites were prepared by hot pressing in order to investigate the effects of processing parameters and SiC particle size (20 nm, 200 nm and 5 m m) on the densi /uniFB01 cation behavior. At this study, an experimental design technique (Taguchi method) was used to specify the signi /uniFB01 cance of four factors and to obtain the optimal conditions. The statistical analysis distinguished the applied pressure as the most effective parameter on the relative density of ZrB2 -SiC composites. A relative density of /C24 100% was obtained at the optimal processing conditions: temperature of 1850 1 C, holding time of 90 min, pressure of 16 MPa and SiC particle size of 200 nm. The con /uniFB01 rmation test, performed under optimum conditions, indicated that the experimental outcomes were equal with the expected values from the Taguchi prediction. The mechanisms of enhanced densi /uniFB01 cation of hot pressed ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composites were discussed. & 2015 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
Keywords: A. Hot pressing; Densi /uniFB01 cation; Taguchi method; ZrB2 -SiC composite

question: What microhardness value was achieved for the ZS10C composite in Khoeini et al. 2015 when sintered at 2000°C for 2 hours?
answer: The microhardness value achieved for the ZS10C composite in Khoeini et al. 2015 when sintered at 2000°C for 2 hours was 4502 Hv [3].

3.4. Mechanical properties
The summery of obtained mechanical properties is presented in Table 2. Bar charts depicting the microhardness of the composites with different amounts of SiC at different temperature are shown in Figs. 6 and 7, respectively. It is expected that the microhardness of the investigated composites has been found to depend on their SiC content as well as temperature and duration of sintering, as is obvious from the results depicted in Figs. 7 and 8. The range of experimentally determined microhardness values as shown in Fig. 5 agrees well with those reported earlier in the published literature [33,34].
The results in Figs. 9 and 10 indicate that the microhardness scales with the SiC content of the composites, as expected. Examination of the results in Figs. 9 and 10 showed that the average values of microhardness obtained for the ZS10C composite sintered for 2 h at 1800 1 C and 2100 1 C are 3520 Hv and 3860 Hv, respectively, both of which are signi /uniFB01 cantly lower than the value recorded for the composite sintered for same duration at 2000 1 C (4502 Hv). These results suggest that the microhardness values scale with the amount of densi /uniFB01 cation, besides the SiC content, and therefore it is appropriate to infer that the optimum temperature for sintering in the present study is 2000 1 C.

3.4. Mechanical properties
Fig. 10. Bar charts depicting of relative microhardness of the sintered composites as function of the sintering temperature for the ZS10C composite.
As shown in Fig. 10, the average microhardness of the composites sintered for 2 h increases by 55.5%, as their SiC content is increased from1 to 10 wt%, which is attributed to the combined effect of higher hardness of SiC than that of the ZrB2 matrix, as well as decrease in ZrB2 grain size and increased densi /uniFB01 cation with increasing SiC content. The increase in ZrB2 grain size is also expected to cause increase in the amount of microcracking due to internal residual stresses generated during cooling from the processing temperature because of thermal expansion anisotropy between different crystallographic directions.
With decrease in the ZrB2 grain size and increase in the amount of densi /uniFB01 cation, the possibility of microcrack formation in the matrix during cooling is expected to be minimized, which is believed to be responsible for rise in the matrix microhardness. The results of earlier studies [34,35] have shown that the increase in grain size of the initial ZrB 2 particle size has a relatively insigni /uniFB01 cant effect on hardness of the ZrB2 -SiC composites. However, as the observed increase in the matrix grain size during sintering in the present study is E 2 -3 times more than that reported by Rezaie et al. [35] and Guo et al. [36], its in /uniFB02 uence on microhardness is also found to be more signi /uniFB01 cant.
In tune with the above discussion, the decrease in microhardness on sintering at 2000 1 C for durations beyond 2 h (as shown in Fig. 10) can be attributed to the increase in the average matrix grain size, as shown in Fig. 11. Similar explanation can also be proposed for lower microhardness observed for the composite samples sintered at 1800 -2100 1 C, which have lower relative density and larger average matrix grain size than the values recorded for the composite sintered at 2000 1 C.

3.1. Densi /uniFB01 cation and microstructure evolutions
The variations of relative density of the sintered samples (as function of the sintering temperature and SiC volume fraction) are shown in Table 1 and Fig. 2. Fig. 3 indicates that the optimum temperature to achieve maximum relative density by pressureless sintering is 2000 1 C, while the values of relative densities of the composites sintered at 1800 -2100 1 C have been found to be less. However, the results of an earlier study on ZrB2 (1 -10 wt%) SiC composites with 1 -2 wt% C have not shown signi /uniFB01 cant changes [24]. From the results shown in Fig. 5(b), the relative densities of the ZrB 2 -SiC composites are found to be about 99.52% of the corresponding theoretical calculated densities, while that of the ZBC is only /C24 88%. Moreover, the values of relative densities are found to increase in a steady state manner with increase in volume fraction of SiC. These observations suggest that processing of completely dense pure ZrB2 is very dif /uniFB01 cult, while the presence of SiC as reinforcement is signi /uniFB01 cantly bene /uniFB01 cial for promoting the densi /uniFB01 cation. In this study, maximum relative density of 99.65% has been achieved in ZS10C2 composite, which was sintered at 2000 1 C for 2 h after holding at 1650 1 C for 2 h. The values of relative densities obtained in this study are comparable with those of the pressure-less sintered ZrB2 -SiC composites, reported in previous studies [22,24].

3.1. Densi /uniFB01 cation and microstructure evolutions
Furthermore, it is interesting to note that the XRD spectrum (Fig. 4) from a point within the ZrB2 grain shows a peak of B4C in addition to those of Zr and B. This observation suggests that C is probably present forming solid solution, as has been reported in some of the earlier studies [19,22]. The locations appearing darker than SiC in Fig. 3 have been identi /uniFB01 ed as either B4C or C through EDX analyses. The SEM (BSE) images of ZS10C composites sintered at 2000 1 C for 2 h are shown in Fig. 5(a) -(d). As expected, the contrast of different phases or locations in these SEM (BSE) images depends on the average atomic numbers of the constituent elements.
Based on Fig. 5(a) and (b), the average matrix grain sizes in the ZS10C sample are about less than 10 m m. Fig. 6 shows the microstructure at higher magni /uniFB01 cation. The SEM (BSE) images in this /uniFB01 gure show the presence of an interfacial layer appearing relatively brighter than the ZrB2 grains which looks like forming a near-continuous network.

This observation con /uniFB01 rms that signi /uniFB01 cant grain growth occurs in these composites with increase in the temperature of sintering.

question: According to Nayebi et al. 2016, what liquid-phase compound forms at the sintering temperature of 1800°C in the ZrB2-B4C system?
answer: According to Nayebi et al. 2016, the liquid-phase compound that forms at the sintering temperature of 1800°C in the ZrB2-B4C system is diboron trioxide [1].

The formation of moss-like phases on the surface of B4C particles, particularly at the ZrB2/B4C interfaces, during the hot pressing process can be seen in Fig. 1b. Such semi-glassy phases have not been detected in the monolithic B4C ceramics [37]. In addition, it seems that these amorphous moss-like phases contain considerable crystalline nanoparticles. More careful investigation on the fracture surface of the B4C particle (marked zone in Fig. 1b) reveal a thin covering layer. Such a layer morphologically seems to be a solidi /uniFB01 ed phase on the B4C particle, which contains smaller agglomerated particles with deformed shapes. It appears that such transient layers can act as precursors for the formation of sintering interface. Hence, the sintering phenomenon may be conducted by a simultaneous interaction of these solidi /uniFB01 ed layers and the mentioned moss-like phases. EDS spectrum of the discussed mosslike phase showed high concentration level of zirconium and oxygen elements, which can be a sign for the presence/formation of ZrO2 and assistance of the sintering process. It should be noted that diboron trioxide is the only liquid-state phase which can be formed at sintering temperature of 1800 ° C in ZrB2 -B4C system and commonly solidi /uniFB01 es as amorphous phase [38]. As shown in Fig. 7, several dislocations are observed inside the ZrB2 grain. It seems that these dislocations are moving toward the grains boundaries. Accordingly, the ZrB2 grains can be plastically deformed during the hot pressing process due to the applied external pressure. A similar phenomenon has also been reported for ZrB2 -SiC system at temperatures around 1850 ° C [39,40].
Furthermore, the neck formation between the interfacial nanoparticles and ZrB2 matrix is clearly demonstrated in Fig. 7, which is an indication of progression of sintering process at the surface of particles. It is also notable that the neck formation is promoted by the amorphous phase (marked by black arrows), which was in liquid state during the sintering process. 3. Results and discussion
The affinity of the sintering aid with the metallic diborides influences the  arrangement  of  the  cation ' s  distribution  surrounding  B4C.  Fig.  8 shows the phase diagrams of the TMB2 and B4C. All the phase diagrams present a eutectic point with liquid phase formation by reaction between B4C  and  TMB2.  In  the  VB2-B4C  system,  the  eutectic  temperature  is ~2170 ◦ C,  followed  by  the  ZrB2-B4C  system  at  ~2200 ◦ C,  and  subsequently by TiB2- and HfB2-B4C systems at temperatures exceeding 2310 ◦ C. Moreover, one can observe that the eutectic liquid composition is higher in the concentration of VB2  (45 %) and ZrB2  (35 %) compared with HfB2 (22 %) and TiB2 (12 %). Finally, the solubility of V and Zr at the sintering temperature in the liquid is much larger than that of Hf and Ti as the liquidus line in the first two cases is shifted to the left side of the diagrams in Fig. 8. Hence, when the solidification starts, the liquid phase is V- and Zr-rich, which is held until the solidification is completed (i.e., at the eutectic temperature) thus crystallizing a Zr- and V-rich diboride in columnar grains close to the melted region. These diborides formed during the rapid solidification process preferentially grow on the edge of the molten region on the surface of the primary (not molten while sintering)  diborides  grains,  producing  the  so-called ' divorced  eutectic ' microstructure. These features are visible in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7. Notably, VB2 exhibits the lowest melting point among the diborides, suggesting the  potential  occurrence  of  partial  melting  around  the  sintering  aid particles during the sintering process.

All samples, ZSLO sintered at 1850 ◦ C, ZSLB sintered at 1750 ◦ C, and ZSGO sintered at 1800 ◦ C, attained above 99% of theoretical density in less than 5 min. Phase analysis confirmed that all starting materials remained unchanged after SPS and did not show any secondary crystalline phase analysed by XRD [13]. Fig. 1 shows typical bright-field TEM images of (a) ZSLO, (b) ZSLB and (c) ZSGO. Fig. 1(a) shows the distribution of SiC and ZrB2 grains in ZSLO. No grain boundary phases were observed. Previous study suggested that during liquid phase sintering of SiC, La2 O3 reacts with the surface SiO2 on SiC to form a lanthanum silicate phase [13]. Although we have no direct evidence for this in the areas analysed, it seems likely that at the high sintering temperature, ∼ 1800 ◦ C, used in the present study at least some of the RE oxide would have reacted with the surface SiO2 on SiC to form a liquid grain boundary silicate (RE2Si2O7) phase. Intergranular SiC grains were either 6H or 15R polytype and elongated ( ∼ 1-2 𝛍 m length and 0.5 𝛍 m diameter), whereas the intragranular SiC was cubic 3C polytype. question: In the Khoeini et al. 2015 study, how much did the microhardness of the composites increase as the SiC content was increased from 1 to 10 wt%?
answer: The microhardness of the composites increased by 55.5% as the SiC content was increased from 1 to 10 wt% [1].

3.4. Mechanical properties
Fig. 10. Bar charts depicting of relative microhardness of the sintered composites as function of the sintering temperature for the ZS10C composite.
As shown in Fig. 10, the average microhardness of the composites sintered for 2 h increases by 55.5%, as their SiC content is increased from1 to 10 wt%, which is attributed to the combined effect of higher hardness of SiC than that of the ZrB2 matrix, as well as decrease in ZrB2 grain size and increased densi /uniFB01 cation with increasing SiC content. With decrease in the ZrB2 grain size and increase in the amount of densi /uniFB01 cation, the possibility of microcrack formation in the matrix during cooling is expected to be minimized, which is believed to be responsible for rise in the matrix microhardness. The results of earlier studies [34,35] have shown that the increase in grain size of the initial ZrB 2 particle size has a relatively insigni /uniFB01 cant effect on hardness of the ZrB2 -SiC composites. However, as the observed increase in the matrix grain size during sintering in the present study is E 2 -3 times more than that reported by Rezaie et al. [35] and Guo et al. [36], its in /uniFB02 uence on microhardness is also found to be more signi /uniFB01 cant.
In tune with the above discussion, the decrease in microhardness on sintering at 2000 1 C for durations beyond 2 h (as shown in Fig. 10) can be attributed to the increase in the average matrix grain size, as shown in Fig. 11. Similar explanation can also be proposed for lower microhardness observed for the composite samples sintered at 1800 -2100 1 C, which have lower relative density and larger average matrix grain size than the values recorded for the composite sintered at 2000 1 C.

Abstract
The combined effects of SiC and carbon additives on the densi /uniFB01 cation, structure and characteristics of pressureless sintered ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composites were studied. The ZrB2 -SiC (1 -10 wt%) composite powders were mixed by 1 -2 wt% carbon. The prepared powder mixtures were then cold-consolidated and sintered in argon environment in the temperature range of 1800 -2100 1 C for 2 h. The constituted phases and microstructural evolutions were studied using x-ray diffraction and scanning electron microscope equipped with an energy dispersive x-ray detector. The obtained results concluded that the densi /uniFB01 cation was increased by extending the sintering time and also by prior holding of specimens at 1650 1 C. This caused by reduction of such oxide impurities of ZrO2, B2O3 and SiO2 on particle surfaces via the formation of new phases in the system. However, this was totally induced the enhancement of sintering phenomenon. The presence of SiC with average grain size smaller than that for ZrB2 revealed to aid the densi /uniFB01 cation by enhancing green specimen density. Furthermore, increment of the carbon content through erosion of milling media inhibited the matrix grain growth. Both of SiC and carbon additives seemed to enhance the reduction of oxides formation. The addition of SiC and carbon was found to be advantageous for densi /uniFB01 cation up to 99.5% of the theoretical density. According to the examinations, it was demonstrated that the specimen strength was varied with additions of SiC and carbon. Indentation toughness of the samples was examined and results exempli /uniFB01 ed toughness increment with SiC. However, carbon had a detrimental effect on fracture toughness. The microhardness values were increased with increasing SiC and carbon content due to the reduction of porosity volume percentage in specimens.
& 2015 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

A B S T R A C T
There are also several reports on ZrB2-based composites reinforced with  SiC  and  different  forms  of  carbon.  Azizian-Kalandaragh  et  al. produced ZrB2-based composites with a set content of SiC whiskers (25 vol%) and varying amount of carbon nanoparticles [28]. In this work, the effect of carbon addition on microstructure evolution and mechanical  properties  was  analysed.  The  hardness  results  decreased  with increasing C content, reaching 14.6 GPa for 7.5 wt% addition, while fracture toughness increased to 7.1 MPa*m 1/2 for 5 wt% addition. The creation  of  new  phases  such  as  ZrC  and  B4C,  as  well  as  whisker morphology were responsible for mechanical properties improvement. Asl et al. prepared ZrB2-SiC composites doped with 5 wt% graphite and investigated their microstructure and mechanical properties [29]. The average nanohardness values of 15.2, 18.3 and 10.7 GPa were measured for matrix, SiC and matrix-reinforcement interface, respectively. Although it is important to note that the mechanical properties were characterised  by  a  large  deviation.  This  research  was  continued  in another study of Asl et al., were hybrid composites with addition of graphite nanoflakes and silicon carbide whiskers were investigated for densification, microstructure and mechanical properties [30]. Obtained results revealed nearly fully dense sinters, with no new in-situ phases formed during consolidation in the SPS process. In addition, significant improvement in fracture toughness, to 6.2 MPa*m 1/2 ) was achieved for composites with 5 wt% graphite incorporation. Interesting research was conducted  by  Tripathi et al., where  effect of  carbonaceous  reinforcements  on  mechanical  properties  of  ZrB2-SiC  composites  was investigated  [31].  In  this  study,  multi-walled  carbon  nanotubes

4. Conclusion
The following conclusions could be drawn based on the study related to the pressureless sintering of ZrB2 -SiC composites with carbon additions added as graphite:
- 1- The use of carbon as sintering additive promotes densi /uniFB01 cation by reduction of the such oxide impurities comprising ZrO2 and SiO2 in the powder mixture used as raw material.
- 2- Sintering at 2000 1 C has been found to lead to achieve higher relative densities rather than that at either 1800 1 C or 2100 1 C.
- 3- Relative densities of sintered products are found to be
increased with the volume fraction increment of SiC particles. The presence of SiC was also advantageous for densi /uniFB01 cation during sintering. This was due to the reducing participates of B2O3 and SiO2 at the ZrB2 -SiC interfaces, which were restricted grain growth.
- 4- Microhardness of the composites was found to be increased with duration of sintering for 2 h depending on their composition due to increase in relative density of the investigated composites. With further increase in the temperature of sintering, hardness is found to decrease due to grain growth.
- 5- Values of IFT of the composites are found to be closer to those predicted by the rule of mixtures only for the composites containing higher SiC content. This observation has been at tribute to lowering of IFT with increased porosity.

A B S T R A C T
Dual phase (Hf,Ta,Ti,W,Zr)B2-(Hf,Ta,Ti,W,Zr)C high entropy ultra-high temperature ceramics were processed from commercial diboride and carbide powders with and without SiC additions. A 30-min hold at a maximum temperature of 1850 ◦ C in spark plasma sintering resulted in a relative density of ~95 %, which increased to ~100 % with the addition of 7.5 wt% SiC. The compositions did not reach chemical equilibrium under the given sintering  conditions  resulting  in  an  inhomogeneous  distribution  of  transition  metals  in  both  the  boride  and carbide phases. The addition of SiC improved densification by inhibiting grain growth, which reduced the grain size from 9.0 ± 4.2 μ m without SiC to 5.6 ± 2.5 μ m with SiC. The addition of SiC also improved both microhardness and fracture toughness. Microhardness at the maximum indentation load of 10 N increased from 20.3 ± 0.6 GPa without SiC to 22.6 ± 0.6 GPa with SiC, while indentation fracture toughness increased from 2.2 ± 0.3 MPa m1/2 to 4.0 ± 0.9 MPa m1/2. The room temperature thermal conductivity of the dual phase high entropy ceramic was ~15 W/(m.K) due to the high entropy effect which increased to ~22 W/(m.K) with the addition  of  SiC.  The  addition  of  SiC  enhanced  densification,  inhibited  grain  growth,  improved  mechanical properties, and increased thermal conductivity of the dual phase high entropy system.

question: What chemical reaction did Nayebi et al. 2016 propose occurs at the ZrB2/B4C interfaces to form submicron ZrB2 particles?
answer: Nayebi et al. 2016 proposed that the chemical reaction involving the formation of submicron ZrB2 particles at the ZrB2/B4C interfaces is related to the reaction between surface oxide layers and the existed free carbon in B4C starting powder [3]. This reaction leads to the formation of carbon monoxide, which may escape from the structure of the composite or be entrapped in the liquid amorphous layers, resulting in the formation of observed close-type nano-pores at ZrB2/B4C interfaces [3]. Additionally, the formed submicron ZrB2 particles can float in liquid boria, which may be a reason for some submicron ZrB2 particles moving toward regions other than interfaces of ZrB2/B4C grains [2].

chunks.text:
a b s t r a c t
The micro/nanostructural development, sintering process and its controlling mechanisms as well as the interfaces between ZrB2 and B4C particles were investigated using high resolution SEM, XRD and TEM analyses in a hot pressed ZrB2 -40 vol% B4C ceramic composite. Two types of porosities (open and closed) were observed in the microstructure of fabricated porous composite, which were caused by different factors. The results showed that the sintering process was encouraged by the formation of liquid phases at the interfaces, elimination of oxide impurities from the surfaces of ZrB2 particles (due to the chemical reactions with B4C particles and their free carbon), plastic deformation of ZrB2 grains and the diffusion of carbon and/or boron atoms from B4C toward ZrB2 particles. A mixture of nano-particles and nano-porosities at the submicron thickness amorphous ZrB2/B4C interfaces is detected and discussed in detail.

The formation of amorphous phases in vicinity of ZrB2 particle is clearly visible in the mentioned image (black marked areas). The ZrB2 particles not only join B4C particles via forming a nano-scale bonding layer, but also sinter each other very well to enhance the sinterability of the sample. Most of the ZrB2 grains are equiaxed with an average diameter of 2 m m which are similar to the starting powders. Hence, it appears that the sintering conditions have progressed in a way that the grain growth phenomenon was not occurred widely.
Some submicron ZrB2 particles (marked) are formed on the surface of the main zirconium diboride grains in a bed of morphologically amorphous phase. The formation of moss-like phases on the surface of B4C particles, particularly at the ZrB2/B4C interfaces, during the hot pressing process can be seen in Fig. 1b. Such semi-glassy phases have not been detected in the monolithic B4C ceramics [37]. In addition, it seems that these amorphous moss-like phases contain considerable crystalline nanoparticles. More careful investigation on the fracture surface of the B4C particle (marked zone in Fig. 1b) reveal a thin covering layer. Such a layer morphologically seems to be a solidi /uniFB01 ed phase on the B4C particle, which contains smaller agglomerated particles with deformed shapes. It appears that such transient layers can act as precursors for the formation of sintering interface. Hence, the sintering phenomenon may be conducted by a simultaneous interaction of these solidi /uniFB01 ed layers and the mentioned moss-like phases. EDS spectrum of the discussed mosslike phase showed high concentration level of zirconium and oxygen elements, which can be a sign for the presence/formation of ZrO2 and assistance of the sintering process. A ZrB2/ZrB2 grain boundary is presented in Fig. 8 (marked by white arrows) which seems to be completely clean of any impurity or secondary phases. The presence of surface oxide impurities (ZrO2 and B2O3) is known as the most important cause of unfavorable grain growth in monolithic ZrB2 ceramics [8,41]. A similar behavior has been reported for other diboride ceramics such as TiB2 and HfB2 [42 -44]. Therefore, the manipulations to eliminate such impurities may potentially decrease the risk of development of grain coarsening phenomenon. In the present case, chemical reactions (Eqs. (1) and (2)) participated in the removal of impurities from grain boundaries, resulted in inhabiting the extraordinary grain growth of ZrB2 during the hot pressing process.

question: According to Nayebi et al. 2016, what mechanisms control the sintering process in the hot-pressed ZrB2-B4C composite?
answer: The sintering process in the hot-pressed ZrB2-B4C composite is controlled by a combination of interfacial phenomena including the formation of liquid phase at the interfaces, plastic deformation of the particles, and the diffusion of carbon and/or boron atoms from B4C toward ZrB2 particles [2].

chunks.text:
a b s t r a c t
The micro/nanostructural development, sintering process and its controlling mechanisms as well as the interfaces between ZrB2 and B4C particles were investigated using high resolution SEM, XRD and TEM analyses in a hot pressed ZrB2 -40 vol% B4C ceramic composite. Two types of porosities (open and closed) were observed in the microstructure of fabricated porous composite, which were caused by different factors. The results showed that the sintering process was encouraged by the formation of liquid phases at the interfaces, elimination of oxide impurities from the surfaces of ZrB2 particles (due to the chemical reactions with B4C particles and their free carbon), plastic deformation of ZrB2 grains and the diffusion of carbon and/or boron atoms from B4C toward ZrB2 particles. A mixture of nano-particles and nano-porosities at the submicron thickness amorphous ZrB2/B4C interfaces is detected and discussed in detail.
& 2016 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

4. Conclusions
Porous ZrB2 -40 vol% B4C composite were fabricated via hot pressing at 1800 ° C for 30 min under a pressure of 12 MPa. The interfacial and microstructural characteristics of the composite were investigated by SEM, TEM and XRD analyses in order to understand the role of interfacial phenomena in progression of sintering process. The results revealed that the sintering process is controlled by a combination of interfacial phenomena including the formation of liquid phase at the interfaces, plastic deformation of the particles and the diffusion of carbon and/or boron atoms from B4C toward ZrB2 particles. Two types of porosity in the aforementioned composite and their roles in sintering process were also discussed. In addition, a summary of interfacial phenomena were depicted by a schematic model to simply present the investigation discussion.

a b s t r a c t
somewhat limited due to the challenges of poor sinterability, low self-diffusion coef /uniFB01 cients and strong covalent bonding. The fabrication of fully dense ZrB2 or B4C ceramics in the absence of speci /uniFB01 c additives or secondary phases generally requires complicated processes and high sintering temperatures of 4 2000 ° C through hot pressing process. However, the acquired products by this method have generally de /uniFB01 ciencies and drawbacks including the undesirable grain growth and low mechanical properties. Therefore, several researches have been explored to develop the advanced ZrB2 -SiC and B4C -SiC composites with appropriate and improved properties in recent years [10,14 -22]. The ability of SiC in constraining the surface oxide impurities of both ZrB2 and B4C is known as one of the major causes for enhancing the sinterability of these ceramics, which is due to the existence of carbon in the structure of silicon carbide and promoting the reactive sintering phenomenon [4,23 -29]. A similar effect can be possibly occurred by using any other carbon-containing compounds as a reinforcement phase in ZrB2-based ceramics and particularly in B4C [30,31].
Consequently, the possibility of improving the weaknesses of the ZrB2 -B4C counterpart systems can be considered as an interesting research area requiring further investigations by advanced methods and techniques. It has been reported that the addition of B4C into ZrB2 not only assists the sintering process in microwave heating process, but also promotes the densi /uniFB01 cation behavior in pressureless sintering technique [30]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are very limited research studies and publications on ZrB2 -B4C ceramic composites sintered by the hot pressing method. Therefore, it is an important issue to investigate the microstructure, sintering process, densi /uniFB01 cation mechanisms and mechanical/thermal properties of this system.

a b s t r a c t
He et al. reported the effect of ZrB2 contents on B4C -ZrB2 ceramics fabricated by hot pressing at 1950 ° C for 60 min under an applied pressure of 30 MPa. They found that the addition of ZrB2 not only considerably assists the sintering process and produces the fully dense samples, but also improves the mechanical properties of the hot pressed composites [10].
Although the presence of the porosity may play a negative role in case of mechanical properties for ZrB2-based composites as well as their oxidation resistance, it has been concluded that the thermal shock resistance of the composite can be signi /uniFB01 cantly enhanced due to the presence of deliberately formed porosities [32 -34]. From another point of view, porosities can lead to a lower density composite formation, which has probably a broader structural applications and in particular for ZrB2-based composites usages e.g . aerospace components. In the latter case, recent developments in surface treatments have grown opportunities to counteract the porosity-derived drawbacks in ZrB2-based materials. For example, researches have proved that laser treatments can form a glassy borosilicate phase [35] and a dense composite layer [36] on the surfaces of such porous composites. The formed dense layers can enhance the surface oxidation resistance of the designed synthesized material and also protect the inner regions of the composite from diffusing oxygen [35]. In addition, very important subsequent properties such as low thermal conductivity, controlled permeability, high surface area, high speci /uniFB01 c strength and low dielectric constant have been introduced as complementary advantages to the tailored porosities in the engineered ceramic materials [33].
We have recently reported the sintering process of porous ZrB2 -B4C binary ceramics which contained 0 -100 vol% B4C and hot pressed at 1800 ° C for 30 min under 12 MPa. SEM analysis of fracture surfaces showed that the sintering process controls by the formation of ZrO2 phases [37].

Comprehensive study on the effect of SiC and carbon additives on the pressureless sintering and microstructural and mechanical characteristics of new ultra-high temperature ZrB2 ceramics
In contrast to the liquid phase formers, reactive agents such as B4C, C, WC and VC combined with control of the sintering atmosphere can be used to promote densi /uniFB01 cation through solid state sintering by removal of surface oxides (ZrO2 and B2O3 for ZrB2) [19 -30]. In some studies [20 -24,29,30], it has been shown that it is possible to achieve relative density of 4 98% through pressureless sintering of ZrB2 or ZrB2 -SiC composites using B4C and C as additives. Furthermore, Zhang et al. [24] have demonstrated that it is possible to attain relative densities and mechanical properties comparable to those in the hot-pressed ZrB2 based composites by carrying out pressureless sintering of ZrB2 with 1 -10 wt% SiC reinforcement using B4C and graphite as additives. Although WC has been reported to contribute to the process of densi /uniFB01 cation of ZrB2 and ZrB2 -SiC composites [19,20,26,27], the mechanisms proposed by the different investigators appear to be quite varied. It has been shown that WC is relatively less effective in reduction of surface oxides than B4C and C [20] or VC in case of ZrB2 [26].

question: In Zapata-Solvas 2018, what were the calculated activation energies for the 20 vol% SiC-HfB2 (HS20) composite at different temperature ranges during compressive creep testing?
answer: The calculated activation energies for the 20 vol% SiC-HfB2 (HS20) composite during compressive creep testing were 242 kJ/mol in the temperature range of 1500 - 1800 °C and 334 kJ/mol in the temperature range of 1900 - 2000 °C [4].

chunks.text:
3.1. HS20 high temperature mechanical properties
2b shows activation energy versus SiC content calculated by Talmy et al. for ZrB2 in which the activation data for this study are included. Note that Talmy et al. ' s study is from 1200 to 1500 °C. Therefore, the activation energy used to make the comparison is the one obtained in this study in the lower temperature range studied (1500 -1800 °C). Good agreement between our estimated activation energy and the activation energies of Talmy et al. [27] is shown in Fig. 2b, suggesting that SiC content might have a stronger in /uniFB02 uence on the plastic response of MeB2 (Me = Zr, Hf) UHTCs than the kind of MeB2 analyzed. This statement is supported by the plastic response of 1 wt.% boron-doped SiC in Fig. 2b at 1758 °C, which is similar to the plastic response of HS20 at 1800 °C, more creep resistant than ZS30 at 1800 °C, and shows a strong in /uniFB02 uence of B in the high temperature behavior of SiC. Liquid-phase sintered SiC and additive-free nano β -SiC are more than 3 orders of magnitude more creep resistant than 1 wt.% B-doped SiC at the same temperature ( ∼ 1750 °C) [39,40,43], highlighting the enormous enhancement of creep rate or di /uniFB00 usion rate during plastic deformation by an addition of 1 wt.% B. There is a lack of data about plasticity of monolithic MeB2 to con /uniFB01 rm the role of SiC, but Melendez-Martinez et al. studied the plastic response under compressive creep of ZrB2 from 1400 to 1600 °C [44]. In addition, it was reported that at 1500 °C ZrB2 deforms at ∼ 10 -7 s -1 under 400 MPa, which is ∼ 300 times less resistant than HS20 reported herein in spite of ZrB2 having a porosity of 13.5 vol.% [44].

3.2. HS20 microstructural characterization after deformation
In a recent study, Bird et al. con /uniFB01 rmed the activation of grain boundary sliding during deformation of ZrB2-20 vol.% SiC (ZS20) as indentation misalignments were observed after deformation as a consequence of grain boundary sliding, i.e. grain rotation and translation [31]. However, the activation energies reported were 364 and 639 kJ/ mol for temperatures below 1600 °C or above 1600 °C [29], respectively, which are far higher than the ones reported by Talmy et al. [27], Gangirredy et al. [26] and this study, which are all in agreement. In fact, the creep rates reported by Bird et al. for ZS20 at 1800 °C are comparable to those reported for HS20 at 2000 °C in this study and similar to those reported for ZS30 at 1900 °C. This suggests that something is not being considered in Bird. et al. studies as their ZS20 should be more creep resistant than ZS30. It is noticeable in Fig. 15 of Bird et al. [29] that creep strain rate increases around 2 orders of magnitude from 1500 °C to 1550 °C under 100 MPa, while the di /uniFB00 erence in the creep strain rate at 100 MPa over all other temperatures shown is below an order of magnitude for a temperature di /uniFB00 erence of 100 °C. In addition, the experimental procedure shows that the powders were milled with WC-Co milling balls, which produced a WC-Co contamination of ∼ 2.3 wt.% [29]. Co melting point is ∼ 1500 °C highlighting how a relatively small amount of contamination can have a big impact on the high temperature creep response, which was not considered in the original study [29] or further microstructural characterization studies [31,50].

3.1. HS20 high temperature mechanical properties
Fig. 3 compares some of the previously reported data for the stationaries of plastic deformation on UHTCs. Studies at a strain rate below 10 -5 s -1 [27 -29] are not considered as they are out of the range of interest of hypersonic applications. HS20 is between 2 and 3 times more creep resistant than the ZS30 (ZrB2 + 30 vol.% SiC) studied by Gangireddy et al. [22]. The di /uniFB00 erent response could be attributed to the more refractory nature of HfB2 compared to ZrB2 or the lower SiC volume content of HS20 (20 vol.%) compared to ZS30 (30 vol.%). Talmy et al. studied the plastic response of ZrB2 with SiC contents ranging from 0 to 50 vol.% and observed a change in the activation energy from 130 to 509 kJ/mol, showing a plastic behavior characterized by a lower SiC content giving a higher creep resistance. However, highly pure SiC is known to be extremely creep resistant and hard to sinter also, if no impurities or additives are present. Liquid phase sintering (usually from melting of silica contamination) or boron and carbon doping are common approaches in SiC to promote full densi /uniFB01 cation [39 -42] and avoid the use of temperatures of ∼ 2200 °C required to sinter pure polycrystalline SiC by SPS [43]. For example, Fig. 2b reveals that additive-free nano β -SiC is 2 -3 orders of magnitude more creep resistant at 1800 °C than HS20 at 1500 °C. In addition, liquid phase sintered SiC at 1756 °C is 1 -2 orders of magnitude more creep resistant than HS20 at 1500 °C. Therefore, SiC is not playing any reinforcing role, or providing any improvement on the creep resistance, from a structural point of view which agrees with observations from Talmy et al. [27], who determined that the higher the SiC content, the higher the activation energy and the softer/lower the creep resistance. Fig.

3.1. HS20 high temperature mechanical properties
Fig. 1 shows the creep response of HS20 from 1500 °C to 2000 °C approximately over the strain rate range 2.8 × 10 -5 s -1 and 1.4 × 10 -4 s -1 for strains as high as 24%. At 1500 °C (Fig. 1a), HS20 shows a limited ductility (< 6%) for strain rates ε ˙ <6×10 -5 s -1 . At higher strain rates, the deformation is increased to nearly 12% and signi /uniFB01 cant softening is also observed. Deformation-induced softening could be related to either cavitation or phase instabilities. In addition, true steady states of deformation are only observed at 1900 °C or higher temperatures as softening is still active at 1800 °C. Stresses for the steady states of deformation were calculated through extrapolation of steady state to the initiation of the plastic regime at 1900 °C and 2000 °C, as plotted in Fig. 2a. Calculated stress exponents for the steady states of plastic deformation are shown in Fig. 2a, ranging from 3 to 1 as the temperature increases from 1500 °C to 2000 °C. In addition, calculated activation energies are 242 and 334 kJ/mol in the temperature ranges 1500 -1800 °C and 1900 -2000 °C respectively, suggesting the activation of di /uniFB00 erent deformation mechanisms as the stress exponent also changes from 3 to 1 in these same temperature ranges. The last deformation state in Fig. 1c and 1d is not a true stationary as some softening is observed. Therefore, the stress exponent for this last deformation state might not be accurate and this state is not considered for the plot of stationary strain rates versus stress, so the stress exponent shown in Fig. 2a is more accurate than the one displayed for the last deformation state in Fig. 1c and 1d respectively. In addition, no true stationaries were observed at 1800C either, but the data points were considered to allow comparison with previously reported data. The satisfactory agreement of the calculated activation energy value with literature data in Fig. 2b suggests that it was a reasonable assumption.

3.1. HS20 high temperature mechanical properties
Concerning the high temperature creep properties of MeB2 reinforced with MeC, there are a couple of studies in /uniFB02 exure for ZrB2-20 vol.% ZrC and TiB2-20 vol.% TiC in addition to other compositions with MeC contents in excess of 40 vol.%, which are far from the interest of this study [45,46]. ZrB2 + 20 vol.% ZrC deforms at 2000 °C in the stress range 5 -30 MPa with a strain rate range of 1.3-8.7 × 10 -3 s -1 respectively and a stress exponent of n=1, suggesting grain boundary sliding as the deformation mechanism. However, the ZrB2-20 vol.% ZrC is nearly 100 -150 times less creep resistant than the ZrB2-20 vol.% ZrC studied by Gangireddy et al. [26] and HS20 from this study. In addition, contamination during milling of up to 6 wt.% with WC-Co was reported, which could explain the poor creep response of ZrB2-20vol.% ZrC studied by Kats et al. [45] as Co melts at 1500 °C. This example illustrates how important it is to minimize contamination as undesired additional second phases can have a detrimental e /uniFB00 ect on physical properties at temperatures of 2000 °C or higher. Furthermore, monolithic ZrB2 and TiB2 were also studied at temperatures higher than 2000 °C [45,46]. However, if an extrapolation is made to 2000 °C, they are 100 times less creep resistant than ZS30 in the best case, which highlights the importance of controlling second phase impurities as well as the need to obtain reliable data on plasticity of monolithic MeB2.

question: What powder particle sizes and densities were used in Zapata-Solvas 2018 for the HfB2, SiC, and La2O3 starting materials in the HS20 and HS20La composites?
answer: The HfB2 powder used in Zapata-Solvas 2018 had a particle size (d 50) of 5.0 μm and a density of 10.5 g/cm³. The SiC powder had a particle size (d 50) of 0.7 μm and a density of 3.217 g/cm³. The La2O3 powder had a particle size (d 50) of 10 μm and a density of 6.51 g/cm³ [3].

chunks.text:
4. Discussion
XRD analysis indicated all starting materials remained in UHTC composites after SPS and new phases were not formed during sintering. Monolithic UHTCs had the largest grains (Table 3) owing to the higher temperature needed for SPS, 2000 ◦ C for ZrB2 instead of 1950 ◦ C required to sinter ZS20 and ZS20La, 2100 ◦ C for HfB2 instead of 2000 ◦ C for HS20 and HS20La, as well as the longer dwelling time. In addition, ZS20, ZS20La and HS20, HS20La had similar matrix grain sizes (4 𝛍 m and 6 𝛍 m) because of the similar temp/time profile used in processing. With regard to the secondary phase dispersion, hard agglomerates of SiC and La2O3 were not observed in ZrB2based UHTCs. However, in La-doped UHTCs SiC and La2O3 particles tended to remain in close proximity as expected when two particles of similar but small size are mixed with a larger one. The presence of SiC hard agglomerates (20-30 𝛍 m) in HfB2based UHTCs is attributed to the density difference between HfB2 (10.5 g/cm 3 ) and SiC (3.217 g/cm 3 ) which is double than in ZrB2-based UHTCs and makes SiC float in the ethanol solution during thermo-rotary drying. Use of higher solution viscosity or addition of a dispersant to avoid the presence of strong agglomerates after sintering is worthy of investigation.

2. Experimental procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ =6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ =6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (ZS20), ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (ZS20La), HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described previously. 10 The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs, as shown in previous work 10 and Fig. 1, are; (I) high density (>99% of theoretical relative density); (II) ZrB2-based UHTCs contain a homogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the ZrB2 matrix with SiC grain sizes between ∼ 1 and 2 𝛍 m while HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 𝛍 m; (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles.

2. Experimental procedure
HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 μ m, ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( α -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 μ m, ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 μ m, ρ =6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described previously [10]. The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs [11,19] are; (I) high density (> 99% of theoretical relative density); (II) HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 μ m; and (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles and improve the SiC dispersion throughout the HfB2 matrix.

2. Experimental procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma-Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ = 10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma-Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ = 3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma-Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; ZrB2, ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (ZS20), ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (ZS20La), HfB2, HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described in a previous work. 22 The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs, previously described, 22 are; (I) high density (>99% of theoretical relative density) except monolithic ZrB2 which has a density ∼ 95% as a consequence of O impurities which prevent its full densification; (II) ZrB2-based UHTCs contain a homogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the ZrB2 matrix with SiC grain sizes between ∼ 1 and 2 𝛍 m while HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 𝛍 m; (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles.

2.1. Sintering procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ = 10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ = 3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions. ZrB2, HfB2 and 𝛂 -SiC (6H) have a hexagonal structure with lattice parameters of a = 3.17 ˚ A, c = 3.53 ˚ A, a = 3.139 ˚ A, c = 3.473 ˚ A and a = 3.08 ˚ A, c = 15.12 ˚ A, respectively. Powders with the following compositions were prepared, ZrB2, ZrB2+ 20 vol.% SiC (hereafter termed ZS20), ZrB2+ 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (hereafter termed ZS20La), HfB2, HfB2+ 20 vol.% SiC (hereafter termed HS20), HfB2+ 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (hereafter termed HS20La). They were wet ball milled in plastic containers using ZrO2 balls in ethanol for nearly 24 h and dried using a rotary evaporator under vacuum. Then, they were sintered in a spark plasma sintering (SPS) furnace (Type HP D 25, FCT systeme GmbH, Rauenstein, Germany). A 40 mm graphite die was used and covered with graphite felt to reduce heat loss, the temperature was monitored and controlled by an optical pyrometer.

ceramics that show constant thermal conductivity values at high temperatures (1500-1900 °C), when compared to only SiC reinforced di-borides (Fig. 25). Such results are beneficial towards simplifying the design of the components for UHTC applications over this temperature range. It can also be observed that the 20 vol% SiC reinforced ZrB2(ZS20) shows a lower reduction of thermal conductivity than 20 vol% SiC reinforced HfB2(HS20). In a different work, Gasch et al. [365] reported that the thermal conductivity of unreinforced HfB2 (consolidated by SPS) decreases with an increase in temperature from 107 Wm -1 K -1 at 25 °C to 91 Wm -1 K -1 at 600 °C. Similar trend was observed by Wuchina et al. [270] and Loehman et al. [58] for hot pressed HfB2. Even with respect to composites, as evident from the work by Gasch et al. [27,366], thermal conductivities of HfB2-based composites containing 20 vol% SiC, 5 vol% TaSi2 and 0.5 vol% Ir, consolidated via hot pressing, as well as spark plasma sintering, strongly depend on grain size and porosity. Materials with smaller grain size possess greater area fraction of interface, which act as phonon scattering centres and lead to the higher thermal resistances [32,367]. Thermal conductivity of ZrB2 depends on volume fractions of porosity and get reduced with increasing porosity [368]. The same also happens with reduction in grain size. In another work, ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composites have been reported to possess high thermal conductivity. This increment was the function of electron configuration in ZrB2 matrix and phonon contribution in covalently bonded SiC phase [369].

1. Introduction
Ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are those with extremely high melting points that give them a potential advantage for use in thermal protection systems or refractory applications. In particular, zirconium and hafnium diborides and carbides have melting points in excess of 3000 ◦ C. 1 In addition, diborides have higher thermal conductivity than carbides which make them promising candidates in thermal protection systems for hypersonic applications. The combination of high temperature capability and high thermal conductivity is particularly beneficial. However, ZrB2 and HfB2 baseline UHTCs possess insufficient oxidation resistance for hypersonic flight environments, in which temperatures above 2000 ◦ C in highly oxidizing atmospheres are reached. Most research to date has focused on developing more oxidation-resistant UHTCs composites. 2-5 The best oxidation resistance has been found for UHTCs containing SiC, typically additions between 10 and

1. Introduction
Subcritical or slow crack growth has been established as responsible for fracture of UHTCs in flexion at high temperatures ( T ≤ 1500 ◦ C), 23 but still too low to produce plastic deformation. Flexural strength at high temperatures is enhanced by SiC 13,24 and MoSi2 25 additions; however there have been few experimental studies of UHTC high temperature strength due in large part to the practical difficulties of mechanical testing at temperatures >1600 ◦ C.

1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is gaining substantial interest due to its high melting point (3245 °C), good oxidation resistance from room temperature to 2500 °C, low theoretical density, high thermal conductivity and ability to retain strength at elevated temperature. Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) exhibits the intriguing combination of both metal-like and ceramic-like properties such as high melting point (3245 °C), low coe /uniFB03 cient of thermal expansion, high thermal conductivity, low theoretical density and ability to retain strength at elevated temperature [1]. These properties enable ZrB2 to survive extreme temperatures, chemical attack, erosive environments and heat /uniFB02 uxes, which makes it a potential candidate for hypersonic vehicles and Reusable Launch Vehicles (RLVs). ZrB2 exhibit hexagonal close pack arrangement with a strong covalent bond between B -B atoms and Zr -B atoms and the close-packed metallic layer (Zr -Zr) exhibit metallic bond characteristics like high thermal conductivity, high electrical conductivity [2]. The properties of the ZrB2 ceramic generally depend on the pyrolysis temperature and the method employed for its synthesis. Most common methods employed for the synthesis of ZrB2 is by using zirconium oxide (ZrO2), diboron trioxide (B2O3) and carbon (carbothermal reduction), ZrO2 and boron carbide (B4C) (boron carbide reduction) and ZrO2, B4C and carbon (combined reduction) [3,4] and the reactions involved are given in equations (1) -(3). Synthesis of ZrB2 with ZrO2 as the starting material is studied extensively due to low cost and easy availability of zirconium oxide, but pure ZrB2 powders are
