question: In the Makurunje et al. 2017 study of Cf/C-SiC-TiC-TaC composites, what was the nominal heat flux and duration of the oxyacetylene flame test?
answer: In the Makurunje et al. 2017 study of Cf/C-SiC-TiC-TaC composites, the nominal heat flux was 4 MW/m² and the duration of the oxyacetylene flame test was 7.5 s [1].

chunks.text:
2.3. Oxyacetylene flame test: set-up, execution and characterisations
The ablation resistance of the Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composites was determined by the oxyacetylene flame test according to the ASTM E285-80 (2002) standard test method for oxyacetylene ablation testing of thermal insulation materials. The setup is shown in Fig. 1: discs of the Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composites were exposed up to 7.5 s to a flame having a nominal heat flux of 4 MW/m 2 . The heat flux was achieved by attenuating the gas supply to have conditions presented in Table 1. The heat flux was calculated using real-time temperature data collected on a multi-channel data logger (PicoLog 1216) every 175 ms of exposure to the flame. The setup allows to measure the temperature of the specimen using a K-type thermocouple bonded to the rear end of a copper slug connected to a ceramic tube and copper plate at the back face of the specimen of pre-measured mass.
The 3D surface profile of the ablated discs was imaged using the Bruker Contour GT-K 3D optical microscope: the maximum depth determined was used to calculate the linear ablation rate ( R l ) using standard formula. The compounds formed from the burning of the Cf /C-STT composite were characterised by XRD and SEM (mod. EVO60 EP ZEISS).
Fig. 2. The Cf /C (upper row) and Cf /C-SiC-TiC-TaC (lower row) composites oxidised at 1873 K up to 480 s upon air-quenching.
Fig. 3. Mass loss ( /Delta1 m) normalised to initial mass (m0) vs. offset exposure time (t*) of Cf /C and Cf /C-SiC-TiC-TaC (STT) composites in air oxidised at 1873 K up to 480 s: dashed lines connect average values.

a b s t r a c t
The ablation/oxidation resistance of a carbon fibre (Cf)/carbon matrix (C)-SiC-TiC-TaC ceramic matrix composite (CMC) produced by melt infiltration of alloy into a Cf/C preform and tested in severely oxidising conditions was quantitatively determined and discussed. An oxyacetylene flame shot of 7.5 s (4 MW/m 2 nominal heat flux), as well as oxidising conditions imposed by a radiant furnace in air at 1873 K up to 480 s were the selected testing conditions. Detailed post-test microstructure investigations of the oxidised/ablated infiltrated CMC samples, compared to unprotected CMCs tested in nominally identical conditions, enabled to establish an increase in ablation/oxidation resistance of one order of magnitude. The occurrence of a self-generating protective high-temperature glass-ceramic, disclosed by microstructure analyses, played a substantial role for that performance jump during oxidation/ablation. The Cf/C-SiC-TiC-TaC composite herein tested can be a valuable candidate for uses in severe aerospace applications (propulsion and hypersonic flight).

3.1. Oxidation test parameters
The oxyacetylene torch test is one that is gaining commonality in use, but is not completely characterized. Sample temperature is dictated by sample composition (its heat capacity and thermal conductivity), the sample distance from the flame, and the oxygen to acetylene ratio. In addition, the oxyacetylene flame environment is complicated by the presence of carbon species that depend on the flame chemistry. 24,25 The heat flux can be measured by calorimetry. When the flow rates, gas ratio and distance to the flame are set, the heat flux as measured by the calorimetry is fixed. The heat flux was measured to be 880 W cm -2 at 25 mm from the torch exit using an acetylene to oxygen ratio of 1:1.35 with flow rates of 0.8 m 3 h -1 and 1.1 m 3 h -1 respectively.
Fig. 1. (a) Photograph of the torch heating process. The sample is moved in manually from a position out of the flame to a position 25 mm from the flame. A is the water-cooled graphite holder, B is the C/C insert to hold the sample in place and C is the sample. (b) Plot of the measured pyrometer temperature readings during oxyacetylene torch testing of the HfB2-based samples. Temperature was measured at the center of the front face of the sample and is plotted from the beginning of the test until the torch was extinguished. The average temperature was calculated from the last 60 s of exposure.

3.3. Oxyacetylene ablation of C/C-MeC-SiC composites
Fig. 10a-f illustrate temperature, velocity, and species contours for C/C composites during ablation, and the specific numerical model is shown  in  Fig.  10i.  Under  the  action  of  high  velocity  air  flow,  the oxyacetylene flame is radial, with  a  maximum  flame  temperature of 3100 ℃ and a maximum velocity of 212 m/s. In terms of atmosphere, the acetylene (~42 %) and oxygen content (~21 %) are the highest in the central region of the specimen, and at the same time, there is a high amount  of  H2O  (~0.1 %)  and  CO2  (~0.49 %)  in  the  center  of  the specimen. The differences in the flow field temperature, velocity and atmosphere during the ablation process of different systems are small, but there is a significant difference in the wall heat flux density. Further, the surface peak heat flux density of the MeC-SiC ceramic layer during the  30 s  ablation  process  was  calculated  by  the  two-way  flow-solid coupling, as shown in Fig. 10g-h. During the 0 -1 s process, the peak heat flux  is  similar  for  each  system,  which  is  about  4.21 MW/m 2 ,  further demonstrating that the heat flux density in the oxyacetylene ablation criterion is cold-wall heat flux. With the prolongation of the ablation time, there is a clear difference between the systems, and the peak heat flux densities are, in descending order, WC-SiC (1.8 MW/m 2 ) ＞ NbC-SiC ＞ TiC-SiC ＞ TaC-SiC ＞ HfC-SiC ＞ ZrC-SiC ＞ C/C(1.3 MW/m 2 ), with a maximum difference of 0.5 MW/m 2 between WC-SiC and ZrC-SiC.

Journal of the European Ceramic Society
Very recently, Nisar et al., [16] studied the performances of spark plasma sintered massive TaC-SiC composites under plasma arc-jet exposure, and recognised that the protective silica (coming from the SiC reinforcement) provided an extended stability domain for TaC, and therefore a significant improvement in the oxidation resistance.
Apart from the TaC and SiC phases, the application of another refractory compound such as TiC (melting point of 3340 K) in Cf /C composites for oxidation protection has very limited exposure in literature. The use of Ti in ultra-high temperature environments has been through MAX phase composites and titanium matrix composites (mainly in intermetallic form). TiO2 is expected to provide oxidation protection to Cf /C composites because of its melting point of 2123 K, as well as low diffusion coefficient of oxygen in the same. In addition, TiO2 in contact with Ta2O5 at temperatures above 1923 K, according to their binary phase diagram [17], can act as an additional fluid high temperature resistant glassy oxide medium able to support self-repairing mechanisms.
In this investigation the oxidation resistance in air at 1873 K up to 480 s nominal exposure time of a ternary ceramic carbides matrix containing SiC and (Ta,Ti)-mixed carbides was studied and compared to that of the (unprotected) Cf /C composite. The ablation/oxidation behaviour was more thoroughly investigated by exposing the Cf /C-SiC-TiC-TaC composite to an oxyacetylene torch flame characterised by 4 MW/m 2 of nominal heat flux.

question: What were the temperature and maximum exposure time used in the oxidation testing of Cf/C and Cf/C-STT composites by Makurunje et al. 2017?
answer: The temperature used in the oxidation testing of Cf/C and Cf/C-STT composites by Makurunje et al. 2017 was 1873 K, and the maximum exposure time was 480 s [1].

chunks.text:
3.1. C f /C and C f /C-STT composites oxidised at 1873 K in air
The visual appearance of the Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composite discs after oxidation is displayed in Fig. 2. The beneficial influence of the addition of both SiC and (Ta,Ti)C in the matrix is more obvious in Fig. 3. Compared to the Cf /C-STT composites, the Cf /C composites suffered a severe mass loss as shown in the net mass loss vs (offset) exposure time plotted in Fig. 3. In the case of the Cf /C-STT composites a net mass loss ( /Delta1 m) about one order of magnitude lower than that of Cf /C composites was systematically measured. The major efficacy of the presence of SiC and (Ta,Ti)C started taking place after about a nominal duration of 240 s. In fact, the mass loss rate dominated by the consumption of carbon (fibre and matrix) switched into a mass gain regime. In addition, the visual inspection of the oxidised discs (Fig. 2), together with the microstructure analyses by XRD (surface) and FESEM-EDX (surface and cross section), confirmed that an in-situ formed glass started spreading out actively during the longest nominal exposure of 480 s, providing effective protection to the underlying composite's bulk.
It should be stressed that the net mass change a material experiences when subjected to an oxidising environment, although useful for figure-of-merit, is incomplete unless fully described by oxida- tion mechanisms and kinetics, especially when a significant release of volatile by-products is involved. Furthermore, while ultra-fast heating rates and effective duration of the oxidation tests up to 10 min may, to some extent, represent conditions of a re-entry, they are not adequate to carefully investigate oxidation mechanisms and build predictive models.

The 3D surface profile of the ablated discs was imaged using the Bruker Contour GT-K 3D optical microscope: the maximum depth determined was used to calculate the linear ablation rate ( R l ) using standard formula. Mixed oxides of Ti and Ta evolved in the silica-based surface (Gl): bright (B) and grey (G) features in c) correspond to (Ta,Ti)2O5 and (Ti,Ta)O2 mixed oxide. Nano-precipitates decorating the dark grey silica melt (Gl) are visible in Fig. 12c.

3.1. C f /C and C f /C-STT composites oxidised at 1873 K in air
3.2. Polished cross-sections of Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composites oxidised at 1873 K in air
The body of analyses presented in section 3.1 highlighted major microstructural changes which occurred at the external oxidised surfaces of Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composites. This section highlights instead the permanent effects produced by the oxidation attack through the thickness of the discs tested in air at 1873 K.
Fig. 8. FESEM micrographs using secondary (SE) and back-scattered (BSE) mode, from the external surfaces of Cf /C-STT discs oxidised for increasing nominal exposure.
On one hand, a couple of FESEM micrographs in Fig. 9 show relevant portions of the as-produced Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composites. On the other hand, the FESEM micrographs in Fig. 10 emphasise the physical mechanisms which led the Cf /C composites to severely lose mass (see Fig. 2). In Fig. 10 the typical diameter of single fibres (C f ) inside a bundle and the micro-porosity of the carbon matrix (C) in the as-sintered Cf /C composite are shown. A key step, applicable also to the Cf /C-STT composite, was the accessibility of oxygen diffusing through the open spaces between the carbon fibre bundles. Taking the Cf /C composite disc oxidised for 240 s as an example, the FESEM micrograph in Fig. 10c shows the simultaneous presence of carbon fibre bundles still compact and mildly attacked, compared to adjacent ones unravelled and heavily consumed. The diffusion of oxygen drives the consumption of the carbon fibres: after 240 s of exposure to air at 1873 K (see Fig. 10d) the portions of the Cf /C disc closer to the exposed outer surface of the composite suffered a more extensive consumption, i.e. larger mass loss.

3.1. C f /C and C f /C-STT composites oxidised at 1873 K in air
The distribution of the principal phases constituting the Cf /CSTT composite is presented in Fig. 6. In addition, the in-situ reactive melt formed (Ta,Ti) carbides are also more finely distributed as interlayer at the Cf -SiC interfaces.
The XRD patterns collected on a wider surface of the oxidised discs (C f /C and Cf /C-STT) provided insight into the microstructure changes occurring at the sub-surfaces with increasing exposure time. As such, the outputs of the XRD analyses are not necessarily representative the bulk composite, and this is specifically addressed in Section 3.2. The series of XRD patterns in Fig. 7 repre- sent the onset, evolution and crystallisation of the oxide products upon fast quenching from the oxidation tests. The retarded coverage by glassy coating, for instance, exposed some bundles of carbon fibres to be easily attacked by oxygen. The XRD interpretation in Fig. 7 and the mass change trend in Fig. 3 nicely agree. In fact, when the net mass loss prevails for exposure up to 240 s the Cf + carbon matrix peak intensity increases because generated by larger volumes of the Cf + carbon matrix increasingly depleted of SiC and (Ta,Ti)C formerly present. However, as soon as the Cf /C-STT composite enters into an overall (protective) mass gain regime (see Fig. 3), the Cf + carbon matrix peak intensity drastically drops down because the fibre bundles start being more extensively covered by a protective glassy product whose signature with a well-defined bump was identified (see Fig. 7e).
Fig. 6. A representative polished area (micrograph by SE-FESEM) from the polished cross-section of the as-produced Cf /C-STT composite. Four distinct grey levels differentiate the primary phases: (Ta,Ti)C (1, bright), SiC (2, light grey), fibres bundle (3, darker grey) and porosity (black).

question: What were the bulk densities of the Cf/C and Cf/C-STT composites reported by Makurunje et al. 2017?
answer: The bulk densities of the Cf/C and Cf/C-STT composites reported by Makurunje et al. 2017 were 1.54 g/cm³ and 2.83 g/cm³, respectively [1].

2.1. Materials and their characterisations
The Cf /C-SiC-TiC-TaC composite, henceforth labelled Cf /C-STT composite, was prepared from a Cf /C composite preform obtained by three iterative cycles of phenolic resin (SI Group, South Africa) impregnation of a 2/2 twill carbon cloth (ATM Composites, South Africa) by vacuum bagging, cured in air at 373 K for 8 h and pyrolysed in flowing Argon at 1273 K for 1 h.
A 66.7Si-17.9Ti-15.4Ta alloy powder ( -40 𝛍 m particle size) was brought into contact with the Cf /C composite, and the two were heated in a spark plasma sintering (SPS) furnace in a vacuum range of 10-100 Pa up to 1973 K using a graphite crucible. The alloy, prepared from TaSi2 (1-5 𝛍 m nominal particle size) and TiSi2 ( -40 𝛍 m nominal particle size) powders (Alfa Aesar, South Africa), was arcmelted from a mixture of 67 wt.% and 33 wt.%, respectively.
The conditions actuated the melt infiltration of the alloy in the pores (21 v.%) of the Cf /C composite and the reaction of the alloy constituents with the carbon matrix in the preform to give the Cf /C-STT composite. The graphite crucible used was hBN-coated on the inside to prevent the reactive interference of the graphite with the alloy and the Cf /C preform. Discs were cut out of the prepared composites and had dimensions ( ± 0.2 mm) of 20 mm diameter and 3 mm thickness. The Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composites had densities of 1.54 g/cm 3 and 2.83 g/cm 3 , respectively.

question: To what maximum temperature was the Cf/C composite heated during spark plasma sintering in the Makurunje et al. 2017 study?
answer: The Cf/C composite was heated up to 1973 K during spark plasma sintering in the Makurunje et al. 2017 study [3].

chunks.text:
4. Conclusions
Spark plasma sintering processing was used to produce dense short /uniFB01 bre-reinforced ceramic composites based on ZrB2 with a carbon /uniFB01 bres content of 45 vol%. The relative density of 96.7%, achieved for samples sintered at 1900 °C, is signi /uniFB01 cantly higher than analogous specimens produced by conventional hot pressing, which achieved only 85% of the theoretical density. Careful control of the sintering parameters, such as the heating rate, maximum temperature and holding time resulted in materials with near full density and no appreciable damage of the carbon /uniFB01 bres.
- It has also been assessed that above a critical temperature (> 1900 °C), set by the eutectic melt met in the ZrB2-SiC-C system, extreme /uniFB01 bre degradation occurs, with associated brittle behaviour of the composite. It has also been observed that slight deviations in the density level have a strong impact on the fracture behaviour.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx. doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2017.05.029.

Journal of the European Ceramic Society
Very recently, Nisar et al., [16] studied the performances of spark plasma sintered massive TaC-SiC composites under plasma arc-jet exposure, and recognised that the protective silica (coming from the SiC reinforcement) provided an extended stability domain for TaC, and therefore a significant improvement in the oxidation resistance.
Apart from the TaC and SiC phases, the application of another refractory compound such as TiC (melting point of 3340 K) in Cf /C composites for oxidation protection has very limited exposure in literature. The use of Ti in ultra-high temperature environments has been through MAX phase composites and titanium matrix composites (mainly in intermetallic form). TiO2 is expected to provide oxidation protection to Cf /C composites because of its melting point of 2123 K, as well as low diffusion coefficient of oxygen in the same. In addition, TiO2 in contact with Ta2O5 at temperatures above 1923 K, according to their binary phase diagram [17], can act as an additional fluid high temperature resistant glassy oxide medium able to support self-repairing mechanisms.
In this investigation the oxidation resistance in air at 1873 K up to 480 s nominal exposure time of a ternary ceramic carbides matrix containing SiC and (Ta,Ti)-mixed carbides was studied and compared to that of the (unprotected) Cf /C composite. The ablation/oxidation behaviour was more thoroughly investigated by exposing the Cf /C-SiC-TiC-TaC composite to an oxyacetylene torch flame characterised by 4 MW/m 2 of nominal heat flux.

2.1. Materials and their characterisations
The Cf /C-SiC-TiC-TaC composite, henceforth labelled Cf /C-STT composite, was prepared from a Cf /C composite preform obtained by three iterative cycles of phenolic resin (SI Group, South Africa) impregnation of a 2/2 twill carbon cloth (ATM Composites, South Africa) by vacuum bagging, cured in air at 373 K for 8 h and pyrolysed in flowing Argon at 1273 K for 1 h.
A 66.7Si-17.9Ti-15.4Ta alloy powder ( -40 𝛍 m particle size) was brought into contact with the Cf /C composite, and the two were heated in a spark plasma sintering (SPS) furnace in a vacuum range of 10-100 Pa up to 1973 K using a graphite crucible. The alloy, prepared from TaSi2 (1-5 𝛍 m nominal particle size) and TiSi2 ( -40 𝛍 m nominal particle size) powders (Alfa Aesar, South Africa), was arcmelted from a mixture of 67 wt.% and 33 wt.%, respectively.
The conditions actuated the melt infiltration of the alloy in the pores (21 v.%) of the Cf /C composite and the reaction of the alloy constituents with the carbon matrix in the preform to give the Cf /C-STT composite. The graphite crucible used was hBN-coated on the inside to prevent the reactive interference of the graphite with the alloy and the Cf /C preform. Discs were cut out of the prepared composites and had dimensions ( ± 0.2 mm) of 20 mm diameter and 3 mm thickness. The Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composites had densities of 1.54 g/cm 3 and 2.83 g/cm 3 , respectively.

3.4. Experimental verification
thermal conductivity graphite Cf-h (3 mm) were added to the optimized ceramic formulation and treated at 2000 ◦ C for 20 min. The above formulations were prepared by spark plasma sintering: initially, the raw materials were mixed in a planetary ball mill for 60 min at 500 rpm and dried (in ethanol medium). Subsequently, the materials were placed in graphite molds and heated at a rate of 100 ◦ C min GLYPH<0> 1 to 600 ◦ C, then ramped up to 1900 ◦ C with 50 ◦ C min GLYPH<0> 1 , held for 15 min at this temperature (or 35 min for some samples), and finally cooled with the furnace (pressure of 30 MPa). The XRD patterns of the harvested composites are shown in Fig. 11. The harvested composites are shown in the figure to have a single-phase solid solution matrix with an FCC structure.

a b s t r a c t
There is an impressive number of scienti /uniFB01 c papers exploring the resistance of Cf/C-UHTC composites to high-temperature oxidation and ablation. Mainly, /uniFB01 ring tests are carried out using oxygenacetylene burners [7,8,11 e 14,16 e 19,21,22] at working temperatures of 2200 /C14 C [7,12], 2500 /C14 C [11,19], and even 2700 /C14 C [14,16]. In one study, the authors reported heating the composite to 3000 /C14 C during /uniFB01 ring tests by using a continuous wave CO2 laser [17]. In other studies, gas-dynamic testing was performed on varying types of plasma torches [9,20,23,24]. In one of the studies, the inner lining of the combustion chamber of a solid-fuel rocket engine made of Cf/ C-UHTC composite was tested in ablation mode while the engine was operating [15].
The purpose of this work is to obtain a carbon-containing composite with a matrix based on hetero-phase carbide-boride UHTC with minimum SiC content and to study its resistance to high-temperature oxidation and ablation in a high-speed, highenthalpy air plasma /uniFB02 ow.

question: What were the measured mass ablation rates for Cf/C and Cf/C-STT composites exposed to oxyacetylene flame for 7.5 s according to Makurunje et al. 2017?
answer: The measured mass ablation rate (Rm) for Cf/C composite exposed to oxyacetylene flame for 7.5 s was 40.2 ± 0.1 mg/s [1]. For Cf/C-STT composite, the measured mass ablation rate (Rm) under the same conditions was 3.9 ± 0.1 mg/s [1].

2.3. Oxyacetylene flame test: set-up, execution and characterisations
The ablation resistance of the Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composites was determined by the oxyacetylene flame test according to the ASTM E285-80 (2002) standard test method for oxyacetylene ablation testing of thermal insulation materials. The setup is shown in Fig. 1: discs of the Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composites were exposed up to 7.5 s to a flame having a nominal heat flux of 4 MW/m 2 . The heat flux was achieved by attenuating the gas supply to have conditions presented in Table 1. The heat flux was calculated using real-time temperature data collected on a multi-channel data logger (PicoLog 1216) every 175 ms of exposure to the flame. The setup allows to measure the temperature of the specimen using a K-type thermocouple bonded to the rear end of a copper slug connected to a ceramic tube and copper plate at the back face of the specimen of pre-measured mass.
The 3D surface profile of the ablated discs was imaged using the Bruker Contour GT-K 3D optical microscope: the maximum depth determined was used to calculate the linear ablation rate ( R l ) using standard formula. The compounds formed from the burning of the Cf /C-STT composite were characterised by XRD and SEM (mod. EVO60 EP ZEISS).
Fig. 2. The Cf /C (upper row) and Cf /C-SiC-TiC-TaC (lower row) composites oxidised at 1873 K up to 480 s upon air-quenching.
Fig. 3. Mass loss ( /Delta1 m) normalised to initial mass (m0) vs. offset exposure time (t*) of Cf /C and Cf /C-SiC-TiC-TaC (STT) composites in air oxidised at 1873 K up to 480 s: dashed lines connect average values.

3.3. Characterisation of the composites after the oxyacetylene flame test
During the exposure shot the Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composites, though they were exposed to the same nominal heat flux, they have not reached uniform and steady state surface temperatures: for that reason the surface temperatures, not adequately determined, were not reported in the text. However, other useful features such as the mass (Rm) and linear (R l ) ablation rates of the Cf /C-STT and Cf /C composites were evaluated and shown in Table 2. Rm and Rl represent largely used indicators to rank the performance of materials subjected to erosive/ablative testing conditions. In Table 3 Rm and Rl of the present work are compared to other alternative systems: when possible, the improved performances of the UHTC-infiltrated Cf /C composite are compared to the unprotected Cf /C composite counterparts. Figs. 15 and 16 show the status of both the types of composites after testing. The relatively higher Cf /C ablation rates presented in Table 2 can be attributed to the residual open pores (21 vol%) in the composite which was intended for occupation by the STT alloy. On the other hand, the Cf /C composites presented by other investigators were intended to be as fully densified as possible. From Table 3, the work of Zaman et al., [19] is peculiar from the rest in that the ablation rates decreased with increase in exposure time. The linear ablation rate in the present study compares well to that of Paul et al., [21], although the mass ablation rate in the former is more than three times in the latter study. On the other hand, Farhan et al.'s mass ablation rate on the 4-D C/C composite they prepared [20] is two orders higher than obtained in this work, and three orders greater than the rates obtained by Zaman et al., [19].
Fig. 15. The Cf/C composite exposed to oxyacetylene flame: visual appearance (a) and SEM (SE mode) micrographs from the its craterised surface (b,c).

3.4. Multiphase UHTCs-Modified C/C Composites
[125] prepared C/C-SiC-TiC-TaC (C/C-STT) composites using the melt infiltration method and systematically studied their oxidation behavior in air at 1873 K and ablation mechanisms under oxygen-acetylene flames (4 MW/m 2 ). After 480 s at 1873 K in air, the mass lablation rate of C/C-STT was one order of magnitude lower than that of C/C, and it entered a mass gain regime after 240 s. This is attributed to the formation of a glass-ceramic coating (containing SiO2, TiO2, and Ta2O5 eutectics) that fills pores and forms a dense oxide layer. After 7.5 s oxyacetylene ablation, the mass ablation rate (3.9 mg/s) and linear ablation rate (2.2 µ m/s) of the C/C-STT were much lower than those of C/C composites. A four-layer gradient structure formed on the surface, with the third layer achieving crack self-healing through liquid SiO2-Ta2O5 eutectics, significantly delaying the ablation process. The core mechanism of the ablation process is as follows: SiC oxidation forms the SiO2 glass phase, (Ta, Ti) C oxidation forms (Ti, Ta)O2 and Ta2O5, which work together to form a highly viscous glassceramic. Zhang et al. [63], based on the ablation mechanisms of the C/C-MeC-SiC (Me: Hf, Zr, Ti, Ta, Nb, and W) composites, prepared C/C-HfC-ZrC-TaC-SiC composites, which exhibit improved ablation resistance with mass and linear variation rates of 0.175 mg/s and 0.701 µ m/s, respectively, after long-time ablation (1000 s, 40 s × 25 cycle) under oxyacety- lene torch (4.2 MW/m 2 ). Shen et al.

3.3. Characterisation of the composites after the oxyacetylene flame test
In testing the Cf /C-STT composite, very rapid exposure shots of up to 7.5 s were done to ascertain the prompt response to self-generating protective high-temperature glass-ceramic phases at the commencement of oxidation/ablation. Exposure shots of several seconds are typical of the peak heat flux rise during experiments in a rocket motor chamber or during a re-entry mission to Earth. In this respect, very recently Paul et al., [18] evaluated the high performance of HfB2 UHTC particulate filled Cf /C composite using an arc-jet facility: although the short test duration of 10 s, the UHTC particles formed a protective layer at high temperature which was beneficial for the performance of the composite. Tests at extended exposure durations of 60 s or more better fulfil the purpose of calculating the mass ablation rate (Rm) which was anyway calculated (Table 2). An ample summary of published data for exposure durations of some tenths of seconds was presented in Table 3.

question: By what factor did the mass loss of the Cf/C-STT composite differ from the unprotected Cf/C composite during oxidation testing at 1873 K in the Makurunje et al. 2017 study?
answer: The mass loss of the Cf/C-STT composite differed from the unprotected Cf/C composite by a factor of about one order of magnitude during oxidation testing at 1873 K [1].

chunks.text:
3.1. C f /C and C f /C-STT composites oxidised at 1873 K in air
The visual appearance of the Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composite discs after oxidation is displayed in Fig. 2. The beneficial influence of the addition of both SiC and (Ta,Ti)C in the matrix is more obvious in Fig. 3. Compared to the Cf /C-STT composites, the Cf /C composites suffered a severe mass loss as shown in the net mass loss vs (offset) exposure time plotted in Fig. 3. In the case of the Cf /C-STT composites a net mass loss ( /Delta1 m) about one order of magnitude lower than that of Cf /C composites was systematically measured. The major efficacy of the presence of SiC and (Ta,Ti)C started taking place after about a nominal duration of 240 s. In fact, the mass loss rate dominated by the consumption of carbon (fibre and matrix) switched into a mass gain regime. In addition, the visual inspection of the oxidised discs (Fig. 2), together with the microstructure analyses by XRD (surface) and FESEM-EDX (surface and cross section), confirmed that an in-situ formed glass started spreading out actively during the longest nominal exposure of 480 s, providing effective protection to the underlying composite's bulk.
It should be stressed that the net mass change a material experiences when subjected to an oxidising environment, although useful for figure-of-merit, is incomplete unless fully described by oxida- tion mechanisms and kinetics, especially when a significant release of volatile by-products is involved. Furthermore, while ultra-fast heating rates and effective duration of the oxidation tests up to 10 min may, to some extent, represent conditions of a re-entry, they are not adequate to carefully investigate oxidation mechanisms and build predictive models.

As such, the outputs of the XRD analyses are not necessarily representative the bulk composite, and this is specifically addressed in Section 3.2. The series of XRD patterns in Fig. 7 repre- sent the onset, evolution and crystallisation of the oxide products upon fast quenching from the oxidation tests. The retarded coverage by glassy coating, for instance, exposed some bundles of carbon fibres to be easily attacked by oxygen. The XRD interpretation in Fig. 7 and the mass change trend in Fig. 3 nicely agree. In fact, when the net mass loss prevails for exposure up to 240 s the Cf + carbon matrix peak intensity increases because generated by larger volumes of the Cf + carbon matrix increasingly depleted of SiC and (Ta,Ti)C formerly present. However, as soon as the Cf /C-STT composite enters into an overall (protective) mass gain regime (see Fig. 3), the Cf + carbon matrix peak intensity drastically drops down because the fibre bundles start being more extensively covered by a protective glassy product whose signature with a well-defined bump was identified (see Fig. 7e).
Fig. 6. A representative polished area (micrograph by SE-FESEM) from the polished cross-section of the as-produced Cf /C-STT composite. Four distinct grey levels differentiate the primary phases: (Ta,Ti)C (1, bright), SiC (2, light grey), fibres bundle (3, darker grey) and porosity (black).

3.3. Characterisation of the composites after the oxyacetylene flame test
During the exposure shot the Cf /C and Cf /C-STT composites, though they were exposed to the same nominal heat flux, they have not reached uniform and steady state surface temperatures: for that reason the surface temperatures, not adequately determined, were not reported in the text. However, other useful features such as the mass (Rm) and linear (R l ) ablation rates of the Cf /C-STT and Cf /C composites were evaluated and shown in Table 2. Rm and Rl represent largely used indicators to rank the performance of materials subjected to erosive/ablative testing conditions. In Table 3 Rm and Rl of the present work are compared to other alternative systems: when possible, the improved performances of the UHTC-infiltrated Cf /C composite are compared to the unprotected Cf /C composite counterparts. Figs. 15 and 16 show the status of both the types of composites after testing. The relatively higher Cf /C ablation rates presented in Table 2 can be attributed to the residual open pores (21 vol%) in the composite which was intended for occupation by the STT alloy. On the other hand, the Cf /C composites presented by other investigators were intended to be as fully densified as possible. From Table 3, the work of Zaman et al., [19] is peculiar from the rest in that the ablation rates decreased with increase in exposure time. The linear ablation rate in the present study compares well to that of Paul et al., [21], although the mass ablation rate in the former is more than three times in the latter study. On the other hand, Farhan et al.'s mass ablation rate on the 4-D C/C composite they prepared [20] is two orders higher than obtained in this work, and three orders greater than the rates obtained by Zaman et al., [19].
Fig. 15. The Cf/C composite exposed to oxyacetylene flame: visual appearance (a) and SEM (SE mode) micrographs from the its craterised surface (b,c).

3.1. C f /C and C f /C-STT composites oxidised at 1873 K in air
Based on Fig. 13a, when readily accessible, (Ta,Ti)C reacts with oxygen and (Ti,Ta)O2 phase (numbered 2a) forms in a silica melt (numbered 1a): the corresponding EDX spectrum detected neither Ta nor Ti. However, based on Fig. 14, (Ta,Ti)C (numbered 2b) seems to react differently with oxygen giving rise to (Ta,Ti) oxide with a glassy appearance (numbered 1b): the presence of some Si in such a glassy product, within the analytical resolution of the EDX technique, was excluded, whilst C still appears present. The formation of a liquid at 1900 K is compatible with the TiO2-Ta2O5 binary phase diagram [17]. Varying the temperature, the liquid can evolve and crystallise into (Ta,Ti)O2. In Fig. 12b, it is also interesting to note that Cf /C, properly protected by the SiC + (Ta,Ti)C coating and their oxidation products, does not show significant oxidative degradation. Where the silica melt is abundant, notably onto the external surfaces, a couple of immiscible (Ta,Ti) mixed oxides (one richer in Ta and the second richer in Ti) show to have formed. According to FESEM analyses, prolonged exposure favoured the selective growth of the Ta-rich mixed (Ta,Ti) oxide (Fig. 12b,c). These oxides, though a shorter exposure duration, were also detected on the oxyacetylene flame tested surface of the Cf /C-STT composite (see Section 3.3). This finding is very important because the rapid formation of protective oxide products represents the prompt response of the tested Cf /C-STT composite against further degradation.

question: In Ren et al. 2017's study on HfC nanowire-reinforced coatings, what was the estimated length of the synthesized HfC nanowires formed on the SiC coating surface?
answer: The estimated length of the synthesized HfC nanowires formed on the SiC coating surface was about several hundred micrometers [2].

chunks.text:
3.1. Microstructure of the coatings
Fig. 3 is the representative surface and cross-section SEM images of the synthesized HfC nanowires layer on SiC-coated C/C samples. From Fig. 3(a), it can be found that a porous and latticed HfC nanowires layer is formed on the surface of SiC inner coating. These nanowires are randomly oriented with straight or curved morphology and the distribution of nanowires on SiC coating is homogeneous. These nanowires are usually with a diameter range of 200-300 nm and a length range from several tens to hundreds micrometers. From the high-magnification SEM image of a single nanowire (top-right corner in Fig. 3(a)), it can be found that the nanowire has a pyramidal sharp tip and there are no hemispherical catalyst alloy particles at the tip of the nanowire. So the formation mechanism of the nanowires should not be dominated by the typical tip-type vapor-liquid-solid (VLS) mechanism [22]. The interaction between the catalyst alloy droplet and the SiC substrate is strong, so the droplet will be adhered to the substrate during the nanowires growth. According to the preparation technology and microstructures of the as-prepared HfC nanowires, the formation mechanism of the nanowires may be controlled by the bottom-type VLS mechanism. From Fig. 3(b) it can be seen that a nanowires layer with a thickness of about 50 𝛍 m is uniformly covered on the surface of SiC coating.
Fig. 3. Surface and cross-section SEM images of the synthesized HfC nanowires on SiC-coated C/C composites.
Fig. 4. XRD pattern of the HfC nanowire-toughened TaSi2-TaC-SiC-Si coating on SiC-coated C/C composites.

3.1. Microstructure of the coatings
Fig. 2(a) exhibits the surface morphology of the deposition products on SiC coating. Plentiful long and straight nanowires form a porous layer, whose length is about several hundred micrometers. The orientations of these nanowires are different and the distribution of theses nanowires is uniform. The high-magni /uniFB01 cation image (Fig. 2(b)) exhibits that the diameter of these nanowires is 200 -500 nm. Fig. 2(c) is the fracture cross-section image of the nanowire coated sample, from which it can be seen that a nanowire layer is uniformly covered on SiC

Without HfC nanowires, Hardness [GPa] = 12.24 ± 0.73. Without HfC nanowires, Elastic modulus [GPa] = 169.72 ± 30.76. Without HfC nanowires, Fracture toughness [MPa·m 1/2 ] = 13.02 ± 0.72. With HfC nanowires, Hardness [GPa] = 14.39 ± 0.81. With HfC nanowires, Elastic modulus [GPa] = 193.70 ± 31.25. With HfC nanowires, Fracture toughness [MPa·m 1/2 ] = 19.27 ± 0.84
Fig. 7. Variation of acoustic emission signal with sliding load for the coated samples without (a) and with (b) HfC nanowires.
coating, whose thickness is about 60 -130 μ m.It also can be seen that the nanowire layer is porous and the orientation of nanowires is random. As seen in the XRD pattern of the deposition products (Fig. 2(d)), strong and sharp HfC diffraction peaks are detected, indicating the synthesized nanowires are HfC and the crystallinity of HfC nanowires is high. Except for the strong HfC peaks, a very weak SiC peak is also detected, implying the nanowire layer is porous.

3.1. Microstructure of the coatings
Low-magni /uniFB01 cation TEM image of the synthesized HfC nanowires is exhibited in Fig. 3(a). Straight nanowires with a diameter of 200 -300 nm can be found. The energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) pattern (Fig. 3(b)) of a single nanowire tip (area A in Fig. 3(a)) exhibits that the nanowire consists of Hf, C and bits of O. It can be clearly found that there is no hemi-spherical catalyst alloy particle at the nanowire tip and Ni element is not detected by EDX. This is because that the adhesion between catalyst alloy droplet and SiC coating substrate is strong and the catalyst particle is adhered to SiC substrate during nanowire growth. So, the growth of HfC nanowires can be controlled by the bottom-type vapor-liquid-solid (VLS) mechanism [25]. Fig. 3(c) and Fig. 3(d) is the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image recorded from a signal nanowire (area B in Fig. 3(a)), respectively. The obvious diffraction spots and clear lattice fringes indicate that the nanowire possesses single crystal structure. The SAED pattern can be recognized to be along [022] zone axis of face-centered cubic (fcc) HfC. The crystal lattice fringe spacing in HRTEM image is measured to be 0.2587 nm, approaching the (111) interplanar spacing of fcc HfC (0.2679 nm). In cubic system, the crystal orientation and crystal plane with same index is perpendicular, so the synthesized HfC nanowire grew along [111] crystal direction. There is an amorphous layer on the nanowire surface (indicated by red arrow in Fig. 3(d)), whose thickness is about 3 nm. Combined with the EDX analysis, the amorphous layer is HfO2 layer, which is formed due to the little oxidation of nanowires during deposition.

4. Conclusions
HfC nanowires acting as reinforcement materials for ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating were synthesized by CVD. Hardness, elastic modulus and fracture toughness of the outer coating were all improved because of the incorporation of HfC nanowires. After incorporating HfC nanowires, the interface bonding strength between inner and outer coating increased from 5.9 to 7.5 N. During ablation test, HfC nanowires could inhibit the cracking and peeling of outer coating, resulting in an improvement of ablation resistance of the coating. After ablation for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of ZrB2-SiC/SiC coated sample with HfC nanowires decreased from 0.20 to -0.12 mg/s.

a b s t r a c t
To relieve the mismatch of CET between coating and substrate or outer coating and inner coating, SiC whiskers [14], SiC nanoparticles [15], SiC nanowires [13,16-18] and HfC nanowires [12,19] have been incorporated into the ceramic coatings as the reinforcement materials, which could play a positive role in improving the toughness and oxidation resistance of the coatings. Especially, nanowires are more suitable to be used as the reinforcing materials due to their higher aspect ratio and strength [16]. Compared with SiC nanowires, HfC nanowires have higher melting point (4263 K), higher hardness and modulus of elasticity [20,21]. So HfC nanowires have better resistance to scour and erosion in severe oxidative and corrosive atmosphere at higher temperatures. In our previous research [12,19], HfC nanowires have been introduced into silicon-based coatings by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) on C/C substrate. The results show that HfC nanowires could improve the toughness and oxidation resistance of the silicon-based coatings. However, the HfC nanowire-toughened TaSi2-TaC-SiC-Si coating can only effectively protect C/C composites at 1773 K for 100 h, which is due to the CTE of TaSi2-TaC-SiC-Si coating is much higher than that of C/C substrate. So SiC coating should be used as the internal buffer layer. Motivated by our previous research [12], providing that HfC nanowires are incorporated into the TaSi2-TaC-SiC-Si outer coating on the SiC-coated C/C composites, it will suppress the cracking of the outer coating and improve the oxidation resistance of the coated C/C composites. The growth of HfC nanowires on the surface of SiC coating is much different from that on carbon materials, owing to the different surface structure and composition between them.

question: What was the mass ablation rate of the Ren 2017 ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating with HfC nanowires after 90 seconds of oxyacetylene torch ablation?
answer: The mass ablation rate of the Ren 2017 ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating with HfC nanowires after 90 seconds of oxyacetylene torch ablation was -0.12 mg/s [3].

chunks.text:
a b s t r a c t
To improve the ablation resistance of the coated carbon/carbon composites, HfC nanowires acting as reinforcement materials were introduced into ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating. Synthesis, morphology, mechanical properties and ablation resistance of the coating were researched. A porous HfC nanowire layer was uniformly covered on SiC coating. Both of the toughness and interface bonding strength of ZrB2-SiC coating with HfC nanowires were higher than those of the coating without HfC nanowires. HfC nanowires could effectively inhibit the cracking and peeling of outer coating during ablation. After oxyacetylene torch ablation for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of the coated sample without and with HfC nanowires was 0.20 mg/s and -0.12 mg/s, respectively.
© 2017 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
on C/C composites through three-step process including pressing, pyrolysis and reactive silicon in /uniFB01 ltration, and then analyzed the ablation performance of the coating. Previous research proved that ZrB2-SiC multiphase coating could prevent C/C composites from ablation in severe environment. Among all of the preparation methods, SAPS is a very high-ef /uniFB01 ciency and low-cost method to prepare a dense ZrB2-SiC coating [14,15]. The thickness and composition of the coating could be controlled by adjusting the spraying parameters.

3.3. Ablation behavior of the coatings
Ablation performance of the coatings was measured under oxyacetylene /uniFB02 ame. After being ablated for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of pure ZrB2-SiC/SiC coated C/C sample is 0.20 mg/s. While, the mass ablation rate of the coated sample with HfC nanowires is positive ( -0.12 mg/s). During ablation, on the one hand, the coating would gain weight because of its oxidation; on the other hand, mechanical denudation and volatilization of oxide layer would lead to weight loss. By incorporating nanowires, the weight gain of the sample is predominant, so the mass ablation rate is minus. Above results imply that HfC nanowires could inhibit the oxidized layer being denudated, which could reduce the mass loss of the oxide layer.
XRDpattern of the coating with HfC nanowires after ablation for 90 s is shown in Fig. 8. After ablation, ZrO2 phase emerges and the ZrB2 and SiC phases disappear. In ablation environment, ZrB2 would be oxidized to ZrO2 and B2O3, and SiC would be oxidized to SiO2, SiO, CO and CO2. After ablation, only ZrO2 remained on the sample surface owing to its high melting point (2953 K). SiO2, SiO, B2O3 and other gaseous products wouldbeevaporated /uniFB01 ercely in ablation environment, so they could not be found in the XRD pattern.

4. Conclusions
HfC nanowires acting as reinforcement materials for ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating were synthesized by CVD. Hardness, elastic modulus and fracture toughness of the outer coating were all improved because of the incorporation of HfC nanowires. After incorporating HfC nanowires, the interface bonding strength between inner and outer coating increased from 5.9 to 7.5 N. During ablation test, HfC nanowires could inhibit the cracking and peeling of outer coating, resulting in an improvement of ablation resistance of the coating. After ablation for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of ZrB2-SiC/SiC coated sample with HfC nanowires decreased from 0.20 to -0.12 mg/s.

3.3.  Ablation test results
The results of ablation test including weight change percentage (ΔW %), mass ablation rate (Rm), thickness change (% ∆l) and linear ablation rate (Rl) after the test for single-layer SiC and double-layer C/SiC/ZrB2 coatings are presented in Table 2. In order to investigate the performance of the coatings at different temperatures, they exposed to oxypropane and oxyacetylene flame separately. Results show that in the oxyacetylene flame test, the ablation rate of samples is higher than the oxypropane flame. It could be attributed to the higher temperature of oxyacetylene  flame  (about  3000  °C)  in  comparison  with  oxypropane flame (about 2200 °C). On the other hand, in both oxyacetylene and oxypropane  tests,  ablation  rate  has  raised  by  increasing  the  testing time. Considering the data in Table 2, the ablation rate of SiC coating under oxypropane flame after 60s is 5.28 × 10 -4 and the ablation rate of the SiC/ZrB2  coating after 300 s is only 0.426 × 10 -4 . This shows that, the double layer SiC/ZrB2 coating has a higher ablation resistance compared with single-layer SiC coating.
Fig. 9. XRD analysis of: (a) unshrouded (b) solid shrouded SiC/ZrB2 coating.
Fig. 11. SEM image and EDS analysis from SiC coating after ablation test.

3.2. Ablation properties of Cf/ZrC -SiC composites in oxyacetylene torch
An  ablation  test  of  Cf/ZrC -SiC  composites  is  conducted  using  a custom built oxyacetylene torch for 60 s and the mass loss and erosion depth are measured to be 0.52 g (mass ablation rate of 8.7 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 g/s) and  0.66 mm  (linear  ablation  rate  of  11 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 mm/s),  respectively. After oxyacetylene torch test, the ablated surface of Cf/ZrC -SiC composites can be divided into three zones from center to external based on the appearances, i.e. central pit (zone 1 in Fig. 3c), oxidized zone (zone 2 in Fig. 3c) and unaffected zone (zone 3 in Fig. 3c), corresponding well with  the  distribution  of  oxyacetylene  torch  temperature  (inset  in Fig. 3a). As far as the oxyacetylene torch is concerned, it involves high heat flux (~4 MW/m 2 ), high flow velocity ( > 150 m/s), high temperature (~3000 ◦ C), high heating/cooling rates (500 -1000 ◦ C/s) and severe  oxidizing  environment  (high  mole  fractions  of  molecular  and atomic oxygen) [20]. Nevertheless, both the flow velocity and torch temperature decrease sharply away from the flame core, i.e. in the radial directions. In our case, the distance between torch exit and specimen surface was 10 mm. At this location, the flame core has the high flow velocity and temperatures (~3000 ◦ C) and erodes the materials surface like a drill, leaving not only the ablated pit but also the splashing ZrO2 droplet (Fig. 3c). However, the flame has extreme radial gradients of temperature and velocity (Fig. 3a, b). Therefore, the outer oxidized zone experiences lower temperatures (1800 -2200 ◦ C) and much lower flow velocity, so this zone is severely oxidized but not eroded.

question: In the Ren et al. 2017 CVD synthesis of HfC nanowires, what was the temperature range maintained in the high-temperature deposition chamber?
answer: In the Ren et al. 2017 CVD synthesis of HfC nanowires, the temperature range maintained in the high-temperature deposition chamber was 1373 - 1473 K [1].

chunks.text:
2.1. Materials and processing
Cylindrical C/C specimens ( φ 30 × 10 mm) with a density of about 1650 kg/m 3 were used as substrates. Before preparing coating, the samples were ultrasonically cleaned in distilled water and then dried at 373 K.
Schematic diagram of the coating preparation process is shown in Fig. 1. To begin with, pack cementation was employed to prepare SiC inner coating on C/C specimens using 65 -80 wt.% Si, 10 -25 wt.% graphite and 5 -15 wt.% Al2O3 as raw materials. The preparation temperature was 2073 -2373 K and the heat treatment time was 2 h. Detailed experimental process was described in Ref. [19].
Then, HfC nanowires were synthesized on SiC coating by chemical vapor deposition (CVD). The SiC-coated C/C substrates were covered with catalyst particles (Ni(NO3)2) before deposition through means of soaking. HfCl4 powders acting as hafnium source were put in the lowtemperature evaporation chamber; CH4, H2 and Ar served as carbon source, reducing gas and inert gas, respectively. The high-temperature deposition chamber was heated to 1373 -1473Kandmaintainedfor3h.The deposition pressure was set at 10 -20KPa.The /uniFB02 owratesofCH4,H2 andAr were 200 -300, 1000 -2000 and 400 -800 mL/min, respectively. After deposition, HfC nanowires were obtained on SiC coating.

4.2.2. UHTC nanowires reinforced HTA coating
The HfCnw and ZrCnw demonstrates exceptional thermal-mechanical properties, including a significantly higher melting point (~4163 K), superior high temperature mechanical performance, as well as outstanding thermal and chemical stability [104,132]. Despite the influence of nanometer size effect, HfC nanowires demonstrate the ability to maintain their morphological at high temperatures, and these nanowires have been extensively developed in strengthening various composites.  Tian  et  al.  [183,184]  studied  the  growth  behavior  of  HfCnw prepared by CVD. HfCnw with single crystal were synthesized by nickel catalyst assisted CVD at 1025 ◦ C below the eutectic temperature and have a diameter of about 50 nm and a length of tens of microns. In addition,  various  geometries  of  one-dimensional  HfC,  such  as  nanoribbons,  nanorods  and  whiskers,  were  synthesized  by  CVD,  and  the growth of these nanowires primarily followed a gas-liquid solid growth mechanism (Fig. 21(a) and (b)). Based on the above work, Ren et al. [54, 57,58] conducted a comprehensive investigation on the growth characteristics, mechanical properties and ablation resistance of HfC coatings  reinforced  with  HfCnw.  The  introduction  of  HfCnw effectively alleviated the significant CTE mismatch between the coating and the substrate.  Furthermore,  the  HfCnw showed  excellent  physical  and chemical compatibility with the HfC  coating, thereby providing numerous nucleation points for the coating and improving the deposition rate. Notably, a key distinction observed in the HfC coating containing  nanowires  was  the  presence  of  granular  packed  crystal,  as opposed to the conventional columnar arranged crystal, as illustrated in Fig. 21(c). The introduction of HfCnw led to an increase in the interface bond strength between HfC coating and C/C substrate increasing from 4 N  to  5.7  N. 4.3.5. Multiphase UHTC/silicide multilayer coatings
The formed Hf -Si -O oxide species at 1700 ◦ C, showed good hightemperature  stability  as  well  as  self-healing  ability.  This  protective layer lead to protecting the C/C composite for a period of 73 h. As shown before, the UHTCs coatings could be important in improving the underlying substrates oxidation/ablation performance The UHTC coatings cracked  and  deboned  when  they  work  undergo  harsh  aero-thermal conditions or high -low temperature cycles [89,90]. This is because of the intrinsic brittle feature resulted from the strong covalent bonds between atoms in UHTC materials. To investigate this issue, based on the design rule of multi-scale synergistic toughening, nanostructures (whiskers [102], nanowires [102] as well as nanotubes [103]) have been proposed  by  researchers  for  toughening  the  UHTC  coatings.  These nanostructures  mentioned  can  possess  high  strength  and  modulus (commonly applied for toughening of ceramics) [103]. Ceramic nanowires or whiskers can applied as common nanostructure. Wang [55] studied  the  effect  of  SiC  whiskers  adding  on  the  HfB2 -Si -SiC/SiC coating ' s  oxidation  behavior.  The  results  showed  the  mass  gain  of composite reduced from 4.86 % to 0.88 % after oxidation for 468 h at 1500 ◦ C.  Ren  et  al.  [103]  proposed  addition  of  HfC  nanowires  for toughening  ZrB2 -SiC/SiC  coating.  After  adding  the  HfC  nanowires hardness,  elastic  modulus  and  fracture  toughness  of  the  obtained improved. Cheng et al. [104] found the PyC layer was important in stabilizing the structure as well as the morphology of SiC nanowires at high  temperatures.  These  researchers  applied  SiC/PyC  core -shell nanowires  for  toughening  ZrB2 -SiC/SiC  [70]  and  TaB2 -SiC

2.1. Preparation of HfC nanowire-toughened UHTCs coating on SiC-coated C/C composites
Two dimensional C/C composites with a density of about 1.73 g/cm 3 were prepared by the thermal gradient chemical vapor infiltration technique, which were used as substrates. The C/C composites were cut into two kinds of small specimens (10 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm for oxidation test and 55 mm × 8 mm × 4 mm for mechanical property test). All specimens were hand-polished with 400 grit SiC paper, ultrasonically cleaned with ethanol and dried at 373 K for 2 h. The SiC inner coating was obtained by in situ reaction method through pack cementation. The powder compositions were given as follows: 65-80 wt.% Si, 10-25 wt.% C and 5-15 wt.% Al2O3. The preparation temperature and time were set at 2373 K and 2 h, respectively. The preparation process of HfC nanowire-toughened UHTCs coating on SiC-coated C/C samples was shown in Fig. 1. Firstly, a HfC nanowires porous layer was synthesized on SiC-coated C/C substrate by CVD. The SiC-coated C/C samples were immersed in Ni(NO3)2/ethanol solutions (molarity, 0.5 ± 0.05 mol/L) for 2 h and then dried at 373 K for 2 h. The deposition temperature and time was set at 1373 K and 60 min, respectively. When the inside temperature reached the reaction temperature, CH4 and H2 gases were simultaneously introduced with a volume ratio of 1:10-1:15. The pressure inside the furnace tube was adjusted to 12-15 kPa.

question: What were the flow rates of CH₄, H₂, and Ar gases specified in Ren et al. 2017 for the CVD synthesis of HfC nanowires?
answer: The flow rates of CH₄, H₂, and Ar gases specified in Ren et al. 2017 for the CVD synthesis of HfC nanowires were 200 - 300 mL/min for CH₄, 1000 - 2000 mL/min for H₂, and 400 - 800 mL/min for Ar [1].

[54, 57,58] conducted a comprehensive investigation on the growth characteristics, mechanical properties and ablation resistance of HfC coatings  reinforced  with  HfCnw.  The  introduction  of  HfCnw effectively alleviated the significant CTE mismatch between the coating and the substrate.  Furthermore,  the  HfCnw showed  excellent  physical  and chemical compatibility with the HfC  coating, thereby providing numerous nucleation points for the coating and improving the deposition rate. Notably, a key distinction observed in the HfC coating containing  nanowires  was  the  presence  of  granular  packed  crystal,  as opposed to the conventional columnar arranged crystal, as illustrated in Fig. 21(c). The introduction of HfCnw led to an increase in the interface bond strength between HfC coating and C/C substrate increasing from 4 N  to  5.7  N. 2.2.2. Chemical vapor deposition
The HfC-based composites were taken into the constant temperature region  of  the  chemical  vapor  deposition  furnace  using  methyltrichlorosilane  (MTS)-H2-Ar  system  to  deposit  SiC  for  densifying  the composites. MTS was supplied into the furnace by vacuum evaporation. The feeding rate of MTS was controlled by needle valve and weighting. The  HfC-based  composites  were  heated  at  1100 -1300 ◦ C  with  the heating rate of 6 ◦ C/min under the protection of argon with the gas flow rate of 500 ml/min. During heat preservation period, the flow rate of Ar was 100 -200 ml/min. The feeding rate of MTS was about 0.2 -0.4 g/min, which was calculated and measured by an electronic balance. Meanwhile, the gas flow rate of H2  was 1000 -2000 ml/min. The SiC deposition lasted for 20 h. After deposition, the cooling rate was 7 ◦ C/min. The  Cf/HfC-SiC  and  SiCf/HfC-SiC  composites  were  obtained.  Finally, these  composites  were  cut  into  the  rectangular  specimens  with  the dimension of 3 × 4 × 40 mm 3 .

4. Conclusions
HfC nanowires acting as reinforcement materials for ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating were synthesized by CVD. Hardness, elastic modulus and fracture toughness of the outer coating were all improved because of the incorporation of HfC nanowires. After incorporating HfC nanowires, the interface bonding strength between inner and outer coating increased from 5.9 to 7.5 N. During ablation test, HfC nanowires could inhibit the cracking and peeling of outer coating, resulting in an improvement of ablation resistance of the coating. After ablation for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of ZrB2-SiC/SiC coated sample with HfC nanowires decreased from 0.20 to -0.12 mg/s.

3.3.1. HfC, ZrC and TaC coatings
The development of CVD-ZrC coating closely parallels that of HfC, as both Zr and Hf belong to the group IVB with similar close atomic radius, and  their  compounds  have  relatively  similar  physical  and  chemical properties. For example, both ZrC and HfC the melting points of both carbides exceed 3000 ◦ C (ZrC: 3540 ◦ C; HfC: 3890 ◦ C) [104,132], and the CVD deposition process for ZrC is similar to HfC (ZrC: ZrCl4 -CH4 -H2-Ar),  their  precursors  (ZrCl4 and  HfCl4)  have  similar volatilization temperatures. To explore the effects of process parameters and precursor proportion on the deposition and growth behavior of ZrC coating, Liu et al. [86] investigated the effect of CVD process parameters on the product phase yield under the ZrCl4 -CH4 -H2-Ar using the thermodynamic software FactSage. The results showed that the yield of ZrC was  heavily  depends  on  the  molar  ratios  of  ZrCl4/(ZrCl4 + CH4)  and H2/(ZrCl4 + CH4), as well as the deposition temperature, while displaying insensitive to the inert gas Ar and the deposition pressure. As shown in Fig. 14(a) and (b), Under the process conditions of high H2 input, low Ar content, low pressure, and temperatures above 1100 ◦ C, ZrC deposition is favored while avoiding the formation of Zr or C rich products. This provides valuable guidance for subsequent experimental activities. Similarly, Park et al. [139] investigated the growth behavior of CVD-ZrC coating at deposition temperatures ranging from 1250 to 1500 ◦ C using ZrCl4, CH4 and H2 as precursors. The activation energy values of coating surface were calculated to be 2.99 kcal/mol and 114.46 kcal/mol for different  temperature intervals, as shown in Fig. 14(c). question: In Zamharir 2015, what relative density was achieved for ZrB2-25vol% SiC at the optimal hot pressing conditions of 1850°C, 90 min holding time, 16 MPa pressure, and 200 nm SiC particle size?
answer: A relative density of 99.6% was achieved for ZrB2-25 vol% SiC at the optimal hot pressing conditions of 1850°C, 90 min holding time, 16 MPa pressure, and 200 nm SiC particle size [2].

Abstract
ZrB2 -25 vol% SiC ceramic composites were prepared by hot pressing in order to investigate the effects of processing parameters and SiC particle size (20 nm, 200 nm and 5 m m) on the densi /uniFB01 cation behavior. At this study, an experimental design technique (Taguchi method) was used to specify the signi /uniFB01 cance of four factors and to obtain the optimal conditions. The statistical analysis distinguished the applied pressure as the most effective parameter on the relative density of ZrB2 -SiC composites. A relative density of /C24 100% was obtained at the optimal processing conditions: temperature of 1850 1 C, holding time of 90 min, pressure of 16 MPa and SiC particle size of 200 nm. The con /uniFB01 rmation test, performed under optimum conditions, indicated that the experimental outcomes were equal with the expected values from the Taguchi prediction. Moreover, the addition of micronsized SiC particles to ZrB2 matrix, which dispread intergranularly along ZrB2/ZrB2 interfaces inhibited the grain growth of the ZrB2 grains [21]. A ZrB2 ( /C24 3 μ m) ceramic composite containing 10 vol% /uniFB01 ne α -SiC (0.8 m m), was fully densi /uniFB01 ed by hot pressing at 1900 1 C for 20 min under 40 -50 MPa. It was indicated that, introducing ultra /uniFB01 ne α -SiC particles was a key factor in enabling both the control of the diboride grain growth and the attainment of full density [17]. In another study, the in /uniFB02 uence of SiC particle size on the density of ZrB2 -SiC composites was investigated. ZrB2 -30 vol% SiC ceramic composites, using four different α -SiC powders with mean particle sizes ranging from 0.45 to 10 m m, were prepared by hot pressing at 1900 1 C for 45 min under 32 MPa. It was indicated that decreasing the average size of the starting SiC particles, from 10 to 0.45 m m, had improved the relative density of ZrB2 -SiC composites from 97.4 to 99.8% [22].

1. Introduction
Although finding an appropriate composition is one of the most critical challenges for achieving the best relative density, hardness, and flexural strength, selecting a proper sintering process and its parameters is another essential issue. ZrB2-20 vol% SiC has been studied as a base composition with a ZrB2: SiC volume ratio of 4:1 and a ZrC: Cf volume ratio of 2:1. Optimum densification and mechanical characteristics, 99.6% relative density, 18.1 GPa hardness and 564.5 MPa flexural strength, have been reported for ZrB2-18.5 vol% SiC-5.0 vol% ZrC-2.5 vol% Cf composite [26]. Among several sintering processes such as spark plasma sintering (SPS) [27-33], pressureless sintering (PS) [34], hot pressing (HP) [35-37], and reactive hot pressing (RHP) [38,39], samples produced by the SPS process were found to have relatively higher mechanical characteristics [40-45]. Because of strong bonding in carbides and borides, it is difficult to achieve superb sintering characteristics by conventional sintering processes [46-50]. question: Why did Zamharir 2015 add ultrafine SiC particles to the ZrB2 matrix for the densification of this ceramic composite?
answer: Zamharir 2015 added ultrafine SiC particles to the ZrB2 matrix to enhance sinterability, inhibit the grain coarsening, improve mechanical properties, and enhance oxidation/reduction resistance [4].

chunks.text:
3. Results and discussion
It is well known that the use of fine powders has beneficial effects on the densification of ZrB2 -SiC, thereby a fine ZrB2 powder (2 𝛍 m) was also chosen as starting material in this study. Fig. 8 shows SEM micrograph from a polished surface of the hotpressed ZrB2-SiCW composite using fine ZrB2 powder: it reveals a nearly full-dense ZrB2-SiCW composite and that the majority of SiC whiskers were retained after hot pressing. Moreover, the SiC whiskers were homogenously distributed in ZrB2 matrix as can be seen in Fig. 8. The measured fracture toughness of ZrB2-SiCW composite was 6.0 ± 0.30MPam 1/2 , which is ∼ 71% higher than that of the ZrB2 ceramic without SiC whisker and is also higher than SiC particle reinforced ZrB2 composite. Fig. 9 shows the path of a crack produced by Vickers indentation on the polished surface of the hot-pressed ZrB2-SiCW composite. Whisker bridging and crack deflection were observed for the composite. Fracture surface examined by SEM shows some whiskers were pulled out and a predominantly transgranular matrix fracture mode (Fig. 10). How- ever, the majority of SiC whiskers were fractured directly and the whiskerpullout is very limited. High interfacial bonding strength of ZrB2-SiCW interface and the decreased strength of SiC whisker are probably responsible for the limited whisker pullouts. The increase in fracture toughness of the ZrB2-SiCW composite was mainly due to SiC whisker bridging and crack deflection. The control of whisker/matrix interfaces and the densification without degradation of SiC whisker for this material will be made in future work. Other densification methods should be taken into account in the future for fabrication this composite with high performance, such as spark plasma sintering due to rapid densification resulting in reduced damage of SiC whisker.

Hence, it is necessary to fabricate dense body sections of ZrB2-based composites. Generally densi /uniFB01 cation of a single-phase ZrB2 is dif /uniFB01 cult and usually needs high temperatures because of its covalent bonding and low bulk and grain boundary diffusion rates [8,13]. Its poor sinterability prevents this material from being used in a wide range of applications; therefore sinterability of ZrB2 must be improved before the potential applications of ZrB 2 can be fully realized.
The introduction of secondary phases or sintering aids has been an appropriate strategy for improving the sinterability of monolithic ZrB2 ceramics [4,14 -17]. With this aim in mind, sintering aids such as boron and carbon, metallic materials (e.g. Ni and Mo), metal silicides or Si3N4 were used to enhance the densi /uniFB01 cation and also reduce the sintering temperature [18 -21].

A B S T R A C T
This  study  presents  new  findings  on  ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  composites,  where  silicon  carbide  and graphene were incorporated into a zirconium diboride matrix. The research focused on the densification process, and the mechanical and electrical properties of the produced materials. A novel aspect of this work was the investigation of the influence of graphene alignment on composite properties. The results indicated that despite SPS curves suggested full consolidation, the final relative density was lower than expected, and SEM observations revealed the presence of pores in the microstructure. This suggests that additional sintering activators may be necessary to achieve full densification of ZrB2-based materials. Furthermore, high consolidation temperature (2000 ◦ C)  caused  partial  decomposition  of  graphene,  with  only  some  flakes  preserving  their  structure.  Agglomerates of silicon carbide particles were also observed in SiC composites. Regarding mechanical properties, all composites showed a significant increase in hardness compared to pure ZrB2. The highest hardness (1750 HV10) and fracture toughness (3.5 MPa m 1 / 2 ) were achieved for the composite with 40 vol% SiC. For electrical properties, all composites showed enhanced conductivity than the reference ZrB2 sample. Notably, the graphene composites exhibited a clear anisotropy. Specimens tested parallel to the graphene alignment showed a conductivity increase of a few orders of magnitude. In conclusion, this study demonstrates that SPS is an effective method for consolidating ZrB2-based composites without sintering aids, leading to significant improvements in hardness and electrical conductivity. However, achieving full densification may require the use of activators. The observed anisotropy of electrical properties in graphene composites highlights their potential for applications where directional conductivity is desired. This work opens new paths for better understanding ultra-high temperature ceramic-based composites.

Signi /uniFB01 cance of hot pressing parameters and reinforcement size on densi /uniFB01 cation behavior of ZrB2 -25 vol% SiC UHTCs
Recent works have shown that the introduction of nanosized SiC particles causes an increase in the sinterability and densi /uniFB01 cation of ZrB2-based ceramic composites at moderate temperatures (1800 -1900 1 C) [23,24]. It was suggested that using nano-sized SiC particles can result in superior density of ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composites. ZrB2 -SiC composites, with different amounts of 30 nm β -SiC (10, 20 and 30 vol%), were manufactured by hot pressing at 1900 1 C for 60 min under a 30 MPa uniaxed pressure, as a relative density of 97.1% was obtained for ZrB2 -10 vol% SiC [23]. A ZrB2-based ceramic composite containing 20 vol% β -SiC particles, which mean particle size of ZrB2 and SiC were 2 μ m and 30 nm, respectively, was fabricated at 1900 1 C for 30 min under a uniaxial pressure of 30 MPa by hot pressing. It was indicated that the grain growth of ZrB2 matrix had impressively graveled by submicron-sized SiC particles, where it was located along the grain boundaries [24].
Generally, the addition of ultra /uniFB01 ne SiC to the ZrB2 matrix is performed to enhance sinterability, inhibit the grain coarsening, improve mechanical properties, and enhance oxidation/reduction resistance [21,22,25 -28]. Hence, introducing SiC particles into ZrB2 yields a ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composite that is more sinterable than the monolithic ZrB2.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have garnering attentions in the last decades because of the development of hypersonic aerospace vehicles [1-6]. From this category of advanced materials, ZrB2 has a series of excellent characteristics such as chemical stability, high melting point, thermal conductivity, hardness, and low theoretical density. These properties make ZrB2-based ceramics as suitable choices in aerospace and other industrial applications such as rocket propulsion systems, hypersonic flight and cutting tools, crucibles for molten metals and high-temperature electrodes [7-11].
Similar to other non-oxide refractory ceramics, ZrB2 generally oxidizes under air exposure at high temperatures. Therefore, the oxidation behavior studies of ZrB2 has been one of the interesting research topics since the 60 s [12-16]. Introducing SiC particles not only provides remarkable improvement in the oxidation resistance of zirconium diboride by supporting the formation of a protective layer of borosilicate glassy phase [17-19], but also restrains the growth of zirconium diboride grains which improves final mechanical behavior such as flexural strength, hardness, and fracture toughness [20-23]. Noteworthy that the densification of ZrB2-based composites is very difficult owing to the covalent character of the bonding, low volume self-diffusion coefficient and presence of oxide films on the particles' surface [24-29]. Therefore, different densification routes are utilized for

question: In Fang et al. 2021, the hydrothermal carbon (HTC) coating applied to carbon fiber in the CF/ZrB2/SiBCN composite had a thickness of 200 nm and was prepared using an environmentally friendly hydrothermal method. How much did this coating increase the surface energy of the carbon fiber compared to the uncoated fiber?
answer: The HTC coating increased the surface energy of the carbon fiber from 9.3 mJ/m² to 43.1 mJ/m², which is an increase of around 4.6 times compared to the uncoated fiber [1].

In summary, HTC coating with the thickness of 200 nm was successfully prepared on the surface of the CF. The wettability of CF was signi /uniFB01 cantly enhanced by the abundant oxygen-containing groups of the HTC coating. The surface energy of HTC/CF was 43.1 mJ/m 2 , increasing by around 4.6 times of CF (9.3 mJ/m 2 ). The improvement of the /uniFB01 ber surface energy could e /uniFB03 ciency optimize the injection process, ultimately contributing to the formation of the compact and uniform ceramic green body. The obtained HCZS revealed the /uniFB02 exural strength and fracture toughness of 206 ± 16 MPa and 6.14 ± 0.91 MPa m 1/2 , which were evidently better than the CZS. The denser matrix of HCZS combined with the HTC coating promoted the isolation between oxygen and /uniFB01 ber, releasing thoroughly the toughening abilities of the CFs. Even in a more aggressive oxidation environment, the HTC coating could act as a sacri /uniFB01 ce layer to prevent the /uniFB01 bers from damage.

In this study, hydrothermal carbon coating was constructed on the surface of CF with the thickness of 200 nm. The surface energy of the modi /uniFB01 ed /uniFB01 ber was investigated by the wetting angle measurement method. ZrB2 slurry was injected into the CF fabric with and without HTC coating, respectively. The improvement of the injection e /uniFB03 ciency after the introduction of HTC coating was demonstrated by the morphology characterization of CF/ZrB2 green bodies. Polyborosilazane was used as the precursor to compact the green body continually by a PIP procedure. Moreover, the /uniFB01 ber damage behavior had also been studied systematically to demonstrate the protecting role of the HTC coating.
