The above results demonstrated that the introduction of HTC coating can e /uniFB00 ectively improve the surface energy of the CF, thereby increasing the density and uniformity of the green body after slurry injection. However, the e /uniFB00 ect of the coating on the performance of the /uniFB01 nal composite product also need to be assessed in practical applications. PBSZ was used as the precursor to compact the green body continually in this work, whose thermal properties were characterized by DSC and TGA and shown in Fig.S3. It should be emphasized that some literature reported that the yield of precursors would be extremely low without a curing procedure [33]. Therefore, the sample of thermal experiment in this work was cured at 200 ℃ for 4 h to form a crosslinking structure. The PBSZ precursor showed an insigni /uniFB01 cant weight loss from 150 ℃ , mainly due to the evaporation of organic solvents and the polymerization of oligomers. Then, Si-H and N-H of the PBSZ will be coupled to obtain the SiBCN ceramics, thus causing a continuous weightlessness stage after 200 ℃ [34]. The yield of the PBSZ was 73.8 % and the DSC curve demonstrated that the whole pyrolysis process was endothermic. Owing to the high oxidation resistance and thermal stability, SiBCN ceramics were a hopeful talent to apply in the environment up to 2000 ℃ . An XRD characterization shown in Fig.5 had been employed to characterize the structure of ceramics under di /uniFB00 erent pyrolysis temperature. There were no obvious peaks in the spectrogram between 1000 ∼ 1400 ℃ , revealing an amorphous feature of the pyrolysis products. The ceramic began to crystallize at 1600 ℃ to form SiC, Si3N4 and BN [35]. A better oxidation resistance could be exhibited when SiBCN ceramics were in a state of amorphous [36].

1. Introduction
With the advantages of short production cycle and facility, slurry injection technology will help explore new possibilities for the preparation of high-performance /uniFB01 ber toughened ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC). Here, hydrothermal carbon (HTC) coating with abundant functional groups is developed on the surface of the carbon /uniFB01 ber (CF) to optimize the slurry injection process and /uniFB01 nally enhance the performance of CF/ZrB2/ SiBCN. The enhanced surface energy of HTC coated CF could e /uniFB03 ciency promote the injection process, ultimately contributing to the formation of the compact and uniform ceramic green body. The denser matrix of the /uniFB01 nally product combined with the HTC coating establish an isolation between oxygen and /uniFB01 ber. Even in a more aggressive oxidation environment, the HTC coating could act as a sacri /uniFB01 ce layer to prevent the /uniFB01 bers from damage. The results identify a unique attraction during the construction of CF/ZrB2/SiBCN with optimal properties and could be extended to other CF/UHTCs.
technical limitations under the comprehensive perspective, such as long preparation cycles and excessive porosity for the /uniFB01 nal product [10]. The use of hybrid approaches or the development of new methods is a promising trend for the preparation of CF/UHTCs [11]. In recent years, the direct injection method of ceramic slurry inspired by construction engineering has been reported many times [12]. The ceramic slurry can be uniformly injected into the /uniFB01 ber braid according to the designed content to form a high-density green body. Then, a PIP method would be adopted to continuously increase the density of the composites and tightly bind the UHTC particles in the CF framework [13].

3.2. Preparation of Ni3B coating on the surface of carbon fibers
After preparing Ni3B and HTC/Ni3B coatings on carbon fibers, Cf/ ZrB2-SiC  composites  were  fabricated  via  slurry  infiltration  combined with precursor impregnation and pyrolysis (PIP) [40,41]. Eleven PIP cycles  achieved over 80 % densification. The composites were designated  as  CZ  (uncoated  fiber),  N-CZ  (Ni3B-coated  fiber),  and  HN-CZ (HTC/Ni3B-coated fiber). All  samples  demonstrated comparable  relative densities, with variations less than 4 % (Fig. S2), thereby effectively minimizing performance deviations caused by porosity. Low-magnification SEM images (Fig. S3) revealed a uniform distribution of  carbon fibers  and  ZrB2  in  all  three  composites.  SiC  was  dispersed within the infiltration-formed regions, where it effectively filled pores and  promoted  densification.  After  multiple  PIP  cycles,  all  samples attained a similar level of densification, which is consistent with the density  evolution  trends  depicted  in  Fig.  S2.  TEM  analysis  was  performed to characterize the fiber-matrix interface in HN-CZ composites, as shown in Fig. 7. SAED of Region I (Fig. 7b) matches the [ 1 00 ] zone axis of ZrB2  (PDF#34 -423), while Region II (Fig. 7c) exhibits diffuse rings corresponding to amorphous  SiC. HRTEM  analysis  at the HTC/carbon  fiber  interface  (Fig.  7d)  revealed  distinct  graphitization differences between the coating and fiber. A well-defined boundary was observed, confirming interfacial contrast resulting from crystallization variations. The HTC interphase exhibits a lattice spacing of 0.33 nm due to crystallization during heat treatment, consistent with observations by Sun  et  al.  [42].  These  TEM  results  confirm  successful  integration  of ZrB2-SiC  matrix  with  carbon  fibers  and  structural  integrity  of  the HTC/Ni3B interphase.

question: According to Fang et al. 2021, how did the hydrothermal carbon coating affect the bulk density of the green body after ZrB2 slurry injection into the carbon fabric in the CF/ZrB2/SiBCN system?
answer: The hydrothermal carbon (HTC) coating on the carbon fiber (CF) increased the bulk density of the green body after ZrB2 slurry injection. The green body generated from the original CF braid had a bulk density of 34.2 vol.% [1], whereas the green body from the HTC/CF fabric reached a bulk density of 42.0 vol.% [1].

The high relative density of the green body favors not only the significantly shortening of densification cycles but also increasing the content and improving the distribution  uniformity  of  ZrB2,  which  is  beneficial  to  enhance  the oxidation and ablation resistance of the composite.
Further densification and strength enhancement can be realized by filling the gaps between the ZrB2  particles utilizing gaseous or liquid ceramic  precursors  with  small  molecules.  However,  compared  to gaseous precursors, liquid precursors are more eligible in operability and densification efficiency, making them an ideal choice of precursors for densification. In order to further densify the Cf/ZrB2 green body, the liquid polycarbosilane (LPCS) was introduced into the interstices of the packing particles under a pressure of exceeding 200 MPa and pyrolyzed under pressure-free conditions. The in-situ pyrolytic SiC not only efficiently filled the pores in the green body but also tightly bonded the ZrB2 particles and carbon fibers together. After only five-time infiltration and pyrolysis cycles, the final Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composite with a relative density as high as 92.1 % was obtained. question: In Fang et al. 2021, what were the mechanical properties (flexural strength and fracture toughness) achieved by the HCZS composite, and how did these compare to the performance after different oxidation exposure times?
answer: In Fang et al. 2021, the HCZS composite achieved a flexural strength of 206±16 MPa and a fracture toughness of 6.14±0.91 MPa m^1/2 [2]. After oxidation exposure, the flexural strength of HCZS decreased to 168±19 MPa after 5 min of oxidation and further dropped to 74±31 MPa after 30 min of oxidation [3]. These values indicate that while the mechanical properties of HCZS did decline with increasing oxidation time, they remained higher compared to the CZS composite under the same conditions.

According to the experiment results and literatures, the protecting mechanism of HTC coating in oxidation condition was speculated, as shown in the Fig.9c and Fig.9d. The low density of CZS resulted in the formation of many large and open pores on its surface, which acted as a channel for oxygen. Most of CFs lost their mechanical properties after reacting with oxygen. However, the denser matrix of HCZS contributed to the isolation between oxygen and /uniFB01 ber. The main reaction source may convert into Si3N4 [41], which was generated during the crystallization of SiBCN matrix. Even so, HTC as the sacri /uniFB01 ce layer could still protect the CF from most of the erosion reaction.

question: In Fang et al. 2021, during the fabrication of CF/ZrB2/SiBCN composites, how did the monofilament tensile strength of carbon fiber change after HTC coating, and what was the significance of this property for the toughening mechanism?
answer: The monofilament tensile strength of the carbon fiber (CF) slightly dropped from 5.2 ± 0.4 GPa to 4.1 ± 0.3 GPa after HTC coating [2]. This strength level was still sufficient for the application in ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTC) [15]. The significance of this property for the toughening mechanism lies in the fact that the high strength of CF is essential to achieve fiber bridging and extraction during the deformation of CF/UHTC, which contributes to the toughening of the composite material [2].

The high strength of the CF was an important prerequisite to achieve the /uniFB01 ber bridging and extraction during the deformation of CF/ UHTC [23]. A substantial sacri /uniFB01 ce of /uniFB01 ber strength would not be tolerated for any /uniFB01 ber surface treatment method [15]. Therefore, it was essential to characterize the /uniFB01 ber strength before and after coating. The mono /uniFB01 lament tensile results were shown in Fig.1g. The strength of the original /uniFB01 ber was 5.2 ± 0.4 GPa, which slightly dropped to 4.1 ± 0.3 GPa after coating. However, this strength level was still surplus in the application of UHTC [15]. The XPS spectroscopy of the CF and HTC/CF contained the spectra of C 1s and O 1s were shown in Fig.1d. The peak intensity of O 1s increased obviously after coating by HTC. The C 1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into four peaks, corresponding to alkyl aromatic groups (284.5 eV), ether groups (285.7 eV), carbonyl groups (287.2 eV) and carboxylic acids group (288.6 eV) [24]. Oxygen-containing groups in Fig.1e were extreme weak due to the highly carbonized structure of CF, which signi /uniFB01 cantly improved after coating by HTC (Fig.1f). The function group on the surface of HTC was signi /uniFB01 cant for the improvement of the wettability between HTC/CF and slurry [25]. In addition, a wetting angle measurement method was employed to visualize the change of wetting ability more intuitively. In general, contact angles with deionized water could be used to evaluate the wettability of the solids directly [26]. However, ethyl alcohols with few polymer dispersants were the main liquid carriers during the process of ceramic slurry injection. The charge properties of the ceramic particles wrapped in the solvent would also have speci /uniFB01 c in /uniFB02 uence on the wetting behavior [27]. The surface free energy may be a better parameter to assess the wettability of CFs in this work [28].

In this study, hydrothermal carbon coating was constructed on the surface of CF with the thickness of 200 nm. The surface energy of the modi /uniFB01 ed /uniFB01 ber was investigated by the wetting angle measurement method. ZrB2 slurry was injected into the CF fabric with and without HTC coating, respectively. The improvement of the injection e /uniFB03 ciency after the introduction of HTC coating was demonstrated by the morphology characterization of CF/ZrB2 green bodies. Polyborosilazane was used as the precursor to compact the green body continually by a PIP procedure. The in /uniFB02 uence of HTC coating on the mechanical and oxidation properties of CF/ZrB2/SiBCN composites was analyzed in detail. Moreover, the /uniFB01 ber damage behavior had also been studied systematically to demonstrate the protecting role of the HTC coating.

4. Conclusions
In summary, HTC coating with the thickness of 200 nm was successfully prepared on the surface of the CF. The wettability of CF was signi /uniFB01 cantly enhanced by the abundant oxygen-containing groups of the HTC coating. The surface energy of HTC/CF was 43.1 mJ/m 2 , increasing by around 4.6 times of CF (9.3 mJ/m 2 ). The improvement of the /uniFB01 ber surface energy could e /uniFB03 ciency optimize the injection process, ultimately contributing to the formation of the compact and uniform ceramic green body. The obtained HCZS revealed the /uniFB02 exural strength and fracture toughness of 206 ± 16 MPa and 6.14 ± 0.91 MPa m 1/2 , which were evidently better than the CZS. The denser matrix of HCZS combined with the HTC coating promoted the isolation between oxygen and /uniFB01 ber, releasing thoroughly the toughening abilities of the CFs. Even in a more aggressive oxidation environment, the HTC coating could act as a sacri /uniFB01 ce layer to prevent the /uniFB01 bers from damage.

4. Conclusions
In this study, the exceptional shear deformation capability and hightemperature self-healing effect of the novel Ni3B interphase were systematically demonstrated through theoretical calculations and experimental verification. Ni3B coatings  and  HTC/Ni3B  coatings  were successfully  fabricated  on  carbon  fibers  through  a  hybrid  process combining electroless plating with thermal treatment. Significant enhancements in both mechanical properties and thermal shock resistance were achieved in Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites via the interface modification. The composite modified with HTC/Ni3B (HN-CZ) demonstrated a flexural strength of  202 ± 34 MPa  and  fracture toughness of 6.07 ± 0.78 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 ,  corresponding  to  47 %  and  65 %  enhancements compared to the unmodified Cf/ZrB2-SiC composite (CZ). The critical thermal shock temperature progressively increased from 428 ℃ to 824 ℃ after  HTC/Ni3B  modification.  The  Ni3B  interphase  effectively deflected interfacial cracks through its superior shear deformation effect,  and  sealed  cracks  via  oxidative  volume  expansion  at  elevated temperatures. The hierarchical HTC/Ni3B interface further optimized stress redistribution by enhancing energy dissipation pathways. These findings  establish  a  generalized  coating  strategy  for  ceramic  matrix composites requiring simultaneous mechanical robustness and thermal stability.

question: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2014, ZS20La (ZrB2 with 20 vol% SiC and 2 wt% La2O3) exhibited a highly protective oxidation kinetic exponent of 8 at 1600°C for oxide layer thickness. What new oxidation products were identified as responsible for this unusually high protective behavior?
answer: The new oxidation products identified as responsible for the unusually high protective behavior in ZS20La (ZrB2 with 20 vol% SiC and 2 wt% La2O3) at 1600°C are MeOx Cy particles, where Me is Zr, Hf, or Si [1]. Specifically, the formation of ZrOx Cy and SiOx Cy particles was noted, which contributed to the high oxidation kinetic exponent of 8 due to their ability to adsorb oxygen-containing species and limit oxygen diffusion [2].

chunks.text:
Abstract
Long-term oxidation kinetics of SiC-reinforced UHTCs and La2O3-doped UHTCs over an intermediate temperature range (1400-1600 ◦ C) reveal partially protective behavior for the former characterized by an oxidation kinetic exponent 1 < n <2. In addition, unstable oxidation behavior was observed in HfB2-based UHTCs due to the presence of SiC agglomerates. On the other hand, La2O3-doped UHTCs were found to be protective over the whole temperature range studied ( n =2), in particular at 1600 ◦ C, where oxidation kinetic exponents as high as 8 were observed as a consequence of formation of new oxidation protective particles, MeO x C y , where Me is Zr, Hf or Si. Adsorption of oxygen-containing species formed protective MeO x C y phases, which enhanced the thermal stability of the oxide scale as well as providing protection against oxidation for long exposure times at 1600 ◦ C.
©2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Keywords: Oxidation kinetics; Oxidation resistance; Borosilicate coating; Protective coating; Coating instability

3.3.2. ZS20La
The presence of La2O3 within the BS melt increases its viscosity and reduces the O2 and CO2 diffusion coefficients, so promoting reaction (8) to take place at 1600 ◦ C. In addition, ZrO2 x C x could react and be oxidized by atmospheric O2 forming an active-protective barrier for inwards oxygen diffusion. The adsorption of species is also noticeable and confirmed by the difference between mass gain kinetics and oxide layer thickness kinetics. Moreover, a large mass gain change produces a relatively small increase of oxide scale thickness as a consequence of oxidation kinetics exponent between mass gain and oxide layer (2 and 8, respectively), as seen in Fig. 2. An oxidation kinetic exponent as high as 8 could be obtained if Fick's law for atomic diffusion is constrained by the presence of a particle sink. 46 Furthermore, the more particles formed within the oxide scale, the more limited the oxygen diffusion. The continuous oxidation of ZrO x C y and SiO x C y particles and continuous recombination or possible adsorption of oxygen-containing species explain how an appreciable change in mass gain produce a small change in oxide layer thickness, addressing the difference in measured values for oxidation kinetics exponents (Table 1). However, the spatial distribution of ZrO2 and ZrO x C y is not clear as it is likely there is more than one mechanism forming ZrO x C y ; this is the subject of further investigation. Therefore, ZrO x C y coatings are presented as new candidates for UHTCs protection against oxidation. Fig. 9 shows an overview of ZS20La oxide layer formed at 1600 ◦ C after 4 h oxidation.
Precipitation of ZrSiO4 was detected and characterized by small light contrast features at micron scale level ( ∼ 0.5 to 2 𝛍 m)

4. Conclusions
Oxidation kinetics have been studied for ZrB2- and HfB2based UHTCs. A partially protective behavior against oxidation was characterized for SiC-reinforced UHTCs (1 < n <2). Moreover, the presence of SiC agglomerates led to a completely unstable and unprotective behavior for long exposures times at 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C in HS20. La2O3 addition is beneficial for oxidation protection as it increases BS melt viscosity and lowers the oxygen diffusion coefficient through the BS melt. On the other hand, an increase of BS melt viscosity led to a more homogeneous mixing between oxide products and BS melt as well as reducing the thickness of the outer BS protective coating, which is detrimental for UHTCs oxidation resistance as it is the oxygen diffusion limiting layer. However, new reactions were observed at 1600 ◦ C to form MeO x C y with a more stable and protective behavior for long exposure times than SiC-reinforced UHTCs. Therefore, it is proposed that MeO x C y -containing coatings are promising and protective coatings for UHTCs, even after longterm exposures. The detection of different phases suggests that the O/C ratio could be tailored to minimize thermal mismatch damage at the interface arising from e.g. thermal fatigue.

3.3.3. HS20
HfB2 has a lower reactivity with oxygen and HfO2 has lower oxygen permeability compared to ZrB2 and ZrO2, respectively. 9 Asaresult B2O3 content in the BS melt in HS20 samples may be lower or even negligible compared to ZS20. Therefore, a much thinner BS layer protects the HfB2-based UHTCs more effectively than in ZrB2-based UHTCs, as seen in Fig. 2. HS20 at 1400 ◦ C (Fig. 2) show a pure parabolic-protective behavior as a consequence of the lower B2O3 content in its BS melt. However, there is an important microstructural difference between ZS20 and HS20, which is that the distribution of SiC particles throughout the HfB2 matrix is not homogeneous and there are large SiC agglomerates (up to 20 𝛍 mdia.). Therefore, once the oxide scale is thicker than 20 𝛍 msomeSiC agglomerates have been already oxidized leaving larger cavities, up to five times larger along one direction and up to 20 times larger in terms of surface covered by SiC agglomerate as seen in Fig. 1. Their presence has the following effects; (i) capillary forces that move BS melt to the top are lower and then oxidation protection is less efficient, and (ii) the generation of a large cavity within a porous layer could be detrimental as it communicates with other pores and could lead to detachment of oxide particles or enhance oxidation system instabilities. As a consequence, the mass gain and oxide layer kinetic exponents are ∼ 0.5 at 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C. This does not mean that the BS coating is completely unprotective. It indicates that for short periods of time (<4 h at 1500 ◦ C and <2 h at 1600 ◦ C)thebehavior is protective and after a critical oxide layer thickness is reached, the oxidation behavior turns into an unprotective linear behavior, which as a result gives a mean kinetic exponent of ∼ 0.5 for the whole process, as depicted in Fig. 2.

3.3.4. HS20La
HS20La behaves similar to ZS20La, with the difference that it is not more oxidation resistant than HS20 at 1400 ◦ C due to lower content of B2O3in the BS layer in HfB2-based UHTCs. However, the presence of SiC agglomerates did not affect the stability of its behavior at 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C, which is protective as shown in Table 1 and Fig. 2. In addition, the oxide layer thickness kinetic exponent at 1600 ◦ C is smaller than ZS20La, which could be related to either the presence of SiC agglomerates, the lower oxidation rates of HfB2-based UHTCs compared to ZrB2-based UHTCs or a lack of evidence of BS melt phase in the oxide layer. Furthermore, the formation of different HfO x C y phases was detected by EDS. Fig. 10a shows the oxide scale layer of HS20La after oxidation at 1600 ◦ C for 3 h, in which an area near the top surface layer and oxide layer interface with unoxidized volume are magnified in Fig. 10b and c, respectively. Oxidation mechanisms are slightly different than in ZS20La because in HS20La SiC gradually oxidized and there are small SiC particles (0.5 -2 𝛍 m) clearly visible at the interface (Fig. 10b). In addition, a new oxide phase, HfO3, was detected. A BS image (Fig. 11), reveals different contrast between HfO2 and HfO3 as well as with La-SiO x C y particles, small particles of HfOxCy (5 - 10 𝛍 m) with high (85 atomic %) C content and small (0.5 - 2 𝛍 m)SiCparticles in the oxide layer. Different EDS
Fig. 11. BS SEM micrograph of HS20La at 1600 ◦ C for 3 h near the interface with un-oxidized UHTC volume.
Fig. 12. EDS spectra of different phases detected after oxidation at 1600 ◦ C for 3 h in HS20La.

question: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2014, the HfB2-based UHTC HS20 showed unstable oxidation behavior with kinetic exponents of 0.5 at 1500°C and 1600°C, in contrast to the protective behavior of ZS20. What microstructural feature was identified as the primary cause of this difference in oxidation kinetics?
answer: The primary cause of the difference in oxidation kinetics between HS20 and ZS20 was identified as the presence of large SiC agglomerates (up to 20 𝛍 m dia.) in the HfB2 matrix of HS20, which led to the formation of larger cavities once the oxide scale became thicker than 20 𝛍 m [1].

Different EDS
Fig. 11. BS SEM micrograph of HS20La at 1600 ◦ C for 3 h near the interface with un-oxidized UHTC volume.
Fig. 12. EDS spectra of different phases detected after oxidation at 1600 ◦ C for 3 h in HS20La.

3.1. Oxidation kinetics characterization
Oxidation kinetics at 1400 ◦ C, 1500 ◦ Cand1600 ◦ Care illustrated in Fig. 2, in which mass gain per unit area and oxide scale thickness versus time are represented. In addition, best fitting curves according to the power law equation (2) are shown. Oxidation kinetics exponents n are summarized in Table 1, which indicates a mix of linear and parabolic behavior (1 < n <2) or pure parabolic behavior ( n ∼ 2) for almost all compositions and conditions, except for HS20 at 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C (highly unprotective or unstable behavior n ∼ 0.5), ZS20La at 1600 ◦ C ( n ∼ 8, highly protective behavior) and HS20La at 1400 ◦ C ( n ∼ 4, highly protective behavior). Moreover, oxidation kinetic exponents n in mass gain were found to be similar to those determined from oxide scale thicknesses, except for ZS20La at 1600 ◦ C ( n ∼ 2 and n ∼ 8, respectively) and HS20La at 1600 ◦ C ( n ∼ 1and n ∼ 2, respectively), which may indicate mass adsorption in the oxide layer as mass gain increases faster than oxide scale thickness.
Fig. 3 shows the oxidation of all UHCTs compositions at 1400 ◦ C without holding time to illustrate the initial oxidation of UHTCs, justifying use of the term x 0 in fitting curves. Therefore, the inclusion of point (0, 0) in previous oxidation kinetics studies is misleading as oxidation takes place during heating as a consequence of low heating rates ( ∼ 10 K/min) in open air box furnace experiments without any holding time. 30,34 This effect is also far from equilibrium and addressed by the term x 0.

Abstract
Long-term oxidation kinetics of SiC-reinforced UHTCs and La2O3-doped UHTCs over an intermediate temperature range (1400-1600 ◦ C) reveal partially protective behavior for the former characterized by an oxidation kinetic exponent 1 < n <2. On the other hand, La2O3-doped UHTCs were found to be protective over the whole temperature range studied ( n =2), in particular at 1600 ◦ C, where oxidation kinetic exponents as high as 8 were observed as a consequence of formation of new oxidation protective particles, MeO x C y , where Me is Zr, Hf or Si. Adsorption of oxygen-containing species formed protective MeO x C y phases, which enhanced the thermal stability of the oxide scale as well as providing protection against oxidation for long exposure times at 1600 ◦ C.

Keywords: Oxidation kinetics; Oxidation resistance; Borosilicate coating; Protective coating; Coating instability

3.3.2. ZS20La
ZS20La showed pure parabolic behavior at 1400 ◦ C ( n ∼ 2) and its oxidation kinetic exponent increases with temperature to
∼ 2.5 at 1500 ◦ C. In addition, values of n for mass gain and oxide scale thickness at 1600 ◦ C are different by almost four times, 2.1 for mass gain and 7.5 for oxide scale, which indicates that an oxygen adsorption mechanism may be active because large changes in mass gain lead to small changes in oxide scale. Oxide scale thickness of ZS20La is smaller than oxide scale thickness of ZS20 at 1400 ◦ C while the converse applies for 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C as seen in Fig. 2, presumably due to the higher volatilization of B2O3 present in the BS layer of ZS20 at these temperatures. Therefore, ZS20La is more oxidation resistant at 1400 ◦ C but not at 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C. The main difference between the oxide layers of ZS20 and ZS20La is the mixing of the BS glassy melt with the ZrO2 scale which is more viscous in ZS20La and does not ascend directly to the top surface, producing a more homogeneous mixing between glassy and oxide phase as seen if Fig. 7 is compared to the ZS20 oxide layer in Fig. 5a or b. Homogeneous mixing of BS melt with ZrO2 could be detrimental for the system as diffusion through ZrO2 is much quicker than through a BS melt even if it has higher viscosity due to the presence of La2O3. La2O3 proximity to SiC promotes the inclusion of La2O3 into the BS phase, increasing viscosity and thermal stability as well as lowering the oxygen diffusion coefficient through the glassy phase. In fact, La2O3 is used as an additive to increase creep resistance of MoSi2, 45 which contains glassy SiO2 at grain boundaries and triple junctions. question: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2014, Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) was used to process ZS20 and ZS20La, but the resulting microstructure showed that La2O3 location affected SiC particle dispersion. How did the proximity of La2O3 to SiC particles influence the homogeneity of the microstructure in the La2O3-doped composition?
answer: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2014, the proximity of La2O3 to SiC particles influenced the homogeneity of the microstructure in the La2O3-doped composition by promoting a more effective relaxation of elastic strains during SPS cooling, which subsequently reduced the fracture toughness of ZS20La compared to ZS20 [3]. This suggests that the presence of La2O3 near SiC particles may lead to less homogeneous microstructural features in the La2O3-doped composition.

chunks.text:
2. Experimental procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ =6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ =6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (ZS20), ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (ZS20La), HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described previously. 10 The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs, as shown in previous work 10 and Fig. 1, are; (I) high density (>99% of theoretical relative density); (II) ZrB2-based UHTCs contain a homogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the ZrB2 matrix with SiC grain sizes between ∼ 1 and 2 𝛍 m while HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 𝛍 m; (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles.

2. Experimental procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma-Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ = 10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma-Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ = 3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma-Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; ZrB2, ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (ZS20), ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (ZS20La), HfB2, HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described in a previous work. 22 The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs, previously described, 22 are; (I) high density (>99% of theoretical relative density) except monolithic ZrB2 which has a density ∼ 95% as a consequence of O impurities which prevent its full densification; (II) ZrB2-based UHTCs contain a homogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the ZrB2 matrix with SiC grain sizes between ∼ 1 and 2 𝛍 m while HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 𝛍 m; (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles.

2. Experimental procedure
HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 μ m, ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( α -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 μ m, ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 μ m, ρ =6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described previously [10]. The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs [11,19] are; (I) high density (> 99% of theoretical relative density); (II) HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 μ m; and (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles and improve the SiC dispersion throughout the HfB2 matrix.

3.1. Microstructure and composition of the as-fabricated composites
The volume fractions of ZrB2, SiC and La2O3 phases is determined as normalized volume fractions excluding open porosity which are illustrated in Fig. 2 a .  Due to the content of La2O3 in the composites is limited, X-Ray diffraction peaks of La2O3 are not detected in Fig. 2 b . Besides, the SiC formed by PCS at 1100 ℃ is  amorphous phase which cannot be detected by XRD in Fig. 2 b . The density and open porosity of the composites were determined and shown in Table 1.
Take sample ZS 4.6 L as an example, the microstructure and element distribution of the composites are characterized by SEM and EDS and illustrated in Fig. 3. The sample ZS 4.6 L shows a similar microstructure feature with ZS which has a multi-layer structure [27,28]. The SI method is efficient to introduce the ZrB2-La2O3  powders into the inter-bundle area of the fiber fabrics. The intra-bundle area is difficult to fill up by ZrB2-La2O3  during SI due to the impeding of the carbon fibers. Therefore,  ZrB2-La2O3 mainly  concentrates  in  the  inter-bundle  area.  By contrast, the SiC matrix can be introduced into the intra-bundle area effectively during the PIP process, due to the PCS precursor is liquid. Therefore, the La2O3 modified Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites present a specific multi-layer structure. Otherwise, the La2O3  covered by SiC phase and distributes in inter-bundle area which prevents the contact and reaction among La2O3, PyC interface and carbon fibers. The PyC interface and fibers remain complete without clear damage.

question: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2014, mass gain kinetic exponents and oxide layer thickness kinetic exponents were measured for ZS20La at 1600°C and found to differ significantly (2.1 vs 7.5). What mechanism was proposed to explain this large discrepancy between the two measurements?
answer: The large discrepancy between the mass gain kinetic exponents (2.1) and oxide layer thickness kinetic exponents (7.5) for ZS20La at 1600°C was proposed to be explained by the activation of an oxygen adsorption mechanism, where large changes in mass gain lead to small changes in oxide scale thickness [1].

However, there is an important microstructural difference between ZS20 and HS20, which is that the distribution of SiC particles throughout the HfB2 matrix is not homogeneous and there are large SiC agglomerates (up to 20 𝛍 mdia.). Therefore, once the oxide scale is thicker than 20 𝛍 msomeSiC agglomerates have been already oxidized leaving larger cavities, up to five times larger along one direction and up to 20 times larger in terms of surface covered by SiC agglomerate as seen in Fig. 1. Their presence has the following effects; (i) capillary forces that move BS melt to the top are lower and then oxidation protection is less efficient, and (ii) the generation of a large cavity within a porous layer could be detrimental as it communicates with other pores and could lead to detachment of oxide particles or enhance oxidation system instabilities. As a consequence, the mass gain and oxide layer kinetic exponents are ∼ 0.5 at 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C. This does not mean that the BS coating is completely unprotective. It indicates that for short periods of time (<4 h at 1500 ◦ C and <2 h at 1600 ◦ C)thebehavior is protective and after a critical oxide layer thickness is reached, the oxidation behavior turns into an unprotective linear behavior, which as a result gives a mean kinetic exponent of ∼ 0.5 for the whole process, as depicted in Fig. 2.

3.1. Oxidation kinetics characterization
Oxidation kinetics at 1400 ◦ C, 1500 ◦ Cand1600 ◦ Care illustrated in Fig. 2, in which mass gain per unit area and oxide scale thickness versus time are represented. In addition, best fitting curves according to the power law equation (2) are shown. Oxidation kinetics exponents n are summarized in Table 1, which indicates a mix of linear and parabolic behavior (1 < n <2) or pure parabolic behavior ( n ∼ 2) for almost all compositions and conditions, except for HS20 at 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C (highly unprotective or unstable behavior n ∼ 0.5), ZS20La at 1600 ◦ C ( n ∼ 8, highly protective behavior) and HS20La at 1400 ◦ C ( n ∼ 4, highly protective behavior). Moreover, oxidation kinetic exponents n in mass gain were found to be similar to those determined from oxide scale thicknesses, except for ZS20La at 1600 ◦ C ( n ∼ 2 and n ∼ 8, respectively) and HS20La at 1600 ◦ C ( n ∼ 1and n ∼ 2, respectively), which may indicate mass adsorption in the oxide layer as mass gain increases faster than oxide scale thickness.
Fig. 3 shows the oxidation of all UHCTs compositions at 1400 ◦ C without holding time to illustrate the initial oxidation of UHTCs, justifying use of the term x 0 in fitting curves. Therefore, the inclusion of point (0, 0) in previous oxidation kinetics studies is misleading as oxidation takes place during heating as a consequence of low heating rates ( ∼ 10 K/min) in open air box furnace experiments without any holding time. 30,34 This effect is also far from equilibrium and addressed by the term x 0.

question: How did cobalt contamination from milling media affect the sintering behavior of TiB2-2(WC-6Co) (TW2) in the Galizia 2024 study, and what was the resulting densification onset temperature?
answer: Cobalt contamination from milling media in the TiB2-2(WC-6Co) (TW2) samples affected the sintering behavior by reducing the densification onset temperature (TON). Specifically, the TON for TW2 was 1010 ◦ C [1], which is quite low compared to other similar TiB2-based materials [50]. This lower TON can be attributed, in part, to the presence of Co traces from milling with WC media [2].

chunks.text:
3.1. Densification behaviour
High-density samples ( > 97 %, see Table 2) were obtained despite a dwell time lower than that reported in literature [48,49]. TW2 samples showed the lowest onset temperature (TON) of 1010 ◦ C. This temperature is quite low compared to other similar TiB2-based materials [50], and is in agreement with the previous work [39]. However, the sintering phenomena start to slow down before the maximum temperature is reached, as indicated by the peak of the densification rate curve occurring well before the dwell step and the plateauing of the curve at 97 % before the start of dwell time (Fig. 1a). This sintering behaviour can be attributed to the presence of Co, which promotes liquid sintering mechanisms that require lower temperatures to be activated, but were not sufficient to complete densification [51]. ZW6 samples reached full densification quickly, while HW6 samples required a longer isothermal hold due to the refractory nature of HfB2. In particular, ZrB2-based material showed the highest densification rate value of 7.3 % min GLYPH<0> 1 allowing full densification within a limited dwell time. It is worth noting that the observed TON of 1400 ◦ C is lower than those reported for ZrB2 with other ceramic sintering aids (Si3N4, SiC, Y2O3, Al2O3, ZrN) [52 -54]. A lower TON was only obtained when using Ni as the sintering aid [55]. However, Ni prevented grain growth, which is desired to increase the thermal conductivity. For this reason, although the activation of densification mechanisms at low temperature and reaching the plateau took less than 15 min, the isothermal hold was extended to 30 min to ensure a coarse and homogeneous microstructure and to raise the thermal conductivity. Noteworthy, the manufacturing process of this work allowed to reduce the starting densification temperature (TON) compared to similar ZrB2 materials [56] containing higher amount of starting WC (15 vol% instead of 4.1 vol%).

3.1. Densification behaviour
Another reason for this lower sintering onset could be the high thickness of pellet (24 -30 mm instead of 10 mm [57]), which allows earlier detection of shrinkage due to measurement sensitivity, even though the actual sintering temperature remains the same. Additionally, Co traces from milling with WC media reduce TON. The lower sinterability of the HfB2-based material, indicated by the higher TON of 1700 ◦ C and lower densification rate (peak value of 3.2 % min GLYPH<0> 1 ), was compensated by a longer isothermal hold. This requirement is due to the more refractory behaviour of HfB2 compared to ZrB2, which includes a higher melting point and lower transport rates [53, 54,58].

In all micrographs, the border between the untreated area (on the left side) and the plasmaexposed area (on the right side) is approximately in the center of the image. The insets show BSE images of untreated area. The length of all scale bars is 2 μ m. A thin dashed line was placed indicatively among the two area.

2.1. Material manufacturing
Commercial powders were used to prepare the ceramic materials. Table 1 shows some characteristics of the raw powders.
A conventional ceramic processing route was employed to produce ZrB2 and HfB2 with 6 mol% WC compositions. First, powder mixtures of ZrB2 + 6 mol% WC (equivalent to 4.1 vol% and 10.0 wt%) and HfB2 + 6 mol% WC (equivalent to 4.0 vol% and 5.9 wt%) were ball milled. In particular, 277.05 g of ZrB2 plus 30.69 g of WC (labelled ZW6) and 239.48 g of HfB2 plus 14.96 g of WC (labelled HW6) were milled for 72 h using 15 mm WC-6 wt.% Co spheres in a PE bottle of 1 L with a powder/ spheres/ethanol weight ratio of 1/2.4/0.2. After drying via rotavapor at 85 ◦ C, the powders were dry milled in the same previous bottles with the same milling media for 4 h in order to disaggregate the powders and make easier the following sieving at 250 μ m.
For the TiB2-WC composition, WC was added through a high energy milling with WC-6 wt% Co spheres, as reported in Ref. [39], to improve the densification thanks to the synergistic action of WC impurities, acting as sintering aid, and size reduction of the starting TiB2 powders [40 -43]. In particular, the starting TiB2 powders were milled by high energy milling in a WC jar using 0.5 mm WC-6Co spheres, in air. The powder mixtures were then separated from the spheres, weighted, dried via rotavapor and sieved. The contamination of WC was expected to be around 2 mol% (equivalents to 1.6 vol% and 5.4 wt%) and for this reason the as produced TiB2-WC composition was labelled TW2.

5.1. TiB2 ceramics
Due to the high melting point, strong covalent bonding, low self-diffusion co-efficient (due to high covalent bonding) and presence of impurities in the starting powders, it is impossible to sinter phase-pure TiB2 to near-theoretical sinter-densities at temperatures below 2000 °C when using the conventional pressureless sintering technique [9] (see Table 4). In particular, the presence of oxide impurity in the powders resulted in the poor densification of TiB2 [9,19]. Baik and Becher [97] analyzed this adverse effect of oxygen contamination in various forms, which gets introduced during the various processing steps (primarily during synthesis and subsequent handling), in terms of further stimulating the grain growth and coarsening of pores. Neutron activation analysis was reportedly used for oxygen measurement. The effects of oxygen content on the sinter-density and grain size of TiB2 is shown in Fig. 9a. It was apparent that oxygen in the starting powders lead to lowering of sinter-density and dramatic increase in the grain size of TiB2. In case of pressureless sintering of TiB2, the densification is controlled by grain boundary diffusion and the grain growth by surface diffusion. In order to achieve good sinter-density and to minimize abnormal grain growth, the total oxygen content of the powders must be limited to < ~0.5 wt% or strong reducing agents/additives need to be used to remove TiOx (present on the surface of powders) below 1600 °C. Even though the role of oxygen (i.e. oxide layer) is still under debate, it is sometimes believed that the presence of oxide or metallic impurities may activate the densification process, but also at the same time enhance grain and pore growth (such as for B2O3), which ultimately limits the maximum achievable density to 95% or less [6,9].

question: In the Galizia 2024 study, what plasma exposure conditions were used for HfB2-6WC, and why were thermal properties considered stable despite plasma irradiation?
answer: In the Galizia 2024 study, HfB2-6WC was exposed to deuterium plasma with a fluence of 1.5e25 ions/m², a flux of 1e21 ions/m² s, and an ion energy of 38 eV/ions [4]. The thermal properties were considered stable despite plasma irradiation because no significant deterioration of the thermal properties of MB2-WC ceramics (M = Ti, Zr, Hf) was observed after the deuterium plasma exposure or proton irradiation [2].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
The thermal properties of ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) in the MB2-WC (M = Ti, Zr, Hf) system and tungsten were studied for potential application as plasma-facing materials in fusion power plants. The sintered UHTC and tungsten samples were subjected to deuterium plasma or protons irradiation. Thermal diffusivity was measured using the laser flash method, and superficial thermal conductivity was analyzed through atomic force microscopy. Results showed that the thermal properties did not degrade when exposed to relevant environments and remained stable over a range of temperatures, unlike the reference tungsten material. Thermal conductivity ranged from 61 to 68 W m GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 for TiB2-2(WC-6Co), from 53 to 63 W m GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 for ZrB2-6WC, from 67 to 75 W m GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 for HfB2-6WC, and from 180 to 119 W m GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 for tungsten across the temperature range from room temperature to 1200 ◦ C. The increasing trend of thermal effusivity, over 19000 J s GLYPH<0> 0.5 m GLYPH<0> 2 K GLYPH<0> 1 at 1200 ◦ C, justifies further testing and of UHTC materials for fusion applications.

4. Conclusion
No significant deterioration of the thermal properties of MB2-WC ceramics (M = Ti, Zr, Hf) was observed after the deuterium plasma exposure or proton irradiation, making these materials promising candidates for fusion applications. The measured thermal diffusivity ranged from 20.0 ± 0.4 to 12.1 ± 0.3 mm 2 s GLYPH<0> 1 for TiB2-2(WC-6Co), from 18.4 ± 0.2 to 14.5 ± 0.2 mm 2 s GLYPH<0> 1 for ZrB2-6WC, from 25.4 ± 0.3 to 14.8 ± 0.2 mm 2 s GLYPH<0> 1 for HfB2-6WC, and from 70.2 ± 3.1 to 38.1 ± 1.3 mm 2 s GLYPH<0> 1 for tungsten reference material, across the temperature range from room temperature to 1200 ◦ C. The corresponding calculated thermal conductivities ranged from 61 ± 1 to 68 ± 2 W m GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 , from 53 ± 1 to 63 ± 1 W m GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 , from 67 ± 2 to 75 ± 2 W m GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 , and from 180 ± 8 to 119 ± 4 W m GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 for TiB2-2(WC-6Co), ZrB2-6WC, HfB2-6WC and W, respectively. A key finding that supports further testing and characterization of these UHTCs for use as plasma-facing materials is their thermal effusivity.

Tungsten-based materials, for instance, present challenges related to structural integrity, including susceptibility to brittle failure [22], as demonstrated by Hong Zhang et al. in Ref. [23]. In this work, tungsten samples were exposed to a deuterium plasma on a linear plasma device with a fluence of 1.5e25 ions/m 2 , a flux of 1e21 ions/m 2 s, and an ion energy of 38 eV/ions (to avoid the creation of defects by displacement damage [24]), with temperature controlled at 127 ◦ C and 450 ◦ C. In this context, the study of transition metal diborides ceramics, MB2 (M = Ti or Zr) [14 -18,25] becomes relevant. In fact, despite their brittle behaviour, as also observed in tungsten, these materials offer intrinsic thermal stability combined with high thermal conductivity ranging from 50 to 120 W m GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 , even at temperatures up to 2000 ◦ C [1 -4,26 -30]. Previous studies show ZrB2, irradiated with 30 keV He + to fluences up to 5e22 He/m 2 at temperatures up to ~850 ◦ C, displayed similar mass loss as tungsten [14]. 6. Conclusion
Two UHTC HfB 2 -SiC-C (graphene) specimens reached 1750 ◦ C when exposed to a supersonic jet of carbon dioxide plasma. Additional laser irradiation increased the temperature at the center of the heated surface of one of these specimens to 2190 ◦ C. After laser irradiation, an area of localized overheating remained on the surface of the specimen. This may be associated with irreversible changes in the surface properties. The emissivity of the specimens decreased during heating in carbon dioxide plasma due to oxidation. The heat flux to the surface of ceramic specimens in the dissociated carbon dioxide flow was lower than the heat flux to the cold copper wall under the same exposure conditions. This is due to the significantly lower catalytic activity of the HfB 2 -SiCC (graphene) material surface heated in the supersonic carbon dioxide plasma with respect to the heterogeneous recombination of O and CO and temperature factor.
Analysis of the surface of ultra high temperature ceramic materials after exposure to supersonic carbon dioxide plasma, as well as combined with the effects of laser radiation, indicates its oxidation: instead of the components of the original ceramics HfO 2 is present as the most refractory and the least volatile oxidation product for HfB 2 . Impurities of more fusible and volatile oxidation products -silicon and boron oxides - were not found using the applied methods of Xray phase and energy dispersive elemental analysis. Despite the similar chemical composition of the oxidized surface of specimens (hafnium dioxide), significant differences in their microstructure were detected: less rough surface of the specimen exposed to the additional influence of laser radiation, probably associated with sintering of individual particles HfO 2 under the influence of significantly higher temperatures established during the tests.

question: In the Galizia 2024 study, what distinct compositional preparation methods were used for TW2 versus ZW6, and how did this relate to their microstructural differences?
answer:
In the Galizia 2024 study, the compositional preparation methods for TW2 and ZW6 differed in the starting material composition. TW2 was prepared using a starting composition of TiB₂ + 2(WC-6Co) [1], whereas ZW6 was prepared using a starting composition of ZrB₂ + 6WC [1]. 

This difference in composition relates to their microstructural differences. For TW2, the main phase identified was hexagonal TiB₂, with secondary phases including orthorhombic WB and orthorhombic CoW₂B₂ [4]. In contrast, ZW6 had hexagonal ZrB₂ as the main phase, with secondary phases such as tetragonal WB and cubic ZrO₂ [4]. Additionally, the higher amount of starting WC in ZW6 compared to TW2 resulted in more prominent WB peaks forming a tetragonal lattice [2]. Furthermore, ZW6 showed the presence of free carbon appearing as a dark elongated shape, while TW2 did not exhibit such features [3].

3.2. Microstructure
XRDpatterns showed crystalline diborides as the main phase (Fig. 4). Various secondary and minor phases were detected in the UHTC samples (Table 3). In case of TW2 sample, the main phase was identified as hexagonal TiB2, with orthorhombic WB and orthorhombic CoW2B2 as secondary phases. For ZW6 sample, the main phase was hexagonal ZrB2. Due to the higher amount of staring WC in ZW6 material compared to TW2(10wt%instead of the expected 5.4 wt%), the WB peaks were more prominent forming a tetragonal lattice as seen in a similar material with 15 vol% of starting WC instead of 4.1 vol% [56] and other ZrB2-based ceramics with WC [61,62]. ZW6 sample also showed traces of cubic ZrO2. For HW6 sample the main phase was hexagonal HfB2. A small amount of staring WC resulted in smaller peaks of tetragonal WB, similar to ZW6. The oxidic HfO2 phase showed a monoclinic lattice. Unlike ZW6, which had dispersed free carbon (Fig. 3b), HW6 had carbon forming cubic HfC. The peak shift toward higher 2-theta angles suggests that HfC is a solid solution containing W atoms [56]. Notably, SEM analysis showed no microcracks in ZW6 or HW6 due to the presence of oxidic phases [48].
Fig. 4. XRD diffractograms from the materials TW2 (bottom diffractogram), ZW6 (centre diffractogram) and HW6 (top diffractogram).

3.2. Microstructure
SEM analysis confirmed low levels of residual porosity in TW2 and HW6 samples, with nearly pores-free ZW6 sample (Fig. 3). A core-shell structure (more pronounced in ZW6 sample) was observed in all samples, indicating the formation of solid solutions of diborides with tungsten. The core consists of MB2, while the shell consists of (M, W)B2 solid solution cores (M = Ti, Zr, or Hf). This result confirms the active role of WC in effectively promoting densification during hot-pressing and indicates that the starting diborides and tungsten are mutually soluble at high temperatures, as previously reported [56,59,60]. The WB phase appeared as a lighter, homogeneously distributed phase. The ZW6 sample showed the presence of free carbon appearing as a dark elongated shape.

3.3. Surface roughness
While no microstructure changes were observed after proton irradiation, deuterium plasma exposure led to surface erosion and texture modifications (Fig. 3). A distinct change in surface topology around the grains is visible in the irradiated region. Despite this, Ra values remained low ( < 0.04 μ m, see Fig. 5), and the tendency for Ra to increase with applied bias voltage was unclear, likely due to the manual polishing. Consistent with observations in similar UHTCs [63,64], the differences between the different borides are mainly due to varying levels of residual porosity. Specifically, the average Ra values ranged from 0.019 μ m for ZW6, to 0.022 μ m for HW6 sample and 0.027 μ m for TW2.

question: In the Chen 2025 first-principles study, how does the addition of titanium to zirconium carbide affect lattice parameters, and what causes this change in lattice constants?
answer: In the Chen 2025 first-principles study, the addition of titanium to zirconium carbide causes a decrease in lattice constants and unit-cell volume [1]. This change in lattice constants is primarily due to the smaller atomic radius of Ti compared to Zr, leading to lattice distortion and reduced symmetry [1]. Additionally, the incorporation of Ti atoms into the ZrC lattice causes changes in bond lengths and angles, resulting in lattice contraction around zirconium atoms and the generation of tensile stress within the crystal [3].

chunks.text:

The lattice constants and unit-cell volume obtained after structural optimization  of  the  SQS  model  generated  through  first-principles calculations using ATAT are listed in Table 1. It can be observed that the lattice constant of zirconium carbide is 4.723 after structural optimization, which is close to the calculation result of Li ' s [43]. Moreover, the lattice  constants  and  volume  of  the  solid  solution  decrease  with increasing Ti content, primarily due to the smaller atomic radius of Ti compared to Zr. 3. Results and discussion
The lattice parameters, interplanar distance and crystallite size of ZrC produced by microwave-assisted carbothermal reduction are shown in Table 1. The interplanar distances of the synthesized ZrC were not significantly affected with the increase in the reaction time and were near the standard crystal structure of ZrC. In contrast, the changing of lattice parameter is attributed to the formation of non-stoichiometric zirconium carbide, Zr1-xC. It can be noted that with the augment in the reaction time, the lattice parameter increased, suggesting the tendency to form stoichiometric ZrC. In order to estimate the Zr/C ratio, four sets of data were chosen from the literature to plot a Zr/C vs. lattice parameter curve, as can be observed in Fig. 4. After a polynomial fit, our experimental lattice parameters were used to estimate the Zr/C ratio. The estimative can be observed on Fig. 4. The green triangles are from M. Gendre at al. data [17], the red squares are from D. Hauser et al. data [40], the blue circles belongs to W. Xu et al. data [41] and finally the brown lozenges are the calculated Zr/C ratio from this work lattice parameter values. The Zr/C ratio values can be observed in Table 2, which confirms the tendency to the stoichiometric ZrC. According to Hauser et al. [40], the non-stoichiometry can be attributed to the presence of substitutional oxygen on the lattice, or also can be attributed to zirconium vacancy, which can play an important role on densification [42]. We believe the defect is due the substitutional oxygen or from zirconium oxide residues or from adventitious oxygen from the carbon monoxide or dioxide layer of the just reduced zirconia.

3.1. Structure and property analysis of the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solution
atomic doping concentration on the structural stability of solid solutions (Fig. 2(a)). As revealed by Fig. 2(a), the formation energy increases with higher Ti content, indicating that Ti atomic incorporation reduces the structural stability of ZrC. This phenomenon can be explained by the gradual increase in the lattice distortion and elevated microscopic internal  stress,  which  collectively  increase  the  total  energy.  The  bond lengths  and  angles  changed  when  ZrC  lattice  incorporates  titanium atoms, thereby causing lattice distortion. Moreover, since the atomic radius of titanium atoms is smaller than that of zirconium atoms, the lattice around the zirconium atoms will contract when titanium atoms occupy the sites of zirconium atoms, and tensile stress will be generated within the crystal. Additional energy is required for solid solution to reconstruct the local structure, thus resulting in an increase in formation energy.  Meanwhile,  the  cohesive  energy  of  the  solid  solution  also gradually decreases, which is primarily because the strength of the Ti-C bonds formed is weaker than that of the Zr-C bonds. question: In the Chen 2025 study, how do the bonding characteristics of Ti-C versus Zr-C bonds in (Zr1−xTix)C solid solution relate to differences in elastic constants C11 and C44?
answer: In the Chen 2025 study, the bonding characteristics of Ti-C versus Zr-C bonds in (Zr1−xTix)C solid solution are related to differences in elastic constants C11 and C44 through the bond strength and bond length. The Ti-C bond exhibits a shorter bond length (2.27 Å) but weaker bond strength (integral crystal orbital overlap population = 0.1435), whereas the Zr-C bond has a longer bond length (2.36 Å) and a higher population (0.1896). The lower population of Ti-C directly correlates with reduced elastic constants, as population reflects the covalent interaction strength between atoms [1]. This weaker covalent interaction in Ti-C bonds results in a reduction in the elastic constants C11 and C44, indicating weakened resistance to both uniaxial compressive stress and shear resistance.

The charge density difference presented in Fig. 5(b) reveals a slight charge transfer between C and Zr atoms, indicating ionic bonding interactions in Zr-C. 4. Conclusions
This study predicted the structure and properties of ZrC-based solid solutions  by  combining  first-principles  calculations  and  SQS  models. The following conclusions are obtained: The addition of Ti to ZrC reduces the structural stability, melting point, Young ' s modulus, hardness and abrasion resistance. The toughness of the solid solution increases, but  it  remains  brittle.  Furthermore,  the  addition  of  N  atoms  to  the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C ternary solid solution improves structural stability. With the increase in nitrogen content, the Young ' s modulus and hardness of the solid solution first increase and then decrease, reaching their maximum values of 422.7 and 24.71 GPa, respectively, at a nitrogen content of 19 at%.  Similarly,  the  melting  point,  Debye  temperature,  and  thermal conductivity reach their  maxima at a nitrogen content of 19 at%. N enhances the electron density between Ti and C atoms, strengthening TiC covalent bonds. Moreover, the pseudogap of the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N solid solution exhibits an overall increasing trend, indicating that the addition of nitrogen atoms augments the strength of the covalent bonds in the solid solution.

The mechanical properties  of  materials  are  intrinsically  linked  to their  electronic  structures.  The  performance-modulation  mechanisms can  be  better  elucidated  by  analyzing  the  bonding  characteristics  of (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solutions. Fig. 6 and Fig. S2 show the total DOS (TDOS) and partial DOS (PDOS) of ZrC, TiC, and (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solutions, with EF set at 0 eV. All of them exhibit nonzero DOS at EF, confirming metallic behavior. In Fig. S2, the TDOS and PDOS of ZrC reveal that the dominant peak below EF arises from hybridization between Zr-4d and C2p  orbitals,  suggesting  strong  covalent  bonding.  The  DOS  peak  near GLYPH<0> 10 eV originates from the hybridization of C-2s and Zr-4d orbitals, while the sharp continuous peaks above EF are attributed to antibonding states of Zr-d orbitals. For the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solutions depicted in Fig. 6, the dominant peak below EF involves the hybridization of Zr-4p, Zr-4d,  Ti-3d,  and  C-2p  orbitals,  demonstrating  covalent  bonding  between C and both Ti and Zr. A pseudogap emerges near EF  owing to orbital reconfiguration and weaker Ti -C bonding compared to Zr -C. The DOS values at EF are 10.48 (ZrC) and 10.64 electrons/eV ((Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C), reflecting similar electronic stability.

question: In the Chen 2025 study, how does nitrogen doping affect the thermodynamic stability of (Zr1−xTix)C solid solution, and what does this mean for synthesis processing temperatures?
answer: In the Chen 2025 study, nitrogen doping enhances the thermodynamic stability of the (Zr1−xTix)C solid solution [1]. This is evidenced by the decrease in mixing enthalpy (ΔHmix), which drops significantly from 11.025 kJ/mol (0 at% N) to 0.59 kJ/mol (50 at% N) as the N content increases [5]. A lower ΔHmix implies reduced heat absorption during solid solution formation, which means that the synthesis processing temperatures can be lowered, as the preparation of the (Zr1−xTix)C,N solid solution becomes easier [4].

A B S T R A C T
In this study, special quasirandom structure (SQS) models of (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N were developed to explore ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs). The structural stability, mechanical properties, thermodynamic and electronic structures of these solid solutions were systematically investigated. Results revealed that the addition of Ti atoms into ZrC reduces the lattice constants and volume of the solid solution as well as its thermodynamic stability. Mechanical and thermal properties, including melting point, Young ' s modulus, hardness, and wear resistance, exhibited a general decline, while the fracture toughness and thermal conductivity increased. The (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C system exhibited mixed covalent -ionic Zr/Ti-C bonds. Conversely, N doping in the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C system enhanced its  thermodynamic  stability.  Meanwhile,  the  mixing  enthalpy  and  mixing  Gibbs  free  energy  of  the  system decreased. The melting point, thermal conductivity, Young ' s modulus, hardness, and wear resistance initially increased and then decreased with increasing N content, peaking at a N concentration of 0.19. Compared to the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solution, N doping in the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N solid solution facilitated the formation of strong covalent bonds between Zr and N atoms while enhancing the strength of Ti-C bonds. These findings provide a theoretical foundation for designing ZrC-based UHTCs.

question: In Liu et al. (2023), C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics were hot-pressed at 1600°C for 2 hours. What phase transformation of the sintering additive was achieved under these sintering conditions?
answer: Under the sintering conditions of 1600°C for 2 hours, the sintering additive ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC [1]. This transformation of the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC contributed to the excellent high-temperature mechanical properties of the C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic [3].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
Sintering additive of ZrSi2  is beneficial to low-temperature densification of ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), nevertheless, residual ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs. In the current work, to achieve low-temperature preparation of ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon (C) was added into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC. C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders were hot-pressed at different temperatures to investigate the phase composition, microstructure and densification behavior. TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. In addition, the high-temperature (1800 ◦ C) strength of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was much higher than that of the C-undoped one. The excellent high-temperature mechanical properties could be attributed to the transformation of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.
be prepared at a lower sintering temperature of ~1600 ◦ C [19,23 -25].

2.2. Hot-press sintering
The  ball-milled  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders  were  hot  pressed  at 1350 -1600 ◦ C. During the hot-press sintering process, the instantaneous displacement,  load  and  temperature  were  recorded  using  TestMark (Fangrui  Technology  Co.,  Ltd.,  Changchun,  China)  to  analyze  the densification behavior. The temperature profile was described as follows: it was increased to a certain sintering temperature with a heating rate  of  10 ◦ C/min and then held for 30 min. When the temperature reached above 800 ◦ C, the uniaxial load began to be applied at a cross speed of 1 mm/min. After reaching 40 MPa, the uniaxial pressure was automatically maintained. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1350 ◦ C, 1450 ◦ C, 1600 ◦ C for 30 min were labeled as ZC1350, ZC1450, ZC1600,  respectively.  The  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics  sintered  at 1600 ◦ C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. In order to clarify the effect of sintering additive on the densification behavior and phase transition, pure ZrSi2 powders were also hot-pressed at 1350 -1600 ◦ C following the same sintering process mentioned above for comparison. The as-sintered ZrSi2 ceramics were labeled as Z1350, Z1450, Z1600.

Ideal reactive products, ZrSi 2 = -. Ideal reactive products, C = -. Ideal reactive products, ZrC = 9.5%. Ideal reactive products, SiC = 15.6%

4. Conclusion
In  order  to  achieve  low-temperature  preparation  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon was doped into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  to prepare ZrB2-based ceramics via in situ reactive sintering.  Effect  of  active  carbon  on  phase composition, microstructure, densification  behavior  and  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs  was  investigated.  The  main  conclusions  are  summarized  as follows:
- (1)  With the sintering temperature increasing, ZrSi2 was successively transformed by active carbon into Zr -Si -C clusters, Zr -Si -C coreshell phases, and nano-sized SiC/ZrC particles.
- (2)  Given the densification behavior and microstructure evolution, two rapid densification stages occurred during the in-situ reactive sintering process, due to the softening of ZrSi2 and liquidation of Si intermediate product, respectively. After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, a fully dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained.
- (3)  The high-temperature strength (1800 ◦ C) and strength retention ratio  of  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic  were  390 ± 21 MPa and 79%.  The  excellent  high-temperature  mechanical  properties could be attributed to the transformation of sintering additives into refractory phases.

trade-off between low-temperature sintering and good high-temperature strength.
In order to simultaneously achieve low-temperature preparation and good high-temperature resistance, it is needed to remove the residual ZrSi2 from the final ZrB2-based ceramic. In the previous studies [32,33], we found that introducing carbonaceous material into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  can reduce the amount of residual ZrSi2  in the final materials, due to the reaction between carbonaceous material and sintering additive of ZrSi2. However, the effects of sintering additive transformation on the densification  process,  phase  composition,  microstructure  and  mechanical properties are still unclear.
In the current study, active carbon was introduced into ZrB2 -ZrSi2 to prepare high-temperature-resistant ZrB2-based ceramics at a relatively low temperature via in situ reactive sintering. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 powders  were  hot-pressed  at  different  sintering  temperatures.  Phase composition, microstructure, densification behavior and hightemperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs were systematically investigated, in order to clarify the effects of active carbon on the densification and high-temperature properties of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics.

3.2. Densification behavior
ceramics during 1300 -1400 ◦ C. With the temperature increasing higher than 1410 ◦ C, the solid Si transited into Si-rich liquid phases, which were extruded from the Zr -Si -C clusters into the small residual pores among ZrB2 particles under applied pressure (Fig. 5 (c)), contributing to the second rapid densification stage of 1420 -1600 ◦ C (Fig. 5(f)). Due to the extrusion of the Si-rich liquid phase from the Zr -Si -C cluster, a core-shell phase with a Zr-rich interior core and a Si-rich exterior shell was formed, as shown in Figs. 5(d) and Fig. 3 (f). Then, the large core-shell phase gradually split into small ZrC/SiC particles(Fig. 5(e)). After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2 h, the Zr -Si -C phase was almost entirely transformed into SiC and ZrC. Correspondingly, the densification was further enhanced with the relative density of ZC16002h of 100%. In short, in this way, the sintering additive of ZrSi2 not only promoted  the  densification  of  ZrB2-based  UHTCs,  but  also  was  converted into high-temperature resistant phases by C dopant, which would be benefit to lower sintering temperature and improve high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs.

question: In Liu et al. (2023), the C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic sintered at 1600°C for 2 hours exhibited what high-temperature strength at 1800°C, and how did this compare to the undoped version?
answer: The C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic sintered at 1600°C for 2 hours exhibited a high-temperature strength of 390 ± 21 MPa at 1800°C [1]. This strength was much higher than that of the C-undoped version [1].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
Sintering additive of ZrSi2  is beneficial to low-temperature densification of ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), nevertheless, residual ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs. In the current work, to achieve low-temperature preparation of ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon (C) was added into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC. C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders were hot-pressed at different temperatures to investigate the phase composition, microstructure and densification behavior. TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. In addition, the high-temperature (1800 ◦ C) strength of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was much higher than that of the C-undoped one. The excellent high-temperature mechanical properties could be attributed to the transformation of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.

3.3. High-temperature flexural strength
Fig. 7 shows the 1800 ◦ C-flexural strength versus sintering temperature of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics with and without C dopant, compared with several typical ZrB2  and HfB2-based UHTCs. In the previous studies, in order to obtain superior high-temperature properties, some refractory materials have been considered to be added into the matrix. The most widely  used  refractory  phases  were  ZrC  [15,40]  and  SiC  [13,38].  In addition, high-temperature resistant phases such as WC [41 -43], B4C [44],  Mo2C  [45]  and  SiB6 [46]  were  also  considered.  For  superior strength at ultra-high temperatures, it is generally not recommended to employ sintering additives with low melting point. Consequently, the sintering temperature of these reported ZrB2-based UHTCs with superior high-temperature strength typically exceeds 1800 ◦ C, resulting in significant energy expenditure. In contrast, introducing sintering additives with low melting point into ZrB2 ceramics can significantly reduce the densification  temperature,  however  the  residual  lower  melting  point phases would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs (e.g., the strength of ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2 at 1800 ◦ C is only 30 MPa). In the current work, active carbon was added into the ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic exhibited excellent flexural strength (390 MPa) at 1800 ◦ C, surpassing that of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC [15,40], ZrB2-30 vol% SiC [38], HfB2-20 vol% SiB6  [46], and HfB2-5 vol% SiC-5 vol% WC [41]. Meanwhile, the densification temperature of C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2

1. Introduction
However,  excessive  sintering  additives  would  reduce  the  hightemperature properties of the as-sintered ZrB2-based UHTCs [26 -30]. Especially, ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs, as the fact that the yield stress of ZrSi2 monotonously decreases to less than 50 MPa with the increasing temperature up to 1500 ◦ C [26]. Previous literature [31] reported that 7.4 vol% ZrSi2-assisted sintered ZrB2  ceramics exhibited a strength reduction from 400 MPa at room temperature (RT) to 220 MPa at 1400 ◦ C. Although few ultra-high-temperature strength ( > 1600 ◦ C) of ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was reported, it is foreseeable that the strength of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics would decrease  to  an  extremely  low  value  at  ultra-high-temperatures.  In contrast,  SiC  or  ZrC-assisted  sintered  ZrB2 ceramics  have  superior high-temperature mechanical properties. For example, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC ceramic sintered at 1900 ◦ C was 348 MPa [15]. The 1800 ◦ C-flexural  strength  of  ZrB2-15  vol%  SiC  ceramics  sintered  at 2100 -2170 ◦ C  could  reach  474  MPa  [13].  Although  the  ZrB2-based UHTCs  without  ZrSi2 sintering  additive  (e.g.  ZrB2 -SiC,  ZrB2 -ZrC, ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC)  exhibit  excellent  high-temperature  strength,  these  ceramics  require  extremely  high  sintering  temperature  (mostly  higher than  1900 ◦ C)  to  prepare  highly  dense  ceramics. 4. Conclusion
In  order  to  achieve  low-temperature  preparation  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon was doped into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  to prepare ZrB2-based ceramics via in situ reactive sintering.  Effect  of  active  carbon  on  phase composition, microstructure, densification  behavior  and  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs  was  investigated.  The  main  conclusions  are  summarized  as follows:
- (1)  With the sintering temperature increasing, ZrSi2 was successively transformed by active carbon into Zr -Si -C clusters, Zr -Si -C coreshell phases, and nano-sized SiC/ZrC particles.
- (2)  Given the densification behavior and microstructure evolution, two rapid densification stages occurred during the in-situ reactive sintering process, due to the softening of ZrSi2 and liquidation of Si intermediate product, respectively. After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, a fully dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained.
- (3)  The high-temperature strength (1800 ◦ C) and strength retention ratio  of  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic  were  390 ± 21 MPa and 79%.  The  excellent  high-temperature  mechanical  properties could be attributed to the transformation of sintering additives into refractory phases.

For comparison, a ZrB2-based UHTC with unconverted sintering additive (ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2, labeled as ZS15) was also tested under the same conditions. question: In Liu et al. (2023), when C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 powders were hot-pressed at 1600°C for 2 hours, what relative density was achieved, and what was the relationship between the microstructural phases and this densification outcome?
answer: In Liu et al. (2023), when C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 powders were hot-pressed at 1600°C for 2 hours, a relative density of 100% was achieved [3]. The relationship between the microstructural phases and this densification outcome is that the Zr-Si-C phase was almost entirely transformed into SiC and ZrC, which contributed to the further enhancement of densification [3].

2.2. Hot-press sintering
The  ball-milled  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders  were  hot  pressed  at 1350 -1600 ◦ C. During the hot-press sintering process, the instantaneous displacement,  load  and  temperature  were  recorded  using  TestMark (Fangrui  Technology  Co.,  Ltd.,  Changchun,  China)  to  analyze  the densification behavior. The temperature profile was described as follows: it was increased to a certain sintering temperature with a heating rate  of  10 ◦ C/min and then held for 30 min. When the temperature reached above 800 ◦ C, the uniaxial load began to be applied at a cross speed of 1 mm/min. After reaching 40 MPa, the uniaxial pressure was automatically maintained. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1350 ◦ C, 1450 ◦ C, 1600 ◦ C for 30 min were labeled as ZC1350, ZC1450, ZC1600,  respectively.  The  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics  sintered  at 1600 ◦ C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. In order to clarify the effect of sintering additive on the densification behavior and phase transition, pure ZrSi2 powders were also hot-pressed at 1350 -1600 ◦ C following the same sintering process mentioned above for comparison. The as-sintered ZrSi2 ceramics were labeled as Z1350, Z1450, Z1600.

- (2)  Given the densification behavior and microstructure evolution, two rapid densification stages occurred during the in-situ reactive sintering process, due to the softening of ZrSi2 and liquidation of Si intermediate product, respectively. After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, a fully dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained.
- (3)  The high-temperature strength (1800 ◦ C) and strength retention ratio  of  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic  were  390 ± 21 MPa and 79%.  The  excellent  high-temperature  mechanical  properties could be attributed to the transformation of sintering additives into refractory phases.

3.2. Densification behavior
