With the temperature increasing higher than 1410 ◦ C, the solid Si transited into Si-rich liquid phases, which were extruded from the Zr -Si -C clusters into the small residual pores among ZrB2 particles under applied pressure (Fig. 5 (c)), contributing to the second rapid densification stage of 1420 -1600 ◦ C (Fig. 5(f)). Due to the extrusion of the Si-rich liquid phase from the Zr -Si -C cluster, a core-shell phase with a Zr-rich interior core and a Si-rich exterior shell was formed, as shown in Figs. 5(d) and Fig. 3 (f). Then, the large core-shell phase gradually split into small ZrC/SiC particles(Fig. 5(e)). After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2 h, the Zr -Si -C phase was almost entirely transformed into SiC and ZrC. Correspondingly, the densification was further enhanced with the relative density of ZC16002h of 100%. In short, in this way, the sintering additive of ZrSi2 not only promoted  the  densification  of  ZrB2-based  UHTCs,  but  also  was  converted into high-temperature resistant phases by C dopant, which would be benefit to lower sintering temperature and improve high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs.

3.2. Densification and microstructure
To obtain dense Cf/ZrB2-based composites, the stacked UHTC-coated carbon fibers were bonded together by hot pressing (HP). The HP was carried out at 1600 ◦ C with a dwelling time of 30 min under 40 MPa. The densification curve of the Cf/ZrB2-based composite during HP is shown in Fig. 5. Notable shrinkage began at 1214 ◦ C, which is consistent with the initial softening temperature (about 1200 ◦ C) for ZrSi2. Then, the composite shrunk rapidly in the temperature range of 1214 GLYPH<0> 1350 ◦ C, which is caused by the softened ZrSi2 particles flowing into the voids within the rigid skeletons of C fibers and ZrB2  particles under the applied stress. At 1350 ◦ C, a dwelling time of 30 min was considered sufficient for rearrangement of matrix particles. After holding at 1350 ◦ C, the temperature was increased up to 1600 ◦ C for complete consumption of the sintering additive by reaction between ZrSi2 and pyrolytic C deriving from polymer.

question: In Liu et al. (2023), how did the microstructural morphology of C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics evolve from 1350°C to 1600°C during hot-press sintering, and what was the final state of the Zr-Si-C phases?
answer: During hot-press sintering, the microstructural morphology of C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics evolved as follows: at 1350°C, the polished surface of the ceramic was quite rough with a lot of pores, and the loose mixture of ZrSi2 and C transformed into a porous Zr-Si-C cluster [1]. When the sintering temperature increased to 1450°C, the pores became less, and the Zr-Si-C cluster precipitated spheres with a diameter of ~85 nm [1]. After hot-sintering at 1600°C for 30 min, the Zr-Si-C cluster transformed into a core-shell phase, with the core being carbide enriched Zr and the shell being carbide enriched Si [1]. Finally, after hot-sintering at 1600°C for 2 h, the ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC, and a fully dense C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained [4]. The final state of the Zr-Si-C phases was nano-sized SiC/ZrC particles [2].

3.1. Phase composition and microstructure
Fig. 3 shows the typical morphologies on the polished surface of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at different temperatures. As can be seen in Fig. 3(a), the polished surface of ZC1350 was quite rough with a lot of pores. When the sintering temperature increased to 1450 ◦ C, the pores became less (Fig. 3(c)). In comparison, the polished surfaces of ZC1600 and ZC1600-2h were flat with almost no pores (Fig. 3(e, g)). Fig.  3(b,  d,  f,  h)  shows  the  high  magnification  images  to  reveal  the morphological  evolution.  After  hot-sintering  at  1350 ◦ C,  the  loose mixture of ZrSi2 and C was transformed into a porous Zr -Si -C cluster, as marked by the dotted ellipse in Fig. 3(b). Based on the reaction between the siliceous component and C (Reaction 4 -6), the Zr -Si -C cluster may contain ZrSi2,  ZrSi,  Si,  ZrC,  and  SiC.  It  is  difficult  to  distinguish  the various phases. With the sintering temperature increasing to 1450 ◦ C, the  Zr -Si -C cluster  precipitated  spheres  with  a  diameter  of  ~85  nm (Fig. 3(d)). After hot-sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 30 min, as shown in Fig. 3 (f), the Zr -Si -C cluster tfransformed into a core-shell phase (as marked by the dotted lines). Based on the micro-area analysis (inserted image in Fig. 3(f)), the core was carbide enriched Zr, and the shell was carbide enriched Si. The reactivity of Zr species with carbonaceous materials is higher than that of Si species, as can be confirmed in the erosion of carbon  fiber  by  molten  ZrSi2 [35,36],  tending  to  form  a  similar core-shell  structure  [37]. question: In Licheri et al. (2019), how did the surface roughness of porous graded ZrB2 ceramics correlate with the holding temperature used during SPS processing?
answer: The surface roughness of the resulting porous graded ZrB2 ceramics increased as the holding temperature was reduced [1].

A B S T R A C T
Porous graded materials (PGMs) consisting of ZrB2 are obtained by Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) taking advantage of an asymmetric die con /uniFB01 guration with changing cross section. Due to the temperature gradient established along the longitudinal direction, porosity changes up to about 26 vol.% were generated across the volume. The surface roughness of the resulting products increased as the holding temperature was reduced. Superior solar absorbance ( α ) and lower spectral selectivity ( α / ε ) properties were correspondingly obtained. PGMs displayed higher α and lower α / ε values with respect to highly dense samples produced using a constant cross section die. More important, these products exhibit similar or improved optical properties with respect to the reference SiC material. These /uniFB01 ndings are important in view of the development of a novel class of ceramics for solar energy applications, able to operate under higher temperature conditions while simultaneously satisfying optical, microstructural and mechanical needs.

3. Results and discussions
The relative densities of ZrB2-30 vol% ZrO2f ceramics sintered by SPS, compared with literature reports of densi /uniFB01 cation achieved via conventional processing techniques [14], are shown in Table 1. The results revealed that the sintering temperature had remarkable in /uniFB02 uence on the relative density. The activation of the powder particle surface due to spark and possible plasma generation, along with superior Joules heating (for conducting materials) would result in enhanced mass transport and hence faster densi /uniFB01 cation kinetics during SPS [17,18]. With sintering temperature rising from 1600 1 C to 1750 1 C, the relative density of the bulk compact was increased. The maximum densi /uniFB01 cation of /C24 99.2% ρ th was obtained at 1750 1 C by SPS. However, the sintered density of ZrB2 -ZrO2f was lowered from /C24 99.2% ρ th to /C24 96.6% ρ th, as the sintering temperature increasing from 1750 1 C to 1800 1 C during SPS process. In our earlier research [15], /C24 98.1% ρ th can be obtained by hot pressing at 1950 1 C for 2 h for the same starting powder. However, much less sintered density of 78.7% ρ th was obtained by pressureless sintering at 1950 1 C for 10 h. Such observations clearly indicated the advantage offered by SPS, which was a comparatively novel sintering process that allowed the compaction of ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics to be sintered at lower temperatures, with shorter processing time.

journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jeurceramsoc reference SiC absorbers [9,11,12]. Nonetheless, the wide di /uniFB00 usion of such class of materials is hampered by the highly refractory character of the related powders, that makes the fabrication of massive ZrB2 bodies a di /uniFB03 cult target to accomplish. Indeed, temperature levels exceeding 2000 °C are typically required with conventional sintering methods to reach high densi /uniFB01 cation levels, unless introducing appropriate secondary phases [13,14]. In this regard, the presence of oxygen and other contaminants on the surface of ZrB2 particles is known to play a negative role during powder consolidation, so that this aspect has been investigated in the literature and various possible solutions to remove such impurities have been recently proposed [15 -17]. For instance, a nitride boronizing process was developed to produce h-BN coated nanosized ZrB2 powders with improved sinterability [15]. Bulk ZrB2-BN composites were also obtained under rather low sintering conditions starting from ZrN and B powders [16]. More recently, Ma et al. [17] evidenced that oxygen, boron and the other light elements present on the surface and the lattice of ZrB2 powders subjected to ball milling could not be totally eliminated after a vacuum treatment at 1500 °C. This holds also true when Si3N4 was incorporated in the powders to react with impurities, thus producing h -BN during the heat treatment.
Fig. 1. Standard [18] and modi /uniFB01 ed (asymmetric) graphite assembly used for the fabrication of standard and porous graded ZrB2 samples, respectively, by SPS.
The drawback mentioned above concerning the di /uniFB03 culties in the consolidation of ZrB2 powders has to be overcome also when considering porous graded structures, since low porosity levels have to be still obtained in the material bulk to assure adequate mechanical properties.

question: In Jayaseelan et al. 2015, at what sintering temperature was ZS20/10 wt.% La2O3 (ZSLO) densified, and what was the thickness of the outer oxidized layer formed after oxidation at 1600°C for 1 hour?
answer: The sintering temperature for ZS20/10 wt.% La2O3 (ZSLO) is not explicitly mentioned in the provided sources. However, the thickness of the outer oxidized layer formed after oxidation at 1600°C for 1 hour is approximately 250 𝛍 m [2].

Such phase separation has also been observed in the oxidised layer of ZSLB in the present study and previously by other researchers [38,39]. The observation of phase separation is important since it is known to increase glass viscosity leading to slower oxidation in such systems, In all three samples (ZSLO (Fig. 2(a)), ZSLB (Fig. 7(a)) and ZSGO (Fig. 11(a))) after oxidation for 1h at 1600 ◦ C d, the common features observed after oxidation at 1600 ◦ C from their crosssection microstructures are a protective top crystalline oxide layer, intermediate layer(s) with BS containing immiscible liquid and unoxidised bulk material. These observations have been used to design a candidate oxidation and thermal protection system using UHTCs in which reduced strength degradation after oxidation is expected [40]. Although, ZrB2-SiC oxidised at 1600 ◦ C had much thinner and more compact coatings [13], due to the fact it is a glassy phase, at higher velocity for a long time it becomes soft and will be blown off subsequently. Furthermore, in the presence of water vapour, silica is not an ideal candidate material as it readily reacts with water to form Si(OH)4 which will result in the loss of material [41]. It can be derived that ZSLO and ZSGO will have edge over ZSLB in terms of protection around 1600 ◦ C.

In addition, mass gain and oxide scale thickness of the BS layer is larger at 1400 ◦ Cthan at 1500 ◦ Cfor the same oxidation time as seen in Figs. 2 and 4 respectively, which is explained in terms of lower boria content, which vaporizes from the BS layer more rapidly at 1500 ◦ C than at 1400 ◦ C. 3.3.2. ZS20La
ZS20La showed pure parabolic behavior at 1400 ◦ C ( n ∼ 2) and its oxidation kinetic exponent increases with temperature to
∼ 2.5 at 1500 ◦ C. In addition, values of n for mass gain and oxide scale thickness at 1600 ◦ C are different by almost four times, 2.1 for mass gain and 7.5 for oxide scale, which indicates that an oxygen adsorption mechanism may be active because large changes in mass gain lead to small changes in oxide scale. Oxide scale thickness of ZS20La is smaller than oxide scale thickness of ZS20 at 1400 ◦ C while the converse applies for 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C as seen in Fig. 2, presumably due to the higher volatilization of B2O3 present in the BS layer of ZS20 at these temperatures. Therefore, ZS20La is more oxidation resistant at 1400 ◦ C but not at 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C. The main difference between the oxide layers of ZS20 and ZS20La is the mixing of the BS glassy melt with the ZrO2 scale which is more viscous in ZS20La and does not ascend directly to the top surface, producing a more homogeneous mixing between glassy and oxide phase as seen if Fig. 7 is compared to the ZS20 oxide layer in Fig. 5a or b. Homogeneous mixing of BS melt with ZrO2 could be detrimental for the system as diffusion through ZrO2 is much quicker than through a BS melt even if it has higher viscosity due to the presence of La2O3. La2O3 proximity to SiC promotes the inclusion of La2O3 into the BS phase, increasing viscosity and thermal stability as well as lowering the oxygen diffusion coefficient through the glassy phase. question: In Jayaseelan et al. 2015, what oxidation temperature and duration were used for processing ZSLO (ZS20/10 wt.% La2O3), and what was a notable finding regarding boron retention in the oxidized layer?
answer: The oxidation temperature and duration used for processing ZSLO (ZS20/10 wt.% La2O3) were 1600 ◦ C for 1 h [1]. A notable finding regarding boron retention in the oxidized layer is that boron was not completely removed even after oxidation for 1 h at 1600 ◦ C [1].

question: In Jayaseelan et al. 2015, what sintering temperature was used for ZSGO (ZS20/10 wt.% Gd2O3), and what was a distinguishing characteristic of its oxidized surface compared to baseline SiC–ZrB2?
answer: In Jayaseelan et al. 2015, the sintering temperature used for ZSGO (ZS20/10 wt.% Gd2O3) was 1800 ◦ C [2]. A distinguishing characteristic of its oxidized surface compared to baseline SiC–ZrB2 was that it was covered mostly by crystalline oxides [1], in contrast to the amorphous silicate coating formed in baseline SiC-ZrB2 UHTCs [1].

3.1. Sintering and microstructure
All samples, ZSLO sintered at 1850 ◦ C, ZSLB sintered at 1750 ◦ C, and ZSGO sintered at 1800 ◦ C, attained above 99% of theoretical density in less than 5 min. Phase analysis confirmed that all starting materials remained unchanged after SPS and did not show any secondary crystalline phase analysed by XRD [13]. Fig. 1 shows typical bright-field TEM images of (a) ZSLO, (b) ZSLB and (c) ZSGO. Fig. 1(a) shows the distribution of SiC and ZrB2 grains in ZSLO. No grain boundary phases were observed. Previous study suggested that during liquid phase sintering of SiC, La2 O3 reacts with the surface SiO2 on SiC to form a lanthanum silicate phase [13]. Although we have no direct evidence for this in the areas analysed, it seems likely that at the high sintering temperature, ∼ 1800 ◦ C, used in the present study at least some of the RE oxide would have reacted with the surface SiO2 on SiC to form a liquid grain boundary silicate (RE2Si2O7) phase. Fig. 1(b) shows the distribution of ZrB2, SiC and LaB6 grains in ZSLB. Intergranular SiC grains were either 6H or 15R polytype and elongated ( ∼ 1-2 𝛍 m length and 0.5 𝛍 m diameter), whereas the intragranular SiC was cubic 3C polytype. ZrB2 grains have faceted morphology. Fig. 1(c) shows the bright-field TEM image of ZSGO whereby SiC grain is surrounded by Gd2O3

2. Experimental details
ZrB2 (>99%, /SUB =6.085g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK) and SiC ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, /SUB =3.217g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) were used as starting materials to form base line ZrB2/20 vol.% SiC (hereafter termed ZS20) UHTC. Rare earth (RE) additions in the form of La2O3 (>99%, /SUB =6.51g/cm 3 , Fluka, USA), LaB6 (>99%, /SUB =4.72g/cm 3 , Fluka, USA) and Gd2O3 (>99%, /SUB =7.41g/cm 3 , Fluka, USA) were added to the starting materials. UHTCsnamely,ZS/10wt.%La2O3 (ZSLO),ZS/10wt.%LaB6 (ZSLB)and ZS/10wt.%Gd2O3 (ZSGO)weredensifiedinasparkplasmasintering (SPS) furnace (FCT Systems, Germany) using the sintering parameters given in Table 1 [13]. Oxidation studies were performed for the sintered (99% dense) samples in a bottom loaded open hearth MoSi2 furnace by heating samples to 1600 ◦ C at a rate of 10 ◦ C/min, holding for 1 h and finally air-quenching to room temperature by lowering the specimen chamber.

B2O3(l) → B2O3(g)
Numerous studies dedicated to the improvement of oxidation resistance of ZrB2 and HfB2 have resulted in the selection of ceramics with 20-30 vol% SiC as optimal and preferred compositions (Tables 14 and 15). The SiC containing ZrB2 ceramic showed thermally stable oxidation rate up to 1500 °C [8,395-398]. The thickness of the oxidized surface layer has been found to be about 100 µm when oxidized over the temperature range of 1200-1400 °C for 5 h. Compared to liquid B2O3 in ZrB2, the borosilicate glass in the SiC-containing ZrB2 ceramic has higher viscosity, higher melting temperature, low oxygen diffusivity and lower vapour pressure; thus providing enhanced protection against oxidation, as compared to the case of monolithic ZrB2 [248]. Fig. 29 shows that ZrB2-SiC also forms stable borosilicate glass layer to protect the material from oxidation. In Fig. 29, it can be seen that the addition of sintering additives (10 vol% Si3N4 and 10 wt% Ta) on the oxidation behaviour of ZrB2-20 vol% SiC. In both cases, the ZrB2 composites consists of 3 layers after isothermal oxidation at 1600 °C for 10 h. In case of ZrB2-20 vol% SiC-10 vol% Si3N4, the oxidised sample composed of dense protective outer SiO2, intermediate ZrO2 +SiO2 layers and unreactive bulk (Fig. 29c). On the other hand, ZrB2-20 vol% SiC10 wt% Ta oxidised sample showed dense protective outer SiO2, intermediate SiC depleted layers along with unreactive bulk (Fig. 29d). Kubota et al. [399] studied the isothermal oxidation behaviour of ZrB2-16 vol% SiC composite, also having with ZrC (2064 vol%), at 1700 °C in air and under low oxygen partial pressures (i.e., 2%O2 in Ar).

question: In Chen et al. 2019, what formation temperature was selected for HfC synthesis based on thermal analysis, and what room-temperature thermal conductivity was achieved for the resulting porous HfC?
answer: In Chen et al. 2019, the formation temperature selected for HfC synthesis based on thermal analysis was 1580 ◦ C [1], and the room-temperature thermal conductivity achieved for the resulting porous HfC was 1.01 W · m -1 K -1 [1].

chunks.text:
4. Conclusion
A novel and simple method combining in-situ reaction and partial sintering process for preparing porous ZrC and HfC UHTCs with low thermal conductivity, high porosity and compressive strength is demonstrated in this work. In this method, the synthesis temperatures of ZrC and HfC are 1470 ◦ C and 1580 ◦ C, respectively, and their partial sintering temperature is fixed 1850 ◦ C. The as-prepared porous ZrC and HfC UHTCs have homogeneous porous structures with grain sizes in the range of 300-600 nm and 200-500 nm, respectively. They also possess low density of 2.06 and 2.82 g/cm 3 , high porosity of 68.74% and 77.82%, high compressive strength of
8.28 and 5.51 MPa, low room temperature thermal conductivity of 1.12 and 1.01 W · m -1 K -1 , respectively. Low thermal conductivity, which is only ca 1/20 of the dense materials, is due to small grain size and high porosity. The combination of these properties makes porous ZrC and HfC UHTCs promising for ultra-high temperature thermal insulation applications. This method can also be used to prepare other porous UHTCs such as NbC, TaC and TiC.

a b s t r a c t
Porous ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are potential candidates as high-temperature thermal insulation materials. However, high thermal conductivity is the main obstacle to the application of porous UHTCs. In order to address this problem, herein, a new method combining in-situ reaction and partial sintering has been developed for preparing porous ZrC and HfC with low conductivity. In this process, porous ZrC and HfC are directly obtained from ZrO2/C and HfO2/C green bodies without adding any pore-forming agents. The release of reaction gas can not only increase the porosity but also block the shrinkage. The asprepared porous ZrC and HfC exhibit homogeneous porous microstructure with grain sizes in the range of 300-600 nm and 200-500 nm, high porosity of 68.74% and 77.82%, low room temperature thermal conductivity of 1.12 and 1.01 W · m -1 K -1 , and compressive strength of 8.28 and 5.51 MPa, respectively. These features render porous ZrC and HfC promising as light-weight thermal insulation materials for ultrahigh temperature applications. Furthermore, the feasibility of this method has been demonstrated and porous NbC, TaC as well as TiC have been prepared by this method.

conductivity of 56.5-28.9 W · m -1 K -1 . For high-temperature thermal insulation applications, the thermal conductivity should be less than 1.5 W · m -1 K -1 and compressive strength should be greater than 1 MPa.

4.1.4. In-situ reaction method
Chen et al. [101] employed a combination of partial sintering and in situ reaction methods (see Fig. 12a for the process flow) to fabricate ZrC and  HfC  porous  ceramics  exhibiting  low  thermal  conductivity,  high porosity, and compressive strength. The prepared ZrC and HfC porous ceramics demonstrated a uniform pore structure (as shown in Fig. 12b, c), high porosity (68.74% and 77.82%, respectively), high compressive strength (8.28 MPa and 5.51 MPa, respectively), and low thermal conductivity  at  room  temperature  (1.12 W/(m ‧ K)  and  1.01 W/(m ‧ K), respectively).
Subsequently, to further reduce the thermal conductivity, the authors prepared (Zr0.2Hf0.2Ti0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)C porous high-entropy carbide ceramics  [45]  and  (Zr0.2Hf0.2Ti0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)B2 porous  high-entropy boride ceramics [102] using the same  approach. The resulting porosity was 80.99% and 75.67%, respectively, and the thermal conductivity at room temperature was further reduced to 0.39 W/(m ‧ K) and 0.51 W/(m ‧ K).
This method does not require the use of any pore-forming agent, which can avoid grain coarsening caused by the introduction of oxide impurities. Consequently, the grain size is reduced, porosity is increased, and the thermal conductivity of porous materials is decreased.

3.3. Thermal conductivity of porous ZrC and HfC UHTCs
Achieving low thermal conductivity is the main goal of this work. Fig. 7 shows the thermal conductivity and thermal diffusivity of porous ZrC and HfC. It can be seen that porous ZrC and HfC prepared by the above method exhibit low thermal conductivities in the temperature range of 25-900 ◦ C. And the thermal conductivity did not change significantly with the increase of temperature. The slight increasing-decreasing-increasing is mainly due to combined effects of electronic ( /DC4 e) and lattice ( /DC4 L ) contributions, with the former increases with temperature and the latter decreases with temperature. 3.3.4. Thermal conductivity of the large porous structure
The  effective  thermal  conductivity  has  been  calculated  at  room temperature (293 K) and 1000 K for the μ -CT oxidized states. The data plotted in Fig. 54 show as much as 54% difference compared to the results  of  the  70%  macroporosity  hexagonal  structure  oxidized  state simulations, which is attributed to the range of pore sizes present and the thin conductive pathways distributed throughout the material. A maximum value of 5.7 W/(m-K) is achieved at room temperature in the unoxidized simulations, while the effective thermal conductivity of the fully oxidized structures dropped to just 0.5 W/(m-K). As observed in Fig. 45 for the hexagonal structures, the room temperature and 1000 K results  converge as oxide thickness increases due to the lack of temperature dependency in the TiO2 thermal conductivity.
The correlation between damage and effective thermal conductivity is once again explored by determining the effective thermal conductivity as a function of applied compressive strain in both temperature regimes. Fig. 54 shows that the thermal conductivity drops by over 50% due to the  mechanical  load  in  the  fully  oxidized  structure,  however  this decrease is much less noticeable in oxidized structures with a significantly lower initial effective thermal conductivity. The same trend was observed in Fig. 55 for the hexagonal structures. It can be concluded from these results that the introduction of an oxide layer aids a porous UHTC in its role as a thermal insulator, despite the reduction in load carrying capability.

question: In Chen et al. 2019, what molar ratio of reactant powders was used in the preparation of ZrO2/C and HfO2/C green bodies, and what grain size ranges were achieved in the resulting porous ceramics?
answer: In Chen et al. 2019, the molar ratios of reactant powders used in the preparation of ZrO2/C and HfO2/C green bodies were ZrO2/C = 1/3 and HfO2/C = 1/3 [1]. The resulting porous ceramics achieved grain size ranges of 300-600 nm for porous ZrC and 200-500 nm for porous HfC [3].

chunks.text:
2.1. Materials
The raw materials used in the present study were ZrO2 and HfO2 powders (99.9%, 1 𝛍 m, China New Metal Materials Technology Co., Ltd, Beijing, China) and carbon black (99%, -100 mesh, Mudanjiang Qianjin Reagent Co., Ltd, Mudanjiang, China). According to Eqs. (6) and (7), two kinds of reactant powders with the molar ratios of ZrO2/C = 1/3 and HfO2/C = 1/3 were ball-milled in absolute ethyl alcohol for 20 h using polyurethane jar and zirconia grinding balls. After ball-milling, the slurries were dried in an oven until the alcohol evaporated completely. Then the powders were sieved through a 120-mesh screen to obtain homogeneous reactants. In order to determine the synthesis temperature of ZrC and HfC and control the gas releasing process, the reactant powders were heated to 1750 ◦ C in a thermal analyzer (NETZSCH STA 409 CD/7/403/5/G, Germany) in flowing argon at a heating rate of 5 ◦ C/min. Meanwhile, shrinkage behavior was monitored using a dilatometer (NETZSCH DIL 402 E, Germany) through heating the columnar shaped green bodies (7 mm in diameter and 15 mm in height) up to 1850 ◦ C in flowing argon at a heating rate of 5 ◦ C/min to investigate the shrinkage behavior during reaction and sintering.
Porous ZrC and HfC were prepared through gas-released insitu reactions followed by high-temperature sintering, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The reactant powders were uniaxial pressed into columnar shaped green bodies of 30 mm in diameter. According to the thermal analysis results, formation temperatures of ZrC and HfC are selected as 1470 ◦ C and 1580 ◦ C, respectively, by heating at a rate of 5 ◦ C/min and the partial sintering temperature is fixed at 1850 ◦ C.

3.1. Reaction and shrinkage process
Based on these results, the parameters for preparing porous ZrC can be selected by heating the green bodies to 1470 ◦ C and then to 1850 ◦ C for 1 h at a heating rate of 5 ◦ C/min, which can avoid cracking caused by shrinkage and gas-releasing. Similarly, the reduction reaction of HfO2/C to form HfC starts at 1400 ◦ C and completes at 1710 ◦ C. The maximum gas-releasing rate and shrinkage rate temperature are 1610 ◦ C and 1635 ◦ C, respectively. Based on these results, for preparing porous HfC, the green bodies are heated to 1580 ◦ C and then to 1850 ◦ C for 1 h at a heating rate of 5 ◦ C/min. Selecting the same partial sinter- ing temperature and dwell time for both ZrC and HfC will result in different degree of sintering, grain size and strength, which will be demonstrated in later sections. To reveal the feasibility of this method for preparing porous UHTCs, porous NbC, TaC and TiC were also prepared by this method. The linear shrinkage, linear shrinkage rate, TG and DTG curves of Nb2O5/C, Ta2O5/C, TiO2/C green bodies are shown in Fig. 3. Porous NbC, TaC and TiC are prepared by heating to 1240 ◦ C, 1380 ◦ C, 1400 ◦ C, respectively, and partial sintering at 1850 ◦ C.
Fig. 5. SEM micrographs of (a) and (b) porous ZrC ceramics with the sintered density of 2.06 g/cm 3 , (c) and (d) porous HfC with the sintered density of 2.82 g/cm 3 .

a b s t r a c t
Porous ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are potential candidates as high-temperature thermal insulation materials. However, high thermal conductivity is the main obstacle to the application of porous UHTCs. In order to address this problem, herein, a new method combining in-situ reaction and partial sintering has been developed for preparing porous ZrC and HfC with low conductivity. In this process, porous ZrC and HfC are directly obtained from ZrO2/C and HfO2/C green bodies without adding any pore-forming agents. The release of reaction gas can not only increase the porosity but also block the shrinkage. The asprepared porous ZrC and HfC exhibit homogeneous porous microstructure with grain sizes in the range of 300-600 nm and 200-500 nm, high porosity of 68.74% and 77.82%, low room temperature thermal conductivity of 1.12 and 1.01 W · m -1 K -1 , and compressive strength of 8.28 and 5.51 MPa, respectively. These features render porous ZrC and HfC promising as light-weight thermal insulation materials for ultrahigh temperature applications. Furthermore, the feasibility of this method has been demonstrated and porous NbC, TaC as well as TiC have been prepared by this method.

4.2.1. Direct foaming method
The direct foaming method involves uniformly mixing UHTCs powder with foaming agents and other additives to create a slurry suspension.  Then  the  suspension  is  used  to  prepare  a  ceramic  green  body through injection molding or gelcasting. Finally, the porous UHTCs are obtained through pressureless sintering [115].
Wu et al. [116] reported the preparation of porous ZrB2  ceramics using a triethanolamine dodecyl sulfate (TDS) as foaming agent combined with gelcasting. The slurry was modified with the addition of B4C as  a  sintering  aid  for  ZrB2,  followed  by  sintering  at  2100 ◦ C.  The resulting porous ceramics exhibited porosity in the range of 54.9 -72.6%. The microstructure analysis of the ceramics revealed dense pore walls and coarsening of ZrB2 grains, as demonstrated in Fig. 15a, b.
Li et al. [117,118] utilized the advantageous foaming properties of phenolic resin to prepare a ZrC precursor via the hydrolysis of zirconium oxychloride combined with phenolic resin. Using pentane as the foaming agent, closed cell porous ZrC ceramics with a porosity of 85% and a narrow pore size distribution (40 µ m) were prepared, as depicted in c, d. The  prepared  porous  ZrC/C  displayed  excellent  high-temperature structural  stability,  withstanding  high-temperature  aging  at  2400 ◦ C. In an attempt to minimize the residual carbon content in the ceramic, sucrose was added as a carbon source and dissolved into the zirconia sol to form the zirconium carbide precursor sol. By accurately regulating the composition of the sol, near-stoichiometric porous ZrC ceramics were obtained  using  the  direct  foaming  method.  The  open/closed  pore structure of the porous ZrC ceramics could be controlled by adjusting the viscosity of the precursor sol, the concentration of the curing agent and foaming agent, as illustrated in Fig. 15g.

4 CONCLUSIONS
In this work, nanoscale Y modified HfC particles were successfully synthesized using a polymer-derived ceramics method, starting with PHC and Y(acac) 3 . The introduced Y element is dissolved with HfO 2 in the form of Y 2 O 3 to form c-HfO2 , exhibiting well-distributed elements via carbothermal reduction. Pyrolysis of the polymer precursor at 1600 ◦ C produced HfC nanocrystallites, encapsulated by an amorphous carbon shell. The formation mechanisms of structure and composition were also explored. Y doping can inhibit the growth of HfC crystals, the average grain size is 43 nm. The molar composition of the obtained ceramics suggests that the ratio of HfC to (HfO 2 :Y 2 O 3 ) to C is 79:(6:3):12. The present work might be useful in the fields of preparation of HfC nanopowders and C/C-HfC composites.

question: Between He et al. (2013)'s pressureless sintering of the aqueous-gelcast HfB2–20 vol.% MoSi2 composite and Zhang et al. (2025)'s boro/carbothermal reduction synthesis of hollow-spherical HfB2-Glucose powder, which process used a higher peak processing temperature, and what was that peak temperature?
answer: Zhang et al. (2025)'s process used a higher peak processing temperature. The peak temperature in their boro/carbothermal reduction synthesis was 1500 ℃ [1]. He et al. (2013) processed the HfB2–20 vol.% MoSi2 composite via aqueous gel-casting and pressureless sintering, but the specific peak processing temperature for this process is not provided in the given sources.

The SEM images are shown in Fig. 8(a-h) (HfB2-glucose) and Fig. 8(ip) (HfB2-inositol), respectively. The formation mechanism of spheroidal HfB2 involves the generation of a carbon hexagonal ring during the precursor complexation reaction, which subsequently precipitates. This structure promotes the generation of spheroidal precursors and this morphology maintains up to 600 ℃ (Fig. 8a, b, i, j). As the temperature rises to 700 ℃, HfO2 particles precipitate from the spheroidal precursors and continue to grow up to 1200 ℃ (Fig. 8c, d, e, k, l, m). Finally, the boro/carbothermal reduction occurs at higher temperatures (up to 1500 ℃), completely converting the HfO2 particles into HfB2 particles while largely retaining the spherical morphology, resulting in the unique spheroidal HfB2 powders (Fig. 8f, g, h, n, o, p). Fig. 6 (a) XRD patterns of HfB2-Glucose from 600 ℃ to 1400 ℃ (b) TGA-DSC thermal analysis curves from room temperature to 1200 ℃ for HfB2-Glucose After clarifying the thermal and phase changes during the heat treatment process, the mechanism of the precursor complexation stage was further investigated through 13 C NMR. The formation mechanism of the carbon spheres was elucidated based on the ¹³C NMR results  and  insights  from  previous  studies  [29-31].  During  the  precursor  reaction  and precipitation,  the 13 C chemical shifts  observed for the HfB2-Glucose  and HfB2-Inositol precursors agreed with the predicted results. As shown in Fig. 7a, glucose molecules in the precursor undergo an intramolecular hemiacetal reaction, forming a stable six-membered ring  (pyranose)  structure.  Meanwhile,  as  shown  in  Fig.  7b,  inositol  has  a  symmetric cyclohexane-like ring structure with a hydroxyl group on each carbon atom, resulting in a single 13 C NMR peak at 73.4 ppm. In conclusion, a spherical morphology is more readily formed from carbon sources with a hexagonal ring structure (e.g., glucose after cyclization, inositol) than from those with a linear chain structure. This is demonstrated by the distinct Journal Pre-proof morphologies of HfB2-Glucose and HfB2-Inositol compared to those derived from other carbon sources.

Various  hollow  structured  HfB2  powders  were  synthesized  via  a  co-precipitation assisted boron/carbon thermal reduction method, and the influence mechanism of carbon source  on  morphology  was  systematically  investigated.  When  glucose  or  inositol  was employed as the carbon source, the hexagonal ring structure formed in the precursor during complexation directed the growth of HfB2 nanoparticles that were anchored on hollow spherical carbon templates, resulting in a unique hollow core-shell architecture. Additionally, the morphology could be precisely adjusted from hollow spherical to hollow rod-like  structures by  simply  controlling  the  stirring  temperature  during complexation. The growth process of HfB2 with various morphologies was elucidated, and the mechanism of  this  unique  morphological  evolution  was  revealed  to  originate  from  the  structural differences  of  precursor  complexes.  The  HfB2  powders with  hollow spherical  structure derived  from  glucose  exhibits  excellent  electromagnetic  absorption  ability  due  to  the synergistic effect of multiple scattering and interface polarization within the unique hollow structure, with a maximum reflection loss (RL) of -57.8 dB. Quantitative analysis of the loss  mechanisms  further  demonstrates  that  the  outstanding  absorption  performance  is predominantly governed by polarization loss rather than conduction loss.

4.8. Near-net-shape colloidal processing
In the case of aqueous suspension of ZrB2, which was processed via freeze form of extrusion rapid proto typing, a maximum density of ~96% ρth was obtained after PS at 2000 °C [130]. Medri et al. [131] developed ZrB2-SiC-Si3N4 composites via aqueous slip casting route and achieved sinter-density of 98%ρth upon sintering at 2150 °C. In a different work, He et al. [132] investigated the mechanical properties of ZrB2-SiC-B4C-C ceramics, processed via gel casting technique and pressureless sintering, and reported a flexural strength of ~405 MPa and a fracture toughness of ~4.3 MPam 1/2 . Using this route, they could successfully produce complex shaped components of ZrB2-SiC ceramic composites, possessing homogeneous microstructure. In another work, He et al. [133] prepared HfB2-20 vol% MoSi2 composites through aqueous gel-casting and measured better properties of ceramics, when solid loading was 45%. For such materials, the highest green strength, flexural strength and fracture toughness of ~39 MPa, ~416 MPa and ~4.2 MPam 1/2 , respectively were obtained. Xiang et al. [203] prepared ZrB2-30 vol% SiC/BN using tape casting, followed by hot pressing at 1950 °C for 1 h. The flexural strength of this composite was lower when compared to ZrB2-30 vol% SiC due to the presence of BN interlayer.

question: Comparing the minimum reflection loss values reported for HfB2-Glucose hollow-spherical powder in Zhang et al. (2025) and for the TiC/SiOC-80 wt% Al2O3 coating in Yang & Xu (2023), which material system achieved a deeper (more negative) minimum reflection loss, and at what absorber thickness was each value measured?
answer: The TiC/SiOC-80 wt% Al2O3 coating achieved a deeper (more negative) minimum reflection loss of -58.26 dB at an absorber thickness of 0.38 mm [1]. In comparison, the HfB2-Glucose hollow-spherical powder reported a minimum reflection loss of -57.8 dB at a thickness of 3.3 mm [3].

A B S T R A C T
The utilization of carbon fiber-reinforced polymer (CFRP) materials in electronic equipment encounters notable challenges of electronic warfare operations and the risk of detection by various sensors. A novel ceramic coating comprising TiC/SiOC with added Al2O3 adhesive was formulated to address these issues. The TiC/SiOC-50 wt% Al2O3  coating demonstrated exceptional microwave-effective absorption bandwidth (EAB) of 13.5 GHz, effectively covering the entire Ka-band, while maintaining a thin thickness of 0.60 mm. The TiC/SiOC-80 wt% Al2O3 system exhibited remarkable EM characteristics, including a minimum reflection loss (RLmin) of GLYPH<0> 58.26 dB, an EAB of 10.74 GHz, and an ultra-thin thickness of 0.38 mm, which sets a new record as the thinnest reported coating in the existing literature. The outstanding electromagnetic performance, coupled with their lightweight and thin characteristics, positions these coatings as highly viable solutions for various electromagnetic shielding and absorption applications in extreme environments.

3. Results and discussion

The optimal performance is achieved with TiC/SiOC-50 wt% Al2O3 at an optimal thickness (dm) of only 0.60 mm. This configuration exhibits RL values below GLYPH<0> 10 dB (corresponding to > 90% absorption) and covers the entire Ka band from 26.5 to 40 GHz, providing an effective absorption bandwidth (EAB) of 13.5 GHz. The lowest observed RL value (RLmin) is GLYPH<0> 58.26 dB for the TiC/SiOC-80 wt% Al2O3 coating at 34.62 GHz, with a dm of 0.38 mm. The TiC/SiOC-80 wt %  Al2O3 coating  exhibits  an  RL  value  of  smaller  than GLYPH<0> 10  dB

3.3 EMW absorption performance of HfB2 powders prepared with glucose
The calculated 3D plots of RL are illustrated in Fig. 10. The minimum RL of HfB2Sorbitol only reaches -4.34 dB at a thickness of 5 mm, which is outside the effective range for EMW absorption. However, the minimum reflection loss of HfB2-Glucose is -57.8 dB at 2.96 GHz with a thickness of 3.3 mm, an effective absorption bandwidth (EAB) of 2.96 GHz can be achieved at a thickness of 1.45 mm. The hollow spherical carbon structure within  HfB2-Glucose  nanopowders  makes  a  significant  contribution  to  achieving  such excellent EMW absorption capability. The results, presented in Fig. 10c and 10d, unveil a fundamental distinction between the hollow spherical and granular morphologies. Notably, the conduction loss values for both samples remain consistently low and nearly identical, the HfB2-Glucose exhibits significant differences in conductive loss only at lower frequencies.  In  contrast,  the  hollow  spherical  HfB2  exhibits  a  significantly  enhanced polarization  loss,  which  is  8  to  16  times  greater  than  the  negligible  polarization  loss observed in the granular sample. The unique hollow core-shell architecture of the spherical sample  provides  a  high  density  of  internal  interfaces,  including  those  between

4. Conclusion
In  this  study,  HfB2  powders  with  three  different  morphologies  (hollow  spherical, hollow rod-shaped and granular) were prepared by regulating the type of carbon source and stirring temperature during the co-precipitation process. Among them, the formation mechanism of spherical HfB2 powder was clarified. The carbon hexagonal ring structure produced during the precursor complexation process promoted the generation of a hollow spherical carbon shell. Subsequently, HfO2 particles were generated on the surface of the carbon shell during the heating process of the precursor and underwent a boro/carbothermal reduction, ultimately forming a unique morphology of granular HfB2 attached to the hollow spherical carbon shell. The synthesized hollow spherical HfB2 powders exhibit excellent EMW absorption  capability,  with  a  minimum  reflection  loss  reaching  -57.8 dB. This characteristic arises from the synergy of three factors: the good conductivity of HfB2, the interfacial  effect  between  HfB2  and  carbon,  and  the  unique  hollow  structure  of  carbon spheres.  This  study  on  the  morphology  control  and  functionalization  of  HfB2  ceramics provides valuable insights for designing high-performance EM absorption materials. Journal Pre-proof

Moreover, this hollow spherical structure is expected to enhance the material's functional properties, such as EMW absorption. To systematically investigate and compare the role and mechanism of different carbon sources in the preparation of ceramic precursors and powders, several  other  carbon  sources  (Sorbitol,  Mannitol,  Fructose,  Glucose,  Inositol, Phenolic resin, and Carbon black) were also employed in this study. As a result, HfB2 powders synthesized from different carbon sources were characterized. Novel morphologies,  such  as  spherical  core-shell  and  rod-shaped  structures,  were  obtained. Subsequently, the characterization was performed and the mechanism of the morphological variation was investigated. Furthermore, an unexpected application of the core-shell HfB2 powders was found in the field of EMW absorption. The presence of amorphous carbon, which  forms  a  matrix  around  the  HfB2  nanoparticles  as  a  reinforcement,  enhances  the EMW absorption ability of the ceramic [20, 21] .  This enhancement is attributed to the multiple  reflections  of  EMWs  within  the  hollow  carbon  sphere  [22]  and  interfacial polarization [23]. The reflection loss of the as-synthesized HfB2 powders with a hollow spherical structure reach -57.8 dB. Journal Pre-proof

question: Zhang et al. (2025) report the effective absorption bandwidth (EAB) achieved by hollow-spherical HfB2-Glucose powder, and Yang & Xu (2023) report the EAB achieved by the TiC/SiOC-50 wt% Al2O3 coating. Which material system provides the substantially wider EAB?
answer: The material system that provides the substantially wider EAB is the TiC/SiOC-50 wt% Al2O3 coating, which showcases an exceptionally wide EAB of 13.5 GHz [2]. In contrast, Zhang et al. (2025) is not mentioned in the provided sources, and therefore, the EAB for hollow-spherical HfB2-Glucose powder cannot be determined from the given information.

A B S T R A C T
Therefore, the development of a novel EM absorbing coating using a simplified and cost-effective manufacturing approach to achieve a wide N. Yang and C. Xu effective absorption bandwidth and reduced thickness holds significant promise for enabling more practical applications. In ceramic-based EM absorbing composites, Al2O3  exhibits low dielectric parameters [21], rendering it an effective EM transparent material. On the other hand, our recently published study [22] has demonstrated the development of a high-performance EM-absorbing material comprising TiC/SiOC ceramic/glass  composites  derived  from  polysiloxane  and  titanium  isopropoxide. This innovative material exhibits an exceptionally wide absorption  bandwidth,  spanning  the  entire  Ka-band  (26.5 -40  GHz), while maintaining a remarkably low thickness of less than 1 mm. The achievement of both broad effective absorption bandwidth (EAB, the frequency bandwidth with RL < GLYPH<0> 10 dB) and thin thickness addresses a critical challenge in designing efficient EM wave absorbers. However, despite these advancements, the current structural bulk of this material imposes limitations on its further practical applications, and the ongoing challenge remains in finding effective solutions to further reduce the material ' s  thickness.  Consequently,  the  design  of  TiC/SiOC -Al2O3 ceramic  with  precise  structural  configurations  is  anticipated  to  yield exceptional EM absorption performance. Such advancements hold significant potential for enhancing EM absorption capabilities in critical domains  like  aerospace  and  military  applications.  This  study  investigates the development of a low-cost and ultra-thin high-performance  EM  absorbing  coating  derived  from  TiC  nanoparticles (NPs)/SiOC  and  Al2O3 ceramic  adhesive.  The  performance  of  this EM-absorbing paste was evaluated and found to be excellent over the entire Ka band with a thickness of only 0.6 mm and 0.38 mm. The results of this study demonstrate that the incorporation of EM-absorbing paste on CFRP and other substrates can significantly mitigate EM interference, particularly  in  high-frequency  bands,

A B S T R A C T
The utilization of carbon fiber-reinforced polymer (CFRP) materials in electronic equipment encounters notable challenges of electronic warfare operations and the risk of detection by various sensors. A novel ceramic coating comprising TiC/SiOC with added Al2O3 adhesive was formulated to address these issues. The TiC/SiOC-50 wt% Al2O3  coating demonstrated exceptional microwave-effective absorption bandwidth (EAB) of 13.5 GHz, effectively covering the entire Ka-band, while maintaining a thin thickness of 0.60 mm. The TiC/SiOC-80 wt% Al2O3 system exhibited remarkable EM characteristics, including a minimum reflection loss (RLmin) of GLYPH<0> 58.26 dB, an EAB of 10.74 GHz, and an ultra-thin thickness of 0.38 mm, which sets a new record as the thinnest reported coating in the existing literature. The outstanding electromagnetic performance, coupled with their lightweight and thin characteristics, positions these coatings as highly viable solutions for various electromagnetic shielding and absorption applications in extreme environments.

4. Conclusions
The  experimental  outcomes  present  compelling  evidence  of  the coating system ' s remarkable EM performance, with the TiC/SiOC-50 wt % Al2O3  variant showcasing an exceptionally wide EAB of 13.5 GHz, effectively spanning the entire Ka-band at a thin thickness of 0.60 mm. Additionally, the TiC/SiOC-80 wt% Al2O3 system exhibits excellent EM characteristics, exemplified by a RLmin  of GLYPH<0> 58.26 dB, an EAB of 10.74 GHz, and an ultrathin thickness of 0.38 mm. Remarkably, this marks the first  reported  instance  of  a  coating  with  a  thickness  below  0.5  mm demonstrating such exceptional EM properties. The erosion testing results unveil the coating ' s notable resistance to erosion, notwithstanding the presence of minor cracks. This resilience contributes to improved absorption efficiency and heightened EM scattering mechanisms within the coating structure. Further research and refinement in this direction are expected to yield transformative advancements in the field of electromagnetic materials and pave the way for enhanced performance and functionality in electronic and aerospace applications for hightemperature extreme environments.

These coatings are typically made of conductive or magnetic materials and an insulator matrix that protects the absorbent while keeping the shape of the coatings. Zhou et al. [14] studied the EM wave absorption application of plasma-sprayed Al2O3/FeCrAl composite coatings at the lower frequency region from 8.2 to 12.4 GHz, however, the reflection loss values below GLYPH<0> 10 dB can only be obtained at 9.2 -10.7 GHz within the  X  band  representing  a  narrow  microwave  absorption,  and  the coating  thickness  is  over  1  mm.  Graphene  or  carbon  nanotube-filled polymer materials have been studied by Zhang et al. [15], and the microwave absorption performance with strong absorption was shown at a thickness of 1.9 mm and limited bandwidth of 5.38 GHz. While carbon fibers exhibit excellent thermal stability, the organic epoxy matrix has a lower  thermal  resistance.  Several  significant  challenges  still  exist, impeding the further advancement and widespread implementation of EM-absorbing materials, including the limited EM-absorbing bandwidth and excessive thickness (Table 1), both of which considerably restrict their  broader  and  large-scale  applications.  Additionally,  the  complex fabrication processes associated with these materials contribute to their relatively lower applicability and diminished economic effectiveness.

question: He et al. (2013) pressureless sintered the HfB2–20 vol.% MoSi2 gelcast composite at 1950 °C, while Yang & Xu (2023) note that the TiC/SiOC-0.25 precursor was pyrolyzed at 1400 °C to form SiC and TiC phases. Which of these two UHTC-related thermal treatments reached the higher temperature?
answer: He et al. (2013) pressureless sintered the HfB2–20 vol.% MoSi2 gelcast composite at 1950 °C, while Yang & Xu (2023) note that the TiC/SiOC-0.25 precursor was pyrolyzed at 1400 °C to form SiC and TiC phases. The thermal treatment by He et al. (2013) reached the higher temperature of 1950 °C [4].

4. Discussion
ceramics studied to date (217 MPa at 1800 ◦ C) for matrix grain size of 8 𝛍 m. However, plastic deformation occurred at 1800 ◦ C for matrix grain sizes lower than 5 𝛍 m. Elastic behaviour with relatively high strength was found with increasing ZrB2 grain size introducing a microstructural tailoring factor that controls the high temperature mechanical response. A systematic study of grain size effect and SiC content on high temperature strength of ZrB2 based ceramics is required to understand the mechanical responses and failure mechanisms of these systems. In addition, this is an effect that has been characterised for room temperature strength. The effect of sintering method on mechanical properties has been analysed. Pressureless sintered ZrB2-20 MoSi2 had higher strength at 1500 ◦ C than hot-pressed ZrB2-20 MoSi2 while the room temperature strengths remained similar. In addition, hot-pressed ZrB2-15 MoSi2 and SPSed ZrB2-15 MoSi2 have similar room and high temperature strength. This effect is explained in terms of the secondary phases formed during hot-pressing and SPS of these materials, such as SiO2 and ZrO2 which soften the grain boundaries at high temperatures, especially the residual silica pockets. The addition of carbon to the system avoided the residual silica pocket formation during hot-pressing increasing substantially high temperature strength of ZrB2-15 MoSi2. In the present study, the general trend is that HfB2-based UHTCs had higher flexural strength at high temperature than ZrB2-based UHTCs (Tables 6 and 7). The strength at 1400 ◦ C of ZS20 and ZS20La and HS20 and HS20La is approximately twice that of ZrB2 and HfB2, 37 respectively. The reduction of the strength at 1400 ◦ C in the La2O3-doped UHTCs could be attributed to La2O3 inducing grain boundary softening.

a b s t r a c t
Predictably, if high porosity and high strength are simultaneously realized, the high entropy ultra-high temperature ceramics with light weight and excellent heat-shielding performance would be obtained.
In general, the sintering of highly porous UHTCs is very difficult because of their superhigh melting point and low self-diffusion coefficient [13] . Previous works on processing dense UHTC samples demonstrated that it was hard to obtain highly compact material even by using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) or Hot-Pressing (HP) method above 20 0 0 ° C. It was reported that TaC sample only reached the density of 92% by using SPS technique under pressure of 30 MPa at 2300 ° C [14] . As for high entropy UHTCs, the sintering and multi-component solid solution of principal elements are harder than those of a sample with single principal element. Consequently, nearly all reported works were subjected to reactive sintering by SPS and HP at relatively high temperatures of 22002300 ° C [ 9 , 15-18 ]. Evidently, it is necessary to adopt external driving force to densify UHTCs. In this context, multifarious sintering additives such as SiC, WC, MoSi 2 , TaSi 2 , B 4 C and C were employed to promote sintering kinetics of UHTCs, which facilitated the removal of surface oxides [19-22] . Among these additives, SiC has attracted great interest, because it not only enhanced sinterability, but also improved mechanical properties and oxidation resistance of ultra-high temperature ceramics [22] . Until now, SiC was basically added as a composite component in many literatures about high entropy ceramics. Thereinto, SiC was found to play an essential role in phase formation during sintering of high entropy UHTCs (borides or carbides)/SiC composites [23] . Besides, SiC-reinforced high entropy carbides have been reported to show remarkable mechanical properties by Lu et al. [24] and oxidation resistance by Wang et al. [25] .

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC) such as TiC, ZrB2, TiB2, and HfN are a particular type of materials which are characterized by their high strength, excellent corrosion resistance, good wettability with some liquid metals, high hardness, machinability using electrical discharge machining, high melting temperature (above 3000 °C), and high electrical and thermal conductivity [1 -10]. These unique properties make these ceramics suitable candidates for high-tech and extremely hot conditions such as solar energy plants, gas turbine stator blades, heat exchangers, and cutting tools [11 -25]. Due to the high melting point, powerful covalent bonding, and low self-di /uniFB00 usion coef/uniFB01 cient of UHTCs, it is arduous to densify such materials without implementing high temperatures and pressures [26 -34]. In this regard, methods such as pressureless sintering [35,36], hot pressing (HP) [37 -40] or hot isostatic pressing [31,32,35,36,41 -46], spark plasma sintering (SPS) [47 -56] microwave sintering [57,58] and laser sintering [59,60] are used widely to manufacture the UHTCs.

HfC showed a relative densification of ~75% at 2000 ◦ C, however, with 1 vol % of MoSi2 addition, the relative densification of ~97% was achieved at 1900 ◦ C, while 97 + % relative densification was obtained with 3 vol % and 9 vol % addition (even between 1700 ◦ C and 1800 ◦ C). Also, synthesized HfC powders, consolidated via SPS at 2300 ◦ C, 100 MPa, 30 min without using any sintering aid, elicited ~99.3% relative densification with inter-granular and intra-granular residual porosity in the microstructure [105].
Moreover,  a  pressureless  technique  has  also  been  employed  to densify UHTC carbides. The unreinforced and MoSi2 reinforced ZrC, TaC and HfC composited were cold pressed at 350 MPa and then sintered in Ar atmosphere at 1950 ◦ C for 1 h [106]. ZrC exhibited densification of ~73%,  which  increased  to  96.8%  with  20  vol  %  MoSi2 addition. Whereas, densification increased from 70% to 99.1% in HfC with 10 vol % MoSi2, and densification in TaC increased from 91%, to 98.4% with 10 vol % MoSi2 [106]. An enhanced densification has shown to induce damage  tolerance  (improving  the  mechanical,  oxidation,  and  wear behavior) in TMCs [95,107,108]. In general, monolithic carbides can be densified  using  pressure  assisted  techniques  at  higher  temperatures (2100 ◦ C)  and  pressures  (70 -100 MPa), which result in exaggerated grain growth and entrapped intragranular porosity with poor mechanical properties. However, addition of carbide or silicide reinforcement like SiC or MoSi2  or/and carbonaceous reinforcement reduce sintering temperature and improve densification.

1. Introduction
To thrive under extreme high-temperature conditions, the design of UHTC coatings prioritizes exceptional ablation resistance, high mechanical performances and interfacial adhesion, homogeneous material distribution, and a matched coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) with the substrate. For a coating to be deemed qualified, it must endeavor to fulfill these stringent design criteria to the highest possible extent. In order to enhance the performance and service life of UHTC coatings in long-term high temperature and high velocity airflow, researchers began to study UHTC coatings in terms of both structural design and material optimization. Hu et  al .  [13]  designed a SiC/UHTC/SiC  three-layer  coating  on  C/SiC  composites via CVD  and  brushingsintering. The inner SiC layer (CVD at 1050-1150 ℃) reduced thermal mismatch. The ZrB2-based UHTC middle layer (ZrB2/SiC/SiO2/B2O3/Al2O3) was sintered at 900 ℃ in Ar/air, forming borosilicate glass to seal cracks. The outer SiC layer (CVD at 12001250  ℃)  inhibited  B2O3  evaporation  and  oxidized  into  SiO2  at  high  temperatures. During oxidation at 1100-1500 ℃, ZrB2 reacted with oxygen to form ZrO2 and B2O3, while the B2O3-SiO2 glass phase exhibited fluidity to seal cracks. This synergy achieved low  mass  losses  (0.36-3.45  mg/cm²  after  68-114  h),  enabling  long-term  protection through oxygen barrier formation, self-healing, and thermal stress mitigation. Feng et al .  [14]  developed  a  spent  MoSi2-modified  ZrB2-SiC-MoSi2  composite  coating  on carbon substrates via spark plasma sintering (SPS). The coating was sintered at 1500 ℃ under 30 MPa pressure, forming a pore-free structure (~800 μm thick) with continuous and tight metallurgical bonding at  the  interface. 4. Discussion
XRD analysis indicated all starting materials remained in UHTC composites after SPS and new phases were not formed during sintering. Monolithic UHTCs had the largest grains (Table 3) owing to the higher temperature needed for SPS, 2000 ◦ C for ZrB2 instead of 1950 ◦ C required to sinter ZS20 and ZS20La, 2100 ◦ C for HfB2 instead of 2000 ◦ C for HS20 and HS20La, as well as the longer dwelling time. In addition, ZS20, ZS20La and HS20, HS20La had similar matrix grain sizes (4 𝛍 m and 6 𝛍 m) because of the similar temp/time profile used in processing. With regard to the secondary phase dispersion, hard agglomerates of SiC and La2O3 were not observed in ZrB2based UHTCs. However, in La-doped UHTCs SiC and La2O3 particles tended to remain in close proximity as expected when two particles of similar but small size are mixed with a larger one. The presence of SiC hard agglomerates (20-30 𝛍 m) in HfB2based UHTCs is attributed to the density difference between HfB2 (10.5 g/cm 3 ) and SiC (3.217 g/cm 3 ) which is double than in ZrB2-based UHTCs and makes SiC float in the ethanol solution during thermo-rotary drying. Use of higher solution viscosity or addition of a dispersant to avoid the presence of strong agglomerates after sintering is worthy of investigation.

a b s t r a c t
Due to advancements of hypersonic vehicles, ultra-high temperature thermal insulation materials are urgently requested to shield harsh environment with superhigh heat flux. Toward this target, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are the only choice due to their excellent capability at ultra-high temperatures. We herein report a novel highly porous high entropy (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C fabricated by foam-gelcasting-freeze drying technology combined with in-situ pressureless reaction sintering. The porous (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C exhibited ultra-high porosity of 86.4%-95.9%, as well as high strength and low thermal conductivity of 0.70-11.77 MPa and 0.164-0.239 W/(m /22c5 K), respectively. Specifically, SiC sintering additive only locates at the pit of the surface of sintering neck between UHTC grains, and there is no secondary phase or intergranular film at the grain boundary. Besides, the oxidation resistance of high entropy carbide powders is greatly improved compared with that of the mixed five carbide powders. This work clearly highlights the merits of highly porous high entropy (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C as an ultra-high temperature thermal insulation material.

4. Conclusions
The spark plasma sintering of a TiB2 sample was simulated numerically to obtain temperature and current density distribution during the process. The governing equations and fundamental laws, including Fourier ʼ s law and Kirchho /uniFB00 junction rule, solved using the /uniFB01 nite element method employing COMSOL Multiphysics. Each part of the SPS system was assumed to be at room temperature (300 K) and zero electrical potential at the initial time. The time-dependent voltage was applied, and the temperature was reached to 2000 °C at the center of the sintered TiB2 sample. According to the simulation results, the maximum temperature of the sample was observed at the center, and the temperature di /uniFB00 erence between the center and the outside of the sample was about 70 °C. The current density was higher in the sample compared to the die, which resulted in a higher amount of generated heat and temperature in the sample.

6. Silicides and MAX phases
A silicide in the context of intermetallics is a compound formed between any metallic species and Si, e.g., MoSi2, TaSi2, etc. [200]. Though these silicides were investigated for their use in improving the oxidation resistance of MB2  materials, later, these have been known to serve as effective sintering aids. As an example [201], microstructure of HfB2 processed via SPS at a maximum temperature of 2200 ◦ C possessed an extensive level of porosity while as HfB2 reinforced with 2.5 wt % Si3N4 processed via hot pressing showed improved densification (~99%) with marginal grain refinement [201]. Sciti et  al.  [202]  obtained  98 -99% dense ZrB2 -MoSi2 composites (15 -20 vol % MoSi2), Fig. 27, irrespective of the adopted processing technique while as Bellosi et al. [149] processed ZrB2 -15 vol % MoSi2  composite via SPS and hot pressing. SPS resulted in dense material in comparison to hot press processed composites.  These  silicides  limit  the  excessive  growth  of  borides  and  get accommodated  in  the  voids  left  by  the  matrix  grains  (due  to  their plasticity) [201]. Table 6 presents the role of various silicides, used as sintering aids in compounds of MB2 and MC, on the maximum operating temperature,  relative  theoretical  density,  and  important  mechanical properties of the processed composites. It can be observed from Table 6 that  the  introduction  of  ~10 -20%  silicide  phase  has  resulted  in  an improved densification (94 -99.9%) in all the composites at sintering temperatures less than 2000 ◦ C, which resulted in better mechanical performance,  i.e.,  attaining  high  hardness,  elastic  modulus,  fracture toughness,  and  flexural  strength.  Silicides  are  ductile  under  the  processing  conditions  and  their  deformation  contributes  to  densification during pressure assisted sintering [9].

[44] found that the addition of carbides such as ZrC, HfC, and TiC to the ZrB2-SiC material system has less effect on the mechanical properties compared to the addition of SiC, due to their CTE being closer to ZrB2. Adding silicides like MoSi2 as a third phase to the ZrB2-SiC system can also enhance its strength [45]. This is because MoSi2 added to the ZrB2-SiC system form (Mo,Zr)B2 solid solution, which retains high strength even at  high  temperatures.  In  addition,  MoSi2  can  act  as  a  sintering  aid  to  facilitate  the densification of ZrB2-SiC powders, contributing to the improvement of the composite material's  mechanical  properties  [46].  Wang et  al .[47]  fabricated  the  ZrB2-5%SiCTaSi2 matrix via SPS at 1700 ℃ with 35 MPa during heating in a vacuum environment. Incorporating  5%-15%  (in  mass)  TaSi2  into  a  ZrB2-5%  SiC  matrix  significantly enhances the fracture toughness of the composite from 5.44 MPa·m 1/2 to 7.91 MPa·m 1/2 , while  gradually  reducing  the  flexural  strength  from  595  MPa  to  444  MPa.  The toughening mechanism primarily arises from the mismatch in coefficients of thermal expansion between TaSi2 (14×10 -6 /K) and ZrB2 (6.7×10 -6 /K), which induces interfacial stresses promoting crack deflection and branching. Additionally, TaSi2 acts as a soft second phase that weakens grain boundary bonding, altering the fracture mode from transgranular to a mixed intergranular-transgranular pattern, thereby increasing crack propagation  resistance. question: He et al. (2013) report the solids loading that produced the most homogeneous HfB2–MoSi2 gelcast microstructure, and Zhang et al. (2025) report the size of the individual HfB2 particles that aggregate into hollow spheres in HfB2-Glucose powder. What is the optimal solids loading vol.% found in He et al. (2013), and what is the individual HfB2 particle size range reported in Zhang et al. (2025) before they aggregate into spheres?
answer: The optimal solids loading vol.% found in He et al. (2013) is 45 vol.%, which produced the most homogeneous HfB2–MoSi2 gelcast microstructure [1][3]. The individual HfB2 particle size range reported in Zhang et al. (2025) before they aggregate into spheres is less than 100 nm [4].

chunks.text:
4. Conclusions
HfB2-MoSi2 ultra high temperature ceramic composites were prepared through aqueous gelcasting route. The HfB2 and MoSi2 aqueous suspensions could be dispersed and stabilized when using 0.8 wt.% PAA as dispersant at pH value of 10. The solids loading in the HfB2-MoSi2 composite suspensions had significant effects on the relative green density, relative sintered density, microstructure and mechanical properties of HfB2-20 vol.% MoSi2 composites. With the increase of the solids loading, both the relative green densities and relative sintered densities increased firstly, and then decreased once the solids loading exceeded 45 vol.%. The highest relative green density and relative sintered density and the most homogeneous microstructure of the HfB2-MoSi2 green and sintered bodies was obtained when the solids loading was 45 vol.%. The HfB2-MoSi2 composites with 45 vol.% solids loading had the best mechanical properties. The highest green strength, flexural strength and fracture toughness were 38.7 ± 5.3 MPa, 415.9 ± 17.0 MPa and 4.24 ± 0.22 MPa m 1/2 , respectively.

3. Results and discussion
adsorbedontotheparticlesurfaceand totally ionized at pH 10, finally causing enlarging the zeta potential and enhancing electrostatic repulsion. When pH below 10, the zeta potential was low and the viscosity was high. When pH above 10, the ion concentration increased, compressing the double-charge layer and reducing the zeta potential, finally increased the apparent viscosity of the suspension. Besides, it was also found that the HfB2 and MoSi2 suspension had the lowest viscosity when using 0.8 wt.% PAA (see Fig. 4b). When PAA content below 0.8 wt.%, it was unsaturated adsorbed onto the particle surface, resulting in low electrostatic repulsion between ceramic particles and thereby forcing particles together, finally caused a high viscosity. When PAA content above 0.8 wt.%, it was over-saturated adsorbed, inducing an inevitable increase of viscosity. Consequently, it was believed that HfB2 andMoSi2 could achieve stable when using 0.8 wt.% PAA at pH 10.
In addition, Fig. 5 gives the relationship of shear rates and the viscosities of the HfB2-MoSi2 suspensions with different solids loading. When the solids loading was below 45 vol.%, the suspension could maintain low viscosities. However, once the solids loading reached to 48 vol.%, the viscosity of the suspension increased sharply. Over-high solids loading caused the inhomogeneous microstructure in the green body, finally inducing nonuniformity in the sintered body and the decrease in mechanical properties. Considering high solids loading resulted in low drying and sintering shrinkages, high green and sintered strength, and homogeneous final-microstructure, the optimum solids loading seemed to be chosen as 45 vol.%.
3.2. Effect of solids loading on microstructure of HfB2-MoSi2 composites

3. Results and discussion
In the above sections, the sample with 45 vol.% solids loading had the highest relative green and sintered densities, as well as the most homogeneous green and sintered microstructure. Therefore, the HfB2-MoSi2 composite with 45 vol.% solids loading was believed to have the highest mechanical properties. Table 1 lists the mechanical properties of the HfB2-MoSi2 composites with different solids loading. The variation trends of the mechanical properties, such as greenandsinteredflexural strength, and fracture toughness showed agoodagreementwiththeaboveexperimentalresults.Themechanical properties of the composites with 35 and 40 vol.% solids loading were low due to their low densities and high residual porosities resulting from low solids loading. However, the mechanical properties of the composite with 48 vol.% solids loading was also low because of its inhomogeneous microstructure resulting from the high viscosity of the suspension. In our study, the green strength, flexural strength and fracture toughness reached to the highest values when the solids loading of the suspensions was 45 vol.%, and they were 38.7 ± 5.3 MPa, 415.9 ± 17.0 MPa and 4.24 ± 0.22 MPa m 1/2 , respectively. Silvestroni et al. prepared a HfB2-20 vol.% MoSi2 composite using cold isostatic pressing and pressureless sintering technique, and the relative sintered density, flexural strength and fracture toughness were 98%, 405 ± 72 MPa and 4.1 ± 0.4 MPa m 1/2 , respectively [9]. The sintering conditions, relative sintered densities and mechanical properties for the cold isostatic pressing-pressureless sintered and gelcasting-pressureless sintered HfB2-MoSi2 composites are listed in Table 2 for comparison. It was clearly found that the density and mechanical properties of the HfB2-MoSi2 composite prepared through aqueous gelcasting and pressureless sintering in our research reached the reported values in the literature.

3. Results and discussions
After confirmation of phase composition, according to the SEM images shown in Fig. 2B, morphology and structure of HfB2 powders are strongly influenced by the choice of carbon sources. The HfB2 powders prepared with carbon black, mannitol, phenolic resin, and  fructose  exhibit  similar  morphologies,  consisting  of  agglomerated  granules  with  a particle size of approximately 300 nm. However, a significant amount of free carbon exists in these HfB2 powders, which is detrimental to the subsequent sintering process. This is because the free carbon typically segregates at the boundaries between ceramic particles, acting  as  a  physical  barrier  that  hinders  direct  interparticle  contact  and  mass  transport during sintering, thereby inhibiting densification and grain growth.[27] In contrast, HfB₂- Glucose and HfB2-Inositol possess a spherical morphology. The HfB2 particles themselves are less than 100 nm in size, and these particles aggregate to form spheres with diameters distributed in the range of 3-5 μm. This unique morphology of HfB2 powders contributes to the reduced particle size, indicating a strong correlation between the structure of the raw carbon materials and the nature of the free carbon in the final product [18].

3. Results and discussion
Fig. 6 shows the relative densities of the HfB2-MoSi2 composite green and sintered bodies with different solids loading. With the solids loading increased from 35 to 45 vol.%, the relative green and sintered densities increased. Generally, higher relative green density meant lower linear shrinkage during drying and sintering, as well as higher mechanical properties. Unfortunately, however, over-high solids loading (48 vol.%) caused the viscosity of the suspension increased sharply, leading to a lower relative green and sintered densities.
The microstructures of the HfB2-MoSi2 green bodies are given in Fig. 7. When the solids loading was 35 vol.%, the relative green density of the HfB2-MoSi2 composite was low. With the increasing of the solids loading, the green densities of the HfB2-MoSi2
Fig. 7. Microstructures of the HfB2-MoSi2 green bodies with different solids loading: (a) 35 vol.%; (b) 40 vol.%; (c) 45 vol.%; (d) 48 vol.%.
Fig. 8. Microstructures of the HfB2-MoSi2 sintered bodies with different solids loading: (a) 35 vol.%; (b) 40 vol.%; (c) 45 vol.%; (d) 48 vol.%.
composites increased correspondingly. The microstructure of the HfB2-MoSi2 composite green body was homogeneous and uniform, and the relative green density was the highest. However, when the solids loading was 48 vol.%, the microstructure became inhomogeneous and there were lots of agglomerates observed, owing to the bad dispersibility and high viscosity of the HfB2-MoSi2 suspension.

question: He et al. (2013) report that the 45 vol.% solids-loading HfB2–MoSi2 gelcast sample had the highest relative densities among all loadings tested, while Zhang et al. (2025) report that the hollow rod-shaped HfB2-Glucose sample performed far worse in electromagnetic wave absorption than its spherical counterpart despite also being hollow. What ranking did the 45 vol.% sample achieve for density in He et al. (2013), and what ranking did the rod-shaped sample achieve for absorption in Zhang et al. (2025)?
answer: The 45 vol.% sample achieved the highest relative green density and relative sintered density among all loadings tested [3]. The rod-shaped sample achieved a much worse ranking for absorption, with a minimum reflection loss (RLmin) of 6.12 dB, compared to the hollow spherical HfB2-Glucose sample [1].

3.3 EMW absorption performance of HfB2 powders prepared with glucose
The  electromagnetic  wave  absorption  performance  of  the  three  distinct  HfB2 morphologies  (hollow  spherical,  hollow  rod-shaped,  and  granular)  was  systematically evaluated. While the hollow spherical HfB2-Glucose demonstrates exceptional absorption performance, both the hollow rod-shaped and granular morphologies exhibit significantly weaker  absorption  capabilities. To  elucidate the  underlying  electromagnetic  wave absorption  mechanism,  the  hollow  spherical  and  granular  samples  were  selected  as representative morphologies for detailed investigation. The  real  part  (μ'),  imaginary  part  (μ''),  and  magnetic  loss  tangent  (tanμ)  of  the complex permeability for both the hollow spherical and granular samples are shown in Fig. S2a-c. The results indicate that the profiles and values of these magnetic parameters are remarkably similar and consistently low for both morphologies. This clearly demonstrates that the difference in their electromagnetic wave absorption performance does not originate from  magnetic  properties.  Furthermore,  the  negligible  magnetic  loss  confirms  that  the dominant loss mechanism is dielectric in nature. Therefore, the subsequent analysis focuses primarily on the differences in dielectric loss between the two samples. Journal Pre-proof

3. Results and discussion
In the above sections, the sample with 45 vol.% solids loading had the highest relative green and sintered densities, as well as the most homogeneous green and sintered microstructure. Therefore, the HfB2-MoSi2 composite with 45 vol.% solids loading was believed to have the highest mechanical properties. Table 1 lists the mechanical properties of the HfB2-MoSi2 composites with different solids loading. The variation trends of the mechanical properties, such as greenandsinteredflexural strength, and fracture toughness showed agoodagreementwiththeaboveexperimentalresults.Themechanical properties of the composites with 35 and 40 vol.% solids loading were low due to their low densities and high residual porosities resulting from low solids loading. However, the mechanical properties of the composite with 48 vol.% solids loading was also low because of its inhomogeneous microstructure resulting from the high viscosity of the suspension. In our study, the green strength, flexural strength and fracture toughness reached to the highest values when the solids loading of the suspensions was 45 vol.%, and they were 38.7 ± 5.3 MPa, 415.9 ± 17.0 MPa and 4.24 ± 0.22 MPa m 1/2 , respectively. Silvestroni et al. prepared a HfB2-20 vol.% MoSi2 composite using cold isostatic pressing and pressureless sintering technique, and the relative sintered density, flexural strength and fracture toughness were 98%, 405 ± 72 MPa and 4.1 ± 0.4 MPa m 1/2 , respectively [9]. The sintering conditions, relative sintered densities and mechanical properties for the cold isostatic pressing-pressureless sintered and gelcasting-pressureless sintered HfB2-MoSi2 composites are listed in Table 2 for comparison. It was clearly found that the density and mechanical properties of the HfB2-MoSi2 composite prepared through aqueous gelcasting and pressureless sintering in our research reached the reported values in the literature.

4. Conclusions
HfB2-MoSi2 ultra high temperature ceramic composites were prepared through aqueous gelcasting route. The HfB2 and MoSi2 aqueous suspensions could be dispersed and stabilized when using 0.8 wt.% PAA as dispersant at pH value of 10. The solids loading in the HfB2-MoSi2 composite suspensions had significant effects on the relative green density, relative sintered density, microstructure and mechanical properties of HfB2-20 vol.% MoSi2 composites. With the increase of the solids loading, both the relative green densities and relative sintered densities increased firstly, and then decreased once the solids loading exceeded 45 vol.%. The highest relative green density and relative sintered density and the most homogeneous microstructure of the HfB2-MoSi2 green and sintered bodies was obtained when the solids loading was 45 vol.%. The HfB2-MoSi2 composites with 45 vol.% solids loading had the best mechanical properties. The highest green strength, flexural strength and fracture toughness were 38.7 ± 5.3 MPa, 415.9 ± 17.0 MPa and 4.24 ± 0.22 MPa m 1/2 , respectively.
