question: Comparing peak processing temperatures for ultra-high-temperature-ceramic powders: Zhang et al. (2025) synthesized hollow-spherical HfB2-Glucose powder via boro/carbothermal reduction, and Yang & Xu (2023) formed the TiC/SiOC-0.25 ceramic precursor via pyrolysis. Which synthesis reached the higher peak temperature?
answer: The synthesis by Zhang et al. (2025) reached a higher peak temperature. According to the source, the peak temperature during the synthesis of HfB2-Glucose powders was 1400 ℃ [3]. In contrast, the peak temperature for the pyrolysis of the TiC/SiOC-0.25 ceramic precursor by Yang & Xu (2023) is not provided in the given sources. Therefore, based on the information available, the synthesis by Zhang et al. (2025) reached the higher peak temperature.

In  this  regard,  numerous  strategies  for  synthesizing  ceramic  powders  have  been reported. Techniques such as sol-gel [9, 10] and co-precipitation are commonly employed for precursor preparation, while the final powders are typically synthesized via methods including boro/carbothermal reduction, borothermal reduction [11, 12], and selfpropagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) [13]. For instance, Venugopal et al. [14] combined  a  sol-gel  process  with  boro/carbothermal  reduction  to  obtain  pure-phase nanoscale  HfB2 and  investigated  the  effect  of  raw  material  ratios.  Furthermore,  the influence  of  carbon  sources  on  the  particle  size  and  morphology  of  HfB2 has  been documented  [15].  Ni  et  al.  [16]  synthesized  HfB2  powders  using  boro/carbothermal reduction of HfO2 and elucidated the reaction pathway. Meanwhile, our previous work [17] reported the preparation of ultra-fine HfB2 powders via dispersant-assisted co-precipitation combined with boro/carbothermal reduction. Journal Pre-proof

In our previous works [3,8,46,47], it was proposed to combine the stages of nanodispersed silicon carbide synthesis and UHTC production at moderate temperatures of 1700-1900 °C, i.e., to carry out hightemperature compaction of HfB2-(SiO2-C) obtained by the sol-gel method, in accordance with the ideology of reaction sintering. The UHTC of HfB2-xSiC composition (x = 10-65 vol%) obtained in a similar way were studied for resistance to airflow oxidation in the thermal analysis mode at temperatures of 20-1400 °C [46]. And for HfB2-30 vol % SiC materials synthesized at minimum temperatures of 1700 and 1800 °C, single tests were carried out under the influence of subsonic [8] and supersonic dissociated air flow on a high-frequency induction plasmatron [3,47], which showed that they behave at least as well as those obtained during spark plasma sintering of coarse-crystalline SiC and HfB2 powders [9-11].
The objective of this work is to compare the behaviour of ultra-hightemperature HfB2-30 vol% SiC ceramic composites obtained by hot pressing of a highly dispersed HfB2-(SiO2-C) composite powder at a higher temperature of 1900 °C, under the influence of subsonic and supersonic dissociated air flow of a high-frequency induction plasmatron.

The formation mechanism of the carbon spheres was elucidated based on the ¹³C NMR results  and  insights  from  previous  studies  [29-31].  During  the  precursor  reaction  and precipitation,  the 13 C chemical shifts  observed for the HfB2-Glucose  and HfB2-Inositol precursors agreed with the predicted results. As shown in Fig. 7a, glucose molecules in the precursor undergo an intramolecular hemiacetal reaction, forming a stable six-membered ring  (pyranose)  structure.  Meanwhile,  as  shown  in  Fig.  7b,  inositol  has  a  symmetric cyclohexane-like ring structure with a hydroxyl group on each carbon atom, resulting in a single 13 C NMR peak at 73.4 ppm. In conclusion, a spherical morphology is more readily formed from carbon sources with a hexagonal ring structure (e.g., glucose after cyclization, inositol) than from those with a linear chain structure. This is demonstrated by the distinct Journal Pre-proof morphologies of HfB2-Glucose and HfB2-Inositol compared to those derived from other carbon sources.

A  very  promising  group  of  these  advanced  materials  that  were developed as potential systems for ultra-high temperature applications is  the  so-called  dual-phase  high-entropy  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics (DPHE-UHTCs).  As the first, Qin et al. [21] processed DPHE-UHTCs from  binary  borides  and  binary  carbide  powders  with relative densities higher than 99% consisting of a hexagonal HEB phase and a cubic HEC phase. The thermodynamic relation that governs the compositions  of  the  boride  and  carbide  phases  in  equilibrium  was described and a thermodynamic model was proposed. The hardness of the prepared DPHE-UHTCs was higher than the weighted linear average of the two single-phase high-entropy UHTCs, which are already harder than the RoM averages of individual binary carbides and borides. The results proved that the microstructure parameters and mechanical and tribological properties of DPHE-UHTCs can be designed by changing the phase  fraction  of  the  HEB  and  HEC  phases.  Luo  et  al.  [22]  recently developed fine-grained dual-phase, high-entropy ceramics (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)B2-(Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)C  with  different phase  ratios  using  powders  synthesized  via  a  boro/carbothermal reduction. High-density, homogeneously distributed DPHE-UHTCs, with different  boride-to-carbide  phase  ratios,  were  prepared  by  SPS  processing at 2000 ℃ for 10 min under 30 MPa applied load. They developed systems with very fine microstructure with average grain size from 0.5 µ m to 1.5 µ m, and the system with 18 vol% boride phase exhibited the highest Vickers hardness and fracture toughness with values 24.2 ± 0.3 GPa and 3.19 ± 0.24 MPa ⋅ m 0.5 ,  respectively. question: He et al. (2013) report the optimal polyacrylic acid (PAA) dispersant content for stabilizing HfB2–MoSi2 aqueous suspensions, and Zhang et al. (2025) identify which carbon sources promote a spherical (rather than granular) HfB2 morphology. What was the optimal PAA content and pH in He et al. (2013), and which carbon source structure does Zhang et al. (2025) say favors the spherical HfB2 morphology?
answer: The optimal PAA content and pH in He et al. (2013) for stabilizing HfB2–MoSi2 aqueous suspensions were 0.8 wt.% PAA and pH 10, respectively [1]. Zhang et al. (2025) identified that using glucose or inositol as the carbon source promotes a spherical HfB2 morphology [3].

3. Results and discussion
3.1. Stability and apparent viscosities of HfB2-MoSi2 aqueous suspensions
It is well known that the suspension with high stability and low viscosity can help to obtain bodies with homogeneous microstructure, high relative green density, high green strength and high sintered strength [24-26]. The parameters, such as pH value, dispersant content and solids loading, have significant effects on the stability of the suspension.
Fig. 2 shows the zeta potentials of HfB2 and MoSi2 as a function of pH value, respectively. The isoelectric points (IEP, the pH which the net charge on the particle surface is zero) for HfB2 and MoSi2 was pH 6.4 and 4.3, respectively. At pH 10-11, HfB2 and MoSi2 had the maximum zeta potentials, /C0 35 and /C0 44 mV, respectively. In this study, PAA was used as dispersant. In order to find the optimum stabilizing condition for HfB2 and MoSi2 suspensions, the effects of pH value and PAA content on the stability of HfB2 and MoSi2 suspensions were studied using sedimentation test, as shown in Fig. 3a and b. It was clear found that the HfB2 suspension even had a good stability after 24 h sedimentation when using 0.8 wt.% PAA at pH 10. The sedimentation tests also showed that the optimum dispersing pH value and PAA content for MoSi2 aqueous suspension were 0.8 wt.% and pH 10, respectively.

4. Conclusion
In  this  study,  HfB2  powders  with  three  different  morphologies  (hollow  spherical, hollow rod-shaped and granular) were prepared by regulating the type of carbon source and stirring temperature during the co-precipitation process. Among them, the formation mechanism of spherical HfB2 powder was clarified. The carbon hexagonal ring structure produced during the precursor complexation process promoted the generation of a hollow spherical carbon shell. Subsequently, HfO2 particles were generated on the surface of the carbon shell during the heating process of the precursor and underwent a boro/carbothermal reduction, ultimately forming a unique morphology of granular HfB2 attached to the hollow spherical carbon shell. The synthesized hollow spherical HfB2 powders exhibit excellent EMW absorption  capability,  with  a  minimum  reflection  loss  reaching  -57.8 dB. This characteristic arises from the synergy of three factors: the good conductivity of HfB2, the interfacial  effect  between  HfB2  and  carbon,  and  the  unique  hollow  structure  of  carbon spheres.  This  study  on  the  morphology  control  and  functionalization  of  HfB2  ceramics provides valuable insights for designing high-performance EM absorption materials. Journal Pre-proof

Various  hollow  structured  HfB2  powders  were  synthesized  via  a  co-precipitation assisted boron/carbon thermal reduction method, and the influence mechanism of carbon source  on  morphology  was  systematically  investigated.  When  glucose  or  inositol  was employed as the carbon source, the hexagonal ring structure formed in the precursor during complexation directed the growth of HfB2 nanoparticles that were anchored on hollow spherical carbon templates, resulting in a unique hollow core-shell architecture. Additionally, the morphology could be precisely adjusted from hollow spherical to hollow rod-like  structures by  simply  controlling  the  stirring  temperature  during complexation. The growth process of HfB2 with various morphologies was elucidated, and the mechanism of  this  unique  morphological  evolution  was  revealed  to  originate  from  the  structural differences  of  precursor  complexes.  The  HfB2  powders with  hollow spherical  structure derived  from  glucose  exhibits  excellent  electromagnetic  absorption  ability  due  to  the synergistic effect of multiple scattering and interface polarization within the unique hollow structure, with a maximum reflection loss (RL) of -57.8 dB. Quantitative analysis of the loss  mechanisms  further  demonstrates  that  the  outstanding  absorption  performance  is predominantly governed by polarization loss rather than conduction loss.

After confirmation of phase composition, according to the SEM images shown in Fig. 2B, morphology and structure of HfB2 powders are strongly influenced by the choice of carbon sources. The HfB2 powders prepared with carbon black, mannitol, phenolic resin, and  fructose  exhibit  similar  morphologies,  consisting  of  agglomerated  granules  with  a particle size of approximately 300 nm. However, a significant amount of free carbon exists in these HfB2 powders, which is detrimental to the subsequent sintering process. This is because the free carbon typically segregates at the boundaries between ceramic particles, acting  as  a  physical  barrier  that  hinders  direct  interparticle  contact  and  mass  transport during sintering, thereby inhibiting densification and grain growth.[27] In contrast, HfB₂- Glucose and HfB2-Inositol possess a spherical morphology. The HfB2 particles themselves are less than 100 nm in size, and these particles aggregate to form spheres with diameters distributed in the range of 3-5 μm. This unique morphology of HfB2 powders contributes to the reduced particle size, indicating a strong correlation between the structure of the raw carbon materials and the nature of the free carbon in the final product [18].

question: Zhang et al. (2025) attribute the exceptional electromagnetic wave absorption of hollow-spherical HfB2-Glucose primarily to interface polarization, while Yang & Xu (2023) attribute part of the TiC/SiOC–Al2O3 coating's absorption to the nanoscale surface area of TiC nanoparticles. What dissipation mechanism does Zhang et al. (2025) identify as dominant, and what property of TiC nanoparticles does Yang & Xu (2023) say enables enhanced absorption?
answer: Zhang et al. (2025) identify interface polarization as the dominant dissipation mechanism [3]. Yang & Xu (2023) state that the nanoscale surface area of TiC nanoparticles enables enhanced absorption [1].

3. Results and discussion
The  absorption  mechanism  in  the  SiOC/Al2O3 matrix,  involving nano-sized  TiC  and  SiC  particles,  is  a  complex  interplay  of  multiple factors that contribute to the enhanced EM absorption properties. In the SiOC/Al2O3 matrix, the small size of TiC and SiC phases allows for better dispersion  within  the  matrix,  preventing  aggregation  and  ensuring  a uniform distribution throughout the material. This specific morphology contributes  to  their  efficient  scattering  properties,  enabling  multiple scattering  events  with  electromagnetic  waves  and  enhancing  absorption.  Nano-sized  absorbents play  a  critical  role  in  enhancing  EM  absorption capabilities [24]. TiC NPs offer a significantly higher surface area-to-volume  ratio  compared  to  larger-sized  absorbents,  enabling more interaction sites and repeated scattering events with EM waves. The  size  and  shape  of  these  nano-sized  absorbents  can  be  precisely engineered to match specific resonance frequencies of EM waves, as demonstrated  in  our  previous  work  [22].  Notably,  TiC  NPs  exhibit efficient EM absorption even at reduced thicknesses while maintaining high absorption performance. This phenomenon can be attributed to nanoscale effects [25 -28]. The nanoscale size of TiC nanoparticles introduces unique electromagnetic properties. The high surface area-to-volume ratio of nanoparticles enhances their interaction with incident electromagnetic waves, allowing for increased EM absorption efficiency, even with a lower material thickness.

Under an alternating  electromagnetic  field,  these  interfaces  act  as  potent centers  for  charge accumulation and relaxation, leading to the dramatically increased polarization loss. The granular structure, lacking such an intricate interface network, fails to generate substantial polarization, resulting in its poor absorption performance. Furthermore, the unique architecture provides numerous solid-void and HfB2/carbon  interfaces. These  heterointerfaces  act  as  centers  for  charge  accumulation under the alternating electromagnetic field, leading to intensive interface polarization and associated  relaxation  [37-39]. 3.3 EMW absorption performance of HfB2 powders prepared with glucose
The  electromagnetic  wave  absorption  performance  of  the  three  distinct  HfB2 morphologies  (hollow  spherical,  hollow  rod-shaped,  and  granular)  was  systematically evaluated. While the hollow spherical HfB2-Glucose demonstrates exceptional absorption performance, both the hollow rod-shaped and granular morphologies exhibit significantly weaker  absorption  capabilities. To  elucidate the  underlying  electromagnetic  wave absorption  mechanism,  the  hollow  spherical  and  granular  samples  were  selected  as representative morphologies for detailed investigation. The  real  part  (μ'),  imaginary  part  (μ''),  and  magnetic  loss  tangent  (tanμ)  of  the complex permeability for both the hollow spherical and granular samples are shown in Fig. S2a-c. The results indicate that the profiles and values of these magnetic parameters are remarkably similar and consistently low for both morphologies. This clearly demonstrates that the difference in their electromagnetic wave absorption performance does not originate from  magnetic  properties.  Furthermore,  the  negligible  magnetic  loss  confirms  that  the dominant loss mechanism is dielectric in nature. Therefore, the subsequent analysis focuses primarily on the differences in dielectric loss between the two samples. Journal Pre-proof

Various  hollow  structured  HfB2  powders  were  synthesized  via  a  co-precipitation assisted boron/carbon thermal reduction method, and the influence mechanism of carbon source  on  morphology  was  systematically  investigated.  When  glucose  or  inositol  was employed as the carbon source, the hexagonal ring structure formed in the precursor during complexation directed the growth of HfB2 nanoparticles that were anchored on hollow spherical carbon templates, resulting in a unique hollow core-shell architecture. Additionally, the morphology could be precisely adjusted from hollow spherical to hollow rod-like  structures by  simply  controlling  the  stirring  temperature  during complexation. The growth process of HfB2 with various morphologies was elucidated, and the mechanism of  this  unique  morphological  evolution  was  revealed  to  originate  from  the  structural differences  of  precursor  complexes.  The  HfB2  powders with  hollow spherical  structure derived  from  glucose  exhibits  excellent  electromagnetic  absorption  ability  due  to  the synergistic effect of multiple scattering and interface polarization within the unique hollow structure, with a maximum reflection loss (RL) of -57.8 dB. Quantitative analysis of the loss  mechanisms  further  demonstrates  that  the  outstanding  absorption  performance  is predominantly governed by polarization loss rather than conduction loss.

1. Introduction
Carbon fiber reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramics (Cf/UHTCs) have attracted significant attention due to their exceptional properties, including  high  melting  points  (over  3000 ◦ C),  low  oxygen  diffusion coefficients(Li  et  al.,  2025),  outstanding  mechanical properties(Wang et al., 2024), and high-temperature chemical stability(Bai et al., 2023). The excellent performance of Cf/UHTCs in extremely high-temperature environments makes them ideal materials for applications in hypersonic vehicle thermal protection systems (Wyatt et al., 2023).

2.  Basic properties of UHTCs
UHTCs  exhibit  exceptional  high-temperature  stability,  mechanical strength,  and  oxidation  resistance,  making  them  materials  of  choice  in extreme environments. They are particularly well-suited for thermal protection  systems  in  aerospace,  military,  and  energy  applications  [12]. Among the most representative UHTC materials are ZrB2, HfB2, ZrC, and HfC, which have been extensively studied and applied due to their distinctive mechanical, thermophysical, and chemical properties. Basic physical and mechanical properties of these UHTCs are compiled in Table 1.

1. Introduction
Key thermal protection components of hypersonic vehicles, such as the  nose  cone  and  leading  edges  of  the  wings,  continuously  endure extreme and complex environments involving ultra-high temperatures, strong oxidation, and intense thermal shocks during their service life [1, 2].  Among  the  various  material  systems,  carbon  fiber  reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (Cf/UHTCMCs) have emerged  as  ideal  candidates  for  thermal  protection  systems  and load-bearing thermal structures in hypersonic vehicles, owing to their exceptional properties [3 -6]. As hypersonic flight technology advances toward practical application and industrialization, the mass production of hypersonic vehicles imposes increasingly stringent demands on the cost-effectiveness and production cycle time of Cf/UHTCMC fabrication technologies [7]. This requires scaling up raw material production to reduce unit costs. It also necessitates innovations in forming processes and densification techniques to create a system that ensures both economic efficiency and  reliability,  meeting  the  engineering  application requirements for thermal protection components in the next generation of hypersonic vehicles [8 -10].

1. Introduction
Hypersonic  flighting  vehicles  are  a  new  technology  point  for  the future development of aerospace. The aerodynamic heating generated by the re-entry of the aircrafts leads to the damage of thermal structures. Particularly,  the  ablation  behavior  of  thermal  structural  materials casued by the aerodynamic force, oxidation and airflow scouring is a critical  issue.  Therefore,  the  thermal  structural  materials  in  extreme environments should have good stability against oxidation and ablation at high temperatures [1 -6].
Ultra-high temperature ceramics have become ideal thermal structural materials in the aerospace field due to their extremely high melting point  ( > 3000 ◦ C)  and  good  chemical  stability  [7 -10].  Among  them, Hf-based  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (HfB2 and  HfC)  exhibit excellent oxidation and ablation resistance, and become the potential candidates for key components of hypersonic vehicles (such as wing leading  edge,  nose  cone  and  tail  nozzle)  to  meet  high  temperature ablation  conditions  [11 -18].  Zhang  et  al.  [11] introduced  different thicknesses  of  HfC  coatings  on  carbon/carbon  composites  (C/C)  by chemical vapor deposition (CVD). The effect of coating thickness on the ablation  resistance  and  the  microstructural  evolution  of  the  ablation layer  was  investigated.  The  results  show  that  solid  HfO2 grains

1. Introduction
Me borides and carbides (Me = Zr or Hf) with melting points in excess of 3000 °C are promising candidates for thermal protection systems (TPS) in hypersonic vehicles, including sharp-leading edges (SLE) and sharp-nose cones (SNC) [1,2], and belong to a family of materials termed ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs). Ideally, UHTCs components for hypersonic applications have to maintain a stable structure under the operating conditions, which involve no phase transformations and no shape changes during operating conditions. No shape changes require high creep resistance and, e.g the components should deform no further than the elastic limit to be reusable. In addition, UHTCs components require a combination of properties at high temperatures, such as high temperature strength, oxidation resistance, with high thermal conductivity being particularly desirable to maximize thermal transport during exposure in high-temperature reactive environments [3]. Me borides possess a better combination of oxidation resistance and thermal conductivity than Me carbides and have been the subject of intense research over the last 15 years [4,5]. Moreover, ZrB2 and HfB2 exhibit higher thermal conductivity ( ∼ 100 W/(m K)) at room temperature (and > 50 W/(m K) at 1900 °C) [6] than their respective carbides (<40 W/(m K) from room temperature to 800 °C) [5]. However, the oxidation resistance of MeB2 is relatively poor which could compromise the structural stability of UHTCs components under hypersonic operating conditions. As a consequence, UHTCs research has focused over the last decade on the oxidation resistance of MeB2based UHTCs and di /uniFB00 erent approaches to improve oxidation resistance

question: Kai Zhao's ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramic sample S-30 (30 vol.% ZrC hollow microspheres) has 14.05% closed porosity; is its thermal conductivity higher or lower than the 0.94 W/(m·K) thermal conductivity Zhuojie Shao reports for single-component porous ZrC at 85% porosity?
answer: Kai Zhao's ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramic sample S-30 has a thermal conductivity of 1.69 W/(m·K), which is higher than the 0.94 W/(m·K) thermal conductivity reported by Zhuojie Shao for single-component porous ZrC at 85% porosity [3][4].

Generally, it can be observed that the thermal conductivity of these materials decreases gradually with increasing porosity. Furthermore, the thermal conductivity varies among different material systems. (5) to calculate the thermal resistance at the ZrC/SiC interface as 1.81 × 10 -10 m K W -1 , the air/ZrC interface thermal resistance as 4.96 × 10 -8 m K W -1 , and the air/SiC interface thermal resistance as 1.07 × 10 -8 m K W -1 . The thermal resistance of the solid-gas interface is significantly higher than that of the solid-solid interface. Moreover, the thermal resistance of the air/ZrC interface is higher than that of the air/SiC interface. Consequently, as the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increases, the thermal resistance of the material can be effectively elevated, leading to a reduction in thermal conductivity. This also explains the lowest thermal conductivity of the S-30 sample.

a b s t r a c t
However, considering the high intrinsic thermal conductivity of typical UHTC, e.g. 29.3 W/(m · K) for HfC, 33.5 W/(m · K) for TaC, and 33.5 W/(m · K) for ZrC [11] , it would be very di/uniFB03cult to obtain new types of UHTC insulators by merely enhancing the porosity. Therefore, in order to achieve lower thermal conductivity, further adoptions of multicomponent concept may be helpful to enhance the phonon scattering during the thermal conduction through solid skeletons [12-14] . For example, the reported porous high entropy UHTCs with the porosity of 80.99% has a low thermal conductivity of 0.39 W/(m · K), as well as high strength of 3.45 MPa. The thermal conductivity is obviously lower than that (0.94 W/(m · K)) of single-component porous ZrC with the porosity of 85% [8] . Predictably, under the situation of the superhigh porosity ( > 90%), a lower thermal conductivity and better heat-shielding performance would be achieved on the basis of the multicomponent solid solution.

a b s t r a c t
Porous ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are recognized as novel candidates for fulfilling the requirements of thermal protection systems of hypersonic aircrafts, as they possess excellent hightemperature resistance and low thermal conductivity. Currently, the reported porous UHTCs predominantly exhibit an open pore structure. By contrast, closed-cell UHTCs, formed by employing ceramic hollow microspheres (HMs) as pore-forming agents, hold great potential for achieving superior thermal insulation performance. Unfortunately, the implementation of this strategy has been hindered by the scarcity of raw materials and preparation techniques.
In this paper, ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics were first successfully prepared through a combination of tape casting and chemical vapor infiltration (CVI) techniques, utilizing the self-developed ZrC HMs as the primary raw material. The morphology, microstructure, and thermal insulation properties of the obtained ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics were investigated. The results indicate that when the content of ZrC HMs is 30 vol.%, the density of the prepared porous ceramics is 2.09 g cm -3 , with a closed porosity of 14.05% and a thermal conductivity of 1.69 W (m K) -1 . The results clearly prove that the CVI process can successfully convert ZrC HMs into closed pore structures within porous ceramics. The introduction of ZrC HMs suppresses the contribution of free electrons to thermal conductivity and brings about a large number of solid-gas interfaces, which increases the interfacial thermal resistance and significantly reduces the phonon thermal conductivity. Consequently, the as-prepared ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics show excellent thermal insulation properties. This study provides a new idea and method for the development of porous UHTCs and offers a more reliable material choice for thermal protection systems.

answer: The question cannot be answered based on the provided sources, as there is no mention of Ambreen Nisar's work or spark plasma sintering (SPS) consolidation of (Ta,Nb,Hf,Ti)C multicomponent ultra-high temperature carbide powders in the given sources. 3.6. Chemical vapor infiltration (CVI)
Chemical Vapor Infiltration (CVI) is a technique for producing highperformance composites. CVI, involving gaseous precursor deposition under controlled conditions, results in composites with excellent mechanical  properties,  whereas  PIP  is  faster  but  requires  multiple  reinfiltration  and  re-pyrolysis  cycles  to  reduce  porosity  to  acceptable levels [95]. The CVI process, developed from chemical vapor deposition (CVD),  infiltrates  ceramic  material  into  porous  fiber  preforms  using reactive gas at higher temperatures like  900 -1500 ◦ C, forming high-purity,  dense  ceramic  matrices  with  excellent  mechanical  and anti-ablation properties. This method is widely applied to form C and SiC matrices, such as in C/C -SiC composites prepared by depositing SiC into carbon preforms with gaseous methyl trichlorosilane (CH3SiCl3) at moderate  temperatures  [96].  In  CVI,  a  porous  fiber  preform  in  a high-temperature  reactor  is  exposed  to  precursor  gas,  which  decomposes and deposits ceramic material, allowing control over infiltration duration, volume fractions, and interphase thickness [95]. Hu et  al.  designed  a  novel  sandwich-structured  C/C -SiC  composite  via two-step electromagnetic coupling CVI (E-CVI) within 20 h, featuring exterior C/SiC layers, gradient C/C -SiC layers, and a C/C core [97]. Furthermore, CVI is effective for densifying UHTC  composites, leveraging its success in ceramic-modified C/C composites [96]. CVI has S.M.A. Rishad et al.
Fig. 6. The effects of sintering additives on the densification and mechanical property (hardness) of (a) ZrB2, (b) TiB2 [90] (Permission granted with license number: 501965914), (c) SPS technique [87,91] (Open Access).
Fig. 7. Schematic of PIP process [93]. (Permission granted with license number: 5966591131597).

2.1. Chemical vapor infiltration/deposition (CVI/D) [1,28-30]
CVI/D is a ceramic engineering process whereby matrix material is infiltrated into carbon fibrous preforms by use of reactive gases at elevated temperatures (900-1200 °C) to form fiber-reinforced composites. Well-processed CVI-derived composites with a highpurity and well-controlled composition and microstructure matrix generally possess excellent mechanical and anti-ablation properties as a consequence of the slow, steady build-up of matrix material around the fiber network. CVI/D has been widely employed for preparation of C and SiC matrix and is also viable for fabricating UHTC composites. For example, HfC and ZrB2 can be prepared according to the following equations, respectively:
<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
The major drawbacks of CVI/D are the very slow rate of deposition, leading to a large material energy input and a high final cost, as well as the very limited infiltration depth, especially for the big Hf, Zr or Ta-containing radicals. So the CVI/D is mainly used to prepare the UHTC coating, such as TaC, HfC/TaC and HfC/ZrC coating; and only a few references about preparation of the bulk composites are reported [31-35] . Sayir used this method to fabricate C/HfC, C/TaC and C/HfC/TaC composites with a pyrolytic graphite interface by using TaCl5-CH4-H2-Ar, HfCl4-TaCl5-CH4-H2-Ar and HfCl4TaCl4-CH4-H2-Ar systems as gas sources [1] .

2.1. Composites preparation
The 2.5  D  needled  carbon  fiber  preforms  (400 -600 kg/m 3 T300PAN-based, Yixing Tianniao High Technology co., China) were densified to 1200 -1300 kg/m 3 by the chemical vapor infiltration (CVI) process,  with  the  pyrolysis  temperature  of  900 -1200 ℃ for  CH4.  The deposition time was 10 -60 h, and the deposition pressure was 2 -10 kPa. The microstructure of the low-density C/C composite is shown in Fig. S1. Post-densification, the porous C/C composites were machined into cylindrical specimens with dimensions of Φ 28 mm × 10 mm. C/C-MeC-SiC composites (Me: Hf, Zr, Ti, Ta, Nb, W) were generated by introducing MeSi2  (1 -5 μ m,  99.5  %  purity,  Jinzhou  Haixin  Metal  Material  Co., China) to react with the C/C composites. Distinct systems were labeled as Hf, Zr, Ti, Ta, Nb, and W systems. The Hf, Zr, Ti systems were prepared at 1800 ◦ C, while the Ta, Nb, W systems were required 2100 ℃ . The holding times were 2 -3 h. The prepared specimens were removed from the surface of the residual powder and ultrasonically cleaned and dried in an oven for 12 h. The preparation process of C/C-HfC-ZrC-TaCSiC composites is similar to the above process. The difference is that the raw materials were HfSi2, ZrSi2, TaSi2  powders according to the molar ratio of 4:4:1 in the planetary ball mill at the speed of 400 r/min for 8 h. The preparation temperature was 1800 -1900 ℃ , and the holding time was 2 -3 h.

Journal of Materials Science & Technology
The chemical vapor infiltration (CVI) method is a gas phase process to fabricate ceramic matrix. By using the CVI technique, the structure of the preform will not be damaged during a series of processes including the diffusion and reaction of gas precursor, and deposition of product, and the near-net-size preparation of ceramics can be achieved at a lower temperature. Therefore, it is expected to enable the preparation of closed-cell ceramics at a lower temperature without destroying the structure of ceramic HMs by adopting the CVI method. In this work, based on the ZrC HMs successfully synthesized in the previous study [3] , ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics were first prepared using a combination of tape casting and the CVI method. The microstructure and thermal insulation properties of the as-received closed-cell ceramics were characterized and analyzed. The results of this study present novel research ideas and techniques for thermal insulation UHTCs, thereby contributing to the advancement of TPS for hypersonic vehicles.

A B S T R A C T
ISTEC,  Italy,  and  Tecnalia,  Spain,  working  within  the  C 3 Harme programme [8 -10], it is not simple to process UHTCMCs, especially those that do not also contain SiC, using conventional sintering techniques such as hot pressing and spark plasma sintering since they require very high sintering temperatures ( > 2273 K) that can result in damage to the fibres  and/or  grain  growth  of  the  matrix  [7].  Since  we  aim  for  a maximum operating temperature of > 2000 ◦ C, the presence of SiC could lower significantly the ability to survive extreme conditions aimed for as SiC undergoes active oxidation and indeed can starts volatilizing above 1600 ◦ C. Hence the present work specifically focused on Cf-ZrB2 based composites without any SiC additions. Thus, an alternative approach was therefore to use non-sintering-based techniques such as chemical vapour  infiltration  (CVI)  [11 -13],  reactive  melt  infiltration  (RMI) [14,15] and precursor infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP) [16].
Chemical vapour infiltration is a leading technique for fabricating carbon / carbon (C/C) composites [17] and fibre-reinforced ceramic matrix composite materials, particularly silicon carbide / silicon carbide (SiC/SiC) composites [18,19]. The most common chemical reactions are the  dissociation  of  methane  and  methyl  trichlorosilane,  respectively, both typically at around 1273 K.

Therefore, it is not possible to determine which sintering or heating temperature is higher based on the given information.

A B S T R A C T
Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) is the predominant sintering technique for  achieving  dense  entropy-stabilized  UHTC.  The  first  high-entropy diboride  (HEB)  was  successfully  produced  using  SPS  at  2000 ◦ C  for 5 min, using transition metal diboride powders subjected to high-energy ball milling as starting materials [23]. Gild et al. [24] introduced a new approach named reactive flash Spark Plasma Sintering (reaFSPS), which combines rapid SPS and the flash sintering technique to quickly densify high-entropy UHTC. The utilization of reaFSPS enabled the achievement of  dense  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)B2 and  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)C ceramics,  which  contained  3  wt%  graphite  sintered  in  120  s.  While advanced sintering techniques can successfully yield dense high-entropy UHTC,  conventional  methods  often  involve  sintering  temperatures exceeding 1800 ◦ C,  leading  to  increased  energy  consumption and the development of a coarse microstructure [9].
Ultra-fast high-temperature sintering (UHS) was developed in 2020 by Wang et al. [25], where the sintering of a green body was achieved within seconds by placing a pellet between two Joule-heated carbon felts,  with  an  ultra-high  heating  rate  of  10 3 -10 4 ◦ C/min  achieved without the aid of external pressure. UHS has clear advantages over SPS, including a reduced processing time, ' light heating elements ' with low thermal capacity, simple experimental equipment, and the possibility of obtaining components with complex geometries [25 -27]. Due to the use of  thermally  insulating  graphite  heating  elements,  the  temperature within the felt can exceed 2500 ◦ C, thus enabling the densification of different refractory compounds  including SiC [28], Si3N4 [29], ZrB2-based  materials  [30,31],  WC  [32],  ZrC  [33] and  high-entropy carbides [34].

Spark Plasma Sintering
Spark plasma sintering (SPS) is a processing technique in which ceramic powders can be sintered very rapidly to full density. It is similar to HP and is carried out in  a  graphite  die/punch  assembly.  High-energy  plasma  can  be  generated  by  an instantaneous pulsed direct current applied through electrodes at the top and bottom of the die punch. Such plasma is expected to bring about phenomena that can enhance densification by particle surface activation, local melting and evaporation on the surface of the powder particle, so that heat transfer and mass transfer can be completed rapidly. In nearly all the investigations on SPS/field assisted sintering (FAS) indicated the higher rate of heating, lower sintering temperatures and shorter dwelling times compared to those attained in conventional sintering techniques (pressureless  sintering,  hot  pressing  etc).  Rapid  heating  rates  (hundreds  of  °C/ min) minimize coarsening and facilitate rapid densification, which leads to better thermo-mechanical properties. SPS has recently been applied to densify UHTCs based on ZrB 2 - and HfB 2 - with SiC/MoSi 2 /ZrC as secondary phases. Additions of 3wt% lanthanum yielded 99% RD (Kim et al., 2003). Mechanical alloyed Zr-B-C powder mixture resulted in 98% RD ZrB 2 -ZrC composite after exposure at 1800°C for 5 min (Tsuchida et al., 2004). Fast densification of HfB 2 and ZrB 2 based powder mixtures resulted in ~100% RD composites at 2100°C for 2 min (Bellosi et al . 2006) with a reduced grain size (compared to pressureless sintering) of ~1.4-2μm. Results of SPS studies on ZrB 2 - and HfB 2 - based ceramics are summarized in Table 4.

3. Results and discussions
The relative densities of ZrB2-30 vol% ZrO2f ceramics sintered by SPS, compared with literature reports of densi /uniFB01 cation achieved via conventional processing techniques [14], are shown in Table 1. The results revealed that the sintering temperature had remarkable in /uniFB02 uence on the relative density. The activation of the powder particle surface due to spark and possible plasma generation, along with superior Joules heating (for conducting materials) would result in enhanced mass transport and hence faster densi /uniFB01 cation kinetics during SPS [17,18]. With sintering temperature rising from 1600 1 C to 1750 1 C, the relative density of the bulk compact was increased. The maximum densi /uniFB01 cation of /C24 99.2% ρ th was obtained at 1750 1 C by SPS. However, the sintered density of ZrB2 -ZrO2f was lowered from /C24 99.2% ρ th to /C24 96.6% ρ th, as the sintering temperature increasing from 1750 1 C to 1800 1 C during SPS process. In our earlier research [15], /C24 98.1% ρ th can be obtained by hot pressing at 1950 1 C for 2 h for the same starting powder. However, much less sintered density of 78.7% ρ th was obtained by pressureless sintering at 1950 1 C for 10 h. Such observations clearly indicated the advantage offered by SPS, which was a comparatively novel sintering process that allowed the compaction of ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics to be sintered at lower temperatures, with shorter processing time.

4.4.1. Sintering techniques modification and advancements
Recently,  alongside  property  enhancements,  SPS  was  used  to enhance  the  sintering  process  of  double-phase  TiB2-SiC  UHTCMCs, reducing sintering temperatures to 1200 -1400 ◦ C and achieving optimal properties  at  1400 ◦ C,  including  high  hardness  (24.85  GPa),  tensile strength (309 GPa), fracture toughness (4.80 MPa · m 1/2 ), and low density [138]. However, UHTCMCs exhibit enhanced oxidation resistance due to the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure formed during plasma exposure; Nisar et al. synthesized Hf6Ta2O17 via solid-state reaction between HfO2 and Ta2O5 during SPS, with compositions of  50HO-50TO  and 70HO-30TO.  Rietveld  analysis  confirmed  Hf6Ta2O17  as  the  principal phase,  and  high-velocity  plasma  jet  tests  showed  50HO-50TO  withstanding intense plasma due to higher Hf6Ta2O17 content (~84 %), with its phase increasing to 94.7 % post-exposure, maintaining mechanical integrity with minimal hardness change (~13.3 GPa to 11.2 GPa) [139]. Qin et al. examined W-containing HE-carbide and Si reactions at high temperatures, preparing (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Ta0.2W0.2)CxSi ( x = 2.5, 5, 10 wt %) samples via spark plasma sintering at 1500 ◦ C and 1700 ◦ C. Adding 2.5 or 5 wt % Si forms SiC and stable (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Ta0.2W0.2)C1-x, while wt  %  Si  forms  transient  phases  decomposing  into  non-equimolar HE-carbide  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Ta0.2W0.2-y)C1-z and  WSi2.  Thus,  SPS

question: Which UHTC has a higher relative density: Ambreen Nisar's SPS-consolidated binary (Ta,Nb)C carbide, or Zhuojie Shao's densest porous high-entropy carbide sample, which has 86.4% porosity?
answer: The relative density of Zhuojie Shao's densest porous high-entropy carbide sample cannot be directly calculated from the provided information, as only the porosity (86.4%) is given. However, Ambreen Nisar's SPS-consolidated binary (Ta,Nb)C carbide is described as having a relative density higher than 95% [5], which is significantly higher than the relative density of Shao's sample, considering that a porosity of 86.4% implies a relative density of approximately 13.6% (assuming 100% density corresponds to 0% porosity). Therefore, Ambreen Nisar's SPS-consolidated binary (Ta,Nb)C carbide has a higher relative density.

chunks.text:
3.4. Compressive strength and thermal conductivity
This is because high entropy effect can efficiently reduce thermal conductivity of solid skeleton due to significant phonon scattered at the disordered cation sublattice.
Besides, Fig. 6 (b) plots the trends of thermal conductivity of porous samples with different porosities as a function of testing temperature. As can be seen, thermal conductivity of porous high entropy carbide slowly increases with elevated temperatures. Thermal conductivity of porous (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C with porosity of 86.4% is 0.239 W/(m /22c5 K) and 0.436 W/(m /22c5 K) at room temperature and 300 ° C, respectively. Moreover, the sample with higher porosity has lower thermal conductivity than those samples with lower porosity at both room and high temperatures. The values range from 0.436 W/(m /22c5 K) to 0.326 W/(m /22c5 K) when porosities increase from 86.4% to 95.9% at 300 ° C. Specially, thermal conductivity for porous ZrC [34] with porosity of 85% is shown in Fig. 6 (b) for comparison. From room temperature to 300 ° C, thermal conductivity of ZrC has a larger variation from 0.94 W/(m /22c5 K) to 1.35 W/(m /22c5 K), which is much higher than those of porous high entropy carbide with porosity of 86.4% (from 0.239 W/(m /22c5 K) to 0.436 W/(m /22c5 K)). High entropy effect effectively reduces thermal conductivity of UHTC sample. Based on the above comparison, it is clear that our sample has enhanced mechanical and thermal properties than those carbides in previous works, as well as superior properties compared with typical oxides. Hence, it can be concluded that the specimens in this work have better comprehensive performance.

a b s t r a c t
Due to advancements of hypersonic vehicles, ultra-high temperature thermal insulation materials are urgently requested to shield harsh environment with superhigh heat flux. Toward this target, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are the only choice due to their excellent capability at ultra-high temperatures. We herein report a novel highly porous high entropy (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C fabricated by foam-gelcasting-freeze drying technology combined with in-situ pressureless reaction sintering. The porous (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C exhibited ultra-high porosity of 86.4%-95.9%, as well as high strength and low thermal conductivity of 0.70-11.77 MPa and 0.164-0.239 W/(m /22c5 K), respectively. Specifically, SiC sintering additive only locates at the pit of the surface of sintering neck between UHTC grains, and there is no secondary phase or intergranular film at the grain boundary. Besides, the oxidation resistance of high entropy carbide powders is greatly improved compared with that of the mixed five carbide powders. This work clearly highlights the merits of highly porous high entropy (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C as an ultra-high temperature thermal insulation material.

4.2.1. Direct foaming method
Wu  et  al.  [119]  synthesized  porous  multi-component  UHTCs (Hf1/3Ta1/3Nb1/3)C with ultra-high porosity (90.0 -94.0%) by employing three carbide ceramic powders as raw materials and sodium dodecyl sulfate as a foaming agent. The microscopic morphologies of the obtained porous ceramics are shown in Fig. 15e, f. The prepared samples exhibited low density (0.70 -1.17 g/cm 3 ), medium compressive strength (0.34 -1.34 MPa), and low thermal conductivity (0.089 -0.098 W/(mK)). Similarly, Shao et al. [120] synthesized high-entropy porous ceramics (Zr1/5Hf1/5Nb1/5Ta1/5Ti1/5)C also utilizing sodium dodecyl sulfate as a foaming  agent.  The  porosity  of  the  material  ranged  from  86.4%  to 95.9%, and the thermal conductivity was 0.164 -0.239 W/(m ⋅ K).
The  ultra-high  temperature  porous  ceramic  material  prepared  by direct  foaming  method  has  high  closed  porosity,  few  pore  structure defects and a wide pore size distribution. However, the foaming process is difficult to control and has high process requirements.

3.4. Compressive strength and thermal conductivity
Compressive strength is one of the most important mechanical properties for porous ceramics. While thermal conductivity is a crucial index to measure thermal insulation materials. As is well-known, porosity has a great influence on strength and thermal conductivity. Fig. 6 displays the dependance of compressive strength and thermal conductivity on porosity for our samples. In Fig. 6 (a), it is seen that compressive strength of porous high entropy carbide decreases from 11.77 to 0.70 MPa with porosity varying from 86.4% to 95.9%. The pore morphology, pore size distribution, strut thickness and densification of cell wall all have notice- able effects on the strength of porous materials. As porosity increases, it is obviously presented in Fig. 2 that pore size distribution gets larger and the cell wall becomes thinner, which results in relatively weak ability to resist external load and yields lower strength. Vice versa, the porous sample with lower porosity has higher strength due to smaller pore size and thicker cell wall. It should be mentioned here that the strength of our samples is superior to those of other porous oxides and carbides with similar porosities [34-39] , which are plotted and compared in Fig. 6 (a). It is noted that the strength (11.77 MPa, porosity: 86.4%) in this work is higher than that (3.45 MPa, porosity: 80.99%) of porous (Zr 0.2 Hf 0.2 Ti 0.2 Nb 0.2 Ta 0.2 )C [12] . The basic reason is due to differences in microstructure. Concretely, owing to high sintering tem- perature and the use of SiC sintering additive, our sample has tighter sintering neck, denser skeleton and larger contact area of grain than those obtained from partial sintering method. Besides, when compared with the work of highly porous multicomponent (Hf 1/3 Ta 1/3 Nb 1/3 )C of our team [40] , the higher strength in this work is also due to differences in micromorphology.

4. Conclusions
Ta-based carbonitride materials belong to the UHTC family but they have been less studied than Hf-based or Zr-based carbides, nitrides or carbonitrides. In this study, we have shown that it is possible to sinter and obtain dense pellets of TaC, Ta2C1.5N0.5, Ta2CN, Ta2C0.5N1.5  and TaN by SPS. These UHTCs were sintered at 1873 K, which is at least 200 K lower than usually reported for this kind of materials and a pressure of 50 MPa. With these parameters, all the samples show a relative density higher than 95 %. Next to their main phase, the samples have less than 10 vol% of an oxide phase coming from the initial commercial powders and around 1 vol% of a W-rich phase, from the use of WC ball milling jar. A hexagonal ε -TaN phase is present in the Ta2C0.5N1.5. Beside the main hexagonal ε -TaN phase ( i.e. 59 vol%), the TaN sample contains a cubic δ -TaN phase ( i.e. 26 vol%).
UHTC materials usually have interesting mechanical properties. In the  present  case,  the  Young ' s  moduli  of  the  whole  series  ( approx. 600 -700  GPa)  as  determined  by  nano-indentation  were  even  higher than those reported for TaC or other UHTC ( i . e . 294-560 GPa). The nanoand micro-hardness values of the Ta-based carbonitride samples were in agreement  with  literature  and  confirmed  the  good  quality  and  high density  of  the  samples  obtained  with  the  sintering  process.  The  mechanical characterization performed on these samples could establish a hierarchical correlation with N content with higher N content giving rise to higher hardness. At the same time, the share of plastic deformation work  over  the  total  indentation  work  decreased  with  increasing  N content.

question: Ambreen Nisar's introduction states that formation of Hf6Ta2O17 oxide improves the oxidation resistance of multicomponent UHTCs (MC-UHTCs) by more than 40 times; by how many degrees Celsius does Zhuojie Shao's TG-DSC data show a 2 wt.% SiC additive raising the oxidation exothermic peak temperature of high-entropy carbide powders?
answer: Zhuojie Shao's TG-DSC data shows that a 2 wt.% SiC additive raises the oxidation exothermic peak temperature of high-entropy carbide powders by 181.4 °C [1].

chunks.text:
3.6. Oxidation behavior
The five exothermic peaks of mixed raw powders clearly correspond to sequential oxidations of the five carbide components. The single exothermic peak of high entropy carbide powders means that it really is the oxidation of a single-phase high entropy carbide. The initial oxidation temperatures and maximum exothermic peaks of high entropy carbide powders are higher than those of the mixed powders and further enhanced with more SiC addition. It shows that high entropy carbide powders have better oxidation resistance than mixed raw powders, which enhances about 119.2 ° C. The addition of SiC additive also improves oxidation resistance of high entropy carbide powders, and the increment reaches 181.4 ° C when the amount of SiC additive is 2 wt.%. TG-DSC results suggest that synergetic effects of high entropy modification and SiC additive effectively boost the oxidation resistance of UHTCs.

A B S T R A C T
Our  earlier  studies  [18,19]  revealed  that  the  gradual  addition  of UHTC  components  to  form  binary,  ternary,  and  quaternary  UHTCs improved their mechanical properties and oxidation resistance remarkably. The resistance to indentation damage proved up to 72 % at an applied load of 200 N, while the formation of protective complex mixed  oxides  with  Hf6Ta2O17 (as  the  primary  phase)  improves  the oxidation  resistance  by > 40  times  in  MC-UHTCs.  Hf6Ta2O17 has  a melting temperature > 2400 ◦ C, low thermal conductivity, and oxygen diffusion, and no phase transformation from RT up to the melting point. In  addition,  the  author  highlighted  the  crack  patching  behavior  in ternary and quaternary UHTCs. This finding was similar to the plasticity of the (HfTaZrNb)C grains during the micro-pillar compression along {110}, < 110 > slip systems [20]. Additionally, Ab initio molecular dynamics simulations validated by experimental fracture testing show that the ductility, plasticity, and fracture resistance of MC-UHTCs improves with increased valence electron concentration (VEC) [21,22].

3.6. Oxidation behavior
Thermogravimetric analysis combined with differential scanning calorimetry was employed to measure the oxidation behaviors qualitatively. Fig. 9 demonstrates TG-DSC curves of the mixed raw powders and high entropy carbide powders with 0 wt.%, 0.6 wt.% and 2 wt.% SiC in air to compare oxidation behaviors. There is obvious difference between DSC curves of mixed raw powders and high entropy carbide powders in Fig. 9 (a), i.e. five exothermic peaks in mixed raw powders and only one exothermic peak in high entropy carbide powders. DSC results show that the mixed raw powders and high entropy carbide powders with 0 wt.%, 0.6 wt.% and 2 wt.% SiC start to oxidize at about 274.5, 371.2, 408.7 and 523.5 ° C, respectively. The mixed raw powders reach their maximum exothermic peak at ∼ 467.5 ° C, while high entropy carbide powders with 0 wt.%, 0.6 wt.% and 2 wt.% SiC are at around 586.7, 597.6 and 768.1 ° C, respectively. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 9 (b), TG results show that all samples exhibit weight gains, and corresponding maximum weight gain points act the same rule as maximum exothermic peak of DSC results.

4. Conclusions
The complex mixed oxide phase of Hf6Ta2O17 has been synthesized by  solid-state  reaction  during  SPS  processing  of  ball-milled  powder mixtures of HfO2  and Ta2O5  in varying compositions. Phase quantification via Rietveld analysis revealed the content of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase was respectively ~83.7% and ~78.2% in 50HO-50TO and 70HO-30TO samples. Exposing the samples against high-temperature plasma further revealed that the sample with higher content of Hf6Ta2O17 phase (50HO50TO) could withstand the extreme re-entry conditions while the 70HO30TO sample underwent failure. The failure of the 70HO-30TO sample has also been attributed to the 0.64% strain in the Ta2O5 phase after SPS processing  which  further  increased  to  0.85%  post-exposure.  During exposure, the temperature exceeds > 2500 ◦ C during plasma exposure, A. Nisar et al.

Double-headed arrows  show the  oxide  scale  thickness  representing  melting  and  re-solidification  of  the  oxide  phase  during plasma exposure.
high enough for the invariant transformation of the liquid phase into t -Ta2O5 and o -Hf6Ta2O17. Due to this, the content of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase increased after plasma exposure to ~94.7% in 50HO-50TO and ~91.2% in 70HO-30TO. The study provides an insight into the contributions of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase toward protecting the oxidation of UHTCs. This can help  design  MC-UHTCs  with  improved  oxidation  resistance  under extreme service conditions for the TPS in hypersonic applications.

1. Introduction
Thermal protection systems of high-speed vehicles and spacecraft reentry require structural materials that are not only resistant to high temperatures,  but  also  have  long-term  mechanical  stability  [1,2]. Ultra-high-temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs),  especially  UHTC  carbides, are considered the most promising thermal protection materials due to their extremely high melting points, considerable hardness, and chemical stability [3 -6]. However, the high-melting-point oxides of ZrC and HfC (i.e., ZrO2 and HfO2) are porous and undergo phases transformation at the medium-high temperatures (1200 -1600 ◦ C), which severely restricts their practical applications [7 -9]. According to previous studies [6,10 -15], the main strategies to improve the oxidation resistance of the UHTCs at the medium-high temperatures are forming solid solutions with multi-elements or multicomponent ceramics. However, SiC/HfC composites  still  have  poor  oxidation  resistance  at  the  medium-high temperatures,  especially  at  lower  temperatures  such  as  1200 ◦ C  and 1300 ◦ C, because of the presence of severe cracking [16]. Although SiC has good oxidation resistance at 1600 ◦ C and below, the SiO2 formed at 1300 ◦ C and below is not enough to form a dense oxide scale with HfO2

question: In Kai Zhao's ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics, SiC is introduced via CVI using a specific MTS-to-H2 gas ratio; what weight percentage of SiC additive did Zhuojie Shao find optimal for densifying porous high-entropy (Zr1/5Hf1/5Nb1/5Ta1/5Ti1/5)C?
answer: The provided sources do not mention the specific weight percentage of SiC additive that Zhuojie Shao found optimal for densifying porous high-entropy (Zr1/5Hf1/5Nb1/5Ta1/5Ti1/5)C.

chunks.text:
3.2. Microscale
(Fig.  5(e)).  Similarly,  Shao et al. [221] fabricated highly porous high-entropy
(Zr1/5Hf1/5Nb1/5Ta1/5Ti1/5)C ceramics using foam-gelcasting and freeze-drying followed by in-situ pressureless sintering. These materials achieved ultra-high porosity (86.4 %-95.9 %) along with relatively high strength (0.70 -11.77 MPa) and low thermal conductivity (0.164 -0.239 W  m GLYPH<0> 1K GLYPH<0> 1 ) (Fig. 5(f)).

3.1. Formation and characterization of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics
Table 1 presents the density, porosity, and phase composition of the three ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics. It is evident that as the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increases, the density of ZrC-SiC closedcell ceramics gradually decreases, while both open porosity and closed porosity exhibit a gradual increase. Combined with the process of SiC preparation by CVI, it can be inferred that the increase of ZrC HMs volume fraction reduces the porosity of the preform and makes the pore structure of the preform more complex. Consequently, this affects the diffusion of reactive gasses during the CVI process, resulting in a gradual reduction in the deposited SiC amount. During the CVI process, the pores within the preform are filled with SiC. When the volume fraction of ZrC HMs is low, the porosity within the preform is high, and the pore size is large. This high porosity and large pore size facilitate the diffusion of the reaction gas, leading to a higher amount of SiC being generated within the preform and a lower porosity. However, as the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increases, the porosity of the preform decreases, and the corresponding pore size becomes smaller. The reduced pore size will hinder the diffusion of the reaction gas into the preform, resulting in a bottleneck effect that impedes the complete filling of internal pores with CVI SiC, ultimately leading to a higher porosity. Further analysis reveals that the closed porosity of porous ceramics is proportional to the volume fraction of ZrC HMs by 50%, which is close to the volume fraction of the cavity formed by individual ZrC HMs. This indicates that the cavity of ZrC HMs has successfully transformed into the closed porosity of ceramics through the CVI process.

4. Conclusions
A single-phase (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C with ultra-high porosity (95.9%) was successfully fabricated by foam-gelcastingfreeze drying and in-situ pressureless reaction sintering with SiC additive. To our knowledge, it ranks the highest porosity for porous ultra-high temperature ceramics reported in literatures. Porous high entropy carbide is the pure-phase with a rock-salt structure and the component is homogenous at multiple scales. Porous samples exhibit a microstructure of numerous, uniform interlinking pores and thin cell wall. The as-fabricated samples possess excellent overall performance, such as controllable ultra-high porosity (86.4-95.9%), high compressive strength (11.77-0.70 MPa) and low thermal conductivity (0.164-0.239 W/(m /22c5 K)). Due to the addition of SiC sintering additive, the sinterability of quinary ultra-high temperature carbide is obviously improved. Surprisingly, the added SiC only locates at the pit of the external surface of sintering neck between carbide grains. Most importantly, there is no secondary phase or intergranular film at the grain boundary of the thin skeleton. TG-DSC analyses indicate that high entropy carbide powders have improved oxidation resistances than mixed raw powders of five carbides and the addition of SiC additive obviously further enhances the oxidation resistances of porous high entropy UHTCs. Our work demonstrates an efficient approach to fabricate a single-phase ultra-high porosity (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C with high strength, low thermal conductivity and good oxidation resistance. The as-prepared porous (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C can be used in the key field of ultra-high temperature thermal insulation applications.

a b s t r a c t
Due to advancements of hypersonic vehicles, ultra-high temperature thermal insulation materials are urgently requested to shield harsh environment with superhigh heat flux. Toward this target, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are the only choice due to their excellent capability at ultra-high temperatures. We herein report a novel highly porous high entropy (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C fabricated by foam-gelcasting-freeze drying technology combined with in-situ pressureless reaction sintering. The porous (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C exhibited ultra-high porosity of 86.4%-95.9%, as well as high strength and low thermal conductivity of 0.70-11.77 MPa and 0.164-0.239 W/(m /22c5 K), respectively. Specifically, SiC sintering additive only locates at the pit of the surface of sintering neck between UHTC grains, and there is no secondary phase or intergranular film at the grain boundary. Besides, the oxidation resistance of high entropy carbide powders is greatly improved compared with that of the mixed five carbide powders. This work clearly highlights the merits of highly porous high entropy (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C as an ultra-high temperature thermal insulation material.

a b s t r a c t
Porous ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are recognized as novel candidates for fulfilling the requirements of thermal protection systems of hypersonic aircrafts, as they possess excellent hightemperature resistance and low thermal conductivity. Currently, the reported porous UHTCs predominantly exhibit an open pore structure. By contrast, closed-cell UHTCs, formed by employing ceramic hollow microspheres (HMs) as pore-forming agents, hold great potential for achieving superior thermal insulation performance. Unfortunately, the implementation of this strategy has been hindered by the scarcity of raw materials and preparation techniques.
In this paper, ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics were first successfully prepared through a combination of tape casting and chemical vapor infiltration (CVI) techniques, utilizing the self-developed ZrC HMs as the primary raw material. The morphology, microstructure, and thermal insulation properties of the obtained ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics were investigated. The results indicate that when the content of ZrC HMs is 30 vol.%, the density of the prepared porous ceramics is 2.09 g cm -3 , with a closed porosity of 14.05% and a thermal conductivity of 1.69 W (m K) -1 . The results clearly prove that the CVI process can successfully convert ZrC HMs into closed pore structures within porous ceramics. The introduction of ZrC HMs suppresses the contribution of free electrons to thermal conductivity and brings about a large number of solid-gas interfaces, which increases the interfacial thermal resistance and significantly reduces the phonon thermal conductivity. Consequently, the as-prepared ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics show excellent thermal insulation properties. This study provides a new idea and method for the development of porous UHTCs and offers a more reliable material choice for thermal protection systems.

question: Ambreen Nisar reports quaternary (Ta,Nb,Hf,Ti)C achieved a flexural strength of 726 MPa; how does this compare to the highest compressive strength Zhuojie Shao measured for porous high-entropy (Zr1/5Hf1/5Nb1/5Ta1/5Ti1/5)C?
answer: Ambreen Nisar reports that quaternary (Ta,Nb,Hf,Ti)C achieved a flexural strength of 726 MPa [4]. Zhuojie Shao measured the highest compressive strength of 11.77 MPa for porous high-entropy (Zr1/5Hf1/5Nb1/5Ta1/5Ti1/5)C [5]. The flexural strength of quaternary (Ta,Nb,Hf,Ti)C is significantly higher than the compressive strength of the porous high-entropy (Zr1/5Hf1/5Nb1/5Ta1/5Ti1/5)C.

3.2. Evaluating mechanical properties of MC-UHTCs
The typical  load-displacement  curves  of  binary,  ternary  and  quaternary UHTCs during the 4-point bend test are shown in Fig. 3. The higher the peak load, the higher the flexural strength of the material (see Table 1 and Fig. 3). The flexural strength ( σ f, calculated using Eq. (3)) increased from ~273 MPa for binary to ~585 MPa for ternary to ~726 MPa for quaternary UHTC. The highest flexural strength in quaternary UHTC is attributed to complete solid-solution in the sample [18] (also, see Fig. 1). The lattice distortion evident from the shift in the high intensity (111) peak in the XRD pattern [18] is directly proportional to the compressive strength during the 4-point bend test on the samples. The higher  the  number  of  the  UHTC  components,  the  higher  will  be  the lattice distortion/ strain. In the solid-solution synthesis of metallic alloys, this distortion leads to the development of localized stress fields, consequently  enhancing  the  stiffness  of  the  material  [4].  Hence,  the quaternary UHTC showed the highest flexural strength. This strengthening mechanism is engineered in the material at the unit cell level. Further, the energy dissipation for fracture is calculated from the area under the load-displacement curve, which increases from ~13 mJ for binary to ~57 mJ for ternary and ~ 86 mJ for quaternary UHTC. It represents the materials ' toughness and its ability to withstand bending forces before failure. The trend is similar to the indentation fracture toughness of these materials. Quaternary UHTC, owing to the highest toughness of 4.7 MPa m 0.5 , absorbs the highest energy, 86 mJ, eliciting the  resistance  to  deformation  and  fracture  at  a  higher  load

4. Conclusions
A single-phase (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C with ultra-high porosity (95.9%) was successfully fabricated by foam-gelcastingfreeze drying and in-situ pressureless reaction sintering with SiC additive. To our knowledge, it ranks the highest porosity for porous ultra-high temperature ceramics reported in literatures. Porous high entropy carbide is the pure-phase with a rock-salt structure and the component is homogenous at multiple scales. Porous samples exhibit a microstructure of numerous, uniform interlinking pores and thin cell wall. The as-fabricated samples possess excellent overall performance, such as controllable ultra-high porosity (86.4-95.9%), high compressive strength (11.77-0.70 MPa) and low thermal conductivity (0.164-0.239 W/(m /22c5 K)). Due to the addition of SiC sintering additive, the sinterability of quinary ultra-high temperature carbide is obviously improved. Surprisingly, the added SiC only locates at the pit of the external surface of sintering neck between carbide grains. Most importantly, there is no secondary phase or intergranular film at the grain boundary of the thin skeleton. TG-DSC analyses indicate that high entropy carbide powders have improved oxidation resistances than mixed raw powders of five carbides and the addition of SiC additive obviously further enhances the oxidation resistances of porous high entropy UHTCs. Our work demonstrates an efficient approach to fabricate a single-phase ultra-high porosity (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C with high strength, low thermal conductivity and good oxidation resistance. The as-prepared porous (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C can be used in the key field of ultra-high temperature thermal insulation applications.

(Zr1/5Hf1/5Nb1/5Ta1/5Ti1/5)C ceramics using foam-gelcasting and freeze-drying followed by in-situ pressureless sintering. These materials achieved ultra-high porosity (86.4 %-95.9 %) along with relatively high strength (0.70 -11.77 MPa) and low thermal conductivity (0.164 -0.239 W  m GLYPH<0> 1K GLYPH<0> 1 ) (Fig. 5(f)).

A B S T R A C T
Solid-solutioning in multicomponent ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTCs) has been shown to improve their thermo-mechanical properties unattainable by conventional UHTCs. Herein, MC-UHTCs are synthesized by varying the components from binary up to quaternary in (Ta,Nb,Hf,Ti)C system using spark plasma sintering (SPS). The present work identifies real-time quantitative failure events such as cracking, crack propagation and fracture using a high-speed camera during 4-point flexural testing in MC-UHTCs. Quaternary UHTCs showed the highest flexural strength of 726 MPa, representing an improvement of ~166 % over binary and ~ 24 % over ternary UHTCs. This has been attributed to processing-induced solid solutions and sub-micron feature defects, such as dislocations, intergrain twisting, and plasticity, revealed from the high-resolution microscopy. Crackpropagation  rate  significantly  depreciated  over  37  times  in  quaternary  UHTC.  An  improvement  in  crack shielding is observed in quaternary UHTC, showcasing the highest fracture toughness at 4.7 MPa ⋅ m 0.5 , surpassing binary and ternary UHTCs by ~270 % and ~ 166 %, respectively. The lower mechanical properties in binary UHTCs are also attributed to high porosity. Post-fracture microstructural analysis supports this finding due to the presence of river patterns contrived by crack-arrest at grain boundary or crack re-initiation in different orientations.  The  study  reveals  the  exceptional  damage  tolerance  of  quaternary  UHTCs  over  other  compositions, making them a potential structural material for hypersonic applications.

3.4. Compressive strength and thermal conductivity
Compressive strength is one of the most important mechanical properties for porous ceramics. While thermal conductivity is a crucial index to measure thermal insulation materials. As is well-known, porosity has a great influence on strength and thermal conductivity. Fig. 6 displays the dependance of compressive strength and thermal conductivity on porosity for our samples. In Fig. 6 (a), it is seen that compressive strength of porous high entropy carbide decreases from 11.77 to 0.70 MPa with porosity varying from 86.4% to 95.9%. The pore morphology, pore size distribution, strut thickness and densification of cell wall all have notice- able effects on the strength of porous materials. As porosity increases, it is obviously presented in Fig. 2 that pore size distribution gets larger and the cell wall becomes thinner, which results in relatively weak ability to resist external load and yields lower strength. Vice versa, the porous sample with lower porosity has higher strength due to smaller pore size and thicker cell wall. It should be mentioned here that the strength of our samples is superior to those of other porous oxides and carbides with similar porosities [34-39] , which are plotted and compared in Fig. 6 (a). It is noted that the strength (11.77 MPa, porosity: 86.4%) in this work is higher than that (3.45 MPa, porosity: 80.99%) of porous (Zr 0.2 Hf 0.2 Ti 0.2 Nb 0.2 Ta 0.2 )C [12] . The basic reason is due to differences in microstructure. Concretely, owing to high sintering tem- perature and the use of SiC sintering additive, our sample has tighter sintering neck, denser skeleton and larger contact area of grain than those obtained from partial sintering method. Besides, when compared with the work of highly porous multicomponent (Hf 1/3 Ta 1/3 Nb 1/3 )C of our team [40] , the higher strength in this work is also due to differences in micromorphology.

3.1. Nanoscale
In addition, the high-entropy alloys concept has been extended to UHTCs, yielding HE-UHTCs composed of five or more carbides, nitrides, or  borides  in  near-equimolar  ratios  [4,151,152].  Compared  to  traditional UHTCs, HE-UHTCs exhibit superior ablation resistance [153] and improved mechanical properties [154,155] through the formation of complex  solid  solutions  [156].  For  example,  Ti-Zr-Nb-Hf-Ta  high-entropy borides increase compressive strength from 400 ± 47 MPa at room temperature to 752 ± 23 MPa at 1800 ◦ C, attributed to suppressed crack nucleation and minimal residual stress [157]. Similarly, due to unique bonding and solid-solution strengthening, Hf-Ta-Zr-Nb-C HE-UHTCs  attain  36 GPa  Vickers  hardness,  which  is  about  30 % higher than monolithic carbides [158]. Hardness in HE-UHTCs further correlates with electronic valency, Hall-Petch strengthening, and overall composition [4,151], while chemical disorder can modify active slip systems to tune ductility [159]. Under compressive creep at 1600 ◦ C, (Hf-Ta-Zr-Nb)C HE-UHTCs exhibit creep rates an order of magnitude lower  than  monolithic  carbides,  with  deformation  dominated  by grain-boundary sliding and diffusion-assisted dislocation glide [160]. These  exceptional  properties  stem  from  the  complex  interplay  of atomic-scale factors such as lattice distortion, bond hybridization, and dislocation slip behavior, all of which are intensified by the inherent compositional complexity of high-entropy ceramics [161]. question: Zhuojie Shao's introduction cites an intrinsic thermal conductivity for ZrC; is this value higher or lower than the intrinsic ZrC thermal conductivity Kai Zhao's interfacial-thermal-resistance model assigns to κZ for the ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics?
answer: Zhuojie Shao's introduction cites an intrinsic thermal conductivity for ZrC of 13.2 W/(m·K) [9] and 6.45 W/(m·K) [41]. Kai Zhao's interfacial-thermal-resistance model assigns a value of 20.5 W/(m·K) to κZ for the ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics [1]. Therefore, the intrinsic ZrC thermal conductivity cited by Zhuojie Shao is lower than the value assigned by Kai Zhao's model.

3.1. Formation and characterization of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics
where κ ITR HMs is the equivalent thermal conductivity of ZrC HMs considering the interfacial thermal resistance. κ A , κ Z , and κ S are the intrinsic thermal conductivities of air, ZrC, and SiC, which take the values of 0.023, 20.5, and 15 W (m K) -1 , respectively. α = R k κ S / r is a dimensionless parameter indicating the strength of the interfacial thermal resistance. ν = ( 1 + t / r ) -3 is a dimensionless geometric parameter. t and r are the wall thickness and diameter of the ZrC HMs, which take values of 200 nm and 1 µ m, respectively.
Substituting the relevant data into Eq. (6) , the equivalent thermal conductivity of ZrC HMs was calculated to be 5.04 W (m K) -1 . Further, fit it with the thermal conductivity of porous SiC fitted by the binary model, and the result is shown in Fig. 8 (a). As the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increases, the overall trend of ZrC-SiC thermal conductivity gradually decreases. At lower volume fractions of ZrC HMs, the experimental test values are closer to the calculated values of Maxwell-Eucken model 1. However, as the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increases, the experimental test values progressively approach the calculated values of Maxwell-Eucken model 2. By analyzing and comparing the two models, it can be found that the calculation model of Maxwell Eucken Model 1 disperses low thermal conductivity phases in a high thermal conductivity matrix, while the calculation model of Maxwell Eucken Model 2 disperses high thermal conductivity phases in a low thermal conductivity matrix. This phenomenon is consistent with the change of thermal conductivity of SiC porous ceramics with the change of volume fraction of ZrC HMs in this experiment.

4. Conclusions
- (1) ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics were successfully prepared through tape casting combined with the CVI method. The ceramics exhibited a sandwich structure, with dense SiC coatings on the upper and lower surfaces, and a ZrC-SiC composite porous ceramic interior. The CVI process was effective in preserving the structural integrity of ZrC HMs and successfully converting them into closed pores within porous ceramics.
- (2) The addition of ZrC HMs introduces a large number of pores, which significantly increases the resistivity of the material and suppresses the contribution of free electrons to the thermal conductivity. Simultaneously, the introduction of ZrC HMs increases the number of solid-gas interfaces, leading to increased interfacial thermal resistance. This aggravates the effect of phonon scattering, resulting in a significant decrease in phonon thermal conductivity, ultimately leading to a reduction in the overall thermal conductivity of the material.
- (3) Considering the effect of interfacial thermal resistance on thermal conductivity, the equivalent thermal conductivity model was employed to characterize the thermal conductivity of ZrC HMs within the ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics. Subsequently, the effective thermal conductivity of ZrC HMs was calculated to be 5.04 W (m K) -1 . By combining this value with the corresponding volume fraction, a fitting model for the thermal conductivity of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics was developed. It was found that as the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increased, the fitting model gradually transited from Maxwell-Eucken model
1 to Maxwell-Eucken model 2, which is consistent with the present experimental results.

a b s t r a c t
However, considering the high intrinsic thermal conductivity of typical UHTC, e.g. 29.3 W/(m · K) for HfC, 33.5 W/(m · K) for TaC, and 33.5 W/(m · K) for ZrC [11] , it would be very di/uniFB03cult to obtain new types of UHTC insulators by merely enhancing the porosity. Therefore, in order to achieve lower thermal conductivity, further adoptions of multicomponent concept may be helpful to enhance the phonon scattering during the thermal conduction through solid skeletons [12-14] . For example, the reported porous high entropy UHTCs with the porosity of 80.99% has a low thermal conductivity of 0.39 W/(m · K), as well as high strength of 3.45 MPa. The thermal conductivity is obviously lower than that (0.94 W/(m · K)) of single-component porous ZrC with the porosity of 85% [8] . Predictably, under the situation of the superhigh porosity ( > 90%), a lower thermal conductivity and better heat-shielding performance would be achieved on the basis of the multicomponent solid solution.
