3.1. Formation and characterization of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics
To further analyze the influence of ZrC HMs on the thermal conductivity of porous ceramics, the resistivity of three kinds of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics was measured using the standard two-line method. Based on Eqs. (3) and (4) , the contribution of phonon heat conduction and electron heat conduction to thermal conductivity were calculated, and the results are presented in Table 2 . Overall, comparing the electron thermal conductivity with the phonon thermal conductivity, the contribution of free electron motion to thermal conductivity in porous ceramics is very low and can be ignored. Further analysis reveals that as the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increases, the resistivity of the material gradually increases, leading to a gradual decrease in the contribution of electrons to heat conduction. The resistivity of the three porous ceramics is in the same order of magnitude. Compared with the theoretical resistivity of ZrC (4.3 × 10 -7 /Omega1 m) [38] , the resistivity of porous ceramics increased by nearly 10 5 times. This significant increase can be attributed to the presence of numerous solid-gas interfaces within the porous ceramics, with the theoretical resistivity of air being 3 × 10 13 /Omega1 m. At the same time, SiC introduced via the CVI process is a typical semiconductor with a resistivity of 20 /Omega1 m [39] , which is considerably higher than the theoretical resistivity of ZrC. According to the equivalent circuit model, the material can be represented as a combination of capacitors, resistors, and inductors, which significantly increases the resistivity of the material and reduces the contribution of free electrons to thermal conduction. Therefore, the introduction of ZrC HMs results in the formation of numerous pores, which increases the resistance of electrons in the transport process, thereby suppressing the contribution of electrons to thermal conductivity.

3.4. Compressive strength and thermal conductivity
In general, thermal conductivity of high-porosity materials at room temperature is mostly determined by heat conduction through solid skeleton. As volume fraction of solid skeleton decreases with increased porosity, room-temperature thermal conductivity gets weaker. Actually, when compared to other porous oxide ceramics with similar porosity, the thermal conductivity of our samples is slightly higher than those of typical porous Si/mullite (0.178 W/(m /22c5 K) for 86.7%) [36] and (La 0.2 Nd 0.2 Sm 0.2 Gd 0.2 Yb 0.2 ) 2 Zr 2 O 7 (0.102 W/(m /22c5 K) for 88.0%) [37] , but lower than that of porous Al 2 O 3 (0.180 W/(m /22c5 K) for 95.9%) [39] . The main reason is that intrinsic thermal conductivity of high entropy UHTC (6.45 W/(m /22c5 K) [9] and 13.2 W/(m /22c5 K) [41] ) is slightly higher than those of the oxides, such as ∼ 6 W/(m /22c5 K) for mullite [42] and 2.5 W/(m /22c5 K) for La 2 Zr 2 O 7 [43] , except for 33 W/(m /22c5 K) for Al 2 O 3 [44] . Even though intrinsic thermal conductivity of carbide is very high, thermal insulation performance of ultra-high porosity high entropy carbide can still be comparable to that of conventional oxide insulation materials. Thermal conductivity of the present sample is lower than the same material system with porosity of 80.99% [12] , which is benefited from higher porosity obtained by foam-gelcasting-freeze drying method. More importantly, it is worth noting that thermal conductivity of high entropy carbide is distinctly smaller than that (0.94 W/(m /22c5 K) for 85.0%) of single-component ZrC [34] .

question: Ambreen Nisar reports approximately 14% porosity in her binary (Ta,Nb)C UHTC. Which of Kai Zhao's ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramic samples has an almost identical closed porosity value, and what is that sample's ZrC hollow-microsphere content?
answer: The ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramic sample S-30 has an almost identical closed porosity value of 14.05%, and its ZrC hollow-microsphere content is 30 vol.% [3].

chunks.text:
a b s t r a c t
Porous ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are recognized as novel candidates for fulfilling the requirements of thermal protection systems of hypersonic aircrafts, as they possess excellent hightemperature resistance and low thermal conductivity. Currently, the reported porous UHTCs predominantly exhibit an open pore structure. By contrast, closed-cell UHTCs, formed by employing ceramic hollow microspheres (HMs) as pore-forming agents, hold great potential for achieving superior thermal insulation performance. Unfortunately, the implementation of this strategy has been hindered by the scarcity of raw materials and preparation techniques.
In this paper, ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics were first successfully prepared through a combination of tape casting and chemical vapor infiltration (CVI) techniques, utilizing the self-developed ZrC HMs as the primary raw material. The morphology, microstructure, and thermal insulation properties of the obtained ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics were investigated. The results indicate that when the content of ZrC HMs is 30 vol.%, the density of the prepared porous ceramics is 2.09 g cm -3 , with a closed porosity of 14.05% and a thermal conductivity of 1.69 W (m K) -1 . The results clearly prove that the CVI process can successfully convert ZrC HMs into closed pore structures within porous ceramics. The introduction of ZrC HMs suppresses the contribution of free electrons to thermal conductivity and brings about a large number of solid-gas interfaces, which increases the interfacial thermal resistance and significantly reduces the phonon thermal conductivity. Consequently, the as-prepared ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics show excellent thermal insulation properties. This study provides a new idea and method for the development of porous UHTCs and offers a more reliable material choice for thermal protection systems.
© 2024 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.

4.3. Thermal insulation of porous UHTCs
When  focusing  on  a  specific  material  system  combined  with  a particular level of thermal conductivity, it becomes apparent that the porosity  of  porous  UHTCs  prepared  through  different  processes  can significantly  differ  between  the  two  materials  with  similar  thermal conductivity  levels  and  same  material  systems.  This  suggests  that different pore structures caused by different preparation processes can greatly affect the thermal insulation properties of the materials.
For instance, a comparison between literature [127] and literature [98] on ZrC porous ceramics shows that the corresponding porosity of the materials is 42.41% and 55.4%, respectively, at a thermal conductivity of about 2.7 W/(m ‧ K). The difference in pore structure between the two materials primarily lies in the fact that the ZrC porous ceramics prepared in literature [127] are closed cell ceramics made by using ZrC hollow  microspheres  as  a  pore-making  agent,  with  a  pore  size  of approximately 1 µ m. In contrast, the ZrC porous ceramics prepared in literature [98] use an in-situ reaction method that relies on the release of CO from the carbothermal reduction reaction to form connected pores with a pore size of about 4 µ m.
Based on the solid-phase heat conduction mechanism of UHTCs, it is known that the closed cell structure reduces the contribution of gas heat conduction to the thermal conductivity in the material. Meanwhile, the smaller pore size not only further suppresses the gas-phase heat conduction in the material but also increases the number of solid-gas interfaces,  causing  a  considerable  amount  of  phonon  scattering  that further  reduces  the  thermal  conductivity  of  the  material.  Therefore, compared to  porous  ceramics  with  open  pores  and  large  pore  sizes, closed  cell  ceramics  with  small  pore  sizes  exhibit  superior  thermal insulation effects.

3.1. Formation and characterization of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics

The pore size distribution of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics, with varying volume fractions of ZrC HMs, was measured using the mercury intrusion method, as depicted in Fig. 6 (a). The pore sizes of all three ceramics are less than 2 µ m. As the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increases, the peak corresponding to the pore size gradually shifts towards a smaller size, from 1.05 µ m corresponding to S-10 to 0.67 µ m corresponding to S-30. This indicates that the majority of pores within the porous ceramics are submicron in size, effectively hindering gas heat conduction within the material [29] .

3.1. Formation and characterization of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics
Table 1 presents the density, porosity, and phase composition of the three ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics. It is evident that as the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increases, the density of ZrC-SiC closedcell ceramics gradually decreases, while both open porosity and closed porosity exhibit a gradual increase. Combined with the process of SiC preparation by CVI, it can be inferred that the increase of ZrC HMs volume fraction reduces the porosity of the preform and makes the pore structure of the preform more complex. Consequently, this affects the diffusion of reactive gasses during the CVI process, resulting in a gradual reduction in the deposited SiC amount. During the CVI process, the pores within the preform are filled with SiC. When the volume fraction of ZrC HMs is low, the porosity within the preform is high, and the pore size is large. This high porosity and large pore size facilitate the diffusion of the reaction gas, leading to a higher amount of SiC being generated within the preform and a lower porosity. However, as the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increases, the porosity of the preform decreases, and the corresponding pore size becomes smaller. The reduced pore size will hinder the diffusion of the reaction gas into the preform, resulting in a bottleneck effect that impedes the complete filling of internal pores with CVI SiC, ultimately leading to a higher porosity. Further analysis reveals that the closed porosity of porous ceramics is proportional to the volume fraction of ZrC HMs by 50%, which is close to the volume fraction of the cavity formed by individual ZrC HMs. This indicates that the cavity of ZrC HMs has successfully transformed into the closed porosity of ceramics through the CVI process.

4.2.2. Hollow microspheres method
The hollow microsphere method is a technique that involves adding a pore-forming agent to prepare porous ceramics, using ceramic hollow microspheres as the agent. Ceramic hollow microspheres and ceramic powders are used simultaneously as raw materials in this method, and porous ceramics can be formed through sintering, gelcasting, and other methods. Closed pores can be created within the ceramics as long as the hollow microspheres remain structurally intact during the preparation of  the  porous  ceramics.  The  closed  porosity  of  the  material  can  be regulated  by  controlling  the  volume  fraction  of  the  ceramic  hollow microspheres, while the pore structure of the ceramic material can be controlled by adjusting the diameter and wall thickness of the hollow microspheres. This method has been widely applied in oxide and nitride ceramics [121 -123]. However, due to the current challenges in producing UHTCs hollow microspheres, this method is currently not widely utilized.  The  primary  factor  impeding  the  preparation  of  closed  cell UHTCs using the hollow microsphere method is the limited preparation methods  available  for  UHTCs  hollow  microspheres,  with  only  four reports available at present.
Fig. 15. SEM micrographs of as-sintered porous ceramics ： (a, b) ZrB2  porous ceramics [116]; (c, d) ZrC porous ceramics [117]; (e, f) porous (Hf1/3Ta1/3Nb1/3)C ceramic [119].

4.2.2. Hollow microspheres method
Zhao  et  al.  [127]  prepared  ZrC  ceramic  hollow  microspheres  by template  method  combined with molten salt method. They used the homemade C hollow microspheres as template and carbon source, and ZrH2 as zirconium source to obtain ZrC hollow microspheres with good morphology in molten salt environment at 900 ◦ C. The growth mechanism of the microspheres depends on the low solubility of carbon in the molten salt to maintain its spherical morphology, and the diffusion rate of Zr atoms in the molten salt is very fast, which can quickly diffuse to the surface of the carbon microspheres and react with carbon to form zirconium carbide hollow microspheres. At the same time, this method was extended to other carbide ceramics, and HfC and TiC hollow microspheres were obtained, which proved the universality of this method. Then, ZrC hollow microspheres were mixed with ZrC powder and ZrC porous ceramics were obtained by partial sintering. The results showed that the addition of ZrC hollow microspheres could inhibit the shrinkage of ceramics during the sintering process. At the same time, most of the pores in the ceramics were spherical pores formed by hollow microspheres. The thermal conductivity of porous ceramics decreased with the increase of the volume fraction of hollow microspheres, showing good thermal insulation performance, and the lowest thermal conductivity was 1.82 W/(m ‧ K) with the porosity of 53.49%. The preparation process and microstructure of the products are shown in the Fig. 16f.

question: Kai Zhao's mercury-intrusion analysis shows ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramic pore sizes shifting toward about 1.05 μm for sample S-10; how do these submicron pore sizes compare to the large-pore size range Zhuojie Shao reports for sample HEC-10 of the porous high-entropy carbide?
answer: Kai Zhao's mercury-intrusion analysis shows that the pore sizes in ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics are submicron, with the peak pore size for sample S-10 being about 1.05 μm [1]. In contrast, Zhuojie Shao reports that the large-pore size range for sample HEC-10 of the porous high-entropy carbide is about 65-296 μm [5]. Thus, the submicron pore sizes in ZrC-SiC ceramics are significantly smaller than the large-pore size range in the porous high-entropy carbide.

The pore size distribution of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics, with varying volume fractions of ZrC HMs, was measured using the mercury intrusion method, as depicted in Fig. 6 (a). The pore sizes of all three ceramics are less than 2 µ m. As the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increases, the peak corresponding to the pore size gradually shifts towards a smaller size, from 1.05 µ m corresponding to S-10 to 0.67 µ m corresponding to S-30. This indicates that the majority of pores within the porous ceramics are submicron in size, effectively hindering gas heat conduction within the material [29] .

3. Results and discussion
After RMI processes, the density and open porosity of the composite material were measured by the Archimedes drainage method, which are 2.71 ± 0.73 g/cm 3 and 7.07 ± 1.29 %, respectively. To characterize the changes in pore structure before and after siliconization, samples sized 5 mm × 5 mm × 5 mm were extracted from the C/Ta4HfC5-C intermediate and the final C/Ta4HfC5-SiC composite material for mercury intrusion porosimetry (MIP). The experimental results are shown in Fig. 4. The porosities of the intermediate and siliconized samples were 38.23 % and 6.51 % respectively, indicating a significant reduction in porosity after siliconization. Fig. 4a and b reveal a bimodal pore size distribution in the C/Ta4HfC5-C intermediate, with nanometer and submicron pores below 1.6 μ m and larger pores ranging from 100 to 500 μ m. These larger pores provide pathways for molten Si during RMI process, though excessively large pores may hinder the Si-C reaction, resulting in residual Si in the matrix  [29].  Fig.  4c  depicts  the  cumulative  intrusion  curve  for C/Ta4HfC5-SiC composite, showing a median pore size of 1.6 μ m with a multi-modal distribution. Fig. 4d illustrates another cumulative intrusion curve for C/Ta4HfC5-SiC composite, indicating pore sizes mainly consisting of nanometer pores below 40 nm, micrometer pores ranging from 0.04 to 8.5 μ m, and a small number of larger pores from 10 to 285 μ m. Post-siliconization, significant reduction in larger pores is observed, while  the  number  of  micrometer  and  nanometer  pores  shows  slight decrease.

3.1. Formation and characterization of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics
Table 1 presents the density, porosity, and phase composition of the three ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics. It is evident that as the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increases, the density of ZrC-SiC closedcell ceramics gradually decreases, while both open porosity and closed porosity exhibit a gradual increase. Combined with the process of SiC preparation by CVI, it can be inferred that the increase of ZrC HMs volume fraction reduces the porosity of the preform and makes the pore structure of the preform more complex. Consequently, this affects the diffusion of reactive gasses during the CVI process, resulting in a gradual reduction in the deposited SiC amount. During the CVI process, the pores within the preform are filled with SiC. When the volume fraction of ZrC HMs is low, the porosity within the preform is high, and the pore size is large. This high porosity and large pore size facilitate the diffusion of the reaction gas, leading to a higher amount of SiC being generated within the preform and a lower porosity. However, as the volume fraction of ZrC HMs increases, the porosity of the preform decreases, and the corresponding pore size becomes smaller. The reduced pore size will hinder the diffusion of the reaction gas into the preform, resulting in a bottleneck effect that impedes the complete filling of internal pores with CVI SiC, ultimately leading to a higher porosity. Further analysis reveals that the closed porosity of porous ceramics is proportional to the volume fraction of ZrC HMs by 50%, which is close to the volume fraction of the cavity formed by individual ZrC HMs. This indicates that the cavity of ZrC HMs has successfully transformed into the closed porosity of ceramics through the CVI process.

3.1. Formation and characterization of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics
The schematic diagram of the equivalent model for the ZrCSiC porous ceramic structural unit is shown in Fig. 7 . The inter- tion caused by high-entropy solid solution. Compared with these research results, the porosity of the ZrC-SiC closed pore ceramic in this work is at a lower level, but it also achieves a good insulation effect. This can be attributed to its unique pore structure, which results in many heterogeneous interfaces, forming a large amount of interfacial thermal resistance. As a result, the material achieves satisfactory insulation performance even at lower porosity levels.

3.2. Microstructure of porous high entropy carbide
Fig. 2 presents micrographs of HEC-10, HEC-15 and HEC-20. It reveals that all samples exhibit a uniform isotropic multiple pore structure, including large pores, windows and small pores in the skeleton. As displayed in Fig. 2 (a), (d) and (g), size distributions of large pores for HEC-10, HEC-15 and HEC-20 are about 65-296 µm, 67-267 µm and 60-242 µm, respectively. Thereinto, some of larger pores might be related to the merging of two or more pores. Size distributions of the windows for HEC-10, HEC-15 and HEC-20 are about 19-155 µm, 13-130 µm and 12-110 µm, respectively. Small pores in the cell wall are observed in Fig. 2 (b), (e) and (h), and their size distributions are around 0.2-4.7 µm, 0.2-4.2 µm and 0.23.5 µm, respectively. As can be seen, there are only very few small pores in the cell wall which is nearly dense skeleton, which contributes to good mechanical properties. Fig. 2 (c), (f) and (i) display the well-sintering of neighboring particles and grain growth of high entropy UTHC grains. The particle sizes are 0.35-4.91 µm, 0.22-3.63 µm and 0.11-2.40 µm, and the mean values are about 2.17 µm, 1.31 µm and 0.85 µm for HEC-10, HEC-15 and HEC-20, respectively.

3.1. Formation and characterization of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics
Fig. 5 (b) shows the ZrC particles embedded in the wall of ZrC HMs, characterized by grain sizes below 50 nm. The highresolution image in Fig. 5 (d) demonstrates a crystal plane spacing of 0.234 nm, corresponding to the (200) crystal plane of ZrC. Such nano-scale particles can significantly increase the number of grain boundaries and play a scattering role for phonon transport during solid-phase heat conduction, which is conducive to improving the thermal insulation properties of the material. Moreover, Fig. 5 (c) shows the bright field image of SiC outside the microsphere, revealing long rod-shaped crystals with a diameter of approximately 50 nm. The growth direction of these crystals is perpendicular to ZrC HMs, with numerous parallel stripes present within each columnar crystal, oriented perpendicular to the growth direction. This is due to the high-density stacking faults generated in the CVI process. Its high-resolution image ( Fig. 5 (e)) shows that the crystal plane spacing is 0.251 nm, corresponding to the (111) crystal plane of 3C-SiC ( β -SiC).
3.2. Pore structure and thermal insulation property
In order to investigate the effect of ZrC HMs content on the thermal insulation performance of ZrC-SiC closed-cell ceramics, the preforms with ZrC HMs volume fraction of 10 vol.%, 20 vol.%, and 30 vol.% were prepared by tape casting process by adjusting the volume fraction of ZrC HMs in the slurry. Subsequently, the CVI process was employed to produce closed-cell ZrC-SiC ceramics, named S-10, S-20, and S-30, respectively.

3. Results and discussion
3.1. Analysis of precursor pyrolysis products and densification process of composite materials
Fig.  2 illustrates  the  pyrolysis  powder  products  of  the  self-made Ta4HfC5  precursor and the corresponding XRD scan results. As shown in the figure, the powder exhibits as uniformly sized particles and has distinct metallic luster after 1800 ◦ C high-temperature treatment. The XRD  pattern  reveals  sharp  diffraction  peaks  at  2 θ values  of  34.62 ◦ , 40.19 ◦ , 58.13 ◦ , 69.45 ◦ , 73.02 ◦ and 86.76 ◦ , which closely matches the diffraction  angles  reported  in  literature  [20,21,27]  and  confirms  the formation of a single Ta4HfC5 solid solution after high-temperature heat treatment of the self-made precursor.
Even after CVD PyC interface modification, the hardness of the 3D needled structure will still be inadequate. Thusly, to avoid the carbon fiber loss during cutting, relatively large bulk preforms (150 mm × 120 mm × 30 mm) were involved in the initial preparation process, which was cut into samples with dimensions of 60 mm × 35 mm × 30 mm for subsequent  preparation  processes  after  5  cycles  impregnation  of  the Ta4HfC5 precursor, when the intermediate has a certain hardness. 3.2. Microstructure of porous high entropy carbide
X-ray tomography measurement was employed to reconstruct three-dimensional morphology of HEC-20. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), a large field of view with a low resolution of 3.1 µm (small pores in the wall are indistinguishable in the figure) is used to characterize large pores and cell windows. As can be seen, it presents uniform interlinking pores in the sample, and it clearly shows that the sizes of large pores are generally below 250 µm. Besides, in order to analyze small pores in the cell wall, a microscopic field of view with a resolution of 64 nm is shown in Fig. 3 (b). There are a few small pores (0.3-3.1 µm) in the skeleton, and it indicates a relatively dense skeleton. XRT results accord closely with the results provided by SEM image analysis. In the light of reconstructed spatial distribution, the volume fraction of large pores and small pores are computed, which are 86.5% and 7.9%, respectively. Total volume fraction of pores is 87.6% and it is close to the value of 86.4% that was calculated by Eq. (1) .

question: Huang et al. 2016 deposit a ZrB2-SiC coating on PIP-C/SiC composites by slurry and CVD method, while Borrelli et al. 2009 apply a ZrB2-SiC coating to the outer C/SiC dome of the Nose_2 reentry-vehicle nose cap by plasma spray deposition. Which coating is thicker, and roughly by what factor?
answer: Huang et al. 2016 deposit a ZrB2-SiC coating with a thickness of 50 µm [4], while Borrelli et al. 2009 apply a ZrB2-SiC coating that is about 500 µm thick [1]. The coating applied by plasma spray deposition is thicker by a factor of 10.

chunks.text:
2. Nose 2 structural concept
The basic idea of the Nose_2 design is to couple conventional C/SiC materials to novel ultra-high temperature ZrB2-SiC ceramics in order to create a multi-material structure able to withstand the severe condition associated with slender-shaped hot structures and non-conventional reentry mission profiles. The main components of the Nose_2 demonstrator are the tip and the dome. The latter, its turn, is made of two subcomponents: the outer dome and the inner dome (Fig. 1).
The conical tip, which is intended to sustain the greatest thermal load in the whole nose cap structure, is made of ultra-high temperature ZrB2-SiC ceramic. The tip was produced by hot pressing sintering and then finished by EDM (electrical discharge machining) [10]. Unfortunately, just during the EDM of the conical tip base, an extra current (due probably to a 'not suitably flushed material') caused the detachment of an excessive amount of material, as a consequence the tip presented a small chipped part (Fig. 2). The width, the height and the depth of the defect, after a re-machining aimed at smoothing the damage were, respectively, 7, 5, 1mm. Safety numerical analyses proved that the presence of the defect does not change significantly temperature and stress distributions on the massive cone and the structural integrity of the Nose_2 was assured [11], so that the tip was considered suitable for the PWT test.
The outer dome is made of C/SiC and has the shape of a hollow frustum of cone. It was manufactured by polymer infiltration and pyrolis (PIP) process. Components obtained by PIP are usually characterized by high residual porosity so that a thin protective coating is needed to protect the fibres from oxidation. In this case, a ZrB2-SiC coating, about 500 /afii9839 m thick, was applied by plasma spray deposition technique on the external surface of the outer dome.

3.6.  Other methods
In  addition  to  spray  coating,  in-situ  synthesis  and  electric  spark plasma sintering, additional techniques including chemical vapor deposition (CVD), sol-gel and magnetron sputtering have been employed for coating preparation [84,85]. Li et al. [86] developed dense ZrB2/ SiO2 coatings reinforced with ZrB2 particles  on  SiC-coated  C/C
Fig. 14. Schematic diagram of the SPS coating preparation method. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [82], © Elsevier 2019.
Fig. 15. Schematic diagram of the CVD process. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [87], © Elsevier 2022.
composites using sol-gel dipping. The coating provided effective oxidation protection at 1773 K for 160 h, with a weight loss of 6.9 mg/cm 2 . Ouyang et al. [12] prepared ZrB2-SiO2 coatings on SiC-coated carbon/ carbon  composites  by  infiltrating  silicon  sol  into  porous  ZrB2 layers through the sol-gel method. After 330 h of oxidation at 1500 °C, the ZrB2-SiO2  composite coating exhibited a weight loss of 158 g/m 2 . Gai et al. [87] performed thermodynamic calculations and verification experiments  on  HfB2 coatings  prepared  by  chemical  vapor  deposition (CVD)  (as  shown  in  Fig.  15).  The  dense  and  uniform  HfB2 coating prepared at 1150 °C demonstrated effective ablation protection for C/C composites during 30-second oxygen-acetylene combustion, with mass and linear ablation rates of 15.61 mg/s and 15.58 µm/s, respectively. Kiryukhantsev-Korneev et al. [88] deposited Zr-Si-B-N coatings through magnetron sputtering at varying nitrogen partial pressures. Appropriate doped nitrogen reduced the grain size of the coatings and induced an amorphous structure, resulting in a dense and defect-free coating with enhanced properties.

16.2  TPS: TEST ARTICLES AND PROTOTYPES
Nose_1 and Nose_2 consisted of a monolithic ZrB 2 -based UHTC conical nose tip with height of 100 mm attached to a truncated cone base (height 404 mm and diameter 304 mm) with a graphite core and an external layer of C/SiC (Fig. 16.2). The same UHTC coating (ZrB 2 and SiC with 75 and 25 wt%) was deposited by plasma spray on the truncated cone bases of both prototypes in order to improve their oxidation resistance. In the case of Nose_1, coating adhesion to the C/SiC substrate was not uniform due to the relatively large coating thickness of about 1 mm. These adhesion problems were not observed on the C/SiC base of Nose_2 where the coating thickness was reduced to about 300-500 µm.
The chemical composition of the monolithic nose tips is the main difference between Nose_1 and Nose_2: ZrB 2 +  15  vol% SiC  +  2  vol% MoSi 2 (ZS) in the former and ZrB 2 +  10  vol% HfB 2 +  15  vol% SiC  +  2  vol% MoSi 2 (ZHS) in the latter.
SiC addition was found to improve the processing by lowering sintering temperatures of diborides-based UHTCs [19-21] and improving the oxidation resistance. In fact the oxidation of pure ZrB 2 above 1200°C produces B 2 O 3 which, being characterized  by  intensive  volatility,  is  not  able  to  form  a  stable  and  protective  oxide  scale
Figure 16.2.  configuration of test articles. nose_1: breaking of nose tip during the plasma test.
[22-28]. The addition of SiC enhances resistance to the oxidation of diborides-based monolithic  ceramics  up  to  1600°C  through  the  formation  of  borosilicate  glass [18, 24-26, 29-43].

4. Conclusions
UHTCs based coatings (ZrB2 e SiC coating and ZrC e SiC coating) for PIP-C/SiC composites are deposited by slurry and CVD method, then the thermal shock behavior of the coated samples is studied, and the effect of thermal cycling on their /uniFB02 exural property and microstructures is discussed. The following conclusions have been reached:
- (1) ZrB2 e SiC (ZrC e SiC) coating is fabricated by slurry and CVD method with a thickness of 50 m m.
- (2) The coated samples showed the degradation of the /uniFB02 exural strength properties and no mass variation. Compared with the as-received C/SiC composites, the mode of the fracture behavior of coated C/SiC composites was changed, the samples exhibited an obvious brittle fracture behavior, and the coatings are able to improve the mechanical properties of the composites.
- (3) ZrB2 e SiC coated C/SiC composites are no longer a singlephase of ZrB2, after thermal cycling tests, SiC and C are detected. The surface of ZrC e SiC coated C/SiC composites is no longer a single-phase of ZrC, SiC and C are also detected either.
- (4) The damage of the UHTCs based coatings caused by tensile stress could in /uniFB02 uence the mechanical properties of PIP-C/SiC composites under thermal cycling tests, and the /uniFB02 exural property of C/SiC composites is reduced.

3.3.  Spraying method
Shi  et  al.  [68] prepared  a  La-Mo-Si-O-C  coating  on  porous  SiCcoated C/C composites using supersonic atmospheric plasma spraying. The coating  exhibited  a  dense,  crack-free  microstructure  with  glassy La2O3, La2SiO5, and SiO2  phases after oxidation at 1773 K for 85 h in air,  effectively blocking oxygen diffusion. Hao et al. [69] applied atmospheric plasma spraying to prepare ZrB2-SiC-Al2O3 (ZSA) and ZrB2SiC-Si (ZSS) coatings on C/C composites. The addition of alumina reduced  the  viscosity  of  the  glass  phase  and  promoted  the  healing  of surface cracks and voids in the ceramic coating at high temperatures. Wang  et  al.  [70] fabricated  a  plasma-sprayed  ZrO2-modified  LaB6MoSi2  coating on SiC-coated C/C composites. The coating exhibited a crack-free structure with strong interlayer adhesion. After oxidation at 1773 K for 140 h, the mass loss was only 0.96 %, and after 30 thermal cycles between 1773 K and room temperature, it was 0.61 %. The excellent oxidation and spallation resistance were attributed to the formation of a continuous Zr-La-Si-O glass layer and inlaid phases that suppressed oxygen diffusion and crack propagation. Ma et al. [71] developed  Y2O3-modified  ZrB2-SiC  coating  on  C/C  composites  through atmospheric plasma spraying (as shown in Fig. 12). The ZSY10 coating exhibited a mass loss rate of 5.77 % after 10 h of oxidation at 1450 ℃ . Plasma spraying technology is also suitable for fabricating multilayer coatings.  For  instance,  Ariharan  et  al.  [72] deposited  a  double-layer coating  composed  of SiC-ZrB2/Al2O3-carbon  nanotube  (CNT)

question: Yang et al. 2025 sinter porous YB2C2 ceramics at the temperature required to obtain a phase-pure product, while Huang et al. 2016 deposit a CVD-SiC coating on PIP-C/SiC composites at a fixed reduced-pressure temperature. Which process uses the higher processing temperature?
answer: Yang et al. 2025 sinter porous YB2C2 ceramics at 1950 ◦ C [4]. The processing temperature for the PIP-C/SiC composites with a CVD-SiC coating by Huang et al. 2016 is not specified in the provided sources. Therefore, based on the information given, the process that uses the higher processing temperature is the sintering of YB2C2 ceramics by Yang et al. 2025.

4.5. Polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP)
transfer [161]. Yan et al. created a water-soluble HfC precursor used with SiC blends to infiltrate 3D and 4D Cf performs over 20 PIP cycles, resulting  in  higher  density  and  performance  composites  with  higher SiC/HfC ratios [162]. Xie et al. synthesized an air-stable ZrB2 precursor with a 65.5 wt % ceramic yield at 1200 ◦ C, but it needed to be dissolved in solvent for PIP and resulted in a matrix that was not phase-pure [163]. Studies  on  preceramic  polymer  blends  [164,165]  often  require  long processing cycles, resulting in porous composites that deteriorate ablation properties [166]. Recent advancements in ultra-high-temperature ceramic  matrix  composites  (UHTCMCs)  have  focused  on  enhancing ablation resistance, mechanical  properties, and  thermal  stability. Modified C/C -ceramic composites fabricated via precursor infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP) demonstrated improved ablation resistance due to optimized phase compositions, defect healing mechanisms, and stable oxide layer formation under extreme thermal environments [167 -169]. High-entropy ceramic matrix composites reinforced with carbon fibers exhibited  superior  flexural  strength,  fracture  toughness,  and  thermal protection  capabilities,  with  tailored  phase  compositions  and  microstructural  design  strategies  effectively  balancing  mechanical  performance and ablation resistance at elevated temperatures [170,171]. In case of the ablation property and resistance-related attribute enhancement, the UHTCMC modified with C/C composites can be divided into categories.  Thus,  advancements  in  UHTCMCs  have  focused  on  optimizing the PIP process and precursor development to enhance densification, mechanical properties, and oxidation resistance, while addressing  challenges  like  embrittlement,  porosity,  and  the  need  for improved ceramic yields.

A B S T R A C T
modified  C/C  composites  can  address  the  issue  of  oxidation  at  high temperature and enhance the mechanical and thermodynamic properties of C/Cs [8 -10]. Xie et al. [11] prepared SiC nanowires and toughened SiC coatings with carbon and SiC dual protective layers on C/C composites using CVR, PECVD, PIP, and pack cementation technologies. Upon complete exposure to air, the surface of the SiC coating exhibited rapid oxidation. Subsequently, a SiO2  film was formed on the coating surface. The melt SiO2 has low oxygen permeability, which enables it to heal cracks and protect the C/C composites. Techniques for preparing carbide UHTCs-modified C/Cs include chemical vapor infiltration/deposition  (CVI/CVD)  [12],  reactive  melt  infiltration  (RMI) [13], and polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP) [9,14]. The PIP process  involves infiltrating  the precursor  of the  ceramic phase into the interior of the fiber preform through vacuum or pressure impregnation. Then, a high-temperature heat treatment process is used to decompose the precursor into a ceramic matrix in a high-temperature environment. Dense  ceramic  matrix  composites  can  be  obtained  through  multiple

3.1. Preparation of porous YB2C2
The phase compositions of samples sintered at 1200, 1400, 1600, 1800, and 1950 ◦ C were investigated to analyze phase evolution. Fig. 5 and Table 4 reveal that only the diffraction peaks of C, B4C, and Y2O3 appeared  in  the  sample  sintered  at  1200 ◦ C  indicating  no  reaction occurred.  Upon  increasing  the  sintering  temperature  to  1400 ◦ C,  the diffraction peaks of YBO3  were detected, due to the reaction between Y2O3 and part of B4C. Based on studies by Li et al. on YbB2C2 and Chen et al. on ScB2C2, it is speculated that the unassigned diffraction peak at 13 ◦ observed during boron carbide thermal reduction of YB2C2  corresponds to the YBxCy phase [47,48]. Faint YB2C2 diffraction peaks were detected at 1600 ◦ C, signaling the onset of the formation of YB2C2. In addition,  traces  of  YBO3,  YB4,  and  an  unknown  YBxCy  phase  were formed following the reaction Eq. (7) [7]:
<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
At  1800 ◦ C,  the  intensity  of  the  diffraction  peaks  of  YB2C2  were enhanced, and YB2C2  became the dominant phase of the sample. The peaks of YBO3 and YBxCy disappeared, while minor quantities of YB4 and C remained, as shown in the reaction Eq. (8) [7]:
<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
When sintered at 1950 ◦ C, only the diffraction peaks of YB2C2  were observed, illustrating that the remaining YB4  reacted with C to form YB2C2 according to the following reaction Eq. (9) [7]:
<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
In summary, high purity porous YB2C2  ceramics were successfully obtained at 1950 ◦ C via a novel method combining PEP and in-situ borocarbothermic reduction.

2.3. PIP
In the PIP process, polymers, which form ceramic by thermal decomposition, are in /uniFB01 ltrated into a carbon /uniFB01 ber-based preform, and a CMC matrix is formed by pyrolysis. For the formation of SiC matrices, the decomposition of polycarbosilane (PCS) is caused by the following reactions [48]:
<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
PIP process yields highly porous matrices. This is because reactions (5) and (6) provide ceramic yields of 89.6% and 68.9%, respectively while inducing a simultaneous volume shrinkage [48]. The repetition of these reactions (at least six times or more) is mandatory to achieve a dense matrix [48 -50]. However, this process does not require complex experimental systems and high process temperatures (temperatures in the range of 900 -1000 °C are required for the decomposition of polymers and the formation of SiC). Thus, the PIP process is widely used for the preparation of C/UHTC composites [48 -77].
Wang et al. [49] fabricated Cf/ZSC composites with an open porosity of 5 -7% and a /uniFB01 ber volume fraction of ∼ 40% by the PIP process.
ZrC was added as a powder slurry, and SiC was formed by the decomposition of PCS. Li et al. [62] demonstrated the direct preparation of UHTC matrices by the PIP method. They prepared Cf/ZSC composites with a /uniFB01 ber volume fraction of 34 -56% by decomposing organic Zrcontaining precursors along with PCS. These decompositions were conducted at 900 °C for 30 min and were repeated nine times. A Cf/ZSC composite with an open porosity of 7 -8% was obtained. Recently, the combination of the PIP process and other processes has also been investigated to obtain dense matrices in a shorter processing time [46,78 -81].

question: In Huang et al. 2016, ZrC-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites have an initial (pre-thermal-cycling) flexural strength. In Borrelli et al. 2009, the massive ZrB2-SiC nose tip of the Nose_2 demonstrator reaches a maximum principal stress at the tip-pin coupling contact surface during the PWT test rebuilding analysis. Which value is larger?
answer: The flexural strength of ZrC-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites in Huang et al. 2016 is not provided in the given sources. However, the maximum principal stress at the tip-pin coupling contact surface in Borrelli et al. 2009 is 211 MPa [1]. Without the specific value from Huang et al. 2016, it is not possible to determine which value is larger.

1. Introduction
For these reasons, at the present time, lots of technology projects are moving towards ceramic protection systems based on hafnium, zirconium and titanium borides. These materials, known as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), are characterized by very high melting point and, if blended with a proper reinforcing phase (such as silicon carbide, SiC), they exhibit good oxidation resistance. As matter of fact, in oxidation environment, up to 1500 ◦ C and at atmospheric pressure, SiC undergoes passive oxidation with the formation of silicon dioxide (SiO2). In regime of SiC passive oxidation the presence of zirconium and hafnium diborides leads to the formation of a vitreous borosilicate layer which, being characterized by a very low oxygen permeability, restricts the degradation processes to the surface of the ceramic sample [7-9].
Among these modern projects, the SHS program is focused on the assessment of UHTCs to the fabrication of high performance and slender-shaped hot structures for critical parts of reusable launch vehicles. These critical parts of re-entry vehicles, such as nose cap, has to be firstly qualified on-ground and then tested and validated in flight conditions. To this aim a dedicated plasma wind tunnel (PWT) test campaign has been designed on several technological demonstrator dubbed Nose_1, Nose_2 and Nose_x. They are full scale specimens, thus, the technological problems occurred during their manufacturing are the same that probably will recur when the launchers will be tested in flight condition.
In the present paper, the results coming from the thermo-structural rebuilding FEM analyses of the PWT test on Nose_2 are reported. The main aim is to demonstrate the capability of the adopted FEM tool to reproduce the real thermo-structural behaviour of the nose cap by comparing numerical temperature distribution to the experimental data obtained during the test. The numerical results are also used to give a reasonable interpretation of the experimental data and to help in understanding the thermo-mechanical behaviour of the nose cap subcomponents and their interactions during the test.
The paper is organized as follows:

question: Borrelli et al. 2009 describe how the ZrB2-SiC conical tip of the Nose_2 reentry-vehicle nose cap was fabricated, and Yang et al. 2025 describe how porous YB2C2 ceramics were fabricated. Which of these two ultra-high-temperature ceramic components was produced by additive manufacturing (powder extrusion printing) followed by reaction sintering, versus which was produced by hot pressing followed by machining?
answer: The porous YB2C2 ceramics described by Yang et al. 2025 were produced by powder extrusion printing followed by reaction sintering [4]. In contrast, the ZrB2-SiC conical tip of the Nose_2 reentry-vehicle nose cap, as described by Borrelli et al. 2009, was produced by hot pressing sintering followed by machining [2].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  possess  high  melting points and the ability to resist oxidation and thermal stresses at higher temperatures, which are suitable for applications in extreme environments, such as nose cones of ballistic missiles, leading edges of hypersonic vehicles, rocket engines, aerospace re-entry vehicles, etc. [1,2]. UHTCs are traditionally fabricated by sintering processes such as hot pressing (HP), pressure-less sintering (PS), and spark plasma sintering (SPS). However, it is extremely difficult to obtain complex shapes of UHTCs due to the geometry limitation from the mold or chamber.
Additive  manufacturing  (AM)  is  one  of  the  forerunners  in  the disruptive manufacturing of materials with complex geometries. AM has the potential to radically change the way how products are made and distributed [3]. AM effectively reduces the production time and automates  the  process  with  minimal  human  interaction.  Tooling,  cutting fluids,  coolants  are  absent  in  the  AM  process  [4].  AM  process  needs lower energy input and greatly reduces the cost in machining [5]. The
* Corresponding author.
E-mail address: cheryl.xu@ncsu.edu (C. Xu).
1 These authors contributed equally to this work.

2. Nose 2 structural concept
The basic idea of the Nose_2 design is to couple conventional C/SiC materials to novel ultra-high temperature ZrB2-SiC ceramics in order to create a multi-material structure able to withstand the severe condition associated with slender-shaped hot structures and non-conventional reentry mission profiles. The main components of the Nose_2 demonstrator are the tip and the dome. The latter, its turn, is made of two subcomponents: the outer dome and the inner dome (Fig. 1).
The conical tip, which is intended to sustain the greatest thermal load in the whole nose cap structure, is made of ultra-high temperature ZrB2-SiC ceramic. The tip was produced by hot pressing sintering and then finished by EDM (electrical discharge machining) [10]. Unfortunately, just during the EDM of the conical tip base, an extra current (due probably to a 'not suitably flushed material') caused the detachment of an excessive amount of material, as a consequence the tip presented a small chipped part (Fig. 2). The width, the height and the depth of the defect, after a re-machining aimed at smoothing the damage were, respectively, 7, 5, 1mm. Safety numerical analyses proved that the presence of the defect does not change significantly temperature and stress distributions on the massive cone and the structural integrity of the Nose_2 was assured [11], so that the tip was considered suitable for the PWT test.
The outer dome is made of C/SiC and has the shape of a hollow frustum of cone. It was manufactured by polymer infiltration and pyrolis (PIP) process. Components obtained by PIP are usually characterized by high residual porosity so that a thin protective coating is needed to protect the fibres from oxidation. In this case, a ZrB2-SiC coating, about 500 /afii9839 m thick, was applied by plasma spray deposition technique on the external surface of the outer dome.

4.6.  Additive manufacturing technology, AM
To date, notable progress and breakthroughs have been achieved in the additive manufacturing of carbon fiber-reinforced ceramic matrix composites [143]. Kemp et al. [144] utilized direct ink writing (DIW) technology with a composite ink consisting of ZrB2  powders, SiC precursors,  and  SiC  short  fibers.  The  printing  was  conducted  at  room temperature, followed by pyrolysis at 1200 °C to fabricate SiCf/ZrB2-SiC composites. However, as the fiber content increased, issues such as fiber misalignment and material cracking arose due to gas release and matrix shrinkage during polymer curing and pyrolysis. Additionally, the pyrolyzed components often exhibited defects, limiting their mechanical performance. Li et al. [145] addressed these issues by employing DIW combined with ultrasound-assisted fiber separation. During the direct ink  writing  of  continuous  carbon  fiber/ceramic  composites,  ceramic slurry was impregnated into continuous carbon fiber bundles. A subsequent low-temperature hot-pressing process was used to enhance the robustness of the fabricated structures, indirectly resulting in Cf/ZrB₂SiC ceramic matrix composites (as shown in Fig. 23).
Despite these advancements, several challenges remain in the additive manufacturing of UHTC, such as the high brittleness of materials leading to crack formation and the control of thermal stresses during the  fabrication  process.  Future  research  should  focus  on  optimizing process  parameters,  developing  new  material  systems,  and  exploring the  integration  of  multiple  manufacturing  techniques.  AM  offers  a highly flexible and promising pathway for fabricating ceramic matrix composites,  especially  UHTC.  While  challenges  persist,  ongoing  research and technological innovations are expected to position AM as a pivotal technique in the manufacturing of high-temperature materials, driving  the  widespread  application  of  ceramic  matrix  composites  in aerospace, defense, and other critical fields.
Fig. 23. Schematic illustration of Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites fabricated by direct ink writing and hot-press sintering. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [145], © Elsevier 2022.

A B S T R A C T
Controlling crystallographic texture and pore architecture in porous ultra-high temperature ceramics is critical for tailoring their performance in extreme environments, yet achieving this dual control remains a formidable challenge.  Here,  we  demonstrate  a  novel  strategy  to  fabricate  architected  porous  YB2C2  ceramics  exhibiting strong crystallographic and mechanical property anisotropy by synergizing powder extrusion printing with in-situ reaction  synthesis.  The  core  of  our  approach  is  a  templated-growth  mechanism,  where  shear  forces  during extrusion  align  sacrificial  graphite  flakes  within  the  green  body.  These  aligned  flakes  subsequently  serve  as heterogeneous  nucleation  and  growth  sites  for  plate-like  YB2C2  grains  during  boro/carbothermal  reduction, effectively  translating  the  macro-scale  printed  path  into  a  micro-scale  crystallographic  texture.  This  process yields a remarkable hierarchical structure, featuring unidirectionally aligned pores and a strong [001] crystallographic texture parallel to the printing direction. The resulting ceramics exhibit a highly tunable porosity (50 -75 vol%) and exceptional compressive strength, reaching 76.7 ± 14.7 MPa at ~50 % porosity. Crucially, this microstructural control imparts a pronounced mechanical anisotropy, a key attribute for designing components with directionally dependent loads. This work establishes a new paradigm for manufacturing ultra-high temperature ceramics, enabling the concurrent design of geometry and crystallographic texture to achieve tailored thermomechanical properties.
when used as a reinforcement phase in Cu-based composites [5,6]. These properties position YB2C2 as an ideal material for structural components in harsh environments such as thermal protection systems, nuclear reactors, and hypersonic vehicle engines [7 -9]. However, realizing the full potential of porous YB2C2  ceramics hinges on the ability to precisely control  their  porosity,  crystallographic  texture,  and  macroscopic  geometry -a challenge that has yet to be fully addressed by conventional fabrication techniques.

4.11.3. Combination of solid and liquid state
This  type  of  technique  can  also  be  employed  to  produce  dense matrices of phases that are challenging to achieve through conventional methods.  For  instance,  Kannan  et  al.  aimed  to  incorporate  TaC  into UHTC matrices to enhance mechanical strength and ablation resistance, as the Ta2O5 layer formed at elevated temperatures acts as a barrier to oxygen diffusion. To circumvent the need for extremely high sintering temperatures to fully densify the composite, they utilized a combination of reactive HP and PIP (10 cycles) at 1200 ◦ C to create dense composites [273]. Servadei et al. fabricated UHTC matrix composites using a hybrid technique combining water-based powder slurry infiltration, Polymer Infiltration and Pyrolysis, and Hot Pressing to determine the optimal sequence for ZrB2-SiC matrix, with the best results obtained from two PIP  cycles  followed  by  HP,  yielding  significantly  higher  flexural strengths, especially at high temperatures, and offering a more efficient manufacturing  method  compared  to  traditional  PIP  processes  [274]. Recently, ultrahigh-temperature ceramic matrix composites were produced  using  slurry  infiltration  in  2D  woven  carbon  fabric  and  hot pressing  at  2000 ◦ C,  optimizing  the  slurry ' s  mixing  time  and  solid loading (20 % -30 %) to achieve agglomerate-free composites with a nearly  dense  matrix  layer,  a  porous  matrix  within  fiber  layers,  and non-brittle mechanical properties. In this case, the hybrid techniques of SI  and  hot  pressing  proved  to  be  time-efficient  [275].  Mishra  et  al. examined the phase volume fractions, and matrix porosity in UHTCMCs, using a hybrid fabrication technique involving slurry infiltration and hot pressing, revealing that it performs greater infiltration than others, with insights into matrix porosity formation [276]. They also discovered that fiber  disorientation and partial degradation can lead to lower elastic modulus,  flexural  strength,  and  fracture  toughness,  although

A B S T R A C T
Owing to their high melting points and ability to resist extreme thermal stresses, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are important materials for critical applications such as hypersonic flights, space re-entry vehicles, and rocket engines. Traditional manufacturing processes restrict the freedom to manufacture UHTCs with complex geometries  due  to  the  limitations  of  die  and  mold  designs.  Electron  beam  melting  (EBM)  is  an  established powder-bed layer-by-layer additive manufacturing (AM) process for metal parts. In this research, an effort was made to evaluate the feasibility of EBM for the AM fabrication of UHTC-based materials, and to investigate the microstructures of the fabricated materials under different processing conditions. A mathematical model was developed to simulate and optimize the processing parameters for the fabrication of ZrB2-30 vol% ZrSi2  UHTC using EBM. The simulation results were compared with experimental observations. For EBM fabrication of ZrB230 vol% ZrSi2 composites, the optimal processing parameters are beam power of 500 W with scanning speeds of 500, 750, and 1000 mm/s, and beam power of 1000 W with scanning speed of 1000 mm/s. This study demonstrates the potential for additive manufacturing of UHTCs with complex geometries by the EBM technique.
mechanical properties of the AM materials can be comparable to those of materials fabricated by traditional processes. However, currently AM technologies are only matured enough for the fabrication of metals and metal-based composites, and more efforts are needed in advancing AM for other types of materials such as refractory ceramics [6].

1. Introduction
For these reasons, at the present time, lots of technology projects are moving towards ceramic protection systems based on hafnium, zirconium and titanium borides. These materials, known as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), are characterized by very high melting point and, if blended with a proper reinforcing phase (such as silicon carbide, SiC), they exhibit good oxidation resistance. As matter of fact, in oxidation environment, up to 1500 ◦ C and at atmospheric pressure, SiC undergoes passive oxidation with the formation of silicon dioxide (SiO2). In regime of SiC passive oxidation the presence of zirconium and hafnium diborides leads to the formation of a vitreous borosilicate layer which, being characterized by a very low oxygen permeability, restricts the degradation processes to the surface of the ceramic sample [7-9].
Among these modern projects, the SHS program is focused on the assessment of UHTCs to the fabrication of high performance and slender-shaped hot structures for critical parts of reusable launch vehicles. These critical parts of re-entry vehicles, such as nose cap, has to be firstly qualified on-ground and then tested and validated in flight conditions. To this aim a dedicated plasma wind tunnel (PWT) test campaign has been designed on several technological demonstrator dubbed Nose_1, Nose_2 and Nose_x. They are full scale specimens, thus, the technological problems occurred during their manufacturing are the same that probably will recur when the launchers will be tested in flight condition.
In the present paper, the results coming from the thermo-structural rebuilding FEM analyses of the PWT test on Nose_2 are reported. The main aim is to demonstrate the capability of the adopted FEM tool to reproduce the real thermo-structural behaviour of the nose cap by comparing numerical temperature distribution to the experimental data obtained during the test. The numerical results are also used to give a reasonable interpretation of the experimental data and to help in understanding the thermo-mechanical behaviour of the nose cap subcomponents and their interactions during the test.
The paper is organized as follows:

4.1.1.  Hot pressing sintering technology, HP
Hot pressing sintering (HP) is a traditional and widely used ceramic fabrication technique that densifies ceramic powders under high temperature and high pressure. Its principle involves simultaneously applying  heat  and  pressure  to  promote  particle  contact  and  diffusion, thereby  reducing  porosity  and  forming  a  dense  ceramic  structure [67,68]. For instance, Gui et al. [69] used nanoscale ZrB2  powder as raw  material  and  prepared  ZrB2-SiC  ceramics  by  single-step  hot pressing  at  1500-1800 °C  and  two-step  hot  pressing  optimized  at 1500 °C  (as  shown  in  Fig.  8).  Fully  dense  ZrB2-SiC  was  obtained  by single  step  hot  pressing  at  1800 °C,  with  a  bending  strength  of 1044   ±   120 MPa and a fracture toughness of 6.21   ±   0.30 MPa·m 1/2 . An  optimized  two-step  hot  pressing  process  was  designed  based  on densification curves to prepare ZrB2-SiC ceramics at low temperatures, resulting in improved bending strength (901   ±   109 MPa) and fracture toughness (6.07   ±   0.22 MPa·m 1/2 ).
Fig. 7. Microstructure of SiC interphase on carbon fibers prepared by PIP. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [65], © Elsevier 2014.

4. Conclusions
In this study, we have developed and validated a novel manufacturing paradigm that synergistically combines powder extrusion printing and in-situ reaction synthesis to create architected porous YB2C2 ceramics with unprecedented hierarchical control over both pore architecture  and  crystallographic  texture.  This  approach  successfully overcomes the intrinsic limitations of conventional fabrication methods, which typically yield stochastic microstructures and isotropic properties. The main conclusions are summarized as follows:
(1)  Orthogonal parameter optimization ( d = 0.15 mm, v = 30 mm/s, f = 130 %) yielded defect-minimized green bodies with a relative density of  98.23 %,  providing  a  robust  precursor  for  anisotropic  ceramic formation.
(2)  A  two-step  solvent-thermal  debinding  route  ensured  uniform binder removal without cracking. Reaction pathway analysis revealed that phase-pure YB2C2  is obtained at 1950 ◦ C, with graphite templates guiding phase formation through localized boro/carbothermal reduction.
(3)  A  mechanistic  model  for  texture  generation  was  established, where printing-induced shear aligns graphite flakes that act as reactive templates. EBSD  confirmed  strong crystallographic texture, with maximum orientation intensities of 37.75 MUD (X-Y surface) and 26.16 MUD (X-Z surface).
(4)  Compressive  strength  decreased  with  porosity  from  76.7 ± 14.7 MPa  to  21.0 ± 2.7 MPa  in  the  X-Y  direction,  and  from  50.0 ± 13.8 MPa  to  3.2 ± 2.5 MPa  in  the  X-Z  direction.  Fractography revealed direction-dependent failure modes: the X-Z direction exhibited brittle fracture with rapid crack propagation, whereas the X-Y direction showed enhanced damage tolerance via crack  deflection  and  energy dissipation.

4.1. Hot pressing
matrices via hot pressing and tested for oxidation resistance at 1650 ◦ C, revealing that ZrB2 GLYPH<0> MoSi2 matrix offered the best oxidation protection by forming a compact ZrO2-SiO2 scale, with no visible defects or spallation  after  testing.  Recently,  Kolek  et  al.  [114]  investigated  dense HfC-SiC  composites  (HfC-20  wt.  %  SiC)  fabricated  via  hot-pressing, achieving 99 % relative density at 2100 ◦ C for 1 h under 50 MPa, with a fine microstructure (average grain size ~5 µ m) and Vickers hardness of 22.9 ± 0.8  GPa.  Also,  upon  oxyacetylene  torch  exposure,  a  surface hafnium  oxide  layer  of  410  nm  thickness  formed,  with  an  average recession rate of 0.028 µ m/s observed. The ultrafast high-temperature hot-pressing  technique  was  utilized  to  enhance  the  densification  of ZrC ceramics, achieving > 98 % density with 2.5 wt % B4C additive at 2300 ◦ C  in  3  min,  showing  improved  properties  compared  to  other sintering methods, including finer grain sizes, higher Vickers hardness, and faster processing times [11]. However, Gardini et al. involved in their research [115] by preparing ZrB2-based pastes with varying volumes of short carbon fibers (5 %, 20 %, 45 %, and 60 %) to achieve complex  shapes,  followed  by  hot  pressing,  demonstrating  enhanced fracture toughness and complex shape capability without needing specific graphite inserts. Hence, UHTCMCs have recently made strides in hot-pressing  technique  optimization,  which  has  improved  fracture toughness,  oxidation  resistance,  and  material  densification,  made complicated form fabrication possible, and improved mechanical characteristics by including different reinforcements.

question: Huang et al. 2016 thermally cycle ZrB2-SiC and ZrC-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites over a specified hot-and-cold temperature range simulating low-Earth orbit, while Borrelli et al. 2009 assume an initial temperature at the start of the Nose_2 re-entry trajectory in their FEM model. Which is colder: the low end of Huang's thermal shock range, or Borrelli's assumed initial re-entry temperature of 300 K?
answer: The low end of Huang's thermal shock range is 110 °C [1], while Borrelli's assumed initial re-entry temperature is 300 K, which is equivalent to 27 °C. Therefore, Borrelli's assumed initial re-entry temperature is colder.

a b s t r a c t
UHTCs based coatings (ZrB2 e SiC coating and ZrC e SiC coating) are prepared on the surface of PIP-C/SiC composites by slurry and CVD method, and the thermal stability is one of the key issues for C/SiC composites. This study aims to study thermal shock behavior of the coated composites. The results show the degradation of /uniFB02 exural strength without obvious variation, and the fracture mode and pull-out mechanism are also changed. The composition of coatings is characterized by XRD, SEM and XPS. The decrease of /uniFB02 exural strength during the tests is due to the debonding of the coating-matrix interfaces. Compared to uncoated C/SiC composites, the UHTCs based coatings are able to improve the mechanical properties of the composites.

2.2. Atmospheric Re-entry
Starting at a 120 km altitude and an initial ~ Mach 25 velocity, NASA space shuttles were required to end missions by landing on a runway 91 m wide [33], - a challenge for blunt-shaped bodies. The accuracy of a body during re-entry is related to leading edge geometry. Sharp geometries have higher ballistic coefficients (BC) and lower drag forces/coefficients than blunt geometries. However, as BC increases, so too do maximum heating rates. If a material can withstand ultra-high temperatures, the BC of the vehicle can be increased via sharper, more streamlined geometries that allow several advantages, like significantly altering the altitude of maximum deceleration. The lower the altitude of deceleration, the shorter the amount of time spent in the atmosphere, or the more rapidly the vehicle arrives at the surface of the Earth. The benefits of a high BC compared to a low BC are listed in Table 2.
Opeka et al. indicated the 500 ◦ -2000 ◦ C range as being the necessary materials window for hypersonics [34]. Technically, hypersonic velocity is defined as any speed exceeding the speed of sound, but is typically more  specifically  defined  as  beginning  at  Mach  5,  or  in  the  range 1702-3403 m/s. Hypersonic velocities are less demanding the further from the Earth ' s atmosphere due to decreasing air density. The Space Shuttle re-entering the Earth ' s  atmosphere at nearly Mach 25 in low earth  orbit  is  a  substantially  different  thermal,  aerodynamic,  and oxidation scenario than Mach 25 a few kilometers above the surface.
As discussed earlier, the maximum usage temperature for any SiC based thermal protection system (TPS) is ~1727 ◦ C and for comparative purposes, SiC is viewed as protective up to1727 ◦ C. Over a temperature range of 300-1700 °C, the optimized coating containing 15 vol% TaB2 extended the initial oxidation temperature from 300 °C to 500 °C, reducing dynamic oxidation loss by 75.85% compared to the 60HfB2-40MoSi2 coating. However, the linear temperature change mode is difficult to  reproduce  the  transient  competition  mechanism  of  thermal  shock and oxidation coupling during space reentry, which limits the accuracy of  predicting  the  oxygen  diffusion  path  of  coatings  under  extreme thermal gradients.

THERMAL SHOCK RESISTANCE
After the recent tragic explosion of Space Shuttle Columbia, the findings of the Columbia Accident Investigation Board and the recent Space Exploration Initiative suggest that safety be made the top priority in the design of new reentry vehicles (Wang et al., 2007). During reentry from Low Earth Orbit (LEO), such as from the Space Station or parking orbits, relatively high speeds (on the order of 8 km/s) are reached (Monteverde et al., 2007). External thermal protection systems of reusable space vehicles, in the form of rigid surfaces in areas of ultra-high working temperatures (beyond 1800 °C), must be able to withstand high temperatures, high heat flux and mechanical stresses associated with vibrations at launch and re-entry in Earth's atmosphere (Tang et al., 2007). Most ceramic matrix composites, including ZrB 2 matrix ceramics, have inherently low fracture toughness and are thus susceptible to catastrophic fractures caused by thermal stresses under the above-mentioned conditions. Consequently, the thermal shock resistance of the ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic must be estimated.
In  the  present  work,  the  thermal  shock  resistance  of  the  ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic was investigated by the water quenching method, and the microstructure of the specimen before and after water quenching was observed. Furthermore, the temperature and thermal stress distributions in the specimen during instantaneous water quenching were simulated and analyzed in detail by finite element analysis.

1. Introduction
With the increased severity of hypersonic re-entry vehicles ' operating  conditions,  the  propulsion  system  requires  materials  that  can withstand  temperatures > 2000 ◦ C  [1].  Ceramic  matrix  composites (CMCs) such as C/C and C/SiC fibers are the existing materials of choice due  to  their  light-weight,  excellent  mechanical,  and  thermal  shock resistance [2]. However, carbon is susceptible to oxidation above 400 ◦ C and has poor stability in an erosive environment [3,4]. The addition of SiC only improves its stability until 1100 ◦ C due to the volatile nature of SiO2  at higher temperatures [5]. Due to the thermal limitations of C/C and  C/SiC,  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  considered good additives for conventional C/C composites. UHTCs are carbides, nitrides, and borides of early transition metal elements (group IV and V) with  a  combination  of  metal  and  ceramic-like  properties  that  offers excellent  stability  at  temperatures > 2000 ◦ C.  UHTC  is  a  potential candidate for a thermal protection system (TPS) for reusable re-entry vehicles. TPS  is necessary for spacecraft that enter the earth ' s

In recent years, slurry injection technology has demonstrated potential for shortening process cycles in SI by directly introducing high-solid-content ceramic slurries into fiber preforms. 15-18 According to the study by Liu et al., 19 the process was optimized through the injection of ZrB2 -phenolic resin slurry, achieving a density of 2.10 ± 0.05 g/cm 3 and porosity of approximately 40%. However, subsequent densification processes are required after slurry injection into preforms. Venkatachalam et al. 20 fabricated C f -ZrB 2 -Cm composites using slurry injection combined with radio-frequency-assisted CVI, achieving 90% relative density for C f -ZrB 2 within 24 h. Under oxyacetylene ablation, the composites exhibited mass ablation rate of 1.7 mg/s and linear ablation rate of 1.6 µ m/safter 60 s (peak temperature ∼ 2600 K). However, residual 10% porosity in the 2.5D carbon fiber preform, caused by complex pore structures and CVI process limitations, significantly reduced ablation performance. Yang et al. 21 obtained C/ZrB2-SiC with 86% relative density and 215 MPa flexural strength using 30 vol.% ZrB 2 slurry injection combined with 12 PIP cycles. Hu et al. 22 achieved a 286 MPa flexural strength for Cf /ZrC-SiC through eight PIP cycles. Although PIP enables gradient distribution control of matrix phases, its requirement for multiple cycles (typically > 10) extends fabrication time to hundreds of hours. 23 RMI rapidly produces high-density ceramic matrix composites through molten metal reactions with preforms. It is particularly suitable for complex-shaped components, having demonstrated efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and high application potential. 24,25

question: Huang et al. 2016 cycle ZrB2-SiC and ZrC-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites up to an upper temperature during thermal shock testing, and Borrelli et al. 2009 report the temperature threshold below which the space shuttle orbiter's low-temperature reusable surface insulation (LRSI) tiles were designed to keep the structure. Converting Huang's upper cycling temperature to Kelvin, is it above or below the LRSI threshold reported by Borrelli et al. 2009?
answer: Huang et al. 2016 report that the thermal shock testing involved a temperature difference of up to 1000 ◦ C [3]. However, the specific upper cycling temperature is not explicitly stated in the provided text. Borrelli et al. 2009 report that the low-temperature reusable surface insulation (LRSI) tiles were designed to keep structural temperatures less than 920K [1]. Without the exact upper cycling temperature from Huang et al. 2016, it is not possible to definitively determine if it is above or below the LRSI threshold.

1. Introduction
As an example, the current space shuttle orbiter TPS configuration has been designed to keep structural temperatures less than 450K. The thermal protection is composed of two types of reusable surface insulation (RSI) tiles. The RSI tiles covering the orbiter are made of coated silica fibre. The two types differ only in surface coating to provide protection for different temperature environments. The low-temperature insulation (LRSI) consists of 8-in.-square silica tiles and covers the top of the vehicle where temperatures are less than 920K. The high-temperature insulation (HRSI) consists of 6-in.-square tiles that cover the bottom and some leading edges of the orbiter where temperatures are below 1530K.
In addition, reinforced carbon-carbon (RCC) is used for the nose cap and wing leading edges where temperatures are above 1530K. Finally flexible reusable surface insulation (FRSI) and advanced flexible reusable surface insulation (AFRSI) are used at locations where temperatures are less than 640K [1,2].
Much effort is currently dedicated in the aerospace community to study future vehicles reentering the earth atmosphere [3-5]. The word space agency and industries are trying to develop fully reusable space transportation systems able to perform multiple launches in a safe, cost-effective manner [6]. In order to match these requirements, future concepts for space launchers are addressed towards sharp hot structures (SHS).
This kind of architecture (aircrafts-like) offers several advantages with respect to current blunt shapes: manoeuvrability improvement, decrease of electromagnetic interferences and communication black-out and drag reduction during the ascent phase. As main negative aspect, the aerodynamic heat fluxes increase dramatically over the vehicle profile, exceeding conventional values of 650-800kW/m 2 . The conventional ceramic matrix composites (CMCs) such as C/C and C/SiC, although reliable and well tested, are not able to sustain so high thermal loads.

Compared to uncoated C/SiC composites, the UHTCs based coatings are able to improve the mechanical properties of the composites.

1. Introduction
Carbon /uniFB01 ber reinforced silicon carbide (C/SiC) composites are considered as potential candidate materials for high temperature structural components due to their low density, high strength, excellent corrosion resistance and good fracture toughness [1 e 3]. However, at higher temperatures ( > 1200 /C14 C), the oxidation behavior of the C/SiC composites is cooperatively in /uniFB02 uenced by the oxidation of the /uniFB01 bers, inter-phase and matrix [4,5]. Consequently, C/SiC composites require anti-oxidation protection when used at a higher temperature environment.
In the family of Ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), two of the most interesting materials for thermal shielding applications are zirconium diboride (ZrB2) and zirconium carbide (ZrC), due to their extremely high melting temperatures, high hardness, and high retained strength at high temperature [6]. ZrB2 e SiC and ZrC e SiC ceramics have been proved as effective coatings for protecting carbon materials at high temperature [5 e 8].
The anti-oxidation ability of coatings is commonly tested at an invariable temperature [4 e 8]. However, most of space vehicles have to work in low-earth orbit environment, such as thermal shock, high vacuum (10 /C0 7 Pa), atomic oxygen, and so on. Vacuum is

Thermal Protection Systems
The Space Shuttle Orbiter typifies ceramic thermal protection for reusable space applications. A thermal protection system is employed to insulate the vehicle from aeroheating during reentry. Prior to the reusable Orbiter, ablative materials were used predominantly in the role of thermal protection for ballistic entry. As a lifting-body reentry vehicle, the Space Shuttle Orbiter requires a shape-stable system to control the aerothermal heating environment, to permit consistent aerodynamic performance, and provide for a reusable system. 4 Ceramic tiles with glass-based coatings have been the mainstay of this design philosophy for over three decades. Initially, the thermal efficiency was paramount to minimize the thermal protection system weight. The porous ceramic tiles in acreage areas were comprised of silica fibers and had a nominal density of 0.14 g/cm 3 . A glaze was employed on the tile to minimize the incoming heat load by controlling emissivity and limiting catalysis for oxygen and nitrogen recombination from the plasma on reentry.
Fig. 1. Oxide ceramic matrix composite exhaust ground test demonstrator consisting of a 1.60-m-diameter nozzle (reveal showing underneath the titanium-alloy faring) and 1.14 m diameter by 2.34 m conical centerbody with titanium end cap inspection portal.
This tile/coating combination was an effective insulator meeting the Shuttle Orbiter thermal requirements; however, the relatively low strength and impact resistance introduced a heavy maintenance cycle required to ready the Orbiter for the next flight. Thus, the initial thermal efficiency requirement was augmented to address maintenance drivers. Durability improvements were desired to reduce the maintenance burden of the thermal protection system. Multiple improvements were made during the operational lifetime of the Shuttle Orbiter including new tile substrates and coatings. 5,6

question: Yang et al. 2025 describe the fabrication route for porous YB2C2 ceramics, and Borrelli et al. 2009 describe the fabrication route for the outer C/SiC dome of the Nose_2 reentry-vehicle nose cap before its protective coating is applied. Which of these two ultra-high-temperature ceramic components is manufactured using an additive manufacturing (3D printing) technique?
answer: The porous YB2C2 ceramics described by Yang et al. 2025 are manufactured using an additive manufacturing (3D printing) technique [3]. The outer C/SiC dome of the Nose_2 reentry-vehicle nose cap, as described by Borrelli et al. 2009, is manufactured by the polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP) process [2].

A B S T R A C T
Zr-based  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  widely  used  as  the  key  parts  of  aircrafts  in  thermal protection systems. However, such materials usually have a high sintering temperature and it is difficult to obtain complex parts using traditional manufacturing methods. This study reports on the success preparation of complex Zr-based UHTCs by a light-curing 3D printing process. The results indicate that the smaller the precursor particle size, the higher is the viscosity of the slurry used for printing. Slurries with a better curing performance can be prepared by mixing various active diluents and photoinitiators with an optimized certain proportion. The stability of the slurry can be improved by adding coupling agents and dispersants. The main components of the 3D printed green parts are ZrC, SiC and pyrolytic carbon at 1800 ◦ C, yielding a high compressive strength (102 ± 2 MPa) and elastic modulus (65 ± 1 GPa). ZrC/SiC composite ceramic parts with low open porosity (3.99 %) were obtained by 3D printing after heat treatment, which provides an effective way to design complex UHTC parts for extreme environment applications.

However,  these  materials  have  strong  chemical bonds,  low  diffusion  coefficient,  and  high  hardness  and  brittleness, which poses the need for higher sintering temperatures and pressures for molding. Moreover, due to the high hardness and brittleness of ceramic parts, they are difficult to machine. Hence, it is difficult to produce more complex ceramic parts. In addition, through traditional manufacturing methods, and it is not only easy to damage the tool, but also easy to produce  burr  cracks  and  other  defects  during  processing  [10 -12]. Therefore, traditional manufacturing methods are difficult to meet the requirements of high-performance ceramic component forming. Thus, accurate and rapid integrated forming of ultra-high temperature ceramic parts with complex configurations has become a key problem in the field of extreme application environments.
