2. Nose 2 structural concept
The basic idea of the Nose_2 design is to couple conventional C/SiC materials to novel ultra-high temperature ZrB2-SiC ceramics in order to create a multi-material structure able to withstand the severe condition associated with slender-shaped hot structures and non-conventional reentry mission profiles. The main components of the Nose_2 demonstrator are the tip and the dome. The latter, its turn, is made of two subcomponents: the outer dome and the inner dome (Fig. 1).
The conical tip, which is intended to sustain the greatest thermal load in the whole nose cap structure, is made of ultra-high temperature ZrB2-SiC ceramic. The tip was produced by hot pressing sintering and then finished by EDM (electrical discharge machining) [10]. Unfortunately, just during the EDM of the conical tip base, an extra current (due probably to a 'not suitably flushed material') caused the detachment of an excessive amount of material, as a consequence the tip presented a small chipped part (Fig. 2). The width, the height and the depth of the defect, after a re-machining aimed at smoothing the damage were, respectively, 7, 5, 1mm. Safety numerical analyses proved that the presence of the defect does not change significantly temperature and stress distributions on the massive cone and the structural integrity of the Nose_2 was assured [11], so that the tip was considered suitable for the PWT test.
The outer dome is made of C/SiC and has the shape of a hollow frustum of cone. It was manufactured by polymer infiltration and pyrolis (PIP) process. Components obtained by PIP are usually characterized by high residual porosity so that a thin protective coating is needed to protect the fibres from oxidation. In this case, a ZrB2-SiC coating, about 500 /afii9839 m thick, was applied by plasma spray deposition technique on the external surface of the outer dome.

A B S T R A C T
Controlling crystallographic texture and pore architecture in porous ultra-high temperature ceramics is critical for tailoring their performance in extreme environments, yet achieving this dual control remains a formidable challenge.  Here,  we  demonstrate  a  novel  strategy  to  fabricate  architected  porous  YB2C2  ceramics  exhibiting strong crystallographic and mechanical property anisotropy by synergizing powder extrusion printing with in-situ reaction  synthesis.  The  core  of  our  approach  is  a  templated-growth  mechanism,  where  shear  forces  during extrusion  align  sacrificial  graphite  flakes  within  the  green  body.  These  aligned  flakes  subsequently  serve  as heterogeneous  nucleation  and  growth  sites  for  plate-like  YB2C2  grains  during  boro/carbothermal  reduction, effectively  translating  the  macro-scale  printed  path  into  a  micro-scale  crystallographic  texture.  This  process yields a remarkable hierarchical structure, featuring unidirectionally aligned pores and a strong [001] crystallographic texture parallel to the printing direction. The resulting ceramics exhibit a highly tunable porosity (50 -75 vol%) and exceptional compressive strength, reaching 76.7 ± 14.7 MPa at ~50 % porosity. Crucially, this microstructural control imparts a pronounced mechanical anisotropy, a key attribute for designing components with directionally dependent loads. This work establishes a new paradigm for manufacturing ultra-high temperature ceramics, enabling the concurrent design of geometry and crystallographic texture to achieve tailored thermomechanical properties.
when used as a reinforcement phase in Cu-based composites [5,6]. These properties position YB2C2 as an ideal material for structural components in harsh environments such as thermal protection systems, nuclear reactors, and hypersonic vehicle engines [7 -9]. However, realizing the full potential of porous YB2C2  ceramics hinges on the ability to precisely control  their  porosity,  crystallographic  texture,  and  macroscopic  geometry -a challenge that has yet to be fully addressed by conventional fabrication techniques.

A B S T R A C T
Conventional methods for producing porous UHTCs, such as direct foaming [6,7], sacrificial templating [8], sol-gel [9],  and partial sintering  [10],  face  difficulties  in  creating  complex,  intricately  shaped structures  [3,5].  A  promising  alternative  is  additive  manufacturing (AM), which has emerged as an effective approach for fabricating porous UHTC components with complex geometries [11 -14]. Among various AM techniques, vat photopolymerization (VP) shows significant potential for complex ceramic structure fabrication due to its high precision and efficiency [15]. However, the use of VP for printing intricate UHTC components remains underexplored in the literature. It has been proposed that the primary challenge for the VP of UHTCs is the printability of raw UHTC materials [16]. The dark gray or black appearance of UHTC

These materials can form  ceramics  at  relatively  low  temperatures  and  are  tailored  in composition,  providing  a  pathway  to  overcome  these  obstacles  and enable the additive manufacturing of complex-shaped UHTCs [17].

1. Introduction
For these reasons, at the present time, lots of technology projects are moving towards ceramic protection systems based on hafnium, zirconium and titanium borides. These materials, known as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), are characterized by very high melting point and, if blended with a proper reinforcing phase (such as silicon carbide, SiC), they exhibit good oxidation resistance. As matter of fact, in oxidation environment, up to 1500 ◦ C and at atmospheric pressure, SiC undergoes passive oxidation with the formation of silicon dioxide (SiO2). In regime of SiC passive oxidation the presence of zirconium and hafnium diborides leads to the formation of a vitreous borosilicate layer which, being characterized by a very low oxygen permeability, restricts the degradation processes to the surface of the ceramic sample [7-9].
Among these modern projects, the SHS program is focused on the assessment of UHTCs to the fabrication of high performance and slender-shaped hot structures for critical parts of reusable launch vehicles. These critical parts of re-entry vehicles, such as nose cap, has to be firstly qualified on-ground and then tested and validated in flight conditions. To this aim a dedicated plasma wind tunnel (PWT) test campaign has been designed on several technological demonstrator dubbed Nose_1, Nose_2 and Nose_x. They are full scale specimens, thus, the technological problems occurred during their manufacturing are the same that probably will recur when the launchers will be tested in flight condition.
In the present paper, the results coming from the thermo-structural rebuilding FEM analyses of the PWT test on Nose_2 are reported. The main aim is to demonstrate the capability of the adopted FEM tool to reproduce the real thermo-structural behaviour of the nose cap by comparing numerical temperature distribution to the experimental data obtained during the test. The numerical results are also used to give a reasonable interpretation of the experimental data and to help in understanding the thermo-mechanical behaviour of the nose cap subcomponents and their interactions during the test.

A B S T R A C T
Eckel et al. first reported a novel ceramic 3D printing technique in 2016 [19], which combines the advantages of ceramic precursors and AM technology, opening a new path for 3D printing of ceramics. The preparation of UHTC materials by the precursor conversion method can avoid the sample defects caused by the traditional forming and sintering technology such as powder pressing and sintering at high temperatures to a certain extent, and can realize the regulation of the composition and properties of UHTC materials at the molecular level. There is great potential for application in AM of UHTCs by the methods. Compared with other additive manufacturing techniques such as selective laser melting (SLM) [20], selective laser sintering (SLS) [21,22], material extrusion (MEX) [23] and binder jetting (BJ) [24], the digital light processing (DLP)  [25]  technology  provides  higher  precision  and  better  forming ability, and has therefore gradually become an ideal process for fabricating AM ceramics. Precursors are very suitable for light curing 3D printing due to their excellent formability, and ceramic precursors such as polycarbosilane, polysiloxane, and polysilazane are currently more and  more  used  in  3D  printing  [26,27].  By  using  liquid  casting,  soft lithography and nano stereolithography, Zanchetta et al. realized the preparation of high-precision micro-sized precursor ceramics, and the prepared SiOC porous ceramic microelements have a support size of about  200 μ m  [28].  Hazan  et  al.  used  allyl  hydrogenated  polycarbosilanes (AHPCS) and multifunctional acrylates to prepare UV-curable resins which were then photocured upon 3D printing. The printed raw material was pyrolyzed at 1300 ◦ C to obtain SiC-rich ceramics with complex structure [26]. However, due to the stronger UV absorption of non-oxide ceramic powders than for oxide ceramics, and the larger difference in refractive index with resin, it is challenging to use  DLP  technology  to  form  Zr-based  UHTCs.  At  present,  non-oxide ceramic  materials  for  DLP  are  coated  with  low

2.1. Preparation of porous YB2C2  ceramics
Commercially  available  Y2O3  powders  (100  mesh,  Changsha  Tijo Metal Material Co. Ltd.,  Changsha,  China),  B4C  powders (200  mesh, Jingangzuan Materials Co. Ltd., Jinzhou, China), and graphite powders (100 mesh, Qingdao Tianyuan Graphite Co. Ltd., Qingdao, China) were used as raw materials. These were mixed in a molar ratio of 0.7:0.8:4.2 for  10 h  in  a  polypropylene  jar  containing  agate  balls,  and  absolute ethanol was used as medium. Subsequently, the mixture was dried and sieved.
As depicted in Fig. 1, the process of preparing porous YB2C2 ceramics consists of the preparation of feedstocks, printing, debinding, and sintering.  Firstly,  the  mixed  raw  powders  and  polymer  binders -comprising stearic acid (SA), paraffin wax (PW), polyethylene glycol (PEG), low-density polyethylene (LDPE), and high-density polyethylene (HDPE) -were  stirred  in  a  mixer  at  165 ◦ C  for  5 h.  And  then  the resultant  mixture  was  pelletized  as  feedstocks  after  cooling.  Before printing, a G-code file was generated by the slicing software (UPRISE 3D, 3.39.81) according to the 3D model data. The printing platform and cavity temperatures of the PEP printer (UPRISE-CR502, China) were set at 85 ◦ C and 165 ◦ C, respectively. The nozzle diameter was specified as 0.6 mm, and the printing route direction interval was set at 90 ◦ for each layer.  The  printing  parameters  were  optimized  using  the  orthogonal experimental method, and the layer thickness ( d ), nozzle travel speed ( v ), and flow rate ( f ) were systematically investigated with four levels for each factor, as shown in Table 1.

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are promising candidates for thermal structures in extreme environments  requiring  exceptional  resistance  to  temperatures  above  2000 ◦ C.  Processing  these  materials  via  vat photopolymerization-based  additive  manufacturing  remains  unattainable,  one  major  obstacle  is  the  high absorbance  of  conventional  ceramic  powders.  This  study  introduces  an  innovative  solution  utilizing  lowabsorbance ZrB2  precursors. The results indicate that carbon sources significantly influence the absorbance of precursors,  with  sorbitol-derived  precursors  exhibiting  notably  lower  absorbance  values  (0.16)  at  405  nm compared to pure ZrB2  powders (0.83). The corresponding photosensitive slurries demonstrate favorable photocuring behavior, facilitating successful additive manufacturing processes. Post-processing through carbonization  and  boro/carbothermal  reduction  yields  ZrB2-based  ceramics  with  high  mechanical  strength  and  low thermal  conductivity,  similar  to  the  properties  of  most  porous  UHTCs  produced  by  conventional  methods. Notably, the printed gyroid-shaped components display mechanical properties comparable to those of conventionally manufactured simple-shaped ones. This approach holds potential for extending the production capabilities to other types of UHTCs.

question: Comparing porous YB2C2 ceramics at about 50% porosity fabricated by Yang et al. 2025 with ZrB2-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites after 200 thermal shock cycles fabricated by Huang et al. 2016: which material system reports the higher room-temperature strength value?
answer: The sources provided do not mention the room-temperature strength of ZrB2-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites after 200 thermal shock cycles fabricated by Huang et al. 2016. However, the porous YB2C2 ceramics at about 50% porosity fabricated by Yang et al. 2025 are reported to have compressive strengths that exceed the values reported by Chen et al. for partially sintered porous YB2C2 in the 34.78 -9.32 MPa range [2]. Since the question is about room-temperature strength and the specific value for the ZrB2-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites is not provided in the sources, it is not possible to compare the two materials based on the given information.

3.3. Compressive behavior of porous YB2C2  samples
Finally,  benchmarking  against  other  porous  UHTCs,  as  shown  in Fig. 15 and Table 5, highlights the competitiveness of porous YB2C2. The expected decline of compressive strength with porosity is observed, yet our  PEP  plus in-situ boro/carbothermal reduction sintering  route  delivers  substantially  higher  strengths  at  comparable  porosities.  For example, at 57 -75 % porosity, the strengths reported by Chen et al. for partially  sintered  porous  YB2C2 fall  in  the  34.78 -9.32 MPa  range, whereas the present samples exceed these values over the same porosity window [38]. 1. Introduction
However,  excessive  sintering  additives  would  reduce  the  hightemperature properties of the as-sintered ZrB2-based UHTCs [26 -30]. Especially, ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs, as the fact that the yield stress of ZrSi2 monotonously decreases to less than 50 MPa with the increasing temperature up to 1500 ◦ C [26]. Previous literature [31] reported that 7.4 vol% ZrSi2-assisted sintered ZrB2  ceramics exhibited a strength reduction from 400 MPa at room temperature (RT) to 220 MPa at 1400 ◦ C. Although few ultra-high-temperature strength ( > 1600 ◦ C) of ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was reported, it is foreseeable that the strength of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics would decrease  to  an  extremely  low  value  at  ultra-high-temperatures.  In contrast,  SiC  or  ZrC-assisted  sintered  ZrB2 ceramics  have  superior high-temperature mechanical properties. For example, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC ceramic sintered at 1900 ◦ C was 348 MPa [15]. The 1800 ◦ C-flexural  strength  of  ZrB2-15  vol%  SiC  ceramics  sintered  at 2100 -2170 ◦ C  could  reach  474  MPa  [13].  Although  the  ZrB2-based UHTCs  without  ZrSi2 sintering  additive  (e.g.  ZrB2 -SiC,  ZrB2 -ZrC, ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC)  exhibit  excellent  high-temperature  strength,  these  ceramics  require  extremely  high  sintering  temperature  (mostly  higher than  1900 ◦ C)  to  prepare  highly  dense  ceramics. KEYWORDS
In recent years, slurry injection technology has demonstrated potential for shortening process cycles in SI by directly introducing high-solid-content ceramic slurries into fiber preforms. 15-18 According to the study by Liu et al., 19 the process was optimized through the injection of ZrB2 -phenolic resin slurry, achieving a density of 2.10 ± 0.05 g/cm 3 and porosity of approximately 40%. However, subsequent densification processes are required after slurry injection into preforms. Venkatachalam et al. 20 fabricated C f -ZrB 2 -Cm composites using slurry injection combined with radio-frequency-assisted CVI, achieving 90% relative density for C f -ZrB 2 within 24 h. Under oxyacetylene ablation, the composites exhibited mass ablation rate of 1.7 mg/s and linear ablation rate of 1.6 µ m/safter 60 s (peak temperature ∼ 2600 K). However, residual 10% porosity in the 2.5D carbon fiber preform, caused by complex pore structures and CVI process limitations, significantly reduced ablation performance. Yang et al. 21 obtained C/ZrB2-SiC with 86% relative density and 215 MPa flexural strength using 30 vol.% ZrB 2 slurry injection combined with 12 PIP cycles. Hu et al. 22 achieved a 286 MPa flexural strength for Cf /ZrC-SiC through eight PIP cycles. Although PIP enables gradient distribution control of matrix phases, its requirement for multiple cycles (typically > 10) extends fabrication time to hundreds of hours. 23 RMI rapidly produces high-density ceramic matrix composites through molten metal reactions with preforms. It is particularly suitable for complex-shaped components, having demonstrated efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and high application potential. 24,25

1. Introduction
ZrB2-based composites have an extremely high melting point (>3000 /C176 C), high thermal and electrical conductivities, low density, and excellent mechanical and chemical stability at high temperatures [1-4]. Recently, ZrB2-based ceramic composites have been considered as potential candidates for application in critical thermal protection systems and other components of future hypersonic aircraft or re-entry vehicles [1-7]. Silicon carbide (SiC) is commonly used as an additive to improve the oxidation resistance and mechanical properties of ZrB2. ZrB2-20 vol% SiC particulate composites (ZS) are regarded as the baseline material in this research. However, unsatisfactory thermal shock resistance remains the obstacle to the development of ZrB2-SiC ceramic on an industrial scale. To avoid or mitigate catastrophic failure of ZrB2SiC ceramics during thermal shock, most current researches focus on composition design to improve the performance by selecting different additives [8-12]. Carbon is also sometimes used as an additive to enhance thermal shock resistance [12-14]. Recently, Levine et al. [13] studied the mechanical properties of ZrB214 vol% SiC-30 vol% C. They concluded that the addition of carbon could reduce strength but increases fracture toughness. Hong et al. [14] fabricated ZrB2-SiC-graphite composite, which exhibits increased thermal shock resistance compared with monolithic ZrB2-SiC. The toughness and hence the reliability and thermal

Densification Behavior
The sintering behavior of the ZrB2-ZrC-SiC composite, C system, is similar to the one observed for A system. In both the powder mixtures belonging to A and C systems, the sintering temperature of the SPS process is about 200 1 C higher than the HP temperature (Table I). Details for the densification behavior of materials fabricated in system B are reported in a previous work. 16
Concerning the ZrB2-MoSi2, B system, the SPS process allows a shrinkage rate six times higher than the HP one and reduced sintering time. Differently from systems A and C, the final sintering temperature to achieve high final densities is the same for the SPS and HP techniques.

[14], offer limited control over pore architecture and microstructural uniformity. Soto et al. prepared porous SiC via sacrificial templating, but achieved  only  poorly  distributed  porosity  and  a  low  compressive strength  ( ≤ 30 MPa),  with  fracture  propagation  driven  by  stress  concentration at randomly distributed pores [10]. Increased porosity typically induces an exponential decrease in strength due to the reduction in load-bearing cross-sectional area and accelerated microcrack growth. Furthermore,  high-temperature  sintering  of  SiC  often  triggers β to α phase transformation, abnormal grain coarsening, and the loss of grain boundary  strengthening, collectively intensifying brittleness [11]. Similarly, replica templating has yielded TiN foams with 90 % porosity but only ~0.75 MPa compressive strength [12], while direct foaming has  produced  Si3N4  foams  (90.8 -96.4 %  porosity)  with  strengths  of merely 0.37 -5.53 MPa [13]. Moreover, the intrinsic sintering resistance and tendency for abnormal grain coarsening in UHTC powders further exacerbate the difficulty of achieving dense, defect-free ceramics with desired  pore  structures  [15 -17]. In-situ boro/carbothermal  reduction sintering offers improved densification kinetics through reaction-driven phase evolution, as demonstrated by Wang et al. in fabricating porous ZrC-SiC  [18].  Wen et  al. in  synthesizing  nine-cation  high-entropy diboride  foams  [19].  Li et  al. synthesized  TiB2  powder  via  the  boro/carbothermal reduction method using TiO2, B4C, and C as precursors [20]. Nevertheless, it remains challenging to fabricate components with complex external shapes and designed internal architectures using this method alone.

3.3. High-temperature flexural strength
Fig. 7 shows the 1800 ◦ C-flexural strength versus sintering temperature of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics with and without C dopant, compared with several typical ZrB2  and HfB2-based UHTCs. In the previous studies, in order to obtain superior high-temperature properties, some refractory materials have been considered to be added into the matrix. The most widely  used  refractory  phases  were  ZrC  [15,40]  and  SiC  [13,38].  In addition, high-temperature resistant phases such as WC [41 -43], B4C [44],  Mo2C  [45]  and  SiB6 [46]  were  also  considered.  For  superior strength at ultra-high temperatures, it is generally not recommended to employ sintering additives with low melting point. Consequently, the sintering temperature of these reported ZrB2-based UHTCs with superior high-temperature strength typically exceeds 1800 ◦ C, resulting in significant energy expenditure. In contrast, introducing sintering additives with low melting point into ZrB2 ceramics can significantly reduce the densification  temperature,  however  the  residual  lower  melting  point phases would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs (e.g., the strength of ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2 at 1800 ◦ C is only 30 MPa). In the current work, active carbon was added into the ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic exhibited excellent flexural strength (390 MPa) at 1800 ◦ C, surpassing that of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC [15,40], ZrB2-30 vol% SiC [38], HfB2-20 vol% SiB6  [46], and HfB2-5 vol% SiC-5 vol% WC [41]. Meanwhile, the densification temperature of C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2

3.1 Microstructure and properties at different infiltration temperatures
As shown in Table 1, the Cf /ZrB 2 -SiC composites fabricated at different infiltration temperatures exhibited variations in bulk density and open porosity. Driven by capillary forces, molten Si infiltrated the internal pores of the preforms and reacted with resin-derived carbon derived from phenolic resin. The infiltration efficiency was governed by the temperature-dependent interplay between Si fluidity and reaction kinetics: elevated temperatures ( > 1500 ◦ C) accelerated Si-C reactions, leading to premature pore clogging, while lower temperatures ( < 1500 ◦ C) hindered Si mobility due to increased viscosity. Optimal densification was achieved at 1500 ◦ C, where the composites displayed the lowest open porosity (6.87%) and highest bulk density (3.03 g/cm 3 ). This critical temperature effectively balanced the competing mechanisms of molten Si viscosity and reaction kinetics, enabling efficient pore filling and minimal residual porosity.
FIGURE 2 X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of C f /ZrB2 -SiC composites prepared at different infiltration temperatures.
Figure 2 shows the XRD patterns of C f /ZrB 2 -SiC composites prepared at different infiltration temperatures. The primary phases identified include ZrB 2 (PDF #340423), Si (PDF #80-0018), and SiC (PDF #73-1665). The SiC phase resulted from reactions between molten Si and resin-derived carbon derived from phenolic resin. The composites fabricated at 1500 ◦ C exhibited intense SiC diffraction peaks, consistent with their high densification and elevated SiC content. Although SiC peak intensity at 1600 ◦ C surpassed that at 1450 ◦ C, the lack of discernible C diffraction peaks suggests significant degradation and consumption of carbon fibers at this temperature. Additionally, residual Si diffraction peaks was observed in the 1450 ◦ C sample due to incomplete infiltration.

a b s t r a c t
For the sake of reliability and safety of high temperature structural components of future hypersonic aircraft or re-entry vehicles, the strength of ZrB2-based composites at elevated temperatures is an important factor for applications in extreme environments. However, the study of high temperature properties has been mostly limited to oxidation behavior, an area which is also well understood [7,15-17]. Very few studies focus on high temperature mechanical properties [18-21]. Neuman et al. reported the flexure strength of ZrB2 with 0.5 wt% carbon to be 216 MPa at 2300 /C176 C in argon [18]. Bellosi et al. [19] fabricated ZrB2-10 vol% SiC ceramics with or without ZrC additions by using spark plasma sintering (SPS) and studied the strengths at 1500 /C176 C in argon or air atmosphere. The flexural strength of the ZrB2-10 vol% SiC sample tested at 1500 /C176 C did not decrease appreciably compared with the values at room temperature. The favorable effects were mainly attributed to the clean grain boundaries in the as-SPSed samples. Zou et al. [20] reported the high-temperature bending strength, internal friction, and stiffness of ZrB2-20 vol% SiC ceramics up to 1600 /C176 C in high purity argon. The high temperature bend strength of ZS ceramics started to decrease above 1000 /C176 C, whereas E -modulus decreased with the increase of the temperature. The bend strength at 1600 /C176 C was 460 ± 31 MPa, and E -modulus at 1300 /C176 C was 443.5 GPa. The corresponding strength and E modulus retention was 84% and 88%, respectively. Hu and Wang [21] reported that the flexural strength and fracture behavior of the ZrB2-SiC composites at ultra-high temperature significantly depends on the grain size and SiC content. The flexural strength of the ZrB2-15 vol%

question: What electrical resistivity per unit dimension does the Li/Ce-doped La2Ti2O7:0.2MnO2 ceramic retain when exposed to 1000 degrees Celsius?
answer: The Li/Ce-doped La2Ti2O7:0.2MnO2 ceramic retains an electrical resistivity of ~2.4 × 10^7 Ω·cm when exposed to 1000 degrees Celsius [3].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
In the industry of high-temperature sensors and actuators, materials capable of delivering high ferroelectric, dielectric,  and  specifically  stable/high  piezoelectric  performances  above  1000 ◦ C  are  in  demand.  Hence, perovskite-like layered structured (PLS) have gained popularity due to their high Curie temperature ( TC ) and ferroelectric properties, but the low piezoelectric coefficient ( d33 ) at high temperature ( > 1000 ◦ C) is the problem statement to be explored and improved. Herein this research, La1.97(LiCe)0.03Ti2O7:xwt%MnO2  (LLCTO:xMn) with  x = 0 -0.3  ceramic  series  has  been  explored  to  study  the  ultra-high  structural,  ferroelectric,  electric, dielectric, and piezoelectric properties. Among all compositions, LLCTO:0.2Mn ceramic has demonstrated ultrahigh performances with remnant polarization ( Pr ) of ~1.84 μ C/cm 2 , piezoelectric co-efficient ( d33 ) of 9 pC/N, resistivity of ~10 11 Ω .cm, relative dielectric constant ( ε r ) of 46, and minor dielectric loss (tan δ ) of 0.17 which are much improved compared to pure La2Ti2O7  (LTO) and pristine La1.97(LiCe)0.03Ti2O7  (LLCTO) ceramics. The LLCTO:0.2Mn ceramic has exhibited the high TC of 1415 ◦ C. Moreover, thermally stable multifunctional performances are measured where LLCTO:0.2Mn ceramic has demonstrated a high d33 of ~8.5 pC/N at 1200 ◦ C and resistivity  of  ~2.4 × 10 7 Ω .cm  even  at  1000 ◦ C,  which  are  much  better  than  the  earlier  reports.  From  the analysis, LLCTO:0.2Mn ceramic has demonstrated the potential to be utilized in high-temperature ( > 1000 ◦ C) piezoelectric devices.

A B S T R A C T
La2Ti2O7 (LTO) ceramics, ideal for ultra-high temperature applications with a Curie temperature near 1460 ◦ C, were previously limited by a low piezoelectric coefficient ( d 33) of 1.5 pC/N. In this work, the incorporation of Bi 3 + and V 5 + dopants have been employed to augment the piezoelectric properties of LTO. An enhanced d 33 ~ 4.0 pC/N is achieved in La1.91Bi0.09Ti1.97V0.03O7 (LBTV9) ceramics, while maintaining an ultra-high Curie temperature of 1437 ◦ C. This improvement is attributed to local heterogeneity in the lattice structure induced by the doping. LBTV9 ceramics also exhibit excellent high-temperature stability and insulation properties, maintaining a d 33 at 4.0 pC/N after annealing at 1000 ◦ C for 48 h and stabilizing at 4.2 pC/N across temperatures from 200 ◦ C to 1600 ◦ C, with a DC resistivity of 1.7 × 10 6 Ω⋅ cm at 1000 ◦ C. These enhancements make LBTV9 ceramics suitable for extreme condition applications, thus unlocking new possibilities for their use in ultra-high temperature piezoelectric devices.
applications in extremely high-temperature environments. As is well known, the higher the Curie temperature of a material, the lower its piezoelectric coefficient ( d 33). The challenge for LTO is its relatively low piezoelectric properties with the d 33 only around 1.5 pC/N. Therefore, the research focus on LTO ceramics is to enhance its electrical properties. Notably, due to the prolonged operation at ultra-high temperatures, the crystal structure and electrical properties of materials may be influenced by the elevated temperatures. Therefore, piezoelectric ceramics applied in ultra-high temperature environments require excellent temperature stability, which is a key factor to ensure that their performance remains unaffected by temperature changes [11].

A B S T R A C T
MnO2 has been significantly studied worldwide due to its multivalence and several polymorphic structures in the fields of renewable energy  storage  devices  [15,16],  ceramics  for  improving  piezoelectric properties [17], and solar cells to improve their stability [18]. Mn has the unique properties to achieve multivalence nature at different sintering temperatures, making it an ideal candidate to occupy the A- or Bsite vacancies or substitutions. Due to the acceptor nature of Mn, its ionic radii are suitable to replace Ti 4 + at the B-site, ideally to cause the oxygen vacancies at perovskite lattices and create the soft and hard effects for ferroelectric and piezoelectric properties [19]. Over the years, scientists have explored the potential of the Mn-ions for various ceramic compositions and achieved success in improving the ferroelectric and piezoelectric performances. Following the concept from our previous studies, we  have  scratched  the  ideal  composition  of  La1.97Li0.015Ce0.015Ti2O7 ceramics with the addition of suitable MnO2 content to analyze the influence and reported the significant improvement in piezoelectric and ferroelectric properties, suitable for high-performance applications.
In  this  study,  Li/Ce  dual  doped  La1.97Li0.015Ce0.015Ti2O7  ceramic composition has further been explored under the influence of multivalence Mn-ions as La1.97Li0.015Ce0.015Ti2O7:xwt%MnO2  (x = 0 -0.3) ceramics (LLCTO:xMn) to improve their ferroelectric, dielectric, resistive, and  piezoelectric  properties.  LLCTO:0.2Mn  ceramic  composition  has demonstrated ideal results with Pr ~ 1.84 μ C/cm 2 , d33 of 9 pC/N, and resistivity  of  ~10 11 Ω .cm  at  room  temperature.  These  properties remained very stable even at the varying temperature ( > 1000 ◦ C) with d33 of  ~8.5 pC/N at 1200 ◦ C and resistivity of ~2.4 × 10 7 Ω .cm at 1000 ◦ C.  Such  revolutionized  outcomes  confirm  the  potential  of  the composition for high-temperature piezoelectric applications.

A B S T R A C T
The study conducts an in-depth analysis of the role of the crystal phase and particle size of TiO2  in the preparation  of  La2Ti2O7 (LTO)  ceramics  via  the  conventional  solid-state  method.  Comparative  studies  on  crystal structure, micromorphology, electrical properties and mechanical properties were performed, revealing that the selection of TiO2  raw materials greatly influences the structure and performance of LTO ceramics, resulting in significant differences in the grain sizes, electrical properties, and mechanical properties of the ceramics. Among the various TiO2  sources, the LTO ceramics synthesized with nano-rutile TiO2  exhibit superior overall performance. The smaller grain size of 1.43 nm for the ceramic and higher relative density of 98.27 %, significantly enhance their electrical and mechanical properties. The piezoelectric constant is 1.5 pC/N at room temperature and 1.4 pC/N after annealing at 1000 ◦ C for 48 h. The high Curie temperature with 1450 ◦ C, high resistivity with 3.93 × 10 6 Ω cm at 600 ◦ C, high hardness of 9.89 GPa and small change in elastic modulus make it excellent candidates for high-temperature electronic component applications.

A B S T R A C T
Currently, the improvement in electrical performance of LTO is primarily achieved through advancements in fabrication techniques and doping strategies. For instance, LTO treated under high pressure demonstrates a notable improvement in its piezoelectric constant, reaching 4.7 pC/N [12]. The application of Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) to produce La1.85Ce0.15Ti2O7 ceramic was reported, resulting in a d 33 of 3.9 pC/N [13]. A study on textured ceramics using SPS produced Bi0.1La1.9Ti2O7, achieving a d 33 of 3.6 pC/N [14]. Nevertheless, the elevated costs associated with these approaches make them less viable for industrial-scale applications. This economic challenge highlights the advantages of solid-state methods. Using a solid-state method to dope Ce ions into LTO ceramics show a d 33 of 3.6 pC/N[15], while doping V ions
*
into LTO ceramics achieves a higher d 33 of 4.8 pC/N [16]. However, the resistivity of these materials remains insufficiently high. For La1.95Ce0.05Ti2O7 ceramics, the resistivity stands at 1.2 × 10 6 Ω⋅ cm at 650 ◦ C [15], whereas La2Ti1.96V0.04O7 ceramics show a resistivity of 1.3 × 10 6 Ω⋅ cm at 600 ◦ C [16]. Therefore, further research is essential for LTO ceramics to achieve applications under extreme high-temperature conditions.
In this work, LTO ceramics doped with Bi 3 + at A-site and V 5 + at B-site were designed and prepared using the conventional solid-state sintering method. The primary objective is to enhance the electrical performance of LTO ceramics while maintaining high-temperature stability. The prepared ceramics exhibited significantly improved electrical properties and remarkable thermal stability. Systematic characterization and analysis were conducted to elucidate the effects and underlying mechanisms of Bi 3 + and V 5 + co-doping on the performance of LTO ceramics.

question: How does the introduction of manganese in the La1.97Li0.015Ce0.015Ti2O7:0.2MnO2 system influence the switching behavior of electric domains within the crystal lattice?
answer: The introduction of manganese in the La1.97Li0.015Ce0.015Ti2O7:0.2MnO2 system influences the switching behavior of electric domains within the crystal lattice by acting as a soft agent for the LLCTO host counterpart, which varies the charge domain mobility resulting in excessive domain switching in the ceramics [2].

A B S T R A C T
MnO2 has been significantly studied worldwide due to its multivalence and several polymorphic structures in the fields of renewable energy  storage  devices  [15,16],  ceramics  for  improving  piezoelectric properties [17], and solar cells to improve their stability [18]. Mn has the unique properties to achieve multivalence nature at different sintering temperatures, making it an ideal candidate to occupy the A- or Bsite vacancies or substitutions. Due to the acceptor nature of Mn, its ionic radii are suitable to replace Ti 4 + at the B-site, ideally to cause the oxygen vacancies at perovskite lattices and create the soft and hard effects for ferroelectric and piezoelectric properties [19]. Over the years, scientists have explored the potential of the Mn-ions for various ceramic compositions and achieved success in improving the ferroelectric and piezoelectric performances. Following the concept from our previous studies, we  have  scratched  the  ideal  composition  of  La1.97Li0.015Ce0.015Ti2O7 ceramics with the addition of suitable MnO2 content to analyze the influence and reported the significant improvement in piezoelectric and ferroelectric properties, suitable for high-performance applications.
In  this  study,  Li/Ce  dual  doped  La1.97Li0.015Ce0.015Ti2O7  ceramic composition has further been explored under the influence of multivalence Mn-ions as La1.97Li0.015Ce0.015Ti2O7:xwt%MnO2  (x = 0 -0.3) ceramics (LLCTO:xMn) to improve their ferroelectric, dielectric, resistive, and  piezoelectric  properties.  LLCTO:0.2Mn  ceramic  composition  has demonstrated ideal results with Pr ~ 1.84 μ C/cm 2 , d33 of 9 pC/N, and resistivity  of  ~10 11 Ω .cm  at  room  temperature.  These  properties remained very stable even at the varying temperature ( > 1000 ◦ C) with d33 of  ~8.5 pC/N at 1200 ◦ C and resistivity of ~2.4 × 10 7 Ω .cm at 1000 ◦ C.  Such  revolutionized  outcomes  confirm  the  potential  of  the composition for high-temperature piezoelectric applications.

3. Results and discussion
~1.84 μ C/cm 2 and Pmax of ~4.75 μ C/cm 2 (Fig. 3d), which is maximum of the till date reported ferroelectric behavior of LTO-based ceramics at 150 kV/cm. The LLCTO:0.1Mn, LLCTO:0.2Mn, and LLCTO:0.3Mn ceramics have exhibited well saturated P-E loops at 150 kV/cm and 1 Hz. The P-E loop  of  pure  LTO  ceramic  has  not  been  fully  polarized;  its behavior is consistent with the previous reports (Fig. 3a), while pristine LLCTO  ceramic  has  demonstrated  better  polarization  even  at  high electric fields, consistent with the literature (Fig. 3b) [14]. The Pmax and Pr values of LLCTO:0.1Mn (Fig. 3c), and LLCTO:0.3Mn (Fig. 3e) ceramics are  2.89 μ C/cm 2 ,  4.22 μ C/cm 2 and  1.087 μ C/cm 2 ,  1.73 μ C/cm 2 , respectively, which are much lower than the Pr and Pmax values of the LLCTO:0.2Mn ceramics. Increment in the ferroelectric behavior from LLCTO ceramic to LLCTO:0.2Mn ceramics (Fig. 3f) is associated with the Mn additive, which possesses the ability to act as a soft agent for LLCTO host counterpart and varies the charge domain mobility resulting the excessive domain switching in the ceramics [22]. While the decrement in the Pr and Pmax values for the LLCT:0.3Mn ceramic composition is related to the percolation threshold value of the MnO2 additive, which is the specific content of the additive, where its diffusion limit at the lattice spaces reaches and excessive additives ions start to accumulate at the grain boundaries, resulting in variation in the conductive nature of the ceramics in the form of leakage current and causing the degradation in ferroelectric properties [23].

4. Conclusion
The conventional solid-state reaction method has been employed for the fabrication of La1.97(LiCe)0.015Ti2O7:xwt%MnO2 (LLCTO:xMn) with x = 0 -0.3 ceramics to measure their ferroelectric, piezoelectric, dielectric,  and  electric  properties.  LLCTO:0.2Mn ceramic has demonstrated the best merits, with the highest Pr of ~1.84 μ C/cm 2 , d33 of 9 pC/N, resistivity of ~10 11 Ω .cm, the lowest leakage current of 1.57 × 10 GLYPH<0> 9 A, ε r of 46, and minute tan δ of 0.17, much improved as compared to pure LTO ceramic  and  pristine  LLCTO  ceramic.  To  analyze  the  ability  of  the LLCTO:0.2Mn  ceramics  utilization  in  high-temperature  piezoelectric properties, thermal stability of d33 is observed. LLCTO:0.2Mn ceramic has shown the d33 of ~8.5 pC/N at 1200 ◦ C (5% less than room temperature value), tan δ of < 1 at 1200 ◦ C. and resistivity of ~2.4 × 10 7 Ω . cm even at 1000 ◦ C. Such outstanding outcomes of the LLCTO:0.2Mn ceramic  are  clearly  associated  with  its  high TC of  1415 ◦ C.  This comprehensive analysis of the LLCTO:0.2Mn ceramic confirms its potential  for  next-generation  high-temperature  ferroelectric  and  piezoelectric devices.

A B S T R A C T
it shows a high TC of 1461 ◦ C [10]. In 2009, Yan et al., reported the high resistivity of ~10 6 Ω .cm even at the high temperature of 700 ◦ C, and textured LTO exhibited a d33 of 2.6 pC/N [11]. Such low piezoelectric response is associated with the difficulty of poling the ceramics, and such high operating temperature range can also cause the high leakage current, resulting in a significant drop in the ferroelectric/piezoelectric response.  But  such  behaviors  have  opened  up  the  window  for  researchers to explore this material to improve its ferroelectric and d33 values.  Doping/addition  of  suitable  elements  at  A  or  B  sites  with improved fabrication parameters are the key features to enhance d33 value of the LTO ceramics. Hence, Li et al., have reported the improved d33 of 3.6 pC/N in Bi0.1La1.9Ti2O7 textured ceramic [12]. Later on, Xiong et al., applied the high-pressure sintering treatment to the LTO ceramics to improve the d33 value up to 4.7 pC/N [13]. Recently, Tang et al., have prepared the La2-x(LiCe)x/2Ti2O7  ceramic series via solid-state reaction method, where La1.97Li0.015Ce0.015Ti2O7 ceramic composition has demonstrated the high d33 value of 6.4 pC/N [14].

3. Results and discussion
To further substantiate the perspective that local heterogeneity caused by doping, the lattice structures of pristine LTO and doped LTO samples were observed. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of LBTV0 and LBTV9 ceramics are shown in Fig. 4(a) and (b), respectively. The SAED image of LBTV0 suggests a highly ordered structure with distinct spots. However, the LBTV9 presents some slight blurring spots, demonstrating that Bi 3 + and V 5 + ions doping has introduced some degree of disorder into the crystal lattice. Therefore, highresolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was utilized to observe the microstructural of samples and depicted in Fig. 4(c) and (d). LBTV0 presents a clear layered structure with an equal crystal plane spacing of 1.29 nm. Although LBTV9 presents a layered structure as well, the interplanar spacing is not uniform in the region. Notably, the area marked with a red elliptical curve exhibits conspicuous point defects, further proving that dopant ions have penetrated the lattice of LBTV0 and caused local heterogeneity, as inferred from our above calculations. For piezoelectric ceramics, local heterogeneity plays a pivotal role in enhancing performance by introducing microstructural variations, which are essential for optimizing polarization and electromechanical responses [22,23]. The dynamic interplay between local polar order and disorder contributes to a more adaptable and responsive polarization under the influence of an external electric field [24 -27]. The strong fluctuations of polarization vectors at the nanoscale caused by local heterogeneity, and the rapid response of the resulting polarization clusters under an external electric field [28], can significantly enhance the piezoelectric performance of ceramics. In addition, some sub-micron domains were observed in Fig. 4(e) and (f). The sub-micron domain size of LBTV9 is larger than that of LBTV0, which is consistent with the previous results of increasing domain size by ion doping in PFM.

question: What minimum thermal decomposition point characterizes the class of materials known as ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs)?
answer: The minimum thermal decomposition point characterizing the class of materials known as ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) is a melting point exceeding 3000 ◦ C [1].

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  a  class  of  ceramics whose  melting  point  exceeds  3000 ◦ C  [1].  These  materials  provide various  benefits,  including  a  remarkably  high  melting  point  and exceptional  performance  at  high  temperatures.  Consequently,  these exceptional characteristics make UHTCs well-suited for diverse applications in demanding environments [2,3].
In ultra-high temperature ceramics, carbide ceramics are considered ideal materials for fabricating high-temperature structural components for hypersonic aircraft due to their high melting points and excellent thermal-physical properties [4,5]. HfC and TaC exhibit a face-centered cubic (FCC) crystal structure, with lattice constants of 0.3289 nm for HfC and 0.3169 nm for TaC, respectively. The atomic radii of Hf (0.1585 nm) and Ta (0.1457 nm) displays a difference of < 15%, allowing the formation of solid solutions known as Ta1-xHfxC. These solutions are notable  for  their  excellent  high-temperature  performance  [6,7].  According to the reports, when x = 0.2,  Ta1-xHfxC  exhibits  the  highest known melting point (4300 K) [8]. Additionally, Ta1-xHfxC solid solutions possess exceptional mechanical properties [9,10], making them widely attractive.
Currently,  three  main  methods  are  used  for  preparing  Ta1-xHfxC

1. Introduction
Nowadays, a critical area of research revolves around the exploration of materials  capable of resisting extreme  conditions. Ultra-hightemperature ceramics (UHTCs) represent a class of refractory ceramics encompassing carbides and borides of early transition metals, characterized by a melting point above 3000 ◦ C [1,2]. These materials exhibit a high  thermal  conductivity  and  exceptional  resistance  to  ablation  in harsh environments. The most studied UHTCs are based on zirconium diboride  for  the  relatively  low  density,  elevated  melting  point,  high strength, and good electrical and thermal conductivity [3,4]. While ZrB2 benefits from these properties, it also suffers from poor fracture toughness  constraining  its  application  in  scenarios  involving  mechanical stresses or thermal shock [5]. To enhance the damage tolerance of the UHTCs, reinforcement with short or long fibres have been used [6 -9], resulting in the development of ultra-high-temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs). A key challenge for UHTCMCs is oxidation at high  temperatures,  particularly  in  oxygen-rich  environments.  The oxidation of ZrB ₂ -based UHTCMCs occurs through the formation of ZrO ₂ and B ₂ O ₃ , where the latter can volatilize at temperatures above 1200 ◦ C, reducing the protective effect of the oxide layer [10]. The addition of different  species  such  as  SiC  as  a  secondary  phase  is  commonly employed to improve oxidation resistance by forming a viscous borosilicate glass with a low vapour pressure, which helps to seal the surface
* Corresponding author. E-mail address:

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  endowed  with  a  high melting point ( > 3000 ◦ C) [1] are classified as borides, carbides, and nitrides of transition metal elements from group IVB and VB [1 -3]. The recent advancement in the UHTCs has renewed its focus by employing high entropy alloys (HEAs) in metals, leading to new vast materials in the  compositional  space  [4,5].  These  new  materials  are  typically  a combination of four or five UHTCs in equimolar composition. Due to mutual solubility, these materilas favor complete solid-solution single phase  formation  exhibiting  FCC  or  BCC  structure.  This  new  class  of multi-component  UHTCs  is  often  called  high-entropy  UHTCs  (HEUHTCs) [6]. However, the authors favor referring to this class of UHTCs as  multi-component  UHTCs  (MC-UHTCs).  Because  these  MC-UHTCs elicit  a  single-phase  due  to  their  mutual  solubility  leading  to  solidsolution formation not necessarily having ' high entropy ' [2,7,8].
MC-UHTCs are considered attractive materials for thermal protection systems (TPS) of structural components such as hot structures and
* Corresponding author. E-mail address: agarwala@fiu.edu (A. Agarwal).

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are a class of ceramic refractory materials interesting for their high melting point (> 3200 °C), chemical stability and resistance to erosion and ablation. For this reason, carbides and borides, such as ZrB2, TiB2, HfB2, ZrC, HfC, TaC, are potential candidates components that work in extreme environment condition; thermal protection systems, leading edges in hypersonic /uniFB02 ights and nozzles for rockets [6 -8]. Among the others, ZrB2 has the lowest density, high strength-to-weight ratio and good oxidation resistance if combined with carbides such as B4C, SiC or metal silicides such as MoSi2 [8,9]. On the other hand, like the others UHTCs, it is susceptible to brittle fracture (KIC < 5 MPa m 1/2 ) and displays low thermal shock resistance. It is well known that monolithic ceramics are not toughened by promoting plasticity, but they can be toughened up to 10 MPa m 1/2 by the frictional interlocking of grains (grain bridging) during intergranular fracture [10]. Since UHTCs cannot exploit in situ phase transformations, glassy /uniFB01 lms, and have not any ferroic order to allow the domains switch at the coercive stress at crack tip, the main strategy to (i) enhance the intergranular fracture [11] rather than the transgranular crack propagation, and (ii) increase the tortuous crack path was the development of large elongated or plate-like grains [12]. In the last years, renewed research e /uniFB00 orts improved the damage-tolerance of ZrB2 by moving from ceramics to Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs). The /uniFB01 rst CMCs enabled a toughness increase by adding short SiC /uniFB01 bers [13,14], and started a novel class of materials called UltraHigh Temperature Ceramic Matrix Composites (UHTCMCs) [15].

1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) [1] are a class of materials known for their ability to withstand extreme temperatures and extreme environments  [2].  UHTCs  primarily  comprise  borides,  carbides,  and nitrides of transition metals. The main features of these materials are melting  points  above  3000 ◦ C  and  the  ability  to  maintain  their  mechanical strength and chemical stability above 1600 ◦ C [3], which makes them ideal for applications that require materials that can withstand extreme heat and mechanical loads.
Currently, UHTCs are primarily used in aerospace [4], particularly for thermal protection systems, nose cones and leading edges, propulsion systems, and other components of hyper-velocity vehicles [5].
ZrB2-SiC composites [6] offer several advantages over other UHTCs [7],  such  as  being  more  cost-effective  and  lighter  compared  to HfB2-based materials [4], while having higher ablation resistance than carbides, which makes  them  a preferable choice for aerospace applications.
The most critical disadvantages that limit their widespread use are low fracture toughness between 4 and 5 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 [8], resulting in poor thermal shock resistance [9], which can lead to material failure under

question: What synergistic combination of thermal and mechanical properties renders TiB2, ZrB2, and HfB2 diborides suitable as protective barriers in high-stress thermal environments?
answer: The synergistic combination of high melting point, high thermal conductivity, excellent mechanical properties (such as high hardness and elastic modulus), and resistance to erosion and oxidation renders TiB2, ZrB2, and HfB2 diborides suitable as protective barriers in high-stress thermal environments [1][4]. These properties allow them to maintain structural integrity and functional performance under extreme thermal conditions, making them ideal candidates for use in thermal protection systems.

1. Introduction
The development of new generation of space vehicles is currently reviving an impressive interest into a special class of highly refractory materials popularly known as ultrahigh temperature ceramics (UHTCs) for potential use as thermal protection structures and engine parts in next-generation (hypersonic) re-entry space vehicles, as well as in critical ground-based applications such as refractory linings and cutting tools [1 -6]. The diborides of group IVB transition metals are attracting considerable attention, with HfB2 and ZrB2 being the most studied candidates because of their unique combination of chemical stability, superior mechanical properties, high thermal conductivities, and resistance to erosion and oxidation [4,7 -9]. As TiB2-based ceramics have many similarities with ZrB2 and HfB2-based ceramics, it has also been summarized together with ZrB2 and HfB2 in this review.
Table 1 lists the major structural, physical, and thermodynamic properties of TiB2, ZrB2 and HfB2 [8 -15]. The high percentage of covalent bonding in such materials results in a high melting

3.3.2. HfB2  and ZrB2  coatings
Boride  UHTC  coatings  are  mainly  composed  of  transition  metal diborides,  including  HfB2,  ZrB2,  TaB2 and  TiB2.  These  compounds exhibit metal-like and ceramic-like properties, such as a wide range of densities, high thermal conductivity, low resistivity, high melting point, high elastic modulus, hardness and good flexural strength. The unique combination of these properties renders them potential candidates for a variety of applications in high temperature and harsh environments [31, 104]. To date, HfB2 and ZrB2 have received the most extensive attention among boride UHTC coatings, mainly due to their ability to produce oxide phases with high melting point and a self-healing boride glass phase. This section focuses on the CVD-HfB2 and ZrB2 coatings, as well as their failure behaviors during high temperature applications.
HfB2 is  an  important  member  of  boride  UHTC  coatings.  As J. Zhang et al.
mentioned in Section 2.2, there are two kinds of deposition systems of HfB2: (i) the Hf(BH4)4 → HfB2 system cleaved from a single precursor; (ii) the HfCl4-BCl3-H2-Ar system using HfCl4  and BCl3  as precursor. Under the Hf(BH4)4 → HfB2  system, HfB2  films were grown by CVD using Hf [BH4]4 as a single precursor in ultra-low pressure (5 × 10 GLYPH<0> 9 Torr) and hydrogen-rich  sedimentary  environment.  The  obtained  films  under these conditions displayed high-purity nanostructures without halogen and organic pollutant. The mismatch between GaN and the HfB2  film was only 1.4 %, which can be used as a buffer layer on Si (111) substrate and applied in the field of microelectronics [154]. However, this method is difficult to be widely used in high temperature applications because of the thin coating thickness and the low deposition efficiency.

1. Introduction
Currently, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are commonly used as thermal protection materials in non-ablative thermal protection systems [6-9]. UHTC materials typically include transition metal diborides and carbides (such as ZrB2, HfB2, ZrC, HfC, TaC).  UHTC  materials  possess  unique  comprehensive  properties,  including  high melting  point,  high  thermal  conductivity,  excellent  mechanical  performances  at elevated temperature and high chemical stability [10-12]. These characteristics enable UHTCs to operate in extreme thermal environments. In particular, ZrB2 and HfB2 have attracted significant attention due to their high melting points and the ability to form refractory oxide scales. Despite these UHTC materials exhibit various advantages, they still have inherent limitations such as low fracture toughness and poor thermal shock resistance, which restrict their application. With the rapid development of hypersonic vehicles,  advanced  UHTCs  based  coating  systems  are  needed  to  meet  the  need  for thermal protection in extreme environment conditions.

1. Introduction
Transition metal diborides (HfB2  and ZrB2), based Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics (UHTCs), characterized by extreme melting point ( > 3000 ◦ C) [1], high oxidation resistance (OR) at elevated temperatures (~1400 -1500 ◦ C) [2], and excellent mechanical properties (Hardness > 17 GPa, Elastic Modulus: 300 -400 GPa) [3,4], establishes them as potential material for thermal protection systems. Nonetheless, the presence of covalent bonds and lower value of self-diffusion coefficient of these ceramics restricts them from being fully sintered and, therefore, methods such as spark plasma sintering (SPS) [5], hot pressing (HP) [6, 7], and microwave sintering [8], are employed along with carbide-based sintering  additives  like  Silicon  carbide  (SiC),  Boron  Carbide  (B4C), Carbon Nanotubes (CNT), etc. [9,10]. SiC reinforcement in HfB2  and ZrB2  increases  densification  (up  to  100  %)  [11],  improves  hardness (from 13 to 25 GPa) [12,13], fracture toughness (up to 6.5 MPa.m 0.5 ) [14], and provides oxidation resistance through development of a protective SiO2  barrier at 1200 ◦ C [15]. Consequently, ZrB2 with 20 vol%

13.10  CONCLUSIONS
This  chapter  focused  on  the  processing,  thermophysical,  thermomechanical,  and oxidation properties of TiB 2 -based ceramics. The combination of properties including refractoriness, high hardness, elastic modulus, and chemical resistance is attributed to the crystal structure and atomic bonding. The low self-diffusion coefficient and high melting points of monolithic TiB 2 necessitate  sintering  at  high  temperatures  for  long  periods, which can deteriorate the mechanical properties due to grain growth. To mitigate these issues, advanced sintering techniques and additives have been employed to attain high relative densities at lower temperatures and shorter times. Ceramic additives such as SiC, Si 3 N 4 , MoSi 2 , and TiSi 2 are more suitable than conventional metallic additives for retention  of  mechanical  properties  at  higher  temperatures. Among  the  advanced  sintering techniques, SPS limits grain coarsening, resulting in superior mechanical properties. The effects of different processing routes and additives on the microstructure and properties of TiB 2 were described. In addition to the mechanical properties, thermal conductivity, TSR, oxidation behavior, and wear properties were reviewed. TiB 2 is suitable for a variety of applications in extreme environments. To realize the suitability of these diborides for specific  Ultra-High-temperature  applications,  the  relevant  properties  must  be  further characterized  at  elevated  temperatures  and  under  harsh  conditions.  Comprehensive investigation is still lacking, and simulation of response to such conditions is still needed.

question: In which aerospace applications have TiB2, ZrB2, and HfB2 diborides been proposed for use as thermal insulating systems?
answer: TiB2, ZrB2, and HfB2 diborides have been proposed for use as thermal insulating systems in hypersonic aerospace vehicles, specifically for use in leading edges and as thermal protection structures in next-generation (hypersonic) re-entry space vehicles [1][4]. Additionally, they are considered for use as thermal insulators between hot external UHTC components and cooler interior components [1].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
Due to the harsh flight environment of hypersonic platforms, Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics (UHTCs) imbued with macroscale porosity have been introduced as candidates for providing thermal insulation of sensitive onboard components. This study employs an oxidized state approach for modeling the coupled effects of surface oxidation  and  continuum  damage  in  the  effective  thermomechanical  response  of  such  materials  within  the framework of computational micromechanics. Using an in-house Material Point Method (MPM) computational tool, two-dimensional plane strain simulations were performed on representative hexagonal structures of TiB2 with 10%, 40% and 70% macroporosity. Oxide layers ranging from 5 to 50 μ m were applied to the structures in place of a transient, diffusion-based oxidation solver. Simulations with applied compressive strains of up to 30% were then performed on hexagonal structures with and without the oxide layer present. A range of dynamic thermal gradients were also applied to investigate the damage due to thermal expansion coefficient mismatch. Finally,  the  oxidized  state  modeling  approach  was  applied  to  large  porous  structures  derived  from  micro computed tomography images of TiB2 foam.
thermal  expansion,  high  thermal  conductivity,  and  high  bending strength  at  high  temperatures  [6].  Given  these  benefits,  several  refractory diborides (ZrB2, HfB2, and TiB2) have been proposed for hypersonic applications, specifically for use in leading edges [4,7]. Taking advantage of multiple ceramic processing techniques, Tallon et al. [8] proposed multiscale porous UHTC materials to be used as thermal insulators  between  hot  external  UHTC  components  and  cooler  interior components.

1. Introduction
Among ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), refractory transition metal diborides e.g. ZrB2 and HfB2 are pivotal ceramics for being utilized in extreme applications such as propulsion systems and thermal protection systems in hypersonic aerospace vehicles due to their unrivaled combination of high hardness, strength retention at elevated temperatures, high melting temperature, good corrosion resistance against molten iron, and slags and relatively oxidation [1 -9]. ZrB2 has several advantages compared with HfB2 e.g. better resistant to oxidation, lighter and cheaper, which has made it applicable for aerospace industrial usages [10 -17]. However, it is very challenging to densify single-phase ZrB2 due to the intrinsic constraint of this ceramic including strong covalent bonds, low self-di /uniFB00 usion coe /uniFB03 cient, and presence of oxide impurities on its surfaces [18 -22]. Therefore, it is an important issue to select the best technique and modify the composition and microstructure of such kind of ceramics to manufacture the high quality performance products [23 -39].

1. Introduction
There has been considerable interest in developing hightemperature ceramic-matrix composites for use in advanced aerospace and critical ground-based applications. For ultrahigh temperature applications, composites based on diborides of refractory metals Zr, Ti, Hf, and Ta have been proposed [1-7]. These diboride composites possess high melting temperatures, high hardness, good thermal shock resistance, and excellent oxidation resistance. The oxidation resistance of the diboridematrix composites is further improved by adding silicon carbide, which also inhibits grain coarsening of the ZrB2 phase. Carbon is sometimes added to the ZrB2-based composites for thermal shock resistance. High-temperature strength, fracture toughness, oxidation resistance, and thermal shock resistance of these ceramic composites have been reported elsewhere [4].
For advanced structural applications, these composites must be joined to themselves and to other high-temperature materi- als, including heat-resistant metals and alloys such as titanium. Robustassemblyandintegration technologies, therefore, play an essential role in fabricating composite components [8]. Among the joining approaches currently in vogue, active metal brazing is a particularly simple and cost-effective method to join both ceramics [9,10] and ceramic-ceramic composites [11-16] to metals using braze powders, pastes, ribbons, or wires that are melted, allowed to wet and spread in the joint region, and solidified to form joint interfaces. Unfortunately, very few studies [17] have examined the flow and spreading of liquid brazes on ZrB2-based ceramics, and scant information is available on brazing response of ZrB2-based ceramic-matrix composites to metals and ceramics.
∗ Corresponding author. Tel.: +1 216 433 8883. E-mail address: msingh@grc.nasa.gov (M. Singh).

1. Introduction
The development of new generation of space vehicles is currently reviving an impressive interest into a special class of highly refractory materials popularly known as ultrahigh temperature ceramics (UHTCs) for potential use as thermal protection structures and engine parts in next-generation (hypersonic) re-entry space vehicles, as well as in critical ground-based applications such as refractory linings and cutting tools [1 -6]. The diborides of group IVB transition metals are attracting considerable attention, with HfB2 and ZrB2 being the most studied candidates because of their unique combination of chemical stability, superior mechanical properties, high thermal conductivities, and resistance to erosion and oxidation [4,7 -9]. As TiB2-based ceramics have many similarities with ZrB2 and HfB2-based ceramics, it has also been summarized together with ZrB2 and HfB2 in this review.
Table 1 lists the major structural, physical, and thermodynamic properties of TiB2, ZrB2 and HfB2 [8 -15]. The high percentage of covalent bonding in such materials results in a high melting

9.1  IntroductIon
ZrB 2 and HfB 2 are proposed for use in hypersonic vehicles and reentry spacecraft where they will be used as thermal management materials for extreme thermal environments. These applications typically include sharp control surfaces (leading/trailing edges) as well as scramjet engine components [1-5]. Hypersonic control surfaces need thermal conductivities ( k )  that  are  as  high  as  possible  to  conduct  heat  generated  from  atmospheric friction through the material so that it can be dissipated elsewhere [1, 3]. In addition, higher k values increase thermal shock resistance in high heat flux situations. Because engine components cannot radiate to the atmosphere, lower k refractory materials could be beneficial, depending on the cooling capabilities of the vehicle [3]. From a materials engineering perspective, the effects of processing, composition (intentional additions or from impurities), and the resulting microstructure need to be characterized to design materials with appropriate properties. The goal of this chapter is to review the state-of-the-art  with  respect  to  thermal  conductivity  for  single-phase  and  composite ZrB 2 and HfB 2 ceramics.

question: What computational thermodynamic methodology did Cacciamani and colleagues use to predict phase stability and thermodynamic properties across multiple interacting metallic and ceramic components?
