chunks.text:
5.1. Challenges and remedies
Computational forecast limitations: Although calculation of phase diagrams  (CALPHAD),  density  functional  theory  (DFT)  and  special quasirandom structures (SQS) supercell models are promising computational  tools,  the  challenges  of  scaling-up  to  multi-component  high entropy ceramics are computationally costly [181]. Recording configuration disorders, local lattice distortion and temperature-based simulations make the existing simulation procedures difficult to achieve and expensive too. So, rather than depending solely on costly DFT modeling, it is recommended to engage in machine-learning inter-atomic models (e.g. SNAP, GAP) that are to be trained on representative ab initio data. These can be used together with Monte Carlo or MD simulation in order to capture, efficiently, both disorder and lattice relaxations at a wide range of composition and at lower cost [182]. Also, it is imperative to make use of cluster expansion (CE) technique to map configurational space onto simplified Hamiltonians. As soon as it is trained with DFT, CE models can forecast phase stability over multiple configurations at a very affordable cost [183]. Moreover, the use of phase-field techniques in conjunction with thermodynamic information (CALPHAD, DFT) to model microstructural changes, grain-boundary dynamics and temperature without having to explicitly model each atom is essential. These techniques are effective to scale simulations to device-level status [171]. Again, following an integrated computational materials and engineering (ICME) process, which involves connecting DFT, CE, phase-field, and continuum models should be prioritized. Multi-scale property prediction from the atomic level up to the macroscopic scale can be achieved via interfaces  using  standardized  data  formats  (e.g.  HDF5)  [184,185]. Recommended  also  is  to  generate  simple  descriptors  of  disorder, including mass and lattice disorder parameters based on the DFT data or experimental data, and use it to predict HECs ' properties such as thermal conductivity or phase stability without performing large scale simulations [186]. Through these methods, efficiency is improved and costs are reduced in HEC development.

5. Conclusions
The objective of this work was to experimentally test the predictions of the analytical and computational thermodynamic approach outlined in a companion publication. This methodology was applied to the prediction of substrate and oxide composition after oxidation of a high entropy carbide and boride containing Hf, Zr, Ti, Ta and Nb in equimolar amounts. The experimentally determined oxide and substrate compositions were found to be in good agreement with the thermodynamic predictions, which describe well the local equilibrium at the reaction interface for both the carbide and the diboride case. In general, the oxidation of high entropy materials studied here occurred by preferential oxidation of each component according to the relative thermodynamic favorabilities of their respective oxides. Group IV oxides, being most favorable, formed preferentially. The experimental results further elucidated that within each group, preferential oxidation can also occur according to relative thermodynamic stability: Hf and Zr preferentially oxidize over Ti as shown in the boride case, while Ta preferentially oxidizes over Nb as shown in the carbide case. The substrate composition of group V metal-rich carbides and borides, which are or have the potential to form phases with different crystal structures from the original ceramic, was accurately predicted. Whereas complex oxides were observed experimentally, this is not a significant limitation to the prediction of preferential oxidation, as the formation of these complex oxides was dominated by predicted oxide constituents.
This work shows that the approach outlined in Part I provides insight into the thermodynamics driving the oxidation behavior of high entropy materials and can be used as a tool for the design of high entropy materials for oxidation resistance.

A B S T R A C T
Dual-phase  compositionally  complex  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  were  formulated  by  incorporating different Groups V and VI metals such as V, Nb, Ta, Cr, Mo, or W into a base composition containing the Group IV elements, Hf, Ti, and Zr. Metal distribution was predicted using first-principles-based thermodynamics simulations  and  compared  with  experimental  results.  Moreover,  phase  stability,  microstructure,  and  mechanical properties were evaluated for all of the ceramics. Compositions containing Cr, V, Nb, or Ta formed dual-phase ceramics containing only one boride and one carbide phase, while compositions containing Mo or W developed an additional third phase. The experimental metal distribution trends generally aligned with thermodynamic predictions, except for compositions containing V, which showed unexpected segregation behavior that was influenced by complex interactions of the coexistence of boride and carbide structures. From the dual-phase ceramics, the composition containing V exhibited the highest hardness (HV1 = 25.5 ± 0.6 GPa) combined with smaller grain sizes (0.99 ± 0.33 μ m for the boride and 1.15 ± 0.31 μ m for the carbide phases). Our findings provide insights into phase formation and elemental segregation and help the design of next-generation dualphase UHTCs with tailored properties.
dual-phase (HEDP) UHTCs, summarizing compositions that have been studied and key findings. Table 2 highlights the metals used in each study, revealing that while Hf, Nb, Ta, Ti, and Zr are commonly utilized, metals such as W, Cr, V, and Mo are incorporated less frequently. Filipovi ´ c  et  al.  [4] and Guo et al.  [5] reported  additional  phases  when adding significant amounts of W or Mo, though phase stability in these systems remains unknown.

4.1. High-throughput computing
Recent studies have successfully incorporated HTC into the field of UHTCs, particularly in the accelerated development of HE-UHTCs. By improving  the  efficiency  of  property  prediction,  materials  screening, and  design  optimization,  HTC  addresses  long-standing  challenges  in ceramic materials discovery and development [299 -301]. For example, Nisar et al. [151] employed high-throughput first-principles calculations with ML to create a quantitative database of thermodynamically stable HE-UHTCs, enabling rapid prediction of single-phase solid solutions and reducing trial-and-error experiments. Nath et al. [302] developed an HTC framework combining Taylor expansion with DFT calculations to predict UHTC thermoelastic properties, significantly reducing computational costs while maintaining accuracy. Kim et al. [303] also used high-throughput DFT calculations to investigate the structure, elasticity, and  thermodynamic  properties  of  the  Hf-Ta-C-N  solid  solution  for high-temperature applications. They found that at lower temperatures, the solid solution occupies a broader phase space, with stable compositions shifting from Ta- and N-rich regions to Hf- and C-rich regions at higher  temperatures.  These  compositional  shifts  influence  bonding characteristics, such as metallic and covalent bonds, which affect mechanical properties like Poisson ' s ratio and Pugh ' s constant. Additionally, Xu et al. [304] demonstrated the power of data-driven approaches by combining ML with high-throughput experimentation to establish correlations among composition, processing parameters, and hardness in ultra-hard ceramics. Their methodology involved sequential steps of data acquisition, feature selection, model training, global optimization, and  iterative  validation.  By  employing  a  random  forest  algorithm coupled  with  active  learning,  they  successfully  identified  a  novel high-entropy  nitride  coating,  (AlCrNbTaTi)N,  with  a  hardness  of 40.1 GPa, approximately 9 % higher than the best-performing quinary compositions in the initial dataset, and only 1 GPa  below the six-component system.

I. INTRODUCTION
The synthesis of materials with desired properties often comes with the challenge of selecting proper chemistry as the overall property is predominantly dependent on phase formation (single-phase solid solution vs multi-phase). Thus, phase prediction in these multicomponent ceramic systems is currently a main topic for investigation, which may broaden the compositional range of high-entropy ceramics, unlocking possibilities for their utilization across diverse scientific domains. The design of these materials is often challenging due to the sheer number of unexplored compositions, and the scarcity of the literature data has posed an obstacle in generalizing any suitable design strategy. Sarker et al. discovered the entropy-forming ability (EFA) descriptor utilizing the ab initio approach, which indicates the single-phase formability of any given composition, i.e., higher the EFA value, higher the chance of singlephase formation. 32 Very recently, a new thermodynamic parameter has been introduced, namely, disordered enthalpy -entropy descriptor (DEED), which captures the balance between entropy gains and enthalpy costs on the formation of homogeneous solid solutions for any class of multicomponent ceramics. 33 First, the choice of elements in these multicomponent ceramics is found to be a critical factor. It is prudent to have prior knowledge of the combination of elements for a particular HEC to synthesize a single-phase pure multicomponent HEC successfully. Second, the choice of precursor is equally important. Using earth-abundant precursors, like oxides, will make the synthesis attractive and relatively economical for technologically important structural applications. In this connection, it is crucial to derive data-driven prediction tactics combined with an easy synthesis route. However, the accuracy comes at a cost of intense calculation time and the complexity of calculations for the assessment of the density of states. On the contrary, machine learning (ML) offers an effective alternate high-throughput design approach for designing these materials. Machine learning, being a data-driven tool, can predict the outcome without requiring any atomic structural information.

question: What specific surface phenomena between Group 4 metal diborides and transition metal components have been experimentally characterized to understand their interaction mechanisms?
answer: The specific surface phenomena between Group 4 metal diborides and transition metal components that have been experimentally characterized include the formation of eutectic compositions and interfacial layers, which increase wettability [5]. Additionally, the oxidation mechanisms involving surface reactions, oxygen diffusion through porous oxide scale and liquid boria, as well as permeation and evaporation of the latter, have been studied [3].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
The  oxidation  mechanisms  of  group  IV,  V  and  VI  carbides  and diborides have been studied extensively due to their potential extreme environment applications [1], such as hypersonics and nuclear reactors. The high entropy design paradigm has been employed to broaden the compositional palette for UHTC carbides [2,3] and diborides [4]. The increased configurational entropy in high entropy materials has been credited with the stabilization of solid solution phases. In addition to the potential for improved mechanical and thermal properties, this expansion of compositional space could lead to the discovery of more oxidation resistant compositions.
The thermodynamic favorability of group IV oxides as well as their high melting temperatures suggest that designing an oxidation resistant high entropy ceramic from the collection of group IV, V and VI elements necessitates  starting  with  Hf,  Zr  and  Ti  [5].  The  other  two  ceramic components, assuming the choices are limited to the palette of group IV, V and VI transitional metals were chosen from Ta, Nb, Mo and W. Cr and V were not considered because their additions are expected to result in poor  oxidation  resistance  at  temperatures  well  below  those  under consideration. Cr -O vapor species have a high vapor pressure at the

14. Conclusions and outlook
A state-of-the-art review on transition metal di-boride based ultra-high temperature ceramics has been presented in this paper. In the context of the structural applications at ultra-high temperatures, the di-borides exhibit excellent mechanical and physical properties, such as refractoriness, high hardness, high elastic modulus, good fracture strength, good thermal conductivity, good electrical conductivity and notable oxidation resistance. It is possible to further tune most of the basic properties by adopting preferable processing routes, compositions and developing desired microstructures. However, the practical applications of the diborides are presently limited mainly due to their poor sinterability and moderate fracture toughness. In this regard advanced sintering techniques, with or without the use of sinter-additives, have been successfully employed to obtain dense transition metal diborides with desired combination of properties. Nevertheless, the use of advanced sintering techniques, such as hot pressing, flash sintering and spark plasma sintering, increases the processing related expenses and restricts the shape/size of the components. Similarly, particulate and fiber reinforcements have been investigated to improve the fracture properties of the di-borides; however, with limited success to-date with respect to the magnitudes of the improvements achieved. In addition to the attempts towards improving the sinterability and basic properties of the di-borides, in order to enhance the applicability and scaling-up of the production of ultra-high temperature ceramics, more research efforts need to be directed towards economical scale-up of the synthesis routes for phase pure di-boride powders. In this regard, more recently, precursor derived routes have been used to successfully produce Group IV metal borides and their composites.

600 -1300 ◦ C.
The understanding of the oxidation mechanisms of the HEB here investigated  and  the  role  played  in  this  regard  by  SiC  involve  both thermodynamic and kinetic considerations. In fact, oxidation of metal diborides involves several phenomena such as surface reaction, oxygen diffusion  through  porous  oxide  scale  and  liquid  boria,  as  well  as permeation and evaporation of the latter. Parthasarathy et al. [41] and, more recently, Mogilevsky and Cinibulk [42] provided, for the case of individual metal borides-based systems, an excellent schematization of the latter phenomena, as well as a mathematical model to describe them. However, when considering HEBs, the oxidation mechanism is made further complicated by the formation of several multicomponent phases, as confirmed in the present study by the various individual and mixed oxides detected by XRD analysis on the surface of the oxidized samples (Fig. 6). Overall, the identification of the detailed oxidation mechanism of  this  quinary  system  clearly  requires  a  thorough  experimental  and theoretical investigation, which is beyond the scope of the present work and deserves a dedicated study. In  this  regard,  to  provide  a  reliable oxidation mechanism for such complex systems, the behavior of simpler multicomponent  borides  (binary  and  ternary),  involving  the  same transition metals should be first considered. This approach will permit, taking also advantage of the information available in the literature for individual  diboride  systems,  to  the  understanding  of  the  related mechanism.
Although further studies are needed to draw definitive conclusions, based on the outcomes described above, it is possible to state that the presence of SiC clearly hinders the progressive loss of volatile Mo and W oxides, so that the degradation rate of the HEB phase under investigation was markedly lowered.

4. Conclusions
A CALPHAD thermodynamic database for phase diagram calculations in the quinary system B-Hf-Ni-Ti-Zr has been implemented. Thermodynamic models for 30 different phases have been determined and interaction parameters for all the 10 binary and 10 ternary sub-systems have been included. Interaction parameters have been taken from the literature, when available, but a few binary systems (B-Hf, B-Ti, B-Zr, Hf-Ni, Ni-Zr) have been slightly modified with respect to the previous assessments and several ternary systems (B-Hf-Ni, B-Ni-Ti, B-Ni-Zr, B-Hf-Zr, B-Ti-Zr, Hf-Ni-Ti, Hf-Ni-Zr, Hf-Ti-Zr, Ni-Ti-Zr) have been completely assessed or re-assessed in this work with particular attention to the self-consistency of the models adopted.
Assuming that higher order interactions may be approximated by ideal extrapolation of binary and ternary terms, quaternary and higher order equilibria may be predicted. Prediction of phase equilibria in higher order systems not investigated experimentally is further supported by a careful self-consistency check of the phase modelling and interaction parameters adopted from the literature or evaluated in this work.
The database has been especially built for application to the investigation of the interactions between Group 4 metal diboride UHTC and nickel, especially to predict and/or understand phase Hf-Ni-Zr invariant reactions involving liquid.

chunks.text:
1 | INTRODUCTION
Ultra-high-temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  a  class  of materials that include diborides (ie, ZrB 2 , HfB2), carbides (ie,  ZrC, HfC), and nitrides (ie, ZrN, HfN) of early transition  metals. 1 UHTCs  have  melting  temperatures  over 3000°C and the capability to tolerate extreme heating environments. 2-4 Increasing  interest  in  hypersonic  vehicles has led the development of new UHTC materials for wing leading edges and nose tips, as well as propulsion system components.  Diboride-based  UHTCs  have  performance advantages  in  some  hypersonic  applications  compared  to the carbides and nitrides due to better oxidation resistance and ability to transfer and redistribute heat (thermal conductivity > 100 W/m K at 25°C) at elevated temperatures. 5 Extensive  research  has  been  conducted  over  the  years  to understand  the  oxidation  behavior  of  zirconium  diboride (ZrB2)  in  the  low  to  high-temperature  regimes.  Previous

1. Introduction
Low-risk trajectories and sharp configurations (i.e. leading edges with high curvatures) call for TPS able to withstand high temperatures. Indeed, convective heat transfer increases with the curvature, so that it is of great importance to accurately predict how this heat transfer is distributed in the TPS material. At the typical orbital reentry conditions, convective heat transfer to the surface is partly conducted in the solid and partly re-radiated in the atmosphere. When a steady state is achieved, global radiative equilibrium is established, in the sense that the (surface) overall convective heat flux is perfectly balanced by the overall surface radiative flux. When conduction in the solid may be neglected (i.e. TPS materials with very low thermal conductivity), radiative heat transfer locally balances convective heat transfer (local radiative equilibrium). In both cases, the corresponding surface temperature is referred to as 'radiative equilib- rium temperature'. This temperature, depending on flight conditions, may be of the order of up to 3000 K, a temperature that only very specialized materials can withstand. A class of such materials is Ultra High Temperature Ceramics (UHTCs) [6-8,11,15,20,23,29].
The characteristics of UHTCs, such as tensile strength at high temperatures and oxidation resistance, allow them to be used in extreme environments like those associated with hypersonic flight, atmospheric reentry and rocket propulsion. Some compounds belonging to the UHTC family are borides such as Zirconium Diboride (ZrB2) and Hafnium Diboride (HfB2), which have melting temperatures above 3300 K. These materials also retain their strength (up to 150 MPa) at temperatures above 1500 K, exhibit good thermal resistance, and can be modified with additives such as Silicon Carbide (SiC) to promote oxidation resistance and improved strength.
Recent works have been dedicated to hypersonic viscous flows over sharp geometries and on possible improvements related to the utilization of UHTC materials [3,12,19,28,31].

1. Introduction
The  advances  in  power  conversion  and  high-speed  aerial  transportation  necessitates  the  development  of  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs),  capable  of  operating  in  aggressive  environments  at T > 2000 ◦ C [1 -4]. The family of high-temperature ceramics is mostly represented by oxide ceramics, Si3N4- and SiC-based composites, and SiC-coated carbon/carbon composites [5 -9]. The carbon-carbon composites provide the highest strength (especially at high temperatures) but are prone to rapid ablation at T > 500 ◦ C [7 -13]. The composites based on SiC and Si3N4  are the current benchmark of oxidation resistance at temperatures below 1600 ◦ C, but suffer from low thermal shock resistance and corrode in presence of water vapors or alkali ions [14].
UHTCs are often based on ZrB2 due to the high thermal conductivity 60  W/m*K,  low  coefficient  of  thermal  expansion  (6 -7) ⋅ 10 GLYPH<0> 6 ◦ C -1 , refractoriness, and high hardness, which endow ZrB2 with a high thermal shock resistance [5 -7,15 -17]. The oxidation of refractory diborides is limited by the formation and destruction of the B2O3 layer [3 -6,18]. B2O3 rapidly evaporates at T > 1100 ◦ C, prompting a fast oxidation-driven  degradation  of  the  ZrB2 ceramics. question: In SHAO et al. 2023, what were the primary crystalline phases identified in the oxidized surface layers formed on the zirconium carbide-silicon carbide-molybdenum disilicide coatings after exposure to oxyacetylene flames across all three heating conditions?
answer: In SHAO et al. 2023, the primary crystalline phases identified in the oxidized surface layers formed on the zirconium carbide-silicon carbide-molybdenum disilicide coatings after exposure to oxyacetylene flames across all three heating conditions were fine ZrO2 grains and glassy SiO2 phase [5]. Additionally, in Condition 3, the ZrSiO4 phase was identified as continuous and distributed uniformly with SiO2, forming a ZrSiO4-SiO2 double layer [5].

3.3. Phase composition and microstructure of TZS coatings after ablation
Fig. 8 is an XRD diffraction patterns of TZS-#3, TZS-#2 and TZS-#1 coatings after ablation. According to the XRD results, TZS-#3, TZS-#2 and TZS-#1 coatings have extremely oxidation behavior after ablation. The main phase in TZS-#3, TZS-#2 and TZS-#1 coatings is Ta2Zr6O17, Ta2O5 and TaO are also detected. Ta2Zr6O17 is a composite oxide, which is rich in Zr and O elements and consistent with the design of the initial spray  powder.  During  ablation  by  an  oxyacetylene  flame,  the  hightemperature  flame  flow  causes  the  low-melting-point  SiC  within  the TZS coating to oxidize first, forming a SiO2  glassy layer over TaC and ZrC.  As  high-temperature  exposure  continues,  the  SiO2 volatilizes, exposing TaC and ZrC to the flame. These subsequently oxidize to form Ta2O5 and ZrO2. At elevated temperatures, Ta2O5 and ZrO2 interdiffuse and  react,  ultimately  generating  Ta2Zr6O17  [7,33].  Furthermore,  the TaO phase was detected in the XRD pattern. TaO is thermodynamically unstable under typical ablation conditions, and the oxygen-rich environment of the oxyacetylene flame would promote further oxidation of TaO  to  higher-valence  Ta2O5.  We  speculate  that  the  observed  TaO presence is attributed to the short ablation duration, where insufficient time prevented complete conversion to stable oxides.

The dispersed phase is formed by the aggregation of white particles,  with  a  dendritic-like  structure  (Fig. 7(f)). Under Condition 3 (Figs. 7(i-l)), a relatively complete structure is formed in the central area, and agglomerated  ZrO2  grains  are  embedded  in  the porous  liquid  phase.  In  the  transition  area,  the ZrSiO4 phase is continuous and distributed uniformly with SiO2, forming ZrSiO4-SiO2 double layer,  which  can  provide  oxidation  and  ablation protection for C/C-SiC-ZrC composites.

question: According to Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013, what specific temperature-dependent thermal transport mechanism did lanthanum oxide addition provide to stabilize the heat flow properties of zirconium diboride-silicon carbide composite materials at ultra-high temperatures?
answer: According to Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013, the addition of lanthanum oxide to zirconium diboride-silicon carbide composite materials helped to stabilize thermal transport within the volume of UHTCs at high temperatures, as evidenced by the reduction of thermal diffusivity from 1500 ◦ C to 1900 ◦ C being ∼ 10% for SiC-doped UHTCs and ∼ 4% for La2O3-SiC-doped UHTCs [4].

1. Introduction
Structural applications at ultra-high temperatures require materials with excellent thermo-mechanical and chemical stability. Examples of such applications include rocket nozzles and hypersonic leading edges. Among the most attractive candidate materials are the refractory diborides of zirconium and hafnium; a comprehensive review of their properties can be found in Ref. 1. In addition to their high melting temperature, high strength at high temperatures and excellent thermal shock resistance, nominally pure diborides show good oxidation resistance up to ∼ 1500 K. In order to improve the resistance to oxidation above this temperature several additives have been extensively tested. The addition of silicon carbide as a second phase was found to promote the formation of an outer borosilicate glass layer, which reduces oxygen diffusion to the substrate thus improving oxidation resistance of UHTC diborides up to ∼ 1900 K. Other additives are under study to further improving oxidation resistance of these compounds at higher temperatures. A review covering the oxidation behaviour of ZrB2 and HfB2 can be found in Ref. 2.
∗ Corresponding author. Tel.: +44 0207 594 7130. E-mail address: m.pettina12@imperial.ac.uk (M. Pettinà).
As these materials are required to operate at extreme temperatures under load, creep is another phenomenon that has to be considered. 3 After some early investigations on ZrB2 reported by Rhodes et al. 4 back in 1970, the interest in creep has been recently growing. 5-10 Researchers have focused on ZrB2, with SiC contents in the range 0-30% in volume. The observed stress exponents suggest diffusion creep as the predominant deformation mechanism, in agreement with the relatively low applied stresses. At higher stresses and temperatures in excess of 1500 K, however, power law creep is expected to become the dominant deformation mechanism. 11

1. Introduction
Low-risk trajectories and sharp configurations (i.e. leading edges with high curvatures) call for TPS able to withstand high temperatures. Indeed, convective heat transfer increases with the curvature, so that it is of great importance to accurately predict how this heat transfer is distributed in the TPS material. At the typical orbital reentry conditions, convective heat transfer to the surface is partly conducted in the solid and partly re-radiated in the atmosphere. When a steady state is achieved, global radiative equilibrium is established, in the sense that the (surface) overall convective heat flux is perfectly balanced by the overall surface radiative flux. When conduction in the solid may be neglected (i.e. TPS materials with very low thermal conductivity), radiative heat transfer locally balances convective heat transfer (local radiative equilibrium). In both cases, the corresponding surface temperature is referred to as 'radiative equilib- rium temperature'. This temperature, depending on flight conditions, may be of the order of up to 3000 K, a temperature that only very specialized materials can withstand. A class of such materials is Ultra High Temperature Ceramics (UHTCs) [6-8,11,15,20,23,29].
The characteristics of UHTCs, such as tensile strength at high temperatures and oxidation resistance, allow them to be used in extreme environments like those associated with hypersonic flight, atmospheric reentry and rocket propulsion. Some compounds belonging to the UHTC family are borides such as Zirconium Diboride (ZrB2) and Hafnium Diboride (HfB2), which have melting temperatures above 3300 K. These materials also retain their strength (up to 150 MPa) at temperatures above 1500 K, exhibit good thermal resistance, and can be modified with additives such as Silicon Carbide (SiC) to promote oxidation resistance and improved strength.
Recent works have been dedicated to hypersonic viscous flows over sharp geometries and on possible improvements related to the utilization of UHTC materials [3,12,19,28,31].

INTRODUCTION
Zirconium, hafnium, tantalum based diborides (ZrB 2 ,  HfB 2 and  TaB 2 ),  carbides (ZrC, HfC and TaC) and nitrides (ZrN, HfN and TaN) with silicon carbide (SiC) up to 30vol% are promising refractory non-oxide ceramics for future generation re-entry vehicles in nose cones, thermal protection and propulsion systems. These composites have to perform under the hostile environments of high temperatures and thermal cycling. These materials, generally referred as ultra-high temperature ceramics  (UHTCs),  have  high  melting  temperatures  (>3000ºC)  and  moderate erosion-oxidation-thermal shock resistance, are good electrical and thermal conductors.  In  addition,  refractory  carbides  exhibit  a  brittle-to  ductile  transition  at high temperature depending on stoichiometry. In reusable space vehicles (under hostile environments), the external thermal protection systems in the form of rigid surfaces are required to withstand temperatures beyond 1800°C and, in addition to being refractory, must be able to tolerate the high-heat-flux and mechanical stress associated with vibrations at launch and re-entry into the atmosphere. Therefore, it is necessary that thermal protection materials provide good oxidation resistance, thermal-shock resistance, ablation resistance as  well  as  dimensional  stability.

3. Results
Fig. 2 plots thermal diffusivity of all UHTC compositions from RT to 1900 ◦ C. It is noticeable that the spread of data is reduced with temperature, ZS20, ZS20La, HS20 and HS20La values being similar at 1900 ◦ C. In fact, the reduction of thermal diffusivity from 1500 ◦ C to 1900 ◦ C is ∼ 10% for SiC-doped UHTCs and ∼ 4% for La2O3-SiC-doped UHTCs. Therefore, La2O3 addition to ZS20 and HS20 UHTCs helps to stabilize thermal transport within the volume of UHTCs at high temperatures. Thermal conductivity was calculated from RT to 1900 ◦ C for each UHTC using Eq. (1) with the measured thermal expansion and thermal diffusivity, and with the calculated specific heat as shown in Fig. 3. Thermal conductivity behavior is similar to thermal diffusivity and the reduction of thermal conductivity from 1500 ◦ C to 1900 ◦ C is ∼ 7% for SiC-doped UHTCs, whereas it can be considered constant for La2O3-SiC-doped UHTCs.

1. Introduction
Due to excellent oxidation and ablation resistances and mechanical performance  at  high-temperatures,  ultrahigh  temperature  ceramics (UHTC)  composites  are  currently  widely  used  in  thermal  protection systems (TPS) of hypersonic and long-haul vehicles [1 -6]. Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) [7 -9] is one of UHTC, having an extremely high melting point ~3040 ◦ C. By introducing silicon carbide (SiC) particles into ZrB2 matrix, the oxidation resistance of ZrB2-SiC UHTC can be significantly improved. This is benefited by the formation of a borosilicate glass layer at ultrahigh temperatures, which offers higher viscosity, higher boiling point and lower vapor pressure than those of B2O3 [10]. However, wide applications of ZrB2-SiC UHTC are still limited by its inherent brittleness, which can be efficiently overcome by adding the third soft phases, i.e., graphite flakes [11 -14]. It is reported that the fracture toughness of ZrB2-SiC is significantly  enhanced from ~4.0 to ~6.1 MPa √ m, if  a small volume percent of graphite is added. Unraveling the deformation and failure mechanisms of ZrB2-SiC-graphite (labelled ZrB2-SiC-G) upon external  loading  at  service  temperature  (1200 -2000 ◦ C)  is  vitally important for structural design and lifetime assessment of UHTC based TPS. However, in the application environment, the UHTC always suffers from  strong  thermal-mechanical-chemical  coupling  loading,  which

1. Introduction
To protect C/C composite in extremely high-temperature conditions, the protective coating layers having heat-resistant and anti-oxidative properties  are  required.  Among  the  protective  materials,  ultra-high temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  particularly  noteworthy  due  to their extremely high melting temperatures around 3000 ◦ C or higher, exceptional hardness, as well as excellent chemical stability and strength under  high-temperature  conditions  [5,6].  Among  UHTCs,  zirconium carbide (ZrC) has a remarkably high melting point of 3530 ◦ C [7,8]. Additionally, its oxide, ZrO2, also has a high melting point of 2700 ◦ C [9].  Furthermore,  ZrC  exhibits  excellent  resistance  to  corrosion  and possesses a density of 6.73 g/cm 3 [10], which is notably lower compared to other UHTCs such as HfC (12.7 g/cm 3 ) [11], TaC (14.5 g/cm 3 ) [12], and WC (15.8 g/cm 3 ) [13].
To coat ZrC layer on the C/C composite, it is crucial to utilize the corresponding ZrC powders in the VPS method. Consequently, it becomes inevitable to consider the effects of the particle size of the raw powders on the quality of the coating layer. For example, when the particle size increases, it is generally anticipated to achieve a higher coating rate but there is a possibility of surface damage due to the impact of un-melted powders. Conversely, smaller particle sizes contribute a

3.1. Oxidation behaviour and multi-oxide layer formation
After the first thermodynamic event, all the composites S1, S2, S3, S4, and S5 exhibited an oxidation resistance region. The start and ending temperatures for this region for each composite are reported in Table 5. In the oxidation-resistant region, no mass gain was observed due to the formation of a protective SiO2-B2O3 oxide layer, as previously reported. The variation in oxidation resistance among the composites is attributed to differences in the percentages of ZrB2, TiB2, SiC, and complex ceramic oxide systems, as well as variations in the activation energies of the individual  constituents.  From  the  observed  starting  and  ending  temperatures,  it  can  be  inferred  that  an  increase  in  ZrB2 content  and  a decrease  in  TiB2 content  broaden  the  oxidation  resistance  region. Among the five composites studied, S4 (comprising 40 % ZrB2 and 20 % TiB2) exhibited the widest oxidation resistance region, which can likely be attributed to the predominant influence of the zirconium phase. This dominance is thought to arise from differences in melting points and the formation  of  complex  ceramic  oxide  systems  involving  zirconium. However, increasing the ZrB2 content beyond 40 % slightly narrows the oxidation resistance region, possibly due to enhanced oxidation kinetics at higher temperatures. Notably, S4 showed the least mass gain at 1400 ◦ C while maintaining a broad oxidation-resistant behaviour.

A B S T R A C T
Ablation-resistant  zirconium  carbide  (ZrC)  layers  were  coated  onto  carbon/carbon  (C/C)  composites  using vacuum plasma spraying. The structural and ablative characteristics of ZrC layers applied in single-versus multistep coating processes were compared. An increase in the number of coating cycles from 4 to 20 in single-step coating increased the ZrC layer ' s thickness from 32.3 μ m to 116.2 μ m even though the coating rate per cycle was reduced from 8.1 μ m/cycle to 5.8 μ m/cycle. By increasing the number of cycles, the substrate surface was continuously heated, enhancing the lateral spread of molten droplets and improving the porosity and hardness of the ZrC layer. On the other hand, multi-step coating, involving repetition of the single-step process, yielded a 20% thickness  increase  of  ZrC  layers  with  a  higher  coating  rate  while  maintaining  comparable  mechanical properties despite an identical total number of coating cycles. Furthermore, the multi-step-coated layers showed better thermal oxidation resistance than the single-step-coated layers due to the formation of ZrC/ZrC interfaces having larger grain boundaries and the thicker ZrCxOy layers, effectively barring oxygen diffusion. The multi-step coating of identical ZrC layers suggested the better thermal oxidation resistant protection way for C/C composite without the reduction of coated layer ' s properties.

(a) Thermal ablation test with an HVOF facility and surface temperature changes of ZrC layers during the ablation test (inset), comparing single-step coating (black line) and multi-step coating (red line). (b) Cross-sectional morphologies of ZrC layers coated in 20 single-step cycles (Case Z3), (c) double-step-coating cycles (Case Z4), and (d) quintuple-step coating cycles (Case Z5) after the 60-s thermal ablation test. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

3. Results and discussion
oxidation occurred in the coating layer near the C/C composite substrate due to the protection of as-prepared ZrC layer.
The Zr 3d peaks relating to ZrO2 as well as ZrC components indicate that the oxidation from the ablation test extended from the surface to the bottom layer, which was also confirmed by the C 1s and O 1s peaks. As summarized in Table 2, the atomic weight percentages of Zr 3d and C 1s from the surface to the bottom layer increased from 22.71% to 32.29% and  from  25.23%  to  31.26%,  respectively,  while  the  atomic  weight percentage of O 1s decreased from 52.06% to 36.45%. The presence of oxygen  in  ZrC  and  carbon  in  the  ZrO2 layer  could  be  related  to contamination due either to the vertically-cut sample preparation effects As  seen  in  Fig.  7 and  Table  2,  the  oxidized  top  layer,  Layer  1, appeared porous and showed horizontal microcracks due to the volume expansion  caused  by  oxidation.  Layer  2  contained  a  relatively  small oxygen concentration with Zr and C compared to Layer 1, which suggested that the oxygen was dissolved in ZrC at the interface, forming zirconium  oxycarbide  (ZrCxOy)  [44 -47].  Layer  3  undergoes  slow oxidation due to the limited oxygen that has barely penetrated through layer (Layer 2) effectively barring oxygen diffusion by sealing the micropores and microcracks through ZrCxOy formation [44 -47].

1. Introduction
Structural applications at ultra-high temperatures require materials with excellent thermo-mechanical and chemical stability. Examples of such applications include rocket nozzles and hypersonic leading edges. Among the most attractive candidate materials are the refractory diborides of zirconium and hafnium; a comprehensive review of their properties can be found in Ref. 1. In addition to their high melting temperature, high strength at high temperatures and excellent thermal shock resistance, nominally pure diborides show good oxidation resistance up to ∼ 1500 K. In order to improve the resistance to oxidation above this temperature several additives have been extensively tested. The addition of silicon carbide as a second phase was found to promote the formation of an outer borosilicate glass layer, which reduces oxygen diffusion to the substrate thus improving oxidation resistance of UHTC diborides up to ∼ 1900 K. Other additives are under study to further improving oxidation resistance of these compounds at higher temperatures. A review covering the oxidation behaviour of ZrB2 and HfB2 can be found in Ref. 2.
∗ Corresponding author. Tel.: +44 0207 594 7130. E-mail address: m.pettina12@imperial.ac.uk (M. Pettinà).
As these materials are required to operate at extreme temperatures under load, creep is another phenomenon that has to be considered. 3 After some early investigations on ZrB2 reported by Rhodes et al. 4 back in 1970, the interest in creep has been recently growing. 5-10 Researchers have focused on ZrB2, with SiC contents in the range 0-30% in volume. The observed stress exponents suggest diffusion creep as the predominant deformation mechanism, in agreement with the relatively low applied stresses. At higher stresses and temperatures in excess of 1500 K, however, power law creep is expected to become the dominant deformation mechanism. 11

1. Introduction
Low-risk trajectories and sharp configurations (i.e. leading edges with high curvatures) call for TPS able to withstand high temperatures. Indeed, convective heat transfer increases with the curvature, so that it is of great importance to accurately predict how this heat transfer is distributed in the TPS material. At the typical orbital reentry conditions, convective heat transfer to the surface is partly conducted in the solid and partly re-radiated in the atmosphere. When a steady state is achieved, global radiative equilibrium is established, in the sense that the (surface) overall convective heat flux is perfectly balanced by the overall surface radiative flux. When conduction in the solid may be neglected (i.e. TPS materials with very low thermal conductivity), radiative heat transfer locally balances convective heat transfer (local radiative equilibrium). In both cases, the corresponding surface temperature is referred to as 'radiative equilib- rium temperature'. This temperature, depending on flight conditions, may be of the order of up to 3000 K, a temperature that only very specialized materials can withstand. A class of such materials is Ultra High Temperature Ceramics (UHTCs) [6-8,11,15,20,23,29].
The characteristics of UHTCs, such as tensile strength at high temperatures and oxidation resistance, allow them to be used in extreme environments like those associated with hypersonic flight, atmospheric reentry and rocket propulsion. Some compounds belonging to the UHTC family are borides such as Zirconium Diboride (ZrB2) and Hafnium Diboride (HfB2), which have melting temperatures above 3300 K. These materials also retain their strength (up to 150 MPa) at temperatures above 1500 K, exhibit good thermal resistance, and can be modified with additives such as Silicon Carbide (SiC) to promote oxidation resistance and improved strength.
Recent works have been dedicated to hypersonic viscous flows over sharp geometries and on possible improvements related to the utilization of UHTC materials [3,12,19,28,31].

1. Introduction
Ceramic materials with melting points in excess of 3000 ◦ C, so-called ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) such as zirconium and hafnium diborides and carbides, 1 have potential for use in thermal protection systems (TPS) or refractory applications. In addition, zirconium and hafnium diborides have higher thermal conductivity ( ∼ 100 W/(m K) at room temperature and >50 W/(m K) at 1900 ◦ C) 2 than their respective carbides (<40 W/(m K) from room temperature to 800 ◦ C). 3 The combination of high temperature capability and high thermal conductivity is particularly beneficial and highly desirable for hypersonic applications, providing thermal transport by conduction and radiation during exposure to extremely high-temperature reactive environments. 4 TPS for hypersonic applications include sharp nose cones (SNC) and sharp-leading edges (SLE) which improve maneuverability and maximum operating speed of hypersonic vehicles, opening up a new range of hypersonic and re-entry trajectories compared to aerospace vehicles equipped with blunt TPS such as the NASA space shuttle orbiter. However, SNC and SLE maximum surface temperatures increase slightly when the surface radius of curvature decreases under the same heat flux conditions 5 (30 ◦ Cdifference at 1800 ◦ C from blunt, 5 mm, to SNC, 0.5 mm) and temperature reached during hypersonic re-entries range from 1500 ◦ Cto 2000 ◦ Cor even higher depending on re-entry conditions which controls the exposed heat flux. 6,7 In addition, it is highly desirable for a hypersonic vehicle to be reusable and during the last 30 years the only reusable space vehicle has been the space shuttle which is equipped with blunt nose cone and leading edges. Therefore, long-term oxidation kinetic studies are required to understand how UHTCs behave for long exposure times. As mentioned above, ZrB2 and HfB2 potentially fulfill the stringent requirementsforTPSinhypersonicapplications.However,ZrB2

Room-temperature dislocation activity during mechanical deformation of polycrystalline ultra-high-temperature ceramics
In recent years, diborides of transition metals, such as zirconium (Zr) and hafnium (Hf), and their composites have emerged as potential candidates for hightemperature aerospace applications because of their extremely high melting temperature (>3000 /C176 C), hightemperature strength retention, good oxidation resistance at elevated temperatures, etc. [1]. Understanding the mechanical response in this class of ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) is essential for the design of structural components made out of these materials. Recent indentation [2,3] and scratch [2,4] studies by the authors on a polycrystalline zirconium diboride-silicon carbide (ZrB2-5 wt.% SiC) composite have revealed readily detectable metal-like plastic deformation features called slip-lines. Figure 1(a) and (b) are optical micrographs of room-temperature static indentation imprints on polycrystalline ZrB2 and hafnium diboride (HfB2) ceramics, respectively, where intense slip-line or surface-step formation in the vicinity of the indented regions are seen. Such macroscopic slip-steps are clear evidence of dislocationinduced plastic deformation and dislocation mobility at room temperature in these ceramics. Owing to the extremely high melting temperature of these UHTCs, such room-temperature dislocation mobility is unexpected [5]. However, dislocation studies using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) have been extremely limited in ceramics such as ZrB2 and HfB2 [6], particularly in polycrystalline UHTCs. In this work, which also employs TEM, we have investigated the room-temperature dislocation activity responsible for the observed randomly oriented slip-lines in ZrB2 ceramics and identified the slip systems activated during the mechanical deformation. In addition, we have attempted to build an understanding on the origin of room-temperature dislocation activity and mobility in ZrB2 ceramics from crystal structure and chemical bonding perspectives.

question: In SHAO et al. 2023 research on protective ceramic coatings for composite materials, how did the dominant degradation mechanisms of the zirconium carbide-silicon carbide-molybdenum disilicide coating transform across three sequential stages as the intensity of the oxyacetylene flame environment progressively increased?
answer: In SHAO et al. 2023 research, the dominant degradation mechanisms of the zirconium carbide-silicon carbide-molybdenum disilicide coating transformed from oxidation to oxidation plus evaporation and then to the combination of oxidation, evaporation and denudation as the intensity of the oxyacetylene flame environment progressively increased [1].

Ablation behavior and mechanisms of ZrC-SiC-MoSi2 coated C/C-SiC-ZrC ceramic matrix composites under oxyacetylene torch
Meng-meng SHAO, Zhao-ke CHEN, Xin-shuang WANG, Qing-bo WEN, Xiang XIONG
State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China Received 7 September 2021; accepted 1 April 2022
Abstract: To  further  improve  the  anti-ablation  property  of  C/C  composites  in  different  ablation  environments, ZrC-SiC-MoSi2  coatings  containing  34 wt.%  and  60 wt.%  Zr  were  prepared  on  C/C-SiC-ZrC  ceramic  matrix composites via slurry painting (SP) method. And the ablation behavior of the coated composites was investigated in oxyacetylene torch  environment with  three  kinds  of  fluxes  of  oxygen  and  acetylene.  The  results  show  that  with  the increase of Zr content, the particle sizes of ZrC and SiC in the coatings are significantly reduced, with the particles distributing more homogeneously. The linear ablation rates of the coatings with 60 wt.% Zr increase with the increase of oxygen and acetylene fluxes, while for the coatings with 34 wt.% Zr, the linear ablation rates increase at the beginning and then decrease. The ablation mechanisms of the coatings under different conditions were discussed as well. With the increase  of  oxygen  and  acetylene  fluxes,  main  ablation  mechanisms  change  from  oxidation  to  oxidation  plus evaporation and then to the combination of oxidation, evaporation and denudation.
Key words: C/C-SiC-ZrC composite; ZrC-SiC-MoSi2 coating; slurry; ablation

1 Introduction
1003-6326/© 2023 The Nonferrous Metals Society of China. Published by Elsevier Ltd & Science Press deposition (CVD) [11], plasma spray (PS) [12], and slurry  painting  (SP)  [13]  are  main  methods  to prepare UHTC coatings. Among these methods, SP should be a promising method for preparing UHTC coatings  on  the  C/C-SiC-ZrC  composites.  For example,  ZHANG  et  al  [14]  prepared  a  bilayer coating  with  a  HfB 2 -MoSi2 outer  layer  fabricated by SP method on C/Cs composites, which possesses excellent  oxidation  resistance.  QIAN  et  al  [15] reported a Y2O3-doped mullite-ZrSiO4 coating prepared  by  SP,  which  exhibits  good  corrosion resistance.
In  the  past  decades,  much  attention  has  been paid to the composition, structure design and preparation method of anti-ablation coatings as well as  to  their  ablation  behavior  in  a  single  ablation environment [16-18]. However, the ablation behavior  and  mechanism  of  UHTC  coatings  in different ablation environments is seldom explored. In  fact,  the  application  environment  of  thermal structural components in aerospace is rather complicated.  Therefore,  investigating  the  ablation behavior of thermal structural components in different gas flux environments is of key importance for their practical applications [19].
In this work, ZrC-SiC-MoSi2 ceramic coatings with two different Zr contents were prepared on the C/C-SiC-ZrC substrates by slurry painting  (SP)  technology.  In  order  to  improve  the density  of  the  ceramic  coatings  and  the  bonding strength  with  the  substrate,  Al 2 O3  and  CeO2  were used as sintering aids. The microstructure and phase composition of the as-prepared coatings were characterized  using  SEM  and  XRD,  respectively. The ablation behavior of the ZrC-SiC-MoSi2 ceramic coatings was tested by oxyacetylene flame with  three  fluxes  of  oxygen  and  acetylene.  The corresponding ablation mechanisms were discussed as well.

4. Conclusions
A  non-sintered  HfSi2 repair  coating  was  designed  to  restore  the protective function of damaged in-situ ceramic coatings on C/C -SiC -ZrC composites, providing essential short-term ablation resistance to prevent costly components from premature scrapping. Ablation tests conducted under an oxyacetylene flame with a heat flux of 2.38 MW/m 2 for 120 s demonstrated that the repair coating achieved ablation resistance comparable to the  original  in-situ  coating,  effectively  preserving  the substrate and confirming the feasibility of the on-site repair strategy. During ablation, the coating exhibited sequential microstructural evolution:  short-term  exposure  led  to  rapid  HfO2  formation  and  surface densification; intermediate exposure resulted in the development of a two-layer  structure  consisting  of  an  outer  HfO2 layer  and  an  inner HfO2 -Si layer, maintaining structural integrity; and prolonged exposure induced  further  densification  and  lamellar  transformation  of  HfO2, ensuring continued substrate protection, while the two-layer structure gradually disappeared as the Si phase in the inner layer was oxidized and volatilized, leaving a predominantly HfO2 layer.
Compositional and structural optimization produced an HfSi2 -HfO2 repair coating, which reduced the discrepancy between linear and mass ablation  rates  and  promoted  the  formation  of  a  unique  composite structure  that  combines  dense  oxygen-barrier  phases  with  a  porous microstructure, enabling both effective oxidation resistance and longterm thermomechanical stability. This demonstrates that tailoring the repair system can further enhance high-temperature stability and protective performance. Overall, the proposed on-site repair strategy provides a reliable and adaptable approach for restoring and maintaining the ablation resistance of ultra-high-temperature ceramic composites in extreme aerothermal environments.

4.1.1. B modified Si-based ceramic coatings
environment.  To  investigate  the  impact  of  coating  thickness  and chemical composition ratio on the resistance to oxidation and failure mechanism of coatings, the surface of C/SiC composites was coated with various  Si -B -C  compositions  (double-layer  Si -B -C,  SiC/Si -B -C,  and SiC/SiC) by Zuo et al. [9], and these coatings were oxidized in static air for  50  h  to  evaluate  their  anti-oxidation  performance.  The  weight changes of the three samples after oxidation at 700, 1000, 1200 and 1300 ◦ C, respectively (Fig. 17(c)). Significantly, the SiC/Si -B -C coating exhibited a remarkably low weight loss rate across various temperature intervals, primarily attributed to the protective SiC coating that effectively  prevents  oxidation  of  the  intermediate  Si -B -C  coating.  This coating proficiently induced self-healing glass phase formation to repair defects within the coating (Fig. 17(d)). Furthermore, Zhang et al. [19] investigated the water-oxygen behavior and protective mechanism of C/SiC composites coated with Si -B -C coatings. As illustrated in Fig. 17 (e), the weight loss curve of Si -B -C coating oxidized for 6 h was studied at  different  temperatures  in  wet  oxygen.  The  oxidation  products generated by the coating in wet oxygen environment mainly include Si (OH)4, SiO2, H3BO3, B2O3 and B2O, and the rapid volatilization of H3BO3 at the low temperature (700 ◦ C) in wet oxygen environment was identified as the primary cause of coating weight loss in this temperature range. The schematic diagram of oxidation behavior of Si -B -C coatings at different temperatures is shown in Fig. 17(f).

3.3.1. HfC, ZrC and TaC coatings
Based on the excellent high-temperature protection performance of ZrC coating, Wang and Sun et al. [144,145] investigated the effect of microstructures on the ablation resistance of ZrC coatings in different environments. The results showed that the coatings with the preferred orientation of (200) exhibited the lowest ablation rate, and as the heat flux  of  oxyacetylene  flame  increased  from  2.38  to  4.2  MW/m 2 ,  the ablation resistance of ZrC coating significantly decreased, and oxidative erosion became the primary damage mechanism. Besides, the ablated coatings  with  three  different  structures  (outer  ZrO2 layer,  transition ZrCxOy layer and residual ZrC layer) were formed (Fig. 14(g)). The increase in heat flux facilitated the continuous oxygen diffusion, which accelerated the oxidation process of ZrC, and this resulted in the formation  of  ZrCxOy initially  and  eventually  transitioning  to  ZrO2.  In different heat flux environments, the coating exhibited effective against mechanical denudation, with both the transition ZrCxOy and outer layer ZrO2 acted as efficient barriers for oxygen diffusion. In both cases, the corresponding surface temperature is referred to as 'radiative equilib- rium temperature'. This temperature, depending on flight conditions, may be of the order of up to 3000 K, a temperature that only very specialized materials can withstand. A class of such materials is Ultra High Temperature Ceramics (UHTCs) [6-8,11,15,20,23,29].
The characteristics of UHTCs, such as tensile strength at high temperatures and oxidation resistance, allow them to be used in extreme environments like those associated with hypersonic flight, atmospheric reentry and rocket propulsion. Some compounds belonging to the UHTC family are borides such as Zirconium Diboride (ZrB2) and Hafnium Diboride (HfB2), which have melting temperatures above 3300 K. These materials also retain their strength (up to 150 MPa) at temperatures above 1500 K, exhibit good thermal resistance, and can be modified with additives such as Silicon Carbide (SiC) to promote oxidation resistance and improved strength.
Recent works have been dedicated to hypersonic viscous flows over sharp geometries and on possible improvements related to the utilization of UHTC materials [3,12,19,28,31].

A B S T R A C T
This  study  presents  new  findings  on  ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  composites,  where  silicon  carbide  and graphene were incorporated into a zirconium diboride matrix. The research focused on the densification process, and the mechanical and electrical properties of the produced materials. A novel aspect of this work was the investigation of the influence of graphene alignment on composite properties. The results indicated that despite SPS curves suggested full consolidation, the final relative density was lower than expected, and SEM observations revealed the presence of pores in the microstructure. This suggests that additional sintering activators may be necessary to achieve full densification of ZrB2-based materials. Furthermore, high consolidation temperature (2000 ◦ C)  caused  partial  decomposition  of  graphene,  with  only  some  flakes  preserving  their  structure.  Agglomerates of silicon carbide particles were also observed in SiC composites. Regarding mechanical properties, all composites showed a significant increase in hardness compared to pure ZrB2. The highest hardness (1750 HV10) and fracture toughness (3.5 MPa m 1 / 2 ) were achieved for the composite with 40 vol% SiC. For electrical properties, all composites showed enhanced conductivity than the reference ZrB2 sample. Notably, the graphene composites exhibited a clear anisotropy. Specimens tested parallel to the graphene alignment showed a conductivity increase of a few orders of magnitude. In conclusion, this study demonstrates that SPS is an effective method for consolidating ZrB2-based composites without sintering aids, leading to significant improvements in hardness and electrical conductivity. However, achieving full densification may require the use of activators. The observed anisotropy of electrical properties in graphene composites highlights their potential for applications where directional conductivity is desired. This work opens new paths for better understanding ultra-high temperature ceramic-based composites.

Abstract
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are valued for their extremely high melting temperatures and resistance to active oxidation. However, their low fracture strengths and the difficulties in shaping them into complex geometries hamper their widespread application. This study aims to fabricate zirconium diboride-silicon carbide (ZrB2 -SiC) composites reinforced with aligned SiC fibers by formulating suspensions containing a preceramic polymer, ZrB 2 , and SiC fibers. This study assessed the influence of fiber alignment on electrical and thermal conductivities, as well as on mechanical strength. The results revealed a significant enhancement in thermal conductivity, particularly when the fibers were aligned, effectively doubling it compared with the non-aligned parts. Additionally, increasing the fiber content significantly improved the fracture strength, with composites containing 22.5 vol% fibers reaching fracture strengths over 57 MPa. However, the final values did not meet the theoretical expectations because the porosity in pyrolyzed parts exceeded 10%. Furthermore, the study demonstrated a 10-fold increase in electrical conductivity with fiber alignment compared to that for non-aligned composites. These results highlight the capability of paste extrusion-based additive manufacturing in tailoring ultrahigh-temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) with aligned fibers, realizing their suitability for aerospace applications.

Room-temperature dislocation activity during mechanical deformation of polycrystalline ultra-high-temperature ceramics
In recent years, diborides of transition metals, such as zirconium (Zr) and hafnium (Hf), and their composites have emerged as potential candidates for hightemperature aerospace applications because of their extremely high melting temperature (>3000 /C176 C), hightemperature strength retention, good oxidation resistance at elevated temperatures, etc. [1]. Understanding the mechanical response in this class of ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) is essential for the design of structural components made out of these materials. Recent indentation [2,3] and scratch [2,4] studies by the authors on a polycrystalline zirconium diboride-silicon carbide (ZrB2-5 wt.% SiC) composite have revealed readily detectable metal-like plastic deformation features called slip-lines. Figure 1(a) and (b) are optical micrographs of room-temperature static indentation imprints on polycrystalline ZrB2 and hafnium diboride (HfB2) ceramics, respectively, where intense slip-line or surface-step formation in the vicinity of the indented regions are seen. Such macroscopic slip-steps are clear evidence of dislocationinduced plastic deformation and dislocation mobility at room temperature in these ceramics.
