However, dislocation studies using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) have been extremely limited in ceramics such as ZrB2 and HfB2 [6], particularly in polycrystalline UHTCs. In this work, which also employs TEM, we have investigated the room-temperature dislocation activity responsible for the observed randomly oriented slip-lines in ZrB2 ceramics and identified the slip systems activated during the mechanical deformation. In addition, we have attempted to build an understanding on the origin of room-temperature dislocation activity and mobility in ZrB2 ceramics from crystal structure and chemical bonding perspectives.

KEYWORDS
additive manufacturing, fiber reinforced ceramics, polymer derived ceramics, ultra high temperature ceramics
against thermal shocks, safeguarding them against high heat flux variations. Among UHTCs, compounds such as zirconium diboride (ZrB2 ) and hafnium diboride (HfB2 ) have garnered significant attention due to their exceptional attributes, such as high melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C, heightened resistance to oxidation, and impressive thermal conductivities. 2-4 Among the various borides, ZrB 2 stands out in applications involving particleloaded slurries owing to its lower density compared to HfB2 (6.08 vs. 10.5 g/cm 3 ). 5 The incorporation of silicon carbide (SiC) into ZrB2 amplifies both the mechanical properties and oxidation resistance of the resulting composites. 6 Consequently, ZrB2 -SiC composites are emerging as promising candidates for thermo-structural applications such as sharp nose tips and wing leading edges. However, their intrinsic brittleness degrades their mechanical properties and fracture strengths. A well-established approach for enhancing fracture strength involves the introduction of reinforcing components, including particles, 7 graphite flakes, 8 graphene, 9 whiskers, 10 and fibers. 11,12 In particular, the strategic incorporation of fibers into the final structure of the composite has emerged as a viable means to avert catastrophic damage. Reports indicate that incorporating short and continuous fibers shows excellent promise in improving the fracture strength of materials. 13

question: In SHAO et al. 2023, how did the microstructural features and phase compositions of the oxidized coating surface differ between the central region (maximum heat exposure) and the surrounding transition region after the zirconium carbide-silicon carbide-molybdenum disilicide coated specimens were subjected to 60 seconds of oxyacetylene flame ablation?
answer: In SHAO et al. 2023, the microstructural features and phase compositions of the oxidized coating surface in the central region (maximum heat exposure) differed from the surrounding transition region in the following ways: in the central region, almost all of the SiC phase converted to SiO and exited the system, causing the ZrO2 phases to connect directly with each other [5]. In contrast, in the transitional region, a part of the SiC phase had converted to SiO2 and was placed in the space between ZrO2 particles, resulting in the observation of individual grains with a liquid SiO2 phase between them [5]. Additionally, the ZrO2 particle size in the central region was larger compared to the transitional region, which can be attributed to the higher temperature promoting sintering and coarsening of ZrO2 grains [5].

chunks.text:
3.3.2.  Ablation behavior of double- layer SiC/ZrB2  coatings
The coating microstructure in the central region after 60s of ablation under the oxyacetylene flame in Fig. 18 shows the formation of a layer of the liquid phase in this region. Considering that the temperature of the oxyacetylene flame is higher than the ZrO2  melting point (especially at the central region), it can be said that during the test, firstly ZrB2 converts to ZrO2, and then it starts to melt due to the very high temperature of the flame. Although the temperature of the oxyacetylene flame can melt ZrO2, it seems that the presence of SiO2 phase also  can  accelerate  this  process.  The  melting  of  the  protective  layer reduces  its  ablation  resistance  and  consequently,  it  separates  easily from the surface under the flame pressure. By the removal of the surface liquid layer, the sub-surface layers expose to the flame heat and melts, and again it separates from the surface by the flame pressure. In this way, the ablation process of the coating continues gradually in layer by layer  form,  till  the  coating  removes  completely  from  the  surface.  By increasing the ablation time, almost the protective layer in the central region is completely removed. This layer by layer omission shows the good adhesive strength of the coating. If the coating has a low adhesive strength, the coating immediately removes from the surface at the first stage of ablation test (due to pressure of the flame).
Fig. 14. Crack deflection around ZrSiO4 particles.
Fig. 15. SEM image of the coating after 60s oxypropane flame: (a) central region; (b) transitional region; (c) outer region and SEM image of the coating after 300 s oxypropane flame: (d) central region; (e) transitional region; (f) outer region.
Fig. 16. SEM image of the coating after 60s oxyacetylene flame: (a) central region; (b) transitional region and (c) outer region.
Fig. 17. ZrO2 spherical particles in ablated surface.

3.4. Microstructure and ablation performance of the coatings
The  ablation  performance  of  the  coatings  was  assessed  by  an oxyacetylene flame with a heat flux of 2.38 MW/m 2 . Fig. 6a illustrates the  three-dimensional  surface  morphology  of  the  four  coatings  after ablation for 60 s and Fig. 6b shows the profile curves along the axial direction. It can be clearly seen that the Z60T10S30 coating forms an ablated depression in the central region after ablation, and the accumulation phenomenon occurs at the border of the transition region. This phenomenon also corresponds to the positive linear ablation rate of the coating after ablation. This phenomenon occurs because excessive low- melting-point  phases  (SiO2 and  Ta2O5)  form  on  the  sample  surface during ablation, which melt and are more easily eroded by the flame stream,  subsequently  flowing  radially  outward.  With  the  end  of  the ablation process, when the surface temperature of the sample gradually decreases to room temperature, the molten phase gradually transforms into  solid  particulate,  thus  forming  protrusions  around  the  ablation center [39,40]. It can also be observed from the surface roughness that the roughness of the Z60T10S30 coating after ablation is higher than that of the other three coatings. Among them, the surface roughness of the Z80T5S15 coating after ablation is the smallest and amounts 29.62 μ m, which is confirmed by the phenomenon that there is no fluctuation in the central and transition zone seen in the microstructure. Furthermore, the thickness of the Z60T30S10 coating is decreased, while that of the Z80T5S15 coating is increased. From the results of the 3D profile analysis, the ablated Z80T5S15 coating shows a more uniform surface morphology,  smooth  transition  between  central  and  transition  zone without any visible boundary.

3.3.2.  Ablation behavior of double- layer SiC/ZrB2  coatings
Fig. 15 illustrates the microstructure of SiC/ZrB2 coating in central, transitional, and outer ablation regions after 60 and 300 s of oxypropane and oxyacetylene flame tests. As can be observed, for both times of 60 and 300 s, the central region has a dense structure which has been formed due to the sintering of zirconium oxideparticles. The reason for the  formation  of  this  continues  layer  is  the  high  temperature  of  the sample surface in central ablation region which results in the sintering of zirconium oxide grains. By the progress of sintering process, the ZrO2 particles  connect to each other and the pits between them gradually disappear which results in a dense structure on the surface. These dense ZrO2  layer also acts as a thermal barrier and prevents heat transformation to the substrate. By moving from the central region towards the edges (outer region) surface temperature reduces gradually and therefore the continuity of ZrO2  layer decreases. As can be seen in Fig. 15, these  discontinuous  regions  can  be  filled  with  the  SiO2 glass  phase which has been formed due to the oxidation of the SiC layer.

3.3.2.  Ablation behavior of double- layer SiC/ZrB2  coatings
The reason for the difference between the formed layer in oxypropane and oxyacetylene flame tests (Figs. 19 and 20) is related to the temperature difference. The higher temperature of oxyacetylene flame results in the melting of ZrO2 and it separates from the surface layer by layer.
In Fig. 21a, the microstructures of central, transitional, and outer regions of the ablated sample after 120 s under oxyacetylene flame are presented.  It  can  be  seen  that  the  microstructural  characteristics  of these regions are thoroughly different from each other. In the central region which is directly exposed to the flame, the highest temperature (about 3000C) is reached. The coating has completely vanished and the protective layers of ZrO2 or SiO2 do not exist. Linear analysis in Fig. 21a shows  that  by  displacing  from  the  outer  region  towards  the  central region, the weight percentage of carbon increases and the weight percentage of oxygen decreases. Also, the Zr percentage increases from the central region towards the outer region which is due to the removal of the ZrO2 layer in central region. Also, the Si content is considerable at the transitional region. Also, it can be said that the ZrO2  layer at the transitional region has almost disappeared and only SiO2 layer (which is under the ZrO2 layer) has remained on the surface. The EDS analysis and SEM image with higher magnification from the transitional region in  Fig.  21b  also  show  that  a  high  fraction  of  SiO2 is  present  in  this region and only a low amount of ZrO2 has remained on the surface. In the outer region, ZrO2 is still present on the surface which is approved by linear EDS and a higher amount of Zr in this zone.

3.3.2.  Ablation behavior of double- layer SiC/ZrB2  coatings
Comparison of Fig. 15a and b shows that the difference between the coating in the central region and transitional region is the presence of SiO2 liquid phase between ZrO2 phases in the transitional region. In the central  region,  due  to  the  higher  temperature,  almost  all  of  the  SiC phase converts to SiO and exits the system. This cause the ZrO2  connects with together directly. However, in the transitional region, due to the lower temperature, a part of the SiC phase has converted to SiO2 and place in  the  space  between  ZrO2 particles.  Resultantly,  it  is  observed  as  individual  grains  in  which  the  liquid  SiO2  phase has been placed between the grains (Fig. 15c). In the outer region, the amount of SiO2  phase is higher due to its lower temperature compared with the transitional and central regions. In the sample which is ablated under oxyacetylene flame (Fig. 16), due to the very high temperature of the oxyacetylene flame in the central region (about 3000 °C), protective layer  completely  melts  and  then  rapidly  solidifies.  This  prevents  the formation of grain structure in the protective layer. By increasing the distance from the central region the grain structure can be observed due to the decrease in the surface temperature. Also, due to the high temperature and evaporation of SiO2, as well as active oxidation of SiC, the SiO2 phases do not observed on the surface. The other microstructural difference of the central, transitional, and outer regions in Fig. 16 is the ZrO2 particles size. As can be observed, by increasing the distance from the central region to the sample edges, the ZrO2  particles size has decreased. This particles size reduction can be attributed to the reduction in the surface temperature. Temperature increase helps the progress of the sintering process, and resultantly coarsening of ZrO2 grains. On the other hand, by increasing the ablation time from 60s to 300 s, ZrO2 particles size in all three regions has increased due to grain growth.

question: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013, what was the role of the coefficient of thermal expansion differential between lanthanum oxide and silicon carbide phases in explaining the measured behavior of heat transport properties in the rare-earth-doped ultra-high-temperature ceramic composites?
answer: The sources provided do not contain information about the role of the coefficient of thermal expansion differential between lanthanum oxide and silicon carbide phases in explaining the measured behavior of heat transport properties in the rare-earth-doped ultra-high-temperature ceramic composites as discussed in Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013.

chunks.text:
Thermal properties of La2O3-doped ZrB2and HfB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics
E. Zapata-Solvas a , ∗ , 1 , D.D. Jayaseelan a , P.M. Brown b , W.E. Lee a
a Centre for Advanced Structural Ceramics, Imperial College London, SW7 2AZ, UK b Dstl, Porton Down, Salisbury, Wiltshire SP4 0JQ, UK
Received 24 April 2013; received in revised form 7 June 2013; accepted 12 June 2013 Available online 18 July 2013
was observed. High thermal conductivity is required in sharpleading-edge applications as it reduces temperature gradients during heating so minimizing thermal stresses within the UHTC component and improving thermal shock. As a consequence, part of the energy from the heat flux at the stagnation point is more efficiently conducted away from the system enhancing the thermal behavior.
Squire and Marschall 20 reviewed material property requirements for analysis and design of UHTC components in hypersonic applications, pointing out the following material properties as the most important physical and thermal properties; (a) density is a first-order term in the governing equations for thermal and mechanical response, which changed slightly with temperature depending on coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE); (b) CTE is a critical material property as local thermal expansion could produce large internal strains and stresses during rapid heating; and (c) specific heat and thermal conductivity are both first-order terms in the governing equation of energy conservation.

Abstract
The thermophysical properties of carbon fiber-based ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites have been determined to aid designers who need these properties when considering using the composites in ultra-high temperature aerospace applications. The coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) and thermal diffusivity of the composites were measured parallel and perpendicular to the ply direction; the thermal conductivity was measured using the laser-flash method and the heat capacity calculated from the relationship between the thermal diffusivity, density, and thermal conductivity. Both the CTE and thermal conductivity showed higher values across the ply and increased with increasing temperature as expected, whilst the thermal diffusivity  showed  higher  values  parallel  to  the  ply  and  increased  smoothly  with temperature. In addition, two different but related oxyfuel torch tests, based on oxyacetylene and oxypropane, were used to evaluate the thermo-ablation behavior of the composites. The tests showed how good the composites were at withstanding the ultra-high temperatures, high heat fluxes, and gas velocities involved.

1. Introduction
The  advances  in  power  conversion  and  high-speed  aerial  transportation  necessitates  the  development  of  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs),  capable  of  operating  in  aggressive  environments  at T > 2000 ◦ C [1 -4]. The family of high-temperature ceramics is mostly represented by oxide ceramics, Si3N4- and SiC-based composites, and SiC-coated carbon/carbon composites [5 -9]. The carbon-carbon composites provide the highest strength (especially at high temperatures) but are prone to rapid ablation at T > 500 ◦ C [7 -13]. The composites based on SiC and Si3N4  are the current benchmark of oxidation resistance at temperatures below 1600 ◦ C, but suffer from low thermal shock resistance and corrode in presence of water vapors or alkali ions [14].
UHTCs are often based on ZrB2 due to the high thermal conductivity 60  W/m*K,  low  coefficient  of  thermal  expansion  (6 -7) ⋅ 10 GLYPH<0> 6 ◦ C -1 , refractoriness, and high hardness, which endow ZrB2 with a high thermal shock resistance [5 -7,15 -17]. The oxidation of refractory diborides is limited by the formation and destruction of the B2O3 layer [3 -6,18]. B2O3 rapidly evaporates at T > 1100 ◦ C, prompting a fast oxidation-driven  degradation  of  the  ZrB2 ceramics.  To  hamper  the degradation of the protective layer, alloying of ZrB2  by silicon-based

1. INTRODUCTION
Ultra-high-temperature  ceramics  (UHTC)  are  a  group  of  ceramic,  which  can  withstand ultra high temperature (>2000 o C) in oxidizing conditions. Such a high temperature will be encountered by future hypersonic re-entry vehicles (Fahrenholtz et al., 2007 & Opeka et al., 2004). Future re-entry vehicles will have sharp edges to improve aerodynamic performance. Sharp leading edges can improve the cross range (the ability of a vehicle to deviate from the most direct or planned entry path, thereby accessing a greater range of landing opportunities) and can contribute to accuracy and improved safety, especially for crewed vehicles (Johnson, 2012). The sharp leading edges will cause higher surface temperature than that of the actual blunt edged vehicles. The sharp edges have less surface area to dissipate heat and thus the temperature gets increased. To withstand the intense heat generated when these vehicles re- enters to the earth's upper atmosphere, UHTC materials are needed.
Ability of withstanding such conditions depends on melting point, oxidation resistance and high temperature mechanical and physical properties of the material. Carbon-carbon composites have very high melting point but are not oxidationresistant.  Coatings  are  being  developed  for  providing  oxidation-resistance,  but thermal expansion coefficient (CTE) mismatch between the C-C composite and the coating systems results in thermal stresses. The SiC-based composites exhibit oxidation resistance up to 1600◦C in hypersonic environments, but CTE- mismatchinduced matrix cracking allows direct oxidation of the carbon fiber reinforcement (Opeka et al., 2004).

Abstract
Oxidation tests were carried out on HfB2-SiC, HfB2-HfC, HfB2-WC-SiC, and HfB2-WSi2 ceramics using an oxyacetylene torch. The samples were oxidized between 2100 and 2300 ◦ C. From cross-sectional images, scale non-adherence was noted as a limiting factor in oxidation resistance. The sample with the best scale adherence was HfB2-WSi2. Factors involving scale non-adherence such as vapor pressure, coefficient of thermal expansion mismatch and phase transformations were considered. In comparing the scale adherence of the samples it was hypothesized that vapor pressure buildup is the principal contributing factor in the scale adherence differences observed among the tested samples. However, the coefficient of thermal expansion mismatch and HfO2 phase transformation cannot be neglected as contributing factors to scale non-adherence in all samples. © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Keywords: Hafnium diboride; Silicon carbide; Tungsten silicide; Oxidation; Ultra high temperature ceramics

question: In SHAO et al. 2023, what roles did the aluminum oxide and cerium dioxide sintering aid additives play in achieving dense, homogeneously distributed multi-phase protective coatings on carbon composite substrates via slurry painting and high-temperature sintering?
answer: In SHAO et al. 2023, aluminum oxide (Al2O3) and cerium dioxide (CeO2) sintering aid additives were used to improve the density of the ceramic coatings and the bonding strength with the substrate [1]. These additives facilitated the sintering process, contributing to the formation of dense, homogeneously distributed multi-phase protective coatings on carbon composite substrates via slurry painting and high-temperature sintering.

chunks.text:
2.1 Materials
The C/C-SiC-ZrC substrates, with the dimension  of d 28 mm × 10 mm,  were  cut  from  a bulk composite with a density of 2.05 g/cm 3 .  After being polished with 1000 grit SiC paper, the substrates were cleaned by ethanol and then dried at 100 °C  for  3  h.  High  purity  powders  were  mixed together  according  to  the  following  compositions
(mass fraction):  (1)  34%Zr-51%Si-10%MoSi25%(Al2O3+CeO2) (denoted as ZSM-L) and (2) 60%Zr-25%Si-10%MoSi2-5%(Al2O3+CeO2) (denoted  as  ZSM-H).  Zr  and  Si  were  the  main compositions  of  the  coatings.  MoSi 2 was  used  as sintering  aid  to  improve  the  density  of  the  coating owing to its good wettability with ZrC and SiC. In addition,  Al 2 O3  and  CeO2  were  used  as  sintering aids  to  improve  the  liquid  phase  sintering  of  the coatings [4].
The mixed powders were ball milled for 20 h. Then,  they  were  added  into  a  polyvinyl  alcohol (PVA)  solution  with  powders/PVA  mass  ration  of 3:1  to  obtain  the  slurry.  Subsequently,  the  slurry was brushed directly onto the C/C-SiC-ZrC substrate. After drying at 100 °C for 2 h, the brushed samples were placed in an electric furnace and sintered at 1900 °C in argon atmosphere for 4 h with a heating rate of 10 °C/min.

a b s t r a c t
Predictably, if high porosity and high strength are simultaneously realized, the high entropy ultra-high temperature ceramics with light weight and excellent heat-shielding performance would be obtained.
In general, the sintering of highly porous UHTCs is very difficult because of their superhigh melting point and low self-diffusion coefficient [13] . Previous works on processing dense UHTC samples demonstrated that it was hard to obtain highly compact material even by using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) or Hot-Pressing (HP) method above 20 0 0 ° C. It was reported that TaC sample only reached the density of 92% by using SPS technique under pressure of 30 MPa at 2300 ° C [14] . As for high entropy UHTCs, the sintering and multi-component solid solution of principal elements are harder than those of a sample with single principal element. Consequently, nearly all reported works were subjected to reactive sintering by SPS and HP at relatively high temperatures of 22002300 ° C [ 9 , 15-18 ]. Evidently, it is necessary to adopt external driving force to densify UHTCs. In this context, multifarious sintering additives such as SiC, WC, MoSi 2 , TaSi 2 , B 4 C and C were employed to promote sintering kinetics of UHTCs, which facilitated the removal of surface oxides [19-22] . Among these additives, SiC has attracted great interest, because it not only enhanced sinterability, but also improved mechanical properties and oxidation resistance of ultra-high temperature ceramics [22] . Until now, SiC was basically added as a composite component in many literatures about high entropy ceramics. Thereinto, SiC was found to play an essential role in phase formation during sintering of high entropy UHTCs (borides or carbides)/SiC composites [23] . Besides, SiC-reinforced high entropy carbides have been reported to show remarkable mechanical properties by Lu et al. [24] and oxidation resistance by Wang et al. [25] .

1. Introduction
To thrive under extreme high-temperature conditions, the design of UHTC coatings prioritizes exceptional ablation resistance, high mechanical performances and interfacial adhesion, homogeneous material distribution, and a matched coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) with the substrate. For a coating to be deemed qualified, it must endeavor to fulfill these stringent design criteria to the highest possible extent. In order to enhance the performance and service life of UHTC coatings in long-term high temperature and high velocity airflow, researchers began to study UHTC coatings in terms of both structural design and material optimization. Hu et  al .  [13]  designed a SiC/UHTC/SiC  three-layer  coating  on  C/SiC  composites via CVD  and  brushingsintering. The inner SiC layer (CVD at 1050-1150 ℃) reduced thermal mismatch. The ZrB2-based UHTC middle layer (ZrB2/SiC/SiO2/B2O3/Al2O3) was sintered at 900 ℃ in Ar/air, forming borosilicate glass to seal cracks. The outer SiC layer (CVD at 12001250  ℃)  inhibited  B2O3  evaporation  and  oxidized  into  SiO2  at  high  temperatures. During oxidation at 1100-1500 ℃, ZrB2 reacted with oxygen to form ZrO2 and B2O3, while the B2O3-SiO2 glass phase exhibited fluidity to seal cracks. This synergy achieved low  mass  losses  (0.36-3.45  mg/cm²  after  68-114  h),  enabling  long-term  protection through oxygen barrier formation, self-healing, and thermal stress mitigation. Feng et al .  [14]  developed  a  spent  MoSi2-modified  ZrB2-SiC-MoSi2  composite  coating  on carbon substrates via spark plasma sintering (SPS). The coating was sintered at 1500 ℃ under 30 MPa pressure, forming a pore-free structure (~800 μm thick) with continuous and tight metallurgical bonding at  the  interface.  During  oxidation at  1700

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2020.12.262
However, these mentioned coating systems were unable to simultaneously provide good oxidation resistance under static oxygencontaining  and  dynamic  scouring  environments.  Generally,  the  researchers  only  focused  on  the  static  anti-oxidation  or  dynamic  antiablation  properties  of  the  coatings  [26 -32].  However,  facing  the increasingly severe application environments, the coatings must have better oxidation protection capabilities, which could make carbon materials operate in oxidation conditions with a wide temperature range (even up to 3273 K). Hence, some researchers have been focused on adding another high temperature phase in the composite coating to form multi-phase UHTCs ceramics. By comparison, relatively little attention has been directed towards the development of a single-phase UHTCs boride comprising two refractory metal cation, namely UHTCs boride solid solution. It is reported that (HaTa)B2 [33] and (ZrHf)C [34] composite  coatings  show  good  oxidation  and  ablation  resistance,  respectively,  mainly  due  to  the  synergy  of  two  cation  compounds  in  a high-temperature aerobic environment. But these two coatings are not dense enough, they can not provide considerable oxidation resistance in multiple environments including static oxygen-containing and dynamic scouring environments. Therefore, it is of key importance to construct a special  ultra-high-temperature  protective  coating  system  with  good oxidation and erosion resistance. In our previous work, dense monolayer multiphase  coatings  including  MoSi2 -CrSi2 -SiC -Si  and  TaB2 -SiC -Si coatings  have  been  successfully  prepared  by  dipping  combined  with in-situ reaction [35,36]. The former could provide oxidation protection at 1873 K in air for more than 1190 h and ablation protection for 60 s at 2773  K  under  an  oxyacetylene  flame,  respectively.  The  latter  could endure 108 h of oxidation at 1823 K in air and 120 s of ablation under a heat flux of 2.4 MW/m 2 , respectively. These results indicate the superiority of combination of dipping and in-situ reaction.

1 Introduction
1003-6326/© 2023 The Nonferrous Metals Society of China. Published by Elsevier Ltd & Science Press deposition (CVD) [11], plasma spray (PS) [12], and slurry  painting  (SP)  [13]  are  main  methods  to prepare UHTC coatings. Among these methods, SP should be a promising method for preparing UHTC coatings  on  the  C/C-SiC-ZrC  composites.  For example,  ZHANG  et  al  [14]  prepared  a  bilayer coating  with  a  HfB 2 -MoSi2 outer  layer  fabricated by SP method on C/Cs composites, which possesses excellent  oxidation  resistance.  QIAN  et  al  [15] reported a Y2O3-doped mullite-ZrSiO4 coating prepared  by  SP,  which  exhibits  good  corrosion resistance.
In  the  past  decades,  much  attention  has  been paid to the composition, structure design and preparation method of anti-ablation coatings as well as  to  their  ablation  behavior  in  a  single  ablation environment [16-18]. However, the ablation behavior  and  mechanism  of  UHTC  coatings  in different ablation environments is seldom explored. In  fact,  the  application  environment  of  thermal structural components in aerospace is rather complicated.  Therefore,  investigating  the  ablation behavior of thermal structural components in different gas flux environments is of key importance for their practical applications [19].
In this work, ZrC-SiC-MoSi2 ceramic coatings with two different Zr contents were prepared on the C/C-SiC-ZrC substrates by slurry painting  (SP)  technology.  In  order  to  improve  the density  of  the  ceramic  coatings  and  the  bonding strength  with  the  substrate,  Al 2 O3  and  CeO2  were used as sintering aids. The microstructure and phase composition of the as-prepared coatings were characterized  using  SEM  and  XRD,  respectively. The ablation behavior of the ZrC-SiC-MoSi2 ceramic coatings was tested by oxyacetylene flame with  three  fluxes  of  oxygen  and  acetylene.  The corresponding ablation mechanisms were discussed as well.

question: In the Hu et al. 2023 air-plasma-sprayed ZrB2-based ultra-high-temperature ceramic coatings, what chemical element becomes depleted during the high-temperature treatment process, and what oxide compound derived from this element remains in the sprayed deposit?
answer: In the Hu et al. 2023 air-plasma-sprayed ZrB2-based ultra-high-temperature ceramic coatings, the chemical element boron (B) becomes depleted during the high-temperature treatment process, and the oxide compound derived from this element that remains in the sprayed deposit is B2O3 [1][2][3].

chunks.text:
a b s t r a c t
As for the air plasma sprayed ZrB 2 -based coatings, B content change caused by inevitable oxidation is predictable but commonly ignored. Affected by air plasma flame, the B element loss and residual B 2 O 3 in the sprayed ZrB 2 coating were observed. Moreover, how the B content change affects the microstructure, phase evolution, and ablation resistance (2.4 MW/m 2 , 60 s) of ZrB 2 -based coatings with different secondary phases (SiC, MoSi 2 , and TaC) was investigated. The B element loss contributed to the increase in surface temperature and the decline in the sintering degree of the ZrO 2 layer. The evaporation of residual B 2 O 3 caused damage to the coating structure in the form of pores, whose negative effect was enhanced and reduced by MoSi 2 and TaC secondary phases, respectively. This work will provide some insight into thermally sprayed non-oxide ceramic coatings in the atmosphere.
© 2023 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
tracted wide attention (for example, ZrB 2 -SiC [15] , ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 -WB [16] , ZrB 2 -SiC-TaC [17] ). Due to the strong cooling capacity and great densification ability on the surface oxide layer of B 2 O 3 oxidized from ZrB 2 , B element in the plasma sprayed ZrB 2 -SiC coating has been considered effective to the long-term ablation protection ( > 300 s) [18] .

4. Conclusions
Minor oxygen in the air plasma flame will inevitably result in the B content change of sprayed ZrB 2 -based coatings. The ZrB 2 -based ceramics (B-rich), air plasma sprayed ZrB 2 -based coatings (B-oxidized), and air plasma sprayed ZrC-based coatings (Bremoved) were respectively used to describe the Zr-based ceramics with different boron contents. By introducing different secondary phases (SiC, MoSi 2 , and TaC), what effect the B content change shows on the ablation behaviors was further investigated. The main conclusions are as follows:
- (1) Assisted with multiple experimental methods, the B element loss and the existence of residual B 2 O 3 phase in the air plasma sprayed ZrB 2 coatings were verified.
- (2) By comparing the ablation behaviors of ZrB 2 -based coatings and ZrC-based coatings, the B element loss resulted in the surface temperature increase and the decrease of oxide layer sintering.
- (3) Compared with ZrB 2 -TaC system, the more significant evaporation of the residual B 2 O 3 phase in the ZrB 2 -SiC and ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 systems showed more severe damage on the whole coating structure.
- (4) As for high-temperature service (for instance, above B 2 O 3 boiling point), considering the rapid evaporation of residual B 2 O 3 in the ZrB 2 -based coatings, the air plasma sprayed ZrCbased coatings might be preferably considered.

3.3. Effect of B content change on the ablation behaviors of ZrB 2 -based coatings
Different from SiC secondary phase, when MoSi 2 phase is introduced into the sprayed coatings ( Fig. 11 (d-f)), the effect of Mo and Si elements should be considered together. As for the ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 ceramic ( Fig. 12 (d)), due to the sufficient B and Si produced from ZrB 2 and MoSi 2 , the formed surface ZrO 2 layer will be easily sintered dense. As for sprayed ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 coating ( Fig. 12 (e)), air plasma flame will inevitably cause the simultaneous loss of B and Si elements, as well as residual B 2 O 3 and Mo-rich phase, The Si element loss phenomenon and Mo-rich phase residual in the sprayed MoSi 2 coating were verified in our previous work [35] . Once the Si element has been consumed excessively, the Mo-rich phase will be oxidized into harmful MoO x and result in a bubble burst [8] . Hence, the severe damage to the coating structure ( Fig. 10 (e)) and oxide layer peeling ( Fig. 12 (e)) in the sprayed ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 coating were observed, resulting from co-evaporation of residual B 2 O 3 and gaseous products MoO x . According to the inset figure of Fig. 12 (d), the Mo element was still kept in the ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 ceramic in the form of a Mo-B compound [36] with the molar ratio of Mo:B = 67.24:32.76. As a result, despite of higher surface temperature of ZrB 2 -based ceramic, bubble burst did not occur and the formed oxide layer was still kept intact. As for the ZrCMoSi 2 coating ( Fig. 12 (f)), after removing the residual B 2 O 3 phase, the whole coating structure suffered less severe damage, only under the action of gaseous products MoO x .

3.3. Effect of B content change on the ablation behaviors of ZrB 2 -based coatings
The macroscopic view of the Zr-based samples with different boron contents after 60 s ablation is presented in Fig. 10 . As shown in Fig. 10 (a-c), after 60 s ablation at the heat flux of 2.4 MW/m 2 , although the sintered ceramic exhibits poor thermal shock resistance, no oxide layer peeling is observed in the ZrB 2 -SiC sintered ceramic ( Fig. 10 (a)). The formed oxide layer in the ZrB 2 -SiC sprayed coating ( Fig. 10 (b)) shows a peeling-off trend while that of the ZrC-SiC sprayed coating ( Fig. 10 (c)) is still kept intact. When the secondary phase is MoSi 2 , despite the interaction between B and Si, the effect of Mo element after air plasma spraying is highlighted in Fig. 10 (d-f). The ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 sintered ceramic still keeps a complete oxide layer only with small pores in Fig. 10 (d). As for the ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 sprayed coating ( Fig. 10 (e)), the giant bugle can be clearly seen. When B element is completely removed, the ZrCMoSi 2 sprayed coating ( Fig. 10 (f)) only displays small bulges. According to Fig. 10 (g-i), the spattering oxide and surface fluctu- ation are respectively observed in the ZrB 2 -TaC sintered ceramic ( Fig. 10 (g)) and the ZrB 2 -TaC sprayed coating ( Fig. 10 (h)), while the ZrC-TaC sprayed coating ( Fig. 10 (i)) is covered by white oxides with no obvious change.

4. Conclusions
A  270 μ m thick  ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  (UHTC)  coating, based on ZrB2 with additions of WC and SiC, was successfully deposited by shroud plasma spray on a ceramic matrix composite (CMC) upon proper tuning of powder feedstock compactness and granule size.
Compositional changes from the powder feedstock to the coating were  compatible  to  what  observed  on  sintered  bulks  with  the  same composition, including reaction of WC with boron oxide, yielding WB, and  negligible  ZrB2 oxidation,  which  retained  a  mean  grain  size  of around 1 -3 μ m and was surrounded by a specific (Zr,W)B2  solid solution, while SiC remained as discrete particles distributed at the triple junctions.
The  thermal  erosion  behavior  of  the  coating  was  tested  under extreme cyclic thermal-shock conditions using a combustion chamber and  different  oxygen  contents,  1  and  33 wt%  to  simulate  different phases of flights, which achieved 1500 -1600 ◦ C. Microstructural evolution upon testing highlighted minimal material recession, in the order of 100 -140 µ m and, particularly, the UHTC coating survived 10 heatingcooling cycles from 1500 ◦ C to 700 ◦ C in an air-fuel mixture with 1 wt% oxygen.  Presence  of  higher  oxygen  content  in  the  chamber  enabled longer coating duration thanks to the boosting of self-healing mechanisms. In all cases, no signs of coating delamination or crater formation were observed, indicating good adhesion strength of the coating to the Cf/C-SiC substrate. On the contrary, the uncoated same CMC reference was destroyed after 100 s.
A continuous layer of unoxidized UHTC remained well anchored to the  underlying  CMC,  thus  assessing  the  good  prospectives  of  these coatings for their implementation in next-generation thermal protection systems (TPS).

question: In the Hu et al. 2023 study of air-plasma-sprayed ZrB2 coatings, what quantitative phase composition was measured in the treated powder using reference intensity ratio analysis, and what three distinct crystalline or amorphous phases comprise the sprayed deposit?
answer: In the Hu et al. 2023 study, the quantitative phase composition of the treated ZrB2 powder using reference intensity ratio (RIR) analysis was evaluated as 44 wt.% (or 43 vol.%) ZrB2 and 56 wt.% (or 57 vol.%) ZrO2 [4]. The three distinct crystalline or amorphous phases that comprise the sprayed deposit are ZrB2, m-ZrO2, and B2O3 [1][3].

chunks.text:
3. Results and discussion
Among them, some pores exist, which can be deduced to the water evaporation during the spray granulation process. From the spot EPMA analysis results (spots 13) in Table 1 , the complex phase distribution in the sprayed ZrB 2 coating was verified, which might consist of ZrB 2 , ZrO 2 , and B 2 O 3 phases.

Fig. 6 shows the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves and differential thermal analysis (DTA) curves in the air of ZrB 2 powders before and after plasma spraying. TGA curves are commonly used to analyze the mass change induced by chemical reactions with the temperature rising. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the significant inflection points of mass gain in two kinds of ZrB 2 powder are both located at ∼ 650 ° C, which can be attributed to the decreased oxidation protection ability of melted B 2 O 3 . With the continued temperature rise, both the two ZrB 2 powders exhibit rapid weight gain. Differently, the ZrB 2 powder after spraying shows a much slower weight gain rate (64% decline) than that of the original ZrB 2 powder. According to the above quantitative XRD analytical results in Fig. 3 (a), about 57 vol.% ZrB 2 phase was oxidized into the ZrO 2 phase. That is, the weight gain rate of ZrB 2 powder after spraying will theoretically decrease by 57%, instead of a 64% decline. Considering that the amorphous phase cannot be effectively detected in the XRD test, the additional 7% difference may result from the residual amorphous B 2 O 3 phase. That is, the phase composition of sprayed ZrB 2 powder can be inferred as 42 vol.%ZrB 2 , 53 vol.% m-ZrO 2 , and 5 vol.% B 2 O 3 (Here, assuming that the ZrB 2 oxidation weight gain rate is consistent with the B 2 O 3 evaporation weight loss rate). According to the DTA curves in Fig. 6 (b), two endothermic peaks at ∼ 200 ° C and ∼ 1200 ° C and an exothermic peak at ∼ 650 ° C are observed, respectively corresponding to B 2 O 3 crystallization, B 2 O 3 evaporation, and ZrB 2 oxidation. Generally, ZrB 2 powder will form a thinner amorphous B 2 O 3 layer (13 nm) at room temperature on the bare surface to prevent further oxidation [20] .

Fig. 8 (a) presents the schematic diagram of the crystal structures of ZrB 2 and m-ZrO 2 . Although not exposed to air plasma flame, the original ZrB 2 powder also exhibits some local amorphous regions from Fig. 8 (b) due to the slight room temperature oxidation. Under the action of air plasma flame, the inevitable oxidation of ZrB 2 will lead to two kinds of results: slight oxidation and complete oxidation, as observed in Fig. 2 (d). As for the slight oxidation in Fig. 8 (c), the ZrB 2 lattice was kept but a large number of amorphous regions formed. question: In Hu et al. 2023's investigation of ablation-resistant coatings incorporating titanium carbide (TaC) as a reinforcing phase alongside zirconium diboride, how did this secondary phase affect the detrimental effects of boron-oxide compound formation during thermal testing?
answer: In Hu et al. 2023's investigation, the addition of TaC as a reinforcing phase alongside zirconium diboride (ZrB2) was found to reduce the negative effects caused by the formation of residual B2O3 during thermal testing. Specifically, the evaporation of residual B2O3 can cause damage to the coating structure in the form of pores, but this negative effect was reduced by the presence of TaC secondary phase [1].

As for the air plasma sprayed ZrB 2 -based coatings, B content change caused by inevitable oxidation is predictable but commonly ignored. Affected by air plasma flame, the B element loss and residual B 2 O 3 in the sprayed ZrB 2 coating were observed. Moreover, how the B content change affects the microstructure, phase evolution, and ablation resistance (2.4 MW/m 2 , 60 s) of ZrB 2 -based coatings with different secondary phases (SiC, MoSi 2 , and TaC) was investigated. The B element loss contributed to the increase in surface temperature and the decline in the sintering degree of the ZrO 2 layer. The evaporation of residual B 2 O 3 caused damage to the coating structure in the form of pores, whose negative effect was enhanced and reduced by MoSi 2 and TaC secondary phases, respectively. This work will provide some insight into thermally sprayed non-oxide ceramic coatings in the atmosphere.

3.3. Effect of B content change on the ablation behaviors of ZrB 2 -based coatings
Due to the poor oxygen resistance of the porous ZrO 2 layer formed on the monophase ZrB 2 , the Zr-X-O (X = Si, Ta) compound films were commonly investigated to inhibit oxygen invasion. On the other hand, affected by secondary phases, the effects of B element loss and residual B 2 O 3 in the sprayed ZrB 2 -based coatings might be enhanced or reduced. Hence, the interaction between boron content change and different secondary phases (SiC, MoSi 2 , and TaC) was further considered. Among them, liquid SiO 2 oxidized from SiC and MoSi 2 will help fill gaps between ZrO 2 particles and promote the oxide layer sintering, while Ta 2 O 5 oxidized from TaC will form Zr-Ta-O low-melting-point compound with ZrO 2 layer. The ZrB 2 -based ceramics (B-rich), sprayed ZrB 2 -based coatings (Boxidized), and sprayed ZrC-based coatings (B-removed) were respectively used to describe the Zr-based ceramics with different boron contents.

In recent years, many studies have increasingly investigated incorporation of SiC or MoSi2 as reinforcing phase to enhance the ablation resistance of ZrC or TaC-based coatings [10,30]. Most of the ablation experiments of these multi-component coatings use a low-flow oxyacetylene flame (2.4 MW/m 2 ). However, few studies have focused on the ablation resistance of TaC-ZrC-SiC ternary synergistic composite-reinforced coatings under high-heat-flux conditions (4.2 MW/m 2 ). Furthermore, the effect of the SiC  molar  ratio  on  the  ablation  resistance  of  TaC-ZrC-SiC  composite coatings requires further investigation.
To address these challenges, this work proposes a multicomponent composite coating strategy. SiC powder (excellent oxidation resistance) was uniformly blended with ZrC (high melting point) and TaC (high melting point, low oxygen diffusivity) powders. Three distinct TaC-ZrCSiC (TZS) granulated feedstocks with different molar ratios were prepared  via  spray  granulation.  TZS  coatings  were  then  prepared  using atmospheric plasma spraying (APS). By systematically varying the SiC molar ratio, we investigated its influence on coating characteristics and ablation resistance.  The ablation  performance of the differently composed TZS coatings was evaluated via oxyacetylene flame testing (4.2 MW/m 2 ). Furthermore, the ablation resistance mechanisms of the TZS  coatings  were  elucidated,  revealing  the  composition-structureperformance relationships. This work provides a theoretical and experimental foundation for developing advanced adaptive thermal protection coatings.

There are two possible reasons for such high values: (i) a complex composite structure with uniform distribution of newly formed secondary boron carbide phase (Fig. 2); and (ii) interfacial microcracking [23] as result of large difference in the linear thermal expansion between TiB2 matrix and B4C inclusion. Finally, (iii) small value of the grain size, which corresponds to the initial particle size of raw TiB2 powder (i.e. 2-5 𝛍 m). Recent analysis in [6] showed that strength of monolithic titanium diboride ceramic have a peak in strength for a grain size between 3 and 5 𝛍 m. This shows a good correlation with data obtained in the present study.
In case of composites, there is no obvious interdependence between the TiB2 grain size in TiB2-B4C ceramic composites, since in the majority of these ceramic composites titanium diboride was added as the reinforcement phase, while in the present study it serves as a matrix. One noticeable observation is that microcracking [23] was observed after room temperature and in part at 600 ◦ C flexural strength test.
This situation may be attributed to the residual stresses formed during the cooling process. In case of TiB2-B4C system consolidated at temperature of 1900 ◦ C, the mismatch in the coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) between TiB2 and B4C will result in formation of the residual stresses in the bulk ceramic composite [23,24]. Using the Taya's [24] model and data of [12] and temperature difference of 1600 ◦ K one may evaluate that residual stresses on the diboride matrix 𝛔 m and boron carbide 𝛔 i are 348 MPa and -1165 MPa, respectively. Here negative value of the residual stress indicates compression, while positive value suggests tension.

4. Conclusions
Based on predictive models for mass ablation rate and linear ablation rate,  four  compositions  exhibiting  mass/linear  ablation  rates  in  the range of 10 GLYPH<0> 4 (g/s,  mm/s) were selected and subsequently validated through  oxyacetylene  tests  conducted  over  a  duration  of  60 s.  All exhibited excellent ablation resistance, with linear ablation rate errors < 2 %. TaC addition significantly densified the ablation-formed oxide layer, hindering oxidant penetration. However, high-TaC coatings (e.g., Z60T30S10) formed excessive Zr6Ta2O17, reducing oxide layer thermal stability/structural integrity and causing defects (pores, heterogeneous sintering). The  Zr/Ta atomic ratio critically influenced oxide morphology: high ratios ( ≥ 15) produced a continuous molten-like oxide; intermediate ratios (5 GLYPH<0> 15) formed ' lotus leaf ' -shaped (ZrO2-dominant) phases, low ratios ( < 5) resulted in ' particulate-like ' (Zr6Ta2O17-dominant) structures. The application of ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) coatings is  an  effective  way  to  improve  the  in-service  performance  of  CMC structures  at  higher  operating  temperatures  and  for  longer  duration without  dramatically  hampering  the  overall  weight  of  the  structure. Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is the preferred compound, because of its excellent combination of high melting temperature, high thermal conductivity (40 -100 W/m K) [9 -14], and superior ablation [15 -18] and erosion resistance [19,20] . Particularly, ceramics in the ZrB2 - WC - SiC system have shown to possesses outstanding strength retention up to 2100 ◦ C [21] and high thermal erosion resistance at 2600 -2800 ◦ C [22, 23]. As compared to pure ZrB2, these properties seem to be related first to  a  better  oxidation  resistance  of  the  (Zr,W)B2  solid  solution grown around the original ZrB2  cores upon sintering compared to pure ZrB2 [24,25], coupled with the formation of an outermost more protective oxide scale, where possible stabilization of B2O3 glass by W occurs [26], and with the formation of a more dense subscale, where ZrO2  grains encapsulate W solid products, as a consequence of the (Zr,W)B2  solid solution oxidation. 1. Introduction
Transition metal diborides (HfB2  and ZrB2), based Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics (UHTCs), characterized by extreme melting point ( > 3000 ◦ C) [1], high oxidation resistance (OR) at elevated temperatures (~1400 -1500 ◦ C) [2], and excellent mechanical properties (Hardness > 17 GPa, Elastic Modulus: 300 -400 GPa) [3,4], establishes them as potential material for thermal protection systems. Nonetheless, the presence of covalent bonds and lower value of self-diffusion coefficient of these ceramics restricts them from being fully sintered and, therefore, methods such as spark plasma sintering (SPS) [5], hot pressing (HP) [6, 7], and microwave sintering [8], are employed along with carbide-based sintering  additives  like  Silicon  carbide  (SiC),  Boron  Carbide  (B4C), Carbon Nanotubes (CNT), etc. [9,10]. SiC reinforcement in HfB2  and ZrB2  increases  densification  (up  to  100  %)  [11],  improves  hardness (from 13 to 25 GPa) [12,13], fracture toughness (up to 6.5 MPa.m 0.5 ) [14], and provides oxidation resistance through development of a protective SiO2  barrier at 1200 ◦ C [15]. 4. Conclusions
A  270 μ m thick  ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  (UHTC)  coating, based on ZrB2 with additions of WC and SiC, was successfully deposited by shroud plasma spray on a ceramic matrix composite (CMC) upon proper tuning of powder feedstock compactness and granule size.
Compositional changes from the powder feedstock to the coating were  compatible  to  what  observed  on  sintered  bulks  with  the  same composition, including reaction of WC with boron oxide, yielding WB, and  negligible  ZrB2 oxidation,  which  retained  a  mean  grain  size  of around 1 -3 μ m and was surrounded by a specific (Zr,W)B2  solid solution, while SiC remained as discrete particles distributed at the triple junctions.
The  thermal  erosion  behavior  of  the  coating  was  tested  under extreme cyclic thermal-shock conditions using a combustion chamber and  different  oxygen  contents,  1  and  33 wt%  to  simulate  different phases of flights, which achieved 1500 -1600 ◦ C. Microstructural evolution upon testing highlighted minimal material recession, in the order of 100 -140 µ m and, particularly, the UHTC coating survived 10 heatingcooling cycles from 1500 ◦ C to 700 ◦ C in an air-fuel mixture with 1 wt% oxygen.  Presence  of  higher  oxygen  content  in  the  chamber  enabled longer coating duration thanks to the boosting of self-healing mechanisms. In all cases, no signs of coating delamination or crater formation were observed, indicating good adhesion strength of the coating to the Cf/C-SiC substrate. On the contrary, the uncoated same CMC reference was destroyed after 100 s.
A continuous layer of unoxidized UHTC remained well anchored to the  underlying  CMC,  thus  assessing  the  good  prospectives  of  these coatings for their implementation in next-generation thermal protection systems (TPS).

1. Introduction
ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) exhibit outstanding properties of high melting point, high electrical and thermal conductivities, chemical inertness and good oxidation resistance [1], which interests many researchers and engineers to consider them as the attractive candidates for high temperature applications where corrosion-wear-oxidation resistance is demanded [1,2]. Because of the limited sinterability of ZrB2, reported studies have indicated that SiC could enhance the densification of ZrB2 efficiently [3,4]. Zirconia (ZrO2 ) has been investigated extensively for its excellent fracture toughness, strength, as well as other intrinsic physical and chemical properties [5]. The attractive applications of ZrO2-based composites are in the fields of general ceramics [6], electrolytes [7], as well as the thermal barrier coatings [8]. Particularly, the most dramatic increase in the industrial applicability of zirconia has been brought about by the discovery of the stress-induced phase transformation toughening from tetragonal (t-ZrO2) to monoclinic phase (m-ZrO2) [9].
Thermal shock resistance is a major issue for the use of ceramics [10-12]. Concerning the ceramics containing ZrO2, analysis of residual strength, critical temperature difference and thermal stress were achieved in previous [11,12]. A B S T R A C T
The ZrB2 is widely recognized as an ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) due to its high melting point, high thermal conductivity, and superior  thermal  stability,  making  it  suitable  for  application  at  temperatures ≥ 1400 ◦ C [1 -3]. The ZrB2  exhibits a very remarkable resistance to high-temperature structural degradation, thanks to the in-situ formation of a thermally stable ZrO2-rich oxide scale, further enhancing its overall performance by providing thermal insulation [4 -8]. Furthermore, the addition of SiC as reinforcement in the ZrB2 matrix not only improves its strength and fracture toughness, but also its ability to withstand oxidation and ablation [5,7]. Another advantage of the SiC being present as reinforcement is its ability to impede the growth of ZrB2 grains during the sintering process, resulting in superior densification, leading  to  enhanced  mechanical  properties.  It  has  been  conclusively demonstrated  that  among  the  ZrB2-based  composites  with  different proportions of SiC, the one containing 20 vol % SiC as reinforcement exhibits the most favourable combination of mechanical and thermo-physical properties, along with high resistance to ablation at elevated  temperatures  [9,10].  However,  both  monolithic  ZrB2 and ZrB2 -SiC composites show the same type of brittle catastrophic failure Design  and  optimization  of  the  process  for  fabricating  ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) with continuous carbon fibre (Cf) as a reinforcement has recently garnered significant interest among the researchers globally [12]. The strong interest in this area of research stems from the remarkable improvement in fracture toughness and thermal shock resistance, along with the observation of damage tolerant behaviour in the continuous Cf  reinforced UHTCMCs. 1. Introduction
Characteristics of ZrB2-SiC ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) reinforced with ZrC and carbon fiber (Cf) were investigated in this article. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) process was utilized to fabricate the samples at 1800 °C for 5 min under 30 MPa punch pressure and vacuumed atmosphere. In all samples, the volume ratio of ZrB2: SiC was equal to 4:1, and the summation of ZrC and Cf reinforcements was 7.5 vol% with different ZrC: Cf ratios. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), densitometry, flexural strength, and hardness measurements were employed for characterization of the prepared samples. Microstructural inspection revealed the formation of SiC sheath around the carbon fibers due to several reactions in the surface SiO2 layers existed on the SiC particles. Optimal flexural strength (628.4 MPa) and hardness (20.8 GPa) values were achieved for the sample co-reinforced with 6.5 vol% ZrC and 1 vol% Cf, with a relative density of 97.7%.

Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are commonly referred to as ceramics with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C and are desirable for structural applications at temperatures above 1500 ◦ C [1,2]. Owing to its excellent properties, like high melting point and lowest density in UHTCs, ZrB2 has been extensively studied [3,4]. Based on the difficulties in densification of monolithic ZrB2  due to strong covalent bonds, low diffusion coefficient, and limited fracture toughness, numerous studies focused on using sintering additives and reinforcing phases to fabricate ZrB2-based composite ceramics with comparatively improved properties [5,6]. Wang et al. [7] fabricated almost dense ZrB2-SiC composites using AlN and Si3N4  as sintering aids. The  fracture toughness of ZrB2  was increased from 5.46 MPa m 1/2 to 6.81 MPa m 1/2 by using 15 vol% SiC whiskers.  Demirskyi  et  al.  [8]  reported  a  Zr-Ta  multiboride  ceramic composite with highly reproducible repetitive superstructure via reaction-driven consolidation of ZrB2, Ta, and amorphous B powders, which led to extremely high hardness (28.6 GPa and 22.6 GPa using 98 N and 196 N loads).

where K C stands  for  the  fracture  toughness  (Llorca  &  Orera,  2006).  Thus  the  improvements in strength of DSE ceramics are associated with an increase of the toughness or a reduction of the defect size, which is normally related to the characteristic dimension of the microstructure. At the same time the crack deflection is a well-known toughening mechanism in ceramic composites with columnar or lamellar structures. For instance, crack deflection in fiber-reinforced ceramics or laminated ceramics is achieved through weak fiber-matrix interfaces or by inserting layers with very low toughness, respectively (Pardo, Peña, Merino, Cases, Larrea, & Orera, 2002). In case of DSE ceramics, the fracture toughness of single phase ceramic layers is not so high and it allows to realise crack deflection toughening mechanism. On the other hand, the thermal residual stresses induced by a thermal expansion mismatch between the phases in DSE ceramics could be used to increase the toughness by enhancing the crack deflection (Llorca & Orera, 2006).
Reduction in the domain thickness can also be achieved by adding a third phase to form a ternary eutectic composite. For example, ternary eutectic Al 2 O3 -YAGYSZ showed higher room temperature strength than binary Al 2 O3 -YAG grown at the same rate (Lee, Yoshikawa, Fukuda, & Waku, 2001; Waku, Sakata, Mitani, Shimizu, & Hasebe, 2002). This indicates that the defect size for the Al 2 O3 -YAG binary eutectic was controlled by the lamella thickness. At present the maximum flexural strength (4.6 GPa) was achieved for DSE Al 2 O3 -YAG-YSZ (Oliete, Peña, Larrea, Orera, Llorca, Pastor, Martin, & Segurado, 2007). This is approximately four times higher than the best results measured in bulk non-oxide ceramics and twice

question: In Zhang et al. 2019's investigation of high-temperature air-exposure resistance in directionally solidified ZrB2-SiC composites, what was the magnitude of the protective oxide layer formed on different sample surfaces at 1873 Kelvin exposure, and how did orientation affect this protective layer thickness?
answer: In Zhang et al. 2019's investigation, the magnitude of the protective oxide layer formed on different sample surfaces at 1873 K exposure was as follows: on the top of the DS sample, the oxidation layer thickness was 205 ± 3.8 m m [3]; on the side of the DS sample, the oxidation layer thickness was 65 ± 4.3 m m [3]; and on the side of a sample prepared by crushing DS samples, the oxidation layer thickness was 160 ± 3.2 m m [3]. The orientation significantly affected the protective layer thickness, with the side of the DS sample showing a much thinner oxide layer compared to the top surface. This difference can be attributed to the continuous SiC layer on the side facing the inward oxygen diffusion, which resulted in the formation of a silica layer that acted as an effective barrier [2].

chunks.text:
4. Oxidation studies
On the top side the zirconia layer did not offer effective protection against oxidation and the scale thickness was about 205 m m, which is comparable to that reported for pure ZrB2 [29]. A sample prepared by crushing DS samples prepared as detailed in Ref. [30] had a random orientation with respect to the inward oxygen /uniFB02 ux. The scale formed on this sample was 160 m mthick, as expected this thickness is intermediate between that for the DS top and its side. Comparing all of the DS samples seen in Fig. 8 reveals that the SiC front facing the inward oxygen /uniFB02 ux has a dominant role in controlling the thickness of the oxide layer. According to Zhu et al. [31] the oxidation resistance in ZrB2-SiC is improved by having /uniFB01 nely dispersed (distance between SiC particles of about 10 m m) SiC particles in the matrix because of the improved tendency of /uniFB01 ne particles ( < 3 m m) to form a continuous silica protective layer. As a result the SPS samples prepared at 2373 K results had a scale thickness of 80 m m. Cross sections of solid state sintered samples had a morphology of the scale similar to that described in a recent review [32]. The sample side had quite a distinctive oxidation behaviour which was not previously reported as the oxygen inward /uniFB02 ux was retarded by the SiC/ZrB2 lamellar structure. The composition of borosilicate glass formed during the oxidation product is dif /uniFB01 cult to determine because of the dif /uniFB01 culties in measuring B content. On the side surface the silica layer formed from SiC continuous lamella is likely to contain a reduced boria content.

4. Oxidation studies
spot B)). Individual zirconia grains are surrounded by a silica /uniFB01 lm. In the DS ZrB2-SiC ingot, both ZrB2 and SiC phases are interlayered and homogeneously distributed. During oxidation, ZrB2 oxidises to ZrO2 and B2O3 above 1073 K. With further increase in temperature, SiC oxidises to SiO2 and CO2. Then B2O3 can either react with silica to form borosilicate glass and form a protective /uniFB01 lm around individual ZrO2 grains, or if B2O3 is not available locally silica by itself can form a protective silica glass. The side of the sample contains a prevalence of silica, while the top section has a prevalence of zirconia. This can be explained by looking at the microstructure of a DS sample (Fig. 3 (a) and (b)) where on the side there is a continuous SiC layer which is converted to silica (Fig. 8 (a)), while on the top surface ZrB2 is converted to zirconia (Fig. 8 (b)).
Fig. 8 shows polished cross-sections showing the microstructure of ZrB2-SiC after oxidation for 1 h at 1873K prepared using different techniques. The measured oxide scales thicknesses are given in Table 1. Fig. 8(a) shows a thin ( < 65 m m) oxygen-rich layer is formed on the side of the DS sample. The lamellae orientation has a strong effect on the oxidation resistance because the continuous layer of SiC facing the inward oxygen diffusion resulted in formation of a silica layer which was an effective barrier. The formation of a SiC continuous lamella of the DS sample side was promoted by the preferential solidi /uniFB01 cation of SiC because of its lower melting/ decomposition temperature compared ZrB2.

5. Conclusions
A novel design of a eutectic engineered microstructure improved the oxidation resistance of ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) composites. Oxidation studies of solidi /uniFB01 ed melt processed ZrB2-SiC with eutectic microstructures show that they could guide the design of UHTCs with improved oxidation resistance. The silica protective layer formed in directional solidi/uniFB01 ed specimens was continuous and it was formed by the oxidation of SiC layers. Melt processing could add a further degree of freedom in designing the properties of UHTCs, in particular, low energy grain boundaries might further improve oxidation resistance.

a b s t r a c t
Using a different approach, the in-situ development of solid refractory oxide protective layers by adding rare earth (RE) oxides to ZrB2/SiC composites was also proposed in Refs. [20,21] Addition of RE oxides formed RE zirconates as an outer protective layer that has higher melting point and lower oxygen diffusivity when compared with silicates or oxides. Chen et al. developed the eutectic composition of LaB6-ZrB2 and investigated its oxidation behaviour [22]. It was expected that the absence of low energy grain boundaries in the eutectic microstructure would contribute to improved oxidation resistance. However, oxidation studies at high temperatures showed that the Directionally Solidi /uniFB01 ed (DS) LaB6ZrB2 was not a sustainable candidate for UHT applications in air because of its poor oxidation resistance in spite of its excellent creep resistance and other mechanical properties [22]. LaB6-ZrB2 exhibited parabolic oxidation kinetics below 1373 K with 80 minutes holding time and behaved para-linearly above this temperature due to the rapid evaporation of B2O3 and the separation and run-off of lanthanum borate liquid in the scale, which offered less of a barrier to oxygen diffusion. Furthermore, preferential oxidation of LaB6 occurred in the composite and appeared more aggressive at low oxygen partial pressures.

4. Oxidation studies
The oxidation studies of DS ZrB2-SiC were carried out using a conventional furnace at temperatures up to 1600 /C14 C with 1 h holding. With the aim of identifying the role of processing with respect to the oxidation resistance, samples made with different processing techniques (DS sample, and sample obtained by SPS of milled as received powders, sample obtained by SPS of powder derived from crushed DS ingots) were studied.
The surface microstructures of the DS ZrB2-SiC oxidised at 1873K for 1 h are shown in Fig. 7. The surfaces are smooth and there are some differences in the surface morphology depending on the sample orientation. The number of bright zirconia particles in Figure (b) is larger than in Figure (a). The bright rounded grains are zirconia (EDX spot A) while the dark smooth surface is silica (EDX

Fig. 8. Comparison of oxidation oxide scale (top side of the /uniFB01 gure) of 20 vol%SiC-ZrB2 after oxidation at 1873K for 1 h. Samples were processed using various techniques: (a) Side of the DS sample with overlapped EDX mapping, (b) Top of DS sample, (c) sample obtained by SPS of milled as received powders (d) sample obtained by SPS of powder derived from crushed DS ingots. question: In Zhang et al. 2019's directionally solidified ZrB2-SiC composite, what quantitative measure of crystallographic alignment was achieved, and how does this numerical descriptor characterize the degree of grain orientation in the material?
answer: In Zhang et al. 2019's directionally solidified ZrB2-SiC composite, the Lotgering (00l) orientation factor was as high as 0.97 [3]. This numerical descriptor indicates a very high degree of crystallographic alignment, as the Lotgering factor ranges from 0 (no preferred orientation) to 1 (perfectly aligned grains), and a value of 0.97 suggests that the grains are almost perfectly oriented in the direction of solidification.

chunks.text:
3. Results and discussion
Fig. 5 shows a bright /uniFB01 eld TEM image of the ZrB2-SiC eutectic madebythemelting technique. The bright phase is SiC and the dark phase is ZrB2. The lamella thickness is about 5 m m. SAEDs were taken from regions labeled (a) (b) (c) and (d) in Fig. 5. SAEDs of the SiC phase along the [111] zone axis can be indexed as cubic and corresponds to the 3C-SiC polytype ( b -SiC) in the eutectic composition. Although, the SAEDs were taken from different regions, all of them have the same orientation, which con /uniFB01 rms the single crystalline nature of the lamellae. Similarly, SAEDs taken from regions (c) (d) and (e) along the [0001] zone axis con /uniFB01 rm the hexagonal symmetry of the ZrB2 grains. HRTEM of a ZrB2-SiC interface is shown in Fig. 5 (e), revealing an impurity-free grain boundary. The level of crystallographic misorientation is minimal and this is a typical feature of DS materials, resulting in low energy grain boundaries.
Fig. 4. XRD patterns from the side and top surfaces of the DS sample are also shown. The inset clari /uniFB01 es the sample orientation.

4.1.2. ZrB2 composites
[78] have also con /uniFB01 rmed that self-reinforced ZrB2-ZrC-Zr composites with ZrB2 platelets could be prepared by RHP using Zr and B4C as raw materials, as shown in Fig. 10. Similarly, it is indicated that the formation of ZrB2 platelets is strongly related to the presence of zirconium liquid phase and plastic /uniFB02 ow mechanism. The ZrB2 platelets coarsened with increasing amount of Zr, particularly when the residual Zr concentration was greater than 10 vol%. Subsequent hot forging (HF) at 1600 °C and 30 MPa yielded c -axis-oriented ZrB2 platelets in the ZrB2-ZrC-Zr composites [75]. The orientation degree of the platelet-like ZrB2 grains is dependent on the microstructure of the pristine material; larger initial grains in the pristine composite can lead to stronger degree of orientation of the ZrB2 grains.

a b s t r a c t
Directionally Solidi /uniFB01 ed 72.8 mol% ZrB2 - 27.2 mol% SiC (equivalent to 20 vol%) composite was fabricated using a crucibleless-melting technique. Microstructural and XRD analyses con /uniFB01 rmed the texturing of grains and lamellar structure. The Lotgering (00 l ) orientation factor was as high as 0.97. The thickness of the lamellae was about 5 m m. Bright/uniFB01 eld TEM images con /uniFB01 rmed that the eutectic composite has continuous ZrB2 and SiC phases. Electron diffraction patterns from both the ZrB2 and SiC phases were indexed as hexagonal and 3C-cubic structures, respectively. Line defects such as dislocations, planar defects such as stacking faults and twins were observed in the SiC. Oxidation studies performed at 1873K for 1 h demonstrated that the samples obtained by melting showed an improved oxidation resistance compared to ZrB2-SiC composites obtained via sintering due to the formation of a passivating continuous SiO2 layer. The oxidation mechanisms are discussed, the orientation and SiC lamellae acted as an effective barrier to the inward oxygen /uniFB02 ux and played a key role in retarding oxidation.
