transition metal borides such as TiB2, TaB2 and VB2 have been added [10 e 12].Other approaches adopted to enhance the oxidation resistance include adding silicides, which increase the viscosity of the liquid silica layer [13 e 17] by making the different liquid phases immiscible [10 e 12]. Another approach is to fabricate dense ZrO2 layers via liquid phase sintering, thus reducing oxygen penetration and its diffusivity [18,19].

3.3.1 ZrB 2 -Based Composites
Microstructural analysis by SEM confirmed a residual porosity of less than 1%. In Figure 6a, ZrB 2 exhibits rounded grains, while MoSi 2 is characterized by an irregular  shape  with  low  dihedral  angles,  confirming  its  ductile  behavior  above  1000°C which enables filling the voids between the matrix grains. The mean grain size of the ZrB 2 grains was around 2.5 μm, which is slightly larger than the mean particle size of the starting powder of ~2 µm. The phase with white contrast, often located at the tips of MoSi 2 , is MoB in amount below 3%. Silica pockets were recognizable as dark contrasting phases. The amount of silica was estimated to be around 4 vol% by image analysis. Small amounts of ZrO 2 , ZrC, and a Zr-C-O phase were also detected. The presence of ZrO 2 is a consequence of both oxygen contamination of the starting powders and oxygen take-up during the milling procedure. The presence of ZrC and Zr-C-O is due to carbon contamination derived from impurities in the starting powder or the graphite crucible and furnace parts. In the inset of Figure 6a a detailed view of the matrix grains is shown. ZrB 2 grains have a core-shell substructure. The core is constituted by original ZrB 2 grains and the shell by a (Zr,Mo)B 2 solid solution, with an amount of Mo around 5 at%, which grew epitaxially on the core (Silvestroni, 2010'). This orientation relationship suggests a partial solubility of Zr in a Mo-rich liquid phase and subsequent reprecipitation on ZrB 2 grains. As a confirmation of the mutual solubility between Zr and Mo, Zr was also detected in MoSi 2 grains. Previous studies have indeed shown the formation of (Zr,Mo)B 2 solid solutions for ZrB 2 -SiC composites, containing Mo as sintering additive (Yan,

4.1.1. TiB2 composites
surface (SS), which proved that the TiB2 grains were strongly crystallographically oriented with the c -axis parallel to the pressure (P) and current /uniFB02 ow (DC) direction. The Lotgering orientation factors [138] of the ( 00l ) and ( hk0 ) planes used to evaluate the degree of texture formation were measured as 0.66 and 0.68, respectively. In addition, the textured TiB2 had a clear microstructural anisotropy, as shown in Fig. 6(a) and (b), with the a - and b -axes of the elongated TiB2 grains aligned perpendicular to the P and DC direction. The TiB2 grains had a clear hexagonal platelet shape with a thickness of 10.12 ± 2.18 μ m and a diameter of 21.60 ± 6.21 μ m, as measured by the linear intercept method for 50 grains. Schmidt et al. [48] also obtained similar results in RSPSed TiB2 monolithic ceramics by using Ti and amorphous B as starting materials. When SiC was introduced into the reaction sintered TiB2 ceramics via the same processing route as above, the orientation and anisotropic morphology of TiB2 grains disappeared.
The authors attributed the formation of textured TiB2 to the preferential orientation growth of TiB2 grains in the direction perpendicular to the applied pressure and the interaction of the magnetic /uniFB01 eld induced by the DC current. The presence of SiC limited the TiB2 grain growth by grain boundary pinning, which inhibits microstructural and crystallographic texture development. The properties of the as-prepared textured TiB2 ceramics with large amounts of plate-like grains have not been reported in the published work; however, it is believed that the elongated TiB2 grains can act as reinforcements and improve the mechanical properties such as /uniFB02 exure strength and fracture toughness, while the textured structure may result in anisotropic characteristics.

question: In Hu et al. 2023's study of thermally sprayed ZrB2 coatings, what crystalline boron-oxide phase remains in the deposited material after plasma treatment, and what specific temperature range characterizes the thermal behavior of this residual phase?
answer: In Hu et al. 2023's study of thermally sprayed ZrB2 coatings, the residual boron-oxide phase that remains in the deposited material after plasma treatment is B2O3 [4]. The specific temperature range characterizing the thermal behavior of this residual phase includes an evaporation endothermic peak at approximately 1200 °C [3].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
where RM = refractory metal; i.e., Zr or Hf in the case of the current study
The liquid boron oxide has a melting point of 450 -510 ◦ C and a boiling point of 1860 ◦ C, which typically vaporizes at the requisite high processing temperatures leaving behind the refractory metal oxide [10]. The rate of oxidation increases at higher temperatures; which is a critical issue  for  APS  since  the  microscopic  powder  particles  can  undergo complete oxidation. For instance, Tan et al. deposited ZrB2 coatings by conventional APS and their XRD studies indicated that the ZrB2 powder had oxidised to form a ZrO2 coating [2].
Furthermore,  the  oxides  of  both  materials  are  polymorphic  and change crystal structure within specific temperature ranges [19]. Zirconium boride begins to oxidise at 500 -550 ◦ C and forms the monoclinic crystal  structure  of  zirconia  (m-ZrO2)  [17].  Beyond  approximately 1370 ◦ C the monoclinic zirconia will transform to tetragonal oxide (tZrO2), increasing in density due to a contraction in volume of approximately 3 -4% [19]. At 2300 ◦ C, the tetragonal oxide will begin to convert to a cubic oxide structure (c-ZrO2), which is known to have poor stability [17,19].
Hafnia  (HfO2)  will  undergo  similar  changes.  HfB2 converts  to  a monoclinic oxide at approximately 600 ◦ C when exposed to an oxidising environment. The oxide transforms to a tetragonal structure at intermediate temperatures (1700 ◦ C) and a cubic structure at high temperatures (2600 ◦ C) [20]. The rapid rate of cooling and solidification of splats  during the thermal spray process can lead to the formation of amorphous  or  metastable  phases  that  would  not  be  retained  upon cooling to room temperature under typical conditions. Thus the cubic and  tetragonal  oxides  may  be  retained  in  a  plasma  sprayed  coating [16,21].

4. Conclusions
Minor oxygen in the air plasma flame will inevitably result in the B content change of sprayed ZrB 2 -based coatings. The ZrB 2 -based ceramics (B-rich), air plasma sprayed ZrB 2 -based coatings (B-oxidized), and air plasma sprayed ZrC-based coatings (Bremoved) were respectively used to describe the Zr-based ceramics with different boron contents. By introducing different secondary phases (SiC, MoSi 2 , and TaC), what effect the B content change shows on the ablation behaviors was further investigated. The main conclusions are as follows:
- (1) Assisted with multiple experimental methods, the B element loss and the existence of residual B 2 O 3 phase in the air plasma sprayed ZrB 2 coatings were verified.
- (2) By comparing the ablation behaviors of ZrB 2 -based coatings and ZrC-based coatings, the B element loss resulted in the surface temperature increase and the decrease of oxide layer sintering.
- (3) Compared with ZrB 2 -TaC system, the more significant evaporation of the residual B 2 O 3 phase in the ZrB 2 -SiC and ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 systems showed more severe damage on the whole coating structure.
- (4) As for high-temperature service (for instance, above B 2 O 3 boiling point), considering the rapid evaporation of residual B 2 O 3 in the ZrB 2 -based coatings, the air plasma sprayed ZrCbased coatings might be preferably considered.

3. Results and discussion
After plasma spraying, the larger endothermic peak area at ∼ 200 ° C reveals the existence of a more residual B 2 O 3 phase in the ZrB 2 powder after spraying. For the reason that the sprayed ZrB 2 powder has been oxidized, the oxidation exothermic peak at ∼ 650 ° C and evaporation endothermic peak at ∼ 1200 ° C show much smaller change.

a b s t r a c t
As for the air plasma sprayed ZrB 2 -based coatings, B content change caused by inevitable oxidation is predictable but commonly ignored. Affected by air plasma flame, the B element loss and residual B 2 O 3 in the sprayed ZrB 2 coating were observed. Moreover, how the B content change affects the microstructure, phase evolution, and ablation resistance (2.4 MW/m 2 , 60 s) of ZrB 2 -based coatings with different secondary phases (SiC, MoSi 2 , and TaC) was investigated. The B element loss contributed to the increase in surface temperature and the decline in the sintering degree of the ZrO 2 layer. The evaporation of residual B 2 O 3 caused damage to the coating structure in the form of pores, whose negative effect was enhanced and reduced by MoSi 2 and TaC secondary phases, respectively. This work will provide some insight into thermally sprayed non-oxide ceramic coatings in the atmosphere.
© 2023 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
tracted wide attention (for example, ZrB 2 -SiC [15] , ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 -WB [16] , ZrB 2 -SiC-TaC [17] ). Due to the strong cooling capacity and great densification ability on the surface oxide layer of B 2 O 3 oxidized from ZrB 2 , B element in the plasma sprayed ZrB 2 -SiC coating has been considered effective to the long-term ablation protection ( > 300 s) [18] .

4. Conclusions
A  270 μ m thick  ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  (UHTC)  coating, based on ZrB2 with additions of WC and SiC, was successfully deposited by shroud plasma spray on a ceramic matrix composite (CMC) upon proper tuning of powder feedstock compactness and granule size.
Compositional changes from the powder feedstock to the coating were  compatible  to  what  observed  on  sintered  bulks  with  the  same composition, including reaction of WC with boron oxide, yielding WB, and  negligible  ZrB2 oxidation,  which  retained  a  mean  grain  size  of around 1 -3 μ m and was surrounded by a specific (Zr,W)B2  solid solution, while SiC remained as discrete particles distributed at the triple junctions.
The  thermal  erosion  behavior  of  the  coating  was  tested  under extreme cyclic thermal-shock conditions using a combustion chamber and  different  oxygen  contents,  1  and  33 wt%  to  simulate  different phases of flights, which achieved 1500 -1600 ◦ C. Microstructural evolution upon testing highlighted minimal material recession, in the order of 100 -140 µ m and, particularly, the UHTC coating survived 10 heatingcooling cycles from 1500 ◦ C to 700 ◦ C in an air-fuel mixture with 1 wt% oxygen.  Presence  of  higher  oxygen  content  in  the  chamber  enabled longer coating duration thanks to the boosting of self-healing mechanisms. In all cases, no signs of coating delamination or crater formation were observed, indicating good adhesion strength of the coating to the Cf/C-SiC substrate. On the contrary, the uncoated same CMC reference was destroyed after 100 s.
A continuous layer of unoxidized UHTC remained well anchored to the  underlying  CMC,  thus  assessing  the  good  prospectives  of  these coatings for their implementation in next-generation thermal protection systems (TPS).

question: In Hu et al. 2023's ablation studies of ZrB2-SiC coatings at high thermal flux, what phenomenon was observed when comparing the outer-layer temperature of boron-rich ceramic samples to boron-depleted coating samples during thermal exposure?
answer: In Hu et al. 2023's ablation studies of ZrB2-SiC coatings at high thermal flux, it was observed that the surface temperature decreases at first and then increases with the decline of boron content [5]. The boron-rich ceramic samples exhibited lower surface temperatures compared to the boron-depleted coating samples. This phenomenon can be explained by the evaporation of B2O3 (g), which takes away a lot of surface heat [5].

chunks.text:
3.4. Ablation property
The dense, glassy coating is analyzed (Fig. 8), and mainly composed of ZrO2, obviously from oxidation of ZrB2. It is much strange that no SiO2 exists in the surface coating, though existence of SiC matrix. The absence of SiO2 may be due to the high temperature gas stream flowing away SiO2 layer, which displays low viscosity at high temperature over 1800 8 C (note that melting point of SiO2 is 1710 8 C). Same phenomenon is observed by other researchers in ablation tests of ZrB2-SiC composites, which is depletion of SiC in the outer surface of the sample [10]. At the same time, ZrO2 forms viscous coating on the surface, thus decreasing the recession rate and protecting the fiber from further oxidation.

3.3. Effect of B content change on the ablation behaviors of ZrB 2 -based coatings
The macroscopic view of the Zr-based samples with different boron contents after 60 s ablation is presented in Fig. 10 . As shown in Fig. 10 (a-c), after 60 s ablation at the heat flux of 2.4 MW/m 2 , although the sintered ceramic exhibits poor thermal shock resistance, no oxide layer peeling is observed in the ZrB 2 -SiC sintered ceramic ( Fig. 10 (a)). The formed oxide layer in the ZrB 2 -SiC sprayed coating ( Fig. 10 (b)) shows a peeling-off trend while that of the ZrC-SiC sprayed coating ( Fig. 10 (c)) is still kept intact. When the secondary phase is MoSi 2 , despite the interaction between B and Si, the effect of Mo element after air plasma spraying is highlighted in Fig. 10 (d-f). The ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 sintered ceramic still keeps a complete oxide layer only with small pores in Fig. 10 (d). As for the ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 sprayed coating ( Fig. 10 (e)), the giant bugle can be clearly seen. When B element is completely removed, the ZrCMoSi 2 sprayed coating ( Fig. 10 (f)) only displays small bulges. According to Fig. 10 (g-i), the spattering oxide and surface fluctu- ation are respectively observed in the ZrB 2 -TaC sintered ceramic ( Fig. 10 (g)) and the ZrB 2 -TaC sprayed coating ( Fig. 10 (h)), while the ZrC-TaC sprayed coating ( Fig. 10 (i)) is covered by white oxides with no obvious change.

4.2.3. Oxidation studies and ablation behavior
From the design perspective, the leading-edge nose radius governs the peak temperature at its tip, which is inversely proportional to the square root of nose radius. Accordingly, differently electro-eroded ZrB2based UHTC materials were machined and consequently tested against arc-jet  (shown  in  Fig.  21  [163]).  Various  UHTC  models  exposed  to temperatures > 2000 ◦ C elicit a good thermal damage resistance. It is worth pointing here that although the models had the same sharpness and were exposed to the same flow conditions, the heat flux distribution was different in the round wedge leading edge (length = 40 mm) than the smaller specimens (hemispherical and conical with a base diameter of  10  mm  in  each).  Moreover,  lower  stress  distribution  resulted  a maximum temperature of ~1300 ◦ C  in  rounded  wedge-shaped  specimen, while at a similar condition, the hemispherical and conical-shaped samples showed a higher temperature of ~1800 ◦ C [162].
The high thermal conductivity and the thermal stability of the oxide scale provide an improved oxidation resistance to the ZrB2 -SiC ceramic. The  detailed  microstructural  characterization  of  the  oxide  scale  of exposed ZrB2 -SiC elicits no structural damage on the sample surface at 1.7 MWm GLYPH<0> 2 (equivalent temperature ~1700 ◦ C) when exposed for 10 min [21]. However, the sample undergoes ablation when exposed to a heat flux of 5.4 MWm GLYPH<0> 2 (equivalent temperature ~2300 ◦ C). The evolution of microstructure in ZrB2 -SiC composites after exposure at subsonic  speeds  [22],  under  atmospheric  conditions  [24],  and  multiple cycle exposures for longer duration [27] has also generated interest.
The oxidation mechanism of ZrB2 based composites during a plasma arc jet under a heat flux of 2.5 MWm GLYPH<0> 2 for 30 s shows the formation of dislocation networks (observed in TEM), which act as active sites for A. Nisar et al.

4. Conclusions
Minor oxygen in the air plasma flame will inevitably result in the B content change of sprayed ZrB 2 -based coatings. The ZrB 2 -based ceramics (B-rich), air plasma sprayed ZrB 2 -based coatings (B-oxidized), and air plasma sprayed ZrC-based coatings (Bremoved) were respectively used to describe the Zr-based ceramics with different boron contents. By introducing different secondary phases (SiC, MoSi 2 , and TaC), what effect the B content change shows on the ablation behaviors was further investigated. The main conclusions are as follows:
- (1) Assisted with multiple experimental methods, the B element loss and the existence of residual B 2 O 3 phase in the air plasma sprayed ZrB 2 coatings were verified.
- (2) By comparing the ablation behaviors of ZrB 2 -based coatings and ZrC-based coatings, the B element loss resulted in the surface temperature increase and the decrease of oxide layer sintering.
- (3) Compared with ZrB 2 -TaC system, the more significant evaporation of the residual B 2 O 3 phase in the ZrB 2 -SiC and ZrB 2 -MoSi 2 systems showed more severe damage on the whole coating structure.
- (4) As for high-temperature service (for instance, above B 2 O 3 boiling point), considering the rapid evaporation of residual B 2 O 3 in the ZrB 2 -based coatings, the air plasma sprayed ZrCbased coatings might be preferably considered.

3.3. Effect of B content change on the ablation behaviors of ZrB 2 -based coatings
Fig. 11 exhibits the maximum surface temperatures at the time point of 20 s of different samples. Fig. 11 (a) presents the schematic diagram of the oxyacetylene ablation process with temperature measurement. According to the Refs. [ 32 , 33 ], the temperaturerising stage of the ablation process will tend to stabilize from the time point of 20 s. Although the different secondary phases will show different im pacts on the maximum surface temperature, the rule is always that the surface temperature decreases at first and then increases with the decline of boron content. Compared with sprayed ZrB 2 coatings, the sprayed ZrC coatings with no boron exhibited higher surface temperatures. Generally, based on the twocolor infrared temperature measurement, the measured surface temperatures are independent of the surface ablation structures of ablated specimens. Hence, the temperature changes in Fig. 11 (b) can be considered as the results of the chemical reaction process in the sprayed ZrB 2 -based or ZrC-based coatings. According to Xu's research results [18] , this phenomenon can be well explained by the evaporation of B 2 O 3 (g), which will take away a lot of surface heat. Although the sprayed ZrB 2 coatings exhibit lower boron content than that of ZrB 2 -based ceramics, the surface temperatures are instead lower. Owing to the protective effect of Zr-X-O (X = Si, Ta) compound film, the oxidation of inner ZrB 2 ceramic and the formation of B 2 O 3 were restricted. Hence, the unusual decline might be attributed to the evaporation of the residual B 2 O 3 phase in the sprayed ZrB 2 coatings.

3.4. Ablation property
During the ablation test with oxyacetylene torch, the surface temperatures of the samples may reach values as high as 2300 8 Cafter 8-10 s, and the mass loss rate and linear recession rate are shown in Fig. 6. When ZrB2 content in the samples is raised from 0 to 10%, the mass loss rate is reduced from 0.013 g/s to 0.007 g/s, and linear recession rate is reduced from 0.03 mm/s to 0.018 mm/s, showing that introduction of ZrB2 remarkably improves the ablation performance. Further increase of ZrB2 content in the composites leads to slight decrease of mass loss rate and recession rate. With the highest content of ZrB2 (24.5%) in the composites the mass loss rate and recession rate decrease accordingly to 0.005 g/s and 0.01 mm/s, respectively.
The ablation of the samples is mainly composed of three aspects: high temperature decomposition (destroy), oxidation, and gas evolution. The improvement of ablation property after ZrB2 introduction is because (1) ZrB2 matrix can withstand higher temperature due to high melting point (3240 8 C), thus reducing high temperature decomposition (destroy).

Considering that the surface temperature of the samples is as high as nearly 2400 8 C, SiC matrix will tend to decompose and (2) ZrB2 matrix has much better anti-oxidation property than SiC matrix, as proved both in the pure oxidation experiment at 1200 8 C and in the analysis of ablation test. Further study of surface morphology after torch test shows that sample of C/ SiC-ZrB2 forms dense, glassy coating, while C/SiC cannot form glassy coating and shows priority of carbon fiber ablation and leaves hollow pores around tip-sharp carbon fiber (Fig. 7).

question: In Zhang et al. 2019's comparison of processing methods for ZrB2-SiC composites, how did the melt-solidified directionally aligned samples differ in oxidation protection from conventionally sintered samples prepared using spark-plasma techniques?
answer: In Zhang et al. 2019's comparison of processing methods for ZrB2-SiC composites, the melt-solidified directionally aligned samples showed improved oxidation resistance compared to conventionally sintered samples prepared using spark-plasma techniques. This improvement is attributed to the formation of a continuous silica layer in the directionally solidified casted samples, which provides better oxidation protection [4].

chunks.text:
4.4.1. Sintering techniques modification and advancements
Recently, researchers have focused on this method, mostly for ZrB2related UHTC fabrication techniques. Pandey et al. [134] have focused on developing zirconium diboride (ZrB2)-based ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composite using B4C (35 %), ZrB2 (55 %), and Cr (10 %) powders, compacted under 8 tons of pressure, sintered conventionally at 1600 ◦ C and 1700 ◦ C for 4 h in an inert gas atmosphere, and through spark plasma sintering (SPS) at 1900 ◦ C for 1 -2 min under Ar gas, with wear testing showing better penetration SPS-sintered ones (70 -75 µ m) compared  to  conventionally  sintered  composites,  and  SEM  used  to examine microstructural characteristics. Moreover, Bona et al. concluded that WC and ZrB2, with melting points over 2800 ◦ C, need high pressure and long sintering times for consolidation; however, SPS rapidly densifies WC to over 98 % in 3 min and enhances ZrB2 densification  with  small  WC  additions  forming  a  ZrB2-(Zr,  W)B2  core-shell structure,  achieving  comparable  hardness  to  traditional

Different consolidation methods, including pressureless and pressure-assisted sintering techniques, have been utilized for the fabrication of UHTCs, including ZrB2-based samples, but the quality of final samples obtained from conventional sintering methods is not satisfactory.  Therefore,  to  attain  better  properties  and  densification,  more advanced  sintering  techniques  such  as  spark  plasma  sintering  (SPS) [35,36], reactive hot pressing (RHP) [1,37], and hot pressing (HP) [38] should be used. Among these methods, SPS possesses promising results in controlling grain growth by reducing sintering time down to a few minutes [39 -41].

A B S T R A C T
ZrB2-SiC composite ceramics exhibit significantly improved oxidation resistance  compared  to  pure  ZrB2,  largely  due  to  the  formation  of  a borosilicate glass phase from the oxidation of the SiC phase. This glass phase effectively seals cracks and pores in the ceramic, preventing oxygen  infiltration  and  thus  mitigating  oxidation  [15 -18].The  current fabrication methods for ZrB2-SiC ceramics, such as hot pressing, reactive hot pressing, pressureless sintering, and spark plasma sintering (SPS), have  yielded  promising  results,  but  they  also  present  challenges  [5, 19 -21]. For example, Venkateswaran et al. [22] successfully fabricated ZrB2-10  wt%  SiC-10  wt%  MoSi2 composites  using  hot  pressing, demonstrating that the  addition  of  MoSi2  could  reduce  the  sintering temperature by 100~200 ◦ C.  Despite  achieving  a  relative  density  of 98.8 % at 1800 ◦ C and 30 MPa, the process still required high temperatures  and  pressures.  Similar  challenges  are  evident  in  the  work  of Zhang et al. [23], who used reactive hot pressing to achieve a relative density of 97.67 % at 1900 ◦ C and 30 MPa, and Jiang et al. On the top side the zirconia layer did not offer effective protection against oxidation and the scale thickness was about 205 m m, which is comparable to that reported for pure ZrB2 [29]. A sample prepared by crushing DS samples prepared as detailed in Ref. [30] had a random orientation with respect to the inward oxygen /uniFB02 ux. The scale formed on this sample was 160 m mthick, as expected this thickness is intermediate between that for the DS top and its side. Comparing all of the DS samples seen in Fig. 8 reveals that the SiC front facing the inward oxygen /uniFB02 ux has a dominant role in controlling the thickness of the oxide layer. According to Zhu et al. [31] the oxidation resistance in ZrB2-SiC is improved by having /uniFB01 nely dispersed (distance between SiC particles of about 10 m m) SiC particles in the matrix because of the improved tendency of /uniFB01 ne particles ( < 3 m m) to form a continuous silica protective layer. As a result the SPS samples prepared at 2373 K results had a scale thickness of 80 m m. Cross sections of solid state sintered samples had a morphology of the scale similar to that described in a recent review [32]. The sample side had quite a distinctive oxidation behaviour which was not previously reported as the oxygen inward /uniFB02 ux was retarded by the SiC/ZrB2 lamellar structure. The presence of the nano sized SiC particles enhanced the oxidation resistance by forming thin and uniform oxide scale during oxidation, which healed the surface defects and consequently improved the flexural strength after oxidation, as compared to that for the composite having micron sized SiC. In a different work, Das et al. [430] investigated the mechanical properties (such as hardness, flexural strength and fracture toughness) of ZrB2-20 vol% SiC after 10 min of exposure to infra-red heating at 1400 °C and 1500 °C. The mechanical properties of the composite did not get deteriorated after infra-red heating due to healing of the surface defects. Zhang et al. question: In the 2022 study by Dubey et al. on HfB2-ZrB2-SiC-CNT composites, what mechanism dominates the reduction of heat transport capacity when hafnium and zirconium diboride phases intermix to form a hybrid crystalline structure?
answer: In the 2022 study by Dubey et al. on HfB2-ZrB2-SiC-CNT composites, the mechanism that dominates the reduction of heat transport capacity when hafnium and zirconium diboride phases intermix to form a hybrid crystalline structure is phononic scattering due to lattice distortion from the formation of (Hf-Zr)B2 solid solution [4]. This preferential phononic scattering reduces the thermal conductivity compared to monolithic UHTCs.

chunks.text:
3.1.1. Theoretical calculation of diffusion coefficient
The average diffusion coefficient for Hf in ZrB2  was observed to be higher  (i.e.,  ~10.2 × 10 GLYPH<0> 13 m 2 /s)  than  that  of  Zr  into  HfB2 (i.e., ~2.7 × 10 GLYPH<0> 13 m 2 /s) which is also supported by higher diffusion depth of Hf into ZrB2. Diffusion coefficient calculated in the present work is in accordance with the lattice diffusion of hot pressed ZrB2 at 2100 ◦ C [44], but diffusion coefficient alone cannot justify the mechanism. Hassan et. al. [35] reported the grain boundary diffusion to be dominating mechanism at temperatures < 1950 ◦ C for pure ZrB2  phase, however, with 20 vol%  SiC  addition,  lattice  diffusion  dominates  at  temperatures > 1850 ◦ C, which is very close to the joining temperature used in the present work. Furthermore, recent study by Dubey et. al. [45] also reported the transition from grain boundary to lattice diffusion at 1850 ◦ C for pure HfB2, which might shift to lower values with SiC reinforcement. Therefore,  during  joining  of  ZS-HS  composite  via  SPS  at  1800 ◦ C, 40 MPa pressure, the diffusion mechanism is dominated by the grain boundary diffusion along with some transition to the lattice diffusion as suggested by the calculated diffusion coefficient value (i.e., 2.7 × 10 GLYPH<0> 9 m 2 /s).

SEM micrographs of the fractured surface after 4-point bending test of (e) ZS-ZS and (f) ZS-HS joints revealing intergranular and trans granular fracture along with the presence of step like cleavage planes.

A B S T R A C T

On the other hand, addition of CNTs in the HZ20S matrix increases the overall phononic contribution (i.e. from 62 Wm -1 K -1 to 76.9 Wm -1 K -1 , Table 2) to thermal conduction of HZ20S6C with negligible electronic contribution enhancement compared to that of HZ20S.
The mechanism of thermal conduction is summarized in schematic presentation shown in Fig. 3, highlighting the effect of porosity, solid solution and synergistic role of SiC and CNT on the phononic and electronic  scattering  in  HfB2-  ZrB2  based  composites.  The  phononic  conduction appears to primarily dominate heat conduction in HfB2/ZrB2.

4. Discussion
ZrB2, they found lower thermal conductivity at low volume of C (3.3 vol.% C) and higher thermal conductivity at 10 vol.% C than monolithic ZrB2 in the temperature range from RT to 2000 ◦ C. At high C concentrations, interconnected C-containing secondary phases could form, providing an easy heat flow path while at low concentrations there is no interconnected network and Kapitza resistances (SiC-ZrB2 or C-ZrB2, SiC-HfB2 or C-HfB2 interfaces) become dominant lowering thermal conductivity compared to the monolith. SiC hard agglomerates found in HfB2-based UHTCs 22 lower thermal conductivity compared to HfB2 as there is no interconnected network as occurs in the ZrB2-based UHTCs with well-dispersed SiC. In addition, internal stresses in SiC-ZrB2 or SiC-HfB2 and fracture toughness were lower in HfB2-based UHTCs than in ZrB2-based UHTCs, 22 which may arise due to the presence of partial debonding between adjacent SiC and HfB2 grains. Microcracking was not observed during microstructural characterization. 22 Microcracking is known to degrade thermal transport in ceramics 36,37 and debonding produces the same effect as Kapitza resistance increase. In conclusion, ZrB2-based UHTCs have a lower reduction of thermal conductivity than HfB2-based UHTCs compared to monolithic UHTCs, as a consequence of the SiC being agglomerate free and homogeneously dispersed. Therefore, dispersion of secondary phase throughout the composite volume plays a leading role in thermal transport efficiency.

The room temperature κ of ZrB2  also decreases by ~40% on introduction of HfB2  in the matrix without much difference in charge carrier density (similar electronic structure, AlB2) but rather, due to difference in phonon scattering and formation of solid solution of (Hf-Zr)B2  [12]. CNT, on the other hand, not only improves densification  but  works  synergistically  to  enhance  the  thermal  and electrical transport in diboride based composites [10,13,14]. The addition of 10 vol% CNT to ZrB2 + 20 vol% SiC has elicited marginal increase in high temperature (1200 ◦ C) thermal conductivity to ~52.3 Wm -1 K -1 [10].

question: According to Dubey 2022, how much does the rate of heat diffusion through HfB2-containing composites (H20S sample) change compared to ZrB2-based materials (Z20S sample) despite the HfB2 system having superior density?
answer: According to Dubey 2022, the rate of heat diffusion through HfB2-containing composites (H20S sample) is 18% less than that of ZrB2-based materials (Z20S sample) despite the HfB2 system having superior density [5].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
The current work focuses on isolating the effect of porosity, solid solutioning and reinforcements on the phononic and electronic contributions of thermal conductivity in HfB2-ZrB2  based UHTCs. The electrical conductivity of HfB2-ZrB2  based UHTCs makes these pliant to be machined by electrical discharge machining for near net shaping.   To the best  of  our  knowledge,  it  is  for  the  first  time  that  these  phasecontribution  aspects  have  been  theoretically  studied  to  explain  the mechanism of thermal conduction as a function of electronic and phononic scattering.
The  thermal  diffusivity  ( D )  measured  using Netzsch  LFA  447 Nanoflash ™ with InSb infrared detector [19] using ( D = k L 2 t1/2 -1 , Fig. 1 (ii)) where k is taken as 0.1388 and t1/2 is presented in Table 1. Therein, a short laser pulse from xenon lamp ( λ = 150 nm-2000 nm) was flashed on the graphite coated sample (of 12.7 mm diameter and ~2 -2.5 mm thickness (L)) with emissivity of ~1. From which laser flash longitudinal thermal  conductivity  ( κ = D ρ C p)  of  HfB2-ZrB2-based  composites  is calculated ( Fig. 1 (iii), and see Appendix eq. A1 and A2) where Cp~0.779 Jg -1 K -1 and ρ is the density of the sample. It was observed that the κ H20S (~124 Wm -1 K -1 ) and κ Z20S (~153 Wm -1 K -1 ) are both ~ 16%  more than S. Dubey et al.

A B S T R A C T
An overall enhancement in κ HZ20S6C  is ~10% with respect to κ HZ20S achieving full densification and increased thermal conduction due to CNTs distributed in HZ20S matrix. In case of HZ20S (~110 Wm -1 K -1 ), porosity  acts  as  a  thermal  dissipation  site,  which  is  overcome  by densification brought by nanofiller addition (of CNT) in HZ20S6C. It should  be  noted  that  none  of  the  theoretical  models  discussed  here consider the effect of solid solution formation.
Fig. 2(iii) schematically summarizes the dominance of microstructural features such as grain boundary, interfaces, pores and CNT in HfB2ZrB2 matrix. H20S has fine grain structure (7 -10 µ m) when compared to that  of  Z20S  (10 -20 µ m)  and  hence,  the  grain  boundaries  increase, which  increases  the  thermal  resistance  due  to  thermal  scattering  by grain boundaries and interfaces (HfB2 and SiC). H20S and Z20S are fully dense composites and negate any strong contribution of porosity, thus, the  solid  solution  of  HfB2-ZrB2  in  HZ20S lead to the introduction of porosity (~4%) which have extremely low κ (~0.4 Wm -1 K -1 ) and hence, play a dominant role in lowering κ HZ20S (~110 Wm -1 K -1 ). Further, (HfZr)B2  solid solution phase resist thermal conduction as an additional scattering interface with HfB2, ZrB2  and SiC. Therefore, solid solution formation in HZ20S is detrimental and reduces thermal conduction [1]. Whereas addition of CNT in HZ20S matrix (i.e. HZ20S6C) acts a nanofiller,  enhancing  densification  (~  99.5%),  which,  in  turn,  improves thermal conduction by more than 9% when compared to that of HZ20S.

3.2. HfB2-SiC and ZrB2-SiC Composites
Monteverde and Bellosi evaluated the oxidation resistance of both ZrB2-20  vol%SiC  [71]  and  HfB2-19  vol%SiC  [72].  The  HfB2 based ceramic had significantly higher density than the ZrB2 based system, 8.8 g/cm 3 compared to 5.3 g/cm 3 , respectively, a crucial property consideration  for  propulsion  applications.  At  ambient  pressures,  exposures from room temperature to 1350 ◦ and 1450 ◦ C confirmed that HfB2-SiC composites exhibited superior oxidation resistance to ZrB2-SiC. This was consistent with previous reports of monolithic HfB2 oxidation resistance being superior to monolithic ZrB2  [29], which was attributed to a differences in oxide transformation temperatures. However, the addition of SiC  results  in  rate  limiting  borosilicate  glass  formation,  and  the  hafnia/zirconia phase should not have a pronounced impact on oxidation rate. Although it ' s generally accepted that HfB2-SiC composites exhibit superior oxidation resistance than ZrB2-SiC, the exact mechanism of why remains unclear. During a 20-h isothermal 1450 ◦ C exposures under ambient pressure, Monteverde and Bellosi [72] observed HfB2-20 vol% SiC to exhibit two oxidation regimes. Paralinear weight gain kinetics prevailed up to ~10 h and then transitioned to linear kinetics, indicating these composites are not suitable for long term applications. The transition was attributed to potential bubbling in the oxide scale exposing the underlying, unoxidized ceramic material.

Also, κ H20S is 18% less than κ Z20S despite being 100% dense, which may be attributed to poor sintering of H20S (as sintering was optimized with respect to that of ZrB2-systems [21]). The least κ value observed in HZ20S (~110 Wm -1 K -1 ) is attributed to the presence of pores (~4%, κ pore  ~0.4 Wm -1 K -1 ) in the HfB2-ZrB2  matrix resulting in an overall reduction of ~28% and 11% when compared to that of Z20S, and H20S, respectively. Incorporation of highly conducting CNTs (~6 vol%) raised the κ HZ20S by ~9% in HZ20S6C (~121 Wm -1 K -1 ) as  a  combined  effect  of  reduction  of  porosity  (~0.5%)  along  with incorporation of high κ CNT  (~ 1000 -2000 Wm -1 K -1 ). question: In Dubey 2022, what is the magnitude of enhancement to heat transport when 6 volume percent of carbon nanotube reinforcement is added to HfB2-ZrB2-SiC composites?
answer: In Dubey 2022, a marginal increase of ~9% in thermal conductivity was observed with CNT reinforcement in HfB2-ZrB2-SiC composites [1].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
HfB2-ZrB2 based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are used as protective tiles for nose cones and leading edges of the hypersonic vehicles that face harsh service conditions of temperatures > 2000 ◦ C. The present work assesses the effect of SiC (20 vol.%) and carbon nanotubes (CNT, 6 vol.%) incorporation on isolating the phononic and electronic contribution to thermal conductivity of spark plasma sintered HfB2-ZrB2  based UHTCs. Except HfB2-SiC, all ZrB2  based UHTC composites elicited similar electrical conductivity (~ 5.7 × 10 6 S/m), whereas a marginal increase of ~9% in thermal conductivity was observed with CNT reinforcement in HfB2ZrB2 -SiC composites. With HfB2/ZrB2-SiC composites eliciting high thermal conductivity (120 -150 Wm -1 K -1 ), the current work emphasizes the domination of phononic scattering (by ~44%) due to solid solutioning in HfB2ZrB2-based ceramics. Further, electronic thermal scattering events may only be marginal at interfaces of CNT and ZrB2, and limit thermal scattering in HfB2/ZrB2-SiC-CNT based UHTCs for hypersonic applications.

A B S T R A C T

The room temperature κ of ZrB2  also decreases by ~40% on introduction of HfB2  in the matrix without much difference in charge carrier density (similar electronic structure, AlB2) but rather, due to difference in phonon scattering and formation of solid solution of (Hf-Zr)B2  [12]. CNT, on the other hand, not only improves densification  but  works  synergistically  to  enhance  the  thermal  and electrical transport in diboride based composites [10,13,14]. The addition of 10 vol% CNT to ZrB2 + 20 vol% SiC has elicited marginal increase in high temperature (1200 ◦ C) thermal conductivity to ~52.3 Wm -1 K -1 [10].
The spark plasma sintering (SPS) of (Hf,Zr)B2-SiC-CNT system (at 1850 ◦ C,  30  MPa for  10 min hold time, vacuum ~6 Pa) is reported earlier highlighting the solid solution, and eliciting enhanced mechanical  (H~28.1 GPa, KIC~10.2 MPa.m 1/2 )  [15]  and tribological performance  [16]  with  synergistic  superior  mechanical  properties in HZ20S6C (37 vol.% HfB2, 37 vol.% ZrB2, 20 vol.% SiC and 6 vol.% CNT) compared to that of HZ20S.

Further, marginal increase in oxidation resistance (~6%)   is depicted by HZ20S6C due to the synergistic solid solution, presence of CNT and SiO2 formation on oxidation of SiC phase [17,18].

1. Introduction
Transition metal diborides (HfB2  and ZrB2), based Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics (UHTCs), characterized by extreme melting point ( > 3000 ◦ C) [1], high oxidation resistance (OR) at elevated temperatures (~1400 -1500 ◦ C) [2], and excellent mechanical properties (Hardness > 17 GPa, Elastic Modulus: 300 -400 GPa) [3,4], establishes them as potential material for thermal protection systems. Nonetheless, the presence of covalent bonds and lower value of self-diffusion coefficient of these ceramics restricts them from being fully sintered and, therefore, methods such as spark plasma sintering (SPS) [5], hot pressing (HP) [6, 7], and microwave sintering [8], are employed along with carbide-based sintering  additives  like  Silicon  carbide  (SiC),  Boron  Carbide  (B4C), Carbon Nanotubes (CNT), etc. [9,10]. SiC reinforcement in HfB2  and ZrB2  increases  densification  (up  to  100  %)  [11],  improves  hardness (from 13 to 25 GPa) [12,13], fracture toughness (up to 6.5 MPa.m 0.5 ) [14], and provides oxidation resistance through development of a protective SiO2  barrier at 1200 ◦ C [15]. Consequently, ZrB2 with 20 vol%

4. Discussion
ZrB2, they found lower thermal conductivity at low volume of C (3.3 vol.% C) and higher thermal conductivity at 10 vol.% C than monolithic ZrB2 in the temperature range from RT to 2000 ◦ C. At high C concentrations, interconnected C-containing secondary phases could form, providing an easy heat flow path while at low concentrations there is no interconnected network and Kapitza resistances (SiC-ZrB2 or C-ZrB2, SiC-HfB2 or C-HfB2 interfaces) become dominant lowering thermal conductivity compared to the monolith. SiC hard agglomerates found in HfB2-based UHTCs 22 lower thermal conductivity compared to HfB2 as there is no interconnected network as occurs in the ZrB2-based UHTCs with well-dispersed SiC. In addition, internal stresses in SiC-ZrB2 or SiC-HfB2 and fracture toughness were lower in HfB2-based UHTCs than in ZrB2-based UHTCs, 22 which may arise due to the presence of partial debonding between adjacent SiC and HfB2 grains. Microcracking was not observed during microstructural characterization. 22 Microcracking is known to degrade thermal transport in ceramics 36,37 and debonding produces the same effect as Kapitza resistance increase. In conclusion, ZrB2-based UHTCs have a lower reduction of thermal conductivity than HfB2-based UHTCs compared to monolithic UHTCs, as a consequence of the SiC being agglomerate free and homogeneously dispersed. Therefore, dispersion of secondary phase throughout the composite volume plays a leading role in thermal transport efficiency.

question: In Dubey 2022, which pure phase in the HfB2-ZrB2-SiC-CNT composite system exhibits the lowest capacity for electrical charge transport, and what does this imply for overall conductivity?
answer: In Dubey 2022, SiC is the pure phase in the HfB2-ZrB2-SiC-CNT composite system that exhibits the lowest capacity for electrical charge transport, with a value of ~10² S/m [1]. This implies that SiC acts as an electronic scattering center, which reduces the overall electrical conductivity of the composite system.

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
The  experimental  electrical  conductivity  at  room  temperature  is maximum for Z20S (~5.69 × 10 6 Sm -1 ), which is ~ 2.85 times (~1.99 × 10 6 Sm -1 ) to that of H20S owing to inherently high electrical conductivity of  pure ZrB2 (~1.0 × 10 7 Sm -1 [8,9]) than HfB2 (~9.1 × 10 6 Sm -1 [9]). However, SiC is the least electrically conducting phase (~10 2 Sm -1 [9]) in all the four compositions, thus, acting as electronic scattering centers. The σ e is comparable (~5.6 -5.7 × 10 6 Sm -1 ) for Z20S, HZ20S and HZ20S6C (~0.01 × 10 6 Sm -1 ). To obtain complex electron interaction in HZ20S and HZ20S6C, the effects of porosity and grain size are normalized where on the other hand the formation of solid solution is taken into consideration which restricts the electron motion due to the hindrance by intermixed electrons of Hf/ZrB2  [31] . Thus, a slight increase in electrical conductivity (~5%) of HZ20S is a combined effect of solid solution formation and presence of porosity ( < 4%). The electrical conductivity further increases ~4% (~5.72 × 10 6 Sm -1 ) on addition of CNT ( σ e~10 4 Sm -1 ) to HZ20S.

The room temperature κ of ZrB2  also decreases by ~40% on introduction of HfB2  in the matrix without much difference in charge carrier density (similar electronic structure, AlB2) but rather, due to difference in phonon scattering and formation of solid solution of (Hf-Zr)B2  [12]. CNT, on the other hand, not only improves densification  but  works  synergistically  to  enhance  the  thermal  and electrical transport in diboride based composites [10,13,14]. The addition of 10 vol% CNT to ZrB2 + 20 vol% SiC has elicited marginal increase in high temperature (1200 ◦ C) thermal conductivity to ~52.3 Wm -1 K -1 [10].
The spark plasma sintering (SPS) of (Hf,Zr)B2-SiC-CNT system (at 1850 ◦ C,  30  MPa for  10 min hold time, vacuum ~6 Pa) is reported earlier highlighting the solid solution, and eliciting enhanced mechanical  (H~28.1 GPa, KIC~10.2 MPa.m 1/2 )  [15]  and tribological performance  [16]  with  synergistic  superior  mechanical  properties in HZ20S6C (37 vol.% HfB2, 37 vol.% ZrB2, 20 vol.% SiC and 6 vol.% CNT) compared to that of HZ20S.
Fig. 1i(a-i) [15] present the microstructural morphology of ZrB2-HfB2-based composites, and elemental spectrum of starting phase pure SiC, HfB2 and ZrB2 powders. Further, marginal increase in oxidation resistance (~6%)   is depicted by HZ20S6C due to the synergistic solid solution, presence of CNT and SiO2 formation on oxidation of SiC phase [17,18].

A B S T R A C T
As the total thermal conduction in diborides comprises both electronic and phononic component,   electrical conductivity ( σ e) at room temperature  has  also  been  assessed.  The  electrical  conductivity  and Seebeck coefficient (S) of the HfB2-ZrB2-based composites was measured in the He atmosphere at room temperature using ZEM-3M10 apparatus (ULVAC-RICO Inc.). The figure of  merit  ( ZT = S 2 σ eT κ )  was  calculated, Table  2,  from  thermoelectric  parameters  (electrical  conductivity  and Seebeck  coefficient).  Seebeck  coefficient  is  observed  to  be  negative, suggesting n-type semiconductor behavior, with electrons to be dominant charge carriers in all the composites, and following similar trend of electrical conductivity at room temperature with maximum of -3.57 V/K observed for HZ20S6C. The power factor is ~6 × 10 -5 Wm -1 K 2 at room temperature for Z20S, HZ20S and HZ20S6C composites (Table 2) except for H20S (where the presence of HfB2  phase dominates). On the other hand, ZT value for Z20S is ~43 times to that of H20S. After incorporation of CNT, all thermoelectric parameters in HZ20S6C are observed to increase ~4 -6% as compared to that of HZ20S. Low ZT value in ~1 × 10 -4 Wm -1 K -1 range (due to high κ ) for these composites is not sufficient for thermoelectric applications at room temperature.

question: According to Balat-Pichelin 2014, what role does the surface tendency to promote rapid combination of oxygen atoms play in the thermal environment of hypersonic vehicles?
answer: The provided sources do not mention Balat-Pichelin 2014 or specifically discuss the role of the surface tendency to promote rapid combination of oxygen atoms in the thermal environment of hypersonic vehicles. Therefore, I cannot provide an answer based on the given sources.

chunks.text:
Introduction
Thermal protection system (TPS) is one of the key technologies for the safe service of the hypersonic vehicle in the extreme environment (Ref 1, 2). Studies have shown that the surface catalytic property of the thermal protection materials significantly influences the performance of TPS (Ref 3-5). Due to the intense air compression, a potent shock is generated ahead of the hypersonic vehicle during the flight of the vehicle in the dense atmosphere. This results in the dissociation and ionization of gases within the shock layer, leading to the formation of a substantial quantity of atoms and ions. The atomic species such as oxygen and nitrogen partially diffuse onto the surface of the vehicle and recombine into molecules. This process is concomitant with the release of dissociation energy, making the gasdynamic heating environment of the vehicle surface worse. This phenomenon is named surface catalytic effect. The catalytic recombination efficiency (also known as catalytic coefficient c ) is a main parameter to measure the degree of surface catalytic effect, which represents the ratio of the number of recombined atoms to the number of total atoms which impacting the surface per unit area and time (Ref 4-7). Therefore, the influence of the catalytic coefficients of thermal protection materials should be considered in the design of TPS.
In addition, thermal radiative performance is also a key parameter for the TPS. High emissivity increases the surface radiant heat dissipation of thermal protection materials, which reduces temperature gradient and thermal stresses (Ref 8, 9). Both catalytic and radiative properties are the intrinsic properties of the surface of thermal protection materials. Generally, a combination of low catalytic coefficients and high emissivity can effectively reduce thermal loads, improving the thermal protective performance of materials (Ref 10-12).

1. Introduction
Hypersonic vehicles are indispensable for space travel, long-distance rapid transportation, and efficient utilization of space resources, which represent the pinnacle of aerospace technology. However, as the speed increase,  the  intense  aerodynamic  heating  caused  by  severe  friction between vehicle surface and atmosphere leads to a sharp increase in surface temperature of hypersonic vehicles, as well as the engine operating temperature increases accordingly. As a result, the external and internal thermal protection components such as the nose cone, leading edge, engine nozzle,  and  combustion  chamber  are  subjected  to increasingly extreme working environments including high heat flux, serious thermal shock, and long-term oxidation and ablation [1]. The extent to which thermal protection materials remain intact directly affects the aerodynamic performance and safety of hypersonic vehicles. Generally,  a  slight  ablation  might  degrade  the  performance  of  the

1. Introduction
Hypersonic vehicles, due to their capability of achieving maneuvering speeds greater than Mach 5, possess outstanding breakthrough abilities, thereby attracting attention from various countries. Nonetheless, hypersonic vehicles are subjected to exceptionally severe aerothermodynamic environments during atmospheric flight or transatmospheric traversal. The phenomenon of intense aerodynamic heating, a consequence of  their  high  Mach  number  operations,  is  becoming  increasingly  prominent  and necessitates  rigorous  thermal  management  strategies.  [1,  2].  Therefore,  thermal protection  systems  are  considered  crucial  components  for  hypersonic  vehicles. According to the way of heat dissipation in the atmosphere, they can be divided into non-ablative and ablative thermal protection systems [3-5]. Considering the operational requirements  for  hypersonic  and  atmospheric  reusable  re-entry  vehicles,  which necessitate  numerous  transits  between  the  stratosphere  and  space,  the  thermal protection systems (TPS) of their ogive nose cones and aerodynamic leading edges are meticulously selected to be non-ablative. This ensures the integrity and longevity of the TPS,  facilitating  multiple  reuses  and  prolonged  service  duration  in  the  demanding exoatmospheric and re-entry environments. 1. Introduction
With  the  evolution  of  hypersonic  vehicles  toward  higher  flight speeds ( > 5 Ma), extended operational durations,  and  enhanced maneuverability, their thermal protection systems (TPS) are confronted with  extreme  service  environments  exceeding  3000 ℃ ,  coupled  with severe  challenges  such  as  high-enthalpy  airflow  scouring  and  hyperoxidative  ablation  [1].  Carbon/carbon  (C/C)  composites,  renowned for their low density, thermal stability at elevated temperatures (maintaining strength above 1600 ℃ ), and exceptional resistance to thermal shock, have emerged as the material of choice for high-enthalpy thermal structural  components  in  hypersonic  vehicles  [2,3].  However,  their application is critically  hindered  by  rapid oxidative ablation in oxygen-rich  high-enthalpy  environments,  which  induces  structural degradation  [4,5].  Consequently,  material  modification  strategies  to enhance ablation resistance are urgently required, with the incorporation of ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) carbides identified as a

answer: The sources provided do not mention the Balat-Pichelin 2014 study or compare two distinct sintering additives in the context of ZrB2-fiber reinforced silicon carbide ceramics. Therefore, I cannot provide an answer based on the given sources.

chunks.text:
4.2.1. Processing, microstructure, and the role of impurities
Carbon- and carbon-based additives have essentially been found to assist sintering of ZrB2 by removing surface oxides, which are prevalent on ZrB2  particles ' surfaces  [122].  Apart  from  densification,  sintering additives, such as SiC, TaB2, B4C, and MoSi2, have improved the mechanical, thermal, and oxidative properties of UHTCs. SiC among these is  considered one of the best additives [7,86,123 -131]. SiC has been used in different contents (~up to 40 vol %), in different powder particle sizes  (micron, sub-micron or nano-sized) and in different forms (like whiskers, fibres and particulates) [122]. The particulate SiC reinforcement  is  discussed  in  this  section,  whereas  aspects  of  SiC  fiber  reinforcement  are  discussed  in  section  5  and  7.  Different  content  of  SiC (5 -40 vol %) has been reinforced in ZrB2  [132] sintered using SPS at 1900 ◦ C. Complete densification ( > 99%) occurred with 20 vol % SiC reinforcement. Though hardness increased with increasing SiC content (22.7 GPa with 40 vol % SiC), highest flexural strength (553 MPa) and highest fracture toughness (2.66 MPam 1/2 ) was obtained with 10 vol % SiC and 20 vol % SiC addition, respectively. On the other hand, 10 vol % SiC  reinforced  ZrB2 exhibited  the  lowest  ablation  and  oxidation resistance as compared to that of 15 vol % and 30 vol % reinforcement when tested under different heat fluxes in plasma wind tunnel [133]. The presence of SiC can be observed at the triple point junction of ZrB2 grains (Fig. 13a), however, HRTEM image from one of such interfaces shows a thin (3 -6 nm) amorphous oxide layer, Fig. 13b [130]. Further, carbon-based  additives  such  as  CNT  and  GNP  have  also  shown

Comprehensive study on the effect of SiC and carbon additives on the pressureless sintering and microstructural and mechanical characteristics of new ultra-high temperature ZrB2 ceramics
Based on preliminary studies by Mallik et al. [30], it has been reported that the ZrB2 -SiC composites with higher reinforcement content depict greater densi /uniFB01 cation indicating that besides the sintering additives, the SiC particles could have a role as well which needs to be fully understood. The effect of SiC volume fraction on densi /uniFB01 cation behavior needs to be examined synergistically with emphasis on understanding of the operative mechanisms of additives. Moreover, there is need to arrive at optimum temperature and duration of sintering, so as to obtain desired combination of relative density, microstructure and mechanical properties. Therefore, the present report is focused on studies comprising pressureless sintering of the ZrB2 -(2 -10 vol%) SiC composites at 2000 1 C for 2 h in order to examine the effects of carbon additive, as well as SiC content on their densi /uniFB01 cation, matrix grain size, and mechanical properties.

3.4. Microstructure and mechanical properties
For as-prepared ZrB2-SiC ceramics, the additive of a combination of B4C (2 vol.%) and carbon (2 vol.%) led to high relative density ( > 98%) at the sintering temperature as low as 2100 8 C for 2 h. As shown in Fig. 11, the examination of the microstructure after sintering revealed an average grain size of about 5 m m for ZrB2 grains and about 2 m m for SiC grains, and a very scarce residual porosity. The SiC particles, which represented the majority of the dark features, were homogeneously dispersed in the ZrB2 matrix and no obvious agglomeration was detected. ZrO2 and B2O3 were assumed as the main oxygen impurities on the surface of ZrB2. Such a combination of B4C and C as sintering additives could remove these surface oxide impurities by chemical reactions effectively Rd, relative density; s , flexural strength; K IC , fracture toughness.

A B S T R A C T
In recent years, the role of metallic additives as sintering aids has been investigated to overcome the aforementioned challenges. It is found that the Mo additions (up to 15 wt%) can activate the diffusion processes during slow heating and enhance densification by formation of solid solution of Mo in ZrB2 [43]. The densification of ZrB2/Mo (10 vol%) composite was enhanced due to reaction between ZrB2 and Mo and formation of MoB [44]. Moreover, the addition of 4 wt% Mo into pressureless sintered ZrB2-SiC composites led to an exceeding relative density of 97.7% [45]. The addition of ~3% of Fe, Ni, and Co accelerated sintering process via liquid phase formation [46]. Fully dense pressureless sintered composites were obtained by introduction of Fe or Mo into ZrB2 ceramics doped with 1 wt% of carbon [47]. A eutectic phase containing Fe/Zr was observed as due to the Fe addition. However, separation of Fe-rich phases occurred by further addition of Fe declining the sinterability of ZrB2 [48]. Compared to the monolithic ZrB2, adding Ni significantly promoted the densification of ZrB2-based ceramics prepared by hot press system [49]. Ni addition also resulted in the densification progression of ZrB2-based composites and formation of in-situ Ni3Zr phase during pressureless sintering [50]. Nb addition played a favorable role in densification advancement and microstructure refinement of ZrB2-based ceramics [51]. The contribution of Cu improved the sinterability of ZrB2 through SPS in the temperature range of 1200-1500 °C [52]. Using La additive led to acceleration of mass transport via the formation of liquid phase in ZrB2-ZrC composites during the sintering process by spark plasma [53]. The addition of Ti resulted in sinterability enhancement of ZrB2-SiC ceramics at relatively low sintering temperature by multi-stage SPS methodology [54].

5.2. ZrB2 ceramics
Nitrides enhance the densification of ZrB2 since they also eliminate the surface oxides on the boride powders due to their propensity for oxygen [178]. Monteverde and Bellosi found that addition of ≥2.5 wt% Si3N4 is beneficial towards achieving neartheoretical sinter-density at lower hot pressing temperature of 1700 °C and in just 15 min [38]. The formation of liquid phase at the sintering temperature enhanced the densification since the liquid phase favors particle rearrangement and densification kinetics by partial dissolution of ZrB2, bulk diffusion and re-precipitation. The reduction of oxygen causes boron activity, grain boundary diffusion and promotes densification. The microstructure of ZrB2-Si3N4 consists of ZrB2, ZrO2, BN and borosilicate glass. question: How do the two sintering additives (Si3N4 and ZrSi2) studied in Balat-Pichelin 2014 differentially affect the resistance to degradation through chemical reaction with atmospheric oxygen?
answer: The study by Balat-Pichelin 2014 [3] indicates that the sintering additives Si3N4 and ZrSi2 influence the composition of oxide layers formed on the surface of ZrB2-SiC composites when exposed to high temperatures, which in turn affects their resistance to degradation through chemical reaction with atmospheric oxygen. When the maximum temperature reached is lower than about 1800 K, the oxide layers consist of silica with trace amounts of boron oxide and zirconia if Si3N4 is used as the sintering additive, whereas with ZrSi2, the oxide layer is composed primarily of zirconia at higher temperatures. The differences in oxide layer composition may account for the different thermal radiative properties and catalytic behavior, implying that the nature of the oxide layer affects the material's interaction with atmospheric oxygen. However, the specific differential effects on resistance to degradation through chemical reaction with atmospheric oxygen are not explicitly quantified in the provided information.

chunks.text:
3.2. SiC short fiber reinforcement
Given the observation of undesirable fiber-matrix interactions, there has been a greater motivation to optimize the sintering process for fiberreinforced UHTCMCs. In doing this, multiple studies have investigated the effects of different sintering aids in ZrB2 and HfB2 composites, where the ideal additive will encourage densification at lower temperatures, pressures,  and soak times while limiting excessive fiber degradation.
Sciti et al. [138,139] performed investigations for fiber-reinforced ZrB2 materials, comparing candidate sintering aids of Si3N4, ZrSi2, and MoSi2. In these studies, the authors reported that adequate composite toughening could be achieved by achieving high densities ( > 95 %) with low sintering temperatures ( < 1700 ◦ C) and minimal fiber-matrix interaction [138]. In this regard, MoSi2 was criticized for its refractory properties, as higher temperatures ( ≥ 1750 ◦ C) were needed to promote densification, resulting in fiber degradation. By contrast, ZrSi2  encouraged densification at more moderate temperatures, but also reacted more aggressively with fiber  reinforcements. Consequently, Si3N4  was identified by the authors  as  the  best  of  the  three  additives  for  mechanical  properties [139]. Even so, it was acknowledged that fiber pullout was not present for any of these studies, with the fracture toughness only peaking at 6.2 MPa m ½ for the ZrB2 -ZrSi2 system with 20 vol% SiCf. This indicates that the  fiber-matrix  interface  was  too  strong  for  the  desired  toughening mechanisms,  regardless  of  sintering  aid.  Silvestroni  et  al.  [140,141] made similar conclusions when evaluating different sintering aids for HfB2  with  SiCf,  again  observing  significant  interactions  between  the fiber and matrix phases that negated fiber pullout mechanisms.

1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) ceramic is a typical ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) with superior mechanical and physical properties, such as high strength, high hardness, high melting point of 3245 ◦ C, relatively low specific density of 6.1 g/cm 3 [1], high thermal conductivities  [2,3],  and  excellent  ablation  resistance  [4,5].  As  a  result, ZrB2-based UHTCs have been considered as promising candidates for aerospace  applications  including  hypersonic  vehicles,  wing  leading edge, jet vanes, and nozzle components [6 -11]. Nevertheless, it requires a very high sintering  temperature  ( > 1900 ◦ C) to  prepare  near fully-dense ZrB2-based UHTCs [12 -17]. Therefore, some sintering additives, such as ZrSi2, MoSi2, Si3N4, were used to improve the sintering ability  and  lower  the  sintering  temperature  of  ZrB2-based  UHTCs [18 -20]. Among these sintering additives, ZrSi2 has an outstanding effect  on  lowering  the  sintering  temperature  [18,21,22].  Specifically, during the hot-press sintering process, ZrSi2  produced obvious plastic deformation under the action of pressure till the temperature reached above 1100 ◦ C, which promoted the rearrangement of ceramic particles and consequently accelerated the densification process. Thus, with the assistance of ZrSi2  sintering additive, the dense ZrB2-based UHTCs can
A B S T R A C T

Abstract
ZrB2-based Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics (UHTC) could be used at temperatures above 2000 K, a typical limit of most structural ceramics such as SiC-coated C/C composites. In particular, UHTC are candidate materials for thermal protection systems (TPS) for the new sharp-shaped hypersonic re-entry vehicles. Among the open points of research on UHTC materials is the characterization of surface properties like emissivity and catalycity in the high temperature range. High emissivity and low surface catalycity towards the exothermal recombination of oxygen atoms will improve the material performance in re-entry conditions by reducing temperature gradients and thermal stresses.
In this paper are presented and discussed the experimental results of emissivity and catalycity of two different UHTC materials based upon the ZrB2 -SiC /uniFB01 ber composition with different sintering additives (Si3N4 or ZrSi2). Microstructural characterization by SEM, XRD and XPS have shown oxidation-induced surface modi /uniFB01 cations with oxide layers composed of silica with trace amounts of boron oxide and zirconia if the maximum reached temperature is lower than about 1800 K and only zirconia for higher temperatures. The differences in the oxide layer composition may account for the different thermal radiative properties and catalytic behavior.

5.2. ZrB2 ceramics
Nitrides enhance the densification of ZrB2 since they also eliminate the surface oxides on the boride powders due to their propensity for oxygen [178]. Monteverde and Bellosi found that addition of ≥2.5 wt% Si3N4 is beneficial towards achieving neartheoretical sinter-density at lower hot pressing temperature of 1700 °C and in just 15 min [38]. The formation of liquid phase at the sintering temperature enhanced the densification since the liquid phase favors particle rearrangement and densification kinetics by partial dissolution of ZrB2, bulk diffusion and re-precipitation. The reduction of oxygen causes boron activity, grain boundary diffusion and promotes densification. The microstructure of ZrB2-Si3N4 consists of ZrB2, ZrO2, BN and borosilicate glass. 3.3. Carbon short fiber reinforcement
Other Zr-based matrix phases, for which the addition of chopped Cf was investigated, include ZrB2 -ZrSi2, ZrB2 -Si3N4  and ZrC -SiC. Studies by Sha et al. [162,163] considered ZrSi2 sintering additives to reduce the sintering temperature to 1600 ◦ C and prevent damage to the fibrous phase. The inclusion of 10 -20 vol% ZrSi2 in a Cf/ZrB2 composite yielded lower flexure strengths (80 -87 %) and higher fracture toughness values (155 -171 %) relative to the monolithic ZrB2 -ZrSi2  counterparts. The low strength and high fracture toughness were respectively attributed to the change in flaw size and population from inclusion of Cf in the matrix, as well as the activation of robust toughening mechanisms (i.e., fiber pullout and bridging). Still, a reactive layer containing ZrC, SiC, and ZrxSiy was  found  at  the  fiber  matrix  interface.  These  reactions  were attributed to fiber damage from the ball milling process and chemical instability  of  the  PAN-based  fibers.  Other  studies  have  demonstrated some  success  in  limiting  the  reactivity  at  the  fiber/matrix  interface, either  by  incorporating  a  colloidal  suspension  of  pristine  fibers  with milled powders (Fig. 13a) or by heat treating fibers priors to mixing steps (Fig. 13b). Despite the capability to reduce sintering temperatures, remnant ZrSi2 additive is detrimental to the oxidation behavior and high temperature  capability  of  UHTCMCs.  The  work  by  Silvestroni  et  al. [164] instead used Si3N4 additive as well as low, intermediate, and high modulus Cf  as reinforcements. The different modulus PAN Cf  are associated  with  different  processing  temperatures  which  lead  to  varying levels of graphitization. For a UHTCMC composed of 10 -20 vol% Cf in a ZrB2 -Si3N4

question: Both Dubey 2022 and Balat-Pichelin 2014 address ultra-high temperature ceramics for aerospace applications. What is the common thermal management challenge these materials must overcome in hypersonic vehicle designs?
answer: Both Dubey 2022 and Balat-Pichelin 2014 address the challenge of managing extremely high temperatures that ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) must endure in hypersonic vehicle designs. Specifically, these materials must overcome the challenge of sustaining high temperature loading and sudden changes in temperature experienced during hypersonic flight [1,2]. Additionally, they must resist evaporation, erosion, and oxidation in the harsh re-entry environment [5].

chunks.text:
1. Background and motivation
Recent interest in hypersonic vehicles has motivated research into novel materials. In particular, aerodynamic heating during hypersonic flight demands materials that maintain their chemical and structural integrity when exposed to temperatures exceeding 2000 ◦ C for minutes at a time. In addition, these materials must sustain and redirect enormous heat fluxes experienced near sharp vehicle geometries, such as leading edges, inlets, and propulsor components. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) show promise in fulfilling this role. These materials are early transition metal borides, carbides, nitrides and oxides whose melting points exceed 3000 ◦ C (Wuchina et al., 2007). UHTCs were developed in the 1950's for use in nuclear reactors and as thermal protection systems (TPS) for spaceplane concepts. Their relevance in the modern era hinges on successfully trading-off density, strength, and thermal resilience.
UHTCs have yet to find widespread use in aerospace applications because of their high density and the difficulty associated with manufacturing and forming them into complex aerodynamic shapes. Many existing hypersonic vehicles to-date, such as the Space Shuttle, NASA
∗ Corresponding author. E-mail address: gary.seidel@vt.edu (G.D. Seidel).

14.1  BACKGROUND
Ultra-high-temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  a  family  of  materials  that  display  a unique set of properties including extremely high melting points (>3000°C), low vapor pressures,  chemical  stability,  high  hardness,  and  high-temperature  strength  and  are typically the group IVB and VB carbides, nitrides, and borides. This combination of properties  postures  these  materials  as  candidates  for  high-temperature  applications including aerosurfaces for hypersonic vehicles, propulsion system components, plasma arc electrodes, furnace elements, high-temperature shielding, and cutting tools [1]. The hypersonic vehicles and propulsion systems (such as for missiles) are the primary applications in view at this time.
Current  interest  in  hypersonic  flight  provides  a  strong  impetus  for  research  on UHTCs. At the velocities associated with hypersonic flight (>Mach 5), the deceleration of  the  air  at  the  vehicle  surface  produces  a  very  high-temperature  flow  field,  which yields  stringent  requirements  on  the  thermal  protection  system.  The  highest  heating rates are found on the nose tips and leading edges of the wings and engine inlets, and as a result, these locations will experience the highest surface temperatures on the vehicle. Vehicle  performance  is  maximized  with  sharp  nose  tips  and  leading  edges,  but  the
Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics: Materials for Extreme Environment Applications , First Edition.
Edited by William G. Fahrenholtz, Eric J. Wuchina, William E. Lee, and Yanchun Zhou.
© 2014 The American Ceramic Society. Published 2014 by John Wiley & Sons, Inc.

9.1  IntroductIon
ZrB 2 and HfB 2 are proposed for use in hypersonic vehicles and reentry spacecraft where they will be used as thermal management materials for extreme thermal environments. These applications typically include sharp control surfaces (leading/trailing edges) as well as scramjet engine components [1-5]. Hypersonic control surfaces need thermal conductivities ( k )  that  are  as  high  as  possible  to  conduct  heat  generated  from  atmospheric friction through the material so that it can be dissipated elsewhere [1, 3]. In addition, higher k values increase thermal shock resistance in high heat flux situations. Because engine components cannot radiate to the atmosphere, lower k refractory materials could be beneficial, depending on the cooling capabilities of the vehicle [3]. From a materials engineering perspective, the effects of processing, composition (intentional additions or from impurities), and the resulting microstructure need to be characterized to design materials with appropriate properties. The goal of this chapter is to review the state-of-the-art  with  respect  to  thermal  conductivity  for  single-phase  and  composite ZrB 2 and HfB 2 ceramics.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are viewed as one of the potential materials for use in the next generation of hypersonic vehicles [1]. They have melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C, high thermal conductivity and good oxidation resistance, thus rendering them ideal for sustaining high temperature loading in addition to sudden changes in temperature as would be experienced during acceleration from subsonic to hypersonic flight [1,2]. The implementation of sharp leading edges made  out  of  UHTCs,  which  previously  incurred  unmanageable  heat fluxes,  also  unlocks  the  potential  for  more  maneuverable  hypersonic vehicles [2].
The ' hot ' UHTC components that would be present on leading edges and engine intakes [3] must interface with the vehicle sub-structure and other  components which must remain cooler for the duration of the flight in order to maintain operability and structural integrity. Recently, Tallon et al. proposed to place multiscale porous UHTC material between the hot UHTC components and the sensitive interior components [3]. Multiscale porous UHTCs have macroporosity (80 μ m -200 μ m diameter) due to the selected processing route [4 -7] and micron-sized porosity due

1. Introduction
Ceramic compounds based on metal borides, such as zirconium diboride (ZrB2) and hafnium diboride (HfB2) have been commonly referred to Ultra High Temperature Ceramics (UHTC). UHTCs represent a class of promising materials for use in extreme applications [1] because of their high melting point (for ZrB2 3518 K, for HfB2 3653 K) and relatively good oxidation resistance in re-entry conditions that can be improved by SiC addition. These promising high performance materials are also characterized by hardness above 20 GPa, high wear resistance, high emissivity, high electrical conductivity and good thermal shock resistance [2,3]. Leading applications are currently found in aerospace, more speci /uniFB01 cally in the possibility to employ them to realize sharp-shaped hot structures like wing leading edges and nose caps able to withstand the severe thermal requirements of next generation of hypersonic re-entry vehicles. The highly thermal demanding trajectories foreseen for future space planes like winged reentry vehicles dictate the need for base materials able to sustain operating temperatures approaching 2500 K, to resist evaporation, erosion and oxidation in the harsh re-entry environment.

The early works were devoted to the production of dense materials by mean of pressure assisted sintering, and to investigate the in /uniFB02 uence of a variety of additives, including carbon and silicon carbide, on the processing and oxidation resistance of Hf and Zr diborides.
