question: What does the negative Seebeck coefficient observed in Dubey 2022 for HfB2-ZrB2 composites indicate about the type of mobile charge carriers responsible for electrical transport?
answer: The negative Seebeck coefficient observed in Dubey 2022 for HfB2-ZrB2 composites indicates that the mobile charge carriers responsible for electrical transport are electrons, suggesting n-type semiconductor behavior [1].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
As the total thermal conduction in diborides comprises both electronic and phononic component,   electrical conductivity ( σ e) at room temperature  has  also  been  assessed.  The  electrical  conductivity  and Seebeck coefficient (S) of the HfB2-ZrB2-based composites was measured in the He atmosphere at room temperature using ZEM-3M10 apparatus (ULVAC-RICO Inc.). The figure of  merit  ( ZT = S 2 σ eT κ )  was  calculated, Table  2,  from  thermoelectric  parameters  (electrical  conductivity  and Seebeck  coefficient).  Seebeck  coefficient  is  observed  to  be  negative, suggesting n-type semiconductor behavior, with electrons to be dominant charge carriers in all the composites, and following similar trend of electrical conductivity at room temperature with maximum of -3.57 V/K observed for HZ20S6C. The power factor is ~6 × 10 -5 Wm -1 K 2 at room temperature for Z20S, HZ20S and HZ20S6C composites (Table 2) except for H20S (where the presence of HfB2  phase dominates). On the other hand, ZT value for Z20S is ~43 times to that of H20S. After incorporation of CNT, all thermoelectric parameters in HZ20S6C are observed to increase ~4 -6% as compared to that of HZ20S. Low ZT value in ~1 × 10 -4 Wm -1 K -1 range (due to high κ ) for these composites is not sufficient for thermoelectric applications at room temperature.

A B S T R A C T
of phonon transport which reduces drastically in presence of impurities such  as  WC  introduced  during  milling,  thus,  lowering  the κ of  the diboride based ceramic [11]. The room temperature κ of ZrB2  also decreases by ~40% on introduction of HfB2  in the matrix without much difference in charge carrier density (similar electronic structure, AlB2) but rather, due to difference in phonon scattering and formation of solid solution of (Hf-Zr)B2  [12]. CNT, on the other hand, not only improves densification  but  works  synergistically  to  enhance  the  thermal  and electrical transport in diboride based composites [10,13,14]. The addition of 10 vol% CNT to ZrB2 + 20 vol% SiC has elicited marginal increase in high temperature (1200 ◦ C) thermal conductivity to ~52.3 Wm -1 K -1 [10].
The spark plasma sintering (SPS) of (Hf,Zr)B2-SiC-CNT system (at 1850 ◦ C,  30  MPa for  10 min hold time, vacuum ~6 Pa) is reported earlier highlighting the solid solution, and eliciting enhanced mechanical  (H~28.1 GPa, KIC~10.2 MPa.m 1/2 )  [15]  and tribological performance  [16]  with  synergistic  superior  mechanical  properties in HZ20S6C (37 vol.% HfB2, 37 vol.% ZrB2, 20 vol.% SiC and 6 vol.% CNT) compared to that of HZ20S.
Fig. 1i(a-i) [15] present the microstructural morphology of ZrB2-HfB2-based composites, and elemental spectrum of starting phase pure SiC, HfB2 and ZrB2 powders. Further, marginal increase in oxidation resistance (~6%)   is depicted by HZ20S6C due to the synergistic solid solution, presence of CNT and SiO2 formation on oxidation of SiC phase [17,18].

10. Challenges, emerging trends, and conclusions
UHTCs  including  ZrB2,  HfB2,  TiB2,  TaC,  HfC,  TiC,  etc.  possess admirable  properties;  however,  ZrB2  can  be  considered  as  the  most suitable UHTC material in its monolithic form due to its lower density (6.10  gcm GLYPH<0> 3 ),  chemical  inertness,  high  thermal  conductivity  (56 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 ), high melting temperature and thermal conductivity of its oxide  (~2660 ◦ C  and  ~2  Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 respectively)  proving  strong candidature of ZrB2 for TPS of hypersonic vehicles. It may be highlighted that the starting powder purity and sintered densification play a very significant role in dictating the structural and high temperature oxidation properties, and merely extrapolated prediction of porous composites may not do justice to with that of phase-pure and dense ZrB2-HfB2SiC based composites. Pressure-assisted sintering techniques such as SPS and HP can provide remarkable properties. Among the second phase sintering additives, SiC results in enhancing the densification, mechanical and oxidative performance of ZrB2. On one hand where > 20% SiC content provides an enhanced fracture strength and low temperature oxidation resistance, whereas on the other hand < 20% SiC content assists high temperature performance of ZrB2-based UHTCs. Further, the finer size of SiC ( < 5 μ m) with optimum content (20 vol %) may impart superior combination of thermo-mechanical properties in ZrB2. However,  incorporation  of  HfB2 may  further  limit  the  oxygen  diffusion through the formation of HfO2 and enhance the oxidation resistance of ZrB2 -SiC-based UHTCs. Further, with higher thermal conductivity of HfB2 (~105 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 ), its incorporation has provided ~30% enhanced oxidation  resistance  in

3.1.1. Theoretical calculation of diffusion coefficient
The average diffusion coefficient for Hf in ZrB2  was observed to be higher  (i.e.,  ~10.2 × 10 GLYPH<0> 13 m 2 /s)  than  that  of  Zr  into  HfB2 (i.e., ~2.7 × 10 GLYPH<0> 13 m 2 /s) which is also supported by higher diffusion depth of Hf into ZrB2. Diffusion coefficient calculated in the present work is in accordance with the lattice diffusion of hot pressed ZrB2 at 2100 ◦ C [44], but diffusion coefficient alone cannot justify the mechanism. Hassan et. al. [35] reported the grain boundary diffusion to be dominating mechanism at temperatures < 1950 ◦ C for pure ZrB2  phase, however, with 20 vol%  SiC  addition,  lattice  diffusion  dominates  at  temperatures > 1850 ◦ C, which is very close to the joining temperature used in the present work. Furthermore, recent study by Dubey et. al. [45] also reported the transition from grain boundary to lattice diffusion at 1850 ◦ C for pure HfB2, which might shift to lower values with SiC reinforcement. Therefore,  during  joining  of  ZS-HS  composite  via  SPS  at  1800 ◦ C, 40 MPa pressure, the diffusion mechanism is dominated by the grain boundary diffusion along with some transition to the lattice diffusion as suggested by the calculated diffusion coefficient value (i.e., 2.7 × 10 GLYPH<0> 9 m 2 /s).
Fig. 6. Optical images of the fractured samples after 4-point bending test (a) ZS-ZS, (b) ZS-HS joints and fractured samples after shear test (c) ZS-ZS, (d) ZS-HS joints. SEM micrographs of the fractured surface after 4-point bending test of (e) ZS-ZS and (f) ZS-HS joints revealing intergranular and trans granular fracture along with the presence of step like cleavage planes.

4. Discussion
Thermal transport in solid materials is a consequence of electron and phonon conduction. Therefore, thermal conductivity is explained in terms of an electronic contribution and a phonon contribution. The phonon contribution depends on number of carriers, phonon velocity and phonon mean free path, which are all constant at temperatures above the Debye temperature. Debye temperatures for ZrB2, HfB2, SiC and La2O3 are 750 K, 30 565 K, 2 1080 K 31 and 160 K, 28 respectively. Therefore, the phonon contribution to conductivity should be constant at temperatures well above the Debye ( ∼ 1000 ◦ C) temperature as has been highlighted for pure ZrB2 and HfB2. 27 Grain boundaries are resistive elements for thermal transport leading to so-called Kapitza resistance. Therefore, thermal conductivity decreases with reduced grain size. 32 The intrinsic Kapitza resistance should be relatively temperature insensitive above the Debye temperature unless physical changes take place with increasing temperature. 18 The dominant part of thermal conductivity of monolithic ZrB2 and HfB2 is the electron contribution which could represent up to 90% of the thermal conductivity in ZrB2 and HfB2 ceramics. 17 SiC addition is known to increase the phonon contribution to thermal conductivity. 17 However, data on the effect of SiC on UHTC thermal conductivity are contradictory. Due to its high thermal conductivity 33 compared with ZrB2 and HfB2, SiC should increase thermal conductivity and the greater the amount of SiC added, the higher the thermal conductivity as a better connected internal network is formed. 13,14,18,34 However, the presence of SiC-ZrB2 or SiC-HfB2 in addition to ZrB2-ZrB2 or HfB2-HfB2 interfaces can lower thermal conductivity below monolithic thermal conductivities values 15,27 as a consequence of Kapitza resistance.

question: What is the resistance to indentation loading measured via Vickers method for Wang et al.'s GdB6 ceramics prepared by reactive spark plasma sintering at 1800°C?
answer: The resistance to indentation loading measured via Vickers method for Wang et al.'s GdB6 ceramics prepared by reactive spark plasma sintering at 1800°C is 18.9 GPa [1][3].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
In this study, a highly dense GdB6  bulk ceramic material was fabricated using one-step in-situ reaction spark plasma sintering technology. The sample with a relative density of 96.7 % and a purity of 96.9 wt% was successfully obtained by sintering at 1800 ◦ C for 30 min under a uniaxial pressure of 50 MPa. The Vickers hardness, elastic modulus and fracture toughness of the as-obtained GdB6 bulk ceramics were 18.9 GPa, 209 GPa and 1.6 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 , respectively. The thermal conductivity of GdB6 at room temperature was 28.68 W ⋅ m GLYPH<0> 1 ⋅ K GLYPH<0> 1 , 70 % of which was mainly attributed to phonon vibration, while the remaining 30 % to the electrical thermal conductivity.  In  addition, the as-obtained GdB6  ceramics showed no phase transformation after heat treatment at a relatively  high  temperature of 2100 ◦ C for 20 min in Ar atmosphere. The highly covalent octahedral boron framework played a critical role in maintaining the phase stability of the GdB6 crystal lattice. This suggested that GdB6 ceramics could be used as potential ultra-high temperature ceramics for aerospace applications and highpower vacuum electronic devices operated at high temperatures.
electronic devices operated at high temperatures of 1500 -1700 ◦ C [14], as well as optical sensors [15] and thin film based emitters [16].

3.2. Mechanical properties
The  indentation  fracture  toughness  of  GdB6  ceramics  sintered  at 1800 ◦ C  (1.6 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 )  was  lower  than  that  sintered  at  1900 ◦ C (1.8 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 ). The relative density and the mean grain sizes of these two samples were relatively similar, so the difference in their fracture toughness could be mainly attributed to the amount of GdBO3 impurity, which has a low hardness. The presence of a secondary phase could lead to the improvement of the indentation fracture toughness due to the crack deflection and crack bridging toughening mechanism. The content of the GdBO3 increased from 3.1 wt% to 4.9 wt% as the sintering temperature increased from 1800 to 1900 ◦ C, which might have resulted in the  improved  indentation  fracture  toughness.  At  the  same  time,  the grain-boundaries of the samples sintered at a higher temperature are usually of a higher quality (less pores and/or cleaner grain boundaries) compared to the sample sintered at a lower temperature, which may also improve the fracture toughness. It should be pointed out that the mechanical properties of GdB6 ceramics have not yet been reported except the Vickers hardness in one work (Table 2). The Vickers hardness of GdB6  ceramics obtained in the present study was significantly higher than that reported by Binder et al. [40]. This can be explained by a very low  relative  density  (85  %)  of  their  sample,  when  compared  to  the relatively dense samples ( > 96 %) in the present work.
Fig. 2. (a) SAED pattern and (b) atomic-resolved HAADF image of GdB6 fabricated at 1800 ◦ C, showing atomic arrangements along [010] direction.
Fig. 3. SEM images of the microstructures and the grain size distribution of GdB6 fabricated at different temperatures: (a) and (d) 1700 ◦ C, (b) and (e) 1800 ◦ C, (c) and (f) 1900 ◦ C.

4. Conclusion
Highly dense GdB6 bulk ceramics were successfully fabricated using an in-situ reaction via SPS. The purity and the relative density of the GdB6 ceramics  sintered  at  1800 ◦ C  were  96.9 wt%  and  96.7 wt%, respectively.  The  Vickers  hardness,  elastic  modulus,  and  fracture toughness of the GdB6 bulk ceramics sintered at 1800 ◦ C was 18.9 GPa, 209 GPa and 1.6 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 ,  respectively. The thermal conductivity of the GdB6  sample at room temperature was 28.68 W ⋅ m GLYPH<0> 1 ⋅ K GLYPH<0> 1 , 70 % of which was attributed to the contribution of phonon vibration. The ultrahigh temperature stability test indicated that the GdB6  ceramics was stable after heat treatment at a relatively high temperature of 2100 ◦ C for 20 min in Ar atmosphere. The octahedral boron framework played a key role in maintaining the phase stability of GdB6. It implies that GdB6 ceramics can be a potential candidate material for ultra-high temperature applications, such as aerospace applications, and high-power vacuum electronic devices operated at high temperatures.

4.1.2.  Spark plasma sintering technology, SPS
Spark plasma sintering (SPS) is a rapid sintering technique that uses pulsed  electric  current  passing  directly  through  ceramic  powders  to generate  instantaneous  high  temperatures  and  plasma,  enabling  the rapid densification of powders [70,71]. For example, Yang et al. [72] successfully  fabricated  nearly  fully  dense  HfB2 ceramics  without  additives using SPS (as shown in Fig. 9). At a sintering temperature of 1950 °C,  the  HfB2 ceramics exhibited  optimal  mechanical  properties, with a hardness of 26.34   ±   2.1 GPa, a fracture toughness of 7.12   ±   1.33 MPa·m 1/2 ,  and  a  flexural  strength  of  501   ±   120 MPa. Mao et al. [73] prepared ZrB2-SiC-ZrC powders via the sol-gel method and sintered them using SPS at 2000 °C under 40 MPa, achieving materials with a relative density of 98 %. After an ablation test at 2000 °C for 200 s, the material exhibited a linear ablation rate of  8.32 × 10 -3 mm/s and a mass ablation rate of - 4.268 × 10 -4 g/s. Fine ZrO2  particles were uniformly distributed within the SiO2  melt, increasing  the  melt's  viscosity  and  acting  as  pinning  agents,  which enhanced the oxygen barrier of oxidation layer and resistance to mechanical erosion.
Fig. 8. SEM images of fracture surfaces of the sample (a) single-step 1500 °C, (b) two-step 1500 °C, (c) single-step 1700 °C and (d) single-step 1800 °C. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [69], © Elsevier 2017.
Fig. 9. SEM images of longitudinal microstructure for HfB2 samples obtained at different sintering temperatures: (a) 1800 °C, (b) 1850 °C, (c) 1900 °C, (d) 1950 °C. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [72], © Elsevier 2022.

3.2. Mechanical properties

question: According to Wang et al., what proportion of the heat transport through GdB6 at room temperature is due to atomic lattice vibrations as opposed to electronic charge carrier movement?
answer: According to Wang et al., 70 % of the heat transport through GdB6 at room temperature is due to atomic lattice vibrations (phonon vibration), while the remaining 30 % is due to electronic charge carrier movement [2][3].

3.3. Thermal conductivity

It is well known that the electron contribution to the thermal conductivity κ e can be calculated using the electrical resistivity of materials, according to the WidmannFrantz law as shown in Eq. Then, the phonon contribution to the thermal conductivity ( κ p ) could be calculated using the following equation [38]:

The results of κ , κ e , and κ p for all GdB6 ceramics sintered at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 5. It was obvious to see that the thermal conductivity of the as-obtained GdB6 ceramics at room temperature was mainly attributed to the phonon vibration (~70 %). In the electronic structure  of  GdB6,  the  Gd  atom  of  the  trivalent  nature  provides  two electrons  to  the  undercharged  boron  sublattice,  leaving  a  surplus  of electrons, and allowing GdB6  to behave as a metal-like conductor [39, 40]. Therefore, the contribution of the charge carriers to the thermal conductivity of the materials at room temperature was not negligible (~30 %).

A B S T R A C T
In this study, a highly dense GdB6  bulk ceramic material was fabricated using one-step in-situ reaction spark plasma sintering technology. The sample with a relative density of 96.7 % and a purity of 96.9 wt% was successfully obtained by sintering at 1800 ◦ C for 30 min under a uniaxial pressure of 50 MPa. The Vickers hardness, elastic modulus and fracture toughness of the as-obtained GdB6 bulk ceramics were 18.9 GPa, 209 GPa and 1.6 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 , respectively. The thermal conductivity of GdB6 at room temperature was 28.68 W ⋅ m GLYPH<0> 1 ⋅ K GLYPH<0> 1 , 70 % of which was mainly attributed to phonon vibration, while the remaining 30 % to the electrical thermal conductivity.  In  addition, the as-obtained GdB6  ceramics showed no phase transformation after heat treatment at a relatively  high  temperature of 2100 ◦ C for 20 min in Ar atmosphere. The highly covalent octahedral boron framework played a critical role in maintaining the phase stability of the GdB6 crystal lattice. This suggested that GdB6 ceramics could be used as potential ultra-high temperature ceramics for aerospace applications and highpower vacuum electronic devices operated at high temperatures.
electronic devices operated at high temperatures of 1500 -1700 ◦ C [14], as well as optical sensors [15] and thin film based emitters [16].

4. Conclusion
Highly dense GdB6 bulk ceramics were successfully fabricated using an in-situ reaction via SPS. The purity and the relative density of the GdB6 ceramics  sintered  at  1800 ◦ C  were  96.9 wt%  and  96.7 wt%, respectively.  The  Vickers  hardness,  elastic  modulus,  and  fracture toughness of the GdB6 bulk ceramics sintered at 1800 ◦ C was 18.9 GPa, 209 GPa and 1.6 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 ,  respectively. The thermal conductivity of the GdB6  sample at room temperature was 28.68 W ⋅ m GLYPH<0> 1 ⋅ K GLYPH<0> 1 , 70 % of which was attributed to the contribution of phonon vibration. The ultrahigh temperature stability test indicated that the GdB6  ceramics was stable after heat treatment at a relatively high temperature of 2100 ◦ C for 20 min in Ar atmosphere. The octahedral boron framework played a key role in maintaining the phase stability of GdB6. It implies that GdB6 ceramics can be a potential candidate material for ultra-high temperature applications, such as aerospace applications, and high-power vacuum electronic devices operated at high temperatures.

3.1. Microstructure and phase composition of GdB6
Fig. 1(a) shows the XRD patterns of GdB6 (JCPDS card No. 38 -1426) ceramics prepared by in-situ reactive spark plasma sintering at different temperatures: 1700, 1800, and 1900 ◦ C. GdB6 was identified as the main phase  in  all  samples.  A  small  amount  of  GdBO3 (JCPDS  card  No. 74 -1932)  impurity  was  also  detected.  According  to  the  Rietveld refinement results, with the increasing temperature from 1700 to 1900 ◦ C, the amount of GdB6 phase slightly decreased from 97.0 wt% to 95.1 wt%, while the content of GdBO3  impurity increased from 3.0 wt% to 4.9 wt%. Fig. 1(b) presents the typical Rietveld refinement of the XRD pattern  of  GdB6 fabricated  at  1800 ◦ C.  The  Rietveld  fitting  results (reliability factors Rwp = 9.6 %) indicated that a cubic structure of GdB6 was obtained (the inset in Fig. 1(b)). The lattice parameter a was around 4.1071 Å (Table 1), which was equal to the theoretical lattice parameter of GdB6 (JCPDS card No. 38 -1426) [14]. The lattice parameter was also in good agreement with the TEM analysis, by which the value of a was confirmed  to  be  4.1641  Å,  according  to  the  selected  area  electron diffraction (SAED) and the atomic resolution high-angle annular dark field  (HAADF)  scanning  transmission  electron  microscopy  (STEM) image  of  GdB6  ceramics,  as  shown  in  Fig.  2.  The  atomic  resolution HAADF image showed the atomic configuration of the atoms in the view of the [001] zone axis. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.4164 nm belongs to the (100) crystal plane of GdB6. The (100) plane spacing and the lattice constant of GdB6  are theoretically equal, due to its simple cubic structure. The experimental value of (100) crystal plane spacing in Fig. 2(b) is close  to  the  theoretical  value  of  the  lattice  constant  of

question: In Zoli et al.'s carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2 composite system, how consistently were the reinforcing filaments distributed throughout the ceramic matrix without clustering?
answer: The sources provided do not mention Zoli et al.'s work or the specific distribution consistency of reinforcing filaments in their carbon fiber-reinforced ZrB2 composite system. Therefore, the answer cannot be determined from the given sources.

chunks.text:
3.2. Densification process of composite materials
Further observations were conducted by randomly selecting regions within carbon fiber bundles. The results show that the ceramic matrix composed of ZrB2, SiC, and C is uniformly distributed within the fiber bundles and fully fills the spaces between individual carbon filaments, thereby providing a microstructural foundation for the material ' s performance (Fig. 5j and k). A single carbon fiber was randomly selected for high-magnification observation, revealing a PyC coating tightly bonded to  the  surrounding  ceramic  matrix  (Fig.  5l).  Due  to  the  use  of  lowtemperature, pressureless conditions during the PIP pyrolysis process, as well as controlled total PIP cycle numbers, damage to the carbon fibers and their PyC coatings was effectively minimized. As a result, the interfaces on both sides of the PyC coating remain distinct and intact, and the PyC layer retains its structural integrity. The intact PyC coating forms a tight sheath around the carbon fiber, preventing chemical reactions between the fiber and oxide impurities present on the surface of ceramic powders, thus protecting the fiber from degradation. In addition,  the  PyC  layer  appropriately  reduces  the  interfacial  bonding strength  between  the  carbon  fiber  and  the  ceramic  matrix,  thereby effectively facilitating the fiber pull-out mechanism, ultimately contributing to the toughening of the composite.
T. Xia et al.
Fig. 5. The morphology and structure of the composite material: (a) the macroscopic morphology of the composite material after three impregnation-pyrolysis cycles of polycarbosilane; (b) the macroscopic morphology of the composite material after three impregnation-pyrolysis cycles of furfuryl ketone resin; (c) the densification process;  (d -f)  the  microstructural  morphology  and  material  composition  of  the  composite  surface;  (g -i)  the  microstructure  and  elemental  composition  of  the composite matrix; (j -l) the microstructure between the fiber bundles of the composite material.

3.2. Densification and microstructure
The  typical  morphology  of  the  polished  surface  of  Cf/ZrB2-based composite is presented in Fig. 7. A marco defect-free composite can be appreciated in the low magnification images, and the continuous carbon fibers were homogeneously distributed in the matrix as shown in Fig. 7 (a)-(c). Both the inter-bundle and intra-bundle regions of fibers were fully filled with the matrix. Almost no pores on a micro-level were found in the matrix, and the C fibers were well surrounded by the ceramic matrix as shown in Fig. 7(d), indicating that the present processing route is  a  promising  way  for  the  fabrication  of  continuous  carbon  fiberreinforced ceramic matrix composites. It should be noted that a very limited nano-sized pores were found at the triple point regions among the tightly packed fibers as shown in Fig. 7(e). The density of the Cf/ ZrB2-based  composite  was  measured  as  3.82 g/cm 3 ,  according  to Archimede ' s  method. Generally, the sintering additive of ZrSi2  has a tendency to react not only with the polymer-derived carbon but also with  C  fibers  to  form  the  in-situ  ZrC  and  SiC  during  sintering  [52]. However, it can be seen in Fig. 7(f), the C fiber mostly presented clear round and smooth profiles, indicating that no evident interfacial reaction occurred between C fibers and matrix during sintering.

4.5.3.  Research progress of "solid-liquid" combination process
The research team led by Zhang [122,125,126] successfully fabricated Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites with highly uniform distribution by introducing  nano-sized  ZrB2 powders  into  three-dimensional  needled carbon fiber  weaves.  It  can  be  seen  that  the  composite  is  near  fully dense within the field of view, without visible defects such as cracks and pores. The pores and cracks in the green body are fully filled by the in-situ pyrolytic SiC (indicated by red arrows in Fig. 19 c and d). There are no obvious defects found in the composite, strongly proving that the high-pressure  infiltration  technology  can  effectively  eliminate  closed pores inside the composite. During the PIP cycles by high-pressure infiltration and low-temperature pyrolysis, the in-situ pyrolytic SiC not only fully fills the gaps between closely packed ZrB2 particles, but also tightly  bonds  the  originally  dispersed  particles,  resulting  in  a  concurrent improvement in the relative density and matrix strength of the composite.
To  further  enhance  the  performance  of  composites,  Zhang  et  al. [124] applied "Solid-Liquid" combination process to fabricate Cf/ZrCSiC composites based on the ZrC system. During the solid-phase stage, they utilized needled carbon fiber weaves as the matrix and introduced ZrC  particles  into  the  fiber  preforms  using  a  vibration-assisted  slip casting  technique,  achieving  a  ceramic  particle  incorporation  rate  of over 45 vol%. Based on this foundation, the researchers introduced a SiC precursor into the green body and performed pressureless sintering at  1300 °C,  resulting  in  highly  densified  Cf/ZrC-SiC  composites.  Microstructural analysis revealed that the sintered composites exhibited uniform and dense ceramic particle distribution in both inter-bundle and  intra-bundle  regions.  Furthermore,  ZrC  and  SiC  ceramics  were highly uniformly arranged and bonded within the carbon fiber weave.

3.1. Microstructural features and mechanical properties
A summary of the bulk densities, open porosities and the mechanical properties,  flexural  strength  and  fracture  toughness,  are  reported  in Table 3.
The  microstructures  of  the  surface  and  cross  section  before  the tribological tests are shown in Fig. 4. The light gray and dark gray phases represent ZrB2  and SiC respectively, while the carbon fibers are black. The matrix was nearly fully dense, with few pores around the fibers (Fig. 4b). The surface (XY plane) shows the random fiber distribution resulting from the novel slurry-based process (Fig. 1). From the cross section of the sample (Fig. 4a) it is possible to notice the anisotropy consistently  with  the  process  adopted.  The  fibers  were  still  homogeneously dispersed in the matrix and randomly oriented in the XY plane, but  they  were  partially  ordered  in  the  XZ  plane.  No  signs  of  fiber degradation were observed and the fibers retained their original shape. As reported in our previous studies [44] the thin organic film deposited on the fiber from the slurry infiltration process acted likely as sacrificial carbon during sintering, therefore minimizing the fiber/matrix interaction.
The mechanical properties of the sample ZS were comparable and in some cases better than samples reinforced with short carbon fibers obtained  via  ball  milling,  having  the  same  ceramic  matrix  and  similar carbon fiber content [45]. In fact, the bending strength was similar with around 107 MPa for the ZS sample of the present study and around 103 MPa for the milled fiber reference sample. However, the fracture toughness  of  the  former  was  significantly  higher  (3.6 MPa · m 0.5 vs 2.4 MPa ⋅ m 0.5 ).  Nonetheless,  compared  to  samples  reinforced  with continuous carbon fibers, bending strength (107 MPa and 218 MPa, ZS and long fiber, respectively) and fracture toughness (3.6 MPa · m 0.5 and 6.1 MPa · m 0.5 , ZS and long fiber, respectively) was significantly lower [46].

3.1 Microstructure of the sintered material
The  studied  material  had  a  density  of  4.03 g/cm 3 and  a  porosity  of  9.2  %.  Carbon  fibres  were homogeneously infiltrated with the ceramic matrix ( Figure 1 a ) and SiC was distributed preferentially at ZrB2 grain borders or near the fibres ( Figure 1 b ). No oxide phases were identified by EDS. The grain sizes of ZrB2 and SiC were unaffected by the sintering process and were ~2.5 µm and ~0.5 µm respectively.  Fine  porosity  inside  the  ceramic  matrix  and  slight  signs  of  fibre  degradation  were observed from the microstructural investigation ( Figure 1 b ).

question: What bulk density fraction was successfully achieved in Zoli et al.'s ZrB2-carbon fiber composites when the holding time during spark plasma sintering was extended to 90 seconds at constant pressure?
answer: The bulk density fraction achieved in Zoli et al.'s ZrB2-carbon fiber composites when the holding time during spark plasma sintering was extended to 90 seconds at constant pressure was greater than 90% [1].

chunks.text:
3. Results and discussion
The bulk density and the residual porosity of the composites are reported in Table 1. The lowest relative density of 84% was obtained for the sample sintered at 1800 °C for a dwell time of 40 s ( A ). When the dwell time was extended to 90 s ( B ), a densi /uniFB01 cation degree higher than 90% was achieved. An increase in the sintering temperature from 1800 °C to 1850 °C led to a decrease of the porosity from 8.3% ( C ) to 4.5% ( D ); a further increase of the sintering temperature to 1900 °C did not have any major e /uniFB00 ects on densi /uniFB01 cation ( E ). When a heating rate of ~3000 °C/min was used, like in /uniFB02 ash SPS [31], extreme damage of the carbon /uniFB01 bres was observed ( F ), as illustrated later. This suggests that in order to achieve dense samples while preserving the /uniFB01 bres structural and morphological integrity, precise temperature control is needed, rather than an ultra-fast processing time, of just a few seconds.
It has to be mentioned that in the ZrB2 -SiC -C system, several eutectic temperatures exist: at 2100 -2200 °C for the three-component system [32], at 2270 °C between ZrB2-SiC [33], and at 2390 °C between ZrB2-C [34]. These set the threshold of temperatures that should not be exceeded during processing and usage.
It is interesting to compare these results with our previous ones where we sintered a composite with 45 vol% of Carbon short /uniFB01 bres [12] at 1800 °C/30 MPa/10 min by hot pressing, reaching just 85% of relative density. This result is quite similar to sample A , which however, underwent a much shorter holding time (40 s vs 10 min). In this case both the enhanced pressure (60 MPa) and the more e /uniFB03 cient heating (Joule heating) allowed a fast densi /uniFB01 cation that was easily improved with the increase of the maximum temperature at 1850 and 1900 °C.

4. Conclusions
Spark plasma sintering processing was used to produce dense short /uniFB01 bre-reinforced ceramic composites based on ZrB2 with a carbon /uniFB01 bres content of 45 vol%. The relative density of 96.7%, achieved for samples sintered at 1900 °C, is signi /uniFB01 cantly higher than analogous specimens produced by conventional hot pressing, which achieved only 85% of the theoretical density. Careful control of the sintering parameters, such as the heating rate, maximum temperature and holding time resulted in materials with near full density and no appreciable damage of the carbon /uniFB01 bres.
- It has also been assessed that above a critical temperature (> 1900 °C), set by the eutectic melt met in the ZrB2-SiC-C system, extreme /uniFB01 bre degradation occurs, with associated brittle behaviour of the composite. It has also been observed that slight deviations in the density level have a strong impact on the fracture behaviour.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx. doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2017.05.029.

4.4. Spark Plasma Sintering
In the case of spark plasma sintering (SPS) of ZrB2 ceramics, the effect of processing parameters (sintering time and sintering temperature) and powder particle size (nano- or micron- sized) on the densification has been shown in Fig. 7b [120]. It can be noted that the densification rate is extremely high for the smaller particle size (≤25 nm) and near-theoretical densification of ZrB2 is attained even at very low sintering temperatures (below 1700 °C) and shorter sintering times of 16 min or lesser. The spark plasma sintered di-borides are usually observed to possess fine microstructure and good combination of properties. In order to address some issues related to non-uniform densification (and properties) primarily due to fast heating rate and lesser holding times, recently we had explored the application of multi-stage spark plasma sintering (MSS-SPS) schedule, which involves holding at one or more intermediate temperatures for few minutes before reaching the final sintering temperatures [98,173-179]. Keeping the total holding time constant (i.e. lesser holding time at the final sintering temperature), more uniform densification and finer microstructures were achieved for ZrB2-SiC-TiSi2 [175] and TiB2-TiSi2 composites [98], resulting in still improved mechanical properties (as compared to similar compositions sintered via the usual single-step SPS route).

4.4. Spark Plasma Sintering
In analogy with reactive hot pressing, starting with stoichiometric amounts of ZrSi2, B4C and carbon, recently dense (up to ~98% ρth) and fine grained (Hf, Zr)B2-SiC composites [SiC: ~200 nm and (Hf, Zr)B2 ~300-500 nm] were developed via RSPS at 1600 °C for 10 min [180]. Gurcan et al. [181] observed that high cooling rates (600 °C/min) deteriorates the HfB2 material due to thermal stress developed during cooling. However, slower cooling rates (50 °C/min) enhanced the sinter-density and hardness, despite the grain size being greater, as compared to that in the fast-cooled composites. HfB2 was fabricated using raw powders of HfO2 and B in SPS at 2050 °C, 30 min and 60 MPa.
Karthiselva et al. [182] reported the low temperature synthesis of monolithic TiB2 ceramic using RSPS. It exhibited good mechanical properties, but as the content of the TiB phase increased, the mechanical properties deteriorated. On somewhat similar lines, our work with TiB2 has suggested that SPS may not always lead to improved combination of properties due to the possible occurrences of deleterious sintering reactions (formation of TiB) with kinetics being accelerated in the presence of the electric field [183]. In a recent work, Sani et al. [184] developed monolithic ZrB2 and TaB2 starting with the elemental precursors via two routes: selfpropagating high temperature synthesis, followed by spark plasma sintering (SHS-SPS) and RSPS. It was reported that densification of ~95-98% ρth could be achieved for ZrB2 and ~94-95% ρth for TaB2. However, in all the cases SHS-SPS led to the maximum sinterdensity. The SHS-SPS processed samples also characterized with relatively small grain size and better mechanical properties when compared to RSPS samples.

A B S T R A C T

[15]  fabricated Carbon fibre reinforced UHTC composites having various compositions including ZrB2, HfB2 base matrix through slurry impregnation and final densification with carbon through CVI technique. The final densities of the composites were in the range of 1.86 -2.11 ± 0.001 g/cm 3 and reported  that  Halfnium  (Hf)  based  UHTC  offered  superior  oxidation resistance [15]. Reactive melt infiltration (RMI) was also used in the development of UHTCMCs, in which carbon fibre performs were infiltrated wuth Zr or Hf based alloys and facilitated in-situ formation of UHTCM phases [16 -18]. Luo et al. [17] fabricated C -SiC-HfC composite by melt infiltration of HfSi2  alloy in to the porous preform of Cf-SiC composite at 1600 -1700 ◦ C. The reported density and porosity of the composite  were  2.60 ± 0.02  g/cm 3 and  17.4 ± 0.1%,  respectively [16 -18]. Very recently, slurry infiltration followed by pressure assisted sintering (hot pressing and spark plasma sintering) was adopted for the development of UHTCMCs [19 -22]. Sciti et al. [19] fabricated carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2 base UHTCMCs by vacuum-bag infiltration and hot pressing, using 1D fabric carbon preforms, having various fiber volume fractions  from  55  to  70  %.  The  reported  bulk  density,  porosity  and flexural strength of these composites are in the range of 2.4 -3.1 g/cm 3 , 16 -28%  and  40 -95  MPa  respectively  [19].  Vinci  et  al.  studied  the oxidation behaviour of carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2 -SiC composites by thermogravimetric  analysis  up  to  1550 ◦ C  and  reported  that  of 800 -1000 ◦ C is  critical  temperature  range,  where  rapid  oxidation

question: What is the density of zirconium carbide (ZrC) in g/cm³ according to Kim et al. 2024 study on vacuum plasma spray coating of ZrC on carbon-carbon composites?
answer: The density of zirconium carbide (ZrC) is 6.73 g/cm³ according to Kim et al. 2024 study [1].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
To protect C/C composite in extremely high-temperature conditions, the protective coating layers having heat-resistant and anti-oxidative properties  are  required.  Among  the  protective  materials,  ultra-high temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  particularly  noteworthy  due  to their extremely high melting temperatures around 3000 ◦ C or higher, exceptional hardness, as well as excellent chemical stability and strength under  high-temperature  conditions  [5,6].  Among  UHTCs,  zirconium carbide (ZrC) has a remarkably high melting point of 3530 ◦ C [7,8]. Additionally, its oxide, ZrO2, also has a high melting point of 2700 ◦ C [9].  Furthermore,  ZrC  exhibits  excellent  resistance  to  corrosion  and possesses a density of 6.73 g/cm 3 [10], which is notably lower compared to other UHTCs such as HfC (12.7 g/cm 3 ) [11], TaC (14.5 g/cm 3 ) [12], and WC (15.8 g/cm 3 ) [13].
To coat ZrC layer on the C/C composite, it is crucial to utilize the corresponding ZrC powders in the VPS method. Consequently, it becomes inevitable to consider the effects of the particle size of the raw powders on the quality of the coating layer. 1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  potential  candidate materials  for  thermal  protection  systems  in  extreme  environments. Preparation of UHTC coatings on fiber reinforced composites is a logical choice  for  improving  the  oxidation  and  ablation  resistance  in  high temperature  and  oxygen  contained  environments  [1 -3].  Among  all UHTCs, ZrC is very attractive for the unique combination of low density (6.73 g/cm 3 ),  high  melting  point  (3540 ◦ C) and good thermal shock resistance [4 -6]. In the past few years, ZrC based coating has attracted lots of attention and SiC has been proved to be a suitable additive to improve the ablation resistance of ZrC coating [7 -9].
Vacuum  plasma  spray  is  particularly  suitable  for  UHTC  coating fabrication and the microstructure of as-sprayed coating has important effect  on  its  oxidation/ablation  behavior.  In  our  previous  work,  we fabricated  ZrB2-20  vol%MoSi2 composite  coatings  by  atmospheric plasma spray (APS) and vacuum plasma spray (VPS), then investigated the effect of fabrication method on the oxidation behavior of the coatings. The results showed the latter exhibited better oxidation resistance

A B S T R A C T
Ablation-resistant  zirconium  carbide  (ZrC)  layers  were  coated  onto  carbon/carbon  (C/C)  composites  using vacuum plasma spraying. The structural and ablative characteristics of ZrC layers applied in single-versus multistep coating processes were compared. An increase in the number of coating cycles from 4 to 20 in single-step coating increased the ZrC layer ' s thickness from 32.3 μ m to 116.2 μ m even though the coating rate per cycle was reduced from 8.1 μ m/cycle to 5.8 μ m/cycle. By increasing the number of cycles, the substrate surface was continuously heated, enhancing the lateral spread of molten droplets and improving the porosity and hardness of the ZrC layer. On the other hand, multi-step coating, involving repetition of the single-step process, yielded a 20% thickness  increase  of  ZrC  layers  with  a  higher  coating  rate  while  maintaining  comparable  mechanical properties despite an identical total number of coating cycles. Furthermore, the multi-step-coated layers showed better thermal oxidation resistance than the single-step-coated layers due to the formation of ZrC/ZrC interfaces having larger grain boundaries and the thicker ZrCxOy layers, effectively barring oxygen diffusion. The multi-step coating of identical ZrC layers suggested the better thermal oxidation resistant protection way for C/C composite without the reduction of coated layer ' s properties.

2.1. Fabrication of C/C-SiC-ZrC composites
Carbon felts (porosity: 70 vol%, density: 0.45 g/cm 3 , Yixing Tiannian High Technology Co., Ltd., Jiangsu, China) were used as reinforcement of the composites, and the schematic illustration of the architecture was shown  in  Fig.  1.  The  zirconium  carbide-containing  polymer  (PZC, [(C4H8O)Zr(acac)2]n,  ceramic  yield  of  ~30%,  Institute  of  Chemistry, Chinese  Academy  of  Science,  Beijing,  China),  polycarbosilane  (PCS, ceramic  yield  of  ~60%,  Institute  of  Chemistry,  Chinese  Academy  of Science, Beijing, China) and liquid xylene (C8H10, carbon conversion of ~80%, Tianjin Tianli Chemistry Reagent Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China) were chosen as ZrC, SiC and pyrocarbon precursor. The PZC precursor was pyrolyzed  at  the  temperature  of  1500,  1800  and  2100 ◦ C  in  an  Ar flowing for 2 h, and ZrC particles with different sizes were obtained. After that, ZrC particles were treated with methylpentanol by ultrasonic dispersion for 5 h, and mixed with PCS and xylene uniformly to prepare a  mixture  solution  (xylene/PCS/ZrC  mass  ratio:  7:3:3).  The  CLVD equipment was shown in Ref. [16]. The carbon felts were immersed into the mixed solution and deposited at 950 ◦ C for 10 h, subsequently the composites with different ZrC particle sizes were obtained and marked as CZS1, CZS2 and CZS3.

1. Introduction
This study aims to provide a comprehensive investigation of oxide growth in ZrC across all relevant length scales, ranging from nanoscale and microscopic-level analysis via HRTEM to mesoscopic and macroscopic scales, complemented by the development of a robust phenomenological model which is capable of capturing the oxide scale thickness evolution. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Material fabrication Zirconium carbide (ZrC) powder (purity: 99.5 %, particle size < 45 µm, Goodfellow, UK) was used as the sole starting material. The powder was placed into a 30 mm diameter graphite die inside a glovebox to minimize oxygen contamination, then transferred to a spark plasma sintering (SPS) furnace (FCT Systeme GmbH, Germany) and sintered under vacuum. A two-step sintering process was carried out: 1500 °C for 120 s followed by 2000 °C for 600 s under 80 MPa uniaxial pressure, with a heating rate of 3.3 °C/s. The initial 1500 °C hold was intended to minimize contamination prior to high-temperature sintering. After sintering, the sample was cooled to room temperature with a cooling rate of 0.4 °C/s and surface graphite was removed using a grinder-polisher (MetaServ 250, Buehler, USA). The resulting ZrC disk had a relative density of 98.7 ± 0.1% calculated by the Archimedes method. The ZrC disk was polished with progressively finer grit papers, culminating in a mirrorlike surface using a 1 µm polishing cloth. Lastly, the ceramic disk was machined into rectangular cuboids with dimensions 6 × 6 × 3 mm 3 using a precision cutting machine (Secotom-60, Struers, Denmark) equipped with a diamond cut-off wheel (M1D20). A total of 24 ZrC cuboids of the same dimensions were prepared for subsequent isothermal static oxidation tests. Journal Pre-proof

3. Results and discussion
Fig. 4 shows the cross-sectional images of FE-SEM and EDS analysis corresponding each process condition from Cases A to G. Cases A and B show the specimens coated with ZrC-49 powder, which presents the severely damaged surfaces due to the impact of high velocity un-melted large particles during the coating process. In Case A, as seen in Fig. 4(a), the underlying C/C composite was exposed and damaged. The reduced damages in Case B [Fig. 4(b)] can be attributed to the increased power and addition of He gas, resulting in decrease of un-melted powder in the plasma flame. However, since the plasma condition might not be sufficient to melt the whole injected particles, the surface damages were still observed  and  the  thin  ZrC  layer  was  partly  coated.  To  mitigate  the damages and to increase coating thickness, a relatively small powder (ZrC-42) was introduced with different current and pressure conditions as described as Cases C, D and E in Table 1. In Case C, it was confirmed that  the  SiC  layer  was  partially  damaged,  while  the  underlying  C/C composite was found to be relatively intact. However, as seen in Fig. 4 (c),  it  was  hard  to  increase  the  ZrC  layer  coating  thickness  only  by reducing the powder size under the same processing conditions. To increase the contact time of particles with the plasma, the gas flow rate was reduced but the chamber pressure was increased for Cases D and E. Relatively thick ZrC layers of 12.5 μ m (Case D) and 25 μ m (Case E) were obtained, which indicated that not only the smaller size of powder but also their sufficient residence time in the plasma were crucial to ensure complete  melting  and  to  form  a  thick  coating  layer.  Therefore,  the coating processes under high power (54 kW) and high pressure (150 mbar) conditions was performed with smaller-sized powders, ZrC-33 (Case  F)  and  ZrC-10  (Case  G). 4. Summary
The size of raw powders introduced for vacuum plasma spray coating is one of important parameters for the quality of coated layers. In order to study the powder size effects, ZrC layers with various powder sizes were coated and compared. When a large-sized powder (D50 = 49 μ m) was employed, it was found the substrate damages due to un-melted powders. The large-sized powders were well melted to form droplets in the plasma by increasing power and helium gas addition. On the other hand, it was also shown that the smaller powders (D50 = 33.85 μ m) was beneficial  to  coat  thick  ZrC  layers.  On  the  other  hand,  the  coating thickness rather decreased by use of smallest powder (D50 = 10.45 μ m)

question: What is the melting point temperature of zirconium carbide (ZrC) in degrees Celsius as stated in the Kim et al. 2024 paper on UHTC coatings?
answer: The melting point temperature of zirconium carbide (ZrC) as stated in the Kim et al. 2024 paper on UHTC coatings is 3530 ◦ C [1].

1. Introduction
To protect C/C composite in extremely high-temperature conditions, the protective coating layers having heat-resistant and anti-oxidative properties  are  required.  Among  the  protective  materials,  ultra-high temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  particularly  noteworthy  due  to their extremely high melting temperatures around 3000 ◦ C or higher, exceptional hardness, as well as excellent chemical stability and strength under  high-temperature  conditions  [5,6].  Among  UHTCs,  zirconium carbide (ZrC) has a remarkably high melting point of 3530 ◦ C [7,8]. Additionally, its oxide, ZrO2, also has a high melting point of 2700 ◦ C [9].  Furthermore,  ZrC  exhibits  excellent  resistance  to  corrosion  and possesses a density of 6.73 g/cm 3 [10], which is notably lower compared to other UHTCs such as HfC (12.7 g/cm 3 ) [11], TaC (14.5 g/cm 3 ) [12], and WC (15.8 g/cm 3 ) [13].
To coat ZrC layer on the C/C composite, it is crucial to utilize the corresponding ZrC powders in the VPS method. Consequently, it becomes inevitable to consider the effects of the particle size of the raw powders on the quality of the coating layer. For example, when the particle size increases, it is generally anticipated to achieve a higher coating rate but there is a possibility of surface damage due to the impact of un-melted powders. Conversely, smaller particle sizes contribute a

1. Introduction
Zirconium Carbide (ZrC) Ultra-High Temperature Ceramic (UHTC) possesses  excellent  mechanical  and  thermal  properties  such  as  high hardness (25 GPa) and high congruent melting point up to 3700 K due to the mixed ionic and covalent bonding. In addition, ZrC has a lower cost in comparison with other UHTCs. These properties render ZrC a promising  material  for  future  extreme  environment  applications  [1 -3]. However, its  susceptibility  to  oxidation  at  elevated  temperatures  for instance above 500 ◦ C [4] remains a major issue. The oxidation response can compromise the structural integrity of ZrC-based materials during high-temperature applications involving air exposure. Consequently, it is essential to evaluate the potential oxidation damage to ZrC UHTC.
The oxidation reaction of ZrC is a complicated heterogenous reaction and its kinetics can be affected by factors such as temperature, oxygen pressure and exposed duration. Additionally, the oxidation kinetics of ZrC can also be affected by sample properties, which include purity, relative density, grain size, surface roughness, surface area, and C/Zr stoichiometry [5,6], stemming from different preparation methods. ZrC in different forms, for example single crystal [7,8], powder [9,10], and sintered pellets [11,12], can also affect the oxidation response. Due to

1 INTRODUCTION
Zirconium carbide (ZrC) is one of the refractory metal monocarbides that are part of the family of ultra high temperature ceramics, UHTCs. Whilst it has the rock salt crystal structure, Fm3m space group, it is typically carbon deficient 13 with x in ZrC x taking values ranging from 0.63 to 0.98. 19,20 This affects both the ceramic's sinterability and its mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties as well as its radiation resistance. 21 Whilst stoichiometric or near stoichiometric compounds are always preferable due to their superior properties, 22 they are rarely achieved by conventional solid-solid reaction synthesis methods. Nevertheless, ZrC offers a high melting point ( ∼ 3530 ◦ C) and hardness ( ∼ 25 GPa) as well as high electrical and thermal conductivity due to the presence of a combination of metallic (Zr-Zr) and covalent (Zr-C) bonds. It also provides high load-bearing capabilities and excellent ablation and corrosion resistance against molten metals and slags. 8,9,13

1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), integrated primarily by carbides, borides and some nitrides of early transition metals, are ultrarefractory materials that exhibit exceptional thermo-chemical stability and good mechanical strength even at high temperatures, which make them highly suitable for extreme environments [1 -3]. UHTCs find practical applications in aerospace [4], nuclear power, and hypersonic vehicles [5], where their excellent thermal and mechanical properties are convenient for the production of thermal shields, furnace linings, and structural components [6,7].
ZrB2 is the most studied UHTC, with a melting point of 3250 ◦ C [1], high strength and rigidity up to 2000 ◦ C [8], high hardness, remarkable chemical stability, and outstanding electrical and thermal conductivity [9 -13]. Despite its exceptional properties, pure ZrB2 faces several limitations for its practical industrial use. Due to its high melting point, it requires sintering temperatures ranging from 2100 to 2300 ◦ C, typically resulting in coarse microstructures with some residual porosity [14]. Additionally, pure ZrB2 exhibits brittle fracture and suffers from rapid oxidation at temperatures above 1127 ◦ C [15]. These weaknesses hinder its widespread adoption in high-temperature applications.

A B S T R A C T
Among the UHTCs, borides and carbides based on Zirconium (Zr) and Hafnium (Hf) stand out as particularly suitable choices. This preference stems from the exceptionally high melting points of their oxide by-products, which contribute significantly to maintaining of structural integrity under extreme conditions. Structurally, borides and carbides of zirconium (Zr) and hafnium (Hf) exhibit a mix of covalent, ionic, and metallic bonding [8]. Among these categories of ceramics, the borides are preferred in comparison to carbides due to their superior thermal conductivity, which is linked to the varying degrees of metallic bond characteristics present in the materials [5]. For instance, the thermal conductivity of fully dense ZrB2 at room temperature is approximately 90 W/m/K [8], while that of ZrC is less by ~78 % (20 W/m/K) [9]. The UHTCs having high thermal conductivity are preferred for the sharp leading-edge components of hypersonic vehicles, because they quickly conduct heat from the narrow edge to the wider area of the component, allowing the heat to be released through radiation. High thermal

conductivity also enhances the thermal shock resistance of UHTC composites [1].
Among the borides, the ZrB2 is particularly favoured due to its lower density, further enhancing its suitability for demanding applications [10 -12]. Additionally, the incorporation of up to 20 vol % SiC in the ZrB2 matrix not only increases its strength and fracture toughness, but also enhances its resistance to both oxidation and ablation [13,14].

2. Experimental procedure
The ceramics were produced by reactive hot-pressing at 1100 -1830 °C and 30 MPa (the load applied before the powder heating) in a graphite die in argon. The die was heated with an AC (50 Hz) current in a DCS-1 hot-press produced by SRC Synthesis (Kiev, Ukraine)
and the University of Hudders /uniFB01 eld. Charge pre-heating was done in an actively pumped vacuum better than 10 -2 Pa. The heating rate (after pre-heating at 500 °C for 10 min) was approximately 100 °C/min. Argon was introduced at this stage in the sintering chamber up to atmospheric pressure. Initially, a set of ceramics was produced at di /uniFB00 erent maximum sintering temperatures to investigate the material densi /uniFB01 cation. The isothermal dwelling time is presented in Table 1.
The sintered ceramics were in the form of 10 mm discs. Fully densi /uniFB01 ed Z6 and ZN discs had a thickness around 4.2 mm.
The bulk density of the sintered ceramics was measured using the Archimedes method and the theoretical densities were estimated according to the rule of mixtures based on the right-hand side of reaction (3) (for Z5 and Z6) and taking into account the CNT content (for ZN).
The crystalline phases were identi /uniFB01 ed by X-Ray Di /uniFB00 raction (XRD) and the identi /uniFB01 cation was supported by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM). For the SEM analysis, ceramic samples were cleaved, allowing to avoid polishing artefacts in a composite with phases with a high hardness di /uniFB00 erence. Vickers hardness measurements were performed with a load of 9.8 N for 20 s on polished surfaces (20 indents per sample). The fracture toughness was estimated by measuring the crack lengths generated by the Vickers indentations with a load of 49 N (10 indents per sample). The indentation toughness was calculated according to the formula of Evans and Charles [30].

coating  layer  was  rapidly  increased,  conversely.  Notably,  the  Case  F exhibited the highest thickness (50 μ m) and very smooth roughness (16 μ m) of ZrC coating layer, which was attributed to the well formation of splats on the surface by increased droplets of melted powders due to the use of smaller powder size (D50 = 33.85 μ m), higher pressure and higher power conditions. In Case G, the coated surface was extremely flat (Sa = 3 μ m) because the smallest particles were completely melted and coated as  the  form  of  splats  even  though  the  thickness  of  ZrC  layer  was decreased due to lower feeding rate and reduction of some powders by vaporization in the plasma.
From the result, it was found that the formation of powder droplets via  complete  melting  was  crucial  for  a  smooth  and  thick  ZrC  layer coating on the rough surface of C/C composite [14,15]. The complete melting of powders could be achieved with the use of smaller powder and with the sufficient contact time of particles in the plasma, which was related to the velocity and residence time of the particles in the plasma. Fig. 7(a) presents a particle image captured for a duration of 5 μ s just before the collision of ZrC powder with the substrate during the VPS process. Given the large number of images obtained using a high-speed camera,  image  processing  was  conducted  using  MATLAB  [16]  to differentiate  particles  within  the  image,  as  depicted  in  Fig.  7(b). Following the completion of image processing, the velocity of ZrC particles was calculated using the acquired data.
Fig. 7(c) displays the measured particle velocity and calculated gas residence time in chamber [17]. Gas residence time means the time the process gas stays in the chamber considering filling the entire chamber and is calculated using the chamber pressure, volume, and total input gas flow. In Case A, as seen in Fig. 4(a), the underlying C/C composite was exposed and damaged. In this work, therefore, we propose a multi-step ZrC coating process on C/C composites to form a homogeneous ZrC multi-layer protecting the  base  material  under  high-temperature  oxidation  conditions.  The structural properties of the ZrC layers formed through multi-step coatings  were  investigated  using  field  emission  scanning  electron  microscopy (FE-SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), a Vickers hardness tester, and a  surface  roughness tester.  The  performance  of  the  ZrC  layers  under high-temperature  oxidation  conditions  was  evaluated  using  a  highvelocity  oxygen  fuel  (HVOF)  facility. In Case G, the coated surface was extremely flat (Sa = 3 μ m) because the smallest particles were completely melted and coated as  the  form  of  splats  even  though  the  thickness  of  ZrC  layer  was decreased due to lower feeding rate and reduction of some powders by vaporization in the plasma.
From the result, it was found that the formation of powder droplets via  complete  melting  was  crucial  for  a  smooth  and  thick  ZrC  layer coating on the rough surface of C/C composite [14,15]. The complete melting of powders could be achieved with the use of smaller powder and with the sufficient contact time of particles in the plasma, which was related to the velocity and residence time of the particles in the plasma. Fig. 7(a) presents a particle image captured for a duration of 5 μ s just before the collision of ZrC powder with the substrate during the VPS process. Given the large number of images obtained using a high-speed camera,  image  processing  was  conducted  using  MATLAB  [16]  to differentiate  particles  within  the  image,  as  depicted  in  Fig.  7(b). Following the completion of image processing, the velocity of ZrC particles was calculated using the acquired data.
Fig. 7(c) displays the measured particle velocity and calculated gas residence time in chamber [17]. Gas residence time means the time the process gas stays in the chamber considering filling the entire chamber and is calculated using the chamber pressure, volume, and total input gas flow. The particles velocity tends to decrease as the gas residence time  increases,  which  means  that  although  the  particle  flight  time ( = spray distance/calculated particle speed) is very short, less than 1 ms, H.S. 2.2. Vacuum plasma spray coating process
The VPS system mainly consists of a plasma spray gun (F4VB, Metco) mounted on a 6-axis robot (IRB1600, ABB) in the cylindrical vacuum chamber (diameter = 1.8 m, length = 2.35 m) as illustrated in Fig. 3(a). The  plasma  with  a  DC  power  (Max = 55  kW)  is  produced  within  a pressure  range  of  30  mbar -900 mbar by introducing argon gas. The plasma gas flows between the electrode and nozzle inside the spray gun, and raw powders are fed into the plasma with the argon carrier gas from a powder feeder. Fig. 3(b) shows the path of the robot during the coating process,  and  the  coating  process  is  carried  out  along  this  path  in  a H.S. Kim et al.
Fig. 3. (a) Schematic illustration of vacuum plasma spray coating system and (b) a path of one cycle to coat whole substrate surface.
reciprocating manner. During the coating process, the spray distance between the spray gun to the substrate is about 300 mm. Table 1 presents the detailed plasma operating conditions such as current, power, injecting  gases  and  chamber  pressure  for  different  cases  with  four different sized powders.

1. Introduction
Recently, carbon-carbon (C/C) composites have been widely employed in various application fields due to their exceptional properties, but their oxidation at relatively low temperature around 500 ◦ C poses a challenge to be used in high-temperature conditions. To address the drawback, the coating of ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) layer on the C/C composites has been suggested as one of promising solutions. However, the quality of UHTC coating layers obtained through vacuum plasma spraying (VPS) is heavily influenced by the size of the introducing raw powders to coat the layers. Therefore, we investigated the size effects of ZrC powders in the range of (10 -50) μ m, one of UHTCs, to optimize the coating quality. By modulating process conditions, the coating quality with thick ZrC layer having lower porosity was obtained when using the powder around 34 μ m. From the results, we found the gas residence time related to the particle velocity in the plasma flame is one of key parameters to be modulated by VPS operational parameters, which could improve the quality of the coating layer even for large powder cases.

3.2. Mechanical properties
Table 1 shows the nanoindentation results and it is also compared with  literature  [31,36 -39].  The  nano-indentation  hardness  of  16.81, 15.23 and 18.62 GPa was measured for ZS-2.5Ta, ZS-5Ta and ZS-10Ta samples, respectively. Additionally, the bulk hardness of ZrB2  composites was measured using the Vickers hardness and almost similar trend and hardness of 16.62, 15.59 and 17.75 GPa was measured for ZS-2.5Ta, ZS-5Ta and ZS-10Ta samples, respectively. The bulk hardness of samples was measured by Vickers hardness equipment (M/s. Shimadzu, HMV, Japan) by applying a load of 19.6 N for 15 s. It indicates that both the nanoindentation results and Vickers hardness values are more or less the same. The hardness values were relatively high for ZS-10Ta composition due to core-rim structure and presence of (Zr,Ta)C phase. On the other hand, ZS-5Ta composition achieved lower hardness due to the presence of  ZrO2 phase.  Dorner  et  al.  [31]  also  observed  that  ZrB2-3at.%  Ta composite achieved lower hardness due to the presence of ZrO2, which is having  lower  hardness  of  12  GPa.  Interestingly,  the  ZrB2-6at.%  Ta exhibited higher hardness than ZrB2-3at.% Ta. From Table 1, it can be realized that the hardness of ZrB2 -SiC slightly lowered (from 16.81 to 15.23 GPa) with 5%Ta and the hardness increased to 18.62 GPa with further addition of 10%Ta and it can be attributed to the absence of ZrO2 phase and the presence of (Zr,Ta)C phase. In case of Ni (upto 8 mol%) added  ZrB2-25mol%  SiC,  there  was  tendency  of  slight  decrease  in hardness [37].

A B S T R A C T
ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composite was fully densified with the addition of tantalum (from 2.5 to 10 wt%) using multi stage spark plasma sintering (MSSPS). The microstructural phases of core-ZrB2  and rim-(Zr,Ta)B2  along with ZrO2,  (Zr,Ta)C  phases  were  observed  with  the  addition  of  tantalum  refractory  metal  to  ZrB2 -SiC.  Both  the nanoindentation and Vickers hardness of ZrB2  composites exhibited similar trend and almost same hardness values.  However,  maximum  nanoindentation  hardness  (18.62  GPa)  and  elastic  modulus  (451.60  GPa)  were measured for ZrB2-20 vol% SiC with 10 wt% Ta. The increase in amount of tantalum reduces the specific weight gain and oxide layer thickness, hence ZrB2-20 vol% SiC with 10 wt% Ta exhibited better oxidation resistance compared to other compositions after oxidation at 1600 ◦ C for 10 h. In all the compositions, the cross-sectional microstructure of oxidation tested samples consists of three different layers such as top passive silica layer, intermediate SiC-depleted layer and unreacted bulk.

It  was  observed  that  the addition of Ta lowered the elastic modulus of ZrB2 [31]. In case of ZrB2 composites (without addition of any transition metal), the hardness and elastic modulus varied in the range of 14.73 -20.2 GPa and 295 -373 GPa, respectively. However, the transition metal added ZrB2 -SiC composites reportedly exhibited hardness between 18.6 and 19.4 GPa and elastic modulus between 440 and 453 GPa. It infers that addition of additives significantly affecting the mechanical properties of ZrB2.

A B S T R A C T
ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composite was fully densified with the addition of tantalum (from 2.5 to 10 wt%) using multi stage spark plasma sintering (MSSPS). The microstructural phases of core-ZrB2  and rim-(Zr,Ta)B2  along with ZrO2,  (Zr,Ta)C  phases  were  observed  with  the  addition  of  tantalum  refractory  metal  to  ZrB2 -SiC.  Both  the nanoindentation and Vickers hardness of ZrB2  composites exhibited similar trend and almost same hardness values.  However,  maximum  nanoindentation  hardness  (18.62  GPa)  and  elastic  modulus  (451.60  GPa)  were measured for ZrB2-20 vol% SiC with 10 wt% Ta. The increase in amount of tantalum reduces the specific weight gain and oxide layer thickness, hence ZrB2-20 vol% SiC with 10 wt% Ta exhibited better oxidation resistance compared to other compositions after oxidation at 1600 ◦ C for 10 h. In all the compositions, the cross-sectional microstructure of oxidation tested samples consists of three different layers such as top passive silica layer, intermediate SiC-depleted layer and unreacted bulk.

In particular,  the  ZS-10Ta  composite  exhibited  maximum  hardness  and elastic  modulus value. From Table 1, phase pure ZrB2  reported with maximum hardness (30.2 GPa) and elastic modulus (582 GPa) and is attributed  to  its  fine  grain  structure  [39].  It  was  observed  that  the addition of Ta lowered the elastic modulus of ZrB2 [31]. In case of ZrB2 composites (without addition of any transition metal), the hardness and elastic modulus varied in the range of 14.73 -20.2 GPa and 295 -373 GPa, respectively. However, the transition metal added ZrB2 -SiC composites reportedly exhibited hardness between 18.6 and 19.4 GPa and elastic modulus between 440 and 453 GPa. The  TaZr2.75O8 phase  formed  during  the  oxidation  of  tantalum containing  ZrB2  ceramics.  It  has  been  already  proved  that  these  reactions were thermodynamically feasible at oxidation temperatures of 1500 ◦ C and above [19,33,34]. Fig. 3(b) shows the variation of specific weight gain and oxide layer thickness as a function of tantalum amount in ZrB2 -SiC composite. The specific weight gain and oxide layer thickness was lowered with increasing Ta amount. The oxide layer thickness of  the  samples  was  calculated  by  using  cross-sectional  images  of oxidized samples. The specific weight gain reduced from 21.04 to 16.65 mg/cm 2 with increasing amount of tantalum. Similarly, the oxide layer thickness was reduced from 320 to 255 μ m with increasing amount of tantalum and this data is compared with the literature in Table 2. He et  al.  [35]  also  observed  weight  gain  and  oxide  layer  thickness  was reduced with increasing amount yttrium addition to ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composite [35]. As the amount of yttrium increased it forms protective silica layer by the formation Y2Si2O7 phase.

A B S T R A C T
ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composite was fully densified with the addition of tantalum (from 2.5 to 10 wt%) using multi stage spark plasma sintering (MSSPS). The microstructural phases of core-ZrB2  and rim-(Zr,Ta)B2  along with ZrO2,  (Zr,Ta)C  phases  were  observed  with  the  addition  of  tantalum  refractory  metal  to  ZrB2 -SiC.  Both  the nanoindentation and Vickers hardness of ZrB2  composites exhibited similar trend and almost same hardness values.  However,  maximum  nanoindentation  hardness  (18.62  GPa)  and  elastic  modulus  (451.60  GPa)  were measured for ZrB2-20 vol% SiC with 10 wt% Ta. The increase in amount of tantalum reduces the specific weight gain and oxide layer thickness, hence ZrB2-20 vol% SiC with 10 wt% Ta exhibited better oxidation resistance compared to other compositions after oxidation at 1600 ◦ C for 10 h. In all the compositions, the cross-sectional microstructure of oxidation tested samples consists of three different layers such as top passive silica layer, intermediate SiC-depleted layer and unreacted bulk.

sintering techniques that has been tried to improve the densification of ZrB2  [21 -24]. In general, the sintering temperature achieved in single step  SPS,  which  causes  to  develop  temperature  gradients  across  the cross-section of sample and deteriorates the mechanical properties [25, 26]. To control these problems multi step (MS)-SPS is suggested for the densification, in which sample is held at intermediate temperatures for certain duration of time before achieving the final sintering temperature.  The  MS-SPS  processed  samples  shows  uniform  microstructure, homogeneous densification without exaggerated grain growth, which leads to improvement in the mechanical properties [21,22,27,28]. In view of the high temperature applications of ZrB2, understanding its oxidation bevaviour is very important [1]. In particular, additives such as SiC and other silicon based ceramics are added to ZrB2 or other high temperature  borides  with  a  view  to  improve  its  oxidation  resistance [29 -43].
In  this  work,  tantalum  metallic  element  was  added  with  varying amounts from 2.5 to 10 wt% to ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composite. The samples were densified using MS-SPS at 1900 ◦ C for holding time of 3 min. The hardness and elastic modulus of ZrB2  composites were evaluated using  nano-indentation  test  using  Oliver  Pharr  method  [44]. question: What was the density of the Xu et al. 2021 ZF-ZS composite sample prepared by reactive infiltration of K2ZrF6 molten salt?
answer: The density of the Xu et al. 2021 ZF-ZS composite sample prepared by reactive infiltration of K2ZrF6 molten salt was 2.75 ± 0.05 g/cm³ [2].

chunks.text:
2.1. Materials preparation
To reduce costs, Zr and Si powders with a particle size of approximately 74 μ m were used. The Zr -Si powder (Zr powder: 99.9 % pure, Beijing Xing Rong Yuan Technology Co., Ltd., China; Si powder: 99.7 % pure, Changsha Tian Jiu Metal Material Co., Ltd., China) prepared with a mass ratio of 3:2 was first mixed with ethanol for 24 h and then dried. The other powders were mixed according to the information given in Table 1. For example, ZF -ZS powder comprised dried Zr -Si  powder, NaCl, and K2ZrF6 at a mass ratio of 8:1:8 (NaCl powder, AR; K2ZrF6 and K2TiF6: 98 % purity, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.). NaCl was utilised as the molten salt medium to reduce the melting point of the composite  molten  salt  [20].  Zr -Si -salt  powder  was  mixed  without ethanol for 24 h. The infiltration powders were subsequently poured onto the surfaces of the C/C composites and heated to 1800 -1900 ◦ C at a rate of 10 ◦ C/min and allowed to maintain at its final temperature for 2 h.  The  residual  salt  was  removed  by  pumping  for  5  min  to  200  Pa pressure; then, argon was flushed into the micro positive pressure at a rate of 20 ◦ C/min and finally cooled to 25 -30 ◦ C using the furnace. ZF -S and TF -S were used to verify that high-valence molten salts can react to form UHTCs. In addition, in order to verify that the molten salt ion melt can infiltrate the C/C matrix at low temperatures ( < 1400 ◦ C), K2ZrF6 -Si and K2TiF6 -Si with an Me 4 + :Si atomic ratio of 1:1 were infiltrated into the C/C matrix, respectively, and heated up to 1200 ◦ C at 10 ◦ C/min for 4 h and cooled to room temperature.

3.3. Ablation properties
Fig. 8 shows the macromorphology of the four samples before and after ablation. The ZF-S and ZF-ZS ablation centres reveal a spatter of white solidified droplets and relatively flat molten oxide scale, respectively. Meanwhile, there are notable macroscopic dents in the ablation centres of TF-S and TF-ZS, and solidified liquid droplets in their edges.
The inner cone diameter of the oxyacetylene welding gun is only 5 mm, which is considerably smaller than the surface area of the tested sample. Therefore, the linear and mass ablation rates of the material could reflect the ablation resistance of the ablation centre and the entire sample, respectively [34]. Based on Table 2, ZF -ZS has the lowest mass and linear ablation rates, i.e. 2.6 ± 0.02 mg/s and 0.82 ± 0.04 μ m/s, respectively. Compared with ZF -S, the mass and linear ablation rates of ZF -ZS  decreased  by  43.5  %  and  50.8  %,  respectively.  The  ablation performance has the order of ZF -ZS > ZF -S > TF -ZS > TF -S. This indicates  that  a  higher  ZrC  content  in  the  composites  results  in  better ablative properties. However, the opposite is true for the TiC content. It has been reported that the poor ablation performance of pure C/C -TiC or C/C -SiC composites can be attributed to the lower melting points of SiO2  and TiO2  [29]. The molten salt infiltration of ZF -S can reach an average  level  of  ablation  resistance  in  the  C/C -ZrC -SiC  composites prepared by traditional RMI [35,36]. ZF -ZS can maintain a low ablation rate under severe ablation conditions despite several defects, such as pores and cavities. Thus, the actual ablation resistance could still be improved.

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high temperature ceramic-modified C/C composites (C/C-UHTCs) were prepared by the reactive infiltration of K2MeF6 (Me = Zr, Ti) mixed with Si and Zr-Si powders. Molten salt infiltration can be divided into two stages: salt ion melt and Me-Si alloy melt. In the temperature range below 1400 ◦ C, Zr and Si dissolve in the molten salt, are carried by the ion melt, and precipitate at the PyC interface to form carbides. Above 1400 ◦ C, a large amount of molten salt volatilises and thermally decomposes. The Me-Si alloy forms a melt and infiltrates the C/C matrix, and finally forms C/C-ZrC-SiC, C/C-Ti3SiC2-SiC, and C/C-ZrC-TiC-SiC composites. The C/C-ZrCSiC composite with the highest ZrC content exhibited the lowest mass rate (2.6 ± 0.02 mg/s) and linear ablation rate (0.82 ± 0.04 μ m/s), which were reduced by 43.5 and 50.8 %, respectively, compared to the unmodified C/CZrC-SiC composite.
silicon alloy melt was used as the carrier fluid for ultra-high temperature metal components that can reduce the infiltration temperature and increase  the  wettability  between  the  metal  and  carbon  [13,15,16]. However, silicides from the incomplete reaction may easily persist in the matrix, and melt, overflow, or damage the structure during high-temperature applications [2,14,17].

3.2. Infiltration model of the molten salt
Fig. 7 shows a schematic of the low-temperature molten salt infiltration mechanism. When the temperature reaches the melting point of the molten salt, K2ZrF6(s) and NaCl(s) form an ionic melt, and then enter the pores of C/C matrix under the action of capillary forces (Eqs. 3,4). The  metal  grains  dissolve  into  fluorinated  ions,  which  can  then  be transported by ion diffusion through the porous medium (Fig. 7a). A heterogeneous reaction may occur near the carbon surface, leading to the deposition of ZrC and SiC. The by-products are fluoride ions and dissolved fluorine. The reaction loop ends with the return of dissolved fluorine and fluoride towards the surface, where they can react with the grains. The loop is valid even without Zr feeding at the surface; Zr can be produced by the reaction of the salt ions and Si (Eq. 6), and it can be redissolved by the emerging fluorine to form ZrF6 2 GLYPH<0> ions (Fig. 7b). A redox reaction occurs between the ZrF6 2 GLYPH<0> /Zr ◦ and SiF6 2 GLYPH<0> /Si ◦ couples. Here, Si appears to be a more reducing agent than Zr. Consequently, SiF6 2 GLYPH<0> yields back SiF4 according to Eq. 5 [14,30,31].
When ZrC is already formed, the diffusion of C through ZrC provides the necessary feed of carbon atoms at the liquid/ZrC interface. Therefore, when the reaction temperature is lower than 1300 ◦ C, the main carrier fluid is molten salt ion melt.
<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
Fig. 7. Schematic diagram of infiltration mechanism of K2ZrF6-Si-NaCl system at low-temperature molten salt infiltration stage: (a) Dissolution of Zr and Si particles in salt; (b) complete loop of molten salt reactions.

question: In Xu et al. 2021, what was the mass ablation rate of the ZF-ZS C/C-UHTC composite when tested under oxyacetylene flame at 2800 degrees Celsius surface temperature for 60 seconds?
answer: In Xu et al. 2021, the mass ablation rate of the ZF-ZS C/C-UHTC composite when tested under oxyacetylene flame at 2800 degrees Celsius surface temperature for 60 seconds was 2.6 ± 0.02 mg/s [4].
