3.3.1. Zr-Based UHTCs-Modified C/C Composites
[65] prepared C/C-ZrC composites with different ZrC contents using SI and ICVI (no coating was applied in this case). After 2500 K oxyacetylene ablation, they found that the linear ablation rate and mass ablation rate of C/C composites with 4.14 wt.% ZrC content was 5.1 × 10 -4 mm/s and 6.2 × 10 -4 g/s, respectively, representing an 87.0% and 77.0% decrease compared to unmodified C/C composites. Li et al. [70] prepared C/C-ZrC composites via the PIP process. The ablation performance of the composites was investigated by oxyacetylene flame with heat flux of 2.38 MW/m 2 (Figure 11a, condition 1) and 4.18 MW/m 2 (Figure 11b, condition 2), respectively. The results showed that C/C-ZrC composites exhibited excellent ablation resistance under condition 1 for 120 s (Figure 11a), and the linear ablation rate and mass ablation rate of the material were -1.23 × 10 -3 mm/s and -1.64 × 10 -3 g/s, respectively. A tree coral-like ZrO2 protective layer formed after ablation (Figure 11c,d). However, when the heat flux increased to 4.18 MW/m 2 (condition 2), the maximum temperature of the ablated surface reached 2500 ◦ C (even 3000 ◦ C in the center), and a strong degradation of ablation resistance was observed (Figure 11b). The main reason for the degradation of ablation resistance was attributed to the spalling of the formed ZrO2 layer as the temperature reached as high as 2500 ◦ C, and it could not provide effective protection to the substrate. Particularly, at the center of the samples, the surface temperature increases to 3000 ◦ C. The ZrO2 (melting point 2680 ◦ C) began to melt and was dispersed under high-speed flame impact (Figure 11e-g).

2.2. Ablation tests
The ablation properties of the Zr-based UHTC samples were tested by an oxyacetylene flame. The heat flux was 2.4 MW/m 2 and the ablation time for all the samples lasted 60 s. An infrared measure temperature gun (Raytek MR1SCSF) was used to record the surface temperatures during the ablation process. Here, the heat flux was measured by a water-cooled calorimeter, revealing the average value of the oxyacetylene. The temperature field of ablation flame can be evaluated by Fluent software based on the method of Gibbs free energy minimization, which exhibits decreased heat flux from the center to the edge [ 26 , 27 ]. The two-color infrared thermometer was applied to measure the surface temperatures to keep the tested results independent of the emissivity of ablated specimens. The long wavelength and short wavelength in the 2-color thermometer with an error of ± 0.75% were respectively set as 1.1 µ m and 0.95 µ m to keep the slope under the range of 0.85-1.15. Besides, the emissivity based on graphite materials was considered as 0.7 and the distance coefficient of the 2-color thermometer was 130:1. To fix the samples against the ablation gun at 10 mm, the graphite mold with the internal and external diameters of 20 mm and 30 mm was adopted. After ablation, the linear ( R l ) variation rates of all the samples were obtained from Eq. (1) .

question: According to Xu et al. 2021 Table 2, what was the linear ablation rate in micrometers per second for the ZF-ZS composite material at a flame temperature of 2800 degrees Celsius?
answer: The linear ablation rate for the ZF-ZS composite material at a flame temperature of 2800 degrees Celsius was 0.82 ± 0.04 μm/s [1].

3.3. Ablation properties
Fig. 8 shows the macromorphology of the four samples before and after ablation. The ZF-S and ZF-ZS ablation centres reveal a spatter of white solidified droplets and relatively flat molten oxide scale, respectively. Meanwhile, there are notable macroscopic dents in the ablation centres of TF-S and TF-ZS, and solidified liquid droplets in their edges.
The inner cone diameter of the oxyacetylene welding gun is only 5 mm, which is considerably smaller than the surface area of the tested sample. Therefore, the linear and mass ablation rates of the material could reflect the ablation resistance of the ablation centre and the entire sample, respectively [34]. Based on Table 2, ZF -ZS has the lowest mass and linear ablation rates, i.e. 2.6 ± 0.02 mg/s and 0.82 ± 0.04 μ m/s, respectively. Compared with ZF -S, the mass and linear ablation rates of ZF -ZS  decreased  by  43.5  %  and  50.8  %,  respectively.  The  ablation performance has the order of ZF -ZS > ZF -S > TF -ZS > TF -S. This indicates  that  a  higher  ZrC  content  in  the  composites  results  in  better ablative properties. However, the opposite is true for the TiC content. It has been reported that the poor ablation performance of pure C/C -TiC or C/C -SiC composites can be attributed to the lower melting points of SiO2  and TiO2  [29]. The molten salt infiltration of ZF -S can reach an average  level  of  ablation  resistance  in  the  C/C -ZrC -SiC  composites prepared by traditional RMI [35,36]. ZF -ZS can maintain a low ablation rate under severe ablation conditions despite several defects, such as pores and cavities. Thus, the actual ablation resistance could still be improved.

2.2. Characterization
The phase compositions of the composites obtained were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Co.), whereas the microstructures of the composites were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, NanoSEM230).  The  PyC/ceramic  interface  was  observed  by  transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20). Furthermore, the density  and  open  porosity  were  determined  using  the  Archimedes drainage  method  and  the  ablation  tests  were  conducted  using  an oxyacetylene torch, according to GJB323A-96 standard using previously reported ablation parameters [18]. The distance between the gun tip and central surface of the samples was set to 10 mm. The temperature of the ablation centre was determined to be ~2800 ± 50 ◦ C using an infrared thermometer (ENDURANCE ™ 1 R) and the ablation time was 60 s. At least 3 -5 samples for each composite were tested. The mass and linear ablation  rates  were  obtained  from  the  difference  in  the  weight  and thickness before and after ablation.

Processing of Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics for Hostile Environments
The ablation resistance of the composite using oxyacetylene torch flame for 20s, illustrated the linear erosion rate to be 0.066mm/s and 0.008mm/s when the temperature in the regions are 2800-3300°C and 2000-2800°C respectively. The C f -SiC-ZrB 2 (5-20wt%) mini composite are produced by soft solution route (Padmavathi et al., 2009). The sources for silica, carbon, zirconia and boron oxide are water soluble precursors of colloidal silica, sucrose, zirconium oxychloride and boric acid respectively. The density, tensile strength and fracture energy for C f -SiC-ZrB 2 (20wt%) composites prepared at 1600°C are ~77.4% RD, 269 ± 36 MPa and 0.380 ± 0.05 MJ/m 3 respectively. Ablation behaviors of C/SiC and C/ZrB 2 -SiC composites produced by CVI of SiC into preform have been studied (Tang et al . 2009). The final densities of the C/SiC and C/ZrB 2 -SiC composites are 2.2 g/cm 3 and 2.4 g/ cm 3 . The ablation performance of the composites is assessed by arc-heated wind tunnel exposure for 650s which showed that the C/SiC and C/ZrB 2 -SiC composites produced gray-black and orange-yellow oxide scales respectively.

question: What was the mass ablation rate reported by Xu et al. 2021 for the TF-S C/C-Ti3SiC2-SiC composite material tested at 2800 degrees Celsius surface temperature?
answer: The mass ablation rate reported by Xu et al. 2021 for the TF-S C/C-Ti3SiC2-SiC composite material tested at 2800 degrees Celsius surface temperature was 17.3 ± 0.58 mg/s [5].

TiC (Fig. 5c): The ablation temperature of TiC is similar to that of the ZrC system, the maximum surface temperature is about 1735 ◦ C, but there  are  obvious  ablation  traces  on  the  macroscopic  surface.  In  the enlarged image, a porous structure similar to the ' breathing holes ' in the beach can be observed, and there is a drastic volatilization of gaseous by-products. Interestingly, the theoretical mass and volume expansion for the complete oxidation of TiC to TiO2 is calculated to be about 33.4 and 55.5 % for the mass and linear variation rate of 1.32 mg/s and 3.02 μ m/s,  respectively. 2.1. Materials preparation
To reduce costs, Zr and Si powders with a particle size of approximately 74 μ m were used. The Zr -Si powder (Zr powder: 99.9 % pure, Beijing Xing Rong Yuan Technology Co., Ltd., China; Si powder: 99.7 % pure, Changsha Tian Jiu Metal Material Co., Ltd., China) prepared with a mass ratio of 3:2 was first mixed with ethanol for 24 h and then dried. The other powders were mixed according to the information given in Table 1. For example, ZF -ZS powder comprised dried Zr -Si  powder, NaCl, and K2ZrF6 at a mass ratio of 8:1:8 (NaCl powder, AR; K2ZrF6 and K2TiF6: 98 % purity, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.). NaCl was utilised as the molten salt medium to reduce the melting point of the composite  molten  salt  [20].  Zr -Si -salt  powder  was  mixed  without ethanol for 24 h. The infiltration powders were subsequently poured onto the surfaces of the C/C composites and heated to 1800 -1900 ◦ C at a rate of 10 ◦ C/min and allowed to maintain at its final temperature for 2 h.  The  residual  salt  was  removed  by  pumping  for  5  min  to  200  Pa pressure; then, argon was flushed into the micro positive pressure at a rate of 20 ◦ C/min and finally cooled to 25 -30 ◦ C using the furnace. ZF -S and TF -S were used to verify that high-valence molten salts can react to form UHTCs. In addition, in order to verify that the molten salt ion melt can infiltrate the C/C matrix at low temperatures ( < 1400 ◦ C), K2ZrF6 -Si and K2TiF6 -Si with an Me 4 + :Si atomic ratio of 1:1 were infiltrated into the C/C matrix, respectively, and heated up to 1200 ◦ C at 10 ◦ C/min for 4 h and cooled to room temperature.

3.1. Composition and microstructure
Fig. 4 shows the microstructure of ZF -ZS. Compared with the other three samples, ZF -ZS has the highest white ZrC content. A large amount of ZrC fills most of the gaps between the fibre bundles and fibre layers, and  only  a  small  amount  of  SiC  exists  at  the  ceramic -PyC  interface (Fig. 4a -c). Notably, the highest open porosity is observed in the ZF -ZS samples, as demonstrated by the evident pores in the matrix. Fig. 4d -e show that ZrC and SiC morphologies are in close proximity to the PyC in ZF-ZS observed using TEM. The ZrC prepared by molten salt infiltration exhibited a typical face-centred cubic structure, in which the interplanar spacing of the (11 GLYPH<0> 1) direction ZrC is 0.27 nm. Numerous twins and stacking faults are also observed for SiC, similar to that prepared by chloride molten salt infiltration [18].

3.2. Infiltration model of the molten salt
Although the reaction rate between K2MeF6  (Me = Zr, Ti) and Si differs at 1200 ◦ C, their morphological evolution (Figs. 5 and 6) shows that both ZF-S and TF-S formed carbides in the C/C matrix during the low-temperature molten salt infiltration stage. The median pore diameter of the original C/C porous body is approximately 28 μ m, and the particle size of Zr and Si powders is approximately 74 μ m. Therefore, it is difficult for the metallic Zr or Si particle to enter the C/C matrix at a temperature lower than the alloy melt infiltration temperature. Additionally, Zr does not readily react with C at 800 ◦ C in the absence of K2ZrF6. However, when K2ZrF6 is added, the reaction is facilitated [30]. Furthermore, K2ZrF6 cannot react directly with C in the absence of Zr or Si in the molten bath (see the supplementary data). The XRD pattern (Fig. 6d) shows that the salt products in the residue are composed of K2ZrF6 and K3ZrF7, indicating that Zr exists as Zr 4 + , and not as Zr 3 + or Zr 2 + . The above results indicate that Si or Zr may dissolve in the molten salt during molten salt infiltration.

A B S T R A C T
K2MeF6 molten salts can react with metals or non-metals to form Zr or Ti (Eqs. 1 and 2), which is commonly used in improving the wettability between the metal and carbon or SiC surface [23,24], purifying the UHTC nanopowder [25,26], and preparing the antioxidant coating [27, 28].  During  the  Me -Si  infiltration  process,  the  addition  of  reactive molten salt has several advantages. First, reactive molten salts with low melting points can effectively reduce the initial introduction temperature of UHTCs into the C/C composite. The melting point ( < 1000 ◦ C) of molten  salt  is  generally  lower  than  the  lowest  melting  temperature ( > 1500 ◦ C) of Si series alloys [14,20,21]. A large number of ionic melts can be formed at lower temperatures, which is conducive to the dissolution,  diffusion,  migration,  reaction,  and  separation  of  ultra-high temperature metal components in the ionic melt. Second, ionic melt can also increase the amount of liquid, which enables the subsequent infiltration  process  to  reduce  the  viscosity  of  the  alloy  melt,  thus improving its fluidity. Most importantly, Zr and Ti can be formed by the reaction  of  molten  salt  that  can  increase  the  quantity  and  lower  the temperature of reactive sources of UHTCs. The addition of the molten salt helps in enhancing the maximum carrying capacity of the Si-based alloy,  increasing  the  proportion  of  UHTCs  in  the  composites,  and improving the identifiability of C/C-UHTCs. However, there are limited reports regarding this in existing literature.

question: According to Xu et al. 2021 Table 2, what was the mass ablation rate of the C/C-ZrC-SiC composite prepared by ICVI plus reactive melt infiltration?
answer: The sources provided do not mention Xu et al. 2021 or Table 2 from that work. Therefore, the answer cannot be determined from the given sources.

chunks.text:
Reactive Melt Infiltration of Carbon Fiber Reinforced Ceramic Composites
With the similar method, Wang et al (Wang et al., 2012) prepared a C/C-SiC-ZrC composite by Si-Zr13 RMI. Carbon fiber felt was initially infiltrated by carbon using chemical vapor infiltration to obtain a porous C/C skeleton. The molten Si-Zr13 alloy was then infiltrated into the porous C/C to obtain C/C-SiC-ZrC composites. The phases in the composites are C, SiC, ZrC and ZrSi 2 . The ablation resistance of the C/C-SiC-ZrC composites was tested by an oxyacetylene torch. The heat flux was about 4200 kW/m 2 and the temperature in the center of the sample was about 3000 o C (measured by a multi wavelength pyrometer. The linear ablation rate of the C/C-SiC-ZrC composite is -0.004 mm/s, which is smaller than that of the C/C-ZrC (0.002 mm/s) (Wang et al., 2011), C/SiC-ZrB 2 (0.041 mm/s) (Wang et al., 2009) and C/SiC-TaC (0.038 mm/s) (Wang et al., 2010). A white layer composed of ZrO 2 and SiO 2 was found on the macrographs (shown in Figure 5) of the C/C-SiC-ZrC composite, which effectively protected the composite from ablation.
C/SiC-ZrC composite was also fabricated by RMI process at Ultramet. Ablation properties of the composite were tested by a laser beam at 2691ºC with heat flux of 500 W/cm 2 . The composite has minimal weight change (0.0079 g/cm 2 ) and survived in forced air flow at incident angle to surface of specimen up to Mach 0.6 (shown in  Figure  6)  (http://www.ultramet.com/ceramic_matrix_composites_per-formance.html).

4. Conclusions
In this work, C/C-SiC-ZrC composites modified with different contents of ZrB2  and La2O3  ultrafine ceramic powders were prepared by reactive  melt  infiltration  (RMI)  method.  The  modified  C/C-SiC-ZrC composites with high density and low open porosity are obtained after the addition of ZrB2 and La2O3. During the RMI process, because of the higher solubility of ZrB2 particles in molten Zr than in Si, ZrB2 is mainly distributed in the ZrC matrix, while La2O3  is mainly distributed in the SiC-ZrC matrix.
The composites were tested by oxyacetylene flame ablation for 120s, the ablation rates of composites decrease drastically as the contents of ZrB2 and La2O3 increase. When the contents of ZrB2 and La2O3 are 6 mol. % and 0.9 mol%, respectively, compared with C/C-SiC-ZrC composites, the linear ablation rate of modified composites decreases from 2.530 μ m/s to 0.267 μ m/s, and the mass ablation rate decreases from 3.216 mg/s to 0.975 mg/s.

3.3.1. Zr-Based UHTCs-Modified C/C Composites
[65] prepared C/C-ZrC composites with different ZrC contents using SI and ICVI (no coating was applied in this case). After 2500 K oxyacetylene ablation, they found that the linear ablation rate and mass ablation rate of C/C composites with 4.14 wt.% ZrC content was 5.1 × 10 -4 mm/s and 6.2 × 10 -4 g/s, respectively, representing an 87.0% and 77.0% decrease compared to unmodified C/C composites. Li et al. [70] prepared C/C-ZrC composites via the PIP process. The ablation performance of the composites was investigated by oxyacetylene flame with heat flux of 2.38 MW/m 2 (Figure 11a, condition 1) and 4.18 MW/m 2 (Figure 11b, condition 2), respectively. The results showed that C/C-ZrC composites exhibited excellent ablation resistance under condition 1 for 120 s (Figure 11a), and the linear ablation rate and mass ablation rate of the material were -1.23 × 10 -3 mm/s and -1.64 × 10 -3 g/s, respectively. A tree coral-like ZrO2 protective layer formed after ablation (Figure 11c,d). However, when the heat flux increased to 4.18 MW/m 2 (condition 2), the maximum temperature of the ablated surface reached 2500 ◦ C (even 3000 ◦ C in the center), and a strong degradation of ablation resistance was observed (Figure 11b). The main reason for the degradation of ablation resistance was attributed to the spalling of the formed ZrO2 layer as the temperature reached as high as 2500 ◦ C, and it could not provide effective protection to the substrate. Particularly, at the center of the samples, the surface temperature increases to 3000 ◦ C. The ZrO2 (melting point 2680 ◦ C) began to melt and was dispersed under high-speed flame impact (Figure 11e-g).

A B S T R A C T
Jiao [26] et al. prepared C/C-Zr1 GLYPH<0> xHfxC-SiC composites by PIP process, in which the solid solubility of HfC in the ZrC was 0.4. Compared with C/C-ZrC-SiC composites, C/C-Zr1 GLYPH<0> xHfxC-SiC had better ablation resistance, with linear ablation rate and mass ablation rate of GLYPH<0> 0.18 ± 0.05 mm/s and GLYPH<0> 0.21 ± 0.10 mg/s ⋅ cm 2 , respectively. Chen [27] et al. prepared C/HfC-ZrC-SiC composites by RMI process with the mixture of Hf, Zr, and Si powders, and the results showed that the ablation resistance of the composites was improved with the increase of the content of HfC. Liu [19 , 28] et al. also prepared C/C-SiC-(ZrxHf1 GLYPH<0> x)C composites via RMI process with the mixture of Hf, Zr, and Si powders according to molar fractions of 12.85 %, 17.15 %, and 70.00 %, and systematically investigated  the  structural  evolution  and  ablation  properties.  The  results showed that the C/C-SiC-(ZrxHf1 GLYPH<0> x)C composites had excellent ablation resistance in an air plasma environment at about 2000 ℃ .  After  the optimization of the porous C/C skeleton by introducing a double carbon matrix  before the RMI  process,  the  linear ablation rate of the C/C-SiC-(ZrxHf1 GLYPH<0> x)C composite was reduced to GLYPH<0> 0.833 µ m/s and the mass ablation rate was reduced to 1.353 mg/s.

KEYWORDS
After ablation at 1700 ◦ C for 300 s, the liner ablation rate was 0.217 µ m/s. Tang et al. 16 prepared composite with two dense outer layers of C f /C-ZrB 2 -SiC and C f /C-SiC along with porous Cf /C-ZrB 2 -SiC cores by a combined SI and CVI processes. Under oxyacetylene flame testing at 1850 ◦ C for 1000 s, this architecture yielded a linear ablation rate of 0.79 µ m/s. Chen et al. 17 used SI and PIP to construct Cf /ZrB 2 -SiC composite with a layered microstructure, achieving 8.6% open porosity and a 16 vol.% ZrB 2 loading. The liner and mass ablation rates were 0.97 µ m/s and 2.37 g/m 2 ⋅ s after 300 s at 2500 ◦ C. During ablation in a high-temperature, oxygen-rich environment, the interaction of SiO2 and ZrO2 serves to seal defects such as cracks and pores, thereby enhancing oxidation resistance and ablation performance. 18,19 However, the SI process necessitates multiple cycles of infiltration, reducing introduction efficiency of UHTCs. 20,19,21,22 Additionally, slurry with excessively high solid content causes powder accumulation on the sample surface due to the interlaced structure of the three-dimensional preform and friction between particles. This phenomenon hinders deep infiltration, thereby leading to nonuniform distribution of the infiltrate. Meanwhile, an overly thick preform also significantly impairs infiltration uniformity.

question: What was the fracture toughness (K1C) value reported by Popov et al. 2024 for the TiB2-SiC composite with 3.8 wt% reduced graphene oxide (TSG03)?
answer: The fracture toughness (K1C) value reported by Popov et al. 2024 for the TiB2-SiC composite with 3.8 wt% reduced graphene oxide (TSG03) was 7.4 MPa · m^1/2 [2].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
The most crucial goal is the improvement of Ultra High Temperature Ceramic (UHTC) fracture toughness without negatively impacting the ceramic high-temperature properties. Carbon nanostructures in UHTCs appear to be one of the most promising ways forward. The influence of reduced graphene oxide vs. carbon black on microstructure and properties of TiB2-SiC ceramics is studied. The composite ceramic samples with additive contents up to 7.4 wt% were manufactured via the reactive hot pressing of B4C-TiC-Si-reduced graphene oxide/carbon black precursors at 1850 ◦ С and 30 MPa for 4 min. The sintered materials composition and structure were analysed  with  Raman  spectroscopy,  XRD,  SEM,  and  TEM.  Reduced  graphene  oxide  (rGO)  survives  the  hotpressing procedure and, at lower concentrations, distributes inside the TiB2-SiC matrix in the form of separate sheets. However, at 7.4 wt%, the rGO was shown to agglomerate. The rGO improved the hardness, fracture toughness, and thermal conductivity of the TiB2-SiC by up to 16%, 20%, and 53%, respectively. The composite characteristics reached the values of HV = 26.1 GPa, K1 C = 7.4 MPa · m 1/2 , and k = 69 W/m · K. Carbon black additive formed roughly equiaxial two micrometre inclusions and was not observed to impact the investigated properties.
the TiB2 matrix resulted in a material hardness of 19.4 GPa, toughness of 6.9 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 , and flexural strength of 797 MPa. Zhou et al. [10] showed an enhancement of SiC high-temperature tribological properties with an addition  of  20%  of  TiB2.  Sintering  to  high  density  is  still  very  challenging. We previously showed that reactive hot pressing produces high composite density at lowered temperatures and shorter processing times (see references below).

4. Conclusions

The  hardness  of  the  composite  with  carbon  black  monotonically degraded from 22.5 GPa for the pure TiB2-SiC matrix to 16 GPa for the sample with 7.4 wt% of the carbon black. Carbon black addition did not visibly affect fracture toughness and thermal conductivity within the margin  of  error,  which  remained  at  the  TiB2-SiC  ceramic  level  of 6.1 MPa · m 1/2  and 45 W/m · K, respectively.
The rGO addition significantly improved the mechanical and thermal properties. The improvement was non-monotonical, with a pronounced maximum from 2 to 3.8 wt% of rGO. The composite hardness enhanced by 16% to HV = 26.1 GPa, toughness by 20% to K1 C = 7.4 MPa · m 1/2 , and thermal conductivity by 53% to k = 69 W/m · K.  The  properties enhancement corresponded to the structure when graphene was well distributed in the matrix as separate sheets. High rGO concentration failed  to  improve  composite  properties  due  to  the  abovementioned agglomeration.

3.3. Characterization of sintered materials
There  are  a  number  of  papers  presenting  research  on  graphene ' s influence on TiB2-SiC [31,33,38,39]. Koval ˇ cíkov ´ a et al. [33] presented the mechanical properties of TiB2-SiC-based materials containing 1, 2, 5, and 10 wt% of graphene. TiB2-SiC matrix in [33] had slightly higher hardness and lower toughness. The toughness dependence on graphene content dependence presented in Fig. 10 for comparison is similar to that for rGO in the current work. In contradiction, the material hardness in [33] showed a monotonical decrease resembling carbon black-containing composites.
Fig. 7. SEM micrographs of the fracture surfaces of TiB2-SiC-based composites: a) reference (TS00); b,d) 3.8 wt% rGO (TSG03); c,e) 3.8 wt% carbon black (TSC03). The marked red areas in b) and c) are shown in d) and e), respectively. Carbon sheets are indicated with red arrows.
Fig. 8. SEM micrograph of the fracture surface of the 7.4 wt% rGO composite (TSG04), showing rGO agglomerates.
The carbon black presence seems to have minimal impact on the thermal conductivity of the TiB2-SiC matrix (see Fig. 11). The rGO additive, however, leads up to a 50% thermal conductivity coefficient rise at  3.8 wt%  as  a  peak  value.  At  higher  rGO  concentrations,  thermal conductivity slumps. The latter, again, could be attributed to the rGO sheet agglomeration at higher carbon contents.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics based on ZrB2-SiC composition have a unique combination of high-temperature strength, good oxidation resistance, high hardness and high thermal conductivity. This allows them to be used as sharp leading edges, control surfaces and nosecones of re-entry vehicles [1], gas turbine parts [2] and cutting tools [3]. However, the fracture toughness of these materials is lower than that of most structural ceramics, and is in the region between 3.6 and 4.2 MPa m 1/2 for conventional nonporous ZrB2-SiC composites [4 -7].
Numeral attempts to improve the material toughness are based on a composite structure engineering. The best results were achieved by nano /uniFB01 bers or nano /uniFB02 akes incorporation. Guicciardi et. al. [8] showed that the K1C of ZrB2-5 vol. % Si3N4 increased from 3.8 to 5.7 MPa m 1/2 by the addition of 20 vol.% of 0.6 μ mSiC whiskers. The incorporation of 15 vol.% of SiC nanowhiskers, as presented by Wang and Wang [9], increased the fracture toughness of ZrB2 from 5.5 to 6.8 MPa m 1/2 . Silicon carbide whisker incorporation rose the K1C of SPS ZrB2 from 3.5 to 4.7 MPa m 1/2 , while graphite nano /uniFB02 akes addition to a ZrB2-SiCw composite further improved the material toughness up to 6.2 MPa m 1/2 [10].
Carbon nanotubes are well known to be the strongest nano /uniFB01 bers with a tensile strength up to 50 GPa [11]. There were many attempts to

A B S T R A C T
The most crucial goal is the improvement of Ultra High Temperature Ceramic (UHTC) fracture toughness without negatively impacting the ceramic high-temperature properties. Carbon nanostructures in UHTCs appear to be one of the most promising ways forward. The influence of reduced graphene oxide vs. carbon black on microstructure and properties of TiB2-SiC ceramics is studied. The composite ceramic samples with additive contents up to 7.4 wt% were manufactured via the reactive hot pressing of B4C-TiC-Si-reduced graphene oxide/carbon black precursors at 1850 ◦ С and 30 MPa for 4 min. The sintered materials composition and structure were analysed  with  Raman  spectroscopy,  XRD,  SEM,  and  TEM.  Reduced  graphene  oxide  (rGO)  survives  the  hotpressing procedure and, at lower concentrations, distributes inside the TiB2-SiC matrix in the form of separate sheets. However, at 7.4 wt%, the rGO was shown to agglomerate. The rGO improved the hardness, fracture toughness, and thermal conductivity of the TiB2-SiC by up to 16%, 20%, and 53%, respectively. The composite characteristics reached the values of HV = 26.1 GPa, K1 C = 7.4 MPa · m 1/2 , and k = 69 W/m · K. the TiB2 matrix resulted in a material hardness of 19.4 GPa, toughness of 6.9 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 , and flexural strength of 797 MPa. Zhou et al. [10] showed an enhancement of SiC high-temperature tribological properties with an addition  of  20%  of  TiB2.  Sintering  to  high  density  is  still  very  challenging. We previously showed that reactive hot pressing produces high composite density at lowered temperatures and shorter processing times (see references below).

3.3. Characterization of sintered materials
TiB2-25 vol%  SiC  ceramics  presented  in  [39]  showed  a  thermal conductivity coefficient of 50 W/mK, which is close to the TS00 reference sample. However, adding 2 wt% of graphene led to an 8% value reduction. The dissimilarity of the data with the results of the current work shows the necessity of additional research on the mechanisms of carbon  additives ' influence  on  ceramic  matrix  composite  thermal properties.
In summary, we can conclude that rGO addition improves mechanical  properties  and  thermal  conductivity  compared  to  pure  TiB2-SiC ceramic or composites with carbon black as an additive. The improved properties could have multiple explanations and contributing factors. There is a higher oxygen content in the reference than in the different composite structures of the sintered materials. Carbon additives themselves can potentially contribute to toughness, hardness, and thermal properties. Since the samples containing carbon black showed similar or worse  mechanical  properties  and  thermal  conductivity,  it  can  be assumed that the reduced oxygen content is not the determining factor. The  larger  areas  of  continuous  SiC  or  TiB2 observed  in  the  rGOcontaining sample could be an explanation for the higher thermal conductivity because of fewer grain boundaries. It should also be noted that the most essential property improvements in rGO-contained composites coincide with the rGO existing in the form of separate sheets. The sheet agglomeration  at  higher  additive  concentrations  results  in  property similarity for graphite/rGO-containing materials. Therefore, it can be assumed that the rGO sheets that remain in the matrix after sintering contribute to the mechanical and thermal properties with specific rGOinherent mechanisms.

3.3. Mechanical properties
Vickers  hardness,  and  fracture  toughness  values  of  the  different samples obtained in this work are shown in Table 1. It should be noted that no  fracture  toughness  data  of additive free (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Mo0.2W0.2)B2 are available so far in literature.
No particular effect was displayed by the use of finer W and/or the addition of graphite on the Vickers hardness of samples fabricated in this work, with HV values in the range of 26.1 -26.8 GPa. The latter values were in line with results (26 GPa) reported by Qin et al. [11], where the HEB ceramic was produced by Reactive SPS from elemental powders preliminarily  activated  by  HEBM.  Superior  hardness  properties  were obtained by Gild et al. [18] and Zhang et al. [17], i.e. 29.4 and 27.7 GPa, respectively,  but  the  secondary  phases  present  in  the  corresponding sintered samples (Table 1) are expected to affect the measured values.
Table 1 also indicates that the SiC free samples possessed very low fracture toughness values, i.e. 2.32 MPa m 0.5 at  most.  Moreover, the introduction of graphite prior to SPS made the resulting KIC worser. This can be explained by the fact that the oxides impurities present in ceramics produced with no graphite hinder the propagation of the cracks formed by Vickers indentation (Figure S6(a)). On the other hand, the reduction of such oxides makes their propagation easier (Figure S6(b)), so that the resulting KIC  is correspondingly lowered. Because of their stress  concentration effect, oxide particles act as crack tip attractors, deviating the crack path from its normal straight direction. In this way, the fracture surface is increased and the KIC value as well.
The  introduction  of  SiC  to  the  HEB  matrix  provided  a  marked improvement on the KIC  value, which increased up to 5.11 MPa m 0.5

terms of the Vickers hardness.
The hardness values were much higher for the samples sintered at 1250 ◦ C and above. Below that temperature the hardness was often a fracture  (12.5 -25%)  of  values  obtained  for  materials  sintered  above 1200 ◦ C.  Additionally,  when  reactant  powders  contained  the  Ti5Si3 intermetallic rather than TiSi2, the final material strength was considerably higher, after the system had fully reacted. However, the hardness of TiB2-SiC composites obtained from reactant powders of B4C-Ti5Si3 at 1100 and 1200 ◦ C was lower than that of any sample of the B4C-TiSi2 group. This phenomenon occurred most likely due to the partial reaction of the B4C and Ti5Si3  phases, observed in SEM/EDS. The Vickers hardness of the B4C-TiSi2 samples also increased with temperature, but it was lower  by  about  8 -9 GPa. This  was  expected  after  the  microstructure observation, due to the phase composition of the samples obtained from
Fig. 13. The microstructure of the stoichiometric TiB2-SiC composite obtained from reactant powders of B4C and Ti5Si3  at 1000 ◦ C, with the elemental distribution maps.

4. Conclusions
The difficult target of synthesizing the (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Mo0.2W0.2)B2 ceramic as a single phase is achieved in this work by combining the SHS and SPS methods. To this aim, the use of relatively fine W precursors and the addition of a small amount of graphite to the SHS powders provide a synergic contribution for the abatement of kinetic limitations present during the synthesis of such high entropy boride, which generally leads to the formation of W-rich secondary phases. The approximately 98.5 % dense and homogeneous product obtained by SPS shows Vickers hardness  of  about  26.8 GPa,  whereas  fracture  toughness  and  oxidation resistance  at  high  temperature  properties  are  both  very  modest.  The latter criticisms can be overcome with the introduction of 20 vol%SiC. Correspondingly, a marked improvement of the KIC value from 2.32 (at most, for the additive free system) to 5.11 MPa m 1/2 (composite sample) is obtained. Moreover, this additive is found to markedly improve the scarce  resistance  of  the  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Mo0.2W0.2)B2 material  when exposed to oxidative environments, which is primarily caused by the volatilization of Mo- and W-oxides. In the composite ceramic, the latter phenomenon can be significantly lowered by the formation of silicate phases, which incorporates such volatile oxides, and consequently leads to a reduced degradation rate.
Generally speaking, it should be mentioned that the addition of SiC reduces the intrinsic refractory nature of the UHTCs, so that their performances  at  temperatures  exceeding  1600 ◦ C  might  be  negatively affected. Nonetheless, when considering the (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Mo0.2W0.2) B2 system investigated in this work, the detrimental volatilization of Wand Mo- oxides occurs at 800 ◦ C, i.e. well below 1600 ◦ C, so that the use of  SiC  provide  a  beneficial  improvement  of  its  resistance  in  a  wider temperature range.

A B S T R A C T
The most crucial goal is the improvement of Ultra High Temperature Ceramic (UHTC) fracture toughness without negatively impacting the ceramic high-temperature properties. Carbon nanostructures in UHTCs appear to be one of the most promising ways forward. The influence of reduced graphene oxide vs. carbon black on microstructure and properties of TiB2-SiC ceramics is studied. The composite ceramic samples with additive contents up to 7.4 wt% were manufactured via the reactive hot pressing of B4C-TiC-Si-reduced graphene oxide/carbon black precursors at 1850 ◦ С and 30 MPa for 4 min. The sintered materials composition and structure were analysed  with  Raman  spectroscopy,  XRD,  SEM,  and  TEM.  Reduced  graphene  oxide  (rGO)  survives  the  hotpressing procedure and, at lower concentrations, distributes inside the TiB2-SiC matrix in the form of separate sheets. However, at 7.4 wt%, the rGO was shown to agglomerate. The rGO improved the hardness, fracture toughness, and thermal conductivity of the TiB2-SiC by up to 16%, 20%, and 53%, respectively. The composite characteristics reached the values of HV = 26.1 GPa, K1 C = 7.4 MPa · m 1/2 , and k = 69 W/m · K. Carbon black additive formed roughly equiaxial two micrometre inclusions and was not observed to impact the investigated properties.
the TiB2 matrix resulted in a material hardness of 19.4 GPa, toughness of 6.9 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 , and flexural strength of 797 MPa. Zhou et al. [10] showed an enhancement of SiC high-temperature tribological properties with an addition  of  20%  of  TiB2.  Sintering  to  high  density  is  still  very  challenging. We previously showed that reactive hot pressing produces high composite density at lowered temperatures and shorter processing times (see references below).

3.2. Discussion on S3 ' s higher mass gain
Among all compositions, S3 (30 % ZrB2 / 30 % TiB2) exhibited the highest total mass gain (40 %) at 1400 ◦ C. This anomaly can be attributed  to  the  balanced  reinforcement  ratio,  which  may  have  caused competitive oxidation reactions between ZrB2  and TiB2. Such simultaneous oxidation can delay the formation of a coherent, passivating oxide scale, resulting in non-uniform oxide distribution and increased surface reactivity in early oxidation stages [16,32,33,35 -38,54]. In addition, S3 showed  a  relatively  higher  open  porosity  (6.9  %)  compared  to  the optimal sample S4 (5.1 %), as presented in Table 3. This higher porosity allows  deeper  oxygen  diffusion  into  the  microstructure,  intensifying internal oxidation and further contributing to mass gain [16,33,56]. The volatility of B2O3, a product of TiB2  oxidation, can also lead to the formation of voids or microcracks in the oxide layer, making the barrier layer more permeable [32,35]. The combination of these effects, oxide volatility, porosity, and delayed barrier formation, likely contributed to the  higher  oxidation  rate  and  net  mass  gain  in  S3,  despite  having  a balanced reinforcement composition.

3. Results and discussion
Relative density values of the composite samples after the SPS process are presented in Fig. 4. With an average value of 99.58%, the highest relative density belongs to sample No. 3. The results of water absorption and open porosity measurements (not reported here), with the lowest values for sample No. 3 compared to other ones, are in good harmony with this outcome. Sample No. 1, as the baseline which is free of ZrC and Cf additives, reached a relative density of 93.66%. The low relative density of this sample can not only be related to the insu /uniFB03 cient sintering temperature, but can also to presence of oxide impurities on the surface of ZrB2 and SiC particles in the absence of carbon additive as a reductant/sintering aid. This is while; the relative density enhances (samples No. 2&3) by the addition of ZrC and Cf up to 5 vol% and 2.5 vol%, respectively. Such positive roles of ZrC and Cf additions on densi /uniFB01 cation behavior of ZrB2 -SiC composites were also reported in the literature [28,36]. Anyway, by adding higher amounts of these reinforcements, the negative role of extra Cf on densi /uniFB01 cation can be expected since the relative densities of samples No. 4 and No. 5 were dropped to 95.38% and 90.67%, respectively. Such a low relative density of sample No. 5 can be related to the agglomeration of carbon /uniFB01 bers in some areas.

3.1. Microstructural features
Process cycles, volumetric amounts of the phases, residual and open porosities, and densities for the composites are reported in Table 1.
Samples labelled HPP and PHP had similar volumetric fractions of carbon fibres (39.9 % vs. 33.5 %), ZrB2  (34.0 % vs. 31.8 %) and SiC introduced with the powder slurry (SiCp) (3.8 % vs. 3.5 %), whilst a larger fraction of polymer-derived ceramic SiC (SiCPDC) was contained in sample PHP (18.2 % vs. 7.9 %). Regardless of the consolidation route used (route A vs. route B), both final porosities were found to be about ¼ of the starting porosity (green samples). This represents a good result in term of shortening of process time and less consumption of SiC precursor compared to PIP process. A final porosity of 22 GLYPH<0> 27 % was reported after three PIP cycles to realize SICARBON and UHTCMCs [57]. Moreover, in our  experience,  at  least  four  PIP  cycles  are  necessary  to  achieve  a porosity of about 13 % (not published data).
The variation of the bulk and relative densities after each manufacturing step are reported in Table 2 while a visual presentation of the variation of the bulk density is shown in Fig. 2. The relative density of a green pellet was ~51 %. In the case of HPP, the preliminary hot pressing treatment caused a remarkable increase of relative density to ~77 %, while a slight relative density variation was recorded after the first and second PIP cycles (83 % and 86 % respectively). 1 Introduction
This work fabricated 2D SiC/SiC composites via the CVI process and evaluated their tensile properties at room temperature, 1000°C, 1250°C, 1350°C, and 1500°C. Multiple characterization tests (OM, SEM, XPS, Raman, XRD, CT, TEM, and push-in) were subsequently utilized to analyze the high-temperature microstructure and phase evolution. The research established correlations between microstructural characteristics and tensile performance, investigating the stress-oxidation-induced degradation mechanism  of  SiC/SiC  composites  under  high-temperature  tensile  conditions,  and providing  foundational  support  for  the  high-temperature  applications  of  SiC/SiC composites.

Journal Pre-proof 2 Materials and experiments 2.1 Material preparation The 2D SiC/SiC composites was prepared by the chemical vapor infiltration (CVI) process, and the reinforcement was Canansas3303 SiC fibers. In preparation process, multiple layers of plain fiber were firstly laminated into 2D woven preform, followed by the deposition of BN interphase and SiC matrix by CVI. Finally, the standard tensile specimens were machined. The density of the 2D SiC/SiC composites was 2.67g/cm 3 , with open porosity of 9.05% and fiber volume friction of 35%. 3.2.3 Fiber damage
Fig. 10 presented TEM images of SiC fibers at fracture surfaces tested at room temperature and 1500 o C. The results demonstrated that fibers exhibited homogeneous nanocrystalline structure with an average grain size of 9.57 nm at room temperature. HRTEM images revealed distinct lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.131 nm,  while  the  corresponding  SAED  pattern  displayed  characteristic  polycrystalline diffraction rings indexed to (311), (220) and (111) crystallographic planes.
Journal Pre-proof Fig. 11 presented the Raman spectroscopy results of SiC fibers at fracture surfaces. The  spectra  obtained  from  both  Tescan  and  Renishaw  in  Via  Reflex  systems consistently showed characteristic peaks of carbon phase and SiC phase. Quantitative analysis revealed a decreasing trend in the ID/IG ratio of the carbon phase, indicating that while high-temperature exposure did not alter the phase composition of SiC fibers, it  reduced  the  structural  ordering  of  free  carbon  and  increased  defect  density.  This microstructural evolution directly contributed to the degradation of fiber performance, providing a mechanistic explanation for the observed strength reduction in SiC fibers during high-temperature dynamic tensile testing and the consequent deterioration of tensile properties in 2D SiC/SiC composites.
Journal Pre-proof Fig. 10 TEM characterization of fractured SiC fibers (a) at room temperature, (b) at 1500 o C After  high-temperature  tensile  testing  at  1500  °C,  the  SiC  fibers  exhibited  a uniform yet coarsened grain structure, with the average grain size increasing to 20.56 nm,  suggesting  notable  temperature-induced  grain  growth.  HRTEM  observations showed an interplanar spacing of 0.234 nm, and SAED patterns retained polycrystalline ring characteristics with identical diffraction planes as room-temperature specimens, confirming no phase transformation occurred during high-temperature exposure.
Fig. 11 Raman results of fractured SiC fibers
(a) Raman spectra at RT, 1000 o C and 1250 o C, (b) ID/IG

3. Results and Discussion
In this work, we used ZrB2-SiC composites to study the critical inclusion size for stress-induced microcracking at high temperatures. Watts et al. reported that spontaneous microcracking occurred at room temperature when the size of the SiC particle embedded in the microstructure of ZrB2-SiC composites was larger than 11.5 µ m[6]. Using Equation (9) and the critical inclusion size for spontaneous microcracking at room temperature, the value of b was calculated and was assumed to be a constant under different temperatures. In the calculations, the relative parameters were determined by experiments and are shown in Table 1 [12,20-24]. As the temperature had little effect on the Poisson's ratio of the UHTCs, the Poisson's ratio of the materials at room temperature was used in the calculation process [12-16]. The effects of small changes to the Poisson's ratio with temperature on the temperature dependence of the mechanical properties and the fracture behavior of the materials were ignored.

Dipartimento di Ingegneria Meccanica-Settore Materiali, University of Padova, Padova 35122, Italy Abstract: In  this  work,  the  tensile  behavior  of  2D  SiC/SiC  composites  was systematically investigated in air environment across a temperature range from room temperature  to  1500°C.  The  degradation  of  components  was  investigated  through microstructural characterization, and the high-temperature damage mechanisms were explained  by  using  micromechanics  theory.  The  results  revealed  that  the  tensile behavior  maintained  a  bilinear  trend  at  elevated  temperatures,  characterized  by  a decrease  in  proportional  limit  stress  from  155.6  MPa  to  121.7  MPa,  a  reduction  in tensile strength from 399.1 MPa to 196.1 MPa, and a decline in tensile modulus from 262.2 GPa to 171.5 GPa. Based on micromechanics theory, the calculated values for tensile modulus, matrix cracking stress, and tensile strength have errors of less than 20% to measured values. That confirmed fiber pull-out, interphase debonding, and matrix periodic cracking remained the dominant failure mechanisms during high-temperature tensile  test.  Furthermore,  the  high-temperature  tensile  damage  mechanism  was established. Prior to the formation of main matrix crack, oxidation induced decrease in matrix modulus, causing deterioration of tensile modulus. After the main matrix crack developed, interphase oxidation induced degradation of the interface shear strength, leading to decrease of matrix cracking stress. Concurrently, the fibers became core load-

3.3.1 Tensile modulus
Under high-temperature tensile conditions, the tensile modulus ( E c) of 2D SiC/SiC composites exhibited significant temperature dependence, decreasing from 262.20GPa at room temperature to 171.50GPa at 1500°C. Prior to matrix cracking, the fibers were not exposed to the air environment, so the fiber modulus was assumed to be a constant value  of  350GPa. The  degradation  behavior  primarily  originated  from  the  modulus attenuation of structural constituents in 2D SiC/SiC composites. Based on experimentally measured variations in the SiC matrix modulus (Fig. 4) and employing the rule of mixtures (Eq. (5)), predictions of E c were obtained summarized in Table 3.

3.1 Degradation of high temperature tensile properties
Fig.  2b-f  illustrated  the  evolution  curves  of  various  tensile  properties  of  2D SiC/SiC composites at distinct temperatures. The proportional ultimate stress (Fig. 2b) fluctuated, albeit not significantly, with fluctuations ranging from 121.7 MPa to 155.6 MPa. This  originated  from  the  temperature-sensitive  nature  of  the  fiber-matrix interfacial bonding state, which generated residual stresses and consequently leads to the appearance of a localized extremum in the matrix cracking stress at this location. As shown in Fig. 2c, the tensile strength exhibited a continuous decrease from 399.1 MPa at  room  temperature  to  196.1  MPa  at  1500 ° C. The  tensile  modulus  (Fig.  2d) showed a decreasing trend from 262.2 GPa to 171.5 GPa.
Journal Pre-proof As shown in Fig. 2e, the modulus of toughness has been observed to increase, approximating  1000 ° C,  1250 ° C,  1350 ° C and rising  to  0.11  MJ/m 3 at  1500 ° C. The modulus of resilience (Fig. 2f) exhibited a continuous decrease from 1.22 MJ/m 3 to 0.29 MJ/m 3 , recovering to 0.43 MJ/m 3 at 1500 ° C. This recovery can be attributed to the SiO2 formed on the fibers during the tensile process, which enhances energy dissipation. This led to synergistic toughening through crack interaction during fracture propagation, resulting  in  the  recovery  of  the  toughness  modulus. The  tensile  properties  of  2D SiC/SiC  composites  were  decreased  when  exposed  to  elevated  temperatures. The tensile strength, tensile modulus, and modulus of resilience all exhibited a downward trend, which was attributed to the degradation of the internal components of 2D SiC/SiC composites  at  high  temperatures. The  proportional  limit  stress  and  the  resilience modulus demonstrated ascending trend, which was correlated to the alterations in the structure and properties of the matrix at elevated temperatures.

(1) Matrix oxidation
As  illustrated  in  Fig.  6b,  the  fine  spectra  of  C1s,  Si2p,  and  O1s  on  surface demonstrated the distinction in content and ratio of the bonds at elevated temperatures. The C1s spectra demonstrated that the C-Si bond manifested at a binding energy of 282.6 eV at room temperature, while the C-Si bond dissipated at elevated temperatures. The C=O bond was not evident at room temperature, yet it emerged at a binding energy of 288.6 eV from 1000 ° C to 1500 ° C, exhibiting a fluctuating pattern. The Si2p spectra indicated the presence of two distinct bonding types in Si2p of 2D SiC/SiC composites at room temperature. Si-C bonding in SiC at a binding energy of 100.7 eV. The peak at a binding energy of 102.3 eV was attributed to Si-O bonds, formed by the reaction of surface SiC with O₂. These oxidized species were identified at 1000°C, 1250°C, 1350°C, and 1500°C, indicating their formation and stability over the entire temperature range.

question: According to Jayaseelan 2011, what is the initial total porosity percentage of the porous C/C composite tubes measured using the Archimedes method?
answer: The initial total porosity percentage of the porous C/C composite tubes measured using the Archimedes method is ∼ 60% [1].

chunks.text:
2.1. Materials
Porous C/C tubes (Fig. 1) of 17.75 mm OD × 3.90 mm ID × 45 mm long were used in the present study for ZrB2 infiltration. The porous C/C composite tubes had a total porosity of ∼ 60% (measured using Archimedes method) and bimodal pore size distribution (determined using laser diffraction particle size analyser) with average pore sizes of 150 𝛍 mand10 𝛍 m, respectively. ZrB2 powder ( d 50 ∼ 45 𝛍 m, 99% purity, Good Fellow, Huntingdon, UK) was used as the UHTC material. The as-received ZrB2 powder was further dry-milled for 30 min in a 'swing-mill' shatter box using a steel container coated with Teflon to reduce the average particle size to 5.11 ± 0.5 𝛍 m. Longer milling times (>30 min) did not result in significant reduction in the particle size. Particle size distribution analysis for milling times of 45 min and 60 min resulted in 50 wt.% of the materials being below 5.04 𝛍 mand 4.89 𝛍 m, respectively.
Fig. 1. Optical photograph of a porous C/C tube.

Densification of the Composite
The advancement of PIP method of the porous C/C composites with an initial bulk density of 0.68g/cm 3 and open porosity of 60% was examined by density measurements. The bulk density of the C/C-ZrB 2 -ZrC-SiC composites produced according to the number of PIP cycles is shown in Figure 7, which corresponds volume ratios of the formed ZrB 2 :ZrC:SiC matrix of 20:30:50 (Table 3). It shows a gradual decrease in the impregnation efficiency with increasing of the number of PIP cycles. After 16 PIP cycles, the bulk density increases by less than 2%.

Abstract
A novel reactive infiltration processing (RIP) technique was employed to infiltrate porous carbon fibre reinforced carbon (C/C) composite hollow tubes with ultra high temperature ceramic (UHTC) particles such as ZrB2. The C/C composite tubes had initial porosity of ∼ 60% with a bimodal (10 𝛍 mand 100 𝛍 m) pore size distribution. A slurry with 40-50% ZrB2 solid loading particles was used to infiltrate the C/C tubes. Our approach combines in situ ZrB2 formation with coating of fine ZrB2 particles on carbon fibre surfaces by a reactive processing method. A Zr and B containing diphasic gel was first prepared using inorganic-organic hybrid precursors of zirconium oxychloride (ZrOCl2 · 8H2O), boric acid, and phenolic resin as sources of zirconia, boron oxide, and carbon, respectively. Then commercially available ZrB2 powder was added to this diphasic gel and milled for 6 h. The resultant hybrid slurry was vacuum infiltrated into the porous hollow C/C tubes. The infiltrated tubes were dried and fired for 3 h at 1400 ◦ C in flowing Ar atmosphere to form and coat ZrB2 on the carbon fibres in situ by carbothermal reaction. Microstructural observation of infiltrated porous C/C composites revealed carbon fibres coating with fine nanosized ( ∼ 100 nm) ZrB2 particles infiltrated to a depth exceeding 2 mm. Ultra high temperature ablation testing for 60 s at 2190 ◦ C suggested formation of ZrO2 around the inner bore of the downstream surface. Crown Copyright © 2010 Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Keywords: Reactive infiltration processing; Carbothermal reaction; C/C composites; ZrB2; Sol-gel; Vacuum infiltration; Ablation resistance

3. Results and discussion
After RMI processes, the density and open porosity of the composite material were measured by the Archimedes drainage method, which are 2.71 ± 0.73 g/cm 3 and 7.07 ± 1.29 %, respectively. To characterize the changes in pore structure before and after siliconization, samples sized 5 mm × 5 mm × 5 mm were extracted from the C/Ta4HfC5-C intermediate and the final C/Ta4HfC5-SiC composite material for mercury intrusion porosimetry (MIP). The experimental results are shown in Fig. 4. The porosities of the intermediate and siliconized samples were 38.23 % and 6.51 % respectively, indicating a significant reduction in porosity after siliconization. Fig. 4a and b reveal a bimodal pore size distribution in the C/Ta4HfC5-C intermediate, with nanometer and submicron pores below 1.6 μ m and larger pores ranging from 100 to 500 μ m. These larger pores provide pathways for molten Si during RMI process, though excessively large pores may hinder the Si-C reaction, resulting in residual Si in the matrix  [29].  Fig.  4c  depicts  the  cumulative  intrusion  curve  for C/Ta4HfC5-SiC composite, showing a median pore size of 1.6 μ m with a multi-modal distribution. Fig. 4d illustrates another cumulative intrusion curve for C/Ta4HfC5-SiC composite, indicating pore sizes mainly consisting of nanometer pores below 40 nm, micrometer pores ranging from 0.04 to 8.5 μ m, and a small number of larger pores from 10 to 285 μ m. Post-siliconization, significant reduction in larger pores is observed, while  the  number  of  micrometer  and  nanometer  pores  shows  slight decrease.

answer: The provided sources do not mention the oxidation time constant (τ_ox) for spark plasma sintered ZrC at 600°C under 2.0% oxygen partial pressure.

Aiming at answering these questions, we fabricated sintered stoichiometric ZrC ceramic (C/Zr ≈ 1) and investigated its oxidation kinetics under a wide range of temperatures and oxygen pressures. ZrC ceramic was made via SPS due to several advantages including reduction of sintering duration, uniform distribution of heat, and high densification compared to other sintering techniques [20 -22]. With controlled density and purity, the oxidation behavior of stoichiometric SPS-sintered ZrC was studied under varying temperatures 600 -1000 ◦ C, oxygen  pressures  2 -21 %  O2,  and  elapsed  time  up  to  10800 s.  A consistent comparison was performed across different parameter ranges in 3D T-P-t plots. Instead of the commonly assumed linear oxidation of ZrC, a passivation stage was discovered and the reason for parabolic-to-linear oxidation rate transition was also uncovered. Furthermore, the Ea of  ZrC  UHTC oxidation as  a function of oxygen pressure  and  temperature  was  derived.  These  findings  could  build  a foundation for designing future oxidation-resistant ZrC materials as well as other carbide ceramics.

A B S T R A C T
radiation  X-ray  diffraction  (XRD),  [11 -13]  ultrasonic  measurements, [14 -17]  Nano-indentation,  [18], etc. The  structural  stability  and compressibility of HfC and TaC upon compression were investigated by using in-situ high-pressure synchrotron radiation XRD and reported by Liang et al. [11], Liermann et al. [12] and Chen et al. [13]. However, in-situ high-pressure  synchrotron  radiation  XRD  has  difficulties  and challenges in obtaining the materials ' shear-related elasticity and thermodynamic  parameters.  Ultrasonic  interferometric  technology  is  an effective way to investigate the elasticity and thermodynamic behavior of materials, such as adiabatic bulk modulus ( B ), shear modulus ( G ) and Debye  temperature  ( Td ).  Few  published  works  have  focused  on  this method to study HfC and TaC, e.g. , Brown et al. [14] and Yang et al. [15] determined  the  zero-pressure  bulk  modulus  of  HfC  using  ultrasonic measurements at ambient pressure, Dodd et al. [16] studied the elastic constants  of  TaC via  in-situ high-pressure  acoustic  experiments  with pressure  up  to  ~0.2 GPa.  Overall,  the  experimental  values  for  the zero-pressure bulk moduli of HfC and TaC are scattered from 200 to 270 GPa [11,14,15] and from 332 to 433 GPa [12 -14,16], respectively. The uncertain elastic modulus greatly hinders their wide application in the engineering field. Moreover, the elasticity of HfC and TaC at high pressure  and  its  pressure  dependences  are  urgently  required  for  the application of HfC and TaC ceramics.

question: According to Arianpour et al. 2016, what is the Vickers hardness (HV10) value for the TaC-HfC-MoSi2 composite sample with 15 vol.% MoSi2 consolidated at 2000°C?
answer: According to Arianpour et al. 2016, the Vickers hardness (HV10) value for the TaC-HfC-MoSi2 composite sample with 15 vol.% MoSi2 consolidated at 2000°C is 18 -19 GPa [1][2][4].

chunks.text:
a b s t r a c t
In this research, binary 4TaC -HfC based composites were consolidated using carbide materials and addition of 0 -15 vol.% MoSi2 by means of spark plasma sintering at 2000 °C. The nearly full dense and monophase specimens were fabricated with a relative density value higher than 99%. Mechanical tests revealed values of 18 -19 GPa and 4 -4.3 MPa·m 1/2 , for average Vickers hardness and fracture toughness of the composites, respectively. Analysis of linear shrinkage during densi /uniFB01 cation revealed that MoSi2 addition increased densi /uniFB01 cation rate and decreased the time required to reach full density at 2000 °C. It is proposed that at the intermediate stage of sintering, mass transfer can be accelerated by formation of a silicide based liquid phase and viscous /uniFB02 ow mechanisms. The formation of binary 4TaC -HfC solid solution phase enhanced the densi /uniFB01 cation process at the /uniFB01 nal stage of sintering.
© 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
have been increased to obtain full dense TaC bodies with enhanced mechanical properties and tailored /uniFB01 ne grain microstructures. In SPS processing of TaC composites, the application of different materials like metallic tantalum powder [13], B4C [14], nano-B4C [15], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [16], graphene [17] and silicon carbide [18] were reported as sintering aid in the literature. Silicides of tantalum and molybdenum (TaSi2/MoSi2) are commonly considered as sintering aids in liquid phase assisted sintering of various types of UHTC borides and carbides [2 -6]. However, degradation and vaporization of Si from silicides were reported at high temperatures and hard vacuum conditions [19], which may lead to Si content loss during sintering [3].

4. Conclusion
Nearly full dense, mono phase 4TaC -HfC solid solution based composites were consolidated by means of spark plasma sintering of TaC and HfC materials using MoSi2 as sintering additive at 2000 °C for
Fig. 6. BSE-SEM image showing the crack growth in THM10 sample.
5 min under 50 MPa pressure. The fabricated samples doped by MoSi2 hadrelative density values more than 99% and average Vickers hardness and fracture toughness of 18 -19 GPa and 4 -4.3 MPa·m 1/2 , respectively. Regarding the densi /uniFB01 cation and microstructural investigations, it was found that MoSi2 forms liquid phase during intermediate stage of sintering which /uniFB01 nally enhances the sintering behavior of the samples in both isothermal and non-isothermal stages. XRD results revealed that TaC and HfC would diffuse to form binary solid solution phase at 2000 °C. The activated crack de /uniFB02 ection phenomenon was observed as the main toughening mechanism in this system.

4.1.2. Mechanical behavior
reinforcement [111]. Although some agglomeration was observed with 5 vol % GNPs reinforcement, fracture toughness still increased, eliciting that  the  toughness  value  is  more  dependent  on  the  content  of  GNPs rather than its dispersion. To fully densify HfC, TaC, and ZrC at 1950 ◦ C via pressure-less sintering; 5 vol %, 10 vol % and 20 vol %, respectively, of MoSi2,  was added. This varying content of sintering aid, required to densify the ceramics, can be ascribed to varying powder size and purity. With the addition of 20 vol % MoSi2, highest Vickers ' hardness of 15.5 GPa, was obtained in HfC based composite as compared to that of 12.1 GPa and 12.7 GPa, in TaC and ZrC composite, respectively. On the other hand, maximum elastic modulus of 476 GPa was obtained in TaC based composite as compared to 385 GPa and 346 GPa in HfC and ZrC based composite [106].

3.4. Mechanical properties
Themechanical property values of the SPSed composites are given in Table 1. The mechanical characteristics of UHTCs are mainly sensitive to various factors such as lattice parameter, stoichiometry, vacancies and chemical composition [1]. The hardness values of MoSi2 containing samples were in the range of 18 -19 GPa. The variations can be attributed to the starting composition, the formation of solid solution and trace phases such as silicides or probable SiC. These values are in the range of the reported ones for monolithic TaC (~18 GPa) and HfC (~20 GPa) [1,2]. The hardness of the TH sample was measured as around 16 GPa which is related to the residual porosity. The highest hardness value is observed for sample THM15, which could be attributed to the full densi /uniFB01 cation state reached for this composite. The average values of Young's moduli were in range of 470 -490 GPa which are in agreement with the values reported for monolithic TaC (~537 GPa) and HfC (~425 GPa)[1].Young's modulus is mainly affected by the amount of porosity and the presence of the secondary phases. On the contrary, in the samples containing MoSi2, Young's moduli show increase with an increased additive content which is due to lower amount of porosity. The fracture toughness values of the doped samples are in the range of 3.9 -4.3 MPa·m 1/2 . In general, TaC shows higher toughness compared to other carbides. For the doped samples, a slight increase in toughness was observed with increase in the initial MoSi2 content. This phenomenon suggests that by adding MoSi2 as a sintering aid and retaining silicide-based phases in the microstructure, some toughening mechanisms such as crack de /uniFB02 ection can be activated [4 -6]. The values of 15 -15.9 GPa and 3.2 -3.9 MPa·m 1/2 for hardness and fracture toughness were reported for the similar compositions which were spark plasma sintered at 1650 °C for 5 min [27].

3. Results and discussion
Finally, a preliminary mechanical characterization of fabricated composites was performed by Vickers indentation tests. Fig. 9 shows the resulting hardness of the fabricated composites as a function of MoSi2 content. The inability to polish the pure ZrB2 samples because of extensive chipping due to the absence of the intergranular phase prevented conducting reliable hardness measurements in those samples. In the composites, as could be expected, measured hardness values show a clear correlation with the relative density of the material (Table 1), clearly increasing with the additive content. And although values were low for 5 and 10 vol% MoSi2, they approached 16 GPa for 15 and 20 vol % MoSi2 contents. This value is consistent with literature reports for similar composites obtained by hot-pressing and SPS [23,24], although lower than the values registered for pure ZrB2 fully densified by SPS (~ 22 GPa). Estimations of indentation fracture toughness in the fully dense composites, 4.2 ± 0.6 and 3.3 ± 0.3 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 for ZrB2-15 and ZrB2-20, respectively, are also in good agreement with those measured in hot-pressed ZrB2-MoSi2 composites [41,42]. It is worth highlighting the fact that the obtained mechanical performance, while not the best reported in the literature for this type of materials is still well within the reported ranges of mechanical properties despite the high level of dendritic porosity present in the green samples and the absence of pressure applied during sintering. Moreover, the presence of impurities in the material might also be responsible for a reduction in the mechanical performance over other reported works, although this extreme should be assessed in future works. If that is the case, future optimization of the heat treatment and/or the starting powders may enable the elimination of such impurities and could lead to further improvements in the mechanical performance of freeze-cast ZrB2-MoSi2 dense composites.

question: In the Arianpour 2016 study, what is the fracture toughness (KIC) value for the 4TaC-HfC-MoSi2 composite with 10 vol.% MoSi2 addition consolidated at 2000°C?
answer: The fracture toughness (KIC) value for the 4TaC-HfC-MoSi2 composite with 10 vol.% MoSi2 addition consolidated at 2000°C is in the range of 3.9 -4.3 MPa·m^1/2 [3].

3.5.2. Fracture toughness
The fracture toughness values obtained by three-point bend tests on the SENB samples, are presented in Table 3. The KIC values obtained for UD-0/0 ◦ -AR and UD-0/0 ◦ -HT composites found as 8.9 ± 1.1 MPa √ m and 7.8 ± 0.4 MPa √ m, respectively, appear reasonably close to one another. In contrast, the UD-0/0 ◦ -US composite has exhibited a lower fracture toughness value of ~5.1 ± 0.4 MPa √ m. It is worth noting that the KIC values found for UD-0/0 ◦ -AR and UD-0/0 ◦ -HT composites are greater by 74 % and 52 %, respectively compared to those reported for the ZrB2 -SiC composites hot-pressed under identical conditions (3 -5 MPa √ m) [1], which indicates a significant increase in fracture toughness due to the presence of continuous Cf fabric. Contrary to the trend observed for UD-0/0 ◦ -AR and UD-0/0 ◦ -HT composites, the UD-0/0 ◦ -US composite has exhibited an average fracture toughness matching closely with the values recorded for the hot-pressed ZrB2 -SiC composite [48]. In an earlier study, Zhang et al. [48] have compared the fracture toughness values obtained for the Cf-ZrB2-SiC composites prepared using nano-slurry brushing and hot pressing, with the results obtained for a hot-pressed ZrB2 -SiC composite. The fracture toughness of the continuous Cf-ZrB2-SiC composite (6.15 ± 0.12 MPa √ m) has been found to be 20 % higher compared to the value (5.12 ± 0.16 MPa √ m) obtained for the hot pressed ZrB2 -SiC composite, indicating a smaller difference compared to that recorded for UD-0/0 ◦ -AR and UD-0/0 ◦ -HT composites in the present study.

4. Conclusion
X-ray patterns of spark plasma sintered samples confirmed formation of Ta1 -x Hf x C solid solution. Increase of sintering aid (MoSi2 or TaSi2) from 4 vol.% to 12 vol.% raised final density of Ta0.8Hf0.2C and fully consolidation was achieved in presence of 12 vol.% sintering aid after spark plasma sintering at 1650 ◦ C under 30 MPa for 5 min.
The RDR curves showed two distinct densification stages. At the first stage, shrinkage was occurred due to plastic deformation of sintering aids particles. The first stage starting temperature of prepared samples at 1340 ◦ C (in presence of MoSi2) and 1100 ◦ C (in presence of TaSi2) was in a good agreement with BDTT of MoSi2 (1200 ◦ C) and TaSi2 (1000 ◦ C), respectively. The second stage of sintering was due to solid solution and liquid phase formation. Although, increasing HfC content did not affect BDTT of sintering aids, it decreased the second stage sintering temperature only in MoSi2 containing samples. According to the fact that spark plasma sintering temperature of TaSi2-containing samples is lower in comparison with MoSi2 ones, it can be expressed that the fully consolidation of TaSi2 containing samples can be achieved at temperature lower than 1650 ◦ C.
Hardness of Ta1 -x Hf x C samples in presence of TaSi2 was higher in comparison with MoSi2 containing samples. However, hardness of both TaSi2 and MoSi2 containing samples increased with raising HfC content. Fracture toughness of Ta1 -x Hf x C with MoSi2 was significantly higher, specially at higher HfC content, mainly due to formation of SiC grains in presence of MoSi2.

3.1. Densi /uniFB01 cation behavior
Table 1 shows the relative density values of the SPSed compositions containing different amounts of additive. While TH sample which contains zero amount of MoSi2 showed a relative density of around 94%, the doped specimens reached over 99% theoretical density. Fully consolidated samples were achieved in the presence of MoSi2 as sintering aid after SPS at 2000 °C for 5 min under 50 MPa. In spark plasma sintering, densi /uniFB01 cation behavior can be followed by the instantaneous relative density changes versus temperature [29], which are given in Fig. 1. As can be observed, TH sample has a relatively poor sintering pro /uniFB01 le according to its low relative density increasing before the holding time. Also its /uniFB01 nal relative density hardly surpassed 94% at the end of sintering process. It appears that the sample continue to densify even after reaching 2000 °C and a longer holding time at 2000 °C or more soaking temperature would increase its /uniFB01 nal relative density. Entrapped porosities or some other factors such as low diffusion activities seem to inhibit reaching full densi /uniFB01 cation state for this sample. Such an observation may be attributed to the absence of MoSi2 which seems to be critical on densi /uniFB01 cation behavior of the doped compositions. The sample THM15 (with the highest volume content of MoSi2) reaches to more than 0.9 TD at 1800 °C in comparison to the other ones (Fig. 1). Densi /uniFB01 -cation of the specimens seems to have undergone a degrading tendency when the volume content of MoSi2 decreases.

question: According to Arianpour et al. 2016, what is the Young's modulus value for the binary TaC-HfC composite without MoSi2 addition (sample TH)?
answer: According to Arianpour et al. 2016, the Young's modulus value for the binary TaC-HfC composite without MoSi2 addition (sample TH) is not explicitly provided in the text. However, it is mentioned that the average values of Young's moduli for the SPSed composites were in the range of 470 - 490 GPa, which are in agreement with the values reported for monolithic TaC (~537 GPa) and HfC (~425 GPa). Since sample TH is a binary TaC-HfC composite, its Young's modulus might be expected to fall within the range of these values, but the exact value is not given [1].

These values are in the range of the reported ones for monolithic TaC (~18 GPa) and HfC (~20 GPa) [1,2]. The hardness of the TH sample was measured as around 16 GPa which is related to the residual porosity. The highest hardness value is observed for sample THM15, which could be attributed to the full densi /uniFB01 cation state reached for this composite. For the doped samples, a slight increase in toughness was observed with increase in the initial MoSi2 content. This phenomenon suggests that by adding MoSi2 as a sintering aid and retaining silicide-based phases in the microstructure, some toughening mechanisms such as crack de /uniFB02 ection can be activated [4 -6]. The values of 15 -15.9 GPa and 3.2 -3.9 MPa·m 1/2 for hardness and fracture toughness were reported for the similar compositions which were spark plasma sintered at 1650 °C for 5 min [27].

3.3. Mechanical properties of SPS sintered Ta2CxNy
The TaC sample exhibited a higher value of Young ' s modulus, (704 ± 35) GPa, than the rest of the series ranging from 606 to 648 GPa. Indeed,  the  tantalum  carbonitride  samples  have  a  Young ' s  modulus closer to TaN, (634 ± 23) GPa. The Young ' s modulus values found in the present  study  tend  to  be  higher  than  literature  values  for  samples prepared with SPS ( i . e . 513 GPa for TaC [14], 450 GPa for HfC [44]). A 9 % higher Poisson ratio ( i.e. 0.22 for all the samples in this work vs. 0.24 for TaC in Refs. [14,52]) would result in an approximately 1 % lower YM that is still not enough to explain the difference in results among the studies. Therefore, the gap between the present values and those of the literature  may  be  due  to  different  techniques  ( e.g. micro-indentation [28],  ultrasonic  measurements  [14]  or  even  DFT  calculations  [50]) used  for  determining  the  Young ' s  modulus.  Considering  the  experimental scatter in the measurements, or taking into account the lower literature value for TaC [14], no trend of the Young ' s modulus across the various carbonitride compositions is visible. This indicates that substituting carbon atoms by nitrogen atoms - and vice versa - in the lattice structure has no significant effect on the elastic properties of the analysed materials.

Based  on  the  zero-pressure  bulk  and  shear  moduli,  the  Young ' s modulus and Poisson ' s ratio for HfC ( E0 = 512.2 GPa, v = 0.20) and TaC ( E0 = 581.1 GPa, v = 0.23) are also determined. Young ' s modulus can also  be  obtained  by  nanoindentation  in  addition  to  ultrasonic  measurements  provided  that  several  grains  of  different  orientations  are measured since the isotropic elastic properties are a statistical average of random  grain  orientations.  The  results  derived  from  the  ultrasonic measurements are in good agreement with the average Young ' s modulus of polycrystalline HfC and TaC derived from the average indentation modulus (M) via E = M ( 1 GLYPH<0> v 2 ) , [1] which are 530 GPa and 557 GPa, respectively.

Effect of Transition Metal Silicides
Young's modulus: Young's modulus variation can be explained on the basis of the features affecting hardness. Despite the differences in the stiffness of MoSi 2 (E=440 GPa) (Nakamura, 1990) and TaSi 2 (E=440 GPa) (Chou, 1996), no significant differences were noticed between the carbides containing a different silicide, surprisingly being the TaSi 2 -composites generally stiffer than the MoSi 2 -ones. This is probably related to the formation of higher amount of SiC phase in TaSi 2 -containing carbides.
Fracture toughness: The fracture toughness results also remained unchanged when MoSi 2 was replaced by TaSi 2 , indicating that this property is mainly dictated by the matrix toughness. The highest values were obtained for TaC-based ceramics, owing to the more metallic nature of Ta-compounds as compared to the other systems. The values obtained were in the range of values reported in the literature for similar UHTCs (Zhang, 2008'; Licheri, 2010).
Room temperature strength: Relevant differences were observed for the flexural strength at room temperature. This property reached the highest value for TaC-based composites with MoSi 2 and  decreased  when  MoSi 2 was  replaced  with  TaSi 2 ,  confirming that strength is strongly influenced by a refined and homogenous grain size and by the absence of critical flaws. The effect of the microstructure on strength can be easily observed for TaC-composites, where a matrix with mean grain size of 2.5 µm
resulted in a strength of 680 MPa, whilst a refinement to 1.2 µm, achieved 900 MPa. In accordance, HfC matrix, which had a mean grain size not very different in HCM and HCT, 0.8-1.2 µm, had mechanical strength in the same range, 460-470 MPa. The same can be said for ZrC-based compounds, where an average grain size of 4-5 µm with maximum grain dimension of 8.0 µm resulted in strength of 470-500 MPa.

a b s t r a c t
Tantalum-hafnium carbide binary solid solutions ((Ta,Hf)C) are kinds of interesting ternary ultra-refractory compounds with general formula of TaxHf1 -xCy [20]. The binary 4:1 mol ratio TaC -HfC solid solution phase (4TaC -HfC or Ta4HfC5) is expected to have a melting point of 4215 °C and hardness of approximately 20 GPa [21]. In addition to extreme melting temperature, this composition shows superior physical/mechanical properties raised from high-strength inter-atomic bonding [21]. Some reports have been published on synthesis of Ta4HfC5 based materials with different techniques such as sol -gel [22], plasma spraying [23] and hot pressing of starting TaC/HfC materials [24]. The synthesis and consolidation of 4TaC -HfC based composites in the presence of MoSi2 and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) by means of SPS were /uniFB01 rstly reported by the authors who clari /uniFB01 ed the sinter ability of solid solution based composites [25]. Ghaffari et al. investigated the pressureless sintering [26] and SPS [27] of different mole ratios TaC -HfC systems using silicide materials. They discussed the diffusion behaviors in binary TaC -HfC, TaC -ZrC and HfC -ZrC compounds at elevated temperatures [26] and claimed to reach nearly full dense specimens using 12 vol.% of MoSi2 by means of SPS [27].
The aim of this study is to consolidate 4TaC -HfC based composites by means of SPS using 0 -15 vol.% MoSi2 as a sintering aid. Physical/ mechanical properties, phase composition and microstructure of sintered composites were then investigated as a function of the MoSi2 amountand compared to the monolithic specimen.

question: In Arianpour 2016, what is the lattice parameter (a in Angstroms) for the binary 4TaC-HfC solid solution phase that formed in the TH sample after SPS at 2000°C?
answer: The lattice parameter for the binary 4TaC-HfC solid solution phase that formed in the TH sample after SPS at 2000°C is 4.45 Å [1].
