3.2. Phase evaluation
Fig. 4 shows the positions of (111) peaks of TaC, HfC and newly formed 4TaC -HfC based solid solution phase of the SPSed samples. It can be considered that the peak shifting of binary solid solution phases in TaC -HfC system is related to the mole ratio of the starting materials [31]. As Fig. 4 shows, by solvation completion of HfC and TaC phases, the (111) peaks of 4TaC -HfC solid solution phase shifted to the higher 2 θ ° which means lower lattice parameters. In addition, the lattice parameter of the solid solution phase could decrease due to the solvation of Mo/Si atoms into its crystal lattice [2,5].
Table 2 summarizes the position of (111) peak and crystal structure parameters of TaC and HfC starting materials as well as solid solution phase of the SPSed composites. According to this table, HfC has larger lattice parameter (4.60 Å) than TaC (4.42 Å). Therefore, by solution of HfC phase in TaC structure, the lattice parameter of the obtained solid solutions increases (4.45 Å for TH sample). It can also be observed that TaC peak shifts to lower 2 θ values from 35.1 to 34.86° in the TH sample. It should be considered that the premature sintering of TH sample could be the actual reason for limited solution of HfC in TaC crystal structure.

Fig. 4. Positions of the (111) peaks of TaC, HfC and solid solution phases (0, 5, 10 and 15 correspond the TH, THM5, THM10 and THM15 samples, respectively).

3.2. Phase evaluation
Regarding the physical properties and X-ray diffraction patterns, the binder less TH sample shows less solution of HfC in TaC which can be attributed to the incomplete sintering, causing less particle contact and longer diffusion distances. Furthermore, by adding MoSi2 as a sintering aid, improvement of solid solution formation is expected. As it is shown in the Table 2, the lattice parameter of TH5 sample has been increased to 4.48 Å in comparison to TH and TaC due to solid solution formation. However, by increasing the amount of sintering aid to 10 and 15 vol.% in TH10 and TH15, despite the better sintering and mechanical properties, the lattice parameter of THM10 and THM15 samples has slightly decreased to 4.47 and 4.46 Å in comparison to THM5 sample, but still larger than TH sample. This phenomenon could be explained by the solution of Mo/Si atoms in the crystal structure of carbides, since they are smaller in the atomic radii than the host Ta/Hf atoms, [2,6]. The 2 θ ° are also shifted to the slightly larger values by increasing the MoSi2 content in these samples. Also, the variations of d spacing are in good accordance with the lattice parameter changes, which are listed in Table 2. It seems that there are two different phenomenon;1) solution of HfC in TaC structure which increases the solid solution lattice parameter, and 2) solution of MoSi2 phase in solid solution which decreases the lattice parameter.

3.2. Phase evaluation
Fig. 3 shows the X-ray diffraction patterns of a raw composite powder and all SPSed samples. THM10G refers to a raw composite powder with the same composition of the THM10 sample. In the XRD pattern of this sample, peaks of TaC, HfC and MoSi2 phases can be observed. In the X-ray spectra of the SPSed samples, the original TaC and HfC peaks are shifted to lower or higher 2 θ °, respectively. It can be attributed to the newly formed binary 4TaC -HfC solid solution phase which its composition is expected to be near Ta4HfC5. As this solid solution phase is TaC-rich, it is marked by TaCSS on the /uniFB01 gure. It is seen that all of TaC are taking part with all HfC phase into the formation of binary 4TaC -HfC solid solution phase, while no other peaks related to other non-stoichiometric solid solution phases (normally observed in the TaC -HfC binary system) could be detected. The X-ray spectra of TH sample shows the presence of a major 4TaC -HfC based solid solution phase closed to TaC peaks' position and also another minor HfC-rich solid solution phase closed to HfC peaks' position. As this minor phase is HfC-rich, it is marked by HfCSS on the /uniFB01 gure. It seems that the monophase 4TaC -HfC solid solution formation was not completed in TH sample due to insuf /uniFB01 cient sintering. However, it is clear that the most of the HfC phase diffused into the TaC grains and form the main 4TaC -HfC based solid solution phase (TaCSS). Moreover, there is a minor solid solution phase whose peaks are closer to HfC position (HfCSS). This phase is HfC-rich and made by diffusion of TaC phase into the HfC grains. In contrast to the monolithic TH sample, only the main 4TaC -HfC based solid solution phase can be seen for the MoSi2 containing composites which proves the completion of solid solution phase formation.

disappeared, whereas the peaks corresponding to TaC increased in intensity. HfO 2 (PDF#34-  0104) was also detected at this point. Both TaC and HfC can form continuous solid solutions (Ta x Hfy C z ) across the entire compositional range. 34 At 1400 ℃ , the peaks corresponding to HfO2 and Hf6Ta2O17  exhibited a significant reduction in their intensity, whereas the full width at half maximum (FWHM)  corresponding  to  the  main  phase  increased, suggesting  that  a  solid  solution  reaction  between  TaC and HfC occurred. The peaks corresponding to HfC were not detected, which was attributed to the formation of a HfC and TaC solid solution. As the temperature rose to 1500-  1600 ℃ , two adjacent diffraction peaks, corresponding to Ta x Hfy C z  and TaC, were observed. This result suggested that the solid solution reaction was still ongoing. A  complete  solid  solution  occurred  at  ~1700 ℃ ,  where the 2θ angles exhibited by the diffraction peaks were in agreement with those corresponding to Ta 4 HfC 5 , as previously reported in the literature. 35 The  lattice  parameter of Ta 4 HfC5 was determined to be 4.485 Å. a b s t r a c t
In this research, binary 4TaC -HfC based composites were consolidated using carbide materials and addition of 0 -15 vol.% MoSi2 by means of spark plasma sintering at 2000 °C. The nearly full dense and monophase specimens were fabricated with a relative density value higher than 99%. Mechanical tests revealed values of 18 -19 GPa and 4 -4.3 MPa·m 1/2 , for average Vickers hardness and fracture toughness of the composites, respectively. Analysis of linear shrinkage during densi /uniFB01 cation revealed that MoSi2 addition increased densi /uniFB01 cation rate and decreased the time required to reach full density at 2000 °C. It is proposed that at the intermediate stage of sintering, mass transfer can be accelerated by formation of a silicide based liquid phase and viscous /uniFB02 ow mechanisms. The formation of binary 4TaC -HfC solid solution phase enhanced the densi /uniFB01 cation process at the /uniFB01 nal stage of sintering.
© 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
have been increased to obtain full dense TaC bodies with enhanced mechanical properties and tailored /uniFB01 ne grain microstructures. In SPS processing of TaC composites, the application of different materials like metallic tantalum powder [13], B4C [14], nano-B4C [15], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [16], graphene [17] and silicon carbide [18] were reported as sintering aid in the literature. Silicides of tantalum and molybdenum (TaSi2/MoSi2) are commonly considered as sintering aids in liquid phase assisted sintering of various types of UHTC borides and carbides [2 -6]. However, degradation and vaporization of Si from silicides were reported at high temperatures and hard vacuum conditions [19], which may lead to Si content loss during sintering [3].

question: According to Goyer and Tallon 2023, what is the dielectric constant (ε3 in F·m⁻¹) for tetrahydrofuran (THF) used in UHTC suspension studies?
answer: The dielectric constant (ε3) for tetrahydrofuran (THF) used in UHTC suspension studies is 36.7 [2].

and x1, x2 are the mole fractions of solvents , V1 and V2 are the molar volumes of the solvents, and P1 and P2 are the molar polarizations of the solvents. As mentioned, DMF and CYC were not used as co-solvents since their differences in polarity lend them to be immiscible. Therefore, the data points labeled CYC are the CYC-THF suspensions and those labeled DMF are the DMF-THF suspensions (Fig. 9). There is no clear relationship between the values of combined dielectric constant and particle packing, which indicates that dielectric constant alone cannot predict suspension stability.
Organic  solvents  have  several  benefits  over  water  for  preparing UHTC  suspensions,  including  lower  attractive  forces  that  lead  to agglomeration as well as lowering the potential tendency of the UHTC particles to oxidize. Models of interaction potential help predict solvents performance in a suspension. However, dielectric constant of the solvent alone  is  not  a  suitable  predictor  of  the  suspension  stability,  as  the functional groups in the dispersant chain and anchor must also perform well to produce a uniform and well-dispersed suspension. While it was described by Pugh and Bergstrom that a solvent with a similar dielectric constant to the powder will minimize attractive forces [1], the results of our work with these four solvents do not necessarily affirm this. The results  presented here emphasize the importance of quantification of dispersant adsorption to more accurately predict performance. However, this parameter, along with other factors such as evaporation rate, confirm that there is never a perfect substitute for empirical data to compare and contrast suspension performance as was done with the rheology and particle packing described within this work. Additionally, the interaction potential model does not have the capacity to account for the effects of other additives that may have incidental surface activity GLYPH<0>'GLYPH<0>b GLYPH<0>5GLYPH<0>Δ

5.4. Combined dielectric constant
Pugh and Bergstrom described a simple method of creating a welldispersed suspension in organic solvents is to choose a solvent with a similar dielectric constant to the powder in order to mask the Van der Waals interaction [1]. This was demonstrated by taking the derivative of the  Hamaker  constant  (Equation  (2))  with  respect  to  the  dielectric constant of the powder. A lower Hamaker constant results in a lower Van der Waals interaction. This derivation results in the expression

This  gives  rise  to  the  conclusion  that  there  is  a  unique minimum for the Van der Waals attractive force when the dielectric constant  of  the  powder  and  the  solvent  are  the  same.  The  dielectric constants of CYC, DMF, and THF are 2, 7.58, and 36.7, respectively and the dielectric constant of the ZrB2 powder is 10. Therefore, it would be expected that suspensions prepared with THF should be more stable than those prepared with CYC. However, this is not the case, so it is likely that this effect does not significantly hinder the Van der Waals forces to prevent agglomeration under the described conditions. But to further  investigate  the  relationship  between  dielectric  constant  and suspension  stability,  particle  packing  was  compared  to  a  combined dielectric constant Dm  for non-ideal mixing of CYC and THF as well as THF and  DMF  is  calculated  for  each  of  the  solvent  ratios  using  the relationship cited in Ref. A B S T R A C T
Suspensions of Ultra-High Temperature Ceramic (UHTC) powder with various organic solvents and co-solvent mixtures were prepared with the intention of verifying and understanding the relationship between interparticle  interactions,  flow  behavior,  and  resulting  particle  packing.  These  relationships  can  be  used  to  predict optimal solvent choices in colloidal processing of UHTCs to guarantee suspension stability through an adequate balance  of  the  interparticle  forces  in  suspension.  Four  organic  solvents  were  selected:  cyclohexane  (CYC), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone (ACE) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The theoretical performance of each suspension, based on interparticle interaction energies, was compared to the empirical results. Detailed flow behavior was studied through rheological measurements and particle packing was measured by Archimedes ' method.  The  relationship  between  interparticle  forces,  viscosity  and  particle  packing  is  discussed.  Other contributing factors such as the performance of the dispersing agent were also considered and surface characteristics were reported using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), providing a modification to the adsorption parameter in the repulsive model. Low viscosity slurries were obtained at approximately 40% solid loading. CYC and THF demonstrated the ability to be effective co-solvents and produce the highest suspension stability with 100% CYC. These findings will help to produce UHTC complex shape component with minimal flaws during processing that can withstand extreme, high temperature oxidizing environments after densification.
compromised mechanical  properties  [5].  It  is  expected  that  creating leading edges with UHTC materials can promote high thermal conductivity that will result in re-radiation of surface heat, dealing with larger heat  fluxes,  effectively  protecting  inner  components  with  minimal maintenance between flights [2]. However, the manufacturing of UHTC complex shapes (as monoliths or as composites with carbon preforms or with carbon, silicon carbide fibers) remains a challenge [6].

6. Conclusion
Stable concentrated suspensions of 40%vol were produced with CYC, DMF and THF, and the modeling of interparticle forces between the powder and solvent  was  a  good  indication  of  the  capability  of  each solvent to produce such suspensions, with the condition that an estimate of  adsorption of the dispersant is available. The high interaction potential of CYC and DMF were largely consistent with low viscosity suspensions that resulted in high  density green bodies, while the lower interaction potential was consistent with the behavior of the ACE suspension due to its low adsorption fraction. The suspensions in all cases had exhibiting shear-thinning behavior. The relationship between viscosity and green density matched the expected result that a lower viscosity resulted in a higher green density in the cases of pure solvents as well  as  combinations  of  THF  and  CYC  as  co-solvents  [75:25,  50:50, 25:75]. Matching the dielectric constant of the solvent to that of the powder did not necessarily lead to more stable suspensions. However, the  use  of  co-solvent  mixtures  and  other  organic  solvents  are  key  to develop new colloidal technologies suitable for UHTCs.

A B S T R A C T
Four solvents will be used in this study: cyclohexane (CYC), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone (ACE) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). These solvents have been selected: i) to afford a spectrum of dielectric constants  (polarity),  which  will  vary  the  electrostatic  interaction  between particles and solvent (described by the Van der Waals attractive potential), and also allow for an examination of interaction between the dispersant and the particles in varied solvent conditions (described by the steric repulsive potential); ii) they are known to be good solvents for most polymers and organic molecules used in advanced manufacturing. The objective of this work is to provide a better understanding of the usefulness as well as the shortcomings of interparticle force models as they  relate  to  colloidal  processing  of  ceramics,  especially  looking  at which parameters remain unable to be well-modeled without empirical data.  This  will  be  carried  out  by  studying  the  relationship  between particle interaction potential, rheological behaviour and particle packing, for each of these solvents (and solvent mixtures) using ZrB2  suspensions  as  a  model  system.  This  is  done  by  comparing  analytical calculations  of  the  interparticle  forces  between  particles  with  actual suspensions prepared with each solvent and solvent mixtures.

5.4. Combined dielectric constant
Fig. 9. Green density of slip cast bodies as a function of dielectric constant in CYC-THF and DMF-THF mixtures. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
J. Goyer and C. Tallon
such  as  gelling  agents,  binders,  and  plasticizers.  Lastly,  from  the manufacturing and processing point of view, it is generally favorable to use a solvent that does not have an excessively high vapor pressure (such as that of THF, which is 173 mmHg at room temperature) to minimize solvent losses during processing that can lead to inconsistency in the solid loading of a suspension. Depending on the complexity and time scale of processing, this can be a dominating factor over the interaction potential.  Overall,  while  the  choice  of  a  solvent  with  high  repulsive interaction  potential  can  be  promising  in  producing  a  well-dispersed suspension,  it  is  merely  a  starting  point  and  the  need  for  empirical data  will  become  stronger  as  the  experiments  gain  complexity  in formulation and processing.

A B S T R A C T
Suspensions of Ultra-High Temperature Ceramic (UHTC) powder with various organic solvents and co-solvent mixtures were prepared with the intention of verifying and understanding the relationship between interparticle  interactions,  flow  behavior,  and  resulting  particle  packing.  These  relationships  can  be  used  to  predict optimal solvent choices in colloidal processing of UHTCs to guarantee suspension stability through an adequate balance  of  the  interparticle  forces  in  suspension.  Four  organic  solvents  were  selected:  cyclohexane  (CYC), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone (ACE) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The theoretical performance of each suspension, based on interparticle interaction energies, was compared to the empirical results. Detailed flow behavior was studied through rheological measurements and particle packing was measured by Archimedes ' method.  The  relationship  between  interparticle  forces,  viscosity  and  particle  packing  is  discussed.  Other contributing factors such as the performance of the dispersing agent were also considered and surface characteristics were reported using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), providing a modification to the adsorption parameter in the repulsive model. Low viscosity slurries were obtained at approximately 40% solid loading. CYC and THF demonstrated the ability to be effective co-solvents and produce the highest suspension stability with 100% CYC. These findings will help to produce UHTC complex shape component with minimal flaws during processing that can withstand extreme, high temperature oxidizing environments after densification.

It  is  expected  that  creating leading edges with UHTC materials can promote high thermal conductivity that will result in re-radiation of surface heat, dealing with larger heat  fluxes,  effectively  protecting  inner  components  with  minimal maintenance between flights [2]. However, the manufacturing of UHTC complex shapes (as monoliths or as composites with carbon preforms or with carbon, silicon carbide fibers) remains a challenge [6].

Colloidal Processing of Complex-Shaped ZrB2-Based Ultra-High-Temperature Ceramics: Progress and Prospects
Guoqian Liu 1 , Changhai Yan 1 and Hua Jin 2, *
- 1 Science and Technology on Space Physics Laboratory, China Academy of Launch Vehicle Technology, Beijing 100076, China; 15201631565@163.com (G.L.); yanchanghai@163.com (C.Y.)
- 2 School of Aerospace Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China
* Correspondence: huaj@xmu.edu.cn
Abstract: Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), such as ZrB2-based ceramics, are the most promising candidates for ultra-high-temperature applications. Due to their strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion, ZrB2-based UHTCs are always hot-pressed at temperatures above 1800 ◦ C. However, the hot-pressing technique typically produces disks or cylindrical objects limiting to relatively simple geometrical and moderate sizes. Fabrication of complex-shaped ZrB2-based UHTC components requires colloidal techniques. This study reviews the suspension dispersion and colloidal processing of ZrB2-based UHTCs. The most important issues during the colloidal processing of ZrB2-based UHTCs are summarized, and an evaluation of colloidal processing methods of the ZrB2based UHTCs is provided. Gel-casting, a net or near-net colloidal processing technique, is believed to exhibit a great potential for the large-scale industrialization of ZrB2 -based UHTCs. In addition, additive manufacturing, also known as 3D printing, which has been drawing great attention recently, has a great potential in the manufacturing of ZrB2 -based UHTC components in the future.
Keywords: ultra-high-temperature ceramics; complex shapes; colloidal processing; additive manufacturing

A B S T R A C T
Four solvents will be used in this study: cyclohexane (CYC), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone (ACE) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). These solvents have been selected: i) to afford a spectrum of dielectric constants  (polarity),  which  will  vary  the  electrostatic  interaction  between particles and solvent (described by the Van der Waals attractive potential), and also allow for an examination of interaction between the dispersant and the particles in varied solvent conditions (described by the steric repulsive potential); ii) they are known to be good solvents for most polymers and organic molecules used in advanced manufacturing. The objective of this work is to provide a better understanding of the usefulness as well as the shortcomings of interparticle force models as they  relate  to  colloidal  processing  of  ceramics,  especially  looking  at which parameters remain unable to be well-modeled without empirical data.  This  will  be  carried  out  by  studying  the  relationship  between particle interaction potential, rheological behaviour and particle packing, for each of these solvents (and solvent mixtures) using ZrB2  suspensions  as  a  model  system.  This  is  done  by  comparing  analytical calculations  of  the  interparticle  forces  between  particles  with  actual suspensions prepared with each solvent and solvent mixtures.

5.4  SUMMARY, RECOMMENDATIONS, AND PATH FORWARD
Colloidal powder processing was an effective approach to manufacture complex-shaped UHTC components. Colloidal processing enabled enhanced reliability by controlling the interparticle forces between particles, removing flaws, and improving the particle packing in the green body. This resulted in a reduced temperature for densification and allowed for PS of as-shaped complex components.
Careful selection of solvent and processing additives is key to developing well-  dispersed, low-viscosity, high-volume-fraction suspensions required for colloidal processing. To minimize  the  risk  of  oxidation  of  UHTC  powders,  nonaqueous  formulations  were  selected. Further advances will be enabled by the development of polymers/dispersants that produce strong steric repulsion in nonaqueous solvents.
Fine powders (in the submicron-size range) are needed for colloidal processing to obtain all the benefits inherent to this approach in terms of green body homogeneity and improved reliability. In addition, fine powders also play a key role in the reduction of densification temperature due to their stronger driving force for densification.
Finally, it is expected that the development of nonaqueous gelcasting and freeze casting chemistries suitable for UHTCs will broaden the shaping capability of UHTC materials.

6. Conclusion
Stable concentrated suspensions of 40%vol were produced with CYC, DMF and THF, and the modeling of interparticle forces between the powder and solvent  was  a  good  indication  of  the  capability  of  each solvent to produce such suspensions, with the condition that an estimate of  adsorption of the dispersant is available. The high interaction potential of CYC and DMF were largely consistent with low viscosity suspensions that resulted in high  density green bodies, while the lower interaction potential was consistent with the behavior of the ACE suspension due to its low adsorption fraction. The suspensions in all cases had exhibiting shear-thinning behavior. The relationship between viscosity and green density matched the expected result that a lower viscosity resulted in a higher green density in the cases of pure solvents as well  as  combinations  of  THF  and  CYC  as  co-solvents  [75:25,  50:50, 25:75]. Matching the dielectric constant of the solvent to that of the powder did not necessarily lead to more stable suspensions. However, the  use  of  co-solvent  mixtures  and  other  organic  solvents  are  key  to develop new colloidal technologies suitable for UHTCs.

The repulsive potential calculation compares the relative effectiveness of the dispersant in maintaining an energetically unfavorable situation if the particles come close to contacting each other (Fig. 2). This occurs by adsorption of the anchoring group on one end of the block copolymer  dispersant  to  the  surface  of  the  ZrB2 particles,  with  the opposing end extending into solution. For HA70, the anchoring group is amino based while a carboxylic acid chain extends into solution [7]. The high polarity of these functional groups makes them soluble in organic solvents to varying degrees. The mixing or compression of the dispersant chains with one another creates an increase in mixing enthalpy or a loss of  configurational  entropy,  respectively,  which  are  both  thermodynamically unfavorable, and therefore repulsion ensues [1]. These results adopted the assumption from Leo et al. [7] as well as Tanurdjaja et al.

4.1. Interparticle force calculations and adsorption measurement
Calculations of the steric interaction forces depend on the specific structure and size of the dispersant molecule. While the exact chemical structure of commercial dispersant Hypermer A70 is unknown, has been previously characterized as a block copolymer with hydroxyl and amine functional groups, by by Tanurdjaja et al. [26]. The thickness of the steric barrier, or adsorbed layer, as listed in Table 1 was measured using gel permeation chromatography [26]. The interaction potential curves were found not to be significantly affected by changes of ± 1 nm in chain length,  with  results  remaining  within  an  order  of  magnitude  of  the original graph. Flory-Huggings parameter ( χ ) quantifies the interaction between the dispersant and solvent and it is usually considered to be between 0.3 and 0.5 for similar systems. χ was not calculated directly for this  work  and  was  estimated  as  0.35  for  all  the  solvents.  If  the Flory-Huggins parameter χ is changed from 0.35 to 0.4 for DMF and ACE (as increasing χ indicates decreasing solvent quality for a given polymer),  the  relative  strength  of  repulsive  interaction  potential  did  not change for these solvents. The interaction between the dispersant and the powder in each solvent was determined by the adsorption parameter (AP). The AP has a significant effect on the repulsive potential, with variation of an order of magnitude if the surface coverage estimate is changed from 70% to 30% (which is within the range of values calculated for the chosen solvents). For this reason, XPS data was gathered to quantify  the  amount  of  adsorption  of  the  dispersant  on  the  powder surface in each of the organic solvents to input into the model. Small variations from the XPS measurements and the subsequent calculations to estimate AP were shown to have a moderate effect in the overall value of  the  interparticle  forces,  but  not  necessarily  in  the  trends  already described for each of the solvents. Bergstrom et al. reported that slight variations  in φ 2 (volume fraction of chains in the adsorbed layer), χ (Flory-Huggins parameter), or v1 (partial molar volume of the solvent) did

5.1. Dispersant solvency conditions
affects the accuracy of the model because the width of the absorbed layer is no longer equal to the chain length of the dispersant. The solvent quality  for  a  given  polymer,  which  allows  for  extension  when  it  is adsorbed  on  a  powder  surface  is  quantified  by  the  Flory-Huggins parameter, χ . For a given molecule in a solvent, previous works have reported that slight changes to the Flory-Huggins parameter χ in steric interaction  calculations  do  not  significantly  impact  the  interparticle interaction curve [7]. But as this parameter is a measurement of solvent quality for a given polymer molecule, this variation with different solvents may be significant enough to cause differences in the graph. Previous  works  with HA70 in both dodecane and cyclohexane used the value of χ = 0.35. However, in more polar solvents such as ACE and DMF, it is difficult to calculate this value without employing molecular dynamics  simulations,  in  addition  to  having  the  precise  molecular structure of HA70 [7,26]. This illustrates the complexity of the steric repulsion  model  and  the  difficulty  of  measuring  every  aspect  of solvent-powder-dispersant interaction to create an accurate prediction of colloidal behavior.

[26] that about 70% of the surface would be covered by the dispersant. However, the degree of adsorption of the dispersant to the surfaces of the particles was found to be very different in each of the organic solvents, which has a pronounced effect on the degree of repulsion. X-ray photoelectron  spectroscopy  (XPS)  yielded  results  that  allowed  an approximation of the fraction of dispersant chains on the powder surface. The specific adsorption of dispersant on the surface of the particle contributes to an increase of the carbon percentage of the surface due to the carbon-based structure of the dispersant. Therefore, the adsorption parameter used to factor in this effect was calculated by using the atomic percentage of surface carbon. CYC was used as a reference to Leo et al. 7 , because this work estimated 70% surface coverage of HA70 in CYC for an identical slip casting suspension. By setting the atomic percentage of surface carbon in CYC to an absorption parameter of 0.7, the relative adsorption  parameters  for  the  other  solvents  based  on  their  atomic carbon percentages were calculated (Table 3).
Each solvent environment was also compared in terms of the C/Zr ratio at the surface with and without HA70. Fig. 3 shows that adsorption is occurring to some extent in all samples, since there is always a higher carbon  content  after  the  particles  have  been  in  suspension  with  the dispersant rather than the solvent alone. In the case of the as-received powder, it is also clear that carbon is already present on the surface, as  is  common  with  most  UHTC  powders.  However,  consistent  with empirical observations, the calculations performed yield that the highest amount of dispersant adsorption on the particles is occurring in CYC or DMF, while the lowest amount of dispersant adsorption occurs in ACE.

2.3. Characterization
The  rheological  behavior  of  the  ZrB2 suspensions  prepared  in different solvents and solvent mixtures as described in previous section was measured using a rheometer (TA instruments, AR-G2), with a 30 mm diameter bob and 34 mm diameter cup configuration. Measurements were taken over 20 min at shear rates ranging from 0.1 to 1000 s GLYPH<0> 1 , with a measurement taken every minute. Tests were performed at ambient temperature with no pre-shearing. The relevant results show viscosity as a function of shear rate to examine the shear-rate dependent behavior as well as acceptable viscosity limits for slip casting. The green density of the resulting green bodies was determined by employing a modified Archimedes ' method, using a paraffin wax of known density to cover the green bodies prior immersion in the liquid (water in this case) with 3 samples recorded and averaged for each condition.
In order to understand the surface chemistry of the ZrB2 particles in different solvents and examine relative adsorption of the dispersant in each  medium,  X-ray  Photoelectron  Spectroscopy  (XPS)  analysis  was used. While it is more traditional to use Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) for measuring adsorption of a dispersant, resolution concerns as well as insolubility of the dispersant leading to settling during centrifugation gave rise to the use of XPS so that the bonds between carbon and zirconium that are associated with adsorption were more accurately quantified. Suspensions with a solid loading of 10%vol. and 0.82 %wt dispersant with respect to the powder (based on calculations for full surface  coverage  by  the  dispersant,  assuming  each  chain  occupies  a surface area equal to circle with a diameter of the dispersant ' s hydrodynamic diameter) were prepared in each of the four solvents, following the procedure described before. The reasoning for using 3 wt% HA70 in the  standard  suspensions  when  0.82%  should  provide  full  coverage arises from the assertion that HA70 acts as a binder in addition to its primary purpose as a dispersant.

2.3. Characterization
The suspensions were first centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min so that the supernatant could be separated, then the sediment pellet was dried in a humidity-controlled oven for at least 2 h to remove residual solvent. Additionally, four samples that were mixed in the solvent alone for 2 h with no dispersant and then dried were submitted as well as the asreceived  powder  (AR),  .  Submission  of  the  AR  powder  ensures  that any changes to the surface chemistry of the ZrB2 in the solvents that do not  involve  the  organic  dispersant  are  quantified.  The  raw  data  for surface composition was then used to calculate a value for the approximate amount of adsorption. This was done by using the assertion by Leo et al. [7] that 70% adsorption of HA70 occurred in CYC. The atomic percentage of carbon in each other solvent was then normalized to a fraction of this parameter for CYC and that value was considered to be the adsorption parameter in each solvent. Although this does not account for the differences in the surface chemistry in the solvent environment alone, it was decided to be more representative due to the fact that initial carbon at surface sites and adsorption of the dispersant are not mutually exclusive. This parameter was directly used to complete the steric repulsion calculations in each solvent environment.

Abstract
Flexural strengths at room temperature, at 1400 ◦ C in air and at room temperature after 1 h oxidation at 1400 ◦ C were determined for ZrB2and HfB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs). Defects caused by electrical discharge machining (EDM) lowered measured strengths significantly and were used to calculate fracture toughness via a fracture mechanics approach. ZrB2 with 20 vol.% SiC had room temperature strength of 700 ± 90 MPa, fracture toughness of 6.4 ± 0.6 MPa, Vickers hardness at 9.8 N load of 21.1 ± 0.6 GPa, 1400 ◦ C strength of 400 ± 30 MPa and room temperature strength after 1 h oxidation at 1400 ◦ C of 678 ± 15 MPa with an oxide layer thickness of 45 ± 5 𝛍 m. HfB2 with 20 vol.% SiC showed room temperature strength of 620 ± 50 MPa, fracture toughness of 5.0 ± 0.4 MPa, Vickers hardness at 9.8 N load of 27.0 ± 0.6 GPa, 1400 ◦ C strength of 590 ± 150 MPa and room temperature strength after 1 h oxidation at 1400 ◦ C of 660 ± 25 MPa with an oxide layer thickness of 12 ± 1 𝛍 m. 2 wt.% La2O3 addition to UHTCs slightly reduced mechanical performance while increasing tolerance to property degradation after oxidation and effectively aided internal stress relaxation during spark plasma sintering (SPS) cooling, as quantified by X-ray diffraction (XRD). Slow crack growth was suggested as the failure mechanism at high temperatures as a consequence of sharp cracks formation during oxidation. © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

4. Conclusions
ZrB2-SiC ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites with ZrC and carbon fiber additives were fabricated by spark plasma sintering at 1800 °C for 5 min under 30 MPa. The volume ratio of ZrB2: SiC was 4:1, and the summation of ZrC and Cf was 7.5 vol% with different ratios. The sintered samples were characterized by FE-SEM, EDS, densitometry, and XRD analyses. The flexural strength and hardness of assintered samples were also determined. The formation of SiC sheaths around the carbon fibers was revealed by microstructural and thermodynamical investigations. Optimal hardness of 20.8 GPa and flexural strength of 628.4 MPa were obtained by co-addition of 6.5 vol% ZrC and 1 vol% Cf to the ZrB2-SiC UHTCs.

1. Introduction
Characteristics of ZrB2-SiC ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) reinforced with ZrC and carbon fiber (Cf) were investigated in this article. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) process was utilized to fabricate the samples at 1800 °C for 5 min under 30 MPa punch pressure and vacuumed atmosphere. In all samples, the volume ratio of ZrB2: SiC was equal to 4:1, and the summation of ZrC and Cf reinforcements was 7.5 vol% with different ZrC: Cf ratios. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), densitometry, flexural strength, and hardness measurements were employed for characterization of the prepared samples. Microstructural inspection revealed the formation of SiC sheath around the carbon fibers due to several reactions in the surface SiO2 layers existed on the SiC particles. Optimal flexural strength (628.4 MPa) and hardness (20.8 GPa) values were achieved for the sample co-reinforced with 6.5 vol% ZrC and 1 vol% Cf, with a relative density of 97.7%.

question: How does the electrical conductivity of ZrB2-5 vol% graphene composites compare to graphene-free ZrB2 when tested at room temperature?
answer: The electrical conductivity of ZrB2-5 vol% graphene composites is not directly provided in the sources. However, based on the information given, the electrical conductivity of ZrB2 composites with 2 vol% graphene showed a significant increase compared to the reference ZrB2 specimen. Specifically, for the series with graphene parallel to alignment, the measured electrical conductivity was in the range of 3.37*10^4 to 9.12*10^4 μS/m, which is a few orders of magnitude higher than the reference specimen [3][4]. Therefore, it can be inferred that ZrB2-5 vol% graphene composites would likely exhibit even higher electrical conductivity than ZrB2 with 2 vol% graphene.

4. Summary and conclusions
This  work investigates the  fabrication  of  ZrB2-SiC/graphene composites  without  sintering  aids  using  Spark  Plasma  Sintering  (SPS), focusing  on  densification,  mechanical  properties,  and  the  resulting anisotropy from graphene alignment.
Despite sintering curves suggesting full consolidation, relative density  measurements  and  SEM  observations  revealed  the  presence  of porosity,  indicating  that  sintering  activators  might  be  necessary  for ZrB2. Composites with SiC had agglomerates, while those with graphene showed partial decomposition of the graphene due to the high sintering temperature (2000 ◦ C).
Regarding  mechanical  properties,  all  composites  demonstrated higher hardness than the pure ZrB2  reference. The highest values for hardness  (1750  HV10)  and  fracture  toughness  (3.5  MPa  m 1 / 2 )  were achieved for the composite with 40 vol% of SiC. In graphene composites, hardness measured 1400 HV10 perpendicular and 1600 HV10 parallel to  alignment,  while  fracture  toughness  remained  low  (2.1 -2.3  MPa m 1 / 2 ).
All  composites  exhibited  superior  electrical  conductivity  than  the ZrB2 reference.  In  graphene  composites,  a  clear  anisotropy  was observed: electrical conductivity was significantly higher in the direction parallel to the graphene alignment (3.37*10 GLYPH<0> 4 to 9.12*10 GLYPH<0> 4 μ S/m), exceeding the reference sample by several orders of magnitude.

3.1 Diffuse interface model with or without graphene
To identify the underlying mechanisms of temperature dependence and interfacial thermal conductance of graphene/ZrB2 composites, we inspected diffuse interface models with or without graphene at different temperatures in Figure 1. Previous theoretical calculations had illustrated that the Zr-terminated (0001) and (1010) surfaces were thermodynamically more favorable than other Zrterminated counterparts in ZrB 2 . 30 At high temperatures (below the melting point of the buck ZrB 2 ), disordered grain boundaries enhance phonon scattering and then generate an increased thermal resistance (Figure 1A). Graphene layers prepared by the exfoliated method are almost free of defects and grain boundaries. Extensive literature reported that exfoliated graphene layers with atomically thin tend to adhere to the ceramic grain boundary via vdWs force. 19 Given the fact that phonons are the main heat carriers of graphene and ceramic materials, the electronic thermal conductivity can be negligible in graphene and ZrB2 materials. 31 The rough edges of 2D materials, such as graphene, and its interlayers are typi- cally much less sensitive to phonon scattering than temperature fluctuation. 32 The graphene heterostructure with or without defects has almost the same temperature jump ∆ T at the heterogeneous interface. 17 That is, graphene layers with or without intrinsic defects (or crystal boundaries or rough edges) expand nearly the same amount with the same temperature fluctuation. So we dislodge phonon scattering from defect scattering and rough edges of graphene layers. The vdWs force between graphene and ZrB2 ceramic particles produces perturbation of the atomic bonding in the graphene near the ZrB2 /graphene/ZrB2 heterogeneous interface. This short-range vdWs interaction only affects the graphene layers (1 # , 3 # ) adjacent to the ZrB2 grain boundaries, which is consistent with the reported literature.

In  the  case  of  graphene,  for  both  series  the  measured  electrical  conductivity  was  higher  than  for  SiC  composites,  and  clear  differences depending on the direction were also observed. it is a few order of magnitude increase when compared to the reference specimen. The results show a clear anisotropy of electrical conductivity, which  is  a  direct  consequence  of  the  alignment  of  graphene  flakes perpendicular to the pressing direction in the SPS furnace. During the SPS process, the uniaxial pressing causes the graphene flakes, which have  a  high  aspect  ratio,  to  orient  themselves  perpendicular  to  the pressing direction. This creates a texture, that facilitates the easy flow of electrons parallel to the flakes, resulting in higher electrical conductivity. At the same time, in the direction perpendicular to the flakes, current flow is hindered due to the higher resistance of the ceramic matrix, leading to a distinctly lower conductivity. This microstructural alignment is therefore the key mechanism responsible for the anisotropy of electrical properties in the tested composites. This shows that graphene, with its exceptional electrical resistivity, is an ideal material for use in composites where it is important to improve electrical properties and maintain high mechanical properties.

Graphene has an ultrahigh in-plane thermal conductivity of more than 5 × 10 3 W ∙ m -1 ∙ K -1 , which is higher than that of metal and graphite. 2 The concurrent superiority of scalability, large surface-to-volume ratio, and high-temperature stability enables graphene to be the best heat reinforcement of UHTCs. 3 The excellent features of dispersion and assembly further make it easy to be incorporated into the UHTCs matrix. These exotic properties ignite strong interest in accelerating the heat transfer of ZrB 2 composites by constructing external heat-dissipation channels with graphene at ZrB2 grain boundaries. Yet graphene layers were either exfoliated from imperfect graphite powder or grown by chemical vapor deposition. 4 The in-plane thermal conductivity of embedded graphene layers, owing to the phonon Umklapp scattering of a heterogenous interface, decreasing from ∼ 10 2 W ⋅ m -1 ⋅ K -1 at room temperature to ∼ 10 1 W ⋅ m -1 ⋅ K -1 at high temperature. 5 Many worksofliterature have reported that the universal phonon scattering of heterogenous interface and Kapitza thermal resistance of graphene layers cause a reversed correlation between thermal conductance and temperature. 6,7 Interestingly, few researchers have found that the thermal conductivity of ZrB 2 composites with or without graphene reduces the diverse amount with the same temperature fluctuation. 5,8 Therefore, the underlying mechanisms behind the temperature-dependent heat transfer deterioration as the highly anisotropic phonon transport of embedded graphene layers remain elusive.

4.4. Spark Plasma Sintering
In the case of spark plasma sintering (SPS) of ZrB2 ceramics, the effect of processing parameters (sintering time and sintering temperature) and powder particle size (nano- or micron- sized) on the densification has been shown in Fig. 7b [120]. It can be noted that the densification rate is extremely high for the smaller particle size (≤25 nm) and near-theoretical densification of ZrB2 is attained even at very low sintering temperatures (below 1700 °C) and shorter sintering times of 16 min or lesser. The spark plasma sintered di-borides are usually observed to possess fine microstructure and good combination of properties. In order to address some issues related to non-uniform densification (and properties) primarily due to fast heating rate and lesser holding times, recently we had explored the application of multi-stage spark plasma sintering (MSS-SPS) schedule, which involves holding at one or more intermediate temperatures for few minutes before reaching the final sintering temperatures [98,173-179]. Keeping the total holding time constant (i.e. lesser holding time at the final sintering temperature), more uniform densification and finer microstructures were achieved for ZrB2-SiC-TiSi2 [175] and TiB2-TiSi2 composites [98], resulting in still improved mechanical properties (as compared to similar compositions sintered via the usual single-step SPS route).

a b s t r a c t
In this paper spark plasma sintering (SPS) of silicon carbide and multi-walled carbon nanotube reinforced zirconium diboride ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites is reported. Systematic investigations on the effect of reinforcement type (SiC and CNTs) and content (10-40 vol.% SiC and 2-6 vol.% CNTs) on densification behavior, microstructure development, and mechanical properties (microhardness, biaxial flexural strength, and indentation fracture toughness) are presented. With the similar SPS processing parameters (1900 ◦ C, 70 MPa pressure, and 15 min soaking time), near-full densification (>99% relative density) was achieved with 10-40% SiC (in ZrB2-SiC) and 4-6% CNT (in ZrB2-CNT) reinforced composites. The SiC and CNT reinforcement further improved the indentation fracture toughness of the composites through a range of toughening mechanisms, including particle shearing, crack deflection at the particlematrix interface, and grain pull-outs for ZrB2-SiC composites, and CNT pull-outs and crack deflection in ZrB2-CNT composites.

1. Introduction
Spark plasma sintering (SPS) route was employed for preparation of quadruplet ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC -Cf ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMC). Zirconium diboride and silicon carbide powders with a constant ZrB2:SiC volume ratio of 4:1 were selected as the baseline. Mixtures of ZrB2 -SiC were co-reinforced with zirconium carbide (ZrC: 0 -10 vol%) and carbon /uniFB01 ber (Cf: 0 -5 vol%), taking into account a constant ratio of 2:1 for ZrC:Cf components. The sintered composite samples, processed at 1800 °C for 5 min and 30 MPa punch press under vacuumed atmosphere, were characterized by densitometry, /uniFB01 eld emission scanning electron microscopy, energy dispersive spectroscopy, X-ray di /uniFB00 ractometry as well as mechanical tests such as hardness and /uniFB02 exural strength measurements. The results veri /uniFB01 ed that the composite co-reinforced with 5 vol% ZrC and 2.5 vol% Cf had the optimal characteristics, i.e., it reached a relative density of 99.6%, a hardness of 18 GPa and a /uniFB02 exural strength of 565 MPa.

It is established that adding 20 vol% SiC to ZrB2 will result in achievement of optimal high-temperature thermo-mechanical application for ZrB2 -SiC composites [25]. Overally, excellent densi /uniFB01 cation behavior, oxidation and thermal shock resistance, because of the smaller coe /uniFB03 cient of thermal expansion compared to ZrB2 and ZrC, make silicon carbide one of the most important materials for making e /uniFB03 cient composites with ZrB2 [26,27].

4. Conclusions
Spark plasma sintering processing was used to produce dense short /uniFB01 bre-reinforced ceramic composites based on ZrB2 with a carbon /uniFB01 bres content of 45 vol%. The relative density of 96.7%, achieved for samples sintered at 1900 °C, is signi /uniFB01 cantly higher than analogous specimens produced by conventional hot pressing, which achieved only 85% of the theoretical density. Careful control of the sintering parameters, such as the heating rate, maximum temperature and holding time resulted in materials with near full density and no appreciable damage of the carbon /uniFB01 bres.
- It has also been assessed that above a critical temperature (> 1900 °C), set by the eutectic melt met in the ZrB2-SiC-C system, extreme /uniFB01 bre degradation occurs, with associated brittle behaviour of the composite. It has also been observed that slight deviations in the density level have a strong impact on the fracture behaviour.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx. doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2017.05.029.

4. Conclusions
Spark plasma sintering was successfully used to sinter fine-grained ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites to near-full densification (>99% relative density). Detailed analysis of punch displacement during SPS sintering indicated that the densification is favored for the ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composite samples in early stage I (particle re-distribution stage) and final stage III (densification starting at earlier times and at lower temperatures). While the fine grains (<2 𝛍 m) were retained in ZrB2-SiC composites, some grain growth with grain size in the range 5-10 𝛍 m was observed for ZrB2-CNT composites. Raman spectroscopy analysis and high magnification SEM micrographs indicated that the multi-walled CNTs were retained in ZrB2-CNT composite samples sintered at high sintering temperature of 1900 ◦ C. The SiC and CNT reinforcement further improved the indentation fracture toughness of the composites through a range of toughening mechanisms, including particle shearing, crack deflection at the particle-matrix interface, and grain pull-outs for ZrB2-SiC composites, and CNT pull-outs and crack deflection in ZrB2-CNT composites.

3.1. C/C-Hf(Zr, Ta)C composites
TaC is another common modifier used to improve the ablation resistance of C/C composites as SRM materials due to its high melting point of 3880 °C and good mechanical properties at high temperatures. Xiong et al. [28] fabricated C/C-TaC composites by PyC and TaC alternately deposited using TaCl5-C3H6-H2-Ar mixtures as raw materials by a CVI process. The composites with a 14 vol.% TaC content were obtained and exhibited the better ablation resistance than C/C for 120 s at over 2000 K under an oxyacetylene torch [92] . In addition, HfC-TaC co-deposited by CVD on graphite fiber composites for rocket thruster applications have been reported. The thruster with a weight ratio HfC/TaC of 7:3 was measured at NASA Lewis, survived two 5-s tests without failure and maintained an internal pressure of approximately 0.5 MPa [6] . These results highlighted the excellent high temperature oxidation and ablation resistance of the HfC/TaC system by the CVI/D and its suitability as a coating for carbon fiber composites for advanced rocket thrusters because of the difficulty of the infiltration of Hf, Zr and Ta-containing radicals in the technique. However, in our works, the addition of TaC was proven to severely deteriorate the ablation properties of C/C composites in oxyacetylene and liquid rocket engine environments because of the strongly mechanical denudation of the liquid Ta2O5 formed under the combustion gas impact [91] . The inconsistency of the ablation results was possibly caused by the different ablation conditions, particularly by temperatures and oxygen partial pressure.
Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of the ablated C/C-HfC composites.
Fig. 3. SEM images of a C/C-ZrC composite nozzle-throat after SRM ablation.

a b s t r a c t
Conventional ultra-high-temperature ceramic-modified carbon/carbon composites (C/C-UHTCs) face limitations such as excessive erosion in the gas environment of solid rocket motors (SRMs). In contrast, metalreinforced C/C-UHTCs exhibit enhanced mechanical properties, thermophysical characteristics, and resistance to high-temperature erosion. These advantages indicate significant potential for metal-reinforced C/C-UHTCs to augment the thrust capabilities of SRMs. This study demonstrated the preparation of (Hf, Zr, Ti)C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)B2 -W-Cu cermet modified C/C composites using slurry infiltration and reactive melt infiltration at 1400 ° C. The microstructure, formation mechanisms, mechanical properties, and thermophysical properties of C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)B2 -W-Cu composite materials, as well as the ablation behavior of the composite materials under the SRM environment were compared. The results showed that the linear and mass ablation rates of the C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)B2 -W-Cu composite material decreased by 112.1 % and 160.4 % (0.074 mm s -1 , 0.086 g s -1 ), respectively, compared with those of the C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C composite material. This could be attributed to the formation of a dense, high-strength W-skeleton composite (Hf, Zr, Ti)O2 oxide layer during the ablation of the C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)B2 -W-Cu composite, which effectively resisted erosion from high-temperature, high-speed and high-particleconcentration two-phase gas flow.

1. Introduction
With the rapid development of hypersonic reentry vehicles, critical thermal structural components, such as the nozzles of the solid rocket motor or the leading edges of the aircraft, will undergo oxidation and ablation at approximately 2000-3000 °C super-high temperature in the aeronautical and aerospace environment. The material requirements for the critical thermal structural components are very stringent [1,2]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs)-modified C/C composites, such as carbides or borides of Zr, Hf, Ta, Ti, etc., have the advantages of good high temperature mechanical properties and ultra-high temperature ablation resistance, which can effectively meet the severe thermal and mechanical requirements [3,4].
In recent years, the preparation methods of UHTC-modified C/C composites mainly include the chemical vapor infiltration (CVI) [5], poly infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP) [6,7], and reactive melt infiltration (RMI) [8,9]. CVI and PIP have a long preparation time, tedious process, and are very expensive, whereas the RMI technology is cheaper with rapid preparation of near net shape materials, which are suitable for scaled and industrialized development. Recent studies on the preparation of UHTC-modified C/C composites through RMI have shown that it is difficult to infiltrate C/C composites by pure ultra-high temperature metals. Me-Si alloys (Me = Zr, Hf, etc.) with lower melting point are commonly used to infiltrate C/C composites [10,11].
However, the preparation of Me-Si-C ceramics-modified C/C composites through the RMI process still experiences two problems. First,
∗ Corresponding author.
E-mail address:
sunweimse@csu.edu.cn (W. Sun).
Received 29 October 2019; Received in revised form 14 November 2019; Accepted 14 November 2019
Available online 16 November 2019

A B S T R A C T
To mitigate ablation loss in carbon/carbon (C/C) composites under extreme thermal conditions ( > 3000 ℃ ), a novel  analytical  framework  of ' extreme  environment  -  microstructure  evolution  -  ablation  resistance ' was developed via two-way coupled simulation and multi-scale characterization. C/C-MeC-SiC (Me: Hf, Zr, Ti, Ta, Nb, W) composites were prepared by reactive melt infiltration, and the oxide film evolution of the composites under oxyacetylene ablation (4.2 MW/m 2 ) was investigated. HfC and ZrC exhibit excellent ablation resistance by virtue of high melting points and stable oxide films (HfO2/ZrO2), but are porous due to phase transition cracks. TiC/NbC has a highly dense ceramic layer but suffers from severe ablation due to low melting point oxide loss. WC has a loose and porous oxide film due to the volatility of the oxidation products. TaSi2 is difficult to penetrate into the matrix and causes serious ablation loss. The optimized design of C/C-HfC-ZrC-TaC-SiC composites, the mass/linear variation rate were 0.175 mg/s and 0.701 μ m/s after long-time ablation (1000 s, 40 s × 25 cycle), provides theoretical foundations and experimental support for the design of UHTCs modified C/C composites in extreme high-temperature environment.
pivotal approach to overcoming this limitation [6,7].

3.1. C/C-Hf(Zr, Ta)C composites
Although the melting point of ZrC (3540 °C) is close to the sublimation temperature of carbon (3550 °C), it has a much lower density of 6.6 g/cm 3 than TaC (14.5 g/cm 3 ) and HfC (12.7 g/cm 3 ), and is cheaper. Besides, its oxidation-resistant property and high strength and stiffness can also effectively improve the operating performance of C/C in SRM. Shen et al. [68] prepared C/C-ZrC composites by an in-situ reaction process as mentioned above, using ZrOCl2 · 8H2O as raw material, and investigated their ablation behaviors in a small SRM hot-fire testing system. The as-prepared composites were exposed to the combustion gases for 3-4 s at a temperature of 3361-3386 K and a chamber pressure of 10.5-15.8 MPa, as well as to the oxyacetylene flame (about 3273 K for 60 s) for a comparison [98] . During the ablation of the composites, the preferential oxidation of ZrC reduced the carbon consumption due to the lower Gibbs free energy for the heterogeneous reactions between ZrC and oxygen-containing species. The melting of solid ZrO2 and the evaporation of liquid ZrO2 absorbed heat from the combustion gases, which relieved the erosive attack on the carbon. Consequently, the presence of ZrC improved the ablation resistance of C/C composites. However, the linear ablation rate in the SRM with highpressure and high-velocity combustion gases was much higher than that in the oxyacetylene flame. Lots of pits and ZrO2 particles, and cone shaped fibers or irregularly shaped carbon fragments, appeared on the ablation surface of C/C-ZrC composites in the SRM (Fig. 3). Different ablation rates and ablation appearance features were attributed to the different ablation mechanisms.

2. Thermal properties of graphene/ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites
Graphene is a promising conductive material with a carrier mobility of up to 1.5 × 10 4 cm 2 /(V s) and thermal conductivity of up to 5.3 × 10 3 W/(m K) at room temperature, making it an ideal thermoelectric material. Graphene is used to enhance the thermal conductivity of the matrix, accelerate heat loss in the local superheated area, and improve its service life.
Simonenko et al. incorporated 2% graphene into HfB2-30% (volume ratio) SiC, and its thermal conductivity increased from 113 to 164 W/(m K), which is an increase of 45%. 53 Cheng et al. found that in a ZrB2-SiC/graphene composite system, the thermal diffusion coefficient, thermal conductivity, and specific heat coefficient significantly improved after the addition of graphene. 54 Excessive addition not only causes the thermal conductivity of the material to decrease, mainly because the graphene itself will aggregate, resulting in a decrease in thermal conductivity, but also causes the grain size of the ceramic matrix to decrease and the porosity to increase.
Graphene is an excellent thermal conductivity interface that can form a good thermal conductivity interface in the ceramic matrix and build an efficient 3D heat transfer channel, thereby improving thermal conductivity. In addition, the introduction of graphene provides a more efficient method for phonon transport, thus improving thermal conductivity.

7.2.1.  Thermogravimetric analysis and wide-temperature-range oxidation

[98] investigated the effect of volume ratios between HfB2 and TaB2 on the oxidation resistance of HfB2-MoSi2-TaB2 coatings. Over a temperature range of 300-1700 °C, the optimized coating containing 15 vol% TaB2 extended the initial oxidation temperature from 300 °C to 500 °C, reducing dynamic oxidation loss by 75.85% compared to the 60HfB2-40MoSi2 coating. Zhu et al. [188] densified a porous SiC-ZrSi2 pre-coating  using  in-situ  generated  SiO2 glass,  producing  a  gradient composite coating with a dense outer layer and a microporous intermediate layer. During dynamic oxidation testing from 573 K to 1773 K, the gradient coating demonstrated enhanced mass retention, improving from 62.9% (pure SiC coating) to 97.94%.
Fig.  36. Oxidation  curve  of  SiC/glaze  precursor-coated  samples  in  air  from room temperature to 1600 °C. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [184], © Elsevier 2017.
The  existing  thermogravimetric  and  wide  temperature  range  oxidation tests have verified the nonlinear relationship between the selfhealing ability of coating oxide films under temperature gradients and temperature. However, the linear temperature change mode is difficult to  reproduce  the  transient  competition  mechanism  of  thermal  shock and oxidation coupling during space reentry, which limits the accuracy of  predicting  the  oxygen  diffusion  path  of  coatings  under  extreme thermal gradients.

3.2. Thermal and electrical properties
Fig. 7(b) shows that the overall thermal conductivity of all composites decreased with increasing temperature. Previous literature on ZrB2-based composites has also reported similar temperature dependence of thermal conductivity [5,6,29,30,43]. Due to the presence of strong covalent and ionic bonding, the thermal conductivity of B4C and SiC is mainly controlled by the interaction of phonons with other phonons, defects, and grain boundaries. As temperature increases, thermal conductivity decreases due to increased phonon scattering [5,6]. For ZrB2, the thermal and electrical conductivity are mainly governed by electronic conduction [44]. As temperature increases, conductivity decreases due to the scattering of conducting electrons by lattice vibrations [45].
Fig. 7(c) presents the variation of specific heat capacity with temperature for all compositions. The results suggest a consistent trend of increasing specific heat capacity with B4C volume fraction, aligning well with findings in the published literature [6,30]. The results reveal that the specific heat capacity of the bottom-most layer was the highest, which has practical implications for in-service conditions. When heat is transferred from the upper layers to this bottom layer, its temperature rise remains comparatively low, thereby protecting the metallic structure directly underneath the FGM. Although the overall specific heat capacity values of the FGMs were almost similar to their corresponding equivalent non-graded compositions, the FGM design has the advantage of allowing a layerwise variation in the specific heat capacity, not achievable in non-graded composites.

A B S T R A C T
The most crucial goal is the improvement of Ultra High Temperature Ceramic (UHTC) fracture toughness without negatively impacting the ceramic high-temperature properties. Carbon nanostructures in UHTCs appear to be one of the most promising ways forward. The influence of reduced graphene oxide vs. carbon black on microstructure and properties of TiB2-SiC ceramics is studied. The composite ceramic samples with additive contents up to 7.4 wt% were manufactured via the reactive hot pressing of B4C-TiC-Si-reduced graphene oxide/carbon black precursors at 1850 ◦ С and 30 MPa for 4 min. The sintered materials composition and structure were analysed  with  Raman  spectroscopy,  XRD,  SEM,  and  TEM.  Reduced  graphene  oxide  (rGO)  survives  the  hotpressing procedure and, at lower concentrations, distributes inside the TiB2-SiC matrix in the form of separate sheets. However, at 7.4 wt%, the rGO was shown to agglomerate. The rGO improved the hardness, fracture toughness, and thermal conductivity of the TiB2-SiC by up to 16%, 20%, and 53%, respectively. The composite characteristics reached the values of HV = 26.1 GPa, K1 C = 7.4 MPa · m 1/2 , and k = 69 W/m · K. Carbon black additive formed roughly equiaxial two micrometre inclusions and was not observed to impact the investigated properties.

[10] showed an enhancement of SiC high-temperature tribological properties with an addition  of  20%  of  TiB2.  Sintering  to  high  density  is  still  very  challenging. We previously showed that reactive hot pressing produces high composite density at lowered temperatures and shorter processing times (see references below).

3.3. Characterization of sintered materials
There  are  a  number  of  papers  presenting  research  on  graphene ' s influence on TiB2-SiC [31,33,38,39]. Koval ˇ cíkov ´ a et al. [33] presented the mechanical properties of TiB2-SiC-based materials containing 1, 2, 5, and 10 wt% of graphene. TiB2-SiC matrix in [33] had slightly higher hardness and lower toughness. The toughness dependence on graphene content dependence presented in Fig. 10 for comparison is similar to that for rGO in the current work. In contradiction, the material hardness in [33] showed a monotonical decrease resembling carbon black-containing composites.
Fig. 7. SEM micrographs of the fracture surfaces of TiB2-SiC-based composites: a) reference (TS00); b,d) 3.8 wt% rGO (TSG03); c,e) 3.8 wt% carbon black (TSC03). The marked red areas in b) and c) are shown in d) and e), respectively. Carbon sheets are indicated with red arrows.
Fig. 8. SEM micrograph of the fracture surface of the 7.4 wt% rGO composite (TSG04), showing rGO agglomerates.
The carbon black presence seems to have minimal impact on the thermal conductivity of the TiB2-SiC matrix (see Fig. 11). The rGO additive, however, leads up to a 50% thermal conductivity coefficient rise at  3.8 wt%  as  a  peak  value.  At  higher  rGO  concentrations,  thermal conductivity slumps. The latter, again, could be attributed to the rGO sheet agglomeration at higher carbon contents.

1. Introduction
Although finding an appropriate composition is one of the most critical challenges for achieving the best relative density, hardness, and flexural strength, selecting a proper sintering process and its parameters is another essential issue. ZrB2-20 vol% SiC has been studied as a base composition with a ZrB2: SiC volume ratio of 4:1 and a ZrC: Cf volume ratio of 2:1. Optimum densification and mechanical characteristics, 99.6% relative density, 18.1 GPa hardness and 564.5 MPa flexural strength, have been reported for ZrB2-18.5 vol% SiC-5.0 vol% ZrC-2.5 vol% Cf composite [26]. Among several sintering processes such as spark plasma sintering (SPS) [27-33], pressureless sintering (PS) [34], hot pressing (HP) [35-37], and reactive hot pressing (RHP) [38,39], samples produced by the SPS process were found to have relatively higher mechanical characteristics [40-45]. Because of strong bonding in carbides and borides, it is difficult to achieve superb sintering characteristics by conventional sintering processes [46-50]. 2.3.2. Boride UHTCMCs of Cf/C e SiC-UHTC type
The ablation behavior of Cf/C e SiC e ZrB2 composites with a leading edge shape was also investigated in the combustion environment of a simulated solid rocket motor (SRM) [159]. The results demonstrated that the introduction of SiC e ZrB2 ceramic phases into the matrix resulted in a 26% reduction in linear ablation rate and a 45% reduction in volume ablation rate, respectively. Therefore, the resistance to chemical ablation in SRM could be signi /uniFB01 -cantly enhanced due to the low reactivity of ceramic phases. However, cracks were observed on the surface of the Cf/C e SiC e ZrB2 composites due to the mismatch in the CTE of different components. Furthermore, the active oxidation of SiC and the formation of a porous ZrO2 layer were unable to resist the exposure of chemical ablation at ultra-high temperatures. The synergistic process of thermochemical ablation and mechanical erosion in a high-speed, two-phase /uniFB02 ow with a high concentration of particles in the SRM plume environment resulted in a signi /uniFB01 cant amount of leading edge entrainment [159].
Composites of Cf/C e SiC-UHTC type based on HfB2 are also intensively being investigated [162,164,165]. Ablation behavior and thermal retardant ability of Cf/C e HfB2 e SiC composites were studied using oxyacetylene torch with a heat /uniFB02 ux of 2.38 MW/m 2 for 90 s [164]. The increase in ablation resistance was mainly attributed to the formation of a mixed layer of hafnium dioxide and borosilicate glass, which could act as an effective barrier against /uniFB02 ame exposure. The main possible reactions during ablation were (2), (9 e 18), (21), (26), (27), (38) and (39) [162,164,165]:

a b s t r a c t
Conventional ultra-high-temperature ceramic-modified carbon/carbon composites (C/C-UHTCs) face limitations such as excessive erosion in the gas environment of solid rocket motors (SRMs). In contrast, metalreinforced C/C-UHTCs exhibit enhanced mechanical properties, thermophysical characteristics, and resistance to high-temperature erosion. These advantages indicate significant potential for metal-reinforced C/C-UHTCs to augment the thrust capabilities of SRMs. This study demonstrated the preparation of (Hf, Zr, Ti)C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)B2 -W-Cu cermet modified C/C composites using slurry infiltration and reactive melt infiltration at 1400 ° C. The microstructure, formation mechanisms, mechanical properties, and thermophysical properties of C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)B2 -W-Cu composite materials, as well as the ablation behavior of the composite materials under the SRM environment were compared. The results showed that the linear and mass ablation rates of the C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)B2 -W-Cu composite material decreased by 112.1 % and 160.4 % (0.074 mm s -1 , 0.086 g s -1 ), respectively, compared with those of the C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C composite material. This could be attributed to the formation of a dense, high-strength W-skeleton composite (Hf, Zr, Ti)O2 oxide layer during the ablation of the C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)B2 -W-Cu composite, which effectively resisted erosion from high-temperature, high-speed and high-particleconcentration two-phase gas flow.

3.1. C/C-Hf(Zr, Ta)C composites
TaC is another common modifier used to improve the ablation resistance of C/C composites as SRM materials due to its high melting point of 3880 °C and good mechanical properties at high temperatures. Xiong et al. [28] fabricated C/C-TaC composites by PyC and TaC alternately deposited using TaCl5-C3H6-H2-Ar mixtures as raw materials by a CVI process. The composites with a 14 vol.% TaC content were obtained and exhibited the better ablation resistance than C/C for 120 s at over 2000 K under an oxyacetylene torch [92] . In addition, HfC-TaC co-deposited by CVD on graphite fiber composites for rocket thruster applications have been reported. The thruster with a weight ratio HfC/TaC of 7:3 was measured at NASA Lewis, survived two 5-s tests without failure and maintained an internal pressure of approximately 0.5 MPa [6] . These results highlighted the excellent high temperature oxidation and ablation resistance of the HfC/TaC system by the CVI/D and its suitability as a coating for carbon fiber composites for advanced rocket thrusters because of the difficulty of the infiltration of Hf, Zr and Ta-containing radicals in the technique. However, in our works, the addition of TaC was proven to severely deteriorate the ablation properties of C/C composites in oxyacetylene and liquid rocket engine environments because of the strongly mechanical denudation of the liquid Ta2O5 formed under the combustion gas impact [91] . The inconsistency of the ablation results was possibly caused by the different ablation conditions, particularly by temperatures and oxygen partial pressure.
Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of the ablated C/C-HfC composites.
Fig. 3. SEM images of a C/C-ZrC composite nozzle-throat after SRM ablation.

4.2. Oxidation and ablation of thermal protection materials
C/C composites are attractive materials for use in applications such as reentry vehicle nose tips, rocket nozzles, and hypersonic wing leading edges. In an inert atmosphere or in vacuum, C/C composites retain their strength, modulus, and other mechanical properties to temperatures higher than those tolerated by other materials [2] . However, the most important high temperature structural applications for C/C composites occurred in oxidizing environments, and the oxidation resistance is a major issue for this kind of the materials. Ceramics (e.g., SiC, ZrC, HfC, ZrB 2 and HfB 2 , etc.) were conventionally introduced into C/C composites to improve their oxidation and ablation resistance. C/C and graphite have almost the same thermal response and their oxidation and ablation rates strongly depend on the relative density irrespective of molecular structure, implying that the testing and optimization of C/C and C/C-based thermal protection materials can be based on graphite and graphite-based thermal protection materials from the viewpoints of thermal response, oxidation and ablation, respectively, since the latter with any composition can be easily prepared at low cost and with a little time. This could provide a powerful strategy to obtain the optimized composition of C/C-based materials quickly for a given service environment at low cost without the need to prepare and test C/C-based materials themselves, instead relying on graphite-based materials. Generally, these materials used as thermal structures should withstand mechanical load beside high temperature oxidation and ablation, and thereby C/C-based materials might be excellent candidates compared to graphite-based materials in a variety of high temperature applications.
Fig. 28. Video images of GSZ during testing under condition 2.
Fig. 29. (a) SEM micrographs of GSZ in the center, and (b) edge regions after test under condition 2 where the outside oxide scale was flaked off during the cooling process.

2.2.1. Carbide UHTCMCs of Cf/C e UHTC type
Li et al. [108] examined the in /uniFB02 uence of /uniFB01 re test parameters on the ablation resistance of Cf/C e ZrC composites. When the heat /uniFB02 ux was 2.38 MW/m 2 , the composites exhibited excellent ablation resistance. A ZrO2 layer formed on the ablated surface partially isolated the heat /uniFB02 ux, thereby retarding the penetration of oxygen into the inner material. As the heat /uniFB02 ux increased to 4.18 MW/m 2 , the ablation resistance of Cf/C e ZrC composites decreased, primarily due to the spalling of the ZrO2 layer as the temperature reached 2500 /C14 C. This resulted in the inability of the ZrO2 layer to provide an effective protection to the substrate [108].
The presence of ZrC improves the ablation resistance of Cf/C composites in an oxyacetylene /uniFB02 ame [56,107 e 110,114 e 117,119,120], under laser ablation conditions [97,110,112], in a solid rocket motor environment [111], in a plasma torch environment [113,118,121]. Thermochemical ablation (chemical erosion) is the main determinant of the ablation properties of the composites in the oxyacetylene /uniFB02 ame [107,119], while mechanical breakage (mechanical denudation) is an important ablation mechanism for the composites under the solid rocket motor environment [111]. The ablation of Cf/C e ZrC composites in a nitrogen plasma torch is primarily determined by chemical erosion, mechanical denudation, and carbon sublimation. However, carbon sublimation plays a relatively minor role in the ablation of the composites [121].
