A B S T R A C T
Although the developed system has prominent microstructure and mechanical properties, there is still a considerable room for improvement, when compared to pure B4C. In order to decrease the gap between those materials, more advanced techniques have to be used. One of them is  spark  plasma  sintering,  was  proved  as  a  very  useful  technique  for synthesis and sintering of advanced metal and ceramic matrix composites [15 -18]. It does not only lead to improved sinterability or reduced sintering temperature, but also allows to endow materials with very high relative density.
The aim of this study was to obtain TiB2-SiC UTHCs via spark plasma sintering in order to improve the microstructure and mechanical properties and further reduce the sintering temperatures. Extensive studies of the phase composition and microstructure determined the effectiveness of  sintering  process  at  lower  temperatures.  Moreover,  the  Young ' s modulus,  Vickers  hardness,  and  fracture  toughness  of  the  prepared materials have been studied. The results and conclusions were compared to the TiB2-SiC and B4C-TiB2-SiC systems previously obtained via reactive sintering.

a b s t r a c t
Spark plasma sintering of TiB2-boron ceramics using commercially available raw powders is reported. The B4C phase developed during reaction-driven consolidation at 1900 ◦ C. The newly formed grains were located at the grain junctions and the triple point of TiB2 grains, forming a covalent and stiff skeleton of B4C. The flexural strength of the TiB2-10 wt.% boron ceramic composites reached 910 MPa at room temperature and 1105 MPa at 1600 ◦ C. Which is the highest strength reported for non-oxide ceramics at 1600 ◦ C. This was followed by a rapid decrease at 1800 ◦ C to 480-620 MPa, which was confirmed by increased number of cavitated titanium diboride grains observed after flexural strength tests.
© 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
This class of TiB2-based composites is naturally restricted to TiB2-AlN [8,9], TiB2-SiC [10,11] and B4C-TiB2 [12-17] ceramic composites, which show high room-temperature strength and toughness. High-temperature performance of these TiB2-based composite is scarcely reported and is limited to the B4C-TiB2 eutectic composites [14-17].
The B4C-TiB2 eutectic composites are known to have a complex preparation route, and have a certain size limitation. Therefore, it was an objective of this study to propose a simple route for manufacturing TiB2-B4C composites, that is by the reaction spark plasma sintering [18-20] using commercially available powders of TiB2 and amorphous boron. A reactive consolidation approach was used in order to ensure homogeneous distribution of secondary phase, which is formed in situ during spark plasma sintering. In the framework of this study, characterization of high-temperature mechanical properties was performed using the three-point flexural strength tests at temperatures between 25 ◦ C and 1800 ◦ C.

Even  with  advanced ceramic processing techniques, the consolidation of UHTC particles is difficult due to strong atomic bonding and low diffusion characteristics. Competition  between  densifying  and  coarsening  mechanisms  during processing  makes  high  densities  not  easily  obtainable  even  with pressure-assisted techniques ( i.e. spark plasma sintering or hot isostatic pressing).  Geometric  complexity  is  often  limited  to  two-dimensional plasma sprayed coatings on metallic substrates or to simple axially-symmetric  shapes  (like  cylinders  or  tiles)  when  sufficient densification is achieved [3]. A B S T R A C T
A series of novel ceramics, specifically HfC x N1x (where x = 0.3, 0.4, 0.5, 0.6, 0.7), HfC0.5N0.5-20 vol% SiB6, and HfC0.5N0.5-20 vol% SiC, were successfully synthesized in this study. The ultra-high temperature oxidation behaviors at 1800 ◦ C in air and under low-pressure air conditions were systematically investigated. The oxidation kinetics of the HfC x N1x ceramics basically follow a parabolic law, and the oxidation resistance of the samples are improved with the increasing nitrogen content in HfC x N1x ceramics. Besides, the incorporation of SiB6 and SiC enhanced oxidation resistance by forming molten SiO2, which facilitates the production of HfSiO4  and fills the porous HfO2  oxide scale. However, HfC0.5N0.5-20 vol% SiB6  generates the volatilizable product B2O3, which results in poorer oxidation behavior compared to HfC0.5N0.5-20 vol% SiC. Additionally, the effect of air pressure (5, 10, 20 kPa) on the oxidation behavior of HfC0.5N0.5-20 vol% SiC was examined. Oxidation at low air pressure leads to the active oxidation of SiC, where the formation of gaseous SiO can easily evaporate, causing a gradual degradation of the protective oxide layer.
HEC with at least 4 cationic components [15,16].

B2O3(l) → B2O3(g)
Numerous studies dedicated to the improvement of oxidation resistance of ZrB2 and HfB2 have resulted in the selection of ceramics with 20-30 vol% SiC as optimal and preferred compositions (Tables 14 and 15). The SiC containing ZrB2 ceramic showed thermally stable oxidation rate up to 1500 °C [8,395-398]. The thickness of the oxidized surface layer has been found to be about 100 µm when oxidized over the temperature range of 1200-1400 °C for 5 h. Compared to liquid B2O3 in ZrB2, the borosilicate glass in the SiC-containing ZrB2 ceramic has higher viscosity, higher melting temperature, low oxygen diffusivity and lower vapour pressure; thus providing enhanced protection against oxidation, as compared to the case of monolithic ZrB2 [248]. Fig. 29 shows that ZrB2-SiC also forms stable borosilicate glass layer to protect the material from oxidation. In Fig. 29, it can be seen that the addition of sintering additives (10 vol% Si3N4 and 10 wt% Ta) on the oxidation behaviour of ZrB2-20 vol% SiC. In both cases, the ZrB2 composites consists of 3 layers after isothermal oxidation at 1600 °C for 10 h. In case of ZrB2-20 vol% SiC-10 vol% Si3N4, the oxidised sample composed of dense protective outer SiO2, intermediate ZrO2 +SiO2 layers and unreactive bulk (Fig. 29c). On the other hand, ZrB2-20 vol% SiC10 wt% Ta oxidised sample showed dense protective outer SiO2, intermediate SiC depleted layers along with unreactive bulk (Fig. 29d). Kubota et al. [399] studied the isothermal oxidation behaviour of ZrB2-16 vol% SiC composite, also having with ZrC (2064 vol%), at 1700 °C in air and under low oxygen partial pressures (i.e., 2%O2 in Ar).

3.2.1. UHTC Temperatures
Zhang et al. hot pressed ZrB2-10-30 vol%SiC composites and studied oxidation in air up to 2500 ◦ C using furnace testing, arc jet testing, and TGA [82]. They found no relationship between SiC content and general borosilicate/zirconia  scale  structure  below  1600 ◦ C,  with  no  SiC depletion layer forming ≤ 1600 ◦ C, in agreement with Shugart ' s calculations [80]. With 10 vol%SiC, no depletion layer was observed ≤ 1900 ◦ C. However, the composites with 20 vol% and 30 vol% SiC formed SiC depletion zones at 1700 ◦ C, suggesting that a high enough volume fraction to form a continuous network of SiC particles is necessary to form a depletion layer. Depleted layer thickness was observed to increase  with  SiC  content,  and  therefore  overall  interconnectivity.  A macrograph of a ZrB2-20 vol%SiC ceramic after arc jet testing at ~2300 ◦ C for  1  h  is  shown  in  Fig.  10.  A  large  amount  of  molten  oxidation product has been blown away. Zhang et al. reported that above 2500 ◦ C, the 20 vol% composite exhibited active oxidation with linear kinetics and postulated that the optimal SiC content should be at ~16 vol%. This indicates that while 20-30 vol% SiC modifications may provide the best oxidation resistance during laboratory exposures, SiC vol% should be judiciously  selected  to  minimize  molten  reaction  product  formation. Carney [35] demonstrated this during arc jet testing of HfB2-20 vol%SiC, observing that specimen mass gain increased by less than 1 mg/cm 2 /100 ◦ C  from  1400 ◦ -1800 ◦ C  but  increased  to  over  4  mg/cm 2 from 1800 ◦ -1900 ◦ C. This correlates well with the temperature range that silica-rich glass is anticipated to readily flow. At 1900 ◦ C, the total scale thickness was roughly three times that of the ceramics exposed at lower temperatures.  Carney  observed  a  change  in  the  borosilicate/HfO2 microstructure  from  equiaxed  to

4. Conclusions
The aero-thermal behavior of ZrB2 ceramics containing 40 vol% of chopped carbon fiber, 5 vol% SiC and 5 vol% of various transition metal silicides, MoSi2, HfSi2 and WSi2, has been evaluated using an oxyacetylene torch at T >2000 °C and compared to a baseline composite containing only 10 vol% of SiC. All composites successfully resisted the harsh conditions and none of them shattered, nor developed columnar ZrO2-grains in the outer-scale, which is generally considered the precursor step before catastrophic material oxidation. Different front and back temperatures were achieved mainly depending on the material porosity and hence its thermal conductivity. The thickness of the growing oxide layer may have also played a role.
The material containing HfSi2 (ZBH) showed the highest capability to resist oxidation in these demanding conditions due to the formation of an adherent oxide scale with a thickness of just 40 μm, thanks to the local formation of solid HfC or HfO2. The other two silicides allowed the oxidation of a thicker portion of material, around 122-125 μm, owing to the formation of volatile Mo- and W-oxides which embrittled the zirconia outer structure. The higher front and back temperature of ZBW compared to ZBM, despite the similar oxide scale thickness, was attributed to the higher initial porosity of the former (17%) compared to that of ZBM (9%).
In view of these results, these new materials are believed to show promise and potential for use in ultra-high temperature applications such as thermal protection systems and solid/hybrid-propellant rocket nozzles.

4. Conclusions
The aero-thermal behavior of ZrB2 ceramics containing 40 vol% of chopped carbon fiber, 5 vol% SiC and 5 vol% of various transition metal silicides, MoSi2, HfSi2 and WSi2, has been evaluated using an oxyacetylene torch at T >2000 °C and compared to a baseline composite containing only 10 vol% of SiC. All composites successfully resisted the harsh conditions and none of them shattered, nor developed columnar ZrO2-grains in the outer-scale, which is generally considered the precursor step before catastrophic material oxidation. Different front and back temperatures were achieved mainly depending on the material porosity and hence its thermal conductivity. The thickness of the growing oxide layer may have also played a role.
The material containing HfSi2 (ZBH) showed the highest capability to resist oxidation in these demanding conditions due to the formation of an adherent oxide scale with a thickness of just 40 μm, thanks to the local formation of solid HfC or HfO2. The other two silicides allowed the oxidation of a thicker portion of material, around 122-125 μm, owing to the formation of volatile Mo- and W-oxides which embrittled the zirconia outer structure. The higher front and back temperature of ZBW compared to ZBM, despite the similar oxide scale thickness, was attributed to the higher initial porosity of the former (17%) compared to that of ZBM (9%).
In view of these results, these new materials are believed to show promise and potential for use in ultra-high temperature applications such as thermal protection systems and solid/hybrid-propellant rocket nozzles.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramic-modified  C/C  (C/C-UHTC)  composites exhibit excellent resistance to high-temperature oxidation and ablation compared to other C/C and C/SiC materials. In addition, C/CUHTCs exhibit good fracture toughness and thermal shock resistance, thereby rendering them one of the best aerospace thermal structural materials [1,2].
In the last decade, a series of methods have been investigated for preparing C/C-UHTCs, such as reactive melt infiltration (RMI) [3 -5], precursor infiltration and pyrolysis [6,7], and slurry infiltration [8]. RMI enables rapid, low-cost, and near-net-shape preparation of C/C-UHTCs by alloy melt infiltration into porous C/C composites. Moreover, this process  is  applicable  for  industrial  production  [9,10].  However,  the existing RMI technology has limitations when employed in high-temperature applications. In particular, it is difficult to infiltrate C/C composites with pure ultra-high temperature metals owing to the high viscosity and poor fluidity of the metal melt at high temperatures ( > 1900 ◦ C)  [11 -13].  Furthermore,  the  high-temperature  metal  melt may erode the carbon fibres, thus deteriorating the mechanical properties of the metals [2,10,14]. To address this issue, low melting-point

a b s t r a c t
The sweating effect of Cu resulted in a decrease in the surface temperature of the material from 1900 ° C to 1750 ° C, thereby improving its erosion resistance. Consequently, the introduction of metals such as W and Cu into C/C-MECs to create a cermet composite material can yield a synergistic effect, enhancing both the thermal and mechanical erosion resistance. Such composites can be used in the nozzle throat of the SRM.
The current approach for introducing cermet phases into C/C composites primarily relies on the reactive melt infiltration (RMI) technique [ 26 ]. This process involves heating and melting the alloy at high temperatures, allowing the molten material to penetrate the pores of the C/C composite through capillary action. The result is the formation of a dense cermet phase via an in situ reaction. However, the RMI process requires extremely high preparation temperatures (exceeding 1800 ° C). At such elevated temperatures, the molten material can react aggressively with the carbon matrix (Cf, PyC), causing damage and reducing the mechanical properties of the composite [ 27 ]. For example, Wang et al. [ 28 ] employed the RMI method to prepare C/C-ZrC-SiC composites; however, the resulting flexural strength was suboptimal, measuring only 105.56 MPa. Thus, developing a method to produce metalceramic-modified C/C composites at lower temperatures is critical.

[23] on the composites prepared with UD-0/0 0 Cf as reinforcement, has reported a fracture toughness of 6.71 ± 1.29 MPa √ m after electrophoretic deposition and hot pressing, which agrees closely with the results of the present work. Furthermore, a few other investigations have demonstrated that the fracture toughness values (7.9 -14 MPa √ m) could be significantly increased by coating the individual Cf with pyrolytic carbon (PyC), creating a weak interface that prevents any reaction around the Cf while being used with ZrB2 or TiB2 as the matrix [17,24,49]. Additionally, B4C and Si3N4 have been utilized as the sintering aids to lower the sintering temperature and protect the Cf from degradation during sintering, as mentioned in Table 4 with citations [40,49]. This practice of using B4C and Si3N4 as sintering aids has proven beneficial for causing a significant increase in the composite ' s fracture toughness up to 10 MPa √ m.

1. Introduction
Characteristics of ZrB2-SiC ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) reinforced with ZrC and carbon fiber (Cf) were investigated in this article. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) process was utilized to fabricate the samples at 1800 °C for 5 min under 30 MPa punch pressure and vacuumed atmosphere. In all samples, the volume ratio of ZrB2: SiC was equal to 4:1, and the summation of ZrC and Cf reinforcements was 7.5 vol% with different ZrC: Cf ratios. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), densitometry, flexural strength, and hardness measurements were employed for characterization of the prepared samples. Microstructural inspection revealed the formation of SiC sheath around the carbon fibers due to several reactions in the surface SiO2 layers existed on the SiC particles. Optimal flexural strength (628.4 MPa) and hardness (20.8 GPa) values were achieved for the sample co-reinforced with 6.5 vol% ZrC and 1 vol% Cf, with a relative density of 97.7%.

In this paper spark plasma sintering (SPS) of silicon carbide and multi-walled carbon nanotube reinforced zirconium diboride ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites is reported. Systematic investigations on the effect of reinforcement type (SiC and CNTs) and content (10-40 vol.% SiC and 2-6 vol.% CNTs) on densification behavior, microstructure development, and mechanical properties (microhardness, biaxial flexural strength, and indentation fracture toughness) are presented. With the similar SPS processing parameters (1900 ◦ C, 70 MPa pressure, and 15 min soaking time), near-full densification (>99% relative density) was achieved with 10-40% SiC (in ZrB2-SiC) and 4-6% CNT (in ZrB2-CNT) reinforced composites. The SiC and CNT reinforcement further improved the indentation fracture toughness of the composites through a range of toughening mechanisms, including particle shearing, crack deflection at the particlematrix interface, and grain pull-outs for ZrB2-SiC composites, and CNT pull-outs and crack deflection in ZrB2-CNT composites.

1. Introduction
Because of the superior mechanical properties at high temperature, carbon/carbon (C/C) composites are broadly applied as thermal structural materials [1 -4]. In practical applications, C/C composites should bear chemical ablation and mechanical denudation in ultra-high temperature (above 2273 K) environment along with severe combustion gas /uniFB02 ow. Unfortunately, the rapid oxidation of C/C composites above 773 K will degrade their mechanical properties. So, it is crucial to increase the oxidation and ablation resistance of C/C composites [5,6]. Preparing an external coating is an effective method to prevent oxidation and ablation of C/C composites in such severe environment [7,8]. Because of its relative lower coef /uniFB01 cient of thermal expansion (CTE), SiC is usually used as a transition layer to decrease the mismatch of CTE between C/C substrate and outer coating [2].
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) (HfC, ZrC, HfB2, ZrB2 and soon) are appropriate ablation resistance coating materials for C/C composites [9]. Among these UHTCs, ZrB2 has high melting point (3313 K), good chemical stability, good ablation resistance and so on [10 -12]. The oxidation products of SiC and ZrB2 could form silicate glass between 1273 K and 2073 K, so adding appropriate SiC could increase the ablation resistance of ZrB2 [13]. Up to now, ZrB2-SiC coating has been successfully synthesized on C/C composites. Yao [14] and Zhang [15] synthesized ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating by supersonic atmosphere plasma spray (SAPS), which could effectively prevent C/C composites from oxyacetylene torch ablation. Wang [16] prepared ZrB2-SiC coating
⁎ Corresponding author. E-mail address: zhangyulei@nwpu.edu.cn (Y. Zhang).

question: How do NbC coatings modified with 75 mol% HfC perform under oxyacetylene flame ablation testing for 180 seconds, and what is the effective ablation time compared to the 50 mol% HfC composition?
answer: The sources do not provide information on the performance of NbC coatings modified with 75 mol% HfC under oxyacetylene flame ablation testing for 180 seconds or a comparison of effective ablation time with the 50 mol% HfC composition.

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
In this work, NbC coatings modified with different contents (0, 25 and 50 mol%) of HfC were deposited on SiCcoated C/C composites via supersonic atmospheric plasma spraying to study the effect of HfC on the ablationresistance  of  the  NbC  coatings  under  an  oxyacetylene  torch.  The  phase  composition  and  chemical  states  of Nb2O5 -66.67  mol%  HfO2  plates  heated  up  to  2100 ◦ C  in  an  Ar  atmosphere  were  investigated.  The  results confirmed that the effective ablation time of the NbC-coating mixed with 50 mol% HfC was approximately 120 s, which  is  more  than  twice  that  of  the  pure  NbC  coating.  During  air  quenching,  NbO  and  NbO2  ultimately transformed  into  Nb2O5,  producing  compressive  stress  that  stabilized  the  t-HfO2 crystals.  Furthermore,  the formation of hafnium oxide significantly improved the thermal stability and viscosity of the Nb -O film, and the niobium  suboxide-based  solid  solution,  possessing  an  attractive  self-healing  performance,  increased  the compactness of the HfO2-based solid solution layer are responsible for the superior ablation performance of the NbC-HfC coating.

3.1. Ablation property
Fig. 2 illustrates the macro-morphology of the coated samples after the oxyacetylene test. A pit is observed in the central ablation region of the NbC coating after ablation for 60 s. However, a white glassy phase that is able to resist the infiltration of O2 and heat forms in the NbC-HfC coatings after ablation for 90 s. In addition, the film of the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating is exfoliated, possibly due to the mismatch of the thermal stresses in the outer oxide layers [19]. After a period of 120 s, severe damage with several pores occurs in the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating, which can be attributed to the denudation of NbO ss .  However, in the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating, only a porous oxide skeleton is observed in the  central  ablation  region,  indicating  a  significant  improvement  in ablation-resistance behaviour.
The temperature variations of the coatings in the oxyacetylene test are  plotted  in  Fig.  3.  The  temperature  of  the  NbC  coating  increases rapidly at the initial stage and then decreases due to the presence of gaseous products. However, the temperature of the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating increases after 80 s, suggesting the formation of a smooth and dense oxide layer. Furthermore, due to the absence of a smooth oxide film of the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating, the temperature of the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating is lower than that of the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating, and  stabilizes  after  75  s.  Additionally,  compared  to  the  pure  NbC coating, each of the NbC-HfC coatings, without temperature reduction at the final stage, achieves a better thermal protection for the SiC transition layer.

3.1. Ablation property
The linear and mass ablation rates of the specimens are listed in Table 3. The mass ablation rates of the NbC-HfC coatings are all negative. In contrast, after ablation for 60 s, the NbC coating has a positive result  with  the  highest  thickness  reduction,  indicating  a  significant consumption of NbC coating by mechanical denudation. Moreover, the net  increments  of  mass  and  thickness  of  the  NbC-HfC  coatings  after ablation for 120 s are less than those after ablation for 90 s, suggesting that the coatings are mechanically eroded by the oxyacetylene flame before 120 s. In addition, at the same ablation time, the net increments of the mass and thickness of the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating are more R. He et al.
Fig. 4. Phase composition of the NbC-HfC coatings after ablation for 120 s and heated Nb2O5 -66.67 mol% HfO2 plates at 2100 ◦ C in Ar atmosphere for 2 h. (a) X-ray diffractograms; (b) enlarged view of (a). ( 25 :  the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating; 50 :  the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating; 66.67 :  the Nb2O5 -66.67 mol% HfO2  plates; t : tetragonal HfO2; m : monoclinic HfO2.)
Fig.  5. XPS  spectra  of  the  Nb2O5 -66.67  mol% HfO2  plates  before  and  after  the  heat-treatment  at  2100 ◦ C.  (a -b)  Nb3d;  (c -d)  Hf4f. 4. Conclusions
To summarize, NbC coatings mixed with various contents of HfC were  successfully  synthesized  on  the  surface  of  SiC-coated  C/Cs  via SAPS.  A  test  of  the  ablation-resistance  of  the  coated  samples  and investigation of the phase composition of the heat-treated Nb2O5 -66.67 mol% HfO2  plates were conducted. The findings  of the  study are  as follows: By increasing the HfC content of NbC-HfC coating from 0 to 50 mol%, the effective ablation time increased by over 100% to 120 s. This outstanding ablation-resistance is attributed to a synergistic effect that the  HfO2  greatly  improves  the  thermal  stability  and  viscosity  of  the Nb -O film, and the NbO ss possessing an excellent self-healing performance increases the compactness and mechanical strength of the HfO2, ss layer.  Furthermore,  Nb2O5 stabilized  the  t-HfO2 crystals  after  the oxyacetylene test of the NbC-HfC coatings, while the crystal structures of HfO2 after the heat-treatment all remained monoclinic; the reasons are that the ionic radius of Nb 5 + is much smaller than that of Hf 4 + ;  and during the air quenching, the niobium suboxides are re-oxidized and eventually  transformed  into  Nb2O5  with  a  volume  expansion  which produces compressive stress on the t-HfO2 crystals. Thus, Nb2O5  stabilized t-HfO2. While, the niobium suboxides without phase change in Ar atmosphere have no effect on the phase transformation as a function of the temperature of HfO2.

3.1. Ablation property
than those of the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating, indicating that the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating is more resistant to erosion. Therefore, modification with  HfC  significantly  improves  the  ablation-resistance  of  the  NbC coating,  with  the  NbC-50  mol%  HfC  coating  displaying  the  best performance.

question: What are the quantified oxide phases and thermal stability of TaC-ZrC binary coatings when exposed to plasma jet conditions exceeding 3000°C?
answer: The sources do not contain information about the quantified oxide phases and thermal stability of TaC-ZrC binary coatings when exposed to plasma jet conditions exceeding 3000°C.

chunks.text:
a b s t r a c t
 T15S15C, Crystallite size (t) of TaC in SPS pellet (nm) Before plasma arc jet test After plasma arc jet test = 46 ± 1. T15S15C, Retention of TaC phase after exposure = ∼ 20%. T15S15C, Thickness of the oxide layer ( 𝛍 m) = 20.1 ± 2.0
subjected to temperature ∼ 3000 ◦ C in presence of oxygen and reported that the formation of oxide layer degrades the stability of TaC both chemically and mechanically. Nieto et al. [10] showed oxidation behaviour of spark plasma sintered TaC-graphene nanoplatelets (1 to 5 vol%) (GNP) composites processed at temperature of 1850 ◦ C under high temperature plasma flow of 30 kW power for 60 s and showed that addition of GNP suppresses formation of oxide layer up to 60%. However, high temperature thermal conductivity for TaC based composites is not available in literature, which is one of the important attributes to survive high temperature exposure.
Improvement of fracture toughness with SiC and CNT addition in TaC-based UHTCs is demonstrated elsewhere [39]. Hence, the scope of the present work is to develop an understanding of the oxidation mechanisms of TaC-SiC-CNT composites to predict the lifetime performance of UHTCs under operational conditions. Oxidation behaviour is analysed in terms of the oxide layer formation during testing. To supplement plasma arc jet testing, thermogravimetric analysis is carried out for 30 min. at 1500 ◦ C in oxygen environment. Thermal conductivity of these materials have been measured using laser flash diffusivity technique and, accordingly, correlation of the heat transfer mechanism during plasma arc jet testing is established. An attempt has been made to associate the synergistic effect of SiC and CNT reinforcement on the performance of TaC-based UHTCs through these investigations.

3.3. Plasma arc jet exposure
Further,  the  cross-sectional  SEM  of  samples  was  conducted  to quantify the thickness of the oxide film formation on the sample surface due to the melting of the Ta2O5  phase, see Fig. 9c and d. The minor cracking in the 50HO-50TO sample vs. wide crack opening in the 70HO30TO sample in the unaffected region elicits that the intense plasma exposure was enough to create thermal stresses, analogous to the strain analysis in Table 1. The thickness of melted and then resolidified oxide film is 210 nm and 297 nm in 50HO-50TO and 70HO-30TO samples. Although there is no significant change in the thickness of melted oxide in both the samples, there are significantly more apparent melted regions in the 70HO-30TO sample. Hence, the 50HO-50TO sample has better  thermal  stability  than  the  70HO-30TO  sample.  In  terms  of improving the oxidation resistance of solid-solution binary UHTCs or MC-UHTCs, it can be anticipated that a composition with equal or higher content of TaC will have a higher probability of Hf6Ta2O17  phase formation during plasma exposure. This complex mixed oxide phase act as a protective layer that suppresses further oxidation. Also, the protective layer in UHTC components is formed due to the preferential oxidation of various  components  at  service  temperature  [16].  The  present  study shows that the complex mixed oxide formation during service ( > 2500 ◦ C) of solid-solution binary UHTCs and MC-UHTCs improves its oxidation resistance due to the thermal stability and mechanical integrity of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase. UHTC composition, thus designed to form a protective and thermally stable Hf6Ta2O17 phase under extreme conditions, will provide new directions to the materials for reusable hypersonic applications.

3.1. Oxide layer formation
Preand post-oxidation analysis of all the composites, show that all the samples (TaC, T15S, T15C and T15S15C) survived the plasma jet flux of 2.5 MW/m 2 for 30 s, see Fig. 3a, and no appreciable erosion was observed on the front face of the TaC-SiC-CNT samples. Apparently, surface was without cracks on all TaC based composites, and the formed oxide scale was adherent to the base material (see Fig. 3a). The back face rise in temperature for all plasma arc tested UHTC composites (shown in Fig. 3b) is observed to decrease with SiC addition and increases with CNT reinforcement in the TaC matrix.
Qualitative changes occurring during oxidation of TaC-based composites are clearly evident from the subsequent appearance of orthorhombic Ta2O5 peaks obtained in XRD spectra (Fig. 4a). However, retention of TaC phase were also observed and the amount of TaC phase was quantified on the surface of exposed samples as an average of area fraction of TaC under XRD peaks (see Table 1). The crystallite size ( t ), evaluated using Scherrer's formula (Eq. (3)) for all as-prepared samples, are of similar order ( ∼ 25-31 nm), however the crystallite size marginally increased after plasma arc jet exposure ( ∼ 40-51 nm), shown in Table 1. It is elucidated that the formation of surface oxide was suppressed with SiC addition as the amount of retained TaC phase was increased from ∼ 25% (for TaC) to 36% (T15S), but decreases with CNT addition to 18% (for T15C) and 22% (T15S15C). CNT addition protects TaC from oxidation as explained in the following section. The formation of other oxides (TaO2 and Ta2O3) were not observed after plasma arc jet exposure, though they have been reported at intermediate temperatures of around 900-1300 ◦ C, depending on the oxygen partial pressure [42].

4. Conclusions
Oxidation behaviour of spark plasma sintered TaC-SiC-CNT composites was evaluated through plasma arc jet exposure under heat flux of 2.5 MW/m 2 . Oxidation behaviour is characterised in terms of oxide layer formation, its thickness and residual stresses. After exposure to harsh aerothermal conditions, the structural integrity of these UHTC composites were measured in terms of critical energy release rate ( G IC ) which increases up to 2.5 times ( ∼ 58 to 139 Jm -2 ) with synergistic reinforcement of SiC and CNT in TaC. The protective SiO2 formation with SiC reinforcement in TaC suggested an extension of stability domain which resulted in an improved oxidation resistance. It is elicited that CNT addition improves oxidation resistance of TaC by grain sealing mechanism. Raman spectroscopic analysis elicited the presence of carbonaceous material even after plasma exposure, further supplemented by TEM analysis of T15S15C. In addition, enhanced thermal conductivity with temperature for all composites provides an indication of improved oxidation resistance via heat dissipation. Thermal studies up to 1500 ◦ C have confirmed that the temperature of oxide formation shifts towards higher temperature (from 735 ◦ C to 901 ◦ C) and decrease in the enthalpy of oxide formation (from 4.5 to 3.5 kJ/g) with SiC and CNT reinforcement. Such an observation insinuates synergetic reinforcement of SiC and CNT in enhancing the thermal stability and oxidation resistance of TaC-based UHTC composites.

1. Introduction
ZrC, a key ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC), has been widely recognized for its capability to provide substantial thermal protection for aerospace vehicles operating in extreme environments [1,2]. However, its performance is hindered by the extremely high melting point (T m ≈ 2710 ◦ C [3]) and detrimental phase-transformation stress [4] of ZrO2, resulting in pulverised oxidation [5,6] and limiting its ability to inhibit  oxygen  diffusion.  Doping  with  Si-based  ceramics  [7,8] forms Si -O glass that can heal surface defects; however, SiO2  evaporates at 1650 ◦ C [9] and SiO sublimates at 1727 ◦ C [10], leading to a porous oxide film. Rare-earth oxides such as La2O3 [11], Yb2O3 [12], and Y2O3 [13] can  stabilise  tetragonal  ZrO2  (t-ZrO2),  thereby  mitigating  stress mismatches; however, these materials also increase the concentration of oxygen vacancies. High-T m components, including TiC [14], W [15], and TaC [16], help maintain the high liquidus temperature of ZrC; however, the thermal stabilities of their oxides are insufficient [17 -19], resulting in discontinuous oxide films. To date, no public reports have demonstrated the formation of dense single-phase ZrO2 films on the surface of ZrC coatings after ablation.
NbC oxidises to a liquid state at 1500 ◦ C in air [20] and forms a dense filmy structure at 2000 -2100 ◦ C under an oxyacetylene flame [21], demonstrating  excellent  wide-temperature  range  flowability.  When

A B S T R A C T
subjected to an oxyacetylene flame at 2.4 MW/m 2 for 300 s, ZrC-20 mol % NbC coating develops  a ' role-reversal  honeycomb ' structure  consisting  of  ZrO2  columnar crystals healed by niobium oxide [22] and exhibits  outstanding  ablation  resistance.  To  study  their  potential  for thermal protection applications, it is crucial to investigate the wide-temperature  range  erosion  performance  of  ZrC -NbC  coatings, particularly their ablation resistance near 2500 ◦ C (oxyacetylene flame at approximately 4.2 MW/m 2 ) and their oxidation resistance up to 1700 ◦ C. However, analysing the physiochemical changes of ZrC -NbC coatings [22] remains challenging owing to their insufficient thickness, low density, and poor bonding strength with the substrate, which cause them to peel off before the formation of stable oxides. To address these issues, this  study  employs  vacuum  hot-pressed  sintering  (VHPS)  technology [23 -25] to prepare dense and size controllable Zr1x Nb x C UHTCs with uniform elemental distributions.
One of the primary challenges in elucidating the oxidation-ablation mechanism of Zr1x Nb x C lies in identifying the phase evolution on its surface. Investigations into the phase composition of Zr -O -Nb systems have been limited to temperatures up to 1950 ◦ C, and the reported solid compounds formed by Nb2O5 and ZrO2, as well as their decomposition points, show inconsistences [26,27]. To address this, ZrO2 -Nb2O5 ceramics were heat-treated in Ar ranging from 1300 to 2100 º C, and the phase  composition  at  2100 º C  was  accurately  determined  [22].

A B S T R A C T

HfC-30 mol% NbC coating forms a ' role-reversal honeycomb ' structure comprising HfO2 columnar crystals bonded by niobium oxide under an oxyacetylene flame at 2.4 MW/m 2 , exhibiting excellent ablation resistance [16]. Before the HfC -NbC coatings are applied in thermal  protection,  their  ablation  resistance  limits  need  to  be  studied, particularly at service temperatures exceeding 2500 ◦ C (oxyacetylene flame at approximately 4.2 MW/m 2 ). However, the density, thickness, and  bonding  strength  with  substrate  of  the  HfC -NbC  coatings  are limited. This results in coating transfer under the oxyacetylene flame before stable oxides form on the surface, complicating the analysis of physicochemical changes in the Hf -C -Nb system. To address these challenges,  vacuum hot-pressed sintering  (VHPS) technology [17,18] was  employed  to  synthesise  NbC-modified  HfC  (Hf1x Nb x C)  UHTCs capable of successfully completing ablation tests under an oxyacetylene flame at 4.2 MW/m 2 . Furthermore, to investigate the wide-temperature range protection performance of Hf1x Nb x C, the anti-oxidation property of Hf1x Nb x C at 1700 ◦ C should be studied.

1. Introduction
To  improve  the  use  of  carbon/carbon  composites  (C/Cs)  in  newgeneration  aircrafts,  coating  technology  is  employed  to  optimize  the anti-oxidation property and ablation-resistance of C/Cs [1 -2]. Coatings consisting  of  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  have  been proven to effectively protect SiC-coated C/Cs [3 -6]. However, a single UHTC coating forms a porous oxide layer due to its pulverized-oxidation behaviour,  thus  limiting  the  thermal  protection  performance.  Recent studies have reported that liquid SiO2 can heal the cracks formed on SiCmodified  UHTC  coatings,  enabling  the  construction  of  a  dense  and smooth oxide layer at high temperatures and improving their ablation behaviour [7 -10]. However, SiO2  volatilizes at 1650 ◦ C [11] and SiO sublimates above 1727 ◦ C [12], these reduce the compactness of the oxide film. Moreover, SiC (Tm ≈ 2700 ◦ C) decreases the melting temperature of the UHTC coating. These drawbacks limit the applications of SiC-modified UHTC coatings.
In a previous study, we reported that among several UHTC coatings, only the NbC coating acquired a smooth and dense oxide layer in the central ablation region after the oxyacetylene test [13]. 1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), particularly HfC, effectively protect aerospace vehicles in extreme environments [1,2]. However, the high melting point (T m ≈ 2810 ◦ C [3]) and phase-transformation stress [4] of HfO2 result in pulverised oxidation of HfC  coating  [5,6],  limiting  its  ability  to  inhibit  oxygen  diffusion. Component modification of HfC coatings has been extensively studied. For instance, under doping with SiC [7], SiO2 evaporates at 1650 ◦ C [8] and SiO sublimates at 1710 ◦ C [9], resulting in a porous oxide film. Introduction of TaC [7], the low T m of Ta2O5 [10] leads to a discontinuous  oxide  film.  For  HfO2 [11],  although  the  formation  of  gaseous products is suppressed, the porous structure of the HfO2 layer does not significantly densify. Furthermore, Y2Hf2O7 coating [12] forms a dense oxide film under an oxyacetylene flame at 2.4 MW/m 2 ; however, Y 3 + increases the concentration of oxygen vacancies [13]. To date, no public reports have documented the formation of a dense single-phase HfO2 film on the surface of HfC coating after ablation.

question: What is the oxidation resistance and preferential oxidation mechanism of multi-component UHTC systems incorporating tungsten carbide (WC) components when exposed to plasma jet at temperatures above 2800°C?
answer: The provided sources do not mention the oxidation resistance and preferential oxidation mechanism of multi-component UHTC systems incorporating tungsten carbide (WC) components when exposed to plasma jet at temperatures above 2800°C.

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
Multi-component ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTCs) has garnered attention due to their improved thermo-mechanical and oxidation properties. MC-UHTC was progressively formed by adding a UHTC component at  each  step,  producing  binary,  ternary  and  quaternary  UHTC  systems,  processed  by  spark  plasma  sintering (SPS). We investigate the role of multi-elements and thermochemical stability of the surface oxide layer formed during plasma arc jet exposure ( T > 2500 ◦ C) of MC-UHTCs using core-level X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) correlated with the structural investigation. The results indicate that adding a UHTC component improves oxidation resistance due to the assemblage of refractory phases forming complex mixed oxides via preferential oxidation. XPS spectra of Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, and Hf4f showed that the nature of the chemical bonds switched from metal carbides to metal oxides (and metal oxy-carbides) in the binary system with retained metal carbides in ternary and quaternary UHTCs. The absence of a metal carbide peak in the high-resolution C1s scan of the binary system also supports this finding. The principal complex mixed oxide phases emanated in the exiguous oxidation of MC-UHTCs are quantified using Rietveld analysis. Overall, this study suggests that MC-UHTC is a promising material for possible application in thermal protection systems (TPS).
sharp leading edges in re-entry vehicles. These materials have gathered quick attention because of their remarkable properties, which include enhanced mechanical properties [9 -12], improved fracture toughness [11,13,14],  tribological  performance  [15 -17],  creep  resistance  [18], and low thermal conductivity [11,19 -21].

4. Summary
The MC-UHTCs showed remarkable improvement in the oxidation resistance during plasma exposure testing compared to binary UHTCs. This has been attributed to the preferential oxidation and a reduction in low melting temperature unary oxide content in the surface oxide scale from > 82% in binary UHTC to < 13% in MC-UHTC. A thermally stable complex protective oxide layer of Hf6Ta2O17 (a primary phase) in MCUHTCs has further suppressed the oxide formation. As a result of the relative thermal stability of transition metals and components in the MCUHTCs, preferential oxidation occurs in MC-UHTCs, i.e., Hf in ternary and Ti in quaternary UHTC system, while both are forming the protective mixed complex oxide layer to remarkably improve ( > 40 times) the oxidation resistance over binary and unary UHTC systems. Both XRD and XPS analysis showed retention of metal carbide phase only in MCUHTCs. The study suggests that MC-UHTCs designed to form a mixed complex protective oxide layer of group IV element under service conditions elicit extended potential in extreme space vehicle applications.

5. Conclusion
In this work, a multi-phase multicomponent UHTC, Hf0.61Zr0.26Ti0.13C- (Hf0.30Zr0.32Ti0.38)5Si3, was designed and fabricated. The oxidation mechanism of the multiphase multicomponent UHTC was investigated through experiments in conjunction with thermodynamic analysis. It is found that, during oxidation, the intraphase preferential oxidation of components (i.e., Hf and Zr) and the inter-phase preferential oxidation toward thermodynamically favored phase (i.e., silicide) would occur synchronously. In this process, the intraphase preferential oxidation led to the formation of a nanoscale self-healing oxide skeleton in  carbide  phase.  More  importantly,  the  synergy  of  the  hierarchical preferential oxidation induced an inter-phase migration of preferentially oxidized components (i.e., Hf and Zr) from carbide phase to the preferentially oxidized silicide phase, which facilitated the development of a micrometer-sized  Hf/Zr-rich  oxide  framework.  This  framework  provided additional support for the nano-sized self-healing oxide skeleton, moreover, the as-formed multi-scale hierarchical structured oxide scale offered an effective protection for the material and improved the ablation performance. The ablation test shows that multi-phase multicomponent UHTC with merely 4.4 vol% of silicide demonstrates a superior ablation resistance compared to the single-phase counterpart (a reduction of 64.7% and 62.5% in MAR and LAR, respectively). More broadly, the results obtained in this work could provide a preliminary basis for the design and optimization of the multiphase multicomponent UHTCs.

4.  Potential pathways to address challenges involved with HEUHTCs
Based on the limited available data in the literature [13,30,46,58], a comparison  of  the  mechanical  and  oxidation  resistance  of  mono, binary,  ternary,  and  multi-component  UHTC  carbides  (referred  to  as HE-carbide) is summarized and plotted in Fig. 7. The improvement in the hardness (see Fig. 7) has mainly been attributed to the solid-solutioning in the binary, ternary, and HE-UHTCs. The onset temperature at which the oxide starts forming has remarkably improved from 900 °C in monolith UHTCs to > 1100 °C for HE-UHTCs (Fig. 7 ) . This is possibly due to the formation of the complex, layered structure as well as the composition of the oxide scale in a multi-component system. The innermost oxide layer near the unoxidized sample usually has a dense structure with lesser pores (all filled with molten particles) followed by a porous oxide structure with lesser pores (see Fig. 5). Also, the complex composition of the mixed oxides due to multi-constituents restricts the inward  diffusion  of  O2 providing  oxidation  resistance,  discussed  in section 3.2. However, whether all components in a solid-solution of the HE-UHTC  system  react  with  oxygen  simultaneously  or  preferential oxidation, still needs to be looked upon in both isothermal and highspeed  plasma  jet  conditions.  Also,  this  brings  up  a  question  of  how many  constituents  (four,  five,  six,  or  more)  in  a  multi-component system would be sufficient for enhancement in the properties in the absence of entropy quantification. To get an insight into it, a study on gradually raising the constituents in UHTCs from mono, binary and so on is required along with the delineation of the entropy contributions to the properties.
Fig.  7. Comparison  of  the  hardness  and  onset  of  oxidation  temperature  in various carbides.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), a class of ceramic materials operating above 2000 ◦ C, primarily include borides, carbides, and nitrides of transition metals[1]. As critical materials for extreme environments, their oxidation resistance directly determines their service lifetime  and  reliability  under  high-temperature  oxidative  conditions. HfB ₂ -SiC is a representative UHTC system, widely applied in hypersonic vehicle nose cones, rocket engine nozzles, and other thermal protection components due to its ultra-high melting point ( > 3000 ◦ C) and exceptional oxidation/ablation resistance[2]. In ultra-high-temperature oxidative  environments,  material  surfaces  undergo  intense  oxidation reactions,  forming  porous  or  volatile  oxide  layers  that  lead  to  rapid

3.2. Phase quantification of surface oxide layer
oxidation of Ta over Nb is seen in all three samples has been attributed to two reasons: (i) Ta2O5 is more stable than Nb2O5, and (ii) Nb is present in  the  lower  content in  the pre-alloyed (Ta, Nb)C (80:20). Similarly, among the two Hf-containing samples, the content of HfO2 in ternary is more than quaternary is due to the high content of HfC in (50 vol%) in ternary,  which  also  led  to  the  segregation  of  the  phase  along  with complete solid-solution phase in the sample [7]. The content of unary oxide phases reduced from 82.7% in the binary compound to 21.5% in ternary and 12.9% in the quaternary compound. Hf-Ta oxide complex phases follow most Ti-Ta-rich oxide at the surface in quaternary UHTC while Hf-Ta complex oxide phase in the ternary system and unary oxide of Ta-Nb in binary UHTC. The majority of the oxide phase in the quaternary  system  is  rich  in  Ti-based  oxide.  This  could  be  due  to  the segregation of the TiC phase (up to 3 wt%, see Fig. 3) near the grain boundaries, which forms additional complex mixed oxide of Ti during plasma oxidation. Also, Ti is the smallest, lightest, and oxidation-prone among  the  metallic  elements  of  (Ta,Nb,Hf,Ti)C  [12].  It  is  worth mentioning that the high-resolution SEM on this sample reported in the recent study by authors could not detect TiC segregation [7] due to the smaller amount of undissolved TiC (~3 wt%) in the sample. EBSD, a more precise technique, could visualize the distribution of phases and quantify it qualitatively (see Fig. 3).

A B S T R A C T
Multi-component ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTCs) has garnered attention due to their improved thermo-mechanical and oxidation properties. MC-UHTC was progressively formed by adding a UHTC component at  each  step,  producing  binary,  ternary  and  quaternary  UHTC  systems,  processed  by  spark  plasma  sintering (SPS). We investigate the role of multi-elements and thermochemical stability of the surface oxide layer formed during plasma arc jet exposure ( T > 2500 ◦ C) of MC-UHTCs using core-level X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) correlated with the structural investigation. The results indicate that adding a UHTC component improves oxidation resistance due to the assemblage of refractory phases forming complex mixed oxides via preferential oxidation. XPS spectra of Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, and Hf4f showed that the nature of the chemical bonds switched from metal carbides to metal oxides (and metal oxy-carbides) in the binary system with retained metal carbides in ternary and quaternary UHTCs. The absence of a metal carbide peak in the high-resolution C1s scan of the binary system also supports this finding. The principal complex mixed oxide phases emanated in the exiguous oxidation of MC-UHTCs are quantified using Rietveld analysis. Overall, this study suggests that MC-UHTC is a promising material for possible application in thermal protection systems (TPS).

These materials have gathered quick attention because of their remarkable properties, which include enhanced mechanical properties [9 -12], improved fracture toughness [11,13,14],  tribological  performance  [15 -17],  creep  resistance  [18], and low thermal conductivity [11,19 -21].

4. Summary
The MC-UHTCs showed remarkable improvement in the oxidation resistance during plasma exposure testing compared to binary UHTCs. This has been attributed to the preferential oxidation and a reduction in low melting temperature unary oxide content in the surface oxide scale from > 82% in binary UHTC to < 13% in MC-UHTC. A thermally stable complex protective oxide layer of Hf6Ta2O17 (a primary phase) in MCUHTCs has further suppressed the oxide formation. As a result of the relative thermal stability of transition metals and components in the MCUHTCs, preferential oxidation occurs in MC-UHTCs, i.e., Hf in ternary and Ti in quaternary UHTC system, while both are forming the protective mixed complex oxide layer to remarkably improve ( > 40 times) the oxidation resistance over binary and unary UHTC systems. Both XRD and XPS analysis showed retention of metal carbide phase only in MCUHTCs. The study suggests that MC-UHTCs designed to form a mixed complex protective oxide layer of group IV element under service conditions elicit extended potential in extreme space vehicle applications.

3.3. Surface oxidation state
Fig. 5 e-g shows the high-resolution elemental scan for Ta, Hf, Nb, and  Ti  metal  cations  in  quaternary  UHTC  system.  The  peaks  corresponding to Ta, Hf, and Nb were similar to those obtained in the ternary UHTC system, albeit with slight shift ( < 5%) due to the addition of TiC in the quaternary system. Similar to ternary UHTC, two additional minor peaks of Ta4f7/2 and Ta4f5/2 with binding energies 24.4 eV, and 26.2 eV, respectively, correspond to the carbide peak of Ta with a shift towards the higher energy due to the solid-solution formation in a four component system. The shift in the M GLYPH<0> C towards higher energy could be due to the formation of oxy-carbide. The substitution of C by O in the M GLYPH<0> C bond leading to the formation of oxy-carbide has shown to have a higher binding energy of 1 -2 eV [28]. The intensity ratio of Ti2p1/2 and Ti2p3/2 is constrained by 1:2, which is maintained in the present case. As per the literature, Ti peaks corresponding to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 sets at 453.74 eV, and 459.79 eV while Ti(IV) oxide (TiO2) peaks corresponding to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 sets at 458.59 and 464.31 eV [46]. The observed peak position of  Ti2p,  which  corresponds  to  2p3/2,  is  at  458.8  eV,  confirming  the presence of the rutile TiO2 phase and a shoulder peak at 457.32 eV. This shoulder peak can be due to Ti ' s other complex mixed oxide phases, as observed in the XRD analysis (see Fig. 2, Table 2). Hence, in the quaternary system, the major oxidation by-products comprises of two mixed complex oxide phases of Ta-Ti-O and Hf-Ta-O (and not TiO2  alone), analogous  to  the  results  obtained  from  Rietveld  analysis  (Fig.  2, Table 2).

3.3. Surface oxidation state
Table 3 summarizes the Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, Hf4f, O1s, and C1s peaks from XPS spectra collected for the plasma exposed binary, ternary, and quaternary UHTCs along with the literature peaks for carbides and oxides. The relative intensity of elements ' peaks in XPS spectra can provide insight into oxidation thermodynamic and kinetic theories. This can be well  understood by comparing the relative intensities of various elements in a particular sample. The Gibbs free energy of oxide formation for Group V (Ta,Nb) is lower than that of Group IV (Hf,Ti), other things being equal [28]. Hence, the preferential oxide growth of Ti and Hf are more favorable once the oxidation process is activated [26], as observed from the XRD analysis (see Table 2). The oxidation in MC-UHTCs is led by the oxygen diffusion inwards and the metal cation outwards with time. This outward diffusion of metal cation depends on the size of the A. Nisar et al.
Fig. 6. High-resolution C1s and O1s spectra collected for (a,b) binary, (c,d) ternary, and (e,f) quaternary UHTC system. The deconvoluted C1s envelops show the metal carbide (M GLYPH<0> C) peak (not in the binary system) along with advantageous carbons (C-C, C-O-C, and O-C = O) peaks. The deconvoluted O1s spectra show the presence of metal oxides (M GLYPH<0> O) and the complex oxy-carbides/surface oxygen peak.

3.1. Ablation property
The linear and mass ablation rates of the specimens are listed in Table 3. The mass ablation rates of the NbC-HfC coatings are all negative. In contrast, after ablation for 60 s, the NbC coating has a positive result  with  the  highest  thickness  reduction,  indicating  a  significant consumption of NbC coating by mechanical denudation. Moreover, the net  increments  of  mass  and  thickness  of  the  NbC-HfC  coatings  after ablation for 120 s are less than those after ablation for 90 s, suggesting that the coatings are mechanically eroded by the oxyacetylene flame before 120 s. In addition, at the same ablation time, the net increments of the mass and thickness of the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating are more R. He et al.

3.1. Ablation property
Fig. 2 illustrates the macro-morphology of the coated samples after the oxyacetylene test. A pit is observed in the central ablation region of the NbC coating after ablation for 60 s. However, a white glassy phase that is able to resist the infiltration of O2 and heat forms in the NbC-HfC coatings after ablation for 90 s. In addition, the film of the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating is exfoliated, possibly due to the mismatch of the thermal stresses in the outer oxide layers [19]. After a period of 120 s, severe damage with several pores occurs in the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating, which can be attributed to the denudation of NbO ss .  However, in the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating, only a porous oxide skeleton is observed in the  central  ablation  region,  indicating  a  significant  improvement  in ablation-resistance behaviour.
The temperature variations of the coatings in the oxyacetylene test are  plotted  in  Fig.  3.  The  temperature  of  the  NbC  coating  increases rapidly at the initial stage and then decreases due to the presence of gaseous products. However, the temperature of the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating increases after 80 s, suggesting the formation of a smooth and dense oxide layer. Furthermore, due to the absence of a smooth oxide film of the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating, the temperature of the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating is lower than that of the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating, and  stabilizes  after  75  s.  Additionally,  compared  to  the  pure  NbC coating, each of the NbC-HfC coatings, without temperature reduction at the final stage, achieves a better thermal protection for the SiC transition layer.

4. Conclusions
To summarize, NbC coatings mixed with various contents of HfC were  successfully  synthesized  on  the  surface  of  SiC-coated  C/Cs  via SAPS.  A  test  of  the  ablation-resistance  of  the  coated  samples  and investigation of the phase composition of the heat-treated Nb2O5 -66.67 mol% HfO2  plates were conducted. The findings  of the  study are  as follows: By increasing the HfC content of NbC-HfC coating from 0 to 50 mol%, the effective ablation time increased by over 100% to 120 s. This outstanding ablation-resistance is attributed to a synergistic effect that the  HfO2  greatly  improves  the  thermal  stability  and  viscosity  of  the Nb -O film, and the NbO ss possessing an excellent self-healing performance increases the compactness and mechanical strength of the HfO2, ss layer.  Furthermore,  Nb2O5 stabilized  the  t-HfO2 crystals  after  the oxyacetylene test of the NbC-HfC coatings, while the crystal structures of HfO2 after the heat-treatment all remained monoclinic; the reasons are that the ionic radius of Nb 5 + is much smaller than that of Hf 4 + ;  and during the air quenching, the niobium suboxides are re-oxidized and eventually  transformed  into  Nb2O5  with  a  volume  expansion  which produces compressive stress on the t-HfO2 crystals. Thus, Nb2O5  stabilized t-HfO2. While, the niobium suboxides without phase change in Ar atmosphere have no effect on the phase transformation as a function of the temperature of HfO2.

A B S T R A C T
In this work, NbC coatings modified with different contents (0, 25 and 50 mol%) of HfC were deposited on SiCcoated C/C composites via supersonic atmospheric plasma spraying to study the effect of HfC on the ablationresistance  of  the  NbC  coatings  under  an  oxyacetylene  torch.  The  phase  composition  and  chemical  states  of Nb2O5 -66.67  mol%  HfO2  plates  heated  up  to  2100 ◦ C  in  an  Ar  atmosphere  were  investigated.  The  results confirmed that the effective ablation time of the NbC-coating mixed with 50 mol% HfC was approximately 120 s, which  is  more  than  twice  that  of  the  pure  NbC  coating.  During  air  quenching,  NbO  and  NbO2  ultimately transformed  into  Nb2O5,  producing  compressive  stress  that  stabilized  the  t-HfO2 crystals.  Furthermore,  the formation of hafnium oxide significantly improved the thermal stability and viscosity of the Nb -O film, and the niobium  suboxide-based  solid  solution,  possessing  an  attractive  self-healing  performance,  increased  the compactness of the HfO2-based solid solution layer are responsible for the superior ablation performance of the NbC-HfC coating.

a b s t r a c t
To improve the ablation resistance of the coated carbon/carbon composites, HfC nanowires acting as reinforcement materials were introduced into ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating. Synthesis, morphology, mechanical properties and ablation resistance of the coating were researched. A porous HfC nanowire layer was uniformly covered on SiC coating. Both of the toughness and interface bonding strength of ZrB2-SiC coating with HfC nanowires were higher than those of the coating without HfC nanowires. HfC nanowires could effectively inhibit the cracking and peeling of outer coating during ablation. After oxyacetylene torch ablation for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of the coated sample without and with HfC nanowires was 0.20 mg/s and -0.12 mg/s, respectively.

A B S T R A C T
By a combination of mechanical activation, combustion synthesis, and spark plasma sintering methods, bulk materials in the Hf-Zr-C-N system with various Hf/Zr atomic ratios were produced. The mechanical activation process in a low-energy mode (350 rpm) for 90 min resulted in the formation of layered Hf/Zr/C composite particles 10 -30 μ m in size, depending on the Hf/Zr ratio. The diffraction spectra of the as-synthesized powders were described by several isomorphic (Hf,Zr)(C,N)ss cubic phases with different nitrogen content. Spark plasma sintering contributed to the homogenization of the chemical composition and allowed production of the dense (~98%) single-phase hafnium-zirconium carbonitrides with an FCC structure. The bulk ceramics exhibited superior hardness (~17 GPa) and fracture toughness (~5 MPa m 1/2 ), relative to (Hf,Zr)C carbides prepared by similar methods.
nitrogen atoms into the structure of amorphous SiC contributed to an increase in scratch and fracture resistance due to the formation of a C ---N triple bond, which inhibits the crystallization of SiC clusters.
The properties of ultra-high temperature carbonitride ceramics can be improved by introducing other refractory metals, for example, Hf, Ta, Nb, Zr, etc. Furthermore, our previous studies have shown [8] that the introduction of tantalum increases the Hf(C,N) oxidation resistance due to the formation of the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure. It was also demonstrated [9] that the introduction of Ti, Hf, and Nb alloying elements to Zr (C,N)  coatings  contributes  to  an  increase  in  wear  resistance  and hardness.

4.1.3. Oxidation studies and ablation behavior
For  hypersonic  and  re-entry  applications,  oxidation  resistance  is needed at temperatures above 2000 ◦ C; however, only limited literature is available discussing the high-temperature oxidation behavior [71,79, 112,113].  The  reinforcement  of  GNPs  in  TaC  suppresses  the  oxide thickness by 60%, improving the oxidation resistance via ' grain sealing ' mechanism  under  high  temperature  plasma  flow  for  60  s  exposure [112]. The combined effect of SiC and CNT on the oxidation behavior of TaC during a plasma jet exposure test with a heat flux of ~2.5 MWm GLYPH<0> 2 [107] exhibits an increased oxidation resistance in all the TaC -SiC-CNT based composites compared to that of TaC. The formation of protective SiO2 and  sealing  of  grains  by  CNTs  results  in  an  improvement  of oxidation resistance. Moreover, an enhanced thermal conductivity of composites (from ~29.9 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1  to 44.3 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 with synergistic addition of SiC and CNTs at 900 ◦ C) has improved the oxidation resistance. Fig. 8a shows the spark plasma sintered pellets of all compositions before, during, and after the plasma jet exposure. It may be noticed that almost negligible thermal damage is observed in the plasma jet-exposed TaC -SiC-CNT pellets (Fig. 8a). Temperature profiles of the back face of pellets during the test are shown in Fig. 8b [107], wherein the role of CNTs in dissipating the heat to minimize the radiative damage is illustrated in Fig. 9 [107].

Journal of the European Ceramic Society
Very recently, Nisar et al., [16] studied the performances of spark plasma sintered massive TaC-SiC composites under plasma arc-jet exposure, and recognised that the protective silica (coming from the SiC reinforcement) provided an extended stability domain for TaC, and therefore a significant improvement in the oxidation resistance.
Apart from the TaC and SiC phases, the application of another refractory compound such as TiC (melting point of 3340 K) in Cf /C composites for oxidation protection has very limited exposure in literature. The use of Ti in ultra-high temperature environments has been through MAX phase composites and titanium matrix composites (mainly in intermetallic form). TiO2 is expected to provide oxidation protection to Cf /C composites because of its melting point of 2123 K, as well as low diffusion coefficient of oxygen in the same. In addition, TiO2 in contact with Ta2O5 at temperatures above 1923 K, according to their binary phase diagram [17], can act as an additional fluid high temperature resistant glassy oxide medium able to support self-repairing mechanisms.
In this investigation the oxidation resistance in air at 1873 K up to 480 s nominal exposure time of a ternary ceramic carbides matrix containing SiC and (Ta,Ti)-mixed carbides was studied and compared to that of the (unprotected) Cf /C composite. The ablation/oxidation behaviour was more thoroughly investigated by exposing the Cf /C-SiC-TiC-TaC composite to an oxyacetylene torch flame characterised by 4 MW/m 2 of nominal heat flux.

A B S T R A C T

[18] processed twelve different equiatomic five-metal carbides using high-energy ball milling and spark plasma  sintering.  These  high  entropy  carbides  show  significantly enhanced hardness over the weighted average of the binary constituent ' s hardness and decreasing tendency with increasing valence electron concentration. In the case of the system (Ti-Zr-Hf-Nb-Ta)C they used different applied loads for the hardness measurement from 10 mN to 500 mN with resultant hardness of approximately 42 GPa to 25 GPa. Ye et. al. They also measured the microhardness of the systems with applied loads from 0.98 N to 9.8 N/49 N and measured indentation load-dependent hardness values.

2.1. Processing and microstructural characterization of MC-UHTCs
The starting UHTC powders of (Ta,Nb) C (pre-alloyed powder with 80 wt% TaC and 20 wt% NbC; 99.6% purity, particle size ~ 0.80 μ m, Inframat  Advanced  Materials,  LLC,  CT,  USA),  HfC  ( > 99.5%  purity, particle size ~ 0.33 μ m, Materion LLC, Cleveland, OH, USA) and TiC ( > 99.9% purity, particle size < 3 μ m, Inframat Advanced Materials, LLC, CT, USA) were procured. The pre-alloyed (Ta,Nb)C with lower wt.% of NbC (20 wt%) was selected as the base material due to the inability associated with NbC to be completely soluble in TaC. This has been attributed to the more significant difference in the atomic radius (Ta = 1.430 Å, and Nb = 1.448 Å) and the electronic structure of these two carbides. The irregularities with NbC segregation even with this prealloyed (Ta,Nb)C is observed, published elsewhere [7]. The raw powders in an equimolar amount corresponding to ternary and quaternary UHTC systems were mixed using ball milling [7]. The samples were then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS, Model 104, Thermal Technologies,  Santa  Rosa,  CA)  at  a  maximum  temperature  of  1850 ◦ C, applied pressure of 60 MPa for 10 mins with a heating rate of 100 ◦ C/ min. A single-phase, chemically homogeneous, and relative densification  of  ~  86%  for  binary,  and  ~  100%  for  ternary  and  quaternary UHTCs were obtained after sintering. The details of the starting powders,  ball-milled  powders,  and  sintering  can  be  found  in  our  recent publication [7]. Post plasma exposure, the oxide layer along the crosssection was examined using Schottky field-emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL-F100 FESEM JEOL Ltd., Akishima, Tokyo, Japan). Currently,  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  commonly  used  in ablation-resistant  coatings  include  ZrC,  TaC,  etc.  [11,18 -25].  Wang et al. used supersonic plasma spraying prepared TaC coatings on C/C composites [26]. Results show that TaC could form a Ta2O5 oxide layer with  a  certain  viscosity  and  fluidity  oxidizes  in  a  high  temperature environment above 1900 ◦ C, which can effectively prevent oxygen from further diffusion into the matrix. However, when the temperature increases to 2100 ◦ C, the viscosity of Ta2O5  decreases and the fluidity increases.  Ta2O5  is  blown  away  from  the  ablation  center  under  the erosion of high-temperature flame flow, forming a pit in the ablation center,  and  the  coating  loses  its  resistance  on  the  C/C  substrate. Compared with Ta2O5, ZrO2  produced by ZrC oxidation has a higher melting point (2680 ◦ C) and better high temperature resistance [27]. However,  ZrO2  does  not  exhibit  a  molten  flow  behavior  at  elevated temperatures, it forms a porous and loosely structures which provides a channel for oxygen to diffuse into the matrix [28]. Previous research has demonstrated that incorporating an optimal quantity of SiC into ZrC enhances the coatings ablation resistance. SiC undergoes oxidation at elevated temperatures, resulting in the formation of SiO2 glass. The SiO2 glass exhibits fluidity at high temperatures and possesses the ability to self-repair  the  loosely  porous  layer  of  ZrO2 generated  during  the oxidation of ZrC, thereby impeding the continued diffusion of oxygen into the material matrix [27,29]. At present, the research on the ablation resistance  of  TaC  and  ZrC  single-layer  coatings  is  well-developed.

a protective SiO2 glass layer besides ZrO2 during the oxidation process. This oxide layer plays a decisive role in blocking oxygen diffusion and enhancing the overall thermal protection performance of the coating [16]. However, at temperatures above 2000 ◦ C, the high volatility of SiO2 and poor sintering behavior of ZrO2 often result in porous, unstable oxide scales, limiting the effectiveness of these coatings for long-term protection  [17].  Therefore,  efforts  have  been  made  to  enhance  the ablation resistance of ZrC-SiC composite ceramic coatings by incorporating the high-melting-point and chemically stable component, tantalum carbide (TaC). The improved performance is primarily attributed to the high melting point (approximately 3880 ◦ C) and superior chemical stability of TaC at elevated temperatures, which considerably enhances the thermodynamic stability and oxidation resistance of the ZrC-TaC-SiC ternary phase ceramics [18]. Additionally, TaC primarily oxidizes  into  tantalum  pentoxide  (Ta2O5)  with  a  melting  point  of approximately 1872 ◦ C. The formed Ta2O5 can generate a continuous, dense protective scale on the coating, effectively preventing the penetration of oxygen and other oxidative gases, protecting the underlying substrate from excessive oxidation and erosion [19]. Furthermore, the molten Ta2O5, with suitable fluidity at high temperatures, actively fills cracks or voids in the oxide layer and solidifies upon cooling to a dense protective layer, substantially extending the service life of the coating [20].

A B S T R A C T
In this work, we integrate ML models with experimental validation to accelerate the performance investigation of ZrC-TaC-SiC ternary ceramic coatings for C/C composites. Based on the predicted performance indicators, four kinds of ZrC-TaC-SiC coating compositions were selected  and  prepared  by  supersonic  atmospheric  plasma  spraying (SAPS). The correlation among composition, oxide scale morphology, and ablation mechanism was examined to identify the optimal formulation for prolonged thermal protection. This study demonstrates the effectiveness  of  ML-assisted  design  in  discovering  high-performance ceramic coatings and provides new insights into the structural evolution of multicomponent UHTC  coatings under extreme thermal conditions.

3.2. Ablation behavior
Fig. 7 indicates the surface, cross-section morphologies and related EDS results of the 60s-ablated ZrC and HfC coatings. The surface of the ablated ZrC and HfC coatings exhibited a porous structure, which was composed of solid particles. There was a penetrating fissure between the oxide scale and the un-oxidized layer, as evidenced in cross-sectional morphologies. The thickness of the un-oxidized coating of ZrC and HfC coating was about 190 and 200 m m, respectively. Considering the original thickness of the as-sprayed coatings (ZrC:370 m m; HfC:280 m m), by difference, the thickness of oxidized coating was 180 and 80 m m, respectively. The oxide scale thickness of the two coatings showed that the HfC coating exhibited better oxidation ablation resistance.
Fig. 8 presents the thickness change of the oxide layer for the ZS and HS coatings. The oxide layer of ZS and HS coatings grew rapidly within the first 150 s, then increased slowly. What counts is that the oxide layer of the HS coating is much thinner than that of ZS coating from the beginning to the end, indicating that the HS coating might offer excellent oxidation/ ablation resistance.

A B S T R A C T
1800 ℃ for 10 hours was 6.729 mg · cm GLYPH<0> 2 . Multi-arc ion plating and pack cementation technologies were used to prepare a MoSi2-Mo [6] coating. And the coating remained intact after 12 hours in an oxidizing environment at a high temperature of 1750 ℃ . A MoSi2-TaSi2 [7] coating was prepared by slurry sintering, vacuum sintering, and pack cementation technologies. The coating has effectively protected the substrate at 1700 ℃ for 12 hours without failure. Slurry sintering and pack cementation technologies were used to fabricate a MoSi2-WSi2-HfSi2-TiSi2 [8] coating, which remained intact after 10.2 hours at 1800 ℃ . In summary, the oxidation resistance of the above-mentioned coatings was all attributed to the formation of an oxide scale (SiO2, ZrO2, ZrSiO4, and HfO2, etc.) with low oxygen permeability and moderate viscosity, as well as good self-healing ability. Besides, electrophoretic deposition and laser melting technologies were used to prepare the ZrB2 [9] coating. It lasted only 60 seconds at 2000 ℃ . The oxidation of ZrB2 resulted in the formation of a large amount of ZrO2, accompanied by the significant vaporization of B2O3, resulting in a large number of cracks and porosity in the coating, which ultimately led to coating failure. Except for the above surface coating preparation technologies, plasma spraying [10,11] is a viable and potential method that can also be used to prepare thermal protective coatings on the Ta alloys, though few literatures have been reported according to the knowledge of the authors. This technology has the advantages of fast deposition rates and easy control of coating thickness [10 -12] and is suitable for the preparation of high melting point ceramic coatings. However, plasma-sprayed thermal protection coatings have relatively high porosities and poor bonding strength with the substrate, which makes it easy for oxygen to penetrate and oxidize the substrate.

chunks.text:
4. Summary
The MC-UHTCs showed remarkable improvement in the oxidation resistance during plasma exposure testing compared to binary UHTCs. This has been attributed to the preferential oxidation and a reduction in low melting temperature unary oxide content in the surface oxide scale from > 82% in binary UHTC to < 13% in MC-UHTC. A thermally stable complex protective oxide layer of Hf6Ta2O17 (a primary phase) in MCUHTCs has further suppressed the oxide formation. As a result of the relative thermal stability of transition metals and components in the MCUHTCs, preferential oxidation occurs in MC-UHTCs, i.e., Hf in ternary and Ti in quaternary UHTC system, while both are forming the protective mixed complex oxide layer to remarkably improve ( > 40 times) the oxidation resistance over binary and unary UHTC systems. Both XRD and XPS analysis showed retention of metal carbide phase only in MCUHTCs. The study suggests that MC-UHTCs designed to form a mixed complex protective oxide layer of group IV element under service conditions elicit extended potential in extreme space vehicle applications.

A B S T R A C T
Multi-component ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTCs) has garnered attention due to their improved thermo-mechanical and oxidation properties. MC-UHTC was progressively formed by adding a UHTC component at  each  step,  producing  binary,  ternary  and  quaternary  UHTC  systems,  processed  by  spark  plasma  sintering (SPS). We investigate the role of multi-elements and thermochemical stability of the surface oxide layer formed during plasma arc jet exposure ( T > 2500 ◦ C) of MC-UHTCs using core-level X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) correlated with the structural investigation. The results indicate that adding a UHTC component improves oxidation resistance due to the assemblage of refractory phases forming complex mixed oxides via preferential oxidation. XPS spectra of Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, and Hf4f showed that the nature of the chemical bonds switched from metal carbides to metal oxides (and metal oxy-carbides) in the binary system with retained metal carbides in ternary and quaternary UHTCs. The absence of a metal carbide peak in the high-resolution C1s scan of the binary system also supports this finding. The principal complex mixed oxide phases emanated in the exiguous oxidation of MC-UHTCs are quantified using Rietveld analysis. Overall, this study suggests that MC-UHTC is a promising material for possible application in thermal protection systems (TPS).
